<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Five Freedoms of the Air. **[3 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Model Answer:**
The Five Freedoms of the Air, codified under the **International Air Services Transit Agreement (1944)**, are the commercial rights granted to airlines of one state to operate into and over the territory of another state:
- **1st Freedom:** Right to overfly a foreign state's territory without landing.
- **2nd Freedom:** Right to land in a foreign state for non-traffic purposes (e.g., refuelling).
- **3rd Freedom:** Right to disembark passengers/mail in a foreign state, taken on board in the airline's own state.
- **4th Freedom:** Right to take on passengers/mail in a foreign state destined for the airline's own state.
- **5th Freedom:** Right to carry traffic between two foreign states on a route originating/terminating in the airline's own state.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly list the freedoms as "stages" rather than "rights." Always phrase them as **commercial rights**, not sequential steps. KTU deducts **1 mark** for this.

### Question 2: What is a Bilateral Air Services Agreement (BASA)? **[3 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Model Answer:**
A **Bilateral Air Services Agreement (BASA)** is a sovereign-to-sovereign treaty between **two countries** that defines the terms under which their respective airlines may operate international air services. It specifies:
- Designated carriers and routes.
- Capacity entitlements (seats/flights per week).
- Tariff filing rules.
- Safety and security obligations.
- Dispute resolution mechanisms.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse BASA with the **Chicago Convention**. The Convention is **multilateral**; BASA is **bilateral**. Confusing these costs the entire 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Policy of Air Transport in India [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the salient features of India's National Civil Aviation Policy (NCAP) 2016. Discuss the role of DGCA, AAI, and MoCA in the Indian aviation regulatory framework.**

**Model Solution:**

The **National Civil Aviation Policy (NCAP) 2016**, notified on **15 June 2016**, is the master document guiding India's civil aviation sector. Its salient features are:

1. **Route Dispersal Guidelines (RDG):** Mandates airlines to operate in remote, Northeast, hilly, and island regions; tier-1 routes subsidise tier-2 and tier-3 routes.
2. **Fare Structure:** Domestic fares capped within a **lower- and upper-band** relative to cost per available seat kilometre (CASK) — the original "200/100" rule later relaxed.
3. **5/20 Rule Abolition:** Historically, Indian carriers needed **5 aircraft and 20 years of domestic operation** to fly international. NCAP replaced it with a **balance-sheet and operational capability** criterion.
4. **Regional Connectivity Scheme (UDAN):** Airfare capped at **₹2,500 for ~500 km** stage length, with **Viability Gap Funding (VGF)** from the government and a **Regional Connectivity Fund (RCF)**.
5. **Open Sky Policy on Limited Routes:** Selective liberalisation to boost tourism.
6. **Cargo & MRO Hub:** India targeted as a global **Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO)** hub with tax incentives.

**Regulatory Architecture:**

- **Ministry of Civil Aviation (MoCA):** Apex policy-making body, frames national aviation policy, negotiates BASAs.
- **Directorate General of Civil Aviation (DGCA):** Statutory regulatory body under MoCA; handles **airworthiness, pilot licensing, ATC standards, air operator permits**, and **safety oversight** (under ICAO SARPs).
- **Airports Authority of India (AAI):** Owns/operates **137+ airports**; provides **Air Navigation Services (ANS)**; implements **UDAN** infrastructure on the ground.

$$\boxed{\text{NCAP} = \text{MoCA Policy} \xrightarrow{\text{Implemented by}} \text{DGCA} + \text{AAI}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Compute the CAGR of domestic air passengers in India, given that 2018 had 137 million passengers and 2023 had 211 million. Forecast traffic for 2030, and discuss the policy implications for airport infrastructure planning.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Variables**
$P_{0} = 137$ million, $P_{n} = 211$ million, $n = 5$ years.

**Step 2 — Apply CAGR Formula**
$$g = \left(\dfrac{211}{137}\right)^{1/5} - 1$$
$$g = (1.5401)^{0.2} - 1$$
$$g = 1.0903 - 1 = 0.0903 \approx 9.03\%$$

**Step 3 — Forecast 2030 Traffic** ($m = 7$ years beyond 2023)
$$P_{2030} = 211 \times (1.0903)^{7}$$
$$\log_{10}(1.0903) = 0.03760$$
$$0.03760 \times 7 = 0.2632$$
$$10^{0.2632} = 1.833$$
$$P_{2030} = 211 \times 1.833 = 386.8 \text{ million}$$

**Step 4 — Policy Implications**
- **Capacity doubling time:** $72/9.03 \approx 7.97 \approx 8$ years. Major airports need **new terminals and runways every decade**.
- **NCAP revision:** Annual RDG, UDAN, and PPP frameworks must scale 2x.
- **MRO and Cargo policy:** Domestic cargo throughput to grow in proportion.
- **ATC modernization:** Mode S radars, satellite-based navigation (GAGAN).

$$\boxed{g = 9.03\%, \quad P_{2030} \approx 386.8 \text{ million Pax, doubling time} \approx 8 \text{ years}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** use simple arithmetic average growth — it gives ~18% which is **wrong**. CAGR is geometric. KTU deducts **2 marks** for using arithmetic mean. Always show the formula and the logarithmic computation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Five Freedoms & Open Sky Policy [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the Five Freedoms of the Air with suitable examples. Differentiate between Bilateral, Multilateral, and Open Sky air services agreements.**

**Model Solution:**

The **Five Freedoms** are codified in the **International Air Services Transit Agreement** and the **International Air Transport Agreement**, both signed at the **Chicago Convention, 7 December 1944**.

1. **1st Freedom — Overfly Right:** Air India aircraft can overfly **German airspace** on its Mumbai–New York route.
2. **2nd Freedom — Technical Landing:** Emirates aircraft refuelling at **Colombo** on a Dubai–Maldives flight.
3. **3rd Freedom — Own Pax Carried Abroad:** Air India carrying Mumbai → London passengers.
4. **4th Freedom — Foreign Pax to Own Country:** British Airways carrying London → Mumbai passengers.
5. **5th Freedom — Cabotage-like Foreign Route:** Singapore Airlines operating **Singapore – Frankfurt – New York**, carrying Frankfurt–New York traffic.

**Comparison of Air Services Agreements:**

| Aspect | Bilateral (BASA) | Multilateral | Open Sky |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parties** | 2 countries | 3+ countries (e.g., EU-US Open Skies) | 2+ countries with full liberalization |
| **Capacity Caps** | Yes (seats/week) | Reduced or removed | **No caps** |
| **Tariffs** | Filed/approved | Mostly free | **Fully free** |
| **Cabotage** | Not allowed | Limited | Sometimes granted (e.g., EU domestic) |
| **Example** | India–UAE BASA | **EU-US Open Skies 2007/2008** | India–US Open Sky (proposed) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write "Open Sky = no rules." Open Sky removes **commercial** restrictions but **safety, security, sovereignty, and environmental rules still apply**. This nuance fetches the **final 1 mark** in KTU valuation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: "The Open Sky Policy is a double-edged sword." Critically analyse this statement in the context of Indian aviation, citing NCAP 2016 and UDAN.**

**Model Solution:**

**The "Double-Edged" Argument:**

**Edge 1 — Benefits (the Proponent View):**
- **Increased Connectivity:** Direct international access boosts tourism and trade. India–US Open Sky could increase seat capacity from ~**35,000/week to virtually unlimited**.
- **Lower Fares:** Increased competition drives yields down — e.g., Delhi–Mumbai economy class fares dropped ~**40%** post-2004 partial liberalisation.
- **Foreign Direct Investment (FDI):** Indian carriers gain global capital, technology transfer, and code-share partners.
- **Hub Status:** India can become a **global hub** like Dubai (Emirates), leveraging its central geography between East Asia, Europe, Africa.

**Edge 2 — Challenges (the Critic View):**
- **Market Domination by Foreign Giants:** Emirates, Etihad, Qatar Airways may overwhelm domestic carriers like IndiGo, Air India, SpiceJet.
- **Predatory Pricing:** Foreign carriers with deeper pockets can set fares below domestic CASK, bankrupting local airlines.
- **Loss of Sovereignty:** Critical decisions on routes, fares, and slots move out of DGCA's hands.
- **Capacity Glut at Tier-1 Airports:** Mumbai, Delhi saturate; secondary airports (Nagpur, Jaipur, Mangaluru) remain under-utilised.
- **NCAP 2016 Response:** Selective open sky, NOT blanket. UDAN shields domestic Tier-2/3 connectivity.

$$\boxed{\text{Open Sky} = \text{Maximum Commercial Freedom} \quad \text{vs} \quad \text{NCAP 2016} = \text{Calibrated Liberalization}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Critical analysis requires **both** sides. Writing only the advantages or only the disadvantages fetches at most **3 of 7 marks**. Always structure as **Pro → Con → Verdict**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Chicago Convention 1944** — Article 1 grants **state sovereignty over airspace**; 19 Annexes contain SARPs.
- [ ] **ICAO (HQ Montréal)** — UN agency; sets SARPs, no enforcement power.
- [ ] **IATA (HQ Montréal)** — Trade body of 290 airlines; handles commercial standards.
- [ ] **Five Freedoms of the Air** — Overfly, Tech-Stop, Carry Own Pax, Carry Return Pax, Carry Foreign Traffic.
- [ ] **Sixth/Seventh/Eighth Freedoms** — Sixth: hub-style; Seventh: own state's cabotage abroad; Eighth: domestic cabotage.
- [ ] **BASA** — Bilateral treaty; capacity caps, tariffs, designated carriers.
- [ ] **Open Sky** — No commercial restrictions; example: **EU-US 2007/2008**.
- [ ] **NCAP 2016 (India)** — Abolished 5/20 rule; capped UDAN fares at ₹2,500/500 km; VGF funding.
- [ ] **UDAN** — Regional Connectivity Scheme, launched 2016, RCS-UDAN 5.0 in 2023.
- [ ] **MoCA** — Apex policy ministry; **DGCA** — safety regulator; **AAI** — airport operator; **BCAS** — security.
- [ ] **CAGR formula** for traffic forecasting: $g = (P_n/P_0)^{1/n} - 1$.
- [ ] **Rule of 72** — Doubling time in years = $72/g\%$.
- [ ] **CORSIA** — ICAO's global CO₂ offsetting scheme; affects aircraft fleet policy.
- [ ] **Article 1 Chicago Convention** — Always cite verbatim in KTU answers for full marks.
- [ ] **5/20 rule was abolished** under NCAP 2016 — not abolished by DGCA or AAI. **Common trick question.**
- [ ] **BASA ≠ Chicago Convention** — BASA is bilateral; Convention is multilateral.
- [ ] **Open Sky ≠ No Rules** — Safety/security/sovereignty rules still apply.
- [ ] **Always use geometric (CAGR), not arithmetic, growth** in KTU calculations.
- [ ] **India is world's 3rd largest domestic aviation market** (since 2017); projected 3rd largest overall by 2030.
- [ ] **IATA Level of Service C** is $1.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{pax}$ for terminal sizing — used in conjunction with traffic forecasts.
- [ ] **NCAP 2016** has 8–10 major features; recall at least **5** for a 7-mark answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always anchor policy answers to the **Chicago Convention (1944)** as the origin point, then descend the **ICAO → IATA → MoCA → DGCA → AAI** ladder. Examiners reward this structured narrative with the **highest marks band (9–10 / 10)** in valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aircraft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aircraft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of airport engineering, an **aircraft** is defined as any machine supported in the atmosphere by the reaction of air upon its surfaces, deriving its lift primarily from aerodynamic forces. For the purpose of **airport planning and design (PECET744)**, the aircraft is treated as the *design vehicle* — the critical load whose physical and performance characteristics dictate almost every geometric element of an airside facility, including runway length, width, pavement thickness, taxiway geometry, and apron layout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The module on *Aircraft* requires students to master the **ICAO Aircraft Reference Code**, aircraft physical dimensions, weight classifications, landing gear geometry, turning radius, and jet blast effects. These form the **input design parameters** for every subsequent runway, taxiway, and apron design calculation.

The relevant aircraft characteristics that govern airport design are classified into the following four engineering categories:

| Category | Parameter | Airport Design Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric** | Wingspan, Length, Height, Tail height | Separation distances, gate sizing |
| **Weight** | Maximum Take-Off Weight (MTOW), Landing Weight | Pavement design, runway strength |
| **Performance** | Stall speed, Approach speed, Take-off/Landing distance | Runway length and width |
| **Operational** | Landing gear configuration, Turning radius, Engine type | Taxiway fillet design, blast walls |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of an aircraft as a **moving architectural load** in the airside environment. Just as a civil engineer studies the *truck* before designing a highway (axle load, wheelbase, turning radius), an airport engineer studies the *aircraft* before designing the runway (gear configuration, approach speed, blast cone). The aircraft is the **"design vehicle"** — every geometric and structural element of the airfield is sized to safely accommodate the *critical (worst-case) aircraft* expected to use the facility.

> [!NOTE]
> The **critical aircraft** is defined as the most demanding aircraft type in regular scheduled operations, whose characteristics (typically the largest, heaviest, or highest-approach-speed aircraft) drive the design. For example, designing for the Airbus A380 automatically accommodates smaller aircraft like the B737 or A320.

### 1.3 Standard ICAO Reference Metrics

The following are the standard ICAO-recognized reference metrics used throughout this module:

- **Approach Speed ($V_{at}$)** : The indicated airspeed at threshold (typically 1.3 × stall speed), measured in **knots**.
- **Wingspan ($b$)** : Maximum straight-line distance between wing tips, measured in **metres**.
- **Outer Main Gear Wheel Span (OMGWS)** : Distance between the outside edges of the main landing gear wheels, measured in **metres**.
- **Maximum Take-Off Weight (MTOW)** : Maximum certified weight at brake release, measured in **kilograms or tonnes**.
- **Aircraft Classification Number (ACN)** : A dimensionless number expressing the *relative* pavement-loading effect of an aircraft on a defined pavement type and subgrade strength.
- **Pavement Classification Number (PCN)** : A dimensionless number expressing the *bearing strength* of a pavement for unrestricted operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Aircraft Classification

#### 2.1.1 ICAO Classification by Wake Turbulence (Heavy / Medium / Light)

Wake turbulence is the disturbed air left behind an aircraft in flight, caused by the vortices at the wing tips. It is a major hazard for following aircraft. ICAO classifies aircraft into three categories:

| Category | MTOW Range | Example Aircraft |
|---|---|---|
| **Heavy (H)** | $\geq 136{,}000$ kg | B747, A380, B777 |
| **Medium (M)** | $7{,}000$ to $< 136{,}000$ kg | A320, B737, A310 |
| **Light (L)** | $< 7{,}000$ kg | Cessna 172, Beechcraft 1900 |

**Why it matters:** The runway-separation time and longitudinal separation minima on approach depend on the wake category of the leading and following aircraft. **Heavy** aircraft produce the strongest wake vortices and require the largest time separation.

#### 2.1.2 ICAO Aircraft Reference Code (Aerodrome Reference Code)

The ICAO Annex 14 Aerodrome Reference Code is a **two-element alphanumeric code** that uniquely classifies each aircraft for the purpose of geometric airport design. The code has two independent parts:

- **Code Number (1 to 4):** Based on the **Reference Field Length (RFL)** or the approach speed, depending on the airport's primary operational requirement. For most runways, it is based on **approach speed** $V_{at}$ in knots:
$$\text{Code Number} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{at}}{10} \right\rfloor \quad \text{(rounded down)}$$

- **Code Letter (A to F):** Based on the **wingspan** and **OMGWS** of the aircraft. The controlling dimension is the larger of the two:

| Code Letter | Wingspan (m) | OMGWS (m) | Representative Aircraft |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | $< 15$ | $< 4.5$ | Cessna 152 |
| B | $15 - < 24$ | $4.5 - < 6$ | Dash 8, ATR 72 |
| C | $24 - < 36$ | $6 - < 9$ | A320, B737, B757 |
| D | $36 - < 52$ | $9 - < 14$ | B767, B777 (small), A310 |
| E | $52 - < 65$ | $9 - < 14$ | B777-300, A330, A340 |
| F | $65 - < 80$ | $14 - < 16$ | B747-400, B747-8 |
| F+ | $\geq 80$ | $\geq 16$ | A380 (Code F+) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The Aerodrome Reference Code determines:
> 1. **Runway width** (e.g., Code Number 3 = 30 m wide runway; Code Number 4 = 45 m wide for Code E)
> 2. **Runway strip width** (e.g., Code Letter C = 150 m wide strip)
> 3. **Taxiway width** and fillet dimensions
> 4. **Runway-to-taxiway centerline separation**

### 2.2 Aircraft Physical Dimensions

The key geometric parameters for each major aircraft type are tabulated below. These values are required for KTU numerical problems on separation distances and gate sizing.

| Aircraft | MTOW (t) | Wingspan (m) | Length (m) | Tail Height (m) | OMGWS (m) | Approach Speed (kt) | Code |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A380-800 | 575 | 79.75 | 72.7 | 24.1 | 14.3 | 137 | 4F |
| B747-400 | 397 | 64.4 | 70.7 | 19.4 | 12.6 | 152 | 4E |
| B777-300ER | 352 | 64.8 | 73.9 | 18.5 | 12.6 | 145 | 4E |
| A330-300 | 242 | 60.3 | 58.8 | 16.8 | 12.6 | 142 | 4E |
| B767-300ER | 186 | 47.6 | 54.9 | 15.8 | 10.8 | 137 | 4D |
| A320-200 | 78 | 35.8 | 37.6 | 11.8 | 8.7 | 137 | 3C |
| B737-800 | 79 | 35.8 | 39.5 | 12.5 | 7.0 | 142 | 3C |
| ATR 72-500 | 22 | 27.0 | 22.7 | 7.7 | 4.1 | 115 | 3B |
| Dash 8-Q400 | 28 | 28.4 | 32.8 | 8.3 | 4.5 | 120 | 3B |

### 2.3 Aircraft Weight Classification

Aircraft weight affects pavement design through the **ACN** (Aircraft Classification Number) and through the equivalent single-wheel load on the landing gear.

| ICAO Class | MTOW Range (kg) | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Class 1** | $< 3{,}000$ | Light single-engine |
| **Class 2** | $3{,}000 - < 6{,}000$ | Light twin |
| **Class 3** | $6{,}000 - < 12{,}500$ | Business jets |
| **Class 4** | $12{,}500 - < 50{,}000$ | Regional turboprop |
| **Class 5** | $50{,}000 - < 100{,}000$ | B737, A320 |
| **Class 6** | $100{,}000 - < 200{,}000$ | B767, A310 |
| **Class 7** | $200{,}000 - < 300{,}000$ | B777, A330 |
| **Class 8** | $300{,}000 - < 500{,}000$ | B747-400 |
| **Class 9** | $\geq 500{,}000$ | A380 |

### 2.4 Landing Gear Configurations

The **landing gear configuration** determines the pavement stress distribution. The standard gear types are:

- **Single Wheel (SW):** Light aircraft, e.g., Cessna 172
- **Dual Wheel (DW):** Business jets and small regional aircraft
- **Dual Tandem (DT):** Four wheels per main gear truck, e.g., B777
- **Double Dual Tandem (DDT):** Eight wheels per main gear truck, e.g., A380
- **Bogie (Twin-wheel bogie on centre line):** Large aircraft with more than 4 wheels per truck, e.g., A380, B747-8

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The **Equivalent Single Wheel Load (ESWL)** concept converts the complex multi-wheel gear to an equivalent single-wheel load for flexible pavement thickness design. The most commonly used method is the **Westergaard** deflection equivalent, and the **FAA** (Federal Aviation Administration) lead to the **Equivalency Factor** ($EF$) used in CBR thickness design:
> $$t = \sqrt{P \cdot \frac{EF}{8.1 \cdot CBR} - \frac{A}{\pi}}$$
> where $P$ = wheel load (kg), $A$ = tyre contact area (cm²), and $CBR$ = California Bearing Ratio of subgrade.

### 2.5 Turning Radius and Nose Gear Steering

Aircraft do not pivot on a single point during taxiing. The centre of the turn is approximately on the **extended centreline of the main gear** (for large aircraft, the extended centreline of the outermost main gear wheel). The **nose gear** can be steered up to a maximum angle (typically $\pm 70°$ to $80°$ for large aircraft), but for normal taxiing, the maximum effective steering angle is $\pm 60°$ to $70°$.

The **minimum turn radius** is given by:

$$R_{min} = \frac{a}{\sin(\theta_{max})} \quad \text{or in practice} \quad R \approx \frac{L}{\tan(\theta)}$$

where:
- $a$ = wheelbase (distance from nose gear to main gear pivot point)
- $\theta_{max}$ = maximum effective nose-wheel steering angle
- $L$ = wheelbase length

For B737 / A320 (Code C): $R \approx 22.5$ m
For B777 / A330 (Code E): $R \approx 30 - 35$ m
For A380 (Code F+): $R \approx 45 - 50$ m

> [!NOTE]
> The practical consequence is the design of **taxiway fillets** — the widened sections at taxiway intersections and curves that allow the main gear to follow the curve while the nose tracks the centreline. The fillet geometry is designed to keep the **outer main gear wheel** within the pavement edge, with a clearance of typically 1.5 m to 4.5 m (depending on the code letter).

### 2.6 Jet Blast and Engine Wake

The **jet blast** (exhaust wake) from aircraft engines can damage pavements, vehicles, and personnel. Key parameters:

- **Idle thrust:** Velocity at ground level $\approx 30 - 50$ m/s
- **Take-off thrust:** Velocity at ground level $\approx 80 - 130$ m/s (for high-bypass turbofans); up to $200$ m/s for older low-bypass engines
- **Effective blast distance:** The distance behind the aircraft beyond which jet blast velocity falls below 50 km/h (≈ 14 m/s), the standard for safe pedestrian exposure.

ICAO Annex 14 specifies that the runway-end safety area (RESA) and the area behind the runway end must be designed assuming **maximum take-off thrust on a stationary aircraft** at the runway threshold. For Code 4 aircraft, the jet blast length behind the runway end can be as long as 60–90 m.

### 2.7 Performance Parameters Affecting Runway Length

The most critical performance parameter for runway length design is the **Reference Field Length (RFL)**, defined as the minimum field length required for take-off at **Maximum Certificated Take-Off Weight (MCTOW)**, **sea level**, **standard atmospheric conditions** (15 °C, 1013.25 hPa), and **zero wind**.

The actual **balanced runway length (BRL)** required at a specific site is corrected from the RFL using:

$$BRL = RFL \times C_{elev} \times C_{temp} \times C_{slope} \times C_{wind}$$

The standard correction factors (FAA/ICAO) are:

| Correction | Symbol | Formula (Standard) | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Elevation | $C_{elev}$ | $1 + 0.07 \times \dfrac{h}{300}$ | $h$ = runway elevation in metres above MSL |
| Temperature | $C_{temp}$ | $1 + 0.01 \times (T - (15 - 0.00698 \cdot h))$ | $T$ = aerodrome reference temperature in °C |
| Slope | $C_{slope}$ | $1 + 0.1 \times \dfrac{s}{100}$ | $s$ = effective runway gradient (%) |
| Wind | $C_{wind}$ | $1 - \dfrac{W}{V_{TO}}$ | $W$ = headwind component; $V_{TO}$ = take-off speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The runway length obtained from the RFL corrections is the **take-off distance**. If the runway is to also serve as a balanced runway for landing, the **landing distance required (LDR)** is calculated separately, and the actual runway length is the **greater** of the two. The balanced field length is then the maximum of: {take-off run required, take-off distance required, landing distance required}.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|
| **ICAO Code Number** | $N = \left\lfloor V_{at} / 10 \right\rfloor$ | Runway design speed category |
| **ICAO Code Letter** | $b$ or $OMGWS$ governs | Taxiway/runway width |
| **Wake Separation (Heavy→Heavy)** | $t = 2$ NM longitudinal | Approach separation |
| **Stall Speed from Approach Speed** | $V_s = V_{at} / 1.3$ | Runway length estimation |
| **Minimum Turn Radius** | $R \approx a / \sin(\theta_{max})$ | Fillet design |
| **BRL Elevation Correction** | $C_{elev} = 1 + 0.07 \cdot h / 300$ | Runway length |
| **BRL Temperature Correction** | $C_{temp} = 1 + 0.01 \cdot (T - T_{ref})$ | Runway length |
| **ACN vs PCN Compatibility** | ACN $\leq$ PCN | Pavement structural design |
| **Effective Gradient** | $s_{eff} = (h_{max} - h_{min}) / L$ | Runway length correction |
| **Equivalency Factor (FAA)** | $EF = f(\text{gear type, load})$ | Flexible pavement thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> All velocities in the above formulas are expressed in **knots (kt)**, where 1 kt = 0.5144 m/s. ICAO documents are written in **imperial/nautical units**, and students must be fluent in **unit conversions** — a common source of mark loss in KTU calculations.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code from Aircraft Data

**Problem Statement:** An Airbus A330-300 has the following certified characteristics: Wingspan = 60.30 m, OMGWS = 12.6 m, Approach speed at maximum landing weight = 142 kt. Determine the ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code.

**Step 1 — Identify the Code Letter**
The Code Letter is determined by the **larger** of (i) Wingspan and (ii) OMGWS. Compare both with the Code Letter table:

Wingspan $b = 60.30$ m. From the table: $52 \le 60.30 < 65$ m → **Code E**.
OMGWS $= 12.6$ m. From the table: $9 \le 12.6 < 14$ m → **Code E**.

Both values fall in Code E, so the **governing Code Letter is E**.

**Step 2 — Identify the Code Number**
The Code Number is determined by the approach speed $V_{at}$ in knots:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{at}}{10} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{142}{10} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 14.2 \right\rfloor = 14$$

But the Code Number only has 4 ranges (1, 2, 3, 4). The Code Number is the **closest matching ICAO bracket**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Approach Speed Range for Code 4: } \quad & V_{at} \ge 143 \text{ kt (per ICAO Annex 14, } V_{at} \geq 60 \text{ nm/hr + threshold)} \\
\text{Approach Speed Range for Code 3: } \quad & 50 \le V_{at} < 143 \text{ kt}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $V_{at} = 142$ kt $< 143$ kt, the **Code Number is 3**.

**Step 3 — Compose the Aerodrome Reference Code**
The Aerodrome Reference Code is the combination: **Code 3E**.

**Step 4 — Airport Design Implications**
For a 3E aerodrome, ICAO Annex 14 specifies:
- Runway width: **45 m**
- Runway shoulder width: 7.5 m
- Runway strip width: **300 m** (150 m each side of centreline)
- Runway-to-taxiway centreline separation: **176 m** (for Code E)
- Taxiway width: **23 m**

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Balanced Runway Length (BRL) Calculation

**Problem Statement:** The manufacturer of a Boeing 737-800 specifies a Reference Field Length (RFL) of 2100 m at sea level under standard conditions. The runway is to be constructed at an elevation of 600 m above MSL, with an aerodrome reference temperature of 32 °C, an effective runway gradient of 0.8%, and a prevailing headwind component of 10 kt. The take-off speed at MTOW is 145 kt. Compute the actual balanced runway length.

**Step 1 — List the Input Data**
- RFL $= 2100$ m
- Elevation $h = 600$ m
- Reference temperature $T = 32$ °C
- Effective slope $s = 0.8$%
- Headwind $W = 10$ kt
- Take-off speed $V_{TO} = 145$ kt

**Step 2 — Apply the Elevation Correction**
The standard formula for elevation correction is:

$$C_{elev} = 1 + 0.07 \cdot \frac{h}{300}$$

Substituting $h = 600$ m:

$$C_{elev} = 1 + 0.07 \cdot \frac{600}{300} = 1 + 0.07 \cdot 2 = 1 + 0.14 = 1.14$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Temperature Correction**
The standard reference temperature at elevation $h$ is:

$$T_{ref} = 15 - 0.00698 \cdot h$$

Substituting $h = 600$ m:

$$T_{ref} = 15 - 0.00698 \cdot 600 = 15 - 4.188 = 10.812 \text{ °C}$$

The temperature deviation is:

$$\Delta T = T - T_{ref} = 32 - 10.812 = 21.188 \text{ °C}$$

The temperature correction factor is:

$$C_{temp} = 1 + 0.01 \cdot \Delta T = 1 + 0.01 \cdot 21.188 = 1 + 0.21188 = 1.21188$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Slope Correction**
The slope correction is:

$$C_{slope} = 1 + 0.1 \cdot \frac{s}{100}$$

Substituting $s = 0.8\%$:

$$C_{slope} = 1 + 0.1 \cdot \frac{0.8}{100} = 1 + 0.1 \cdot 0.008 = 1 + 0.0008 = 1.0008$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Wind Correction**
The wind correction (headwind reduces required length) is:

$$C_{wind} = 1 - \frac{W}{V_{TO}}$$

Substituting $W = 10$ kt and $V_{TO} = 145$ kt:

$$C_{wind} = 1 - \frac{10}{145} = 1 - 0.06897 = 0.93103$$

**Step 6 — Compute the BRL**
The balanced runway length is the product of all correction factors:

$$BRL = RFL \times C_{elev} \times C_{temp} \times C_{slope} \times C_{wind}$$

Substituting each value:

$$\begin{aligned}
BRL &= 2100 \times 1.14 \times 1.21188 \times 1.0008 \times 0.93103 \\
&= 2100 \times 1.14 \times 1.21188 \times 1.0008 \times 0.93103
\end{aligned}$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
2100 \times 1.14 &= 2394 \\
2394 \times 1.21188 &= 2901.241 \\
2901.241 \times 1.0008 &= 2903.563 \\
2903.563 \times 0.93103 &= 2703.234 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$BRL \approx 2703 \text{ m} \approx 2700 \text{ m (rounded up to nearest 100 m)}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation**
A runway length of 2700 m is sufficient for the B737-800 at MTOW under the given site conditions. The runway must be designed to this length, plus any additional **stopway** or **clearway** to meet take-off distance required (TORR) and accelerate-stop distance required (ASDR) requirements.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Turn Radius for Taxiway Fillet Design

**Problem Statement:** An Airbus A320 has a wheelbase $a = 12.65$ m (nose gear to main gear) and a maximum effective nose-wheel steering angle $\theta_{max} = 70°$. Compute the minimum turn radius of the aircraft's nose gear, and hence the fillet radius required at a 90° taxiway intersection.

**Step 1 — Recall the Geometry**
When a wheeled vehicle turns, the centre of the turn lies on the perpendicular to the wheel axis at the pivot. For an aircraft, the main gear (or its extended centreline) is the pivot, and the nose gear traces a circle around it.

**Step 2 — Apply the Turn Radius Formula**
The minimum turn radius (of the nose gear) is:

$$R_{nose} = \frac{a}{\sin(\theta_{max})}$$

Substituting $a = 12.65$ m and $\theta_{max} = 70°$:

$$R_{nose} = \frac{12.65}{\sin(70°)} = \frac{12.65}{0.9397} = 13.46 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Main Gear Centreline Radius**
The main gear is the pivot, so its centre of turn is approximately on the main gear's extended centreline. The radius of the main gear's turn is:

$$R_{main} = a \cdot \cot(\theta_{max}) = 12.65 \cdot \cot(70°) = 12.65 \cdot 0.3640 = 4.60 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Fillet Design at 90° Intersection**
At a 90° taxiway-to-runway or taxiway-to-taxiway intersection, the corner must be widened (a fillet is provided) so that the main gear remains on the pavement. The minimum fillet radius is approximately:

$$R_{fillet} \approx R_{nose} - \frac{W_{t}}{2} + \text{Clearance}$$

where $W_t$ is the taxiway width. For Code C (A320) taxiway width = 15 m and clearance = 1.5 m:

$$R_{fillet} \approx 13.46 - \frac{15}{2} + 1.5 = 13.46 - 7.5 + 1.5 = 7.46 \text{ m}$$

Therefore, the fillet radius at the 90° intersection must be at least **7.5 m** to maintain a 1.5 m clearance from the pavement edge for the main gear.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: ACN Calculation (Conceptual Framework)

**Problem Statement:** Explain the derivation of the ACN (Aircraft Classification Number) for a Boeing 737-800 with MTOW = 79,016 kg, on a flexible pavement with a subgrade CBR of 10%, using a Dual-Wheel (DW) gear configuration.

**Step 1 — Definition of ACN**
The ACN is a **dimensionless number** that expresses the **relative effect of an aircraft** on a pavement for a specified **subgrade strength category**, evaluated at the **equivalent single wheel load (ESWL)** that produces the same pavement damage as the aircraft's actual gear configuration.

**Step 2 — Standard Subgrade Categories**
ICAO defines four subgrade categories:

| Subgrade | CBR (%) | $k$ (MN/m³) | Modulus |
|---|---|---|---|
| High (A) | 15 | 150 | Rigid |
| Medium (B) | 10 | 80 | Rigid |
| Low (C) | 6 | 40 | Rigid |
| Ultra-low (D) | 3 | 20 | Rigid |

For flexible pavements, the four categories are similarly defined.

**Step 3 — ACN Computation Procedure**
The ACN is computed using the FAA's **CBR method** (for flexible pavements) or **Westergaard method** (for rigid pavements).

For flexible pavement, the ACN is computed from the wheel load $P$ and tyre pressure $q$ such that the **derived single-wheel CBR thickness** equals the standard reference. The standard formula is:

$$\text{ACN} = 2 \cdot \log_{10}(P / 1000) + \text{(standard exponent for gear type)}$$

More precisely, ACN is determined by the **pavement thickness** required to support a single wheel load equal to 1% of the aircraft's gross weight (i.e., the same single-wheel load that produces the same deflection under the aircraft's gear).

**Step 4 — Reference Computation**
For the B737-800 with MTOW = 79,016 kg and a DW main gear:

| Component | Value |
|---|---|
| Main gear load | 39,508 kg per truck (≈ 2 trucks) |
| Load per wheel | 9,877 kg ≈ 9,877 × 9.81 N = 96,893 N |
| Tyre pressure | 1.40 MPa |

Iterating with the FAA design chart, the derived CBR thickness for the B737-800 DW on a CBR = 10% subgrade is approximately 50–55 cm. Comparing this to the reference single-wheel load that would produce the same thickness yields:

$$\text{ACN}_{B737-800, \text{CBR}=10\%} \approx 49$$

(Actual ICAO-published ACN values for the B737-800 are approximately: 40 on High (A), 49 on Medium (B), 56 on Low (C), 65 on Ultra-Low (D).)

**Step 5 — Pavement Use Check**
For the airport pavement to be usable without restriction by the B737-800, the **Pavement Classification Number (PCN)** of the pavement must satisfy:

$$\text{ACN} \le \text{PCN}$$

If $\text{ACN} > \text{PCN}$, the pavement is **overloaded**, and operations must be restricted (e.g., reduced MTOW, or limited number of movements per year).

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMICS

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Aircraft Design Parameters Affecting Airport Layout

The diagram below maps the **hierarchical relationship** between the aircraft (as the design vehicle) and the four primary airport design disciplines that depend on it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    aircraftNode[Aircraft Design Vehicle] --> geomNode[Geometric Parameters]
    aircraftNode --> weightNode[Weight Parameters]
    aircraftNode --> perfNode[Performance Parameters]
    aircraftNode --> opNode[Operational Parameters]

    geomNode --> ws[Wingspan b metres]
    geomNode --> lgth[Overall Length]
    geomNode --> hgt[Tail Height]
    geomNode --> omgws[OMGWS]

    weightNode --> mtow[MTOW tonnes]
    weightNode --> mlw[Max Landing Weight]
    weightNode --> acnVal[ACN number]

    perfNode --> vat[Approach Speed Vat knots]
    perfNode --> rfl[Reference Field Length]
    perfNode --> ldr[Landing Distance Required]

    opNode --> gear[Main Gear Configuration]
    opNode --> turnR[Min Turn Radius]
    opNode --> blast[Jet Blast Velocity]

    ws --> codeL[ICAO Code Letter A F]
    omgws --> codeL
    vat --> codeN[ICAO Code Number 1 to 4]
    codeL --> areo[ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code]
    codeN --> areo

    areo --> runwayW[Runway Width and Strip Width]
    areo --> taxiW[Taxiway Width and Fillet Geometry]
    areo --> sepD[Runway Taxiway Separation Distances]

    acnVal --> pavThick[Flexible and Rigid Pavement Thickness]
    rfl --> brl[Balanced Runway Length BRL]
    turnR --> fillet[Fillet Radius Design]
    blast --> resa[Runway End Safety Area RESA]

    runwayW --> aprn[Apron and Gate Sizing]
    taxiW --> aprn
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Start: Identify Aircraft] --> q1{Approach Speed Vat}
    q1 -->|>169 kt| c4[Code Number 4]

    c1 --> letterA
    c2 --> letterB
    c3 --> letterC

    letterA[A: wingspan less than 15 m]

```
