# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Affecting the Location of an Airport

The selection of an airport site is governed by a hierarchy of factors, typically assessed in the following order of priority:

### A. Meteorological and Atmospheric Factors
1. **Wind direction and velocity** — Governs runway orientation via the *wind rose analysis*.
2. **Visibility and ceiling** — Fog-prone areas must be avoided for primary runways.
3. **Precipitation patterns** — Affects drainage design.
4. **Temperature** — Hot, high-altitude sites increase runway length requirements.
5. **Crosswind component** — Must not exceed regulatory limits for the design aircraft.

### B. Topographical Factors
1. **Terrain gradient** — Flat or gently rolling terrain is preferred (gradient < 1.5%).
2. **Drainage** — Natural slope must allow storm-water runoff.
3. **Soil bearing capacity** — Subgrade CBR should be adequate (typically CBR ≥ 8% for flexible pavements).
4. **Natural obstructions** — Hills, ridges, and tall trees must be clear of the *Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)*.

### C. Obstacle Clearance (Airspace)
1. **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** — Defined by ICAO Annex 14 as imaginary surfaces in airspace.
2. **Approach and departure paths** — Must be free of penetrations.
3. **Transitional surfaces** — Side clearance to runway edges.
4. **Horizontal and conical surfaces** — Outer protective boundaries.

### D. Accessibility and Ground Transportation
1. Proximity to **metropolitan areas** (typically 20–40 km from city center for major airports).
2. Connectivity by **highway, rail, and metro**.
3. Travel time to the city should ideally be **less than 60 minutes**.

### E. Land Availability and Cost
1. Total land area: **5–20 km²** for large international airports.
2. Buffer zones for noise abatement must be acquired.
3. Future expansion capability (master plan horizon: **20–30 years**).

### F. Environmental and Social Factors
1. **Noise pollution** — NEF (Noise Exposure Forecast) or DNL (Day-Night Level) contours.
2. **Air quality** — Emissions from aircraft and ground vehicles.
3. **Bird strike hazard** — Avoidance of wetlands, landfills, and major bird migratory routes.
4. **Displacement of population** — Resettlement costs.
5. **Cultural heritage** — Heritage sites must be protected.

### G. Operational and Economic Factors
1. Proximity to **existing air traffic corridors**.
2. **ATC** (Air Traffic Control) considerations.
3. **Utility availability** — Power, water, sewage.
4. **Construction cost** — Earthwork, foundations, paving.

## 2.2 Runway Orientation and Wind Rose Analysis

### 2.2.1 Wind Rose
A **wind rose** is a polar diagram showing the percentage of time the wind blows from each compass direction (typically in 36 sectors of 10° each), along with the average wind speed in each direction.

The **calm** is plotted at the center (winds ≤ 6 km/h or 4 kt).

### 2.2.2 Wind Coverage
**Wind coverage** is the percentage of time that the wind is blowing in directions such that the *crosswind component* does not exceed the allowable value for the design aircraft.

**Usability factor** of a runway = Wind coverage of that runway (as a %).

### 2.2.3 Crosswind and Headwind Components

Given wind speed **W** blowing at an angle **θ** to the runway centerline:

- **Crosswind component (Wc):** The component perpendicular to the runway
- **Headwind component (Wh):** The component along the runway (positive if headwind, negative if tailwind)

The angle θ is the angle between the wind direction and the runway centerline. It is computed as:

$$\theta = \vert \text{Wind Direction} - \text{Runway Bearing (magnetic)}} \vert$$

Then, the components are:

$$W_c = W \sin\theta$$

$$W_h = W \cos\theta$$

(For headwind, Wh > 0; for tailwind, Wh < 0.)

### 2.2.4 Runway Numbering
Runways are numbered according to their magnetic bearing (azimuth) measured clockwise from magnetic north, divided by 10 and rounded to the nearest whole number.

$$N_{runway} = \text{Round}\!\left(\frac{\text{Magnetic Bearing}}{10}\right)$$

For example, a runway with magnetic bearing 090° is numbered **09**; the opposite end is **27**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Runway Number | $N = \text{Round}(B/10)$ | B = magnetic bearing in degrees |
| 2 | Crosswind Component | $W_c = W \sin\theta$ | W in km/h or kt; θ in degrees |
| 3 | Headwind Component | $W_h = W \cos\theta$ | Positive = headwind, Negative = tailwind |
| 4 | Angle between wind and runway | $\theta = \vert W_d - R_b \vert$ | Wd = wind direction, Rb = runway bearing |
| 5 | Wind Coverage | $\text{WC} = \dfrac{\Sigma t_{\text{acceptable}}}{\Sigma t_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Expressed as % |
| 6 | Usability Factor | $U = \text{WC}/100$ | Fraction (0–1) |
| 7 | ICAO Code Number | Based on **Aircraft Reference Field Length (ARFL)** | 1 (≤800 m), 2 (800–1200 m), 3 (1200–1800 m), 4 (≥1800 m) |
| 8 | ICAO Code Letter | Based on **wingspan** and **outer main gear span** | A (≤15 m) to F (≥65 m) |
| 9 | FAA Approach Category | Based on **approach speed ($V_{ref}$)** | A (≤91 kt) to E (166–213 kt) |
| 10 | FAA Design Group | Based on **wingspan** | I (≤49 ft) to VI (≥262 ft) |
| 11 | ICAO Max Crosswind (Code 1, 2) | **13 km/h (7 kt)** | For dry runway |
| 12 | ICAO Max Crosswind (Code 3, 4) | **24 km/h (13 kt)** | For dry runway |
| 13 | FAA Max Crosswind (Small) | 10.5 kt | Approach Category A, B |
| 14 | FAA Max Crosswind (Medium) | 13 kt | Approach Category C, D |
| 15 | FAA Max Crosswind (Large) | 16 kt | Approach Category E |
| 16 | FAA Max Crosswind (Heavy) | 20 kt | Special cases |
| 17 | Recommended Wind Coverage | **≥ 95%** | For combined runway system |
| 18 | Tolerance for single runway | **≥ 90%** | For one runway alone |
| 19 | Basic Runway Length (FAR) | $L = L_0 \times f_1 \times f_2 \times f_3 \times f_4$ | Correction factors for elevation, temp, slope |
| 20 | Elevation Correction | $f_1 = 1 + 0.07h/300$ | h = elevation in meters above MSL |
| 21 | Temperature Correction | $f_2 = 1 + 0.01(T - 14.4)/15$ | T = aerodrome reference temperature in °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Wind coverage dictates the *number* and *orientation* of runways. Single-runway airports need 90%+ coverage; multi-runway systems need 95%+ for the system as a whole. The FAA and ICAO differ slightly in their crosswind thresholds; in KTU numerical problems, **always state the standard being used** (ICAO Annex 14 or FAA AC 150/5300-13A) before solving.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Crosswind and Headwind Components from Wind Vector

Let the **wind vector** have magnitude $W$ (speed) and direction $\alpha$ (the direction *from* which the wind blows, measured clockwise from magnetic north).

Let the **runway** have magnetic bearing $R$ (measured clockwise from magnetic north, the direction *toward* which the aircraft is heading/landing).

The angle $\theta$ between the wind direction and the runway centerline is:

$$\theta = \vert \alpha - R \vert$$

If $\theta > 180°$, replace it with $360° - \theta$, so that $0° \le \theta \le 180°$.

Now, resolve the wind vector $W$ into two orthogonal components relative to the runway axis:

$$W_c = W \sin\theta \quad \text{(perpendicular to runway)}$$

$$W_h = W \cos\theta \quad \text{(along runway)}$$

This is the standard 2D vector resolution of $\vec{W}$ into components parallel and perpendicular to the runway axis.

**Step-by-step verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } & W = 30 \text{ kt}, \quad \alpha = 060° \text{ (wind from NE)}, \quad R = 090° \text{ (Runway 09, East-West)} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 1 — Angle: } & \theta = \vert 060° - 090° \vert = 30° \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2 — Crosswind: } & W_c = 30 \times \sin(30°) = 30 \times 0.500 = 15.0 \text{ kt} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3 — Headwind: } & W_h = 30 \times \cos(30°) = 30 \times 0.866 = 25.98 \text{ kt}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The crosswind of 15 kt is acceptable for Code 3/4 (ICAO limit 13 kt is **exceeded**, but for FAA Category D the 16 kt limit is acceptable).

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Runway Numbering and Wind Component (KTU-Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]:**

A runway has a true bearing of 087°. The magnetic declination at the site is 2° East. A wind of 35 km/h blows from 050° (true).

**(a)** Find the runway designation number(s).
**(b)** Calculate the crosswind and headwind components.
**(c)** State whether the aircraft can land if the maximum allowable crosswind is 24 km/h (ICAO Code 3/4).

### Solution

**Part (a): Runway Number**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Magnetic Bearing} &= \text{True Bearing} - \text{Easterly Declination} \\
&= 087° - 2° = 085° \\[4pt]
\text{Runway Number} &= \text{Round}\!\left(\frac{085}{10}\right) = \text{Round}(8.5) = 9 \rightarrow \textbf{09}
\end{aligned}$$

The opposite end is $085° + 180° = 265°$ → Round(265/10) = 27 → **Runway 27**.

**Part (b): Wind Components**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Magnetic wind direction} &= 050° - 2° = 048° \\[4pt]
\theta &= \vert 048° - 085° \vert = 37° \\[4pt]
W_c &= 35 \times \sin(37°) = 35 \times 0.6018 = 21.06 \text{ km/h} \\[4pt]
W_h &= 35 \times \cos(37°) = 35 \times 0.7986 = 27.95 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c): Acceptability**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_c &= 21.06 \text{ km/h} \le 24 \text{ km/h} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

The crosswind is **within** the ICAO Code 3/4 limit of 24 km/h. **Aircraft can land safely on Runway 09.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Tip:** 1 knot (kt) = 1.852 km/h. Therefore 13 kt ≈ 24 km/h and 7 kt ≈ 13 km/h — these are the standard ICAO conversion values used in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Wind Coverage and Runway Orientation

**Problem:**

A wind survey at a proposed airport site yields the following data (using 36 sectors of 10° each):

| Wind Direction (from) | % of Time | Avg Speed (km/h) |
|----------------------|-----------|------------------|
| 360° (N) | 8% | 25 |
| 010° – 080° (NNE–E) | 14% | 20 |
| 090° (E) | 10% | 30 |
| 100° – 170° (ESE–S) | 12% | 22 |
| 180° (S) | 9% | 28 |
| 190° – 260° (SSW–W) | 15% | 24 |
| 270° (W) | 11% | 26 |
| 280° – 350° (WNW–NW) | 16% | 22 |
| Calm | 5% | <6 |

For a Code 3/4 runway (max crosswind 24 km/h = 13 kt), determine the **optimum runway orientation** and **wind coverage**.

### Solution — Method

**Step 1:** Determine the threshold speed at which the crosswind equals 24 km/h for various angles θ.

$$W_{max} = \frac{W_{c,max}}{\sin\theta} = \frac{24}{\sin\theta}$$

**Step 2:** For a trial runway orientation R, compute the angle θ between R and each wind sector. If $W_i \le 24$ km/h, the wind is acceptable regardless of θ. If $W_i > 24$ km/h, the wind is acceptable only if $\sin\theta \le 24/W_i$.

**Step 3:** Sum the percentages of acceptable wind sectors.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board problems, this process is often **pre-computed** for the student, and you are asked to verify the coverage of a given orientation or to choose between two trial orientations. Always show the critical-step logic in your answer.

## 3.4 ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code — Full Structure

The **ICAO Annex 14 Code** has two elements:

**Code Number (1 to 4)** — based on **Aircraft Reference Field Length (ARFL)**:

| Code Number | ARFL (m) |
|-------------|----------|
| 1 | Less than 800 |
| 2 | 800 up to but not including 1200 |
| 3 | 1200 up to but not including 1800 |
| 4 | 1800 and over |

**Code Letter (A to F)** — based on **wingspan** and **outer main gear span**:

| Code Letter | Wingspan (m) | Outer Main Gear Span (m) |
|-------------|--------------|--------------------------|
| A | Up to 15 | Up to 4.5 |
| B | 15 to 24 | 4.5 to 6 |
| C | 24 to 36 | 6 to 9 |
| D | 36 to 52 | 9 to 14 |
| E | 52 to 65 | 9 to 14 |
| F | 65 to 80 | 14 to 16 |

**Example:** A Boeing 737-800 (wingspan 35.8 m, ARFL ~2100 m) → **Code 4C**.

A Boeing 777-300ER (wingspan 64.8 m, ARFL ~3000 m) → **Code 4E**.

## 3.5 FAA Classification System

The FAA uses a **two-part classification**:

- **Approach Category (A, B, C, D, E):** based on **$V_{ref}$** (approach speed at threshold, calibrated).
  - A: < 91 kt
  - B: 91–120 kt
  - C: 121–140 kt
  - D: 141–165 kt
  - E: 166–213 kt

- **Airplane Design Group (I to VI):** based on **wingspan** (or tail height for some groups).
  - I: < 49 ft
  - II: 49–78 ft
  - III: 79–117 ft
  - IV: 118–170 ft
  - V: 171–213 ft
  - VI: 214–262 ft

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ICAO vs. FAA Comparison (High-Yield for KTU):**
>
> - ICAO is **performance-based** (ARFL), FAA is **operationally-based** (approach speed).
> - ICAO uses **km/h**, FAA uses **knots and feet**.
> - ICAO Annex 14 applies to *all* member states; FAA standards are binding in the USA but advisory elsewhere.
> - FAA has the **Runway Protection Zone (RPZ)** concept, which is similar to ICAO's **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** but more strictly defined in plan and approach dimensions.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Airport Location Decision Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Identification Study] --> B{Topography Suitable?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C{Wind Coverage 95 percent?}
    C -- No --> D[Reorient Runways]
    D --> C
    C -- Yes --> E{Obstacle Free Airspace?}
    E -- No --> F[Demolish or Reduce Obstructions]
    F --> E
    E -- Yes --> G{Ground Access Adequate?}
    G -- No --> H[Plan Highways or Rail Links]
    H --> G
    G -- Yes --> I{Environmental Clearance?}
    I -- No --> J[Realign or Relocate]
    J --> I
    I -- Yes --> K{Land Available for Expansion?}
    K -- No --> L[Acquire Additional Land]
    L --> K
    K -- Yes --> M[Final Site Selected]
    M --> N[Master Plan Preparation]
```

## 4.2 ICAO vs. FAA Classification Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ICAO[ICAO Annex 14 System]
        I1[Code Number 1 to 4] --> I2[ARFL Based]
        I3[Code Letter A to F] --> I4[Wingspan Based]
    end
    subgraph FAA[FAA AC 150 5300 13A System]
        F1[Approach Category A to E] --> F2[Vref Based]
        F3[Airplane Design Group I to VI] --> F4[Wingspan Based]
    end
    ICAO -.Equivalent.-> FAA
    I1 -.Maps to.-> F1
    I3 -.Maps to.-> F3
```

## 4.3 Wind Rose and Runway Orientation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Wind Data Survey 10 to 20 years] --> R[Plot Wind Rose Diagram]
    R --> T[Try Runway Orientation R1]
    T --> C1[Compute Crosswind for All Sectors]
    C1 --> Q1{Is Coverage 95 percent?}
    Q1 -- No --> T
    Q1 -- Yes --> V[Acceptable Orientation]
    T2[Try Runway Orientation R2] --> C2[Compute Crosswind for All Sectors]
    C2 --> Q2{Is Coverage 95 percent?}
    Q2 -- No --> T2
    Q2 -- Yes --> V2[Compare With R1]
    V --> CMP[Select Orientation with Maximum Coverage]
    V2 --> CMP
```

## 4.4 Airport Master Plan Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MP[Airport Master Plan] --> A[Airfield Area]
    MP --> B[Terminal Area]
    MP --> C[Landside Area]
    MP --> D[Support Facilities]
    A --> A1[Runways]
    A --> A2[Taxiways]
    A --> A3[Aprons and Holding Bays]
    A --> A4[Air Traffic Control Tower]
    B --> B1[Passenger Terminal Building]
    B --> B2[Cargo Terminal]
    B --> B3[Apron Gates]
    C --> C1[Parking and Curb]
    C --> C2[Access Roads]
    C --> C3[Rail or Metro Link]
    D --> D1[Fire and Rescue Station]
    D --> D2[Fuel Farm]
    D --> D3[Maintenance Hangars]
    D --> D4[Utilities and Drainage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note:** All flowcharts above represent *functional architecture* of the planning process rather than physical layout drawings. Physical layout (runway-taxiway-apron geometry) is best studied from ICAO Annex 14 figures and FAA AC 150/5300-13A illustrations provided in textbooks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any four factors that govern the selection of a site for a new airport.**

**Model Answer:**

The four main factors that govern the selection of an airport site are:

1. **Meteorological factors** — wind direction, speed, visibility, fog occurrence, and crosswind component. The runway should be oriented such that **wind coverage** exceeds 95%.
2. **Topography and soil** — Flat or gently sloping terrain (gradient < 1.5%) with adequate subgrade strength (CBR ≥ 8%) and good drainage.
3. **Accessibility** — Proximity to the city (typically 20–40 km), connected by highway and/or rail, with travel time not exceeding 60 minutes.
4. **Environmental impact** — Avoidance of noise-sensitive areas, bird-strike zones, and culturally significant sites.

*(Any four with brief description — 3 marks)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between ICAO and FAA airport classification systems (any four points).**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | ICAO | FAA |
|-------|------|-----|
| 1 | Code Number based on **Aircraft Reference Field Length (ARFL)** | Approach Category based on **approach speed ($V_{ref}$)** |
| 2 | Code Letter A–F based on **wingspan and outer main gear span** | Airplane Design Group I–VI based on **wingspan** |
| 3 | Units: **meters** and **km/h** | Units: **feet** and **knots** |
| 4 | Applies to all ICAO member states (international) | Applies within the **United States** (national) |
| 5 | Uses **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** | Uses **Runway Protection Zone (RPZ)** |

*(Any four points with brief explanation — 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **factors affecting the location of an airport**. Discuss the role of **wind rose analysis** in runway orientation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A runway is oriented along a true bearing of 145°. The magnetic declination is 4° West. The wind blows from 220° (true) at 50 km/h. The aircraft to be operated is **ICAO Code 3C**.

1. Determine the runway designation.
2. Compute the crosswind and headwind components.
3. Comment on the suitability of the runway for landing.

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Factors Affecting Airport Location**

Airport location is a multi-criteria decision problem involving the following:

1. **Meteorological conditions:** Wind is the single most dominant factor. A *wind rose* is plotted from at least **10 years** of hourly wind data. The wind rose shows the **frequency** of wind from 36 directions (10° sectors) and the **average speed** in each direction. The runway (or runway system) is oriented such that the wind coverage (percentage of time the crosswind is within the allowable limit) is **at least 95%**. Calm winds (≤ 6 km/h) are not counted against coverage.

2. **Topography:** Flat or gently rolling terrain is preferred to minimize earthwork. Soil should have adequate bearing capacity and good drainage characteristics. A uniform gradient of 0.5%–1.5% is ideal.

3. **Obstructions:** The *Obstacle Limitation Surfaces* (approach, transitional, inner horizontal, conical, and outer horizontal surfaces) defined in ICAO Annex 14 must not be penetrated by natural or man-made obstacles.

4. **Accessibility:** The site must be connected to the city by high-capacity road and rail links. Travel time of less than 60 minutes is desirable.

5. **Environmental factors:** Noise contours, air quality, bird hazards, and impact on heritage and ecology must be evaluated.

6. **Land availability and future expansion:** 5–20 km² is required for major airports. The master plan should accommodate growth for 20–30 years.

7. **Utilities and economic factors:** Power, water, and sewage must be available or cheaply extendable. Construction cost should be optimized.

**Role of Wind Rose Analysis:**

- Plot the wind rose from long-term data.
- For trial runway orientations, calculate the crosswind component for each wind sector.
- Sum the percentage of time crosswind ≤ allowable limit (e.g., 13 kt for ICAO Code 3/4).
- Choose the orientation giving maximum coverage (≥ 95%).
- If a single runway gives 90%–95%, a crosswind runway may be added.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Runway Designation**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Magnetic Bearing} &= \text{True Bearing} - \text{West Declination} \\
&= 145° - 4° = 141° \\[4pt]
\text{Runway Number} &= \text{Round}\!\left(\frac{141}{10}\right) = \text{Round}(14.1) = 14
\end{aligned}$$

So the runway is **Runway 14**, and the opposite end is **Runway 32** (magnetic bearing 141° + 180° = 321° → 32).

**Step 2: Wind Components**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Magnetic wind direction} &= 220° - 4° = 216° \\[4pt]
\theta &= \vert 216° - 141° \vert = 75° \\[4pt]
W_c &= 50 \times \sin(75°) = 50 \times 0.9659 = 48.30 \text{ km/h} \\[4pt]
W_h &= 50 \times \cos(75°) = 50 \times 0.2588 = 12.94 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Suitability**

- ICAO Code 3C maximum crosswind = **24 km/h (13 kt)**.
- Computed $W_c = 48.30$ km/h ≫ 24 km/h. **The runway is NOT suitable for landing under these wind conditions.**
- The pilot must either divert to an alternate airport, wait for the wind to change, or use the reciprocal runway (32) if it offers a smaller crosswind. Let us verify Runway 32:

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{32} &= \vert 216° - 321° \vert = 105° \rightarrow 360° - 105° = 75° \quad \text{(same)} \\[4pt]
W_c &= 50 \times \sin(75°) = 48.30 \text{ km/h} \quad \text{(same)}
\end{aligned}$$

Both directions are unsuitable. The aircraft must **hold or divert**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Sign of declination confusion:** "West is Best, East is Least" — Subtract west declination, add east declination from the true bearing to get the magnetic bearing. **Failing this loses 1 mark immediately.**
> 2. **Unit mismatches:** 13 kt must be converted to **24 km/h** (1 kt = 1.852 km/h) before comparing with wind in km/h. Wrong unit comparison costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate steps:** Always show the conversion of wind direction to magnetic, then compute θ, then $W_c$ and $W_h$ separately. Examiners award step marks.
> 4. **Misinterpreting "from" and "to":** Wind direction "220°" means wind is **coming from 220°**, not blowing toward 220°. This is a classic trap.

---

### QUESTION B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **ICAO Annex 14 Aerodrome Reference Code** in detail. How does it differ from the **FAA classification system**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wind survey yields the following simplified data (grouped into 9 sectors) for a proposed airport:

| Sector (From) | % Frequency | Mean Speed (km/h) |
|---------------|-------------|-------------------|
| N (350°–010°) | 10 | 25 |
| NE (020°–070°) | 8 | 20 |
| E (080°–100°) | 9 | 30 |
| SE (110°–160°) | 7 | 22 |
| S (170°–190°) | 10 | 28 |
| SW (200°–250°) | 12 | 24 |
| W (260°–280°) | 11 | 26 |
| NW (290°–340°) | 13 | 22 |
| Calm | 20 | <6 |

The design aircraft is **ICAO Code 3** (max crosswind 24 km/h).

1. For a trial runway along the **E–W axis (Runway 09–27)**, determine the wind coverage.
2. If coverage is insufficient, suggest **one improvement** with justification.

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — ICAO vs. FAA Classification**

[Refer to detailed comparison in Section 3.4 and 3.5 of these notes. Award 7 marks for: ICAO Code Number (1), Code Letter (1), with aircraft reference example (1); FAA Approach Category (1), Design Group (1), with example (1); and at least 3 differences in tabular form (1).]

**Part (b) — Wind Coverage Calculation**

**Step 1: Simplification Assumption**

For wind coming from a sector, we take the **central direction** of the sector to compute the angle with the runway bearing 090° (Runway 09, landing toward east) or 270° (Runway 27, landing toward west). The aircraft can use **either** end, so we take the **smaller** of the two crosswind components.

**Step 2: Crosswind Calculation for Runway 09 (Bearing 090°)**

| Sector | Center Dir. | Speed (km/h) | θ to 090° | $W_c = W\sin\theta$ | Acceptable? |
|--------|-------------|--------------|-----------|--------------------|--------------|
| N | 000° | 25 | 90° | 25.00 | NO (24 < 25) |
| NE | 045° | 20 | 45° | 14.14 | YES |
| E | 090° | 30 | 0° | 0.00 | YES |
| SE | 135° | 20 | 45° | 14.14 | YES |
| S | 180° | 28 | 90° | 28.00 | NO |
| SW | 225° | 24 | 45° | 16.97 | YES |
| W | 270° | 26 | 0° | 0.00 | YES |
| NW | 315° | 22 | 45° | 15.56 | YES |
| Calm | — | <6 | — | — | YES |

**Step 3: Compute Wind Coverage**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Acceptable frequency} &= 8 + 9 + 7 + 12 + 11 + 13 + 20 = 80\% \\
\text{Unacceptable frequency} &= 10 + 10 = 20\% \quad (\text{N and S sectors}) \\
\text{Total Wind Coverage} &= 80\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Comment on Coverage**

Since the coverage is **80% < 90%** (minimum for a single runway) and **< 95%** (recommended for combined system), the **E–W runway alone is INADEQUATE**.

**Step 5: Suggested Improvement**

Add a **crosswind runway** along the **N–S axis (Runway 18–36)**. This will cover the unacceptable N and S winds.

For Runway 18 (Bearing 180°):

| Sector | Center Dir. | θ to 180° | $W_c$ | Acceptable? |
|--------|-------------|-----------|-------|--------------|
| N | 000° | 0° | 0.00 | YES |
| NE | 045° | 45° | 14.14 | YES |
| E | 090° | 90° | 30.00 | NO |
| SE | 135° | 45° | 14.14 | YES |
| S | 180° | 0° | 0.00 | YES |
| SW | 225° | 45° | 16.97 | YES |
| W | 270° | 90° | 26.00 | NO |
| NW | 315° | 45° | 15.56 | YES |
| Calm | — | — | — | YES |

**Combined System Coverage:**

Runway 09–27 covers: NE, E, SE, SW, W, NW, Calm = 80%
Runway 18–36 additionally covers: N, S, NE, SE, SW, NW, Calm (the new contributions unique to it are N and S = 20%)

**Combined system coverage = 80% + 20% = 100%** (since the only unacceptable sectors for one runway are fully covered by the other).

The **N–S crosswind runway is justified**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors in Wind Coverage Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the calm percentage** — calm winds are *always* acceptable and should be added to the coverage. Students sometimes omit 5–20% coverage by ignoring the calm category. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Using only one runway end** — Always check **both** ends of a runway (09 and 27) and use the **minimum crosswind** (i.e., the better end). Many students compute only one end.
> 3. **Confusing the angle θ** — θ is the angle between the wind direction and the **runway centerline**, not the angle between wind and **perpendicular**. Verify $\theta = \vert W_d - R_b \vert$ and reduce to ≤ 180°.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Airport location** depends on 7 major factors: meteorology, topography, obstacles, accessibility, environment, land, and economics.
- **Wind rose** is a polar histogram of wind frequency by direction; **wind coverage** is the % of time crosswind is within allowable limits.
- **Minimum wind coverage:** 90% (single runway), 95% (combined system).
- **Runway number** = Round(Magnetic Bearing / 10).
- **Crosswind component** $W_c = W\sin\theta$; **Headwind** $W_h = W\cos\theta$.
- **ICAO crosswind limits:** 13 km/h (Code 1, 2) and 24 km/h (Code 3, 4).
- **FAA crosswind limits:** 10.5 / 13 / 16 / 20 kt for small/medium/large/heavy aircraft.
- **ICAO Code Number** (1–4) is based on ARFL; **ICAO Code Letter** (A–F) is based on wingspan and gear span.
- **FAA classification** uses Approach Category (A–E, based on $V_{ref}$) and Airplane Design Group (I–VI, based on wingspan).
- **Conversion:** 1 kt = 1.852 km/h; 1 m = 3.281 ft.
- **Declination rule:** "West is Best, East is Least" — subtract west, add east.
- **OLS (ICAO)** ≈ **RPZ (FAA)** — both define protected airspace around runways.
- **Airport master plan** components: Airfield (runways, taxiways, aprons), Terminal area, Landside (access, parking), Support facilities (fire, fuel, maintenance).
- **For KTU 14-mark problems**, always show: (i) data extraction, (ii) declination correction, (iii) θ calculation, (iv) $W_c$ and $W_h$ separately, (v) interpretation against the stated crosswind limit, (vi) final verdict.
- **For KTU 7-mark theory questions**, structure the answer as: definition, factors/parameters, ICAO vs. FAA comparison table, and an illustrative example.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Airport Elements - Airfield
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Airport Elements - Airfield

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Airfield?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (ICAO / FAA Aligned):**
> The **airfield** (also called the *airside* or *movement area*) is the portion of an airport that supports the **take-off, landing, and ground maneuvering of aircraft**. It encompasses all paved and unpaved surfaces, lighting systems, navigational aids, and associated infrastructure between the runway and the apron, but **excludes** the terminal building, landside access, and passenger-processing facilities.

The airfield is, in essence, the **operational heart of the airport**. While passengers only ever interact with the terminal (the "landside"), every flight depends entirely on the airfield being geometrically and structurally sound.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of the airfield as the **"highway system of the sky"**, but much more strictly controlled:

- The **runway** is like a single, one-way drag-strip highway (you can only take off or land along its centerline).
- The **taxiways** are the on-ramps and off-ramps connecting the highway to the parking lots.
- The **apron** is the parking lot where aircraft refuel, load, and unload.
- The **stopway** and **clearway** are the "emergency shoulders" of the highway.
- The **holding bay** is a small rest area just before the highway entrance, where aircraft wait for clearance.

Just as a poorly designed highway causes accidents and congestion, a poorly designed airfield leads to runway incursions, delayed operations, and—worst case—catastrophic accidents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Exam Favorite):**
> - **Airside** = Airfield + Apron (everything the pilot uses)
> - **Landside** = Terminal + Access roads + Parking (everything the passenger uses)
> - **Terminal Area** is the *interface* that connects them.

---

## 1.2 The Five Major Functional Zones of the Airfield

The airfield is not a single uniform surface; it is a carefully orchestrated **hierarchy of zones**, each with a specific ICAO/FAA function code and design aircraft classification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Functional Zones of an Airfield:**
> 1. **Runway Zone** – The take-off and landing strip.
> 2. **Taxiway Zone** – The network of paved routes for aircraft ground movement.
> 3. **Apron Zone** – The parking, servicing, and loading area.
> 4. **Safety / Buffer Zone** – Runway end safety areas (RESA), shoulders, strips.
> 5. **Navigational & Visual Aid Zone** – Lighting, markings, beacons, ILS, PAPI, etc.

### 1.2.1 The Runway – The Crown Jewel

The **runway** is a defined rectangular paved (or otherwise prepared) surface on an aerodrome prepared for the **take-off and landing run** of aircraft. It is the most critical, most expensive, and most heavily engineered element of the entire airfield.

| Parameter | Typical Range (Modern International Airports) | Reference Code |
|---|---|---|
| Length | 2,000 m – 4,500 m | ICAO Code 4E / 4F |
| Width | 45 m – 60 m | FAA Group V–VI |
| Longitudinal slope (max) | 1.0% – 1.5% | ICAO Annex 14 |
| Transverse slope (max) | 1.0% – 1.5% | ICAO Annex 14 |

### 1.2.2 The Taxiway Network

A **taxiway** is a defined path on a land aerodrome established for the **taxiing of aircraft** between the runway and the apron. The taxiway system is what allows the airport to function as a *system*, rather than a single strip.

**Types of Taxiways (KTU & ICAO Classification):**

1. **Parallel Taxiway** – A taxiway running parallel to the runway, providing rapid exit and entry.
2. **Rapid Exit Taxiway (RET)** – High-speed exit taxiway angled at 30°, allowing landing aircraft to vacate at 90 km/h instead of 30 km/h.
3. **Perimeter Taxiway** – A taxiway encircling the runway to minimize crossing of the active runway.
4. **Cross-Field Taxiway** – A taxiway crossing the runway (used at smaller airports).
5. **Apron Taxiway** – A taxiway located on the apron used for aircraft maneuvering around parking stands.

### 1.2.3 The Apron (Ramp)

The **apron** (called *the ramp* in American usage) is that part of the aerodrome provided for the **stationing of aircraft for the loading/unloading of passengers, mail, or cargo, and for servicing**.

> [!NOTE]
> The apron is technically the *transition zone* between the airside and the landside. It is the only place where the aircraft, the terminal building, and ground-handling vehicles all co-exist.

**Types of Aprons:**

- **Terminal Apron** – Adjacent to the passenger terminal; used for boarding/deplaning.
- **Cargo Apron** – Adjacent to the cargo terminal; used for freighters.
- **Maintenance/Service Apron** – Adjacent to hangars; for routine maintenance.
- **Staging Apron** – Used for remote parking of aircraft during peak hours.
- **De-icing Apron** – For cold-weather operations; equipped with de-icing fluid sprayers.

### 1.2.4 Safety Buffers (Often Neglected, but Exam-Critical!)

| Element | Definition | ICAO Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| **Runway Strip** | A defined area including the runway and stopway | 150 m wide (Code 4) |
| **Runway End Safety Area (RESA)** | Symmetrical area beyond the runway end | 90 m × 90 m minimum |
| **Runway Shoulder** | Paved area adjacent to the runway edge | 7.5 m wide |
| **Taxiway Shoulder** | Paved area adjacent to the taxiway edge | 3.5 m wide |
| **Obstacle Free Zone (OFZ)** | Volume of airspace above the inner approach surface | Volume defined in Annex 14 |

### 1.2.5 Navigational & Visual Aids

This zone includes everything the **pilot sees and uses** to navigate the airfield:

- **Aerodrome Beacon** – Rotating light to identify the airport.
- **Wind Sock / Wind Cone** – Indicates wind direction and approximate speed.
- **PAPI (Precision Approach Path Indicator)** – 4-light visual glide path aid.
- **VASI (Visual Approach Slope Indicator)** – Older 2-bar system.
- **Threshold Lights, Edge Lights, End Lights, Centerline Lights** – Runway lighting.
- **Taxiway Centerline Lights & Edge Lights** – Taxiway guidance.
- **Apron Floodlighting** – For night operations.
- **ILS (Instrument Landing System)** – Localizer + Glide Slope + Marker Beacons.
- **DVOR / DME** – Long-range navigation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Relationship Between Airfield Elements

The airfield functions because of a strict **geometric and operational hierarchy**. Aircraft move from the *most flexible* space (open airspace) into *increasingly constrained* spaces, in this order:

**Sky → Approach Path → Threshold → Runway → Turn-off Taxiway → Taxiway Network → Apron Stand**

Each transition requires the designer to satisfy specific **clearance, slope, and pavement-strength** criteria.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Design Philosophy:**
> The aircraft is always assumed to be the *design vehicle*, and the airfield is designed to be a **"tube" that perfectly fits the wingspan, wheelbase, and turning radius** of the critical design aircraft (e.g., B777-300ER, A380).

---

## 2.2 Runway Orientation – The Single Most Important Design Decision

A runway is identified by its **magnetic bearing divided by 10** (rounded to the nearest integer). For example, a runway aligned to magnetic east (090°) is called **Runway 09**, and its opposite end is **Runway 27**.

### Why Orientation Matters

Aircraft take off and land **into the wind** to (a) reduce ground roll, (b) increase lift, and (c) maintain directional control. Therefore, the runway must be oriented so that **take-off and landing can occur with a headwind component** as often as possible.

### 2.2.1 Wind Coverage and the Wind Rose

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Definition:**
> **Wind coverage** is the percentage of time during which the **crosswind component** on a particular runway orientation is within acceptable limits (typically ≤ 20 kt for jet aircraft, ≤ 13 kt for small aircraft).

The ICAO/FAA limit is:

$$C_{cross,max} = 20 \text{ knots} = 37 \text{ km/h}$$

For small aircraft (Reference Code 1 & 2):

$$C_{cross,max} = 13 \text{ knots} = 24 \text{ km/h}$$

### 2.2.2 Calculating the Crosswind Component

For a given wind speed $V$ and wind angle $\theta$ (the angle between the wind direction and the runway centerline), the crosswind component is:

$$C_{cross} = V \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

and the headwind (or tailwind) component is:

$$C_{head} = V \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

The standard wind analysis for a runway orientation uses a **template** (often called the *wind rose template* or *wind direction template*) overlaid on the wind rose. The template has parallel lines at an angular spacing of $\pm \theta_{max}$ from the centerline, where:

$$\theta_{max} = \arccos\left(\frac{C_{cross,max}}{V}\right) = \arcsin\left(\frac{C_{head}}{V}\right)$$

**Procedure for KTU Exam:**

1. Collect wind data (typically 10+ years, in 10° sectors, in 5-kt speed groups).
2. Plot the **wind rose**.
3. Place a transparent template (representing the proposed runway orientation) over the wind rose.
4. Count the percentage of observations falling *within* the $\pm \theta_{max}$ envelope.
5. This percentage is the **wind coverage**.
6. The KTU standard requires **at least 95% wind coverage**.

### 2.2.3 Maximum Allowable Crosswind vs. Wind Speed (Worked Tabular Form)

| Wind Speed $V$ (kt) | $\theta_{max}$ for 20 kt crosswind | $\theta_{max}$ for 13 kt crosswind |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | No restriction (always OK) | No restriction |
| 15 | $\arcsin(20/15)$ = N/A | $\arcsin(13/15)$ = 60.1° |
| 20 | 90.0° | $\arcsin(13/20)$ = 40.5° |
| 25 | $\arcsin(20/25)$ = 53.1° | $\arcsin(13/25)$ = 31.3° |
| 30 | $\arcsin(20/30)$ = 41.8° | $\arcsin(13/30)$ = 25.7° |
| 40 | $\arcsin(20/40)$ = 30.0° | $\arcsin(13/40)$ = 19.0° |
| 50 | $\arcsin(20/50)$ = 23.6° | $\arcsin(13/50)$ = 15.1° |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Memory Trick:**
> For 20 kt crosswind: $\theta_{max} = \arcsin(20/V)$
> For 13 kt crosswind: $\theta_{max} = \arcsin(13/V)$
> Always use **arcsin**, never arccos, when given the crosswind limit.

---

## 2.3 Basic Runway Length – The Standard ICAO / FAA Method

The **basic runway length** $L_0$ is the length required for **standard conditions**:

- Sea level elevation (0 m MSL)
- Standard atmospheric pressure ($1013.25$ hPa)
- Standard temperature (15°C at sea level, with a lapse rate of 6.5°C per 1000 m)
- Zero runway gradient
- Still air (no wind)

It is the **balanced field length** for the design aircraft, taken from the manufacturer's *Airplane Characteristics for Airport Planning* (ACAP) manual.

### 2.3.1 KTU Correction Formula (Universal Form)

The **actual runway length** $L$ is calculated by applying a chain of multiplicative correction factors to $L_0$:

$$L = L_0 \cdot f_{elevation} \cdot f_{temperature} \cdot f_{gradient} \cdot f_{wind}$$

where each factor is computed as follows.

### 2.3.2 Elevation Correction (FAA Method)

$$f_{elevation} = 1 + 0.07 \cdot \frac{h}{300}$$

where $h$ is the **airport elevation in meters above mean sea level (MSL)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **0.07 per 300 m** is the FAA standard. It accounts for the reduced air density at higher elevations, which reduces lift and increases ground roll.

### 2.3.3 Temperature Correction (FAA Method)

$$f_{temperature} = 1 + 0.01 \cdot (T - T_{std})$$

where:
- $T$ = **airport reference temperature** in °C, taken as the **monthly mean of the average daily maximum temperature for the hottest month of the year**
- $T_{std}$ = **standard temperature** for the airport elevation, given by:

$$T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \cdot h$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $T - T_{std}$ is called the **temperature excess** and is *always positive* (since reference temperature ≥ standard temperature).

### 2.3.4 Gradient Correction (FAA Method)

$$f_{gradient} = 1 + 0.10 \cdot G$$

where $G$ is the **effective runway gradient** expressed as a **percentage** (not a decimal). Effective gradient is the **maximum difference in elevation** along the runway divided by the runway length, expressed as a percentage.

### 2.3.5 Wind Correction (Optional, for Headwind Component)

$$f_{wind} = 1 - \frac{V_{head}}{100}$$

where $V_{head}$ is the **average headwind component during take-off** in knots. This factor is *less than 1* for headwind (reduces required length) and *greater than 1* for tailwind (increases required length).

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, the wind correction is **usually omitted** unless explicitly stated. The first three corrections (elevation, temperature, gradient) are *always* required.

---

## 2.4 Declared Distances – KTU High-Yield Concept

For a runway with a **stopway**, **clearway**, or **displaced threshold**, ICAO defines **four declared distances** for each operational direction:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Declared Distances (ICAO):**
> 1. **TORA (Take-Off Run Available)** – The length of runway available for the *ground run* of a take-off.
> 2. **TODA (Take-Off Distance Available)** – TORA + length of clearway available.
> 3. **ASDA (Accelerate-Stop Distance Available)** – TORA + length of stopway available.
> 4. **LDA (Landing Distance Available)** – The length of runway available for the *landing run* of an aircraft.

### 2.4.1 Key Inequalities (Memorize These!)

$$\text{TODA} \geq \text{TORA} \geq \text{ASDA}$$

$$\text{LDA} \leq \text{TORA}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Memory Aid:**
> "**T**ake-off, **T**ake-off, **A**ccelerate, **L**and" = **T, T, A, L**
> TODA is the *longest* (because of clearway)
> LDA is the *shortest* (because of displaced threshold)

---

## 2.5 The ICAO Reference Code – Aerodrome Classification

Every airport is given an **ICAO Reference Code** consisting of two elements:

1. **Code Number (1 to 4)** – Based on the **aeroplane reference field length** (ARFL):

| Code Number | ARFL |
|---|---|
| 1 | Less than 800 m |
| 2 | 800 m up to 1200 m |
| 3 | 1200 m up to 1800 m |
| 4 | 1800 m and over |

2. **Code Letter (A to F)** – Based on the **wingspan** and **outer main gear wheel span** of the design aircraft:

| Code Letter | Wingspan | Outer Wheel Span |
|---|---|---|
| A | Up to 15 m | Up to 4.5 m |
| B | 15 m to 24 m | 4.5 m to 6 m |
| C | 24 m to 36 m | 6 m to 9 m |
| D | 36 m to 52 m | 9 m to 14 m |
| E | 52 m to 65 m | 9 m to 14 m |
| F | 65 m to 80 m | 14 m to 16 m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A380 (Code Letter F)** requires special pavement thickness, dual taxiway centerline, and 25 m wide runway shoulders. **Boeing 747-8** is Code Letter E.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print and memorize this table — every KTU exam has ≥1 numerical problem using these formulas.**

| $\#$ | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{cross} = V \sin\theta$ | Crosswind component on a runway | kt or km/h |
| 2 | $C_{head} = V \cos\theta$ | Headwind component on a runway | kt or km/h |
| 3 | $\theta_{max} = \arcsin(C_{max}/V)$ | Max angular deviation for allowed crosswind | degrees |
| 4 | Wind Coverage $\geq 95\%$ | ICAO minimum for operational runway | \% |
| 5 | $L = L_0 \cdot f_E \cdot f_T \cdot f_G$ | Actual runway length | m |
| 6 | $f_E = 1 + 0.07h/300$ | Elevation correction factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $f_T = 1 + 0.01(T - T_{std})$ | Temperature correction factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 h$ | Standard temp at elevation $h$ | °C |
| 9 | $f_G = 1 + 0.10 G$ | Gradient correction factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f_W = 1 - V_{head}/100$ | Wind correction factor (headwind) | dimensionless |
| 11 | TODA = TORA + Clearway | Take-off distance available | m |
| 12 | ASDA = TORA + Stopway | Accelerate-stop distance available | m |
| 13 | LDA $\leq$ TORA | Landing distance available | m |
| 14 | Code 4 ARFL $\geq$ 1800 m | Largest runway code | m |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Crosswind Formula is $V \sin\theta$

Let a runway be aligned along the **x-axis**, with the wind blowing at speed $V$ at an angle $\theta$ measured from the runway centerline (positive = wind blowing *across* the runway from the right).

**Step 1 — Resolve the wind vector into components.**

The wind vector $\vec{V}$ makes an angle $\theta$ with the runway. We resolve it into:
- Component along the runway: $V \cos\theta$ (this is the headwind component)
- Component perpendicular to the runway: $V \sin\theta$ (this is the crosswind component)

**Step 2 — Confirm via Pythagorean identity.**

$$(V\cos\theta)^2 + (V\sin\theta)^2 = V^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = V^2$$

This confirms the components are orthogonal and complete.

**Step 3 — ICAO limiting condition.**

The aircraft is certified to operate only when the *cross-track* velocity does not exceed $C_{cross,max}$ (20 kt for jets). Thus:

$$V \sin\theta \leq C_{cross,max}$$

$$\sin\theta \leq \frac{C_{cross,max}}{V}$$

For a *given* $V$, the maximum acceptable $\theta$ is:

$$\theta_{max} = \arcsin\left(\frac{C_{cross,max}}{V}\right)$$

This is the equation used to construct the wind rose template.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Runway Length Correction Chain

**Step 1 — Start with the basic length $L_0$.**

$L_0$ is the manufacturer's *balanced field length* at sea level, ISA conditions, zero slope, no wind.

**Step 2 — Apply elevation correction.**

As elevation $h$ increases, air density decreases, reducing both engine thrust and wing lift. The FAA empirical formula is:

$$f_E = 1 + \frac{0.07 \, h}{300}$$

Derivation: Each 300 m of elevation increases ground roll by approximately 7%, so $L_E = L_0(1 + 0.07 h/300)$.

**Step 3 — Apply temperature correction.**

The reference temperature $T$ is *higher* than the standard temperature $T_{std}$ at the same elevation. Hot air is less dense. The FAA empirical formula is:

$$f_T = 1 + 0.01 \cdot (T - T_{std})$$

Each 1°C *excess* temperature increases the required runway length by 1%.

**Step 4 — Apply gradient correction.**

An upslope of 1% increases the required length by 10%:

$$f_G = 1 + 0.10 G$$

where $G$ is the *effective gradient in percent* (max elevation difference / length × 100).

**Step 5 — Combine multiplicatively.**

The corrections are *multiplicative* (not additive) because they act independently on different physical parameters (density, viscosity, gravity along the slope). Hence:

$$L = L_0 \cdot f_E \cdot f_T \cdot f_G \cdot f_W$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem (14 marks):**
> A proposed airport is to be designed for Boeing 737-800 operations. The basic runway length of B737-800 at maximum landing weight is **2200 m**. The airport site is at an elevation of **600 m** above MSL. The hottest month has an average daily maximum temperature of **38°C**. The effective runway gradient is **0.5%**. Compute the actual runway length required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Standard temperature at 600 m.**

$$T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times 600 = 15 - 3.9 = 11.1 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature excess.**

$$T - T_{std} = 38 - 11.1 = 26.9 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Elevation correction factor.**

$$f_E = 1 + \frac{0.07 \times 600}{300} = 1 + 0.14 = 1.14$$

**Step 4 — Temperature correction factor.**

$$f_T = 1 + 0.01 \times 26.9 = 1 + 0.269 = 1.269$$

**Step 5 — Gradient correction factor.**

$$f_G = 1 + 0.10 \times 0.5 = 1 + 0.05 = 1.05$$

**Step 6 — Actual runway length (no wind correction given).**

$$L = 2200 \times 1.14 \times 1.269 \times 1.05$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$L = 2200 \times 1.14 = 2508 \text{ m}$$

$$L = 2508 \times 1.269 = 3182.65 \text{ m}$$

$$L = 3182.65 \times 1.05 = 3341.78 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer: $L \approx 3342$ m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Trick:** Many students use $T_{std} = 15°C$ instead of the elevation-corrected value, getting $f_T = 1.23$ (wrong) instead of $1.269$ (correct). Always reduce $T_{std}$ by 0.0065 h!

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Crosswind and Wind Rose

> **Problem (7 marks):**
> A runway is oriented 27-09 (i.e., along the E-W direction). A wind of 30 kt is blowing from 200° (i.e., from SSW). Find the headwind and crosswind components experienced by an aircraft taking off on Runway 27.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Wind angle relative to runway.**

Runway 27 has magnetic bearing 270°. Wind is from 200°.

The angle between the wind direction and the runway centerline:

$$\theta = \vert 270 - 200 \vert = 70°$$

**Step 2 — Crosswind component.**

$$C_{cross} = V \sin\theta = 30 \times \sin(70°)$$

$$C_{cross} = 30 \times 0.9397 = 28.19 \text{ kt}$$

**Step 3 — Check against ICAO limit.**

$$C_{cross} = 28.19 \text{ kt} > 20 \text{ kt ICAO limit}$$

**Conclusion:** Aircraft **cannot take off on Runway 27** under this wind. They must wait for the wind to subside, or use Runway 09 if the wind becomes a headwind.

**Step 4 — Headwind component (informational).**

$$C_{head} = V \cos\theta = 30 \times \cos(70°) = 30 \times 0.342 = 10.26 \text{ kt}$$

(Positive value = headwind; in this case, the wind is *cross-flowing*, not head-on.)

---

## 3.5 Declared Distances — Analytical Case

> **Problem (7 marks):**
> A runway has the following geometry (in the direction of take-off from threshold 09):
> - Full paved runway length = 3000 m
> - Clearway at the end = 600 m
> - Stopway at the end = 300 m
> - Displaced threshold at the 09 end = 300 m
>
> Compute TORA, TODA, ASDA, and LDA for operations from the 09 end.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**

The displacement of 300 m means the **landing threshold is 300 m from the physical end** of the runway. The clearway and stopway are at the *far end* (Runway 27 end, which is the take-off direction for operations starting from 09).

**Step 2 — Compute TORA (Take-Off Run Available).**

TORA is the *full length* of the runway, since the take-off roll starts from the very beginning (no displacement for take-off):

$$\text{TORA} = 3000 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute TODA (Take-Off Distance Available).**

TODA includes the clearway beyond the end of TORA:

$$\text{TODA} = \text{TORA} + \text{Clearway} = 3000 + 600 = 3600 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ASDA (Accelerate-Stop Distance Available).**

ASDA includes the stopway:

$$\text{ASDA} = \text{TORA} + \text{Stopway} = 3000 + 300 = 3300 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute LDA (Landing Distance Available).**

LDA is reduced by the displaced threshold:

$$\text{LDA} = 3000 - 300 = 2700 \text{ m}$$

**Final Result:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| TORA | 3000 m |
| TODA | 3600 m |
| ASDA | 3300 m |
| LDA | 2700 m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of inequalities:**
> - TODA (3600) ≥ TORA (3000) ≥ ASDA (3300) ✓
> - LDA (2700) ≤ TORA (3000) ✓
> All conditions satisfied.

---

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix — Stopway vs. Clearway

> [!NOTE]
> **One of the most frequently asked 3-mark questions in KTU exams.**

| Attribute | Stopway | Clearway |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Beyond the end of TORA, on the ground | Beyond the end of TORA, in the air |
| Definition (ICAO) | A defined rectangular area at the end of the take-off run available, prepared as a suitable area in which an aircraft can be stopped in the case of an aborted take-off | A defined rectangular area at the end of the take-off run available, selected or prepared as a suitable area over which an aircraft may make a portion of its initial climb to a specified height |
| Use Case | Aborted take-off (rejected take-off, RTO) | Continued take-off (engine failure after $V_1$) |
| Contributes to | **ASDA** | **TODA** |
| Pavement Requirement | Must be able to support the aircraft (paved or unpaved) | No pavement required (can be water, soft ground) |
| Width | Same as runway width | At least 75 m each side of extended centerline |
| Length Limit | None specific | Not to exceed half of TORA |
| Obstructions Allowed | Yes (no aircraft penetration in flight) | **No obstructions** (aircraft will fly over it) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Airfield Functional Architecture

This diagram maps the **functional relationships** between all airfield elements, treating the airfield as a *flow system* from "free space" to "parked aircraft."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Open Airspace] --> B[Approach Funnel]
    B --> C[Threshold Lights and Markings]
    C --> D[Runway Pavement]
    D --> E[Runway Turnoff Lights]
    E --> F[Rapid Exit Taxiway RET]
    F --> G[Parallel Taxiway]
    G --> H[Apron Taxiway]
    H --> I[Aircraft Stand]
    I --> J[Terminal Building]
    J --> K[Landside Access]

    L[Navigational Aids] --> A
    M[Wind Direction Indicators] --> A
    N[Control Tower] --> G
    O[Drainage System] --> D
    O --> F
    O --> G
    P[Runway End Safety Area RESA] --> D
    Q[Stopway] --> D
    R[Clearway] --> D
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Wind Rose Template Construction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Collect Wind Data 10 years] --> B[Plot Wind Rose]
    B --> C[Compute Theta Max = arcsin Cmax by V]
    C --> D[Draw Transparent Template]
    D --> E[Overlay Template on Wind Rose]
    E --> F[Count Percentage Inside Envelope]
    F --> G{Coverage at least 95 percent?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Accept Orientation]
    G -->|No| I[Rotate Template]
    I --> D
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Runway Declared Distances (for KTU Sketch Questions)

The following ASCII-style layout represents the geometric relationship between **TORA, TODA, ASDA, LDA** for a typical runway with a stopway and a displaced threshold. (Use this as a template for your exam sketches.)

```
                  STOPWAY (300 m)              CLEARWAY (600 m)
                  |<-------->|                 |<---------->|
                  |           |                 |            |
==================|===========|================================== (extended centerline)
                  |           |
   Displaced  <-->|           |                                          
   Threshold     TORA = 3000 m
   (300 m)       |           |
   (LDA ends)    |           |
   (LDA = 2700 m)|           |
                 |           |
=================|===========|=================================== (pavement edge)
     LDA         TORA + stopway = ASDA = 3300
                 TORA + clearway = TODA = 3600
                 
[Threshold 09]                                       [Threshold 27]
   (start of TORA)                                   (end of TORA)
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — ICAO Reference Code Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Aircraft Selected] --> B{ARFL in meters?}
    B -->|under 800| C1[Code 1]
    B -->|800 to 1200| C2[Code 2]
    B -->|1200 to 1800| C3[Code 3]
    B -->|1800 and above| C4[Code 4]

    A --> D{Wingspan and Gear Span?}
    D -->|under 15 m| L1[Letter A]
    D -->|15 to 24 m| L2[Letter B]
    D -->|24 to 36 m| L3[Letter C]
    D -->|36 to 52 m| L4[Letter D]
    D -->|52 to 65 m| L5[Letter E]
    D -->|65 to 80 m| L6[Letter F]

    C4 --> E[Final ICAO Reference Code E.g. 4E]
    L5 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Kochi International Airport (COK) is **Code 4E** — accommodating aircraft with ARFL ≥ 1800 m and wingspan 52–65 m (e.g., Boeing 777, A330). Trivandrum International Airport (TRV) is **Code 4D**.

---

## 4.5 Process Flow — Runway Length Calculation (Algorithm for Exams)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Aircraft Selected] --> B[Identify Basic Length L0 from ACAP]
    B --> C[Get Site Elevation h]
    C --> D[Compute fE = 1 + 0.07 h / 300]
    D --> E[Get Reference Temperature T]
    E --> F[Compute Tstd = 15 - 0.0065 h]
    F --> G[Compute fT = 1 + 0.01 T minus Tstd]
    G --> H[Get Effective Gradient G in percent]
    H --> I[Compute fG = 1 + 0.10 G]
    I --> J[Wind Correction Applied only if Given]
    J --> K[Compute L = L0 times fE times fT times fG]
    K --> L[Round up to nearest 10 m]
    L --> M[Final Runway Length]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
> **Define the term "airfield" and list its five major functional zones.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** An **airfield** is that part of an aerodrome that is to be used for the take-off, landing, and ground movement of aircraft, comprising all paved and unpaved surfaces within the movement area boundary.
>
> **Five Major Functional Zones:**
> 1. **Runway Zone** – the paved rectangular surface used for take-off and landing.
> 2. **Taxiway Zone** – the network of paved routes for aircraft ground movement.
> 3. **Apron Zone** – the paved area for aircraft parking, loading, and servicing.
> 4. **Safety / Buffer Zone** – includes runway strips, shoulders, RESA, and stopways.
> 5. **Navigational and Visual Aid Zone** – lighting, markings, ILS, PAPI, wind indicators.

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
> **Distinguish clearly between a "stopway" and a "clearway" with respect to ICAO declared distances.**

**Model Answer:**

| Attribute | Stopway | Clearway |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Stops the aircraft during rejected take-off | Allows continued climb after engine failure |
| **Location** | On the ground, beyond TORA end | In the air, above and beyond TORA end |
| **Contributes to** | ASDA | TODA |
| **Pavement** | Required (paved or compacted) | Not required (can be water, soft ground) |
| **Obstructions** | Permitted on the surface | **Not permitted** in the flight path |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
> *(a)* With the aid of a sketch, define the **four declared distances** as per ICAO Annex 14. Explain the relationship between them.
> *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A proposed airport is at an elevation of **500 m** above MSL. The **basic runway length** for the design aircraft is **2500 m**. The **monthly mean of the average daily maximum temperature** for the hottest month is **35°C**. The **effective gradient** is **0.8%**. Compute the **actual runway length** required, applying all FAA corrections. Take the **average headwind component** during take-off as **10 kt**.
> *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define each declared distance (4 marks for definitions, 3 marks for relationships).**

> **TORA (Take-Off Run Available):**
> The length of runway declared available and suitable for the **ground run of a take-off**.

> **TODA (Take-Off Distance Available):**
> The length of the take-off run available plus the length of the **clearway**, if provided.

> **ASDA (Accelerate-Stop Distance Available):**
> The length of the take-off run available plus the length of the **stopway**, if provided.

> **LDA (Landing Distance Available):**
> The length of runway declared available and suitable for the **ground run of a landing**.

**Step 2 — Relationships (use sketch above).**

The fundamental ICAO inequalities are:

$$\text{TODA} \geq \text{TORA} \geq \text{ASDA}$$

$$\text{LDA} \leq \text{TORA}$$

If there is no clearway, TODA = TORA. If there is no stopway, ASDA = TORA. LDA is reduced below TORA whenever a displaced threshold is present.

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**

- $L_0 = 2500 \text{ m}$
- $h = 500 \text{ m}$
- $T = 35 \text{ °C}$
- $G = 0.8\%$
- $V_{head} = 10 \text{ kt}$

**Step 1 — Standard temperature at 500 m elevation.**

$$T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times 500 = 15 - 3.25 = 11.75 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature excess.**

$$T - T_{std} = 35 - 11.75 = 23.25 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Elevation correction.**

$$f_E = 1 + \frac{0.07 \times 500}{300} = 1 + \frac{35}{300} = 1 + 0.1167 = 1.1167$$

**Step 4 — Temperature correction.**

$$f_T = 1 + 0.01 \times 23.25 = 1.2325$$

**Step 5 — Gradient correction.**

$$f_G = 1 + 0.10 \times 0.8 = 1.08$$

**Step 6 — Wind correction.**

$$f_W = 1 - \frac{V_{head}}{100} = 1 - \frac{10}{100} = 0.90$$

**Step 7 — Compute the actual runway length.**

$$L = L_0 \times f_E \times f_T \times f_G \times f_W$$

$$L = 2500 \times 1.1167 \times 1.2325 \times 1.08 \times 0.90$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$L = 2500 \times 1.1167 = 2791.75$$

$$L = 2791.75 \times 1.2325 = 3440.83$$

$$L = 3440.83 \times 1.08 = 3716.10$$

$$L = 3716.10 \times 0.90 = 3344.49 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer: $L \approx 3345 \text{ m}$**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:**
> Students frequently forget to use the **elevation-corrected** standard temperature. Using $T_{std} = 15°C$ instead of $11.75°C$ gives $T - T_{std} = 20°C$, leading to $f_T = 1.20$ (incorrect) instead of **1.2325** (correct). The marks lost here are 1–2 per incorrect factor.

---

### QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Alternative]**
> *(a)* Explain the **wind rose method** of runway orientation. State the ICAO crosswind limit for jet aircraft and describe how a "wind coverage" of 95% is achieved.
> *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* The wind data for an airport site is given below. The proposed runway is oriented **090°–270° (East–West)**. The ICAO crosswind limit is **20 kt**. Determine the **wind coverage** of the proposed runway.
>
> | Wind Direction (° from) | Mean Speed (kt) | \% of Time |
> |---|---|---|
> | 360 – 010 | 15 | 5 |
> | 010 – 040 | 20 | 8 |
> | 040 – 070 | 25 | 10 |
> | 070 – 100 | 30 | 15 |
> | 100 – 130 | 25 | 12 |
> | 130 – 160 | 20 | 10 |
> | 160 – 190 | 15 | 7 |
> | 190 – 220 | 10 | 5 |
> | 220 – 250 | 15 | 8 |
> | 250 – 280 | 25 | 10 |
> | 280 – 310 | 30 | 6 |
> | 310 – 340 | 20 | 4 |
>
> *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Wind rose definition (2 marks).**

A **wind rose** is a polar diagram showing, for a given site, the **frequency** of wind blowing from various directions (typically in 10° sectors) at various speed ranges. The length of each "petal" represents the **percentage of time** the wind blows from that direction.

**Step 2 — Crosswind limit (1 mark).**

ICAO limit for jet aircraft:

$$C_{cross,max} = 20 \text{ kt} = 37 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 3 — Wind coverage concept (2 marks).**

**Wind coverage** is the percentage of time during which the **crosswind component** on a particular runway orientation is within the **allowable limit** (≤ 20 kt for jets).

**Step 4 — How 95% coverage is achieved (2 marks).**

- A **transparent template** with parallel lines at $\pm \theta_{max}$ is overlaid on the wind rose.
- $\theta_{max} = \arcsin(20/V)$ is computed for each wind speed group.
- The total % of observations *inside* the template envelope is calculated.
- If coverage is less than 95%, the template is **rotated** and re-tested.
- The orientation that yields the **highest coverage** (≥ 95%) is selected.
- If no single runway can give 95%, a **crosswind runway** is added.

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute $\theta_{max}$ for each wind speed.**

For each wind speed, compute the maximum angle that gives a 20 kt crosswind:

$$\theta_{max} = \arcsin(20/V)$$

| $V$ (kt) | $\theta_{max} = \arcsin(20/V)$ (°) | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | 180.0 (no restriction) | Always acceptable |
| 15 | $\arcsin(1.33)$ = N/A (all OK) | Always acceptable |
| 20 | 90.0 | $\pm 90°$ from runway |
| 25 | 53.1 | $\pm 53°$ from runway |
| 30 | 41.8 | $\pm 42°$ from runway |

**Step 2 — Compute the angular acceptance zone for the 090°–270° runway.**

The runway centerline is along 180° (taking the 270 end) or 0° (taking the 090 end). The acceptance zone for the **combined bidirectional runway** is $\pm \theta_{max}$ from either centerline, which effectively becomes $\pm 90°$ centered on the runway axis.

Since the crosswind limit is 20 kt, for a wind from direction $\alpha$, the angle relative to the runway is $\theta = \alpha - 180°$ (for the 270 end) or $\theta = \alpha$ (for the 090 end). The wind is acceptable on *at least one end* if the angle between the wind and either runway centerline is $\leq \theta_{max}$.

For practical purposes, with the runway oriented **090°–270°**, a wind blowing from any direction between **090° – $\theta_{max}$** and **090° + $\theta_{max}$** is acceptable on the 090 end, and likewise for the 270 end.

**Step 3 — Tabulate acceptability for each sector (1 mark for each row).**

| Direction From (°) | Speed (kt) | \% Time | Angle to Runway 09 | $\theta_{max}$ for $V$ | Acceptable on Rwy 09? | Acceptable on Rwy 27? | Count? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 360 | 15 | 5 | 90° | All OK | Yes (crosswind 15) | Yes | **5** |
| 025 | 20 | 8 | 65° | 90° | Yes | Yes | **8** |
| 055 | 25 | 10 | 35° | 53° | Yes | Yes | **10** |
| 085 | 30 | 15 | 5° | 42° | Yes | Yes | **15** |
| 115 | 25 | 12 | 25° | 53° | Yes | Yes | **12** |
| 145 | 20 | 10 | 55° | 90° | Yes | Yes | **10** |
| 175 | 15 | 7 | 85° | All OK | Yes (crosswind ~15) | Yes | **7** |
| 205 | 10 | 5 | 80° from 09 / 100° from 27 | 180° | Yes (both) | Yes | **5** |
| 235 | 15 | 8 | 145° / 35° from 27 | All OK | No (cross 15) / Yes | Yes | **8** |
| 265 | 25 | 10 | 175° / 5° from 27 | 53° | No / Yes (head 25) | Yes | **10** |
| 295 | 30 | 6 | 205° / 25° from 27 | 42° | No (cross 13) / Yes (head 27) | Yes | **6** |
| 325 | 20 | 4 | 235° / 55° from 27 | 90° | No / Yes | Yes | **4** |

**Step 4 — Sum the wind coverage.**

$$\text{Total Coverage} = 5 + 8 + 10 + 15 + 12 + 10 + 7 + 5 + 8 + 10 + 6 + 4 = 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

$$\text{Wind Coverage} = 100\% \geq 95\%$$

**The proposed runway orientation 090°–270° is operationally acceptable per ICAO standards.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:**
> Students often forget that a **bidirectional runway** can accept wind from **both ends**. They count only one direction and get ~50% coverage, leading to a wrong conclusion. Always consider BOTH centerlines. Marks lost: **3 marks**.
> 
> **Pitfall #3:**
> For wind speeds ≤ 20 kt, $\theta_{max} \geq 90°$, which means the wind is acceptable from *any* direction. Forgetting this leads to under-coverage. Marks lost: **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Warnings and Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Module 1:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply elevation correction to standard temperature** — uses $T_{std} = 15°C$ always.
> 2. **Using a single runway direction** in the wind rose problem instead of both (09/27).
> 3. **Confusing stopway and clearway** — these are tested almost every KTU cycle.
> 4. **Mixing units** — wind speed must be in knots for the crosswind limit (20 kt) but in km/h for the formula $V_{head}/100$ (KTU accepts both, but be consistent).
> 5. **Forgetting the displaced threshold effect on LDA** — LDA is reduced, TORA is not.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Airport Elements (Airfield)**
>
> **Core Definitions (must be memorized verbatim):**
> - ✦ **Airfield:** Movement area of an airport (runway + taxiway + apron + safety areas)
> - ✦ **Runway:** Rectangular paved surface for take-off and landing
> - ✦ **Taxiway:** Paved route for aircraft ground movement
> - ✦ **Apron:** Paved area for aircraft parking, loading, and servicing
> - ✦ **Stopway:** Ground area at runway end for stopping an aborted take-off (contributes to ASDA)
> - ✦ **Clearway:** Air area at runway end for continued climb after engine failure (contributes to TODA)
> - ✦ **Wind Coverage:** % of time crosswind component is within the allowable limit
> - ✦ **RESA:** Runway End Safety Area (90 m × 90 m minimum for Code 4)
> 
> **Universal Formulas (must be at your fingertips):**
> - ✦ $C_{cross} = V \sin\theta$
> - ✦ $C_{head} = V \cos\theta$
> - ✦ $\theta_{max} = \arcsin(C_{max}/V)$
> - ✦ $f_E = 1 + 0.07h/300$
> - ✦ $f_T = 1 + 0.01(T - T_{std})$
> - ✦ $T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065h$
> - ✦ $f_G = 1 + 0.10G$
> - ✦ $L = L_0 \cdot f_E \cdot f_T \cdot f_G \cdot f_W$
> 
> **ICAO Mandatory Thresholds:**
> - ✦ Wind coverage $\geq$ **95%**
> - ✦ Crosswind limit = **20 kt** (jets), **13 kt** (small aircraft)
> - ✦ ICAO Code 4 runway $\geq$ **1800 m** ARFL
> - ✦ ICAO Code Letter F = **wingspan 65–80 m** (e.g., A380)
> 
> **Key Relationships (inequalities):**
> - ✦ **TODA** $\geq$ **TORA** $\geq$ **ASDA**
> - ✦ **LDA** $\leq$ **TORA**
> 
> **Classification Memories:**
> - ✦ Stopway → **ASDA** (mnemonic: **S**topway → **A**SDA)
> - ✦ Clearway → **TODA** (mnemonic: **C**learway → **T**ODA)
> - ✦ Displaced threshold → reduces **LDA**, not TORA
> 
> **Numerical Constants for Quick Calculation:**
> - ✦ $0.07/300$ → elevation factor per meter
> - ✦ $0.01$ per °C → temperature factor per degree
> - ✦ $0.10$ per 1% → gradient factor per percent
> - ✦ $0.0065$ °C/m → standard temperature lapse rate
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terminal Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Terminal Area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

The **Terminal Area** of an airport is the **central operational and processing zone** that interfaces the **airside** (runways, taxiways) with the **landside** (surface access, parking, and city). It encompasses the **terminal building, apron (ramp), aircraft gates, cargo facilities, support buildings, access roads, and landside ground transport infrastructure**. The terminal area is the region of the airport where **passenger enplanement/deplanement, baggage transfer, aircraft servicing, and modal interchange** between air travel and ground transportation take place.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the terminal area is treated as a **multi-modal node** whose design must balance **passenger Level of Service (LoS), aircraft gate requirements, apron geometry, and landside access capacity** within a defined geometric footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Terminal Area):**
> The terminal area is the *spatial and functional heart* of an airport. Its planning drives the **capacity, delay, and passenger experience** of the entire facility. The student must be able to (i) identify components, (ii) compute apron and gate requirements, (iii) compare terminal configurations, and (iv) evaluate ground access sizing.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of the **terminal area as a "hotel lobby plus a bus station plus a security gate"** all rolled into one — but for aircraft.

- The **runway** is the highway.
- The **taxiway** is the driveway leading to the building.
- The **apron** is the parking lot at the front of the building where planes "park."
- The **gate** is the specific stall where the plane stops to let passengers in/out.
- The **terminal building** is the lobby — where you check in, drop bags, walk through security, and wait to board.
- The **curbside + access road** is the front porch and drop-off zone.

Just as a busy hotel lobby must be sized to handle peak check-in/out surges without long queues, the **terminal area must be sized for the *peak hour***, not the average day. If it is too small, you get queues, frustrated passengers, and delayed flights (a phenomenon called **congestion delay**). If it is too big, you waste land and money.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** International Air Transport Association (IATA) recommends a **terminal building floor area of ~18–25 m² per peak-hour passenger (domestic)** and **~25–35 m² per peak-hour passenger (international)**. These are the "magic numbers" examiners love to test.

## 1.3 Functional Sub-Components of the Terminal Area

| Sub-Component | Primary Function | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Terminal Building** | Passenger & baggage processing | Very High |
| **Apron (Ramp)** | Aircraft parking & servicing | Very High |
| **Gates (Contact & Remote)** | Boarding/deboarding interface | High |
| **Cargo Buildings** | Freight handling | Moderate |
| **Access Roads & Curbside** | Vehicle drop-off/pickup | High |
| **Parking (short/long-term)** | Auto storage | High |
| **Support Facilities** | Fuel, catering, MRO, GSE | Moderate |
| **Public Transit Interfaces** | Metro/rail/bus stops | High |

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Logic of Terminal Area Planning

The terminal area is planned top-down in the following logical chain:

1. **Forecast Aviation Demand** → Annual passengers, peak hour passengers, peak hour aircraft movements.
2. **Determine Aircraft Gate Requirements** → Using **gate utilization** and **gate occupancy time**.
3. **Size the Apron** → Based on number of gates + aircraft wingspan + taxi clearances.
4. **Size the Terminal Building** → Based on peak hour passengers and IATA floor area standards.
5. **Size the Landside** → Curbside, parking, access road, public transit.
6. **Choose a Terminal Concept** → Linear, Pier, Satellite, Hybrid — based on traffic and land.
7. **Verify Level of Service (LoS)** → Compare design hour to IATA LoS standards (A–E).

## 2.2 Gate Requirement — The Core Equation

The number of aircraft gates required is governed by:

$$N_{gates} = \frac{V_{ph} \times T_{occ}}{U \times 60}$$

Where:
- $N_{gates}$ = Number of contact/stand gates required
- $V_{ph}$ = Peak hour aircraft movements (arrivals + departures)
- $T_{occ}$ = Average gate occupancy time (minutes), typically **45–60 min** for domestic narrow-body, **90–120 min** for wide-body international
- $U$ = Gate utilization factor (typically **0.7 to 0.85**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why divide by 60?** Because $T_{occ}$ is in **minutes** and one hour = 60 minutes. The numerator is "plane-minutes of demand per peak hour," and the denominator is "plane-minutes supplied per gate per hour."

## 2.3 Terminal Building Floor Area

$$A_{terminal} = P_{php} \times A_{p}$$

Where:
- $A_{terminal}$ = Total terminal building floor area (m²)
- $P_{php}$ = Peak hour passengers (enplanements + deplanements)
- $A_{p}$ = Floor area per peak-hour passenger (m²), from IATA: **18–25 m² (domestic)**, **25–35 m² (international)**

## 2.4 Apron Area

$$A_{apron} = N_{gates} \times A_{per\ gate}$$

Where:
- $A_{apron}$ = Apron area (m²)
- $A_{per\ gate}$ = Apron area per gate (depends on aircraft class), typically:
  - **Narrow-body (B737, A320)**: ~1,500 m²
  - **Wide-body (B777, A380)**: ~3,000–4,500 m²

> [!NOTE]
> The **apron area includes the gate stand + taxilane access + service roads for ground support equipment (GSE)**. A common rule of thumb is that **each gate consumes 2.5×–3× the aircraft footprint**.

## 2.5 Curbside Length

$$L_{curb} = \frac{P_{php} \times 1.2}{C}$$

Where:
- $L_{curb}$ = Curbside length required (m)
- $P_{php}$ = Peak hour passengers
- $C$ = Curb capacity, typically **30–50 passengers/m** of curb (dual lane)

## 2.6 Parking Requirement

$$P_{cars} = P_{php} \times f_{park} \times D_{stay}$$

Where:
- $P_{cars}$ = Number of parking spaces required
- $f_{park}$ = Fraction of passengers who arrive by car (typically **0.6–0.8**)
- $D_{stay}$ = Average duration of stay in hours (business ~3 hr, leisure ~5 days)

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Standard Values | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gates required | $N_g = \dfrac{V_{ph} \cdot T_{occ}}{U \cdot 60}$ | $T_{occ} = 45\text{–}120$ min, $U = 0.7\text{–}0.85$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Terminal floor area | $A_t = P_{php} \cdot A_p$ | $A_p = 18\text{–}35$ m²/pax | m² |
| 3 | Apron area | $A_{ap} = N_g \cdot A_{g}$ | $A_g = 1500\text{–}4500$ m² | m² |
| 4 | Curbside length | $L_c = \dfrac{P_{php} \cdot 1.2}{C}$ | $C = 30\text{–}50$ pax/m | m |
| 5 | Parking spaces | $P_{car} = P_{php} \cdot f_{park} \cdot D_{stay}$ | $f_{park} = 0.6\text{–}0.8$ | spaces |
| 6 | Annual → Peak hour | $P_{php} = \dfrac{P_{annual} \times R_{peak}}{10^6}$ | $R_{peak} \approx 0.0003$ to $0.001$ | pax/hr |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** These formulas are used by **IATA, ICAO, and FAA** in airport master plans globally. For example, a 10 million passenger/year airport will have ~3,000 peak-hour passengers, requiring ~30–40 gates and ~75,000 m² of terminal area.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Gate Requirement Equation

We want to derive the formula $N_g = \dfrac{V_{ph} \cdot T_{occ}}{U \cdot 60}$ from first principles.

### Step 1 — Define the Demand
In one peak hour, there are $V_{ph}$ aircraft movements. Each aircraft **occupies a gate for $T_{occ}$ minutes**. The total "gate-minutes demanded" per peak hour is:

$$D = V_{ph} \times T_{occ} \quad \text{[gate-minutes per hour]}$$

### Step 2 — Define the Supply per Gate
In one hour, one gate is available for $60$ minutes. But due to buffer times (cleaning, turnaround variations, taxi-in/taxi-out), the **effective available time per gate per hour is $U \times 60$ minutes**, where $U$ is the utilization factor.

$$S = U \times 60 \quad \text{[gate-minutes supplied per gate per hour]}$$

### Step 3 — Equate Demand and Supply
The number of gates must satisfy $N_g \times S \geq D$:

$$N_g \times U \times 60 \geq V_{ph} \times T_{occ}$$

Rearranging for the design number of gates (taking equality):

$$N_g = \frac{V_{ph} \times T_{occ}}{U \times 60}$$

This is the **fundamental gate-requirement equation** used by every airport planner.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $U$ is critical. Using $U = 1.0$ means a 100% utilized gate — operationally impossible (no buffer for delays). Using $U = 0.7$ leaves 30% slack, which is industry standard.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An airport forecasts 12 million annual passengers. The peak hour factor is $R_{peak} = 0.0005$. The peak hour has 40 aircraft movements. Average gate occupancy = 50 minutes. Gate utilization factor = 0.8. IATA floor area = 22 m² per peak-hour passenger.

**Step A — Peak Hour Passengers:**

$$P_{php} = P_{annual} \times R_{peak} = 12{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0005 = 6{,}000 \text{ pax/hr}$$

**Step B — Gates Required:**

$$N_g = \frac{V_{ph} \cdot T_{occ}}{U \cdot 60} = \frac{40 \times 50}{0.8 \times 60} = \frac{2000}{48} \approx 41.67 \rightarrow 42 \text{ gates}$$

**Step C — Terminal Floor Area:**

$$A_t = P_{php} \times A_p = 6{,}000 \times 22 = 132{,}000 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step D — Apron Area (narrow-body mix, $A_g = 1800$ m²):**

$$A_{ap} = 42 \times 1{,}800 = 75{,}600 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step E — Curbside Length (using $C = 40$ pax/m):**

$$L_c = \frac{6{,}000 \times 1.2}{40} = 180 \text{ m}$$

**Step F — Parking Spaces (using $f_{park} = 0.7$, $D_{stay} = 4$ hr):**

$$P_{car} = 6{,}000 \times 0.7 \times 4 = 16{,}800 \text{ spaces}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to design:** The 42-gate, 132,000 m² terminal is roughly the size of **Trivandrum International (TRV)** or a medium-sized Indian airport — illustrating how these formulas drive real-world sizing.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Terminal Concepts

| Concept | Geometry | Capacity (mppa) | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
