| **Linear** | Single building, gates on one side | 1–3 | Simple, low cost, easy wayfinding | Long walking distances, low gate density |
| **Pier (Finger)** | Main building + piers extending to apron | 5–15 | Higher gate density, expandable | Long walking, piers can be confusing |
| **Satellite** | Main building + separate satellite(s) connected by tunnel/bus | 10–30 | Many gates, flexible aircraft mix | Requires people mover or bus, costly |
| **Transporter** | Central terminal + remote stands via bus/apron drive | 5–10 | Maximum flexibility, low gate cost | Bus boarding is slow, weather issues |
| **Hybrid (Mid-Field)** | Combination, often satellite + pier | 20–80+ | Best of all, modern choice | Most complex, highest cost |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Terminal Area Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Annual Passenger Demand] --> B1[Peak Hour Forecast]
    A2[Annual Aircraft Movements] --> B2[Peak Hour Movements]
    B1 --> C1[Peak Hour Passengers]
    B2 --> C2[Peak Hour Movements]
    C1 --> D1[Terminal Building Sizing]
    C2 --> D2[Gate Requirement Calculation]
    C1 --> D3[Curbside Sizing]
    C1 --> D4[Parking Sizing]
    D2 --> D4x[Apron Sizing]
    D1 --> E1[Floor Area per Pax]
    D2 --> E2[Gate Occupancy Time]
    D3 --> E3[Curb Capacity]
    D4 --> E4[Modal Split Factor]
    D4x --> E5[Aircraft Footprint]
    D1 --> F1[Terminal Concept Selection]
    F1 --> G1[Linear]
    F1 --> G2[Pier]
    F1 --> G3[Satellite]
    F1 --> G4[Hybrid]
    G1 --> H1[Final Layout]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    G4 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Level of Service Check]
    I1 --> J1{LoS C or Better}
    J1 -->|Yes| K1[Design Approved]
    J1 -->|No| L1[Resize and Iterate]
    L1 --> B1
```

## 4.2 Terminal Area Component Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LANDSIDE] --> B[Access Roads and Curbside]
    A --> C[Parking]
    A --> D[Public Transit]
    B --> E[TERMINAL BUILDING]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Check-in Hall]
    E --> G[Security]
    E --> H[Concourse]
    E --> I[Baggage Claim]
    E --> J[Concessions]
    F --> K[AIRSIDE]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Apron]
    K --> M[Taxiway]
    K --> N[Cargo Apron]
    L --> O[Runway]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.3 Gate Selection Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Passenger Forecast] --> S2{Annual Pax}
    S2 --> S3[Under 3 mppa]
    S2 --> S4[3 to 15 mppa]
    S2 --> S5[Over 15 mppa]
    S3 --> T1[Linear or Small Pier]
    S4 --> T2[Pier or Satellite]
    S5 --> T3[Satellite or Hybrid Mid-Field]
    T1 --> U1[Single Terminal]
    T2 --> U2[Single or Twin Terminal]
    T3 --> U3[Multi Terminal with People Mover]
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define the term 'Terminal Area' and list its four main functional components."**

**Model Answer:**
The terminal area of an airport is the zone where the airside and landside meet, handling passenger processing, aircraft servicing, and ground transport interchange. Its four main components are:
1. **Terminal building** (passenger and baggage processing),
2. **Apron** (aircraft parking and gate stands),
3. **Landside access** (roads, parking, curbside, public transit),
4. **Support facilities** (cargo, fuel, maintenance, GSE).

*(Examiner's note: A clean 4-component list + one-line definition earns full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What is meant by 'gate occupancy time' and what is its typical range for domestic and international operations?"**

**Model Answer:**
Gate occupancy time is the **total time an aircraft remains parked at a gate** between arrival and departure, including deboarding, servicing, refueling, cleaning, and boarding.

Typical ranges:
- **Domestic narrow-body:** 30–60 minutes
- **Domestic wide-body:** 60–90 minutes
- **International wide-body:** 90–180 minutes

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Always show units** in intermediate steps. Students who write "$N_g = 41.67$" without showing the substitution lose 1 mark.
> - **Do not confuse "peak hour passengers" with "peak hour aircraft movements"** — they are different. Aircraft movements give gates; passengers give floor area.
> - **Always state assumptions** (gate utilization, IATA standard used) explicitly. Examiners award 1 mark just for assumptions.
> - **Convert annual to peak hour correctly** — peak hour factor is typically $0.0003$ to $0.001$, *not* "10% of annual."

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the different **terminal building concepts** (Linear, Pier, Satellite, Transporter) with neat sketches, advantages, disadvantages, and capacity ranges. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An airport handles **8 million annual passengers** with a peak hour factor of **0.0006**. The peak hour has **30 aircraft movements**, with an average gate occupancy of **55 minutes** and a gate utilization factor of **0.75**. Calculate:
- (i) Number of gates required
- (ii) Terminal building floor area (use IATA = 24 m² per peak-hour passenger)
- (iii) Apron area (assume 1,800 m² per gate) *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Terminal Concepts — Explanation:**

| Concept | Description | Capacity | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | Single rectangular building; gates along one long side | 1–3 mppa | Simple, low cost, easy wayfinding | Low gate density, long walks |
| **Pier** | Central processor with projecting piers containing gates | 3–15 mppa | High gate density, expandable | Long walking, complex wayfinding |
| **Satellite** | Central processor with separate satellite(s) connected by tunnel or people-mover | 10–30 mppa | Flexible aircraft mix, fewer taxi conflicts | High construction cost, requires transit |
| **Transporter** | Central processor, remote stands reached by bus | 5–10 mppa | Maximum flexibility, low apron cost | Slow boarding, weather-sensitive |

*(Examiner tip: For 7 marks, expect 2 marks for definitions, 2 for advantages, 2 for disadvantages, 1 for capacity ranges.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Peak Hour Passengers:**
$$P_{php} = 8{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0006 = 4{,}800 \text{ pax/hr}$$

**Step 2 — Gates Required:**
$$N_g = \frac{V_{ph} \times T_{occ}}{U \times 60} = \frac{30 \times 55}{0.75 \times 60} = \frac{1650}{45} = 36.67 \rightarrow 37 \text{ gates}$$

**Step 3 — Terminal Floor Area:**
$$A_t = P_{php} \times A_p = 4{,}800 \times 24 = 115{,}200 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Apron Area:**
$$A_{ap} = 37 \times 1{,}800 = 66{,}600 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answers:** 37 gates, 115,200 m² terminal area, 66,600 m² apron area.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional Test Model]**

**(a)** Define **Level of Service (LoS)** for an airport terminal. List the IATA LoS grades (A–E) and explain what grade "C" represents. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design the **curbside length and parking requirements** for an airport with **5,000 peak-hour passengers**, 70% arriving by car, average stay of 4 hours, and a curb capacity of 40 pax/m. Also comment on **public transit integration** as part of the landside design. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Level of Service (LoS) — Definition and Grades:**

**Definition:** Level of Service is a **qualitative measure** of how well a terminal facility (check-in, security, baggage claim, gate lounge, curbside) serves passengers in terms of **space, time, comfort, and convenience** at peak load. IATA defines six grades: **A (excellent), B (high), C (good/acceptable), D (adequate), E (poor), F (unacceptable).**

**Grade C interpretation:** Grade C is the **IATA design target** for most facilities. It represents *good service* — passengers experience **reasonable waiting times, adequate space, and minor queues**, but the system is operating near capacity. Designing to grade C balances cost with passenger satisfaction.

**The six grades:**
- **A** — Excellent: Free flow, ample space
- **B** — High: Stable flow, comfortable
- **C** — Good: Acceptable, stable, minor delays
- **D** — Adequate: Unstable flow, near capacity
- **E** — Poor: Congested, unacceptable delays
- **F** — Unacceptable: System breakdown

*(Examiner tip: 2 marks for definition, 3 marks for grades A–E, 2 marks for explaining grade C in context.)*

**(b) Curbside and Parking Design:**

**Step 1 — Curbside Length:**
$$L_c = \frac{P_{php} \times 1.2}{C} = \frac{5{,}000 \times 1.2}{40} = \frac{6{,}000}{40} = 150 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Parking Spaces:**
$$P_{car} = P_{php} \times f_{park} \times D_{stay} = 5{,}000 \times 0.7 \times 4 = 14{,}000 \text{ spaces}$$

**Step 3 — Public Transit Integration (commentary):**
A modern terminal area **must integrate multi-modal public transit** (metro, BRT, bus, rail) to reduce curbside congestion, parking demand, and emissions. Best practices include:
- Direct metro/rail station **beneath or adjacent to the terminal**
- Bus bays and BRT corridors along the curbside
- Pedestrian skywalks or subways connecting parking, transit, and terminal
- Future-proofing for emerging modes (EV charging, autonomous shuttles, pods)

Public transit can **reduce car-mode share by 20–40%** in well-designed airport landsides, directly reducing parking and curbside requirements.

**Final Answers:** Curbside = **150 m**, Parking = **14,000 spaces**, plus public transit integration commentary.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Terminal area = airside + landside interface zone.** It includes terminal building, apron, gates, cargo, support, access, and parking.
- **Gate formula:** $N_g = \dfrac{V_{ph} \cdot T_{occ}}{U \cdot 60}$. The utilization factor $U$ is **critical** — never use 1.0 in design.
- **Terminal floor area:** $A_t = P_{php} \times A_p$, where $A_p = 18$–$25$ m² (domestic) and $25$–$35$ m² (international) per IATA.
- **Apron area per gate:** ~1,500 m² (narrow-body) to ~4,500 m² (wide-body); add 2.5–3× aircraft footprint for circulation.
- **Curbside length:** $L_c = P_{php} \cdot 1.2 / C$, with $C = 30$–$50$ pax/m.
- **Parking:** $P_{car} = P_{php} \times f_{park} \times D_{stay}$; assume $f_{park} = 0.6$–$0.8$.
- **Terminal concepts** (in increasing capacity): **Linear < Pier < Satellite < Transporter < Hybrid Mid-Field**.
- **IATA Level of Service (LoS)** target for design is **Grade C** (good/acceptable, stable flow).
- **Peak hour factor** ($R_{peak}$) for annual → hourly conversion is typically $0.0003$–$0.001$ (i.e., 0.03% to 0.1% of annual).
- **Show units and assumptions** explicitly in every calculation — KTU examiners reward this 1 mark.
- **Never confuse "aircraft movements" with "passengers"** — they drive different design outputs (gates vs. floor area).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Obstructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Obstructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (ICAO & KTU 2024 Alignment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Obstruction (Airport Engineering Definition):** An obstruction, in the context of airport planning, is any **fixed (permanent) or mobile (transient) object**, including terrain features, vegetation, buildings, towers, masts, or vehicles, which projects above the defined **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** established around a runway and its approach/departure paths, thereby potentially endangering the safety of aircraft operations during take-off, landing, and maneuvering.

According to **ICAO Annex 14**, the airspace around an aerodrome is divided into volumes of protected airspace called **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)**. Any object (natural or man-made) that penetrates these surfaces is classified as an **obstacle**, and the most severe penetrations are termed **obstructions**.

### Key KTU 2024 Highlight:
> [!NOTE]
> The 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) emphasizes understanding of:
> - **Imaginary Surfaces** (per ICAO Annex 14 & FAA FAR Part 77)
> - **Classification of obstructions** (per penetration severity)
> - **Mitigation strategies** (removal, marking, lighting)
> - **Code-based dimensional requirements** (Runway Code 1, 2, 3, 4)

---

## Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving on a highway. The government creates **invisible tunnels of air space** above and around the road so that low-flying aircraft, rescue helicopters, or even large trucks carrying cranes don't crash into each other.

Now, think of an **airport runway** as a special highway for **airplanes** that are taking off, landing, or flying very low during approach. To protect them, aviation authorities (ICAO, FAA, DGCA) draw **invisible 3D "fence-like" surfaces** in the sky around the runway:

- A **flat ceiling** at 45 m above the runway (called the *Inner Horizontal Surface*)
- A **gently sloping ramp** outward (the *Conical Surface*)
- **Wedge-shaped ramps** at the runway ends (the *Approach* and *Take-off Surfaces*)
- **Sloping side walls** along the runway (the *Transitional Surfaces*)

> 🛬 **Analogy Summary:**
> - The runway is the **floor**.
> - The imaginary surfaces are the **walls, ceiling, and ramps** of an invisible protection building.
> - Any object that **breaks into this building** is an **obstruction**.
> - Just like you cannot have a tree growing through your bedroom ceiling, **an airport cannot have a building piercing its approach ramp**.

---

## The Two Governing Standards (KTU Board Favourite)

| Standard | Governing Body | Key Difference |
|----------|---------------|----------------|
| **ICAO Annex 14** | International Civil Aviation Organization | Globally accepted; used for international airports |
| **FAA FAR Part 77** | U.S. Federal Aviation Administration | More detailed; classifies surfaces into Primary, Approach, Transitional, Horizontal, Conical |

> [!TIP]
> For **KTU 2024 Examinations**, the **ICAO Annex 14 framework** is the primary reference unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)

- **Inner Horizontal Surface elevation:** **45 m** above the aerodrome reference point
- **Conical Surface slope:** **5% (1 in 20)** rising outward
- **Conical Surface height:** **100 m** above Inner Horizontal
- **Approach Surface slope (first section):** **2% (1 in 50)**
- **Approach Surface slope (second section):** **2.5% (1 in 40)**
- **Transitional Surface slope:** **14.3% (1 in 7)** outward and upward
- **Take-off Climb Surface slope:** **2% (1 in 50)**
- **Outer Horizontal Surface elevation:** **150 m** above aerodrome elevation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight ICAO Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)

Per ICAO Annex 14, the protected airspace around an aerodrome is defined by **eight** specific imaginary surfaces. Each surface serves a distinct operational purpose.

### 2.1.1 Outer Horizontal Surface
- **Shape:** Circular (radius 15,000 m centred on the aerodrome reference point)
- **Elevation:** **150 m** above the aerodrome elevation
- **Purpose:** Governs tall structures (towers, chimneys) far from the runway

### 2.1.2 Conical Surface
- **Shape:** Circular ring sloping upward and outward
- **Inner boundary:** Periphery of the Inner Horizontal Surface
- **Slope:** **5% (1:20)**
- **Vertical extent:** **100 m** above the Inner Horizontal Surface (i.e., reaches 150 m total elevation)

### 2.1.3 Inner Horizontal Surface
- **Shape:** Circular or elliptical, depending on runway layout
- **Elevation:** **45 m** above the aerodrome elevation
- **Radius:** **4,000 m** from the runway thresholds (or centre point for circular)
- **Purpose:** Protects circling approaches and maneuvering area

### 2.1.4 Approach Surface
- **Shape:** Trapezoidal wedge along the runway centreline extended
- **Origin:** Threshold of the runway
- **Inner edge width:**
  - **150 m** for instrument runways (Code 3 & 4)
  - **60 m** for non-instrument runways
- **Divergence:** **15%** on each side
- **Length & slope:**
  - **Section 1:** 3,000 m at **2% slope**
  - **Section 2:** 8,400 m additional at **2.5% slope** (then horizontal to 15,000 m total)

### 2.1.5 Transitional Surface
- **Shape:** Sloping plane along the runway edges
- **Origin:** Edge of the runway strip
- **Slope:** **14.3% (1:7)** rising outward and upward
- **Termination:** Intersects the Inner Horizontal Surface

### 2.1.6 Inner Transitional Surface (Precision Approach Runways, Cat II/III only)
- **Origin:** Edge of the runway strip (precision runway)
- **Slope:** **33.3% (1:3)** upward and outward
- **Purpose:** Protects aircraft on precision final approach from lateral obstacles

### 2.1.7 Balked Landing Surface
- **Origin:** Threshold of the precision approach runway
- **Length:** **1,800 m**
- **Slope:** **3.33% (1:30)**
- **Purpose:** Provides clearance for missed approach (go-around) procedures

### 2.1.8 Take-off Climb Surface
- **Origin:** End of the runway (or clearway)
- **Inner edge length:** **180 m** (instrument) or **60 m** (non-instrument)
- **Divergence:** **10%** on each side
- **Length:** **1,500 m** (Code 1, 2) or **15,000 m** (Code 3, 4)
- **Slope:** **2% (1:50)**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $h_{\text{IHS}} = h_{\text{ARP}} + 45$ | Inner Horizontal Surface height | metres |
| 2 | $h_{\text{OHS}} = h_{\text{ARP}} + 150$ | Outer Horizontal Surface height | metres |
| 3 | $h_{\text{Conical top}} = h_{\text{IHS}} + 100$ | Top of Conical Surface | metres |
| 4 | $W_{\text{approach}}(d) = W_0 + 2 \times 0.15 \times d$ | Approach surface width at distance $d$ from threshold | metres |
| 5 | $h_{\text{approach}}(d) = h_{\text{threshold}} + 0.02 \times d$ | Height of approach surface at horizontal distance $d$ (for $d \leq 3000$ m) | metres |
| 6 | $h_{\text{approach}}(d) = h_{\text{3000}} + 0.025 \times (d - 3000)$ | Height of approach surface for $3000 < d \leq 11400$ m | metres |
| 7 | $h_{\text{conical}}(r) = h_{\text{IHS}} + 0.05 \times (r - 4000)$ | Conical surface height at radial distance $r$ (for $r > 4000$ m) | metres |
| 8 | $W_{\text{transition}} = W_{\text{runway}} + 2 \times 0.143 \times h$ | Transitional surface width at height $h$ | metres |
| 9 | $h_{\text{transitional}}(x) = 0.143 \times x$ | Transitional surface height at distance $x$ from runway edge | metres |
| 10 | $h_{\text{takeoff}}(d) = h_{\text{end}} + 0.02 \times d$ | Take-off climb surface height at distance $d$ | metres |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:**
> - $h_{\text{ARP}}$ = Aerodrome Reference Point elevation
> - $W_0$ = Inner edge width of the surface
> - $d$ = horizontal distance from origin (threshold or runway end)
> - $r$ = radial distance from runway centre
> - All slopes are in **decimal form** (e.g., 2% = 0.02)

---

## 2.3 Classification of Obstructions (Severity Hierarchy)

ICAO classifies the criticality of an object based on its **penetration of the OLS** and the **operational impact**:

| Class | Definition | Action |
|-------|------------|--------|
| **Obstacle** | Any object penetrating OLS — *no immediate operational impact* | Mark + Light |
| **Obstruction (Critical)** | Penetrates Approach, Take-off, or Transitional surfaces — *direct safety hazard* | Remove OR Re-route OR Reduce height |
| **Severe Obstruction** | Penetration of Inner Horizontal within 4 km of threshold | **Mandatory removal** |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark questions, students must explicitly state **which surface is penetrated** and **what mitigation** is recommended.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

The Obstruction Limitation framework is critical for:

1. **Urban planning around airports** — Building height zoning within 4-15 km radius
2. **Construction permits** — Builders near airports must obtain NOC from DGCA
3. **Communication/navigation equipment** — VOR, ILS localizer arrays have their own OLS
4. **Disaster mitigation** — Crane operations near runways are tightly regulated
5. **Wildlife hazard management** — Birds, though not "obstructions", are managed under similar airspace principles

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Approach Surface Profile (Full Step-by-Step)

**Problem Statement:** For a Code 4 instrument runway with threshold elevation of **30 m AMSL**, determine the elevation of the Approach Surface at horizontal distances of **1000 m**, **3500 m**, and **6000 m** from the threshold. Also find the width of the approach surface at these distances.

**Given:**
- Inner edge width $W_0 = 150$ m
- Section 1: Length 3000 m, slope 2% (1:50)
- Section 2: Length 8400 m, slope 2.5% (1:40)
- Divergence: 15% on each side
- Threshold elevation: $h_{th} = 30$ m AMSL

**Step 1: Height at 1000 m (Section 1)**

$$d_1 = 1000 \text{ m} \quad (\text{since } d_1 \leq 3000 \text{ m})$$

$$h_{1000} = h_{th} + 0.02 \times d_1$$

$$h_{1000} = 30 + 0.02 \times 1000$$

$$h_{1000} = 30 + 20 = 50 \text{ m AMSL}$$

**Step 2: Height at 3500 m (Section 2)**

First, height at the end of Section 1 (3000 m):

$$h_{3000} = 30 + 0.02 \times 3000 = 30 + 60 = 90 \text{ m AMSL}$$

Then, additional rise in Section 2:

$$\Delta h = 0.025 \times (3500 - 3000) = 0.025 \times 500 = 12.5 \text{ m}$$

$$h_{3500} = 90 + 12.5 = 102.5 \text{ m AMSL}$$

**Step 3: Height at 6000 m (Section 2)**

$$\Delta h = 0.025 \times (6000 - 3000) = 0.025 \times 3000 = 75 \text{ m}$$

$$h_{6000} = 90 + 75 = 165 \text{ m AMSL}$$

**Step 4: Width of Approach Surface at Each Distance**

$$W(d) = W_0 + 2 \times 0.15 \times d$$

At $d = 1000$ m:

$$W_{1000} = 150 + 2 \times 0.15 \times 1000 = 150 + 300 = 450 \text{ m}$$

At $d = 3500$ m:

$$W_{3500} = 150 + 2 \times 0.15 \times 3500 = 150 + 1050 = 1200 \text{ m}$$

At $d = 6000$ m:

$$W_{6000} = 150 + 2 \times 0.15 \times 6000 = 150 + 1800 = 1950 \text{ m}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Conical Surface Elevation Profile

**Problem Statement:** The aerodrome reference point (ARP) is at **100 m AMSL**. Find the elevation of the Conical Surface at radial distances of **4500 m** and **6000 m** from the ARP.

**Given:**
- $h_{\text{ARP}} = 100$ m
- $h_{\text{IHS}} = 100 + 45 = 145$ m
- Inner edge of Conical Surface: $r_0 = 4000$ m
- Slope: 5% (0.05)

**Step 1: Elevation at 4500 m**

$$h_{\text{conical}}(4500) = h_{\text{IHS}} + 0.05 \times (4500 - 4000)$$

$$= 145 + 0.05 \times 500 = 145 + 25 = 170 \text{ m AMSL}$$

**Step 2: Elevation at 6000 m**

$$h_{\text{conical}}(6000) = 145 + 0.05 \times (6000 - 4000)$$

$$= 145 + 0.05 \times 2000 = 145 + 100 = 245 \text{ m AMSL}$$

**Step 3: Verify Total Conical Height**

Conical Surface extends to a height of 100 m above IHS, i.e., up to 245 m AMSL. This occurs at:

$$r_{\text{top}} = 4000 + \frac{100}{0.05} = 4000 + 2000 = 6000 \text{ m}$$

✅ Consistent. Beyond 6000 m radial distance, the **Outer Horizontal Surface** takes over at 150 m above ARP = 250 m AMSL.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for Obstruction Mitigation

| Step | Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|------|----------|----------|---------|
| 1 | Does object penetrate OLS? | Proceed to Step 2 | Not an obstruction |
| 2 | Is it on approach/take-off/transitional surface? | **Critical Obstruction** → Proceed to Step 3 | Mark + Light + Monitor |
| 3 | Can the object be **removed**? | Remove immediately | Proceed to Step 4 |
| 4 | Can the object be **lowered** below OLS? | Reconstruct/reduce height | Proceed to Step 5 |
| 5 | Can the **runway be realigned** to avoid the object? | New alignment | Proceed to Step 6 |
| 6 | Is **day/night marking** feasible? | Apply ICAO marking + obstruction lights | Declare runway restricted; file NOTAM |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Complete Solution

**Q:** A hill is located **5000 m** from the threshold of a Code 3 instrument runway, directly on the extended runway centreline. The runway threshold elevation is **20 m AMSL**. The hill top is at **135 m AMSL**. Determine if the hill is an obstruction, and if so, recommend a suitable action.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the height of the Approach Surface at 5000 m.

Since 5000 m lies in Section 2 (3000 < d ≤ 11400 m):

$$h_{\text{approach}}(5000) = h_{th} + 0.02 \times 3000 + 0.025 \times (5000 - 3000)$$

$$= 20 + 60 + 0.025 \times 2000$$

$$= 20 + 60 + 50 = 130 \text{ m AMSL}$$

**Step 2:** Compare with hill top elevation.

$$h_{\text{hill}} = 135 \text{ m AMSL} \quad \text{vs.} \quad h_{\text{approach}} = 130 \text{ m AMSL}$$

$$\Delta h = 135 - 130 = 5 \text{ m penetration}$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion.

> Since the hill penetrates the Approach Surface by **5 m**, it is a **Critical Obstruction** on the Approach Surface.

**Step 4:** Recommended Action.

Per the analytical matrix, since the hill is a natural feature (cannot be removed easily):
- **Option A:** Reduce the hill height by **5 m** (cutting/excavation) — preferred
- **Option B:** Realign the runway threshold by **moving threshold** so the new approach surface is above the hill
- **Option C:** Apply **day marking** (checkerboard pattern) and **night lighting** (high-intensity red obstruction lights) as a temporary measure
- **Option D:** Declare the runway **restricted** and file a **NOTAM** (Notice to Airmen) until the issue is resolved

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: 3D Functional Architecture of Obstacle Limitation Surfaces

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Aerodrome Reference Point ARP] --> B[Inner Horizontal Surface at 45 m]
    A --> C[Conical Surface at 5 percent slope rises 100 m]
    A --> D[Outer Horizontal Surface at 150 m radius 15000 m]
    B --> E[Approach Surface at Threshold diverges 15 percent slope 2 percent]
    B --> F[Transitional Surface at Runway Edges slope 14.3 percent 1 in 7]
    B --> G[Takeoff Climb Surface at Runway End diverges 10 percent slope 2 percent]
    E --> E1[Section 1: 3000 m at 2 percent]
    E --> E2[Section 2: 8400 m at 2.5 percent]
    G --> G1[Length 1500 m or 15000 m based on Code]
    F --> F1[Extends to intersection with Inner Horizontal]
    C --> C1[Extends from r equals 4000 m to r equals 6000 m]
    D --> D1[Beyond 6000 m radius up to 15000 m at constant 150 m elevation]

    classDef surface fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef section fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class A origin

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Obstruction Mitigation Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Object Penetrates OLS Question] -->|No| N2[Not an Obstruction: No Action]
    N1 -->|Yes| N3[Identify Surface Penetrated]
    N3 -->|Inner Horizontal| N4[Critical: Remove or Realign]
    N3 -->|Conical| N5[Mark with Medium Intensity Light]
    N3 -->|Approach or Takeoff| N6[Severe Obstruction]
    N3 -->|Transitional| N7[Mark and Light: Red Obstruction Lights]
    N6 --> N8[Can Object be Removed]
    N8 -->|Yes| N9[Remove Object Immediately]
    N8 -->|No| N10[Can Object be Lowered]
    N10 -->|Yes| N11[Reduce Height Below OLS]
    N10 -->|No| N12[Can Runway be Realigned]
    N12 -->|Yes| N13[Realign Runway Threshold]
    N12 -->|No| N14[Apply Marking and High Intensity Lights]
    N14 --> N15[Issue NOTAM and Restrict Operations]

    classDef decision fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Cross-Sectional Profile (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        direction LR
        P1[Threshold Elevation: h_th] --> P2[Approach Section 1: 0 to 3000 m]
        P2 --> P3[Approach Section 2: 3000 to 11400 m]
        P3 --> P4[Approach Horizontal: 11400 to 15000 m]
    end

    subgraph B[HEIGHT PROFILE FROM THRESHOLD]
        direction LR
        Q1[h_th plus 0 m at 0 m] --> Q2[h_th plus 60 m at 3000 m]

    subgraph C[OBJECT EVALUATION]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Obstruction" in the context of airport engineering. List any four imaginary surfaces specified by ICAO Annex 14.**

**Model Answer:**

An **obstruction** in airport engineering is any fixed or mobile object (terrain, vegetation, building, or structure) that projects above the defined **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** of an aerodrome, thereby posing a hazard to aircraft operations.

**Four ICAO Imaginary Surfaces:**
1. **Inner Horizontal Surface** — at 45 m above the aerodrome reference point
2. **Conical Surface** — slopes upward at 5% (1:20) to a height of 100 m
3. **Approach Surface** — trapezoidal surface extending from the runway threshold
4. **Take-off Climb Surface** — originates at the runway end, diverges at 10%

*(Any four surfaces with correct elevation/slope = full 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the Inner Horizontal Surface? Mention its shape and dimensions.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Inner Horizontal Surface (IHS)** protects aircraft during the **circling approach** and **maneuvering phase** near the runway.

- **Shape:** Circular (in the case of a single circular IHS) or elliptical (when two runways are present, the IHS is the largest combination of circles at each threshold)
- **Elevation:** **45 m** above the aerodrome reference point (ARP)
- **Radius:** Extends **4,000 m** from the threshold(s) of the runway
- **Purpose:** Any object above this surface within 4 km of the threshold is considered a **critical obstruction** requiring mandatory removal or marking.

*(Shape + Elevation + Radius = 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Approach Surface** as defined by ICAO Annex 14 for an instrument runway. Include its **shape, origin, dimensions, slopes, and length** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hill located **4,500 m** from the threshold of an instrument runway, directly on the extended centreline, has its summit at **210 m AMSL**. The threshold elevation is **25 m AMSL**. Determine if the hill is an obstruction, and if so, recommend suitable mitigation measures. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **Approach Surface** is a **trapezoidal imaginary surface** established in the direction of aircraft landing along the runway centreline extended. Its function is to protect aircraft during the **final approach phase** of landing.

**Key Specifications (Code 3 & 4 Instrument Runway):**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Inner edge width** | 150 m (perpendicular to centreline, at threshold) |
| **Location of inner edge** | At the runway threshold |
| **Divergence (each side)** | 15% |
| **Section 1 length** | 3,000 m |
| **Section 1 slope** | 2% (1:50) — rising in the direction of approach |
| **Section 2 length** | 8,400 m |
| **Section 2 slope** | 2.5% (1:40) |
| **Outer horizontal extension** | From 11,400 m to 15,000 m at constant elevation |
| **Total length** | 15,000 m |

**Diagrammatic Representation:**

```
                    ┌──── Section 2: 8400 m @ 2.5% ────┐
                   ╱                                      ╲
                  ╱                                        ╲
                 ╱                                          ╲
                ╱                                            ╲
               ╱ Section 1: 3000 m @ 2%                       ╲
              ╱                                                ╲
             ╱__________________________________________________╲
            ↑              RUNWAY THRESHOLD (150 m wide)         ↑
```

The Approach Surface is critical for instrument runways where pilots rely on ILS guidance. Any object penetrating it directly affects the **Minimum Descent Altitude (MDA)** and **Decision Altitude (DA)**.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $h_{th} = 25$ m AMSL
- $d = 4500$ m (from threshold, on extended centreline)
- $h_{\text{hill}} = 210$ m AMSL

**Step 1:** Determine which section of the Approach Surface applies.

Since 3000 m < 4500 m ≤ 11,400 m, the point lies in **Section 2**.

**Step 2:** Compute the height of the Approach Surface at 4,500 m.

Height at the end of Section 1 (3000 m):

$$h_{3000} = 25 + 0.02 \times 3000 = 25 + 60 = 85 \text{ m AMSL}$$

Additional rise in Section 2 over (4500 − 3000) = 1500 m:

$$\Delta h = 0.025 \times 1500 = 37.5 \text{ m}$$

$$h_{\text{approach}}(4500) = 85 + 37.5 = 122.5 \text{ m AMSL}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with hill summit elevation.

$$h_{\text{hill}} - h_{\text{approach}} = 210 - 122.5 = 87.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

The hill penetrates the Approach Surface by **87.5 m**, making it a **Severe Obstruction** on the Approach Surface.

**Step 5:** Recommended Mitigation (in order of preference):

1. **Excavation / Cutting** — Reduce the hill height by 87.5 m (or more) to bring it below the approach surface.
2. **Runway Threshold Displacement** — Move the threshold inward by an appropriate distance so that the new approach surface clears the hill (a redesign of approach profile).
3. **Runway Realignment** — Change the runway heading to avoid alignment with the hill.
4. **Marking and Lighting** — Apply **high-intensity red obstruction lights** (day and night) and **alternating orange-and-white paint pattern** on the hill as a temporary measure.
5. **Operational Restriction** — File a **NOTAM** and limit the runway usage to **visual approaches only**, increasing the **Minimum Descent Altitude**.

---

### 📗 Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Describe the **Conical Surface** and the **Transitional Surface** as per ICAO Annex 14. State their dimensions, slopes, and operational significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The aerodrome reference point is at **150 m AMSL**. A radio tower is located at a radial distance of **5500 m** from the ARP. The top of the tower is at **370 m AMSL**. Check whether the tower constitutes an obstruction. If yes, suggest appropriate remedial measures. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Conical Surface:**

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Shape** | Circular ring, sloping upward and outward |
| **Inner boundary** | Periphery of the Inner Horizontal Surface (radius 4,000 m from threshold) |
| **Slope** | 5% (1:20) measured in a vertical plane perpendicular to the surface |
| **Vertical extent** | 100 m above the Inner Horizontal Surface |
| **Outer edge elevation** | 145 m (for ARP at 100 m) — same as IHS + 100 m |
| **Outer radial distance** | 4,000 + (100/0.05) = 6,000 m from threshold |

**Operational Significance:**
- Protects visual circling approaches
- Governs obstacles far from the runway (tall structures in the periphery)
- Ensures adequate visual maneuvering airspace

**Transitional Surface:**

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Shape** | Sloping plane along each side of the runway and approach surface |
| **Origin** | Edge of the runway strip (75 m from runway centreline) |
| **Slope** | 14.3% (1:7) outward and upward |
| **Termination** | Where it intersects the Inner Horizontal Surface (at 45 m elevation) |
| **Operational Significance** | Prevents aircraft flying low on final approach from clipping lateral obstacles such as buildings, trees, or terrain |

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $h_{\text{ARP}} = 150$ m AMSL
- Radial distance of tower $r = 5500$ m
- $h_{\text{tower}} = 370$ m AMSL

**Step 1:** Compute the elevation of the Inner Horizontal Surface.

$$h_{\text{IHS}} = h_{\text{ARP}} + 45 = 150 + 45 = 195 \text{ m AMSL}$$

**Step 2:** Determine the Conical Surface height at 5500 m.

Since $r = 5500$ m > 4000 m, the Conical Surface applies:

$$h_{\text{conical}}(5500) = h_{\text{IHS}} + 0.05 \times (5500 - 4000)$$

$$= 195 + 0.05 \times 1500 = 195 + 75 = 270 \text{ m AMSL}$$

**Step 3:** Compare tower elevation with Conical Surface.

$$h_{\text{tower}} - h_{\text{conical}} = 370 - 270 = 100 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

The tower penetrates the Conical Surface by **100 m**, making it a **Critical Obstruction**.

**Step 5:** Check if it penetrates the Outer Horizontal Surface.

$$h_{\text{OHS}} = h_{\text{ARP}} + 150 = 150 + 150 = 300 \text{ m AMSL}$$

Since the tower is at 370 m, it also penetrates the **Outer Horizontal Surface** by 70 m.

**Step 6:** Remedial Measures (in order of preference):

1. **Reduce the tower height** to below 270 m (or below 300 m to satisfy OHS).
2. **Install medium-intensity white obstruction lights** (for daytime marking) and **high-intensity red lights** (for night).
3. **Apply high-visibility paint pattern** — alternating international orange and white bands.
4. **File a NOTAM** with the aviation authority (DGCA in India).
5. **Coordinate with the aviation authority** for an aeronautical study to assess if the tower can remain with operational restrictions (e.g., restricted circling approaches).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to **add the ARP elevation** to the surface elevations (i.e., they report IHS as "45 m" instead of "ARP + 45 m"). Always report **AMSL elevation**, not relative height.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusion between **Conical Surface termination** (at 6000 m radial distance) and **Outer Horizontal Surface start** (also at 6000 m). Beyond 6000 m, the **Outer Horizontal Surface** governs, not the Conical.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to mention the **divergence percentage** (15% for approach, 10% for take-off). This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Stating only the slope without **specifying the length of each section** of the Approach Surface (Section 1: 3000 m, Section 2: 8400 m). Examiners allocate separate marks for these.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing **Transitional Surface (1:7)** with **Inner Transitional Surface (1:3)**. The latter applies ONLY to Precision Approach Cat II/III runways.
> 6. **Common Mistake 6:** For Approach Surface problems, students often use **2.5% slope for the entire 4500 m**, ignoring the section transition at 3000 m. This gives wrong results.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 **8 ICAO OLS Surfaces:** Outer Horizontal, Conical, Inner Horizontal, Approach, Inner Transitional, Transitional, Balked Landing, Take-off Climb
- 🔹 **Inner Horizontal Surface:** Elevation = ARP + **45 m**, Radius = **4000 m**
- 🔹 **Conical Surface:** Slope = **5% (1:20)**, Rises **100 m** above IHS, Extends from 4000 m to **6000 m** radial distance
- 🔹 **Outer Horizontal Surface:** Elevation = ARP + **150 m**, Radius = **15,000 m**
- 🔹 **Approach Surface:** Inner edge = **150 m** (instrument) or **60 m** (non-instrument), Divergence = **15%** each side, Section 1 = 3000 m @ **2%**, Section 2 = 8400 m @ **2.5%**
- 🔹 **Transitional Surface:** Slope = **14.3% (1:7)**, from runway strip edge to IHS
- 🔹 **Inner Transitional Surface:** Slope = **33.3% (1:3)**, only for **Precision Approach Cat II/III**
- 🔹 **Balked Landing Surface:** Length = **1800 m**, Slope = **3.33% (1:30)**
- 🔹 **Take-off Climb Surface:** Inner edge = **180 m** (instrument) or **60 m** (non-instrument), Divergence = **10%** each side, Slope = **2%**
- 🔹 **Approach Width Formula:** $W(d) = W_0 + 2 \times 0.15 \times d$
- 🔹 **Conical Height Formula:** $h(r) = h_{\text{IHS}} + 0.05 \times (r - 4000)$
- 🔹 **Approach Height (Section 2):** $h(d) = h_{th} + 60 + 0.025 \times (d - 3000)$
- 🔹 **Mitigation Hierarchy:** Remove → Lower → Realign Runway → Mark & Light → NOTAM
- 🔹 **Marking:** Day — orange/white paint; Night — high-intensity red obstruction lights
- 🔹 **Always report heights in AMSL** (Above Mean Sea Level), never as relative heights
- 🔹 **KTU Favourite Traps:** Confusing 1:7 with 1:3, forgetting section transition at 3000 m, missing divergence percentages

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approach Zone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Approach Zone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU/ICAO Standard)

The **Approach Zone** (also called the **Approach Surface** or **Approach Funnel**) is an imaginary inclined trapezoidal plane that extends outward from the end of the runway along the direction of the runway centerline. It is a critical component of the **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** defined under **ICAO Annex 14** and **FAA FAR Part 77**, designed to protect aircraft during the final approach phase of landing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The Approach Zone is the imaginary surface which begins at the end of the runway strip (or threshold) and extends outward and upward, defining a protected volume of airspace in which no object, terrain, or structure may project above the specified gradient, except for frangible navigational aids.

**Boundary Geometric Components:**
1. **Inner Edge** – Width measured at the runway threshold.
2. **Outer Edge** – Width at the far end of the surface.
3. **Length** – Total horizontal projection from threshold.
4. **Slope/Gradient** – Inclination expressed as a ratio (e.g., 1:40 or 50:1).
5. **Divergence** – Lateral flare rate of the side edges.

> [!NOTE]
> The inner edge of the approach surface is located at the **Runway Threshold** (or 60 m beyond it for non-instrument runways in ICAO standards), not at the physical end of the runway pavement.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are driving a car up a steep, narrowing ramp into a parking garage entrance. As you ascend, the walls of the ramp slope inward and upward, giving you more room near the ground level and progressively less room as you go higher. Now imagine an airplane descending toward a runway — it needs a "funnel of empty air" sloping upward and outward in front of it. That empty protective corridor is exactly the **Approach Zone**.

If a tall building, radio tower, or even a hilltop were to poke into this invisible funnel, the descending aircraft could strike it. Therefore, urban planners, civil aviation authorities, and airport designers use the approach zone to:
- Restrict building heights near runway ends.
- Reject tall trees or mobile cranes.
- Limit terrain modification.
- Reserve airspace exclusively for flight safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** The infamous **Kanishka Building (CBD Belapur)** near Mumbai airport had to be demolished because it penetrated the approach surface of the secondary runway. This landmark case (1990s) became a textbook lesson in airport planning worldwide.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **ICAO Precision Approach Category I Inner Edge Width:** **150 m**
- **ICAO Approach Slope (Non-precision):** **1:40 (2.5%)**
- **ICAO Approach Slope (Precision Cat I):** **1:50 (2%)**
- **FAA Inner Edge Width (Civil Airports):** **1000 ft (304.8 m)**
- **FAA First Segment Slope:** **50:1 (2%)** for the first **10,000 ft (3048 m)**
- **FAA Second Segment Slope:** **40:1 (2.5%)** for the next **40,000 ft**
- **FAA Divergence:** **10%** on each side (1:10 lateral flare)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The approach surface is generated using five geometric parameters. The logic unfolds as follows:

**Step 1 — Anchor at Threshold:**
The inner edge is placed perpendicular to the runway centerline, **60 m** outward from the runway end (for non-precision runways per ICAO) or directly at the threshold for precision runways.

**Step 2 — Inner Edge Width Definition:**
The width of the inner edge is determined by the runway code number and approach type:
- **Code 1 & 2 Non-instrument:** 60 m
- **Code 1 & 2 Instrument:** 90 m
- **Code 3 & 4 Precision Cat I/II/III:** **150 m**

**Step 3 — Divergence (Lateral Flare):**
The side edges diverge outward at a fixed rate to widen the protected corridor for navigation tolerance:
- **ICAO:** **15%** divergence on each side (i.e., 1.67 m horizontal per 10 m length)
- **FAA:** **10%** divergence on each side

**Step 4 — Slope Definition:**
The surface rises upward at a defined gradient:
- This ensures the protected volume accommodates the aircraft's **3° glideslope** plus a safety margin.
- A shallower slope (e.g., 1:50) means a more protected (steeper forbidden) zone in the horizontal sense — actually no, a shallower ratio means *more* permissive vertical clearance per horizontal distance.

**Step 5 — Termination at Outer Edge:**
The surface terminates at a specified length, defining the outer edge width:
- **ICAO Non-precision:** 3000 m long, 600 m outer width
- **FAA:** 50,000 ft (15,240 m) long, 16,000 ft (4876.8 m) outer width

### 2.2 Why Each Parameter Exists (The 'Why' Behind the Rules)

| Design Parameter | Engineering Reason |
|------------------|-------------------|
| Inner edge width | Accommodates the lateral navigational tolerance of the aircraft at touchdown height |
| Divergence | Compensates for pilot error and instrument deviation during descent |
| Slope | Provides vertical clearance that exceeds the aircraft's nominal 3° glideslope |
| Total length | Matches the practical range of approach lighting systems and pilot decision points |
| Termination height | Defines the upper altitude at which aircraft "join" controlled airspace and is free of OLS restrictions |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are high-yield for KTU university exams.**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|-----------|----------------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Outer Edge Width | $W_o = W_i + 2 \cdot L \cdot d$ | metres | Very High |
| 2 | Height of Surface at distance $x$ | $H(x) = H_i + x \cdot s$ | metres | Very High |
| 3 | ICAO Approach Slope (Non-precision) | $s = 1/40 = 0.025$ | ratio | High |
| 4 | ICAO Approach Slope (Precision Cat I) | $s = 1/50 = 0.02$ | ratio | High |
| 5 | FAA Slope (First Segment) | $s = 1/50 = 0.02$ | ratio | High |
| 6 | FAA Slope (Second Segment) | $s = 1/40 = 0.025$ | ratio | High |
| 7 | ICAO Divergence (each side) | $d = 0.15$ (15%) | ratio | Medium |
| 8 | FAA Divergence (each side) | $d = 0.10$ (10%) | ratio | Medium |
| 9 | Vertical height over distance $x$ (ICAO) | $H(x) = \dfrac{x}{40}$ | metres | High |
| 10 | Height in first 10,000 ft (FAA) | $H(x) = \dfrac{x}{50}$ | feet | High |
| 11 | Height in next 40,000 ft (FAA) | $H(x) = 200 + \dfrac{(x-10000)}{40}$ | feet | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variable Definitions:**
> - $W_i$ = Inner edge width
> - $W_o$ = Outer edge width
> - $L$ = Total length of approach surface
> - $d$ = Divergence ratio per unit length
> - $s$ = Slope (rise per unit run)
> - $H_i$ = Initial height at threshold (usually 0)
> - $x$ = Horizontal distance from threshold

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Approach Zone is foundational to:
- **Master Plan Zoning** of airports (height zoning ordinances).
- **Land Acquisition** in advance of airport expansion.
- **Construction Permitting** in cities neighbouring airports.
- **Aircraft Accident Investigation** (penetration is often a contributing factor).
- **Instrument Approach Procedure (IAP) Design** — works hand-in-hand with ILS, PAPI, and VASIS systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Outer Edge Width from Inner Edge and Divergence

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most commonly asked derivation in KTU university exams (Module 1).

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the outer edge width of an approach surface given the inner edge width, length, and lateral divergence.

**Solution:**

Consider the approach surface as a trapezoid in plan view, with the inner edge perpendicular to the runway centerline at the threshold. Let:
- $W_i$ = width of the inner edge
- $L$ = total horizontal length of the approach surface
- $d$ = divergence ratio (per side)

The side edges of the surface diverge outward symmetrically about the runway centerline. The horizontal offset of one side edge at the outer end is:

$$
\Delta = d \cdot L
$$

Since there are two side edges (one on each side), the total increase in width is $2 \cdot \Delta$:

$$
\Delta_{total} = 2 \cdot d \cdot L
$$

Therefore, the **outer edge width** is:

$$
W_o = W_i + 2 \cdot d \cdot L
$$

This is the fundamental trapezoidal expansion formula applied throughout OLS calculations.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vertical Height of the Approach Surface at Distance $x$

**Statement:** Determine the height of the approach surface at a horizontal distance $x$ from the threshold.

**Solution:**

The approach surface rises linearly from the threshold (where height = 0) with a constant slope $s$ (rise per unit run). The slope is defined as:

$$
s = \frac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}} = \frac{H(x)}{x}
$$

Rearranging for $H(x)$:

$$
H(x) = s \cdot x
$$

**ICAO Numerical Example (Non-Precision, $s = 1:40$):**

For $x = 1000$ m:

$$
H(1000) = \frac{1}{40} \cdot 1000 = 25 \text{ m}
$$

For $x = 3000$ m (full ICAO length):

$$
H(3000) = \frac{1}{40} \cdot 3000 = 75 \text{ m}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — FAA Two-Segment Approach Height (Critical Past Exam Type)

**Statement:** Derive the height equations for the FAA FAR Part 77 two-segment approach surface.

**Solution:**

The FAA approach surface has two segments with different slopes:

**Segment 1:** From $x = 0$ to $x = 10{,}000$ ft, slope = 1:50.

At the end of segment 1 ($x = 10{,}000$ ft), the height is:

$$
H_1 = \frac{10{,}000}{50} = 200 \text{ ft}
$$

**Segment 2:** From $x = 10{,}000$ ft to $x = 50{,}000$ ft, slope = 1:40.

For a point at distance $x$ within segment 2, the additional height above $H_1$ is:

$$
\Delta H = \frac{x - 10{,}000}{40}
$$

Therefore, the total height at distance $x$ in segment 2 is:

$$
H(x) = 200 + \frac{x - 10{,}000}{40}
$$

**KTU Numerical Verification:**

At $x = 50{,}000$ ft (outer end):

$$
H(50{,}000) = 200 + \frac{50{,}000 - 10{,}000}{40} = 200 + 1000 = 1200 \text{ ft}
$$

This matches FAA documentation — the approach surface reaches **1200 ft** above the runway end elevation at its outer terminus.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ICAO vs FAA vs DGCA India

| Parameter | ICAO Annex 14 | FAA FAR Part 77 | DGCA India (CAR) |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| Inner Edge Width | 150 m (Precision) | 1000 ft = 304.8 m | 150 m (Precision) |
| Length | 3000–15000 m | 50,000 ft (15,240 m) | 3000 m+ |
| First Slope | 1:50 (Precision) | 50:1 (1:50) | 1:50 |
| Second Slope | N/A (single) | 40:1 (1:40) | 1:40 (for some Cat) |
| Divergence | 15% (per side) | 10% (per side) | 15% |
| Outer Edge Width | $\approx 900$ m | 16,000 ft = 4876.8 m | $\approx 900$ m |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plan View of the Approach Surface (Trapezoid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RUNWAY\nEND"] -->|Inner Edge 150 m| B["Threshold"]
    B -->|Length 3000 m| C["Outer Edge 1050 m"]
    subgraph "DIVERGENCE 15% PER SIDE"
        D["Side Edge diverges\noutward at 1.67 m per 10 m"]
    end
    A -.-> D
    D -.-> C
```

### 4.2 3D Profile View — Vertical Section Along Centerline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["RUNWAY\nELEVATION 0 m"] -->|Glideslope 3 deg| B["Aircraft on ILS"]
    A -->|Slope 1 to 50| C["APPROACH SURFACE\nInner Edge"]
    C -->|Slope 1 to 50| D["APPROACH SURFACE\nat 3000 m, 75 m high"]
    B -.->|Margin 30 m| D
    E["PROTECTED\nAIRSPACE"] -.-> D
    F["OBSTACLE\nFORBIDDEN"] -.-x D
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Approach Zone Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT PARAMETERS\nRunway Type / Approach Cat\nThreshold Elevation\nSlope s, Divergence d"] --> B["GEOMETRIC ANCHOR\nPlace inner edge at threshold"]
    B --> C["EXPANSION STEP\nW_o = W_i + 2 d L"]
    C --> D["VERTICAL RISE\nH x equals s times x"]
    D --> E["OUTPUT\n3D Trapezoidal Surface"]
    E --> F["VALIDATION\nCheck obstacles using DEM\nplus surface elevation"]
    F --> G{"Any penetration?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["REMEDIATE\nDemolish / Re-route / Limit ILS"]
    G -->|No| I["APPROVED\nSafe to certify runway"]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Section Diagram — Showing Glideslope vs Approach Surface

```mermaid

    B --> C["Runway End\nHeight 0 m"]
    subgraph "PROTECTION MARGIN"
        D["Approach Surface\nsits 30 m below\naircraft glideslope"]
    end
    A -.-> D
    D -.-> B
    E["FORBIDDEN\nOBSTACLE ZONE"] -.-x D
```

### 4.5 Diagram Annotations Table

| Diagram | Visual Purpose | Key Takeaway |
|---------|---------------|--------------|
| 4.1 | Shows plan-view trapezoid | Divergence widens the corridor |
| 4.2 | Profile along centerline | Approach surface parallels but lies below ILS glideslope |
| 4.3 | Functional pipeline | Engineering workflow from input to certification |
| 4.4 | Glideslope protection margin | Confirms vertical clearance between aircraft and OLS |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Conceptual / Definition)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term "Approach Zone" in airport planning. List any two important geometric parameters that define it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Approach Zone** is an imaginary inclined trapezoidal surface that begins at the runway threshold and extends outward and upward along the direction of the runway centerline. It forms part of the **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** and is designed to protect aircraft during the final approach phase of landing from any object, terrain, or structure penetration.
>
> **Two important geometric parameters** (any two, 1.5 marks each):
> 1. **Inner edge width** (e.g., 150 m for ICAO precision runways).
> 2. **Slope/gradient** (e.g., 1:50 for precision approach, 1:40 for non-precision).
> 3. **Length** of the surface (e.g., 3000 m for ICAO, 15,240 m for FAA).
> 4. **Lateral divergence** (15% for ICAO, 10% for FAA).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between the ICAO and FAA standards for the inner edge width of the approach surface.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | ICAO | FAA |
|---------|------|-----|
| Inner Edge Width | 150 m (Precision Cat I/II/III) | 1000 ft ≈ 304.8 m |
| Basis of Classification | Runway code number and approach type | Runway type (civil/utilitarian/military) |
| Philosophy | Code-based modular system | Single fixed value for civil airports |

The ICAO uses a **code-based modular system** that varies the inner edge width with runway code (1, 2, 3, 4) and approach type (non-instrument, non-precision, precision), while the **FAA** uses a **fixed uniform value** of 1000 ft for all civil airports. The FAA value is approximately double the ICAO precision approach value, reflecting broader navigational tolerance margins.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a neat sketch, explain the geometric components of the ICAO approach surface for a **Precision Approach Category I runway** (Runway Code 4). Clearly state the inner edge width, length, slope, and divergence.

**(b)** [7 marks] An airport authority is planning a new Precision Approach Category I runway (ICAO Code 4). The inner edge of the approach surface is 150 m wide. Calculate the **outer edge width** and the **maximum height** of the approach surface at its far end, given the standard ICAO slope of 1:50 and length of 3000 m.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Geometric Components [7 marks]

The ICAO Precision Approach Category I runway (Code 4) approach surface has the following geometric parameters:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Inner edge width | 150 m (placed 60 m outward from runway end) |
| Length of approach surface | 3000 m |
| Slope | 1:50 (i.e., 2% gradient) |
| Lateral divergence | 15% on each side |
| Outer edge width | 150 + 2(0.15)(3000) = 1050 m |

**Sketch Description (Plan View):**
The approach surface is a trapezoid in plan, with:
- Inner edge 150 m wide at threshold.
- Side edges diverging outward at 15% (1.67 m per 10 m of length).
- Outer edge 1050 m wide at 3000 m from the threshold.

**Vertical Section:**
A longitudinal vertical plane through the centerline shows the surface rising linearly from 0 m at the threshold to **60 m** at 3000 m distance, with a slope of 1:50.

**Engineering Significance [1 mark]:**
The 1:50 slope provides a vertical safety margin of approximately **30 m** above the nominal 3° ILS glideslope, ensuring obstacle protection during the final 3000 m of approach.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Inner edge width, $W_i = 150$ m
- Length, $L = 3000$ m
- Divergence, $d = 0.15$ per side
- Slope, $s = 1/50$

**Step 1 — Outer Edge Width:**

$$
W_o = W_i + 2 \cdot d \cdot L
$$

$$
W_o = 150 + 2 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 3000
$$

$$
W_o = 150 + 900 = 1050 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Height at Outer End:**

$$
H_{max} = s \cdot L = \frac{1}{50} \cdot 3000
$$

$$
H_{max} = 60 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Outer edge width = 1050 m**
- **Maximum height = 60 m above runway end elevation**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students commonly forget that the divergence is applied **on each side** and the formula requires multiplication by **2**. Marks are deducted for writing $W_o = 150 + 0.15 \cdot 3000 = 600$ m (this is wrong — missing the factor of 2).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** [7 marks] Describe the **FAA FAR Part 77** approach surface for civil airports. Use a sketch to illustrate the two-segment construction and clearly label the slopes and transition point.

**(b)** [7 marks] A structure is being planned at a distance of 25,000 ft from the threshold along the centerline of a runway governed by FAA standards. Calculate the **maximum allowable height of the structure** (in feet, relative to runway end elevation).

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — FAA Two-Segment Approach Surface [7 marks]

The FAA approach surface for civil airports is a two-segment trapezoidal inclined surface:

**Segment 1: From $x = 0$ to $x = 10{,}000$ ft**
- Inner edge width: 1000 ft (304.8 m)
- Slope: 50:1 (i.e., 1 unit rise per 50 units run = 2%)
- Divergence: 10% per side
- Width at 10,000 ft: 1000 + 2(0.10)(10,000) = 3000 ft

**Segment 2: From $x = 10{,}000$ ft to $x = 50{,}000$ ft**
- Slope: 40:1 (i.e., 1 unit rise per 40 units run = 2.5%)
- Divergence: 10% per side (continues)
- Width at 50,000 ft: 3000 + 2(0.10)(40,000) = 11,000 ft

**Sketch Description:**
A trapezoid in plan with a hinge point at 10,000 ft where the slope changes. The longitudinal profile shows two line segments meeting at a height of 200 ft above the runway end at the 10,000 ft mark.

**Engineering Rationale [1 mark]:**
The shallower first segment (50:1) protects the most critical final approach phase near the runway. The steeper second segment (40:1) recognises that navigational accuracy decreases with distance from the runway, so a larger vertical margin per unit length is acceptable.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Distance from threshold, $x = 25{,}000$ ft
- This lies in **Segment 2** (since 25,000 > 10,000)

**Step 1 — Identify correct formula:**

Since $x = 25{,}000$ ft is in Segment 2, we use:

$$
H(x) = 200 + \frac{x - 10{,}000}{40}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
H(25{,}000) = 200 + \frac{25{,}000 - 10{,}000}{40}
$$

$$
H(25{,}000) = 200 + \frac{15{,}000}{40}
$$

$$
H(25{,}000) = 200 + 375
$$

$$
H(25{,}000) = 575 \text{ ft}
$$

**Final Answer:**
**Maximum allowable structure height = 575 ft above runway end elevation**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A very common mistake is to apply the **50:1 slope uniformly** across the entire 25,000 ft distance:
> $H = 25{,}000/50 = 500$ ft (this is INCORRECT). Students lose 2–3 marks for not recognising the **two-segment transition** at 10,000 ft. Always check whether $x$ falls in Segment 1 or Segment 2 first.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **Definition:** The Approach Zone is a trapezoidal imaginary surface extending outward and upward from the runway threshold, protecting aircraft during final approach.
- **Components to Memorise:** Inner edge width ($W_i$), Outer edge width ($W_o$), Length ($L$), Slope ($s$), Divergence ($d$).
- **Master Formula:** $W_o = W_i + 2 \cdot d \cdot L$ — appears in nearly every numerical question.
- **ICAO Precision Cat I:** $W_i = 150$ m, $L = 3000$ m, $s = 1:50$, $d = 15\%$.
- **ICAO Non-Precision:** $W_i = 150$ m, $L = 3000$ m, $s = 1:40$, $d = 15\%$.
- **FAA Civil:** $W_i = 1000$ ft, $L = 50{,}000$ ft, two segments (50:1 then 40:1), $d = 10\%$.
- **FAA Two-Segment Heights:** End of Segment 1 (at 10,000 ft) = 200 ft. End of Segment 2 (at 50,000 ft) = 1200 ft.
- **Engineering Reality:** The Approach Surface does **not** coincide with the ILS glideslope; it sits slightly below to provide a safety margin.
- **Penetration Consequences:** Any object protruding into the approach surface is a serious flight safety hazard — historical case studies (e.g., Belapur, Mumbai) demonstrate legal and operational consequences.
- **Always check units consistency:** ICAO uses **metres**, FAA uses **feet** — never mix the two in a single calculation.
- **Don't forget the factor of 2** in the outer width formula (for both side edges).
- **Two-segment slopes:** Always identify which segment your $x$ value falls into before applying the height equation.
- **KTU exam weightage:** Approach Zone is a high-yield topic — expect at least 1 Part A (3 marks) and 1 Part B (14 marks) question per semester.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zoning Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Zoning Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Airport Zoning Laws** are a set of legal regulations, statutory restrictions, and geometric airspace protection standards enforced around an aerodrome to control the height of structures, buildings, towers, vegetation, and terrain penetrations that could pose a hazard to aircraft operations during take-off, landing, and maneuvering in the airspace surrounding the airport.

The legal and geometric framework is built upon the concept of **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** — a series of imaginary three-dimensional surfaces defined in airspace around a runway, above which no object or structure is permitted to project, except for specific aeronautical aids that are deemed essential for air navigation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (ICAO-Based):**
> "Zoning laws specify the imaginary surfaces (approach surface, transitional surface, horizontal surface, conical surface, and outer horizontal surface) around an airport beyond which no building or structure shall project, so as to ensure the safety of aircraft operations and to prevent obstacles in the airspace."

The two globally accepted reference standards governing zoning laws are:
1. **ICAO Annex 14** (International Civil Aviation Organization, Aerodromes) — global standard.
2. **FAA FAR Part 77** (Federal Aviation Regulations, United States) — national standard.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **invisible dome (or an inverted stepped pyramid)** sitting on top of the airport runway. Any object tall enough to poke through this invisible dome is considered a hazard — just like a balloon cannot pass through the ceiling of a room. The airport zoning law defines the **shape of this invisible dome**.

> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of zoning laws like the **"no-parking zones" painted on roads near a T-junction**. Drivers (aircraft) need clear sight lines and turning room. You cannot build a tall building or grow tall trees near the corner (the runway end) because they would block the view (approach path). The zoning law is the imaginary "paint" defining what height of structure is allowed at each distance from the runway.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuitive Insight for Students:**
> The further you move **away from the runway**, the **taller the allowable structure becomes** — because aircraft are higher in the air at greater distances from the runway threshold. This is why the imaginary surfaces flare outward in a conical/wedge shape.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics, and Governing Authorities

| Parameter | Value/Standard | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Atmosphere | **ISA (International Standard Atmosphere)** | Used for elevation references |
| Sea Level Temperature | **15°C** | ISA reference |
| Density of Air | **1.225 kg/m³** | At sea level, ISA |
| G-A Constant | **9.81 m/s²** | For gradient calculations |
| Horizontal Surface Height (ICAO) | **45 m above airport elevation** | Inner Horizontal Surface |
| Outer Horizontal Surface Height (ICAO) | **150 m above airport elevation** | Large airports |
| Conical Surface Slope (ICAO) | **1:20 (5%)** | Outward and upward |
| Transitional Surface Slope (ICAO) | **1:7 (~14.3%)** | Lateral |
| Approach Surface Slope (ICAO Non-precision) | **1:50 (2%)** | First 3000 m |
| Approach Surface Slope (ICAO Precision Cat I) | **1:50 (2%)** | First 3000 m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All slopes in zoning laws are expressed as **Ratio (Horizontal : Vertical)**. A slope of **1:50** means for every **1 unit of vertical rise**, the surface moves **50 units horizontally outward**.

---

## 1.4 Objectives of Airport Zoning Laws

The principal engineering objectives are:

1. **Airspace Protection** — Prevent penetration of critical approach and departure paths.
2. **Land Use Control** — Regulate urban development in the airport vicinity.
3. **Noise Abatement Support** — Indirectly guide compatible land use (residential vs. industrial).
4. **Safety Assurance** — Protect aircraft during emergency maneuver and missed approaches.
5. **Bird Strike Mitigation** — Restrict land uses (e.g., garbage dumps) that attract birds.
6. **Future Expansion** — Reserve airspace for future runway extension / new runways.
7. **Visual Approach Protection** — Preserve unobstructed sight for pilots during VFR approach.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five Fundamental Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS) as per ICAO Annex 14

ICAO Annex 14 defines **five principal imaginary surfaces** that constitute the airspace zoning envelope around a runway:

### 2.1.1 Approach Surface (Most Critical)

**Definition:** An inclined plane or combination of planes preceding the threshold of a runway, sloping upward and outward from the runway end.

**Function:** Protects aircraft during the **final approach phase** of landing.

**Geometry:**
- Starts at the runway threshold.
- Inner edge: equal to the width of the runway strip.
- Extends outward symmetrically about the extended runway centerline.
- Diverges uniformly at a specified slope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the approach surface the most critical?**
> During final approach, the aircraft is at its **lowest altitude, lowest speed, and highest descent rate**. It has minimal maneuver energy to evade obstacles. Hence, the approach surface is the most stringently protected.

### 2.1.2 Transitional Surface

**Definition:** A complex surface along the side of the runway strip and part of the approach surface, sloping upward and outward to the inner horizontal surface.

**Function:** Protects aircraft during **side-step maneuvers** and **lateral deviation** during approach.

**Geometry:**
- Slope: **1:7** (approximately 14.3% grade, ~8.13°).
- Extends from the edges of the runway strip and approach surface.
- Joins the inner horizontal surface at the lateral limits.

### 2.1.3 Inner Horizontal Surface (IHS)

**Definition:** A horizontal plane located at a specified height **above the airport elevation**, contained within a circular boundary.

**Function:** Protects aircraft during **circling approaches** and missed approach maneuvering around the airport.

**Geometry:**
- Height: **45 m above the airport reference point (ARP)**.
- Radius: **4000 m** (measured from runway centerline reference points).

### 2.1.4 Conical Surface

**Definition:** A surface sloping upward and outward from the periphery of the inner horizontal surface.

**Function:** Provides a smooth transition from the inner horizontal surface to the outer horizontal surface.

**Geometry:**
- Slope: **1:20 (5%)** outward and upward.
- Extends vertically up to **100 m above the inner horizontal surface** (i.e., up to 145 m above ARP for small airports, or 195 m above ARP for large airports with outer horizontal surface).

### 2.1.5 Outer Horizontal Surface (OHS)

**Definition:** A horizontal plane located at a specified height above the inner horizontal surface, extending to a large radius.

**Function:** Protects aircraft during **high-altitude maneuvering** and is essential for **large airports with precision approaches**.

**Geometry:**
- Height: **150 m above the airport elevation** (so 150 − 45 = 105 m above the IHS).
- Radius: **15000 m** from the airport reference point.

---

## 2.2 FAA FAR Part 77 Imaginary Surfaces (Comparison)

The FAA uses three additional surfaces not explicitly named in ICAO Annex 14 but functionally equivalent:

1. **Primary Surface** — centered longitudinally on the runway, with the same elevation as the nearest point on the runway centerline.
2. **Approach Surface** — similar to ICAO but with different slope categories.
3. **Transitional Surface** — similar to ICAO.

The FAA classifies runways into:
- **Utility Runway** (small aircraft, approach speed < 121 knots).
- **Larger-than-Utility Runway** (faster, heavier aircraft).
- **Precision Instrument Runway**.

---

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### Table 2.1: ICAO Annex 14 — Approach Surface Dimensions

| Runway Classification | Inner Edge Width (m) | Length First Section (m) | Slope First Section | Total Length (m) | Outer Width (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Non-instrument approach | 60 | 3000 | 1:50 | 3000 | 350 |
| Non-precision approach | 60 | 3000 | 1:50 | 3000 | 380 |
| Precision approach (Cat I) | 150 | 3000 | 1:50 | 15000 | 1200 |
| Precision approach (Cat II/III) | 150 | 3000 | 1:50 | 15000 | 1200 |

### Table 2.2: ICAO Annex 14 — Other OLS Dimensions

| Surface | Key Dimension | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Transitional Surface | Slope | 1:7 |
| Inner Horizontal Surface | Height above ARP | 45 m |
| Inner Horizontal Surface | Radius | 4000 m |
| Conical Surface | Slope | 1:20 |
| Conical Surface | Total Height | 100 m above IHS |
| Outer Horizontal Surface | Height above ARP | 150 m |
| Outer Horizontal Surface | Radius | 15000 m |

### Table 2.3: FAA FAR Part 77 — Primary and Approach Surface Dimensions

| Runway Class | Primary Width (ft) | Approach Inner Width (ft) | Approach Length (ft) | Approach Outer Width (ft) | Approach Slope |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Utility (Non-precision) | 250 | 250 | 5000 | 1250 | 20:1 |
| Larger-than-Utility (Non-precision) | 500 | 500 | 10000 | 3500 | 34:1 |
| Precision Instrument | 1000 | 1000 | 50000 | 16000 | 50:1 |

### Table 2.4: Universal Zoning Slope Formulas

$$
\text{Slope Ratio} = \frac{\text{Horizontal Distance}}{\text{Vertical Rise}}
$$

$$
\text{Vertical Rise (h)} = \frac{\text{Horizontal Distance (d)}}{\text{Slope Ratio}}
$$

$$
\text{Horizontal Distance (d)} = \text{Vertical Rise (h)} \times \text{Slope Ratio}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for Exam:**
> To remember ICAO approach slope of **1:50**, recall: *"50 is the new 34 (FAA)* → *ICAO is more conservative for precision Cat I, but FAA Part 77 is used widely in U.S. airports with the gentler 50:1 for 50000 ft."*

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Zoning laws are used by:

- **Airport Authorities** (AAI in India, FAA in USA, EASA in Europe) — to issue No-Objection Certificates (NOC) for structures.
- **Urban Local Bodies / Municipalities** — to control building heights in airport vicinity.
- **Civil Aviation Ministries** — to draft Airport Zoning Acts (e.g., Indian Aircraft Act 1934, Airport Authority of India Act 1994).
- **Developers & Builders** — to verify structural compliance before construction.

In **India**, the **Airport Authority of India (AAI)** issues **NOC for height clearance** for any structure within the *20 km* radius of an airport, applying ICAO Annex 14 standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Height of Structure Allowed at a Given Distance from Runway Threshold (ICAO Approach Surface)

### Given:
- A structure is located at a horizontal distance **D** from the runway threshold, along the extended centerline.
- The runway is a **precision approach Cat I** runway.
- We need to find the **maximum permissible height** of the structure at distance **D**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Identify the slope of the approach surface.**
For ICAO precision approach Cat I, the slope of the first section (0 to 3000 m) is **1:50**.
That is, for every 50 m horizontal, the surface rises 1 m vertically.

**Step 2 — Establish the reference datum.**
The approach surface starts at the **runway threshold elevation** (effectively zero rise at D = 0 m).

**Step 3 — Formulate the rise equation.**
At a horizontal distance **D** from the threshold (assuming D ≤ 3000 m, the first section):

$$
h(D) = \frac{D}{\text{Slope Ratio}} = \frac{D}{50}
$$

**Step 4 — For D > 3000 m (in the second section for precision Cat I).**
The second section has a slope of 1:40 (continued extension to total 15000 m), but typically Cat I uses a uniform 1:50 in the first 3000 m, then continues to outer width at 1200 m. The exact continuation slope is **1:40**.

Therefore, for the second section (3000 m < D ≤ 15000 m):

$$
h(D) = \frac{3000}{50} + \frac{D - 3000}{40} = 60 + \frac{D - 3000}{40}
$$

**Step 5 — Generalize with a piecewise function.**

$$
h(D) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{D}{50} & 0 \le D \le 3000 \text{ m} \\[8pt] 60 + \dfrac{D - 3000}{40} & 3000 < D \le 15000 \text{ m} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical verification.**
Suppose a structure is **5000 m** from the threshold.

$$
h(5000) = 60 + \frac{5000 - 3000}{40} = 60 + \frac{2000}{40} = 60 + 50 = 110 \text{ m}
$$

So at 5000 m from the threshold, no structure (other than approved aids) can exceed **110 m above threshold elevation**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Height Allowed in the Conical Surface Zone

### Given:
- A point is at a horizontal radial distance **R** from the airport reference point (ARP), where **4000 m ≤ R ≤ (4000 + 2000) m** = 6000 m (i.e., the conical zone).
- The conical surface starts at the **outer edge of the inner horizontal surface** (radius 4000 m) and extends upward.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Identify the conical surface slope.**
ICAO conical surface slope = **1:20**.

**Step 2 — Reference height at the start of the conical surface.**
At R = 4000 m, the surface is at the **inner horizontal surface elevation** = 45 m above ARP.

**Step 3 — Derive the height equation.**

$$
h(R) = 45 + \frac{R - 4000}{20} \quad \text{for } 4000 \text{ m} \le R \le 6000 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify maximum height at R = 6000 m.**

$$
h(6000) = 45 + \frac{6000 - 4000}{20} = 45 + 100 = 145 \text{ m above ARP}
$$

**Step 5 — Beyond R = 6000 m.**
The surface transitions to the **outer horizontal surface** at 150 m above ARP (but for most regulatory purposes, the conical zone ends here and a flat surface at 145 m extends out to 15000 m radius, with the OHS applying beyond).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: FAA Part 77 — Maximum Height Under Approach Surface

### Given:
- Runway is **larger-than-utility, non-precision**.
- Structure is at horizontal distance **D** from the runway end.
- Approach slope = **34:1**.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Slope interpretation.**
For every 34 ft horizontal, surface rises 1 ft.

**Step 2 — Height equation (within 10000 ft length).**

$$
h(D) = \frac{D}{34} \quad \text{for } 0 \le D \le 10000 \text{ ft}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example.**
A building is at **4000 ft** from the runway end.

$$
h(4000) = \frac{4000}{34} = 117.65 \text{ ft} \approx 35.86 \text{ m}
$$

So the maximum permitted building height is approximately **117.65 ft above the runway end elevation**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ICAO vs FAA

| Aspect | ICAO Annex 14 | FAA FAR Part 77 |
|---|---|---|
| Jurisdiction | Global (169 signatory states) | United States |
| Approach Slope (Precision) | 1:50 | 50:1 |
| Approach Slope (Non-Precision) | 1:50 | 34:1 |
| Approach Slope (Utility) | 1:50 | 20:1 |
| Horizontal Surface Radius | 4000 m | 5000–10000 ft |
| Horizontal Surface Height | 45 m | 150 ft |
| Conical Slope | 1:20 | 7:1 (from horizontal) |
| Outer Horizontal | 150 m, 15000 m | Not explicitly defined |
| Transitional Slope | 1:7 | 7:1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion sanity check:**
> ICAO 1:50 ≈ FAA 50:1 (same ratio).
> ICAO 1:7 ≈ FAA 7:1 (same ratio).
> The two systems are **dimensionally equivalent** when expressed in consistent units.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Style)

### Problem:
An airport has a precision approach Cat I runway. A chimney of height 80 m is proposed at a distance of 2500 m from the runway threshold along the extended centerline. The runway threshold elevation is at 100 m AMSL. Determine:
(a) Whether the chimney violates the approach surface.
(b) If so, by how much.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute the approach surface height at D = 2500 m.**

$$
h(2500) = \frac{2500}{50} = 50 \text{ m above threshold elevation}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute elevation of the approach surface.**

$$
\text{Absolute Height} = 100 + 50 = 150 \text{ m AMSL}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the absolute elevation of the chimney top.**

$$
\text{Chimney Top Elevation} = 100 + 80 = 180 \text{ m AMSL}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare.**

$$
180 \text{ m AMSL} > 150 \text{ m AMSL}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the violation.**

$$
\Delta h = 180 - 150 = 30 \text{ m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The chimney violates the approach surface by 30 m.** The proposal must be rejected, OR the chimney height must be reduced to ≤ 50 m above threshold elevation (≤ 150 m AMSL).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS
