- **End of Year $n$:** $P_n = P_0(1 + r)^n$

> [!NOTE]
> This recursive logic is the foundation of the **geometric progression**. Each year the *base* itself grows, so compounding is multiplicative, not additive.

To solve for $r$ given $P_0$, $P_n$, and $n$:

$$
P_n = P_0 (1 + r)^n
$$

$$
\frac{P_n}{P_0} = (1 + r)^n
$$

$$
\left(\frac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} = 1 + r
$$

$$
r = \left(\frac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1
$$

This is the **CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate)** formula.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — CAGR-Based Forecast (KTU Typical 7-Mark Problem)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Type Problem]**
> An airport handled **5.0 million passengers** in the year **2020**. The expected average annual growth rate is **8% per annum**. Estimate the passenger volume in the year **2030** (i.e., $n = 10$ years). Also calculate the traffic in **2035** ($n = 15$ years).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**
- $P_0 = 5.0$ million passengers (year 2020)
- $r = 8\% = 0.08$ per annum
- $n_1 = 10$ years (for 2030)
- $n_2 = 15$ years (for 2035)

**Step 2 — Apply the compound growth equation for 2030:**

$$
P_{10} = P_0 (1 + r)^n
$$

$$
P_{10} = 5.0 \times (1 + 0.08)^{10}
$$

$$
P_{10} = 5.0 \times (1.08)^{10}
$$

$$
P_{10} = 5.0 \times 2.1589
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{10} = 10.79 \text{ million passengers in 2030}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the same equation for 2035:**

$$
P_{15} = 5.0 \times (1.08)^{15}
$$

$$
P_{15} = 5.0 \times 3.1722
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{15} = 15.86 \text{ million passengers in 2035}}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification using ratio check:**

The ratio of $P_{15}/P_{10}$ should equal $(1.08)^5$:

$$
\frac{P_{15}}{P_{10}} = \frac{15.86}{10.79} = 1.4697
$$

$$
(1.08)^5 = 1.4693
$$

The tiny rounding difference confirms the calculation is **self-consistent**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Back-Calculating Growth Rate (CAGR Inverse)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Type Problem]**
> An airport's traffic was **2.4 MPPA in 2010** and grew to **6.0 MPPA in 2024**. Determine:
> (a) The Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR).
> (b) The expected traffic in **2030** using the same rate.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — CAGR Calculation:**

Given:
- $P_0 = 2.4$ MPPA (2010)
- $P_n = 6.0$ MPPA (2024)
- $n = 14$ years

$$
r = \left(\frac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1
$$

$$
r = \left(\frac{6.0}{2.4}\right)^{1/14} - 1
$$

$$
r = (2.5)^{1/14} - 1
$$

$$
r = (2.5)^{0.07143} - 1
$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(2.5) = 0.9163$, so $\frac{0.9163}{14} = 0.06545$, then $e^{0.06545} = 1.0676$.

$$
\boxed{r = 0.0676 \text{ or } 6.76\% \text{ per annum}}
$$

**Part (b) — Forecast for 2030:**

The horizon from 2024 to 2030 is **6 years**, so $n = 6$:

$$
P_{2030} = P_{2024} \times (1 + r)^6
$$

$$
P_{2030} = 6.0 \times (1.0676)^6
$$

$$
P_{2030} = 6.0 \times 1.4845
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{2030} = 8.91 \text{ MPPA}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Linear Regression Forecast (Most KTU-Relevant Numerical)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Type Problem]**
> The passenger data for an airport over **7 years** is given below. Using **Linear Regression** of the form $Y = a + bX$ where $X$ is the year code and $Y$ is passengers in lakhs, **forecast the traffic for the 9th year**.

| Year | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Passengers (lakhs) | 20 | 24 | 27 | 31 | 35 | 38 | 42 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build the calculation table (this is the standard KTU full-marks presentation style):**

| $X$ | $Y$ | $X^2$ | $XY$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 1 | 20 |
| 2 | 24 | 4 | 48 |
| 3 | 27 | 9 | 81 |
| 4 | 31 | 16 | 124 |
| 5 | 35 | 25 | 175 |
| 6 | 38 | 36 | 228 |
| 7 | 42 | 49 | 294 |
| **Σ = 28** | **Σ = 217** | **Σ = 140** | **Σ = 970** |

So $n = 7$, $\sum X = 28$, $\sum Y = 217$, $\sum X^2 = 140$, $\sum XY = 970$.

**Step 2 — Compute means:**

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{\sum X}{n} = \frac{28}{7} = 4
$$

$$
\bar{Y} = \frac{\sum Y}{n} = \frac{217}{7} = 31
$$

**Step 3 — Compute slope $b$:**

$$
b = \frac{\sum XY - n \bar{X} \bar{Y}}{\sum X^2 - n \bar{X}^2}
$$

$$
b = \frac{970 - 7 \times 4 \times 31}{140 - 7 \times 4^2}
$$

$$
b = \frac{970 - 868}{140 - 112}
$$

$$
b = \frac{102}{28}
$$

$$
\boxed{b = 3.643 \text{ lakhs per year}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute intercept $a$:**

$$
a = \bar{Y} - b \bar{X}
$$

$$
a = 31 - 3.643 \times 4
$$

$$
a = 31 - 14.571
$$

$$
\boxed{a = 16.429 \text{ lakhs}}
$$

**Step 5 — Write the regression equation:**

$$
Y = 16.429 + 3.643 X
$$

**Step 6 — Forecast for the 9th year ($X = 9$):**

$$
Y_9 = 16.429 + 3.643 \times 9
$$

$$
Y_9 = 16.429 + 32.786
$$

$$
\boxed{Y_9 = 49.215 \text{ lakhs} \approx 4.92 \text{ million passengers}}
$$

**Step 7 — (Optional but high-value for full marks) — Compute $R^2$:**

Predicted values $\hat{Y}_i$ for each $X$:

| $X$ | $Y_{\text{obs}}$ | $\hat{Y} = 16.43 + 3.64X$ | Residual $(Y - \hat{Y})^2$ | $(Y - \bar{Y})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 20.07 | 0.005 | 121 |
| 2 | 24 | 23.71 | 0.084 | 49 |
| 3 | 27 | 27.36 | 0.130 | 16 |
| 4 | 31 | 31.00 | 0.000 | 0 |
| 5 | 35 | 34.64 | 0.130 | 16 |
| 6 | 38 | 38.29 | 0.084 | 49 |
| 7 | 42 | 41.93 | 0.005 | 121 |
| | | **Sum** | **≈ 0.438** | **372** |

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{0.438}{372} = 0.9988
$$

$$
\boxed{R^2 = 0.9988}
$$

This $R^2 \approx 0.999$ indicates a **near-perfect linear fit**, validating the use of the linear model.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Peak Hour Estimation

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Type Problem]**
> An airport is forecasted to handle **9.0 MPPA** in 2030. Using the ICAO peak hour factor of **$\frac{1}{8000}$**, determine the **design hour passenger flow** and the **required terminal area** assuming **15 m² per peak hour passenger** (IATA Level of Service "C" optimum).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert MPPA to annual passengers:**

$$
P_{\text{annual}} = 9.0 \times 10^6 = 9{,}000{,}000 \text{ passengers}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply peak hour factor:**

$$
P_{\text{peak}} = P_{\text{annual}} \times \text{PHF}
$$

$$
P_{\text{peak}} = 9{,}000{,}000 \times \frac{1}{8000}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{peak}} = 1125 \text{ passengers per hour}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute required terminal floor area:**

$$
A_{\text{terminal}} = P_{\text{peak}} \times \text{Area per pax}
$$

$$
A_{\text{terminal}} = 1125 \times 15
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{terminal}} = 16{,}875 \text{ m}^2 \approx 1.69 \text{ hectares}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Aircraft Movements from Passenger Forecast

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Type Problem]**
> The annual passenger throughput of an airport is **6.0 MPPA**. The average aircraft has **180 seats** with an expected **load factor of 80%**. Calculate the **annual aircraft movements** and the **peak hour movements** assuming the **peak hour is 1/4000** of annual movements.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective passengers per aircraft:**

$$
P_{\text{per ac}} = \text{Seats} \times \text{Load Factor} = 180 \times 0.80 = 144 \text{ pax/movement}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual aircraft movements:**

$$
M_{\text{annual}} = \frac{P_{\text{annual}}}{P_{\text{per ac}}} = \frac{6{,}000{,}000}{144}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{\text{annual}} = 41{,}667 \text{ movements/year}}
$$

**Step 3 — Average daily movements:**

$$
M_{\text{daily}} = \frac{41{,}667}{365} \approx 114 \text{ movements/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Peak hour movements:**

$$
M_{\text{peak}} = \frac{41{,}667}{4000} \approx 10.4 \text{ movements/hour}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{\text{peak}} \approx 11 \text{ movements per hour}}
$$

This is the value used to **size the runway system** and the **apron gates**.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Forecasting Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix is the KTU examiner's favourite "compare-and-discuss" question. Memorize it.**

| Method | Data Required | Forecast Horizon | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Trend** | Historical traffic only | Short (5 yr) | Simple | Fails for high growth |
| **Exponential Trend** | Historical traffic only | Short–Medium | Captures % growth | Diverges for very long term |
| **Moving Average** | 3–5 yr historical | Very short | Smooths noise | Lags real trend |
| **Linear Regression** | Traffic + 1 driver | Medium (10 yr) | Statistically robust | Assumes linear relation |
| **Multiple Regression** | Traffic + multi drivers | Medium–Long | Most realistic | Needs large dataset |
| **Market Share** | Airport data + national forecast | Any | Anchored to authority forecast | Inherits parent model's errors |
| **Gravity Model** | City populations + distances | Long (20+ yr) | Captures network effect | Hard to calibrate |
| **Stated Preference Survey** | Survey of 1000+ travellers | Very long | Forward-looking | Expensive; hypothetical bias |
| **Econometric (ARIMA)** | Long time series | Medium | Sophisticated | Needs statistical software |

---

## 3.8 The KTU "Three-Scenario" Forecast Standard

ICAO Doc 8991 and KTU master planning both require **three scenarios** to be presented — Low, Medium, High. This is because **no single forecast is reliable**.

$$
P_{\text{low}} = P_0 (1 + r_{\text{low}})^n
$$

$$
P_{\text{base}} = P_0 (1 + r_{\text{base}})^n
$$

$$
P_{\text{high}} = P_0 (1 + r_{\text{high}})^n
$$

**Typical spread for KTU problems:**
- $r_{\text{low}} = 4\%$, $r_{\text{base}} = 7\%$, $r_{\text{high}} = 10\%$

The **terminal building** is typically designed for the *base* case. The **land-use master plan** must accommodate the *high* case. The **financial feasibility** is tested against the *low* case.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Air Traffic Forecasting Workflow (Block-Level Architecture)

The diagram below maps the **complete decision flow** from raw data to design-year output. This is the mental model KTU expects you to internalize.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Data Collection Layer] --> B[Historical Traffic Data 10 to 20 years]
    A --> C[Socio Economic Indicators]
    A --> D[Demographic Indicators]
    A --> E[Policy and Infrastructure Indicators]

    B --> F[Method Selection Module]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F --> G[Time Series Methods]
    F --> H[Regression Methods]
    F --> I[Market Share Method]
    F --> J[Gravity Model]
    F --> K[Survey Methods]

    G --> L[Forecast Engine]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M[Passenger Forecast 5 10 15 20 yr]
    L --> N[Aircraft Movement Forecast]
    L --> O[Cargo Tonnage Forecast]
    L --> P[Peak Hour Forecast]

    M --> Q[Design Year Master Output]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q

    Q --> R[Runway Length Sizing]
    Q --> S[Terminal Area Sizing]
    Q --> T[Apron Gate Count]
    Q --> U[Pavement Design Load]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Data Collection Layer** is the foundation. Without good data, *no* method works.
- The **Method Selection Module** is the planner's judgment call — there is no single "right" method.
- The **Forecast Engine** consolidates and produces the four canonical outputs.
- The **Design Year Master Output** feeds every downstream module in PECET744.

---

## 4.2 The Forecasting Method Selection Decision Tree

This diagram guides the student to **pick the right method** based on data availability and horizon — a common exam sub-question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Forecast Horizon and Data] --> B{Is historical traffic data available for 10 plus years?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is the relationship with GDP or population strong?}
    B -- No --> D{Is a stated preference survey feasible?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Use Regression Method Multiple Variable]
    C -- No --> F{Is the growth pattern smooth or volatile?}
    F -- Smooth --> G[Use Exponential Trend or Linear Trend]
    F -- Volatile --> H[Use Moving Average or Exponential Smoothing]
    D -- Yes --> I[Use Stated Preference Survey]
    D -- No --> J[Use Market Share Method with national aggregate]
    E --> K[Generate Low Base High Scenarios]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Final Forecast Output]
```

---

## 4.3 The Design Hour Concept (Visual Block)

The 30th Busy Hour concept is best understood as a ranked ordering of all hours of the year. The following block schematic explains it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[All 8760 hours of the year] --> B[Rank hours from busiest to least busy]
    B --> C[Hour 1: Absolute Peak extreme event]
    B --> D[Hour 2 to 29: Above 30th BH]
    B --> E[Hour 30: The 30th Busy Hour DESIGN HOUR]
    B --> F[Hour 31 to 8760: Below design level]
    E --> G[Sizing decision for Terminal Runway Apron]
```

**Why the 30th BH and not the absolute peak?**
Designing for the *absolute* peak would mean sizing for rare events (e.g., a national holiday, a sports final). The 30th BH ensures the facility handles typical busy days comfortably while remaining **economically viable**.

---

## 4.4 The Forecasting Method Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Traffic Forecasting Methods] --> B[Quantitative Methods]
    A --> C[Qualitative Methods]

    B --> D[Univariate Time Series]
    B --> E[Multivariate Causal]
    B --> F[Spatial Interaction]

    D --> D1[Linear Trend]
    D --> D2[Exponential Trend]
    D --> D3[Moving Average]
    D --> D4[ARIMA]

    E --> E1[Simple Regression]
    E --> E2[Multiple Regression]
    E --> E3[Econometric Models]

    F --> F1[Gravity Model]
    F --> F2[Intervening Opportunity Model]

    C --> G1[Delphi Method Expert Panels]
    C --> G2[Market Surveys]
    C --> G3[Analogy with Comparable Airports]
```

This hierarchy is what the **KTU syllabus** calls the "classification of forecasting methods." A direct 3-mark question can be: *"Classify the various methods of air traffic forecasting."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
> **Define air traffic forecasting. List any four factors that affect the future air traffic demand of an airport.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Air traffic forecasting is the systematic estimation of future passenger volume, aircraft movements, and cargo tonnage at an airport over a defined planning horizon, using historical data, socio-economic indicators, and analytical/statistical models.

**Four factors affecting future air traffic demand:**

1. **Economic factors** — GDP growth, per capita income, foreign investment, industrial output.
2. **Demographic factors** — population of the catchment area, urbanization rate, middle-class size.
3. **Policy and infrastructure factors** — Open Sky agreements, new airport/runway openings, low-cost carrier entry, fuel prices.
4. **External disruptive factors** — pandemics, geopolitical events, climate change regulations, high-speed rail competition.

*(Any 4 factors with a one-line description each — full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question 2** (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **What is the 30th Busy Hour concept? Why is it used for airport facility design instead of the absolute peak hour?**

**Model Answer:**

The **30th Busy Hour (30th BH)** is defined as the hour of the year that is exceeded in traffic volume only **29 times per year** — i.e., the 30th-ranked hour when all 8760 hours of the year are sorted by passenger volume in descending order.

It is preferred over the absolute peak hour for design purposes because:

1. **The absolute peak is an extreme outlier** — typically driven by a one-off event (national holiday, sports final, weather diversion). Designing for it leads to **uneconomical over-capacity** for 8759 hours of the year.
2. **The 30th BH represents a realistic busy period** — exceeded roughly once every 12 days, ensuring the facility handles typical peak demand comfortably.
3. **It balances service quality and economic efficiency** — a widely accepted ICAO/FAA planning standard.
4. For **hub airports**, the 20th BH is sometimes used; for **non-hub airports**, the 40th BH is used.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme:** Each module contains either **Choice A** OR **Choice B** (internal choice). Both options are provided below with full model solutions.

---

### **🔹 CHOICE A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question aligned to Dec 2024 Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain the various **methods of air traffic forecasting** in detail, with emphasis on the **regression method**. Discuss the **limitations** of each method. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An airport recorded the following passenger traffic over 6 years. Using the **Linear Regression method**, determine the regression equation and forecast the traffic for the **8th year**. **(7 Marks)**

| Year | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Passengers (lakhs) | 15 | 18 | 22 | 25 | 29 | 33 |

---

#### **Solution to Part A(a) — 7 Marks**

The various methods of air traffic forecasting are classified as follows:

**1. Time Series / Trend Methods** — Use only historical traffic data.
   - *Linear Trend:* $Y = a + bX$. Suitable for steady, near-linear growth.
   - *Exponential Trend:* $Y = a \cdot b^X$. Used when growth is in constant percentage.
   - *Polynomial Trend:* Captures accelerating growth. *Limitation:* Can produce absurd values for long horizons.
   - *Moving Average:* Smooths fluctuations. *Limitation:* Lags the actual trend.
   - *Exponential Smoothing:* $F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$. *Limitation:* Sensitive to smoothing constant.

**2. Regression / Econometric Methods** — Establish cause-effect between traffic and explanatory variables (GDP, population, income).
   - *Simple Linear Regression:* $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$. Predicts traffic from one driver.
   - *Multiple Regression:* $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + ...$. More realistic. *Limitation:* Requires high-quality data and assumes linear relationships.

**3. Market Share Method** — Assumes the airport retains its historical share of the national/regional market. *Limitation:* Inherits errors from the parent forecast; ignores competitive shifts.

**4. Gravity / Spatial Interaction Model** — $T_{ij} = k \cdot P_i^{\alpha} P_j^{\alpha} / D_{ij}^{\beta}$. Best for inter-city traffic. *Limitation:* Hard to calibrate exponents; ignores qualitative factors.

**5. Survey-Based Methods** — Revealed Preference and Stated Preference surveys. *Limitation:* Expensive; sample bias; hypothetical bias in SP surveys.

**6. Qualitative / Judgmental Methods** — Delphi method, expert panels. *Limitation:* Subjective; useful only as a cross-check.

**Comparative Summary:** No single method is universally best. ICAO recommends a **triangulation** — use 2–3 methods and present Low, Base, High scenarios. The **regression method** is most popular in KTU/ICAO practice because it explicitly captures the link between air travel and economic activity.

---

#### **Solution to Part A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the data:**

| $X$ | $Y$ | $X^2$ | $XY$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 15 | 1 | 15 |
| 2 | 18 | 4 | 36 |
| 3 | 22 | 9 | 66 |
| 4 | 25 | 16 | 100 |
| 5 | 29 | 25 | 145 |
| 6 | 33 | 36 | 198 |
| **Σ = 21** | **Σ = 142** | **Σ = 91** | **Σ = 560** |

So: $n = 6$, $\sum X = 21$, $\sum Y = 142$, $\sum X^2 = 91$, $\sum XY = 560$.

**Step 2 — Means:**

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{21}{6} = 3.5, \quad \bar{Y} = \frac{142}{6} = 23.667
$$

**Step 3 — Slope $b$:**

$$
b = \frac{\sum XY - n\bar{X}\bar{Y}}{\sum X^2 - n\bar{X}^2}
$$

$$
b = \frac{560 - 6 \times 3.5 \times 23.667}{91 - 6 \times 3.5^2}
$$

$$
b = \frac{560 - 497.0}{91 - 73.5} = \frac{63.0}{17.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{b = 3.6 \text{ lakhs/year}}
$$

**Step 4 — Intercept $a$:**

$$
a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X} = 23.667 - 3.6 \times 3.5 = 23.667 - 12.6
$$

$$
\boxed{a = 11.067 \text{ lakhs}}
$$

**Step 5 — Regression equation:**

$$
\boxed{Y = 11.067 + 3.6X}
$$

**Step 6 — Forecast for the 8th year ($X = 8$):**

$$
Y_8 = 11.067 + 3.6 \times 8 = 11.067 + 28.8
$$

$$
\boxed{Y_8 = 39.867 \approx 39.87 \text{ lakhs} \approx 3.99 \text{ MPPA}}
$$

---

### **🔹 CHOICE B — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question aligned to June 2024 Pattern]**
> **(a)** Define **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)**. An airport forecasts **7.2 MPPA** in 2030. Using ICAO standard PHF, determine the **peak hour passengers** and the **required terminal building area** at **12 m² per peak hour passenger**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Gravity Model** of air traffic forecasting. An airport city has a population of **6 million** and the destination city has **9 million**, separated by a distance of **1500 km**. If the calibrated constants are $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$, $\alpha = 1$, and $\beta = 2$, calculate the **forecasted annual trips** between them. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition:** The **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** is the ratio of peak hour passengers to the annual passenger volume. It is used to convert annual demand into the design hour demand.

**ICAO Standard PHF:** $\text{PHF} = \dfrac{1}{8000}$ (typical range: $\dfrac{1}{8000}$ to $\dfrac{1}{7000}$).

**Step 1 — Convert MPPA to annual passengers:**

$$
P_{\text{annual}} = 7.2 \times 10^6 = 7{,}200{,}000 \text{ pax}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute peak hour passengers:**

$$
P_{\text{peak}} = P_{\text{annual}} \times \text{PHF}
$$

$$
P_{\text{peak}} = 7{,}200{,}000 \times \frac{1}{8000} = 900 \text{ pax/hour}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{peak}} = 900 \text{ passengers/hour}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute terminal area:**

$$
A = P_{\text{peak}} \times \text{Area per pax} = 900 \times 12
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 10{,}800 \text{ m}^2}
$$

---

#### **Solution to Part B(b) — 7 Marks**

**The Gravity Model** is a spatial interaction model that assumes trip-making between two cities is:
- **Directly proportional** to the product of their "masses" (population, economic activity), and
- **Inversely proportional** to a function of the distance (or travel cost) between them.

**Equation:**

$$
T_{ij} = k \cdot \frac{P_i^{\alpha} \cdot P_j^{\alpha}}{D_{ij}^{\beta}}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute given data:**

- $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$
- $P_i = 6{,}000{,}000$
- $P_j = 9{,}000{,}000$
- $D_{ij} = 1500$ km
- $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$

**Step 2 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$
P_i \cdot P_j = 6 \times 10^6 \times 9 \times 10^6 = 54 \times 10^{12}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$
D_{ij}^{\beta} = (1500)^2 = 2{,}250{,}000 = 2.25 \times 10^6
$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$
T_{ij} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{54 \times 10^{12}}{2.25 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{ij} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 24 \times 10^6
$$

$$
T_{ij} = 1.5 \times 24
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{ij} = 36{,}000 \text{ trips/year}}
$$

This is the **forecasted annual air trips** between the two cities.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS on this topic — Board-Examiner Patterns:**

1. **Unit Confusion:** Students frequently write $r = 0.08$ when the question says "8% growth." Always convert **% to decimal** before substituting into $(1+r)^n$. Reverse if you are back-calculating.

2. **Confusing $n$ in CAGR:** The $n$ in $P_n = P_0(1+r)^n$ is the **number of years between the base year and the forecast year**, NOT the calendar year itself. For "2015 to 2024" → $n = 9$.

3. **Mixing $\bar{X}$ and $\bar{Y}$:** In the regression formula, students frequently write the slope formula upside down. **Memorize:** $b = \dfrac{\sum XY - n\bar{X}\bar{Y}}{\sum X^2 - n\bar{X}^2}$. The denominator always has $X^2$ (not $Y^2$).

4. **Skipping the calculation table:** Even if you do the math correctly, KTU examiners award **methodology marks** for presenting the $\sum X, \sum Y, \sum X^2, \sum XY$ table. Always present it.

5. **Using only one method:** In 7-mark "discuss" questions, presenting only the regression method (even if computed) loses marks. Always mention **at least 3 methods** and a comparative statement.

6. **Ignoring Peak Hour:** Forecasting 8 MPPA but forgetting to compute peak hour pax for terminal sizing is a **direct 2–3 mark loss** in design-related sub-questions.

7. **Forgetting units in final answer:** "$Y_8 = 39.87$" without "lakhs of passengers" is incomplete. Always write units.

8. **Using PHF = 1/365:** This is a very common error. PHF is **not** the daily share; it is the peak hour share. For 24 hours, the average hour would be $1/8760$, not $1/365$.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Air traffic forecasting** = estimating future passengers, aircraft movements, and cargo for a **design year** (typically 5, 10, 15, 20 years ahead).
- ✅ **Five major method families:** Time Series, Regression, Market Share, Gravity Model, Survey.
- ✅ **Most-tested formula:** $P_n = P_0(1+r)^n$ — compound growth equation.
- ✅ **CAGR inverse:** $r = (P_n/P_0)^{1/n} - 1$ — used to back-calculate growth rate.
- ✅ **Linear regression:** $Y = a + bX$, with $b = \frac{\sum XY - n\bar{X}\bar{Y}}{\sum X^2 - n\bar{X}^2}$ and $a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X}$.
- ✅ **Coefficient of determination:** $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$; values above **0.85** indicate good fit.
- ✅ **Elasticity of demand:** Air traffic typically grows at **~1.8 × GDP growth** globally.
- ✅ **30th Busy Hour** is the standard design hour; **PHF = 1/8000** is the ICAO rule of thumb.
- ✅ **Aircraft movements** = Annual passengers / (Seats × Load Factor).
- ✅ **Terminal area** = Peak hour pax × Area per pax (12–18 m²/pax for IATA LoS C).
- ✅ **Always present 3 scenarios:** Low, Base, High growth.
- ✅ **Always use the calculation table** for regression — it is a marks-bearing presentation.
- ✅ **Always state units** in the final answer — examiners mark strictly on completeness.
- ✅ **Time series methods** are simple but limited to short horizons; **regression methods** are robust and most exam-relevant; **gravity models** are best for inter-city pairs.
- ✅ **Forecasting feeds** runway length, terminal area, apron gates, and pavement design — it is the **master input** to airport planning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of New Airport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Development of New Airport

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Airport Development** is a systematic, multi-stage engineering and management process that transforms an identified aviation demand into a fully operational, safe, and economically viable air transport facility. As per ICAO Annex 14 and the KTU 2024 PECET744 syllabus, the development of a *new airport* (often called a **Greenfield Airport**) involves a structured sequence of planning, design, construction, and operational commissioning phases governed by international standards.

In the context of KTU Module 1, **"Development of a New Airport"** encompasses the entire project lifecycle from the moment a regional or national authority identifies the *need* for a new airport until the airport achieves its **Operational Readiness and Airport Transfer (ORAT)** status.

## 1.2 Stages of Airport Development — The Master Lifecycle

The KTU 2024 scheme defines the development of a new airport as comprising **eight canonical stages**:

1. **Need Identification & Demand Assessment**
2. **Feasibility Study & Techno-Economic Viability (TEV) Report**
3. **Site Selection & Land Acquisition**
4. **Master Planning (Long-term 20–30 year horizon)**
5. **Detailed Engineering Design**
6. **Construction & Procurement (EPC / PPP models)**
7. **Commissioning, Trial Runs & Certification**
8. **Operation, Maintenance & Periodic Expansion**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Greenfield Airport** is built from scratch on undeveloped land, while a **Brownfield Project** refers to expansion/upgrade of an existing airport. The KTU syllabus (Module 1) primarily focuses on **Greenfield Development**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "City-Building" Intuition

Think of developing a new airport exactly like **building a brand-new smart city from an empty patch of land**:

- You first **ask the people** if they even need a city (Demand Study).
- You **check the soil, weather, and water supply** (Site Selection).
- You **draw a city map** showing where roads, houses, and parks will go in 10, 20, and 30 years (Master Plan).
- You **build the roads first, then the houses** (Phase-wise Construction).
- Finally, you **open the gates and let people move in** (Operational Commissioning).

Just as you cannot build a city without a master plan, you cannot build an airport without a **comprehensive Airport Master Plan (AMP)**. Every runway, terminal, and taxiway in the future depends on the decisions made in **Module 1**.

## 1.4 Key Physical Standards & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reference Standards for New Airport Development:**
> - **ICAO Annex 14** — Aerodrome design and operations (Code numbers 1–4, Code letters A–F).
> - **ICAO Annex 19** — Safety Management Systems (SMS).
> - **FAA AC 150/5300-13A** — Airport Design (used in US-aligned projects).
> - **AERA (Airports Economic Regulatory Authority)** guidelines for tariff & capacity (India).
> - **AAI (Airports Authority of India)** — CAR Section 4 for aerodrome licensing.

**Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** typically tracked in Module 1:
- **Annual Passenger Demand** (in Millions of Passengers Per Annum — **MPPA**).
- **Peak Hour Demand (PHD)** — usually 0.03% to 0.05% of annual volume.
- **Aircraft Movements Per Hour (MPH)**.
- **Level of Service (LoS)** — graded A to F per IATA.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive — The Eight Stages Explained

### Stage 1: Need Identification & Demand Assessment
- Triggered by **traffic saturation** at existing airports, **regional connectivity** mandates (e.g., India's UDAN-RCS scheme), or **strategic/political** needs.
- Involves **traffic forecasting** using historical airline data, GDP elasticity, and population growth models.

### Stage 2: Feasibility Study (TEV Report)
A structured report evaluating:
- **Technical** viability (site, runway orientation, terrain).
- **Economic** viability (ROI, NPV, IRR, Payback Period).
- **Financial** viability (funding — public, PPP, foreign direct investment).
- **Environmental & Social** impact (EIA, SIA per India's MoEFCC norms).

### Stage 3: Site Selection
Selection is based on a **multi-criteria decision matrix (MCDM)** using weighted scores. The **ICAO Annex 14** requirements dominate:
- **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** must be clear.
- Adequate **land area** for future 4-stage expansion.
- **Soil bearing capacity** ≥ 100 kN/m² for runway pavements.
- **Approach path** clear of mountains, towers, bird sanctuaries.

### Stage 4: Master Planning
The **Airport Master Plan (AMP)** is the cornerstone document. It is prepared for a **20-year planning horizon** with **5-year phasing**. Components include:
- **Airside**: Runway(s), taxiways, aprons, navigation aids (ILS, VOR, DME).
- **Landside**: Terminal building, access roads, parking, cargo, MRO.
- **Support**: ATC tower, fire station (ARFF Category 6–10), utilities.

### Stage 5: Detailed Engineering Design
- Pavement design (Flexible — CBR method, Rigid — PCA method).
- Geometric design of runway, taxiway, apron per **ICAO Code**.
- Terminal architectural design (passenger flow, baggage handling, HVAC).
- Airfield lighting (PAPI, REIL, threshold lights, taxiway centerline lights).

### Stage 6: Construction & Procurement
Delivered via models such as:
- **EPC** (Engineering, Procurement, Construction) — e.g., Delhi T3.
- **PPP / BOT** (Build-Operate-Transfer) — e.g., Cochin, Hyderabad, Bangalore.
- **Hybrid Annuity Model (HAM)** — used in many Indian greenfield airports.

### Stage 7: Commissioning & Certification
- **Calibration flights** (Flight Inspection by AAI).
- **DGPS survey** validation of runway threshold coordinates.
- Issuance of **Aerodrome Licence** under Indian Aircraft Rules.
- ORAT — Operational Readiness and Airport Transfer.

### Stage 8: Operation & Expansion
- Continuous monitoring via **A-SMGCS** (Advanced Surface Movement Guidance and Control System).
- Triggering next **Master Plan revision** when demand reaches 60–80% of design capacity.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

The development framework is used by:
- **AAI (India)** for all state-run airports.
- **NHAI / MoCA** for Greenfield policy airports (e.g., Navi Mumbai, Mopa, Jewar).
- **International consulting firms**: Mott MacDonald, AECOM, Arup, Meinhardt.
- **PPP concessionaires**: GMR, GVK, Adani, Zurich Airport, Fraport.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this stage matters in your KTU exam:** A graduate Civil/Mechanical engineer working at an airport consultancy is expected to *prepare feasibility reports, run wind-rose analyses, evaluate runway orientations, and contribute to the master plan layout*. Module 1 is your **entry point** to this entire profession.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Standard | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Annual passenger growth (compound) | $P_n = P_0 \cdot (1+r)^n$ | $P_0$ = base year passengers, $r$ = growth rate, $n$ = years |
| 2 | Peak hour factor (PHF) | $PHF = \dfrac{\text{Peak hour volume}}{\text{Annual volume} \times k}$ | $k \approx 0.0003$ to $0.0005$ |
| 3 | Design hour passengers | $DHP = P_{30} \cdot 1.5$ | $P_{30}$ = 30th busiest hour passengers |
| 4 | Runway length (basic) | $L = f(\text{AAR}, \text{elevation}, \text{temperature})$ | Per ICAO Annex 14 / FAA AC 150/5325-4B |
| 5 | Runway length correction (elevation) | $L_c = L \cdot \left(1 + 0.07 \cdot \dfrac{h}{300}\right)$ | $h$ in meters above MSL |
| 6 | Runway length correction (temperature) | $L_c = L \cdot \left(1 + 0.01 \cdot \Delta T\right)$ | $\Delta T$ in °C above ISA |
| 7 | ICAO runway code number | $N = \text{rounded}\left(\dfrac{\text{Aeroplane reference field length}}{300}\right)$ | Yields 1, 2, 3, or 4 |
| 8 | ICAO runway code letter | Based on wingspan & outer main gear span | Letters A to F |
| 9 | Passenger Terminal Area (FAA) | $A = 0.0094 \cdot P_{hp} \cdot T$ | $A$ in m², $P_{hp}$ = peak hour passengers, $T$ = avg dwell time (min) |
| 10 | Gate requirement | $G = \dfrac{\text{Peak hour movements}}{\text{Gate occupancy time}}$ | Integer, rounded up |
| 11 | Apron area | $S = n \cdot f \cdot s$ | $n$ = stands, $f$ = gate factor, $s$ = stand area (m²) |
| 12 | Annual Service Volume (ASV) | $ASV = C_w \cdot C_t \cdot D \cdot H$ | Runway hourly capacity × annual factor |
| 13 | Capacity-delay function | $V/C$ ratio ≤ 0.8 for **LoS C** | Per FAA AC 150/5060-5 |
| 14 | Cargo terminal area | $A_c = \dfrac{\text{Annual cargo (tonnes)}}{\text{Throughput intensity}}$ | Empirical, 30–50 t/m²/year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip**: Out of these 14 formulas, **#1, #2, #5, #6, and #7** are the most frequently asked in KTU Module 1 questions. Memorize the ICAO Code number and letter derivation tables — they carry **direct 7-mark weightage** in Part B questions.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Engineering "Why" |
|---|---|
| Need assessment | Justifies the project; prevents white-elephant airports. |
| Feasibility study | De-risks investment of ₹5,000 – 50,000 crores. |
| Site selection | OLS and wind patterns cannot be altered post-construction. |
| Master plan | Locks in land use for 30+ years; very hard to change later. |
| Detailed design | Ensures pavement and structures survive 20-year design life. |
| Construction | Single biggest cost (60–70% of project budget). |
| Commissioning | Required by law before first commercial flight. |
| Operations | Generates revenue but also drives the **next** expansion cycle. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** as per the KTU 2024 valuation scheme. No step is skipped. Each transformation is logically explained.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Passenger Demand Forecasting (Compound Growth Model)

**Problem Statement:** A new airport in Kerala is being planned. The base year (2024) passenger volume is **$P_0 = 2.0$ MPPA**. The region is projected to grow at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of **$r = 8.5\%$**. Estimate the passenger volume at the **end of the 20-year planning horizon (Year 2044)**.

### Step 1: Identify the governing equation

The compound growth formula is the standard tool used in **ICAO Doc 8991 — Manual on Air Traffic Forecasting** and is the simplest defensible model for greenfield airports with limited historical data.

$$
P_n = P_0 \cdot (1+r)^n
$$

where:
- $P_n$ = projected passengers after $n$ years (in MPPA).
- $P_0$ = base year passenger volume.
- $r$ = annual growth rate (decimal).
- $n$ = number of years from base year.

### Step 2: Substitute the numerical values

We are given $P_0 = 2.0$ MPPA, $r = 0.085$, and $n = 2044 - 2024 = 20$ years.

$$
P_{20} = 2.0 \cdot (1 + 0.085)^{20}
$$

### Step 3: Simplify the bracket

$$
P_{20} = 2.0 \cdot (1.085)^{20}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the power

Using logarithms: $\ln(1.085) = 0.08158$, so $0.08158 \times 20 = 1.6316$, and $e^{1.6316} = 5.112$.

$$
(1.085)^{20} \approx 5.112
$$

### Step 5: Final multiplication

$$
P_{20} = 2.0 \times 5.112 = 10.224 \text{ MPPA}
$$

### Step 6: Engineering interpretation

> The terminal must be designed to handle a **Peak Hour Volume** corresponding to this **10.224 MPPA** annual throughput — not the 2.0 MPPA of the opening year. This is exactly why *under-designing* a greenfield terminal is the most common (and most expensive) mistake in Indian airport projects.

**Result:** $P_{20} \approx 10.22$ **MPPA**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Peak Hour Factor & Design Hour Passenger Calculation

**Problem Statement:** Using the 10.224 MPPA from Derivation 1, compute the **Peak Hour Passenger (PHP)** volume using the standard IATA/ICAO factor of $k = 0.00035$. Then estimate the **Design Hour Passenger (DHP)** for terminal sizing.

### Step 1: Governing equation

$$
PHP = P_{annual} \times k
$$

### Step 2: Substitute values

$$
PHP = 10{,}224{,}000 \times 0.00035
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate

$$
PHP = 3{,}578.4 \text{ passengers/hour}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Design Hour multiplier (FAA standard)

The **Design Hour Passenger (DHP)** is conventionally taken as **1.5 × PHP** to ensure comfortable LoS-C operation even during forecast uncertainty:

$$
DHP = 1.5 \times 3{,}578.4
$$

$$
DHP = 5{,}367.6 \approx 5{,}368 \text{ passengers/hour}
$$

### Step 5: Cross-check using the 30th-busy-hour method

Per FAA AC 150/5360-13, the **30th busiest hour** is approximately **0.85 × PHP**. We can verify:

$$
P_{30} = 0.85 \times 3{,}578.4 = 3{,}041.6
$$

This is the operational benchmark; **DHP = 5,368** is the **design benchmark** for terminal area calculation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** DHP is roughly **1.5–1.8 times PHP** in Indian conditions due to high seasonal variation (Onam, Vishu, Diwali, monsoon revival).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Runway Length Correction (Elevation + Temperature)

**Problem Statement:** ICAO's basic runway length for a Boeing 737-800 at **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** and **ISA conditions** is **$L_0 = 2{,}100$ m**. The proposed Greenfield airport site is at **elevation $h = 150$ m AMSL**, with a **reference temperature of $T_{ref} = 32\,^\circ$C** (ISA at MSL is $15\,^\circ$C). Compute the corrected runway length.

### Step 1: Basic ICAO length

$$
L_0 = 2{,}100 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the **elevation correction**

ICAO/FAA standard correction: for every 300 m of elevation, runway length increases by 7%.

$$
L_e = L_0 \cdot \left(1 + 0.07 \cdot \dfrac{h}{300}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute $h = 150$ m

$$
L_e = 2{,}100 \cdot \left(1 + 0.07 \cdot \dfrac{150}{300}\right)
$$

### Step 4: Simplify the bracket

$$
L_e = 2{,}100 \cdot (1 + 0.07 \times 0.5) = 2{,}100 \cdot (1 + 0.035)
$$

$$
L_e = 2{,}100 \cdot 1.035 = 2{,}173.5 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the temperature deviation from ISA

$$
\Delta T = T_{ref} - T_{ISA} = 32 - 15 = 17\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

### Step 6: Apply the **temperature correction**

The standard factor is **1% per °C above ISA**:

$$
L_c = L_e \cdot (1 + 0.01 \cdot \Delta T)
$$

### Step 7: Substitute

$$
L_c = 2{,}173.5 \cdot (1 + 0.01 \times 17)
$$

$$
L_c = 2{,}173.5 \cdot (1 + 0.17) = 2{,}173.5 \cdot 1.17
$$

$$
L_c = 2{,}542.99 \approx 2{,}543 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 8: Engineering interpretation

The runway must be **rounded up to the nearest 10 m** for ICAO compliance:

$$
L_{design} = 2{,}550 \text{ m}
$$

This 21% increase over MSL-ISA conditions illustrates why **Kerala's coastal sites** (Trivandrum, Calicut — both near MSL) have shorter runways than **Bengaluru or Leh** (high-elevation airports).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: ICAO Runway Code Number Derivation

**Problem Statement:** The design aircraft has an **Aeroplane Reference Field Length (ARFL)** of **$2{,}600$ m** at MSL. Determine the **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code Number**.

### Step 1: Governing equation

$$
N = \text{Int}\left(\dfrac{ARFL}{300}\right)
$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$
N = \dfrac{2{,}600}{300} = 8.667
$$

### Step 3: Round to nearest whole number

Per ICAO Annex 14, the code number is the **rounded integer**:

$$
N = 9
$$

Wait — actually ICAO Table 1-1 caps code numbers at **4**. The convention is:

- $ARFL < 800$ m → Code **1**
- $800 \le ARFL < 1200$ → Code **2**
- $1200 \le ARFL < 1800$ → Code **3**
- $ARFL \ge 1800$ → Code **4**

### Step 4: Apply ICAO band

Since $ARFL = 2{,}600 \ge 1{,}800$:

$$
\boxed{N = 4}
$$

### Step 5: Code letter (for wingspan)

For a Boeing 737-800, wingspan $= 35.8$ m and outer main gear span $= 8.7$ m:

| Wingspan range | Code letter |
|---|---|
| $< 15$ m | A |
| $15 - 24$ m | B |
| $24 - 36$ m | **C** |
| $36 - 52$ m | D |
| $52 - 65$ m | E |
| $65 - 80$ m | F |

$$
\boxed{\text{Code Letter} = C}
$$

### Step 6: Final ICAO Reference Code

$$
\boxed{\text{ICAO Code} = 4C}
$$

This is the code for **Kerala's Calicut (CCJ)** and **Trivandrum (TRV)** international airports.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Variables in New Airport Development

| Variable | Typical Range / Value | Source / Standard | Impact on Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Planning horizon | 20–30 years | ICAO Doc 9184 | Low (study cost only) |
| Land area required | 1,000–6,000 acres | IATA Airport Development Reference Manual | **High** (land is scarce in Kerala) |
| Runway orientation | Aligned with **prevailing wind ≥ 95%** | ICAO Annex 14 | Medium |
| Runway length | 2,000–4,500 m | Aircraft mix | **Very High** |
| Terminal LoS target | C (Optimum) | IATA | Medium |
| Peak hour design factor | 0.0003 – 0.0005 | Empirical | High |
| Apron stand requirement | 10–80 stands | Demand | Very High |
| Construction period | 4–7 years | Industry benchmark | — |
| CAPEX (Greenfield) | ₹6,000–25,000 per pax | AAI benchmarking | — |
| Environmental clearance | Mandatory | EIA Notification 2006 | Schedule risk |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Eight Stages of New Airport Development

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Need Identification] --> B[Stage 2: Feasibility Study TEV]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Site Selection and Land Acquisition]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Master Planning 20 to 30 year horizon]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Detailed Engineering Design]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Construction and Procurement]
    F --> G[Stage 7: Commissioning and Certification]
    G --> H[Stage 8: Operations and Expansion]
    H --> I{Master Plan Trigger 60 to 80 percent capacity}
    I -- Yes --> D
    I -- No --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> The decision diamond at the end is critical — **greenfield airports are never "completed"**. They enter a continuous loop of master plan revisions roughly every 10–15 years.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Master Plan Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AIRSIDE[Airside Components]
        RW[Runway]
        TW[Taxiway System]
        AP[Apron and Stands]
        NAV[Navigational Aids ILS VOR DME]
        LGT[Airfield Lighting PAPI REIL]
    end
    subgraph LANDSIDE[Landside Components]
        TB[Terminal Building]
        APR[Access Road Network]
        PK[Parking and Curbside]
        CG[Cargo Complex]
        MRO[Maintenance Repair Overhaul]
    end
    subgraph SUPPORT[Support Infrastructure]
        ATC[ATC Tower]
        ARFF[Fire Station ARFF Cat 7]
        PWR[Power and Utilities]
        SWM[Stormwater Drainage]
        SEC[Perimeter Security]
    end
    RW --> TW --> AP
    AP --> TB
    TB --> PK
    TB --> CG
    ATC --> RW
    ARFF --> RW
    PWR --> LGT
    SWM --> AP
    SEC --> RW
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Map — Feasibility & Site Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Traffic Forecasting] --> D2[Capacity Gap Analysis]
    D2 --> S1[Geographic Screening]
    S1 --> S2[Wind Rose Analysis]
    S2 --> S3[Obstacle Limitation Surface Check]
    S3 --> S4[Soil and Geotechnical Survey]
    S4 --> S5[Environmental Impact Assessment]
    S5 --> S6[Multi Criteria Scoring]
    S6 --> S7[Final Site Selection]
    S7 --> M1[Master Plan Commissioning]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**List any six factors that are considered during the site selection of a new airport.**

**Model Answer (6 points, ~80 words):**
1. **Obstacle-free approach paths** (per ICAO Annex 14 OLS).
2. **Wind coverage** — runway aligned for ≥ 95% crosswind coverage.
3. **Soil bearing capacity** for heavy pavement loads.
4. **Topography and drainage** — flat, well-drained land preferred.
5. **Distance from city** — optimum 20–40 km; short travel time.
6. **Environmental sensitivity** — avoiding bird sanctuaries, wetlands, dense habitation.
7. **Future expansion potential** — minimum 1,000–2,000 acres.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only "location" and "size" as factors. You must name **at least 6 specific engineering factors** to score the full 3 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is a "Greenfield Airport"? How is it different from a "Brownfield Airport Project"?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Greenfield Airport** is built from scratch on previously undeveloped land (e.g., **Navi Mumbai International, Mopa, Kannur**). It allows designers to follow a clean master plan with no legacy constraints.

A **Brownfield Project** involves the **expansion or modernization of an existing operational airport** (e.g., Delhi IGI Terminal 3, Mumbai T2). The engineer must integrate new infrastructure with existing facilities, often under live traffic conditions, which makes it more complex and constrained.

| Feature | Greenfield | Brownfield |
|---|---|---|
| Land status | Undeveloped | Existing operational |
| Design freedom | High | Limited |
| Construction risk | Moderate | High (live operations) |
| Examples | Kannur, Mopa | Delhi T3, Bengaluru T2 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **eight stages of development of a new Greenfield Airport** in detail. Draw a neat flowchart showing the sequential flow and feedback loop with the master plan revision.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** An airport is being designed for a base year passenger volume of **3.5 MPPA** in 2025. The projected CAGR is **9.0%** per annum. Compute: (i) the **annual passenger volume at the end of 2045** (20 years), and (ii) the **Peak Hour Passenger (PHP)** and **Design Hour Passenger (DHP)** using standard factors. Also estimate the **terminal building area** using the FAA rule of thumb (assume dwell time $T = 45$ minutes).

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

The eight stages are:
1. **Need identification** — saturation at existing airports, regional demand.
2. **Feasibility study** — TEV report, demand forecasting, financial modeling.
3. **Site selection** — OLS, wind, soil, EIA.
4. **Master planning** — 20–30 year horizon, 5-year phasing.
5. **Detailed design** — runway, terminal, pavements, drainage.
6. **Construction** — EPC/PPP/HAM model.
7. **Commissioning** — ORAT, calibration, aerodrome licence.
8. **Operations & expansion** — A-SMGCS, capacity monitoring, master plan revision at 60–80% saturation.

(Refer to **Section 4.1 flowchart** for the diagram — draw the same in the exam paper for full marks.)

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Annual volume at 2045**

$$
P_{20} = 3.5 \times (1.09)^{20}
$$

Compute $(1.09)^{20}$: $\ln(1.09) = 0.08618$, $\times 20 = 1.7236$, $e^{1.7236} = 5.604$.

$$
P_{20} = 3.5 \times 5.604 = 19.61 \text{ MPPA}
$$

**Step 2: Peak Hour Passenger (PHP)**

Use $k = 0.00035$:

$$
PHP = 19{,}614{,}000 \times 0.00035 = 6{,}865 \text{ passengers/hour}
$$

**Step 3: Design Hour Passenger (DHP)**

$$
DHP = 1.5 \times 6{,}865 = 10{,}298 \text{ passengers/hour}
$$

**Step 4: Terminal area (FAA rule)**

FAA rule: $A = 0.0094 \times P_{hp} \times T$

$$
A = 0.0094 \times 6{,}865 \times 45
$$

$$
A = 2{,}903.7 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (per peak hour — design unit area)}
$$

For the **total terminal area**, multiply by the **dwell time and circulation factor 3–5** (empirical Indian practice):

$$
A_{total} = 2{,}904 \times 4 \approx 11{,}616 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{20} = 19.61$ MPPA, $PHP = 6{,}865$, $DHP = 10{,}298$, $A_{total} \approx 11{,}600$ m².

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **factors affecting runway length** in the design of a new airport. A basic runway length of **2,200 m** is required for a critical aircraft at **MSL under ISA conditions**. The proposed site is at an **elevation of 600 m AMSL** with a **reference temperature of 28 °C**. Calculate the **corrected runway length** and the **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code Number** (assume ARFL = corrected length).
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **Master Planning** in the development of a new airport. List the **key components of an Airport Master Plan** and explain the **phasing strategy** typically adopted.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

**Factors affecting runway length:**
1. **Aircraft type** (ARFL of critical aircraft).
2. **Elevation** of the airport above MSL.
3. **Reference temperature** (deviation from ISA).
4. **Runway gradient / slope**.
5. **Surface condition** (wet/dry, friction coefficient).
6. **Approach speed** and **obstacles in approach path**.

**Step 1: Elevation correction**

$$
L_e = 2{,}200 \times \left(1 + 0.07 \times \dfrac{600}{300}\right) = 2{,}200 \times (1 + 0.14) = 2{,}508 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Temperature correction**

$\Delta T = 28 - 15 = 13\,^\circ\text{C}$

$$
L_c = 2{,}508 \times (1 + 0.01 \times 13) = 2{,}508 \times 1.13 = 2{,}834 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Round to nearest 10 m**

$$
L_{design} = 2{,}840 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: ICAO Code Number**

Since $ARFL = 2{,}840 \ge 1{,}800$:

$$
\boxed{\text{ICAO Code Number} = 4}
$$

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

**Role of Master Planning:**
- Acts as the **blueprint** for the next 20–30 years.
- Locks in **land use zoning** (airside, landside, support).
- Coordinates **multi-agency stakeholders** (AAI, MoCA, state, airlines, AERA).
- Provides the **basis for funding** and PPP concession agreements.

**Key Components (from ICAO Doc 9184):**
1. **Inventory of existing conditions** (site, traffic, environment).
2. **Demand forecasts** (passengers, cargo, aircraft movements).
3. **Capacity analysis** — demand vs capacity gap.
4. **Airside layout** (runways, taxiways, aprons, navaids).
5. **Landside layout** (terminal, access, parking, cargo).
6. **Environmental and land-use plan**.
7. **Capital Improvement Plan (CIP)** with phased investments.
8. **Financial feasibility and implementation schedule**.

**Phasing Strategy:**
- **Phase 1 (0–5 years):** Initial single runway, terminal for opening day, basic support.
- **Phase 2 (5–10 years):** Apron expansion, second taxiway, terminal extension.
- **Phase 3 (10–20 years):** Second runway (if justified), full parallel taxiway, MRO, cargo city.
- **Phase 4 (20–30 years):** Satellite terminals, automated people movers, multimodal hub.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't forget the rounding** — runway lengths must be rounded up to the nearest 10 m (ICAO compliance).
> 2. **Apply corrections sequentially**, not additively: elevation first, then temperature, NOT vice-versa.
> 3. **Cite ICAO Annex 14 / Doc 9184** in your answer to score "knowledge of standards" marks.
> 4. **Draw the flowchart** for Part (a) — students who skip diagrams lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Show units** in every numerical step — un-numbered answers receive **partial credit only**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Module 1, Topic: Development of New Airport**

- [x] New airport development has **8 stages** (Need → Feasibility → Site → Master Plan → Design → Construction → Commissioning → Operations).
- [x] **Greenfield** = fresh site; **Brownfield** = expansion of existing.
- [x] **ICAO Annex 14** governs aerodrome design; **ICAO Doc 9184** governs master planning.
- [x] **Site selection** is driven by OLS, wind coverage, soil, EIA, and expansion potential.
- [x] **Master Plan** covers 20–30 years with 5-year phasing.
- [x] **Forecasting formula:** $P_n = P_0 (1+r)^n$.
- [x] **Peak Hour Factor** $k \approx 0.0003$ to $0.0005$; $DHP = 1.5 \times PHP$.
- [x] **Runway length corrections**: elevation $+7\%$ per 300 m, temperature $+1\%$ per °C above ISA.
- [x] **ICAO Code Number** = rounded (ARFL / 300), but banded into 1/2/3/4 with cut-offs at 800, 1200, 1800 m.
- [x] **ICAO Code Letter** based on wingspan and outer main gear span (A to F).
- [x] **Terminal sizing** (FAA): $A = 0.0094 \times P_{hp} \times T$ m².
- [x] **Greenfield CAPEX benchmark in India:** ₹6,000–25,000 per peak-hour passenger.
- [x] **PPP models** (EPC, BOT, HAM) dominate modern Indian greenfield airport delivery.
- [x] **AERA** regulates tariffs; **AAI** issues aerodrome licence; **DGCA** certifies airlines.
- [x] **Master Plan revision** triggered when traffic reaches **60–80% of design capacity** (LoS C → D transition).
- [x] **ORAT** (Operational Readiness and Airport Transfer) is the final pre-opening step.
- [x] **Kerala case studies** to remember: **Kannur International (Greenfield)**, **Trivandrum & Calicut (Brownfield expansion)**, **Cochin (PPP model)**.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Requirements of an Ideal Airport Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Ideal Airport Layout** is a comprehensive, geometrically optimized master plan that arranges all operational, functional, and support facilities (runways, taxiways, aprons, terminal buildings, cargo zones, air traffic control towers, navigation aids, and ground access systems) in such a way that aircraft movement, passenger flow, cargo handling, and emergency response are achieved with **maximum safety, minimum delay, optimum land use, and full provision for future expansion**, while complying with **ICAO Annex 14**, **FAA AC 150/5300-13A**, and the **Ministry of Civil Aviation (India) guidelines**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** The ideal layout is NOT a single drawing — it is a *systems-engineering solution* balancing four primary vectors: **Safety, Capacity, Economy, and Expandability** (often remembered as the **"SCEE" rule**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an airport like a **well-planned city**:
- **Runways** are the "highways" (used by the biggest, fastest "vehicles" — aircraft).
- **Taxiways** are the "service roads" that connect highways to buildings.
- **Aprons (Ramps)** are the "parking lots" in front of the terminal.
- **Terminal Building** is the "shopping mall + bus station" — where passengers and goods are exchanged.
- **ATC Tower** is the "traffic police headquarters."
- **Ground Access** is the city's "road network" connecting the airport to the rest of the world.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a city is poorly designed if its highways are blocked by markets, an airport is poorly designed if its runway end is obstructed by a terminal. The **Ideal Layout** ensures that **no two conflicting activities cross paths** — this principle is called **"segregation of flows"**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics of an Ideal Layout (Bold Constants)

- **ICAO/FAA Code Reference Length:** Greater than or equal to **1800 m** for code 4E/4F aircraft (e.g., B777, A380).
- **Minimum Runway Strip Width:** **300 m** (for code 4 runways, ICAO Annex 14).
- **Runway End Safety Area (RESA):** Minimum **90 m** (extends beyond the runway end).
- **Stopway / Clearway provisions:** As per aircraft performance class.
- **Cross-wind Component Limit:** **20 knots (≈ 37 km/h)** for larger aircraft, **13 knots (≈ 24 km/h)** for small aircraft.
- **Runway Shoulder Width:** **7.5 m** (each side, paved, for code 4).
- **Taxiway Separation from Runway Centreline:** **168 m** (for code 4 — precision approach CAT II/III).
- **Terminal-to-Runway Taxi Time Target:** **≤ 15 minutes** (IATA Level of Service "Optimum").
- **Peak Hour Passenger Flow Capacity:** 3000–5000 PAX/hour in a single terminal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The benchmark **land area** for an ideal international layout is approximately **6 to 8 square kilometers** (600–800 hectares) for a medium-traffic airport, and **> 20 km²** for a mega-hub like Delhi's IGIA or Dubai International.

## 1.4 Why "Ideal" ≠ "Maximum"

An ideal layout is **contextual**. A small regional airstrip (code 1B, 800 m strip) has very different requirements than a code 4F international hub. The "ideal" is therefore defined relative to:

1. **Design Aircraft** (critical / reference aircraft).
2. **Annual Passenger Volume (APV)** and **Peak Hour Traffic (PHT)**.
3. **Site topography, wind regime, and obstacle environment**.
4. **Forecast horizon (usually 20–30 years)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillars of the Ideal Airport Layout — Structured Logic

The requirements of an ideal airport layout can be broken down into **seven functional pillars**. Each pillar addresses a specific operational question.

### Pillar 1: Runway Orientation & Configuration
- **Why:** Aircraft takeoff and landing must be aligned **into the prevailing wind** to maximize lift and minimize runway length.
- **How:** Plot the **wind rose** for the site; the runway should be oriented so that the **cross-wind component** does not exceed 20 knots for the design aircraft.
- **KTU Marker:** This is the **single most exam-relevant sub-topic** of Module 1.

### Pillar 2: Number of Runways
- **Why:** A single runway has a practical capacity of **~30 to 45 movements per hour**. Beyond this, delays become exponential.
- **How:** Decide based on the **Annual Service Volume (ASV)** and the **peak hour forecast**. Add a parallel/cross runway when **throughput** > 70% of single-runway ASV.

### Pillar 3: Taxiway Network
- **Why:** Aircraft must transition between runway and apron without conflict, at **taxi speeds up to 60 km/h** (turn-off exits at 90°).
- **How:** Provide **parallel taxiways**, **high-speed exit taxiways** (30° angle, 1000 m length), and **end-around taxiways** to avoid runway crossings.

### Pillar 4: Apron (Ramp) Area
- **Why:** Aircraft need parking, refueling, boarding, and servicing.
- **How:** Size using **gate-parking stand geometry** — a code 4E aircraft (B777) requires a stand width of approximately **75 m** and depth of **120 m** with a 7.5 m wingtip clearance.

### Pillar 5: Terminal Building Sizing
- **Why:** Passenger processing (check-in, security, immigration, baggage) requires space.
- **How:** Use **IATA Level of Service C** as design target. Apply **Fruin's walking speeds (1.3 m/s)** and **queue theory (M/M/s)** for sizing.

### Pillar 6: Air Traffic Control (ATC) & NAVAIDS
- **Why:** Safe sequencing, approach guidance, and ground movement control.
- **How:** Locate the **Control Tower** such that the **controller's eye height** is at least **25 m** above the highest critical operational surface, with **unobstructed sight lines** to all runways, taxiways, and aprons.

### Pillar 7: Ground Access & Landside Facilities
- **Why:** Passengers, staff, and cargo must enter/leave efficiently.
- **How:** Provide a **ring road**, dedicated **flyover/underpass** avoiding terminal curbside congestion, parking for **> 70%** of daily peak demand, and integration with **metro/rail** for hub airports.

## 2.2 Secondary Requirements (Often Overlooked in Exams)

- **Drainage & Grading:** Minimum **1.5% slope** on paved areas, with storm drains sized for a **return period of 10 years** (20 years for critical runway edges).
- **Fencing & Perimeter Security:** **3 m high chain-link** with barbed wire; clear zone of **150 m** from runway centerline.
- **Fire & Rescue Station:** Must reach **mid-runway** within **3 minutes** (ICAO category 6–10 based on aircraft length).
- **Fuel Farm:** Located **≥ 1.5 km** from terminal; with secondary containment **110%** of largest tank.
- **Future Expansion:** **Master plan with phasing** — at least 30% vacant land reserved for Stage 2/3 development.
- **Environmental Buffer:** Noise contours (NEF 30 / DNL 65 dB) must not encroach on **residential/school** zones; green belt of **100 m** minimum.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Most numerical questions in Part B (14-mark) require one of the formulas below. Memorize the variable meanings, NOT just the equation.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Relationship | Units / Constants | KTU Application |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Cross-Wind Component | $C_w = V \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | $V$ = wind speed (kt), $\theta$ = angle between wind & runway | Verify runway orientation against limit (20 kt) |
| 2 | Head-Wind Component | $C_h = V \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | kt | Used to compute effective runway length (FAR/ICAO) |
| 3 | Runway Usability Factor (Wind Coverage) | $U = \dfrac{\text{\% time wind within cross-wind limit}}{\text{Total time}}$ | Expressed as % | Must be $\geq 95\%$ (ICAO) |
| 4 | Annual Service Volume (ASV) — Single Runway | $ASV = C \cdot D \cdot T$ | $C$ = capacity (mov/h), $D$ = demand factor, $T$ = time | Determine when parallel runway is needed |
| 5 | Taxiway Centreline Separation from Runway CL | $S_{tr} = 168$ m (Code 4, CAT II/III) | m | For apron planning |
| 6 | Apron Stand Width (Code 4E, MARS) | $W = L_{wingspan} + 2 \cdot W_{clearance}$ | $L_{wingspan} \approx 65$ m, $W_{clearance} = 7.5$ m | $W \approx 80$ m |
| 7 | Runway Strip Width | $W_{strip} = 300$ m (Code 4) | m | Beyond runway shoulder |
| 8 | RESA Length | $L_{RESA} = 90$ m (min) → $240$ m (recommended) | m | Beyond runway end |
| 9 | Obstacle Limitation Surface (Approach) Slope | $1:50$ (first 3000 m) → $1:40$ (further) | ratio | For obstacle clearance around runway |
| 10 | Peak Hour Passenger Flow | $PHP = \dfrac{ADT \cdot K \cdot D}{60}$ | $K$ = peak hour factor (0.10–0.15), $D$ = directional split | Terminal sizing |
| 11 | Stopway Length | $L_{stopway} = L_{TODA} - L_{TORA}$ | m | When physical end is blocked |
| 12 | Basic Runway Length Correction (Altitude) | $L_{corr} = L_0 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{h}{1000}\right) \cdot 0.07$ | $h$ = airport elevation in m | Apply to sea-level reference length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Discipline:** All absolute values in the table above (e.g., elevation $h$) are written using the $\mid$ or $\vert$ math-mode delimiter, never the markdown table separator $\vert$. This protects table rendering on the KTU e-learning portal.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ICAO Annex 14** is the global standard — any international airport design in India must comply.
- **FAA AC 150/5300-13A** is the US reference, often cross-listed with Indian standards.
- **DGCA CAR Section 4, Series I, Part I** (India) governs licensing of airports.
- The layout directly determines **project CAPEX** — a poor layout may add 25–40% to lifecycle cost through inefficient taxi fuel burn and rework.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cross-Wind Component from a Wind Rose

The **cross-wind component** is the component of wind blowing *perpendicular* to the runway direction. This is a foundational derivation that appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 exam.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
A wind of speed $V$ blows from a direction $\beta$ (measured clockwise from True North). The runway is oriented along a bearing $\phi$ (also measured clockwise from True North). Derive the cross-wind component $C_w$.

### 3.1.2 Setup & Free-Body Geometry

The angle between the wind direction and the runway direction is:

$$
\theta = \beta - \phi
$$

The wind vector $\vec{V}$ can be resolved into two orthogonal components:
- **Along runway (head-wind):** $C_h$
- **Perpendicular to runway (cross-wind):** $C_w$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Resolution

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_h &= V \cdot \cos(\theta) \\[6pt]
    &= V \cdot \cos(\beta - \phi) \\[6pt]
C_w &= V \cdot \sin(\theta) \\[6pt]
    &= V \cdot \sin(\beta - \phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Check:** If wind blows *directly along* the runway ($\theta = 0°$), then $C_w = 0$ (no cross-wind), and $C_h = V$ (pure head-wind). ✅

If wind blows *directly perpendicular* to the runway ($\theta = 90°$), then $C_w = V$ (full cross-wind), and $C_h = 0$. ✅

### 3.1.4 Runway Usability (Wind Coverage)

For a given runway orientation, the percentage of time during which the cross-wind component does not exceed the design limit (e.g., 20 kt) is:

$$
U = \dfrac{\sum_{i} f_i \cdot \mathbb{1}\left[\vert V_i \cdot \sin(\beta_i - \phi)\vert \leq 20\right]}{\sum_i f_i} \times 100\%
$$

where $f_i$ is the frequency (in % time) of wind blowing from sector $i$, and $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function (1 if condition holds, 0 otherwise).

**ICAO Rule:** $U \geq 95\%$ for the design aircraft.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
> The wind data for a proposed airport site is given below. Design the runway orientation such that the cross-wind component does not exceed **15 knots**. Compute the **wind coverage** for the proposed orientation.

| Wind Direction (from) | Wind Speed (kt) | Frequency (% time) |
|----------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 000° (N) | 20 | 10 |
| 045° (NE) | 25 | 15 |
| 090° (E) | 10 | 20 |
| 135° (SE) | 15 | 5 |
| 180° (S) | 30 | 5 |
| 225° (SW) | 5 | 20 |
| 270° (W) | 15 | 15 |
| 315° (NW) | 10 | 10 |

### Solution — Step 1: Hypothesize a Runway Orientation

Let us assume the runway is oriented **North–South** (bearing $\phi = 0°$).

### Solution — Step 2: Compute Cross-Wind Components

Using $C_w = V \cdot \sin(\beta - \phi)$ with $\phi = 0°$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N: \quad & C_w = 20 \cdot \sin(0°) = 0 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
NE: \quad & C_w = 25 \cdot \sin(45°) = 25 \cdot 0.707 = 17.68 \text{ kt} \quad \text{Violation} \\
E: \quad & C_w = 10 \cdot \sin(90°) = 10 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
SE: \quad & C_w = 15 \cdot \sin(135°) = 15 \cdot 0.707 = 10.6 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
S: \quad & C_w = 30 \cdot \sin(180°) = 0 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
SW: \quad & C_w = 5 \cdot \sin(225°) = 5 \cdot (-0.707) = -3.54 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
W: \quad & C_w = 15 \cdot \sin(270°) = -15 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
NW: \quad & C_w = 10 \cdot \sin(315°) = -7.07 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 3: Apply the 15-kt Limit

The NE direction produces **17.68 kt**, which **exceeds** 15 kt. The 15% frequency from NE must be re-allocated or the runway re-oriented.

### Solution — Step 4: Try Runway Orientation $\phi = 045°$–$225°$ (NE–SW)

For each direction, recompute $\theta = \beta - 45°$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N: \quad & \theta = -45°, \quad C_w = 20 \cdot \sin(-45°) = -14.14 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
NE: \quad & \theta = 0°, \quad C_w = 25 \cdot 0 = 0 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
E: \quad & \theta = 45°, \quad C_w = 10 \cdot 0.707 = 7.07 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
SE: \quad & \theta = 90°, \quad C_w = 15 \cdot 1 = 15 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \text{ (at limit)} \\
S: \quad & \theta = 135°, \quad C_w = 30 \cdot 0.707 = 21.21 \text{ kt} \quad \text{Violation} \\
SW: \quad & \theta = 180°, \quad C_w = 0 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
W: \quad & \theta = 225°, \quad C_w = 15 \cdot (-0.707) = -10.6 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
NW: \quad & \theta = 270°, \quad C_w = -10 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The S direction still violates (21.21 kt > 15 kt). Its frequency is 5%.

### Solution — Step 5: Compute Wind Coverage for $\phi = 045°$

$$
U = \dfrac{(10 + 15 + 20 + 5 + 5 + 20 + 15 + 10) - 5}{100} \times 100\% = 95\%
$$

**Result:** $U = 95\%$ — **acceptable** per ICAO. The runway may be oriented NE–SW with a **5% non-coverage** (typically during S-bound winds at 30 kt, where a secondary cross-runway can be considered).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Layout Decisions vs. Requirements

| Layout Decision | Primary Driver | Constraint | KTU Marker |
|-----------------|----------------|------------|------------|
| Number of runways | Annual service volume | ASV / 0.7 | Module 1 |
| Runway orientation | Wind rose | Cross-wind $\leq 20$ kt, $U \geq 95\%$ | Module 1 ⭐ |
| Runway length | Design aircraft + altitude + temp | ICAO Doc 9157 | Module 1/2 |
| Taxiway network | Capacity & safety | Separation from CL (168 m, code 4) | Module 2 |
| Apron area | Gate demand | Stand geometry (B777/A380) | Module 3 |
| Terminal location | Passenger flow | 15-min taxi time | Module 3 |
| ATC tower height | Sightline geometry | $\geq 25$ m above runway | Module 2 |

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Design Iteration Logic for Runway Orientation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Step 0: Collect Wind Data]:::step
    S1[Step 1: Plot Wind Rose]:::step
    S2[Step 2: Hypothesize Runway Bearing Phi]:::step

    S8[ACCEPT Orientation]:::accept
    S9[Step 9: Re-evaluate Phi by 5 degrees]:::step
    S10[Step 10: Consider Cross Runway]:::warn

    S0 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 -- Yes --> S5
    S4 -- No --> S10
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7

    S7 -- No --> S9
    S9 --> S2
    S10 --> S5

```

**Interpretation:** The flow describes the iterative process a planner follows when designing a runway orientation. The decision diamonds check the two ICAO constraints: **cross-wind limit (20 kt)** and **wind coverage (95%)**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define an "ideal airport layout". List any FOUR essential requirements that an ideal airport layout must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **ideal airport layout** is a master plan that integrates all airside, terminal, and landside facilities in a manner that ensures **safety, capacity, economy, and expandability (SCEE)**.

Four essential requirements:
1. **Runway orientation** must comply with the **wind rose** such that the cross-wind component does not exceed 20 kt and the wind coverage is **≥ 95%**.
2. **Adequate separation distances** must be maintained between runway, taxiway, apron, buildings, and perimeter fence as per **ICAO Annex 14** (e.g., taxiway CL 168 m from runway CL for code 4).
3. **Provision for future expansion** — at least **30% of the total land** should be reserved for future runways, terminals, and aprons.
4. **Efficient ground access** — terminal-to-runway taxi time should be **≤ 15 minutes** with ring road connectivity and public transport integration.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for four requirements with brief justification.)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Explain the concept of "Runway Usability Factor" with reference to the ICAO standard.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Runway Usability Factor (U)** is defined as the percentage of time during which the **cross-wind component** on a given runway orientation does not exceed the permissible limit for the design aircraft.

Mathematically:

$$
U = \dfrac{\text{Percentage of time wind produces } \vert C_w \vert \leq C_{w,lim}}{\text{Total time considered}} \times 100\%
$$

The **ICAO standard** requires that $U \geq 95\%$ for the design aircraft. If this criterion is not met with a single runway, planners must consider either (a) re-orienting the runway, or (b) providing a **secondary cross-runway** to handle the remaining 5% of time.

*(Award 1.5 marks for definition + formula, 1.5 marks for ICAO rule and remedial action.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. Answer ANY ONE full question. Each full question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** A wind rose analysis for a proposed airport site is given below. Design the **runway orientation** and compute the **wind coverage** for a **20-knot cross-wind limit**. Discuss whether the orientation is acceptable per ICAO. **(7 marks)**

| Wind From (°) | Wind Speed (kt) | % Time |
|---------------|-----------------|--------|
| 030 | 25 | 12 |
| 060 | 15 | 8 |
| 090 | 10 | 10 |
| 120 | 20 | 5 |
| 150 | 30 | 6 |
| 180 | 25 | 7 |
| 210 | 10 | 15 |
| 240 | 15 | 18 |
| 270 | 20 | 10 |
| 300 | 5 | 5 |
| 330 | 10 | 4 |

**(b)** With neat sketches, describe the **seven functional zones** of an ideal airport layout and explain the requirement of a **30% land reserve for future expansion**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1 — Hypothesis:** Try runway orientation $\phi = 060°$–$240°$ (i.e., runway aligned ENE–WSW).

**Step 2 — Compute cross-wind component $C_w = V \cdot \sin(\beta - 60°)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta = 030°: & \quad C_w = 25 \cdot \sin(-30°) = -12.5 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
\beta = 060°: & \quad C_w = 15 \cdot \sin(0°) = 0 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
\beta = 090°: & \quad C_w = 10 \cdot \sin(30°) = 5 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
\beta = 120°: & \quad C_w = 20 \cdot \sin(60°) = 17.32 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
\beta = 150°: & \quad C_w = 30 \cdot \sin(90°) = 30 \text{ kt} \quad \text{Violation} \\
\beta = 180°: & \quad C_w = 25 \cdot \sin(120°) = 21.65 \text{ kt} \quad \text{Violation} \\
\beta = 210°: & \quad C_w = 10 \cdot \sin(150°) = 5 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
\beta = 240°: & \quad C_w = 15 \cdot \sin(180°) = 0 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
\beta = 270°: & \quad C_w = 20 \cdot \sin(210°) = -10 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
\beta = 300°: & \quad C_w = 5 \cdot \sin(240°) = -4.33 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
\beta = 330°: & \quad C_w = 10 \cdot \sin(270°) = -10 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply 20-kt limit and sum frequencies:**

$$
U = \dfrac{100 - (6 + 7)}{100} \times 100\% = 87\%
$$

**Step 4 — Verdict:** $U = 87\% < 95\%$. **NOT acceptable** per ICAO.

**Step 5 — Re-evaluate:** Try $\phi = 030°$–$210°$ (NNE–SSW):

$$
\begin{aligned}
030°: & \quad C_w = 25 \cdot \sin(0°) = 0 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
060°: & \quad C_w = 15 \cdot \sin(30°) = 7.5 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
090°: & \quad C_w = 10 \cdot \sin(60°) = 8.66 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
120°: & \quad C_w = 20 \cdot \sin(90°) = 20 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \text{ (at limit)} \\
150°: & \quad C_w = 30 \cdot \sin(120°) = 25.98 \text{ kt} \quad \text{Violation} \\
180°: & \quad C_w = 25 \cdot \sin(150°) = 12.5 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
210°: & \quad C_w = 10 \cdot \sin(180°) = 0 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
240°: & \quad C_w = 15 \cdot \sin(210°) = -7.5 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
270°: & \quad C_w = 20 \cdot \sin(240°) = -17.32 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
300°: & \quad C_w = 5 \cdot \sin(270°) = -5 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark \\
330°: & \quad C_w = 10 \cdot \sin(300°) = -8.66 \text{ kt} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
U = \dfrac{100 - 6}{100} \times 100\% = 94\%
$$

Still slightly under 95%. **Add a secondary cross-runway** to cover the 150° (SSE) wind sector (6% of the time).

**Final Answer:** Primary runway oriented **030°–210° (NNE–SSW)** with a secondary cross-runway oriented ~120°–300° (ESE–WNW). The primary runway alone gives 94% wind coverage; the addition of a cross-runway brings combined coverage to ~99%.

*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks for hypothesis, 3 marks for cross-wind table, 1 mark for $U$ calculation, 1 mark for verdict and remedy.)*

#### Model Solution (b):

The **seven functional zones** of an ideal airport layout (with a sketch reference) are:

1. **Airside — Runways:** Paved strips for takeoff and landing. Orientation governed by wind rose.
2. **Airside — Taxiways:** Connecting taxiways, parallel taxiways, high-speed exit taxiways.
3. **Airside — Aprons:** Parking stands for aircraft near the terminal.
4. **Terminal Zone — Passenger Terminal:** Check-in, security, immigration, baggage claim, lounges.
5. **Terminal Zone — Cargo Terminal:** Warehousing, customs, bonded storage.
6. **Landside — Ground Access:** Ring road, parking, public transport interchange.
7. **Support Zone — NAVAIDS, ATC, Fire, Fuel, Fencing.**

**Why 30% land reserve?**
- A master plan has a **20–30-year horizon** — beyond the initial design horizon, traffic may grow 2–3 times.
- Constructing a new runway inside an operational airport (brownfield expansion) is **5–10× more expensive** than greenfield construction.
- A 30% reserve allows **future parallel runway, satellite terminal, MRO hangars, and a metro corridor** without disturbing operations.
- ICAO Doc 9184 (Airport Planning Manual) explicitly recommends this provision.

*(Mark Distribution: 4 marks for zones (1/2 mark each + sketch), 3 marks for justification of 30% reserve with ICAO reference.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **four pillars (SCEE)** of an ideal airport layout. How do they govern runway, taxiway, and apron design decisions? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **required runway length** at sea level for a reference aircraft needing 3000 m at standard conditions, given the following corrections. Apply ICAO correction methodology step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

- Airport elevation: **500 m** above MSL.
- Reference temperature: **ISA + 20°C** (assume 15°C at sea level ISA, lapse rate −6.5°C/km).
- Runway gradient: **0.5%** (effective).

#### Model Solution (a):

The **SCEE pillars** of an ideal airport layout are:
