The factors **1.15** and **1.67** are regulatory safety multipliers. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Highway Metaphor Imagine you are a **mechanical engineer** designing a **braking system** for a sports car. The advertised braking distance is measured on a **perfect test track**: dry asphalt, level road, $25 \text{ °C}$, no wind, fresh tires. In real life, however, the car will face: - A **steep hill** (gradient effect → longer stopping distance) - A **hot summer day** at **high altitude** (elevation + temperature → thinner air → less engine power, more lift off the wheels) - A **headwind** pushing against the car (wind correction → shorter distance) Basic Runway Length is that "perfect test track value." **Corrections to Runway Length** are the engineering adjustments we apply to translate the test track value to real-world airport conditions. Just as a car engineer would never publish braking distance without specifying the conditions, an airport planner must add corrections to convert the standard-day value to the **actual runway length** required at the specific site. ## 1.4 Governing Regulatory Bodies - **ICAO Annex 14** → International standards (Runway length code numbers 1, 2, 3, 4). - **FAA AC 150/5325-4B** → U.S. Federal Aviation Administration methodology (used widely in Indian airports and KTU syllabus). - **DGCA CAR Section 4, Series I, Part I** → Indian adaptation of ICAO/FAA standards. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Corrections Are Needed The basic runway length is the **equilibrium** between the **lift generated by the wings** and the **weight of the aircraft**. This balance is governed by air density ($\rho$), which in turn depends on **pressure**, **temperature**, and **humidity**. When any of these deviate from standard, the aircraft behaves differently: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Air density decreases with:** (a) increase in altitude, (b) increase in temperature, (c) presence of water vapour (humidity). Reduced air density → reduced lift → reduced engine thrust → longer runway needed. Hence, four primary corrections are applied to the basic runway length $L_0$: 1. **Elevation correction** → accounts for reduced air density at altitude. 2. **Temperature correction** → accounts for higher-than-standard air temperature. 3. **Gradient correction** → accounts for runway slope (uphill takeoffs need more distance). 4. **Wind correction** → accounts for headwind or tailwind component (operational, applied to TOD/LD directly, not as a multiplier on $L_0$). ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Correction ### 2.2.1 Elevation Correction - Above MSL, air is less dense. - Engine thrust drops by approximately **7% per 300 m** of elevation. - Therefore, runway length must be **increased by 7% per 300 m**. $$L_{elev} = L_0 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.07 \times h}{300}\right)$$ where $h$ is the airport elevation above MSL in **metres**. ### 2.2.2 Temperature Correction - Standard temperature decreases with elevation at a rate of **6.5 °C per 1000 m**. $$T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times h \quad [\text{in } ^\circ\text{C}]$$ - Airport reference temperature is usually taken as the **monthly mean of the average daily maximum temperature** in the **hottest month** of the year. $$T_{ref} = T_{std} + \Delta T$$ - Runway length is **increased by 1% for every 1 °C rise** above standard. $$L_{temp} = L_{elev} \times \left(1 + \frac{\Delta T}{100}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > The factor **0.01 per °C** is empirical and prescribed by FAA. Some authors use 0.0055 (1% per 1.8°F) which is mathematically equivalent. ### 2.2.3 Gradient Correction - Effective gradient is the **maximum difference in elevation** along the runway divided by the runway length, expressed as a percentage. $$\text{Gradient \%} = \frac{\Delta E}{L} \times 100$$ - Runway length is **increased by 10% for every 1% of effective gradient**. $$L_{gradient} = L_{temp} \times \left(1 + 0.1 \times g\%\right)$$ ### 2.2.4 Wind Correction (Applied Separately) - For every **knot of headwind component**, the runway length can be **reduced by 2%**, up to a **maximum reduction of 20%** (i.e., 20 knots of headwind). - This is a **direct reduction** on $L_{gradient}$, not a multiplicative correction on TOD/LD. $$L_{final} = L_{gradient} \times (1 - 0.02 \times V_{hw})$$ where $V_{hw}$ is the headwind component in knots (capped at **20 knots**). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table** — every numerical problem in the KTU exam on this topic revolves around these equations. | S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |:-----:|:---------|:--------|:------|:------| | 1 | Basic Runway Length (small a/c) | $L_0 = 0.8 \times \max(TOD, LD)$ | m | Aircraft $< 1500$ kg | | 2 | Basic Runway Length (large a/c) | $L_0 = 1.15 \times TOD$ (all-eng) | m | Aircraft $1500 - 5700$ kg | | 3 | Basic Runway Length (transport) | $L_0 = \max(1.15 \cdot TOD, 1.67 \cdot LD)$ | m | Aircraft $> 5700$ kg | | 4 | Standard Temperature | $T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \cdot h$ | °C | $h$ in metres | | 5 | Elevation Correction Factor | $f_e = 1 + 0.07h/300$ | dimensionless | Multiplied to $L_0$ | | 6 | Temperature Correction Factor | $f_t = 1 + \Delta T / 100$ | dimensionless | $\Delta T = T_{ref} - T_{std}$ | | 7 | Gradient Correction Factor | $f_g = 1 + 0.1 \cdot g\%$ | dimensionless | $g\%$ is the effective gradient | | 8 | Wind Reduction Factor | $f_w = 1 - 0.02 \cdot V_{hw}$ | dimensionless | $V_{hw} \leq 20$ knots | | 9 | Corrected Runway Length | $L = L_0 \cdot f_e \cdot f_t \cdot f_g \cdot f_w$ | m | Final design length | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - Runway length dictates **airport site selection** — hilly stations like **Leh** (Kushok Bakula Rimpochee Airport, elevation 3256 m) need disproportionately long runways. - It governs **fuel payload trade-offs** for airlines — on a hot day at high altitude, airlines must **offload cargo/passengers** to reduce take-off weight. - It is the **first input** to pavement thickness design (subgrade reaction, equivalent single wheel load, etc.). - It determines the **Aerodrome Reference Code (ARC)** number as per ICAO Annex 14, which controls runway width, separation distances, and rescue-firefighting levels. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Cumulative Correction Formula The corrected runway length is obtained by **sequential multiplication** of the basic length by each correction factor. Let us derive the closed-form expression. ### 3.1.1 Starting Point The basic runway length $L_0$ is given (from aircraft performance curves) at standard conditions. Each correction modifies this length to suit actual site conditions. **Step 1 — Apply Elevation Correction** $$L_1 = L_0 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{0.07 h}{300}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Apply Temperature Correction on the elevation-corrected length** $$L_2 = L_1 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\Delta T}{100}\right) = L_0 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{0.07 h}{300}\right) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\Delta T}{100}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Apply Gradient Correction** $$L_3 = L_2 \cdot \left(1 + 0.1 \cdot g\%\right) = L_0 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{0.07 h}{300}\right) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\Delta T}{100}\right) \cdot \left(1 + 0.1 g\%\right)$$ **Step 4 — Apply Wind Correction (reduction)** $$L = L_3 \cdot \left(1 - 0.02 \cdot V_{hw}\right)$$ **Final closed-form expression** $$\boxed{L = L_0 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{0.07 h}{300}\right) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\Delta T}{100}\right) \cdot \left(1 + 0.1 g\%\right) \cdot \left(1 - 0.02 V_{hw}\right)}$$ > Each bracket is **dimensionless** and $> 1$ (except wind, which is $\leq 1$). ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU Numerical Pattern > [!NOTE] > This is the **exact pattern** of numerical expected in KTU Part B questions. Practice the substitution and the unit consistency. ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement The basic runway length of an airport is determined to be **1500 m** for a transport aircraft under standard conditions. The airport is located at an **elevation of 450 m** above MSL. The **monthly mean of the average daily maximum temperature** in the hottest month is **38 °C**. The **effective gradient** of the runway is **0.6%**. A **headwind component of 10 knots** is expected during critical operations. Determine the **corrected runway length** as per FAA standards. ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Given Data** - $L_0 = 1500 \text{ m}$ - $h = 450 \text{ m}$ - $T_{ref} = 38 \text{ °C}$ - $g\% = 0.6$ - $V_{hw} = 10 \text{ knots}$ **Step 1: Compute the Standard Temperature at the Site** $$T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times h$$ $$T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times 450 = 15 - 2.925 = 12.075 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 2: Compute the Temperature Difference** $$\Delta T = T_{ref} - T_{std} = 38 - 12.075 = 25.925 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 3: Compute the Elevation Correction Factor** $$f_e = 1 + \frac{0.07 \times h}{300} = 1 + \frac{0.07 \times 450}{300}$$ $$f_e = 1 + \frac{31.5}{300} = 1 + 0.105 = 1.105$$ **Step 4: Compute the Temperature Correction Factor** $$f_t = 1 + \frac{\Delta T}{100} = 1 + \frac{25.925}{100} = 1 + 0.25925 = 1.25925$$ **Step 5: Compute the Gradient Correction Factor** $$f_g = 1 + 0.1 \times g\% = 1 + 0.1 \times 0.6 = 1.06$$ **Step 6: Compute the Wind Reduction Factor** $$f_w = 1 - 0.02 \times V_{hw} = 1 - 0.02 \times 10 = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$$ **Step 7: Substitute into the Master Equation** $$L = 1500 \times 1.105 \times 1.25925 \times 1.06 \times 0.80$$ Performing the multiplication **one bracket at a time** to avoid arithmetic error: $$1500 \times 1.105 = 1657.5 \text{ m}$$ $$1657.5 \times 1.25925 = 2087.36 \text{ m}$$ $$2087.36 \times 1.06 = 2212.60 \text{ m}$$ $$2212.60 \times 0.80 = 1770.08 \text{ m}$$ **Step 8: Final Answer (Rounded Up Practically)** $$\boxed{L \approx 1771 \text{ m (say, 1800 m after rounding to practical design value)}}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Impact of Each Correction (Sensitivity Analysis) The table below illustrates how the corrected length changes when **one variable** is varied while others are held constant. The base case is the worked example above. | Variable | Base Value | Increased To | Resulting $L$ (m) | Percentage Change | |:---------|:----------:|:------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:| | Elevation $h$ | 450 m | 900 m | 1984 | +12.1% | | Temp $\Delta T$ | 25.925 °C | 35.925 °C | 1876 | +6.0% | | Gradient $g\%$ | 0.6 | 1.2 | 1903 | +7.5% | | Wind $V_{hw}$ | 10 kt | 20 kt (max) | 1770 → 1328 | -25.0% | > [!IMPORTANT] > The single most dominant correction in most KTU problems is the **temperature correction** because the difference $T_{ref} - T_{std}$ can be as large as 30 °C in Indian airports. ## 3.4 Special Case — Headwind on TOD vs LD (ICAO Method) > [!WARNING] > Some ICAO-based problems treat wind correction **differently** — by modifying the take-off run rather than the corrected length. Always check whether the problem uses **FAA methodology (F = correction on final length)** or **ICAO methodology (F = correction on TOD directly)**. In the **ICAO method**, wind correction is applied to the take-off distance itself: $$TOD_{corrected} = TOD_{std} \times \left(1 - \frac{V_{hw}}{V_{TO}}\right)$$ where $V_{TO}$ is the lift-off speed. This is more theoretical and rarely appears in KTU numericals; the FAA method above is the **syllabus-standard** one. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Runway Length Determination Workflow The following Mermaid flow diagram represents the **complete procedure** for determining the corrected runway length from aircraft performance data. Each block is a functional stage with its output feeding the next stage. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Identify Critical Aircraft] --> B[Collect Performance Data] B --> C[Fetch TOD from Manufacturer Chart] B --> D[Fetch LD from Manufacturer Chart] C --> E{Apply Safety Multiplier} D --> F{Apply Safety Multiplier} E --> G[Compute Basic Runway Length L0] F --> G G --> H[Read Airport Site Parameters] H --> I[Elevation h in metres] H --> J[Reference Temperature Tref] H --> K[Effective Gradient g percent] H --> L[Headwind Component Vhw knots] I --> M[Compute Elevation Factor fe] J --> N[Compute Standard Tstd at h] N --> O[Compute Delta T = Tref - Tstd] O --> P[Compute Temperature Factor ft] K --> Q[Compute Gradient Factor fg] L --> R[Compute Wind Factor fw] M --> S[Multiply All Factors with L0] P --> S Q --> S R --> S S --> T[Output Corrected Runway Length L] T --> U[Round Up to Practical Design Value] U --> V[END: Final Design Runway Length] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Inputs vs Outputs of Each Correction The diagram below shows the **interactions** between correction factors and emphasises that **elevation, temperature, and gradient corrections are multiplicative**, while **wind correction is a reduction**. ```mermaid graph LR L0[Basic Length L0] --> EL[Elevation Correction fe] EL --> TP[Temperature Correction ft] TP --> GR[Gradient Correction fg] GR --> WD[Wind Reduction fw] WD --> OUT[Corrected Length L] H[Elevation h] --> fe1[fe = 1 + 0.07h/300] fe1 --> EL TREF[Ref Temp Tref] --> fe2[Compute Tstd from h] fe2 --> fe3[Delta T = Tref - Tstd] fe3 --> fe4[ft = 1 + Delta T/100] fe4 --> TP GVAL[Gradient g percent] --> fe5[fg = 1 + 0.1 x g] fe5 --> GR VW[Headwind Vhw knots] --> fe6[fw = 1 - 0.02 x Vhw] fe6 --> WD ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Sequential Multiplication Order ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant L0 as Basic Length L0 participant Elev as Elevation Factor participant Temp as Temperature Factor participant Grad as Gradient Factor participant Wind as Wind Factor participant Final as Final Length L L0->>Elev: L0 multiplied by fe Elev->>Temp: Result multiplied by ft Temp->>Grad: Result multiplied by fg Grad->>Wind: Result multiplied by fw Wind->>Final: Result is L Note over Final: Round up to nearest 50 m or 100 m
for practical construction ``` > [!NOTE] > The order of multiplication is **mathematically irrelevant** (commutativity of multiplication), but the **step-by-step** order shown above is the conventional KTU-valuation-friendly approach because each intermediate result is a tangible length that can be checked for physical reasonableness (must be greater than $L_0$ after non-wind corrections). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define basic runway length. List the standard reference conditions assumed while computing the basic runway length.** **Model Answer:** > **Basic Runway Length** is the length of runway required for the **critical aircraft** to take off or land under **standard atmospheric conditions**, multiplied by the **statutory safety factor** prescribed by FAA/ICAO. > > **Standard Reference Conditions** (any four, ½ mark each): > > 1. **Mean Sea Level (MSL) elevation** — atmospheric pressure of **1013.25 hPa**. > 2. **Standard temperature** at MSL = **15 °C** with lapse rate **6.5 °C/km**. > 3. **No wind** (calm air) on the runway. > 4. **Zero runway gradient** (perfectly level runway). > 5. Maximum **headwind component of 20 knots** is the cap for operational length reduction. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: State the FAA formula for elevation and temperature correction of runway length, and explain the physical significance of each correction factor.** **Model Answer:** > **Elevation Correction:** > $$L_{elev} = L_0 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.07 h}{300}\right)$$ > > **Physical Significance:** With increase in elevation, **air density decreases**, leading to **reduced engine thrust** and **reduced lift on wings**. To compensate, the aircraft needs **more ground roll**. The factor **7% per 300 m** is empirical, derived from jet engine performance degradation. > > **Temperature Correction:** > $$L_{temp} = L_{elev} \times \left(1 + \frac{\Delta T}{100}\right)$$ > > **Physical Significance:** Hot air is **less dense** than cold air (for the same pressure). Higher temperature → **less oxygen** for combustion, **lower engine thrust**, and **reduced wing lift**. A **1% increase in length per 1 °C** rise accounts for this combined effect. **(½ mark for each formula, ½ mark for each physical explanation, 1 mark for combined delivery.)** ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Q(a)** (7 Marks): An airport is to be designed at an elevation of **600 m** above MSL. The **monthly mean of average daily maximum temperature** for the hottest month at the site is **42 °C**. The **effective gradient** of the proposed runway is **0.8%**. A headwind of **15 knots** is expected at the time of critical operations. If the **basic runway length** required for the design aircraft is **1800 m** under standard conditions, determine the **corrected runway length** as per FAA recommendations. **Model Solution:** **Given:** $L_0 = 1800 \text{ m}$, $h = 600 \text{ m}$, $T_{ref} = 42 \text{ °C}$, $g\% = 0.8$, $V_{hw} = 15 \text{ knots}$. **Step 1 — Standard temperature at site:** $$T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times 600 = 15 - 3.9 = 11.1 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 2 — Temperature excess:** $$\Delta T = 42 - 11.1 = 30.9 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 3 — Elevation factor:** $$f_e = 1 + \frac{0.07 \times 600}{300} = 1 + 0.14 = 1.14$$ **Step 4 — Temperature factor:** $$f_t = 1 + \frac{30.9}{100} = 1.309$$ **Step 5 — Gradient factor:** $$f_g = 1 + 0.1 \times 0.8 = 1.08$$ **Step 6 — Wind factor:** $$f_w = 1 - 0.02 \times 15 = 0.70$$ **Step 7 — Apply the master equation:** $$L = 1800 \times 1.14 \times 1.309 \times 1.08 \times 0.70$$ Sequential multiplication: $$1800 \times 1.14 = 2052.0 \text{ m}$$ $$2052.0 \times 1.309 = 2686.07 \text{ m}$$ $$2686.07 \times 1.08 = 2900.96 \text{ m}$$ $$2900.96 \times 0.70 = 2030.67 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{L \approx 2031 \text{ m (round up to 2050 m for practical design)}}$$ > **(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for $T_{std}$, 1 mark for $\Delta T$, 1 mark each for the four factors, 1 mark for substitution, 2 marks for final arithmetic.)** **Q(b)** (7 Marks): Explain with neat sketches the **different categories of critical aircraft** as per FAA AC 150/5325-4B and the corresponding method of computing basic runway length for each. **Model Answer:** | S.No. | Category | Gross Take-Off Weight | Basic Runway Length Formula | Governing Distance | |:-----:|:---------|:---------------------:|:----------------------------|:-------------------| | 1 | Small | $< 1500 \text{ kg}$ | $L_0 = 0.8 \times \max(TOD, LD)$ | Greater of take-off or landing | | 2 | Large (light twins) | $1500 - 5700 \text{ kg}$ | $L_0 = 1.15 \times TOD$ | Take-off dominates | | 3 | Transport | $> 5700 \text{ kg}$ | $L_0 = \max(1.15 \cdot TOD, 1.67 \cdot LD)$ | Whichever is greater | **Explanation (1 mark per category + 1 mark for each formula derivation):** - For **small aircraft**, only 80% of the greater of take-off or landing distance is used because these aircraft operate from shorter strips and have higher climb gradients. - For **large aircraft**, the 15% safety factor on the take-off distance accounts for engine-out scenarios and the need to clear the runway end with a positive climb gradient. - For **transport aircraft**, the 1.15 factor on TOD covers all-engine and engine-out take-off, while the **1.67 factor on landing distance** provides a substantial safety margin for aborted landings and approach speed variations. > **(3 marks for the table, 4 marks for the explanation of safety factors and physical reasoning.)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q(a)** (7 Marks): Discuss in detail the **physical significance of the four correction factors** (elevation, temperature, gradient, and wind) applied to the basic runway length. State the **standard reference conditions** for each. **Model Answer:** > **1. Elevation Correction (2 marks)** > > **Standard reference:** Sea level (MSL), pressure 1013.25 hPa. > > **Physical significance:** At higher altitudes, the atmosphere is **less dense**, resulting in: > - **Reduced engine thrust** (less mass of air ingested per combustion cycle). > - **Reduced wing lift** (lift is proportional to $\frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$). > - **Higher true airspeed required** for the same indicated airspeed. > > The empirical factor of **7% per 300 m** (FAA) reflects the combined degradation of jet engine performance with altitude. > **2. Temperature Correction (2 marks)** > > **Standard reference:** 15 °C at MSL, lapsing at 6.5 °C per km. > > **Physical significance:** Hot air is **less dense** at the same pressure. An increase in temperature: > - **Reduces engine power output** (piston, turbojet, and turbofan all suffer). > - **Reduces lift on wings** during take-off roll. > - **Increases runway length** to achieve lift-off speed. > > A correction of **1% per 1 °C rise above standard** is applied. > **3. Gradient Correction (1.5 marks)** > > **Standard reference:** Zero gradient (perfectly level runway). > > **Physical significance:** An uphill gradient opposes the aircraft's forward motion during the take-off roll, requiring **more thrust for longer** to reach lift-off speed. The empirical factor of **10% per 1% gradient** is based on operational data. > **4. Wind Correction (1.5 marks)** > > **Standard reference:** Calm air (zero wind). > > **Physical significance:** A **headwind** reduces the aircraft's **ground speed** for a given **airspeed**, thereby shortening the ground roll. **Tailwinds** increase the required length but are usually prohibited for take-off above a threshold. The **2% per knot reduction** is applied up to a **20-knot headwind cap**. **Q(b)** (7 Marks): A **transport aircraft** has a **take-off distance of 1500 m** and a **landing distance of 1100 m** under standard conditions at MSL. If the airport is to be built at an **elevation of 750 m** with a **temperature excess of 25 °C** and an **effective gradient of 0.5%**, compute the **basic runway length** and the **final corrected runway length**. Assume a headwind component of **10 knots**. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Basic Runway Length for Transport Aircraft:** $$L_0 = \max(1.15 \times TOD, 1.67 \times LD)$$ $$1.15 \times 1500 = 1725 \text{ m}$$ $$1.67 \times 1100 = 1837 \text{ m}$$ The landing distance term governs: $$L_0 = 1837 \text{ m (the longer of the two)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply corrections:** - **Elevation factor:** $f_e = 1 + 0.07 \times 750 / 300 = 1 + 0.175 = 1.175$ - **Temperature factor:** $f_t = 1 + 25/100 = 1.25$ - **Gradient factor:** $f_g = 1 + 0.1 \times 0.5 = 1.05$ - **Wind factor:** $f_w = 1 - 0.02 \times 10 = 0.80$ **Step 3 — Compute final length:** $$L = 1837 \times 1.175 \times 1.25 \times 1.05 \times 0.80$$ $$1837 \times 1.175 = 2158.475 \text{ m}$$ $$2158.475 \times 1.25 = 2698.09 \text{ m}$$ $$2698.09 \times 1.05 = 2833.00 \text{ m}$$ $$2833.00 \times 0.80 = 2266.40 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{L \approx 2267 \text{ m (round up to 2300 m for design)}}$$ > **(Mark distribution: 1 mark each for $L_0$ calculation steps, ½ mark for identifying the governing case, 1 mark each for the four correction factors, 2 marks for the final arithmetic.)** ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — Read Carefully** > > 1. **Standard temperature lapse rate is 6.5 °C per 1000 m, NOT 1 °C per 100 m.** Many students erroneously use 0.01 °C/m and arrive at $T_{std} = 8.5$ °C at 1000 m instead of the correct 8.5 °C. (Lapse rate is 0.0065 °C/m.) This error can change $\Delta T$ by 5–10 °C and inflate the corrected length by 5–10%. > > 2. **Wind correction is a REDUCTION, not a multiplier on $L_0$ above 1.** Some students set $f_w = 1 + 0.02 \times V_{hw}$, which is the **opposite** of the correct formula. Always remember: headwind **helps**, tailwind **hinders**. > > 3. **For transport aircraft, the basic length is the MAXIMUM of 1.15 × TOD and 1.67 × LD, not the sum or average.** Computing the sum yields a length 2–3 times the actual requirement — a guaranteed mark-zero. > > 4. **The 20-knot cap on headwind is critical.** If a problem states a 30-knot headwind, do NOT use $f_w = 0.40$; cap it at $f_w = 0.60$ (corresponding to 20 knots). FAA regulations prohibit the use of a headwind correction beyond this cap. > > 5. **Effective gradient, not slope ratio alone.** Some students take the elevation difference $\Delta E$ in metres and divide by runway length, forgetting to multiply by 100. The gradient must be in **percentage**. > > 6. **Unit consistency in $h$.** Most problems give elevation in **metres**; if given in **feet**, convert: $1 \text{ ft} = 0.3048 \text{ m}$. Failure to do so changes the elevation factor dramatically. > > 7. **Do not "forget" the temperature step.** Elevation alone changes $T_{std}$, and many students use $T_{ref} - 15$ °C (MSL standard) instead of $T_{ref} - T_{std}$ (site-specific standard). This is a common 2-mark deduction. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Print this section and keep it ready for last-minute revision before the KTU exam.** - **Basic Runway Length** = length under **standard conditions** (MSL, 15 °C, calm, level). - **Critical Aircraft** = the design aircraft imposing the **most demanding** runway requirement. - **FAA Categories** → Small (use 0.8×max), Large (1.15×TOD), Transport (max of 1.15×TOD, 1.67×LD). - **Standard Temperature** → $T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065h$ where $h$ is elevation in **metres**. - **Elevation Correction** → increase $L_0$ by **7% per 300 m** of elevation. - **Temperature Correction** → increase $L_{elev}$ by **1% per 1 °C** of $\Delta T$ above standard. - **Gradient Correction** → increase $L_{temp}$ by **10% per 1% of effective gradient**. - **Wind Correction** → **reduce** $L_{gradient}$ by **2% per knot of headwind**, capped at 20 knots. - **Order of multiplication** is **commutative** but conventionally: $L_0 \rightarrow f_e \rightarrow f_t \rightarrow f_g \rightarrow f_w$. - **Practical rounding** → round the final answer **up** to the nearest 50 m or 100 m for constructability. - **Maximum practical reduction from wind** is **20%**; never apply more even if the headwind exceeds 20 knots. - **ICAO method vs FAA method** → ICAO applies wind correction on TOD directly; FAA applies it as a multiplier on the corrected length. **Default to FAA** unless the problem explicitly says "ICAO". - **Indian airports** typically use **DGCA-CAR**, which is procedurally identical to FAA AC 150/5325-4B. - **Runway length governs** the **Aerodrome Reference Code (ARC) number** — this controls runway width, taxiway width, separation distances, and rescue-firefighting levels. - **High-altitude airports** (Leh, Lhasa, Cusco) experience the **largest combined elevation + temperature correction** — often the corrected length is 40–60% greater than the basic length. - **Always check if the headwind is in knots or km/h** — $1 \text{ knot} = 0.5144 \text{ m/s} = 1.852 \text{ km/h}$.

topics.title: Runway Configurations, Threshold Limits, and Cross Section of Runway
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Runway Configurations, Threshold Limits, and Cross Section of Runway

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Runway Configuration — The Airport's Geometric Blueprint

A **runway configuration** refers to the number, orientation, and geometric arrangement of runways at an aerodrome. It is the single most important geometric decision in airport master planning because it dictates the airport's ultimate **hourly capacity**, **wind coverage capability**, **operational flexibility**, and **land-area footprint**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ICAO Definition (Annex 14, Vol. I):** A runway is *"a defined rectangular area on a land aerodrome prepared for the landing and take-off run of aircraft along its length."*

The configuration is chosen such that aircraft can take off and land **as nearly as possible into the wind** without exceeding the permissible **cross-wind component** (typically **20 knots** for dry runways; **13 knots** when the runway is contaminated with snow/slush).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Junction"

Imagine a busy highway interchange. A **single two-way road** (single runway) handles limited traffic and is highly vulnerable to wind-driven congestion. Adding a parallel bridge (parallel runways) doubles capacity. An interchange with crossing roads (intersecting runways) provides route redundancy. A Y-junction (V-shape) lets you funnel traffic from two directions. Runway configurations work exactly the same way — they are *highway junctions in the sky*, with the wind acting as the traffic police.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wind Rose Coverage Rule of Thumb:** A single runway covers about **95%** of winds for a given cross-wind limit. Two runways (with suitable orientation) cover approximately **98–99%** of all wind conditions.

## 1.2 Threshold Limits — Where the Landing Begins

A **threshold** is the *beginning of the portion of the runway usable for landing*. It is usually located at the beginning of the paved surface, but may be **displaced** (shifted down the runway) when obstacles in the approach path make it unsafe to land at the physical start.

**Threshold Limits** (more correctly, *Threshold Siting Requirements*) define the *maximum distance* the threshold may be displaced from the runway end while still maintaining the required **Obstacle Limitation Surface (OLS)** slopes and approach minima.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Rule:** A displaced threshold **reduces the Landing Distance Available (LDA)** but **does NOT reduce the Take-Off Distance Available (TODA)**. This is one of the most-tested KTU concepts.

## 1.3 Cross Section of Runway — The Engineering Anatomy

The **cross-section of a runway** is the *transverse vertical profile* taken perpendicular to the runway centerline. It includes:

1. The **runway pavement** (the paved landing surface).
2. The **runway crown** (the highest point along the centerline, used for drainage).
3. **Transverse slopes** (camber) sloping down from the crown to the edges.
4. **Runway shoulders** (paved or unpaved side strips).
5. The **runway strip** (the graded area surrounding the runway).
6. The **Runway End Safety Area (RESA)** and **blast pad** at runway ends.
7. **Drainage channels** along the edges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Crown Slope (ICAO/FAA):** The transverse slope of a runway is typically between **1.0% and 1.5%**, with a maximum of **2.0%** permitted to prevent ponding. The runway is crowned at the centerline so water drains off both sides.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Roof of a House"

Picture a flat-roofed house. The roof is slightly higher in the middle (crown) so rainwater runs off to both sides into gutters (drainage channels). The roof itself is the runway pavement, the eaves are the shoulders, the yard is the runway strip, and the front lawn extending past the wall is the RESA. Just as a roof cannot be perfectly flat (water would pool), a runway cannot be perfectly flat — it needs a minimum cross-fall for safety and drainage.

---

## Quick Reference — Standard ICAO Runway Code System

| Parameter | Description | Standard Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Code Number (1–4)** | Based on Aerodrome Reference Field Length (ARFL) | 1: <800m, 2: 800–1199m, 3: 1200–1799m, 4: ≥1800m |
| **Code Letter (A–F)** | Based on Aircraft Reference Wing Span / Outer Main Gear Span | A: <15m, B: 15–23m, C: 24–35m, D: 36–51m, E: 52–64m, F: 65–79m |
| **Cross-wind limit** | Max permissible crosswind component | 20 kt (dry), 13 kt (wet) |
| **Crown slope** | Transverse camber | **1.0% – 1.5%** (max 2.0%) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Runway Configurations

### A. Single Runway
- **Capacity:** 30–45 operations/hour (VFR) / 25–35 (IFR)
- **Wind coverage:** ~95% (with proper orientation)
- **Best suited for:** Small to medium airports
- **Advantage:** Simple, low land requirement, low construction cost.
- **Disadvantage:** No redundancy; one incident closes the airport.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** When asked the *capacity* of a single runway, the universally accepted value is **30 to 45 movements per hour** depending on the mix of arrivals and departures.

### B. Parallel Runways
Two (or more) runways oriented **parallel** to each other.

| Type | Centerline Separation | Capacity (approx.) |
|---|---|---|
| **Close parallel** | 700 ft (213 m) – 2,500 ft (762 m) | 60–70 ops/hr (dependent ops) |
| **Intermediate parallel** | 2,500 ft (762 m) – 4,300 ft (1,310 m) | 70–90 ops/hr (independent) |
| **Far parallel** | > 4,300 ft (1,310 m) | 90–100+ ops/hr (independent) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Independent Parallel Approaches** (centers ≥ 1,310 m / 4,300 ft) allow simultaneous approaches — the hallmark of a Category-III-capable hub airport (e.g., Dubai, Delhi, Frankfurt).

### C. Intersecting Runways
- Two runways that **cross each other** (typically 90°, sometimes 60°–90°).
- **Advantage:** Better wind coverage from multiple directions; redundancy.
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot operate both runways simultaneously when aircraft are near the intersection; complex taxiing.
- **Capacity:** ~60 operations/hour at best.

### D. Open-V / V-Shape Configuration
- Two runways diverging from a common point at an angle of **15°–40°** (open V) or sharing a common threshold (V).
- Used where **prevailing wind direction** has two dominant components.
- **Capacity:** ~50–60 ops/hr; better than intersecting because operations don't physically conflict except near the apex.

### E. Other Configurations
- **Multiple parallel with connectors** (used at Atlanta, Kansai).
- **Tandem** (two runways one behind the other — rare, used at Seoul Gimpo historically).
- **Combination** (parallel + crosswind) — most major hubs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Heuristic:** The **number of runways** required for a target demand *N* (ops/hr) can be approximated by:
> $$N_{runways} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Peak Hour Demand}}{\text{Capacity per Runway}} \right\rceil$$
> For example, a hub needing 120 ops/hr with 60 ops/hr per runway needs **2 parallel runways** with independent operations.

---

## 2.2 Threshold Limits — The Math Behind Displacement

### 2.2.1 Approach Surface Slopes (ICAO Annex 14)

The **Approach Surface** is an inclined plane extending outward from the threshold. Obstacles must NOT penetrate it.

| Runway Type | First Section Slope | Second Section Slope | Length of First Section |
|---|---|---|---|
| Non-instrument approach | **1:50 (2.0%)** | — | 3,000 m |
| Non-precision approach | **1:40 (2.5%)** | 1:50 (2.0%) | 3,000 m |
| Precision approach Cat I | **1:40 (2.5%)** | 1:50 (2.0%) | 3,000 m |
| Precision approach Cat II/III | **1:40 (2.5%)** | 1:50 (2.0%) | 3,000 m |

### 2.2.2 Threshold Siting Criteria

The threshold must be sited such that the **Approach Surface** is clear of obstacles. If an obstacle penetrates, the threshold is **displaced** by a distance *D* so the obstacle lies *just below* the approach surface.

**Core formula for threshold displacement:**

$$D = \frac{h - h_{clearance}}{s}$$

where:
- $D$ = displacement distance of the threshold (m)
- $h$ = height of the controlling obstacle above the threshold level (m)
- $h_{clearance}$ = required vertical clearance at threshold (typically **0 m** for approach surface start)
- $s$ = slope of the approach surface (as a decimal: 1/50 = 0.02, 1/40 = 0.025)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ICAO Rule:** Threshold displacement must be such that **no obstacle penetrates the Approach Surface, Transitional Surface, Inner Horizontal Surface, or Conical Surface** up to the inner edge of the approach surface.

### 2.2.3 Displaced Threshold Effects on Declared Distances

| Declared Distance | Effect of Displaced Threshold |
|---|---|
| **TORA** (Take-Off Run Available) | **Unaffected** |
| **TODA** (Take-Off Distance Available) | **Unaffected** |
| **ASDA** (Accelerate-Stop Distance Available) | **Unaffected** |
| **LDA** (Landing Distance Available) | **Reduced by displacement** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "displacement reduces TORA". It does NOT. Only **LDA** decreases. The threshold markers (piano keys) you see at a displaced threshold are **white arrows pointing into the runway** — they tell pilots: *"You may use this surface for takeoff roll, but you may not land here."*

---

## 2.3 Cross Section of Runway — Geometric Specifications

### 2.3.1 Runway Width

Runway width is a function of the **Code Letter** (Aircraft Wing Span / Gear Span).

| Code Letter | Wing Span | Outer MG Span | Runway Width |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | < 15 m | < 4.5 m | 18 m |
| **B** | 15 – 24 m | 4.5 – 6 m | 23 m |
| **C** | 24 – 36 m | 6 – 9 m | 30 m |
| **D** | 36 – 52 m | 9 – 14 m | 45 m |
| **E** | 52 – 65 m | 9 – 14 m | 45 m |
| **F** | 65 – 80 m | 14 – 16 m | 60 m |

### 2.3.2 Transverse Slope (Camber)

The runway is crowned at the **centerline**, falling symmetrically to both edges.

$$\text{Crown Height} = \frac{W}{2} \times q$$

where:
- $W$ = total runway width (m)
- $q$ = transverse slope (as a decimal, e.g., 0.015 for 1.5%)

**Example:** For a Code D runway, $W = 45$ m, with $q = 0.015$:

$$\text{Crown Height} = \frac{45}{2} \times 0.015 = 0.3375 \text{ m} \approx 34 \text{ cm}$$

### 2.3.3 Runway Strip and Shoulders

- **Runway Strip** = Cleared and graded area surrounding the runway, extending at least:
  - **60 m** on each side of centerline for Code 3/4 runways (150 m for precision approach Cat II/III).
  - **60 m** beyond runway end (RESA included in some standards).
- **Runway Shoulder** = The portion of the runway strip immediately adjacent to the runway edge, paved to support jet blast and prevent erosion. Minimum width: **1.5 m** each side (FAA), wider for jet aircraft.

### 2.3.4 Runway End Safety Area (RESA)

- Symmetrical area beyond the runway end.
- Length: **minimum 90 m** (ICAO standard; ideally **240 m** or longer).
- Width: **twice the runway width**.
- Designed to arrest aircraft that overrun or undershoot.

### 2.3.5 Blast Pad

- Paved area preceding the runway threshold (or at the departure end).
- Length: **60 m** for small aircraft, **120 m** for larger jets.
- Designed to resist **jet blast erosion** during takeoff and landing roll.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wind Coverage | $\% Coverage = \frac{\text{Hours wind within crosswind limit}}{\text{Total hours}} \times 100$ | Should be ≥ 95% |
| 2 | Threshold Displacement | $D = \dfrac{h - h_c}{s}$ | $h$ = obstacle height, $s$ = approach slope (1/40 or 1/50) |
| 3 | Crown Height | $H_c = \dfrac{W}{2} \times q$ | $W$ = width, $q$ = camber |
| 4 | Effective Gradient | $G_{eff} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L} \times 100\%$ | Longitudinal slope, max 1.0% (Code 3/4) |
| 5 | Required Number of Runways | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{D_{peak}}{C} \right\rceil$ | $D$ = demand, $C$ = per-runway capacity |
| 6 | Cross-wind Component | $V_{cross} = V_{wind} \times \sin(\theta)$ | $\theta$ = angle between wind and runway |
| 7 | Longitudinal Slope Change | $ \Delta G \leq 1.5\% $ per 30 m (Code 3/4) | Vertical curve radius rules |
| 8 | Declared Distances (with displaced threshold) | $LDA = TORA - D$ | $D$ = displacement; TORA unchanged |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear in **KTU's Transport Engineering Electives** and **Airport Consultancy Reports**. Real-world use includes sizing new runways (crown height), evaluating approach obstructions (displacement), and master planning (number of runways).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Threshold Displacement from an Obstacle

### Problem Setup
A Code 4 precision approach runway (Category I) has a hill rising to **25 m** above the runway threshold elevation. The hill is located **900 m** from the threshold, along the extended centerline. The approach surface has a slope of **1:40 (2.5%)** for the first 3,000 m, then **1:50 (2.0%)** beyond. Determine the **threshold displacement**.

### Step 1 — Verify Which Section the Obstacle Falls In

The hill is at 900 m from the threshold. Since 900 m < 3,000 m, the obstacle lies within the **first section** of the approach surface, which has a slope of 1:40.

### Step 2 — Set Up the Geometry

The approach surface begins at the threshold with zero elevation (at threshold level) and rises outward with slope $s_1 = 1/40 = 0.025$.

At a horizontal distance $x$ from the threshold, the maximum permitted **approach surface elevation** above threshold is:

$$y_{surface}(x) = s_1 \times x$$

### Step 3 — Compare Obstacle Height to Surface Height

At $x = 900$ m, the maximum permitted surface height is:

$$y_{surface}(900) = 0.025 \times 900 = 22.5 \text{ m}$$

The hill rises to **25 m**, so it penetrates the approach surface by:

$$\Delta h = 25 - 22.5 = 2.5 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Threshold Displacement

We shift the threshold by $D$ meters **toward the runway end**, which effectively shifts the entire approach surface *away* from the hill by $D$. The approach surface at the obstacle's location now starts $D$ meters later, so the new permitted height at the obstacle location becomes:

$$y'_{surface}(900) = s_1 \times (900 - D)$$

Setting this equal to the obstacle height to clear the surface:

$$0.025 \times (900 - D) = 25$$

$$900 - D = \frac{25}{0.025} = 1000$$

$$D = 900 - 1000 = -100 \text{ m}$$

A *negative* displacement means the obstacle is too high to be cleared even if the threshold is at the runway end (i.e., the surface would still be hit). This means **no threshold location is possible within the standard first section geometry** — the obstacle cannot be cleared. We must either **reduce obstacle height** (cut the hill), **use the second section slope (1:50) from a different starting point**, or **reclassify the approach** to non-precision.

### Step 5 — Recalculate Using Combined Slopes (Engineering Compromise)

Suppose we accept a *transition* at $x = 1000$ m (a new "effective first section"). Then within 0–1000 m, slope is 1:40; beyond, 1:50.

For the obstacle at 900 m:

$$y_{surface}(900) = 0.025 \times 900 = 22.5 \text{ m} \quad \text{(still 25 m obstacle)}$$

So even this workaround fails. **Engineering conclusion:** The hill must be **lowered to 22.5 m or less** for the approach to be feasible, or the approach downgraded.

### Step 6 — Retain the Displacement Concept with a Smaller Obstacle

Suppose the hill height is **20 m** (instead of 25 m). Recalculate:

$$0.025 \times (900 - D) = 20$$

$$900 - D = \frac{20}{0.025} = 800$$

$$D = 900 - 800 = 100 \text{ m}$$

**Threshold must be displaced by 100 m inward.** This means:
- $\text{LDA} = \text{TORA} - 100$ m
- $\text{TORA, TODA, ASDA}$ are unaffected.

### Final Model Solution Summary

$$\boxed{D = 100 \text{ m (for } h = 20 \text{ m, slope 1:40)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget that **threshold displacement is calculated based on clearing the approach surface**, NOT simply by the height of the obstacle. The slope of the approach surface is the determining factor. Also, students forget to convert slopes correctly: 1:40 = 0.025 (NOT 0.4 or 40).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Crown Height for a Code E Runway

A Code E runway (used by Boeing 777, A330) has width **W = 45 m** and design camber **q = 1.5%**.

Step 1: Identify the half-width:
$$\frac{W}{2} = \frac{45}{2} = 22.5 \text{ m}$$

Step 2: Apply the crown height formula:
$$H_c = \frac{W}{2} \times q$$

$$H_c = 22.5 \times 0.015$$

$$\boxed{H_c = 0.3375 \text{ m} = 33.75 \text{ cm}}$$

The centerline is **33.75 cm higher than the runway edges**. This small camber is critical: it ensures rainwater flows off the runway in under 15 minutes (ICAO drainage requirement) without imposing dangerous roll moments on landing aircraft.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Configuration Selection Logic

The following matrix helps determine the appropriate configuration for a given airport.

| Demand (Annual Pax) | Peak Hour Ops | Configurations Suitable | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 1 million | < 15 | Single runway | Cost-effective; small regional airport |
| 1 – 5 million | 15 – 35 | Single runway or Open-V | Add crosswind runway if wind coverage < 95% |
| 5 – 20 million | 35 – 75 | Parallel (close or intermediate) | Begin independent operations |
| 20 – 50 million | 75 – 120 | Parallel + crosswind | Hub airport with redundancy |
| > 50 million | > 120 | Multiple parallel / multi-field | Mega-hubs (Atlanta, Beijing-Daxing, Dubai) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Heuristic (KTU Exam Tip):** If asked *"How many runways does airport X need?"*, follow this logic:
> 1. Compute peak hour demand from annual passengers (Pax × 0.0003 ≈ peak hr demand).
> 2. Divide by per-runway capacity (~45 ops/hr single, ~80–100 parallel independent).
> 3. Round up.
> 4. Check wind coverage — add crosswind if needed.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Number of Runways for a New Greenfield Airport

A new airport expects **35 million passengers/year** and will operate as a Code 4E hub. Each runway can handle **45 movements/hour (single runway, IFR mixed operations)**.

**Step 1:** Estimate peak hour demand using the formula:

$$D_{peak} = 0.0003 \times \text{Annual Pax}$$

$$D_{peak} = 0.0003 \times 35{,}000{,}000 = 10{,}500 \text{ ops/year}$$

Convert to peak hour (rule: peak hour ≈ 1/3000 of annual):

$$D_{peak-hour} = \frac{10{,}500}{3000} \approx 35 \text{ ops/hour}$$

**Step 2:** Required number of runways:

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{35}{45} \right\rceil = 1$$

Wait — this is one runway, but with **95% wind coverage** we may still need a **crosswind strip** for full all-weather operations.

**Step 3:** Engineering decision: Provide **one main runway + one crosswind runway (intersecting)** to:
- Cover the remaining 5% winds.
- Provide redundancy for the main runway's maintenance/closures.

$$\boxed{\text{Final Configuration: One main + one intersecting crosswind runway}}$$

This is a classic KTU numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Runway Configuration Decision Flow

The following block-flow architecture maps the logic an airport planner follows when selecting a runway configuration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Site Identified] --> node1[Compute Annual Demand]
    node1 --> node2[Compute Peak Hour Demand]
    node2 --> node3[Select Crosswind Limit 20 kt]
    node3 --> node4[Plot Wind Rose for 5 to 10 yrs]
    node4 --> node5{Is Wind Coverage 95 percent}
    node5 -->|Yes| node6[Single Runway Considered]
    node5 -->|No| node7[Add Crosswind Runway]
    node6 --> node8{Peak Hour Demand less than 45}
    node8 -->|Yes| node9[Adopt Single Runway]
    node8 -->|No| node10[Adopt Parallel Configuration]
    node10 --> node11{Centerline Separation}
    node11 -->|213 to 762 m| node12[Close Parallel Dependent Ops]
    node11 -->|762 to 1310 m| node13[Intermediate Parallel]
    node11 -->|over 1310 m| node14[Far Parallel Independent Ops]
    node7 --> node15[Intersecting or Open V Shape]
    node12 --> node16[Final Layout with Strips and RESA]
    node13 --> node16
    node14 --> node16
    node15 --> node16
    node9 --> node16
    node16[End: Configuration Locked]
```

---

## 4.2 Runway Cross Section — Functional Architecture Block Diagram

The following diagram maps the **transverse elements** of a runway cross-section, from centerline to outer edge.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CS[RUNWAY CROSS SECTION TRANSVERSE VIEW]
        nodeA[Centerline Crown Highest Point] --- nodeB[Inner 1/2 Width Paved Surface]
        nodeB --- nodeC[Runway Edge Painted Line]
        nodeC --- nodeD[Shoulder Paved or Unpaved]
        nodeD --- nodeE[Runway Strip Edge 75 m Offset]
        nodeE --- nodeF[Drainage Channel French Drain]
        nodeF --- nodeG[Cleared Graded Area No Obstacles]
    end
    nodeA -.camber 1 to 1.5 percent.-> nodeC
    nodeD --> note1[Width 1.5 m minimum FAA]
    nodeE --> note2[Strip 150 m wide for Precision Approach]
```

---

## 4.3 Threshold and Displaced Threshold Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TS[THRESOLD SITING GEOMETRY]
        n1[Runway Physical Start] -->|Displacement D| n2[Displaced Threshold Piano Keys]
        n2 --> n3[Runway Centerline Length]
        n3 --> n4[Runway Physical End]
        n1 -.approach surface slope 1 to 40.-> n5[Obstacle Penetration Point]
        n2 -.new surface origin.-> n5
    end
    n2 --> noteA[White Arrow Markings]
    n4 --> noteB[RESA 90 to 240 m Beyond]
```

---

## 4.4 Declared Distances Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startD[Displaced Threshold Located D m from Runway End] --> tora[TORA Take Off Run Available UNCHANGED]
    startD --> toda[TODA Take Off Distance Available UNCHANGED]
    startD --> asda[ASDA Accelerate Stop Distance UNCHANGED]
    startD --> lda[LDA Landing Distance Available REDUCED by D]
    lda --> noteFinal[LDA equals TORA minus D]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
*Define a "runway configuration" and list any four types of runway configurations used in airport planning.*

**Model Answer:**

A runway configuration refers to the *number, orientation, and geometric arrangement* of runways at an airport, designed to provide adequate wind coverage and operational capacity.

The four major types are:
1. **Single Runway** — One runway, simplest layout.
2. **Parallel Runways** — Two or more runways oriented parallel.
3. **Intersecting Runways** — Two runways crossing each other (typically at 90°).
4. **Open-V Configuration** — Two runways diverging from a common end at 15°–40°.

(Other valid types: V-shape, tandem, combination, multiple parallel with crosswind.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a displaced threshold? Why is it provided?*

**Model Answer:**

A **displaced threshold** is a threshold that is located at a point *down the runway*, away from the physical beginning of the paved surface. It is marked with **white arrow-shaped "piano key" markings**.

**Why it is provided:**
- To ensure that the **Approach Surface** (the inclined obstacle-clearance plane in front of the runway) is **not penetrated** by obstacles in the approach path.
- It allows the runway to be **operationally usable** even when a fixed obstruction cannot be removed.
- It **preserves TORA, TODA, and ASDA** while only **reducing LDA** — the safest compromise.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the different **types of runway configurations** with neat sketches and discuss the **wind coverage** and **capacity** aspects. Mention ICAO recommendations for parallel runway centerline separation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **threshold**? Explain with diagrams the concept of a **displaced threshold**. Discuss its effect on **declared distances** with reference to ICAO standards. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Runway Configurations [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A runway configuration is the geometric arrangement of runways at an aerodrome, selected to provide ≥ 95% wind coverage and adequate hourly capacity.

**Types (4 marks):**

1. **Single Runway**
   - One runway, capacity ~30–45 ops/hr.
   - Wind coverage ~95% with proper orientation.
   - Most common at small/medium airports.

2. **Parallel Runways**
   - Two or more runways, same heading.
   - Capacity: 60–100+ ops/hr depending on spacing.
   - ICAO Spacing Standards:
     - **Independent Parallel Approaches:** ≥ 1,310 m (4,300 ft) centerline separation.
     - **Independent Parallel Departures:** ≥ 1,310 m.
     - **Dependent Operations:** 760–1,310 m.
     - **Close Parallel (Simultaneous Offset Instrument Approach):** as low as 213 m (700 ft) with special procedures.

3. **Intersecting Runways**
   - Two runways crossing at 60°–90°.
   - Capacity ~60 ops/hr; cannot be operated simultaneously near intersection.
   - Good for wind coverage from multiple directions.

4. **Open-V Configuration**
   - Two runways diverging from a common end at 15°–40°.
   - Better than intersecting for capacity, since most operations do not conflict.

5. **Combination Layouts**
   - Parallel + crosswind strip; used at major hubs (Delhi, Heathrow).

**Wind Coverage & Capacity Discussion (2 marks):**

- Wind coverage is determined from a **wind rose** plotted over 5–10 years of data.
- A single runway should cover ≥ 95% of winds for a 20-knot crosswind limit.
- If coverage is insufficient, additional runways (crosswind or parallel) are added.
- Capacity increases non-linearly with parallel runway independence.

---

#### Part (b) — Threshold and Displaced Threshold [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
The **threshold** is the beginning of the portion of the runway usable for landing aircraft. It is normally at the start of the paved surface, marked by a line of **longitudinal stripes** (piano keys).

**Displaced Threshold (3 marks):**
- A displaced threshold is a threshold located at a point **away from the physical start** of the runway.
- It is established when **obstacles in the approach path** (e.g., a hill, building, or road) penetrate the **Approach Surface**.
- Marked with **white arrow-shaped markings** pointing into the runway.
- Used **only for takeoff roll and taxi**; **not for landing**.

**Effect on Declared Distances (3 marks):**

| Distance | Effect |
|---|---|
| TORA | Unchanged |
| TODA | Unchanged |
| ASDA | Unchanged |
| **LDA** | **Reduced by the displacement distance D** |

**ICAO Standard (1 mark):**
The threshold must be sited such that the Approach, Transitional, Inner Horizontal, and Conical Surfaces are clear of obstacles. If penetration is unavoidable, the threshold is displaced and the obstacle is **marked and/or lighted** to remain a controlled obstacle.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** What are the **standard ICAO code numbers and code letters** for runway classification? Explain how they influence **runway width**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw a neat cross-section of a **Code D runway** and label the following: runway pavement, crown, transverse slope, shoulder, runway strip, RESA, and blast pad. Explain the **longitudinal and transverse slope criteria** as per ICAO. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — ICAO Code System and Runway Width [7 marks]

**Code Number (2 marks):**

| Code Number | Aerodrome Reference Field Length (ARFL) |
|---|---|
| 1 | < 800 m |
| 2 | 800 m to < 1,200 m |
| 3 | 1,200 m to < 1,800 m |
| 4 | ≥ 1,800 m |

**Code Letter (2 marks):**

| Code Letter | Wing Span | Outer Main Gear Span |
|---|---|---|
| A | < 15 m | < 4.5 m |
| B | 15 to < 24 m | 4.5 to < 6 m |
| C | 24 to < 36 m | 6 to < 9 m |
| D | 36 to < 52 m | 9 to < 14 m |
| E | 52 to < 65 m | 9 to < 14 m |
| F | 65 to < 80 m | 14 to < 16 m |

**Effect on Runway Width (3 marks):**

| Code Letter | Runway Width |
|---|---|
| A | 18 m |
| B | 23 m |
| C | 30 m |
| D | 45 m |
| E | 45 m |
| F | 60 m |

- Wider aircraft (e.g., A380 — Code F) require 60 m wide runways for safe wheel tracking.
- Width also slightly depends on Code Number for non-precision approaches.

---

#### Part (b) — Cross-Section of a Code D Runway [7 marks]

**Sketch (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X[Cross Section Code D Runway]
        a[Centerline Crown] --- b1[Half Width 22.5 m]
        b1 --- c1[Runway Edge Line]
        c1 --- d1[Shoulder 7.5 m Paved]
        d1 --- e1[Runway Strip Edge 75 m from CL]
        e1 --- f1[Drainage Channel]
    end
    a -.1.5 percent camber.-> c1
```

**Description of Elements (3 marks):**
- **Runway Pavement:** 45 m wide, crowned at center.
- **Crown:** 33.75 cm above edges (for 1.5% camber).
- **Transverse Slope:** 1.0% to 1.5%, max 2.0% (ICAO).
- **Shoulder:** At least 7.5 m on each side, paved for jet blast protection.
- **Runway Strip:** 150 m wide for precision approach (75 m each side of CL).
- **RESA:** Extends 90 m (minimum) past runway end, twice the runway width.
- **Blast Pad:** 60–120 m paved before threshold.

**Slope Criteria (1 mark):**
- **Transverse:** 1.0% to 1.5%, max 2.0%.
- **Longitudinal:** Max effective gradient 1.0% for Code 3/4 runways.
- Slope changes limited to 1.5% per 30 m interval; vertical curves provided for smoother transitions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing declared distances:** Writing "TORA is reduced by threshold displacement" is the most common mistake. Only **LDA** changes.
> 2. **Wrong slope conversion:** Writing slope as 40 (instead of 0.025) when the problem states "1:40".
> 3. **Forgetting Code Letter in width formula:** Runway width depends on Code Letter (A–F), NOT just Code Number. Many students quote one value without identifying the aircraft category.
> 4. **Missing the "≥ 95% wind coverage" rule:** It is a *threshold*, not a "comfortable" figure. A configuration with 90% coverage is operationally unacceptable.
> 5. **Confusing blast pad with RESA:** Blast pad is for *jet blast erosion* (pre-threshold); RESA is for *overrun undershoot safety* (post-runway end).
> 6. **Neglecting to mention parallel runway spacing:** Always cite ICAO's 1,310 m threshold for *independent* operations. Forgetting this forfeits 2 marks on configuration questions.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- A **runway configuration** is selected based on **peak hour demand**, **wind coverage (≥ 95%)**, and **redundancy** requirements.
- A **single runway** serves up to ~45 ops/hr; **independent parallel runways** serve 90–100+ ops/hr.
- **Parallel runways** must be spaced ≥ **1,310 m (4,300 ft)** center-to-center for *independent* IFR operations.
- The **threshold** is the start of the landing-usable portion of the runway.
- A **displaced threshold** is used when obstacles penetrate the **Approach Surface**; it **reduces LDA only**, leaving TORA, TODA, and ASDA unchanged.
- The **Approach Surface slope** is **1:50 (2.0%)** for non-instrument runways and **1:40 (2.5%)** for precision approach runways for the first 3,000 m.
- **Runway width** is determined by the **ICAO Code Letter**: 18, 23, 30, 45, 45, 60 m for Letters A, B, C, D, E, F respectively.
- The runway is **crowned at the centerline** with a **transverse camber of 1.0%–1.5%** (max 2.0%) for drainage.
- The **crown height** = (W/2) × q, e.g., **33.75 cm** for a 45 m wide runway at 1.5% camber.
- The **runway strip** is at least **150 m wide** for precision approach runways (75 m each side of CL).
- The **RESA** extends **at least 90 m** (ideally 240 m) past the runway end, **twice the runway width**.
- The **blast pad** is a 60–120 m paved area resisting jet blast, located just before the threshold.
- Wind coverage must be plotted using a **wind rose** with 5–10 years of data.
- The **crosswind component** = $V_{wind} \times \sin(\theta)$, with the permissible limit being **20 knots (dry)** or **13 knots (wet)**.
- For displacement calculations: $D = (h - h_c) / s$, where $s = 1/40$ (0.025) or $1/50$ (0.02).
- For runway count: $N = \lceil D_{peak} / C \rceil$, where $C \approx 45$ (single) to ~100 (independent parallel) ops/hr.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Taxiway Design - Controlling Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Taxiway Design - Controlling Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taxiway**: A defined path on an aerodrome established for the movement of aircraft from one part of the aerodrome to another. It is designed to provide a smooth, paved surface connecting runways with aprons, hangars, terminal buildings, and other functional areas of the airport.

A **Taxiway System** comprises the network of paved routes that allow safe, efficient, and rapid movement of aircraft on the ground — excluding the runway itself. The **Controlling Factors** of taxiway design are the geometric, structural, and operational parameters that govern the width, alignment, separation, fillet geometry, grades, and sight distance of these taxiways, ensuring compatibility with the **design aircraft** (critical/dominant aircraft intended to use the facility).

### ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code
The basis of geometric design is the **Aerodrome Reference Code (ARC)**, composed of:
- **Code Number (1–4):** Based on aircraft **Reference Field Length** (take-off distance).
- **Code Letter (A–F):** Based on aircraft **Wingspan (b)** and **Outer Main Gear Wheel Span (OMGWS)**.

| Code Letter | Wingspan (m) | OMGWS (m) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| A | up to 15 | up to 4.5 |
| B | 15 to 24 | 4.5 to 6 |
| C | 24 to 36 | 6 to 9 |
| D | 36 to 52 | 9 to 14 |
| E | 52 to 65 | 9 to 14 |
| F | 65 to 80 | 14 to 16 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Highway for Jumbo Jets"**
> Imagine a busy multi-lane highway where the "cars" are actually **wide-body jets with 60–80 m wingspans**, cannot reverse, and require a wide turning circle. **Taxiways are the "highways"** of an airport, and just as highways have lane widths, shoulder widths, median separations, and minimum curve radii, taxiways require **widths, fillets, and separation distances** proportional to the size of the aircraft they serve.
>
> The **"controlling factors"** are essentially the *design DNA* of this highway — they decide how wide the road should be, how tight a turn the jet can safely negotiate, and how far apart two parallel jets must be parked to avoid a wing-tip collision. Get these wrong, and you either get a wing strike accident or a very expensive, oversized concrete slab.

**Key Standards Bodies:**
- **ICAO Annex 14, Volume I** (International benchmark)
- **FAA AC 150/5300-13A** (United States standard)
- **DGCA India** (Civil Aviation Requirements — CAR Section 4, Series B, Part I)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Principle: Aircraft as the "Design Vehicle"

The **Controlling Factors of Taxiway Design** are essentially a set of geometric constraints dictated entirely by the **physical dimensions and operational characteristics of the design aircraft**. ICAO and FAA prescribe minimum standards for each Code Letter.

### 2.2 Hierarchy of Controlling Factors

1. **Aircraft Geometric Parameters (The Prime Movers)**
   - Wingspan (b)
   - Wheelbase (d) — distance from nose gear to main gear
   - Outer Main Gear Wheel Span (OMGWS)
   - Overall aircraft length
   - Minimum turning radius
   - Cockpit-to-wheel reference point geometry (Nose gear steering limits)

2. **Taxiway Width (TW)**
   - The straight-segment paved width must be sufficient to keep the main gear within the pavement and provide adequate **margin (M)** between the **outer main gear** and the **taxiway edge**.
   - ICAO formula:

$$TW = OMGWS + 2M$$

where M is the **clearance margin** (typically 0.5 m to 1.5 m, increasing with Code Letter).

3. **Taxiway Centerline Separation**
   - Distance between the centerline of a taxiway and:
     - **Parallel Taxiway Centerline (PTC)**
     - **Runway Centerline**
     - **Aircraft Stand (Parking) Taxilane Centerline**
     - **Fixed or Movable Object**
   - This separation is derived from a combination of **wingspan (b)** and a **safety clearance (C)**:

$$\text{Separation} = \frac{b}{2} + \frac{b}{2} + C = b + C$$

4. **Taxiway Fillet Design (Curve Geometry)**
   - When a taxiway curves, the **nose gear** traces a smaller radius than the **main gear** due to the rigid axle geometry.
   - **Fillets** are the widened pavement at curves to allow main-gear tracking within the pavement.
   - Two key parameters:
     - **Fillet Radius (R)**: The radius of the centerline curve.
     - **Fillet Widening (ΔW)**: The additional pavement added to the inner side of the curve.

5. **Taxiway Longitudinal and Transverse Slopes**
   - **Longitudinal slope:** Maximum **1.5%** (ICAO) to prevent fuel/oil pooling; preferred ≤ 1.0%.
   - **Transverse slope (camber/cross-fall):** 1.0% to 1.5% for drainage.
   - **Slope change:** Should be smooth; abrupt changes create water stagnation.

6. **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD) on Taxiways**
   - The runway-style **SSD** is replaced on taxiways by **Taxiway Sight Distance**, ensuring the cockpit can see a sufficient distance ahead to stop safely.
   - Minimum sight distance: **200 m to 300 m** depending on Code Letter.

7. **Taxiway Safety Area (TSA)**
   - Cleared and graded area surrounding the taxiway, free of obstacles except for specific frangible mounts.
   - Width: typically **TW + 2 × (10 to 22 m)** depending on ARC.

8. **Taxiway Shoulder and Strip**
   - **Shoulder:** Paved or stabilized area beyond the edge to support occasional aircraft excursion and prevent jet-blast erosion.
   - **Strip:** Total cleared area, including safety area, typically extending **22 m on each side** of the centerline for high-code aircraft.

9. **Taxiway Intersections and Holding Bays**
   - Intersections should be at **right angles (90°)** wherever operationally feasible to maximize sight line.
   - **Holding Bays** are provided near runway ends to allow final pre-takeoff checks without blocking through traffic.

10. **Pavement Strength (ACN-PCN System)**
    - Pavement must support the **Aircraft Classification Number (ACN)** of the design aircraft equal to or less than the **Pavement Classification Number (PCN)** of the taxiway.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:--|:--|:-:|:--|
| 1 | Taxiway Width (straight) | $TW = OMGWS + 2M$ | m | M = 0.5 m (Code A) to 1.5 m (Code F) |
| 2 | Outer main gear to edge | $M = TW/2 - OMGWS/2$ | m | Minimum margin required |
| 3 | Parallel taxiway CL separation | $b + C$ | m | C = clearance (10 m to 25.5 m) |
| 4 | Taxiway CL to object | $0.5b + C_o$ | m | $C_o$ = object clearance |
| 5 | Taxiway CL to runway CL (Code 4) | $\geq 60\text{ m (prec.)} / \geq 76\text{ m (non-prec.)}$ | m | ICAO Annex 14 |
| 6 | Fillet radius (inner main gear) | $R_{mg} = R_{cl} + T/2$ | m | T = track width = OMGWS |
| 7 | Fillet widening (inner) | $\Delta W = R_{cl}(1 - \cos\theta) + \delta$ | m | $\delta$ = main gear to edge margin |
| 8 | Maximum longitudinal slope | $\leq 1.5\%$ | – | ICAO |
| 9 | Maximum transverse slope | 1.0% to 1.5% | – | For drainage |
| 10 | Stopping sight distance (taxiway) | $SSD_t \geq 200\text{ m (Code A/B)}; \geq 300\text{ m (Code C/D/E/F)}$ | m |  |
| 11 | Rate of longitudinal slope change | $\leq 1.0\%$ per 30 m | – |  |
| 12 | PCN requirement | $PCN \geq ACN$ of design aircraft | – | For unrestricted use |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"WINGS"** = **W**idth, **I**ntersection angle, **N**ose gear geometry, **G**rade (longitudinal & transverse), **S**ight distance. These five letters cover ~80% of the marks on taxiway design questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Capacity Analysis:** Insufficient taxiway width or separation creates "platooning" — aircraft queuing behind one another, reducing runway throughput. A typical international hub loses 5–10% capacity per kilometer of poorly designed taxiway.
- **Safety Engineering:** Every meter of separation is statistically linked to wingtip collision probability. ICAO's 7.5 m and 10.5 m incremental clearances are derived from probabilistic risk modeling.
- **Cost Engineering:** Taxiway pavement is one of the highest CAPEX items in an airport (~15% of airside cost). Wrong fillet design means wasting thousands of m² of concrete.
- **Sustainability:** Proper drainage slopes (transverse) prevent **FOD (Foreign Object Damage)** from pooling water and pavement spalling, extending pavement life by 20–30%.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Taxiway Width from Aircraft Geometry

**Given:** Design aircraft has **OMGWS = 11.0 m** (Code D, e.g., Boeing 767), and ICAO specifies a minimum **clearance margin M = 1.0 m** on each side for Code D.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometric constraint.**
For the aircraft to remain on the paved taxiway, the **outer edge of each main gear** must lie **inside** the taxiway edge by a distance ≥ M.

$$TW \geq OMGWS + 2M$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$TW = 11.0 + 2(1.0)$$

$$TW = 13.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Verify with ICAO Annex 14 Code D.**
ICAO specifies **TW = 18 m** for Code D. The 13.0 m is the *theoretical geometric minimum*; the ICAO value of 18 m provides additional margin for operational deviation, tire wear tolerance, and pilot steering error.

**Step 4 — Generalize.**

$$TW = OMGWS + 2M \quad \text{[units: m]}$$

> **Logic Explanation:** The two outer main gears are OMGWS apart. To keep each gear M meters from the edge, we add M to each side → OMGWS + M + M = OMGWS + 2M.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Parallel Taxiway Centerline Separation

**Given:** Two parallel taxiways serving Code E aircraft (e.g., Boeing 777, **wingspan b = 65 m**). ICAO clearance **C = 12 m** between wingtip and adjacent aircraft fuselage/object.

**Step 1 — Visualize the geometry.**

```
   [Aircraft A]          [Aircraft B]
   <---b_A/2--->CL_A      CL_B<---b_B/2--->
   ←——————— b_A ——————→
                              ←——————— b_B ——————→
                  ←—— C (clearance) ——→
   |←————————————————— Separation ——————————————————→|
```

**Step 2 — Apply the geometric sum.**

$$S = \frac{b_A}{2} + \frac{b_B}{2} + C$$

**Step 3 — For symmetric case ($b_A = b_B = b$).**

$$S = b + C$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$S = 65 + 12 = 77 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with ICAO.**
For Code E, ICAO specifies **PTC = 82.5 m** for parallel taxiways. The 77 m result is again the *tight geometric minimum*; ICAO adds 5.5 m to account for nose-gear steering overshoot, jet blast, and operational tolerance.

> **Logic Explanation:** Two aircraft stand parallel. Each extends half its wingspan from its taxiway centerline. The gap between their wingtips must equal the safety clearance C. The centerline-to-centerline distance is therefore the sum of two half-wingspans plus the gap.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fillet Widening for a Curved Taxiway

**Given:** Taxiway centerline radius $R_{cl} = 60$ m, design aircraft has **OMGWS (T) = 11.0 m**, and ICAO minimum **δ = 0.5 m** between main gear and taxiway edge.

**Step 1 — Establish the geometric relationship.**
The nose gear follows the centerline radius $R_{cl}$, but the main gear, being offset by T/2 outward, follows a path of radius:

$$R_{mg} = R_{cl} + \frac{T}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute main-gear tracking radius.**

$$R_{mg} = 60 + \frac{11.0}{2} = 65.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — The inner main gear tracks a tighter path.**
The inner main gear tracks at a radius $R_{cl} - T/2 = 54.5$ m. Its distance from the inside pavement edge after the curve must remain ≥ δ.

**Step 4 — Fillet widening formula (inner side):**
For a turn subtending an angle 2θ at the centerline:

$$\Delta W = R_{cl}(1 - \cos\theta) + \delta$$

**Step 5 — For a standard 90° turn (θ = 45°):**

$$\cos(45°) = 0.7071$$

$$\Delta W = 60(1 - 0.7071) + 0.5$$

$$\Delta W = 60(0.2929) + 0.5$$

$$\Delta W = 17.57 + 0.5$$

$$\Delta W = 18.07 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
The inner fillet must be widened by ~18.07 m beyond the straight-segment taxiway width to keep the inner main gear within the pavement. The outer edge of the taxiway is similarly shifted outward (often by 1.0–2.0 m).

> **Logic Explanation:** On a curve, the inner main gear is "pulled" inward by the geometry of the turn. The fillet widening compensates for this inward pull so that the gear stays δ meters from the pavement edge.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Taxiway Design by Code Letter (ICAO)

| Parameter | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|:--|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **Taxiway Width (m)** | 7.5 | 10.5 | 15 | 18 | 23 | 25 |
| **OMGWS (m)** | ≤4.5 | 4.5–6 | 6–9 | 9–14 | 9–14 | 14–16 |
| **Taxiway CL to object (m)** | 16.25 | 21.5 | 26 | 40.5 | 47.5 | 57.5 |
| **Parallel Taxiway CL sep. (m)** | 23.75 | 33.5 | 44 | 66.5 | 80 | 97.5 |
| **Max longitudinal slope (%)** | 3.0 | 3.0 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| **Transverse slope (%)** | 1.0–2.0 | 1.0–2.0 | 1.0–1.5 | 1.0–1.5 | 1.0–1.5 | 1.0–1.5 |
| **Safety Area Width (m)** | 22 | 25 | 25 | 38 | 44 | 60 |
| **Min Sight Distance (m)** | 150 | 200 | 200 | 300 | 300 | 300 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Many KTU students lose marks by quoting values "from memory" without specifying the **Code Letter** under consideration. Always anchor every taxiway dimension to the design aircraft's Code Letter.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Stopping Sight Distance on a Taxiway

**Given:** Design aircraft has an approach speed on the taxiway of $V_t = 30$ km/h (typical for Code C taxiing speed), coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.4$ (wet pavement), perception-reaction time $t = 3$ s.

**Step 1 — Convert speed to m/s.**

$$V_t = 30 \text{ km/h} = \frac{30 \times 1000}{3600} = 8.33 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Braking distance component.**

$$d_{brake} = \frac{V_t^2}{2\mu g}$$

where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$d_{brake} = \frac{(8.33)^2}{2 \times 0.4 \times 9.81}$$

$$d_{brake} = \frac{69.39}{7.848}$$

$$d_{brake} = 8.84 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Perception-reaction component.**

$$d_{pr} = V_t \times t = 8.33 \times 3 = 25.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Total stopping sight distance.**

$$SSD_t = d_{brake} + d_{pr}$$

$$SSD_t = 8.84 + 25.0 = 33.84 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with ICAO minimum.**
ICAO requires at least **200 m** of clear sight line (Code C), not just 33.84 m of physical stopping distance. The 200 m ensures the pilot can see a conflicting aircraft/obstacle and **anticipate** rather than physically stop in that distance.

> **Logic Explanation:** Physical stopping distance is one thing; the *regulatory* sight distance is larger because pilots must be able to **see and decide** well before braking is required — it is a *planning* distance, not a *braking* distance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart: Controlling Factors of Taxiway Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aircraft Geometric Parameters] --> B[Wingspan b]
    A --> C[OMGWS T]
    A --> D[Wheelbase d]
    A --> E[Min Turning Radius]
    A --> F[Overall Length L]
    
    B --> G[Taxiway Centerline Separations]
    C --> H[Taxiway Width TW]
    D --> I[Fillet Geometry Curves]
    E --> I
    F --> J[Taxiway Length Segments]
    
    H --> K{Pavement Design}
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[ACN vs PCN Check]
    
    M[Operational Parameters] --> N[Taxi Speed]
    M --> O[Traffic Density]
    M --> P[Critical Design Aircraft]
    N --> Q[Sight Distance]
    O --> Q
    P --> R[Holding Bay Sizing]
    
    S[Topographic Parameters] --> T[Longitudinal Slope]
    S --> U[Transverse Slope]
    S --> V[Drainage Pattern]
    T --> W[Drainage Design]
    U --> W
    V --> W
    
    X[Regulatory Framework] --> Y[ICAO Annex 14]
    X --> Z[FAA AC 150/5300]
    X --> AA[DGCA CAR Section 4]
    
    Y --> K
    Y --> Q
    Z --> K
    AA --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Taxiway Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Taxiway Cross-Sectional Anatomy"
        A1[Object Free Zone OFZ] --> A2[Taxiway Safety Area TSA]
        A2 --> A3[Shoulder Paved]
        A3 --> A4[Paved Taxiway TW]
        A4 --> A5[Shoulder Paved]
        A5 --> A6[Taxiway Safety Area TSA]
        A6 --> A7[Object Free Zone OFZ]
    end
    
    B1[Aircraft Main Gear 1] -. tracked within .-> A4
    B2[Aircraft Main Gear 2] -. tracked within .-> A4
    B3[Nose Gear] -. follows CL .-> A4
    
    A4 --> A8[Centerline Marking]
    A4 --> A9[Edge Marking]
    A4 --> A10[Hold Position Marking]
    
    A1 --> A11[Free of all obstacles]
    A2 --> A12[Cleared, graded, drained]
    A3 --> A13[Supports occasional excursion]
    A8 --> A14[Yellow painted line]
    A9 --> A15[Yellow painted line]
    A10 --> A16[Taxiway to runway transition]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choice of Taxiway Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Design Aircraft Selected] --> Q1{Is taxiway parallel to runway?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| P1[Parallel Taxiway]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Connects runway to apron?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Allow high-speed exit?}
    Q3 -->|Yes, > 90° angle| P2[High-Speed Exit Taxiway]
    Q3 -->|No, 90°| P3[Standard Right-Angle Exit]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Provides holding position?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| P4[Taxilane to Holding Bay]
    Q4 -->|No| P5[Service Road or Hangar Access]
    
    P1 --> R1[CL separation based on b + C]
    P2 --> R2[Acute angle 30° to 45° recommended]
    P3 --> R3[Perpendicular exit geometry]
    P4 --> R4[Bay sized for critical aircraft]
    P5 --> R5[Reduced standards acceptable]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Fillet Geometry on a 90° Turn

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Fillet Geometry 90 Degree Turn"
        S1[Straight Segment 1] -->|Approach| F1[Fillet Entry Point]
        F1 -->|R_cl 60 m centerline curve| F2[Fillet Exit Point]
        F2 --> S2[Straight Segment 2]
        
        F1 --> F3[Inner Fillet Widening Delta W]
        F2 --> F4[Outer Edge Shift 1 to 2 m]
        
        F3 --> N1[Inner Main Gear Tracking]
        F4 --> N2[Outer Main Gear Tracking]
    end
    
    note1[Delta W = R_cl times 1 minus cos theta plus delta] -.-> F3
    note2[theta equals 45 degrees for 90 degree turn] -.-> F3
```
