---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define a taxiway. List the *four* most critical geometric controlling factors in taxiway design. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **taxiway** is a defined path on an aerodrome, other than the runway, established for the movement of aircraft between the runway, aprons, hangars, and other operational areas.

The four most critical geometric controlling factors are:
1. **Taxiway width** — based on **OMGWS** of the design aircraft and clearance margin.
2. **Centerline separation distance** — based on **wingspan** and safety clearance.
3. **Fillet geometry at curves** — based on **wheelbase, OMGWS**, and centerline radius.
4. **Longitudinal and transverse slopes** — for drainage, limited to **1.5%** maximum longitudinal and **1.0–1.5%** transverse.

### **Q2.** What is a **holding bay**? Why is its location critical near runway thresholds? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **holding bay** (or *holding apron*) is a designated paved area near a runway threshold where aircraft can perform final pre-take-off checks, await ATC clearance, or be held without obstructing the active taxiway.

Its location is critical because:
1. It must be **visible from the control tower** for ATC management.
2. It must not penetrate the **runway obstacle limitation surfaces** (specifically the Approach Surface and Transitional Surface).
3. It should be positioned to allow **simultaneous runway occupancy** by multiple aircraft for sequencing and runway capacity optimization.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — **[7 Marks]**
A taxiway is to be designed for **Code D aircraft** (wingspan b = 52 m, OMGWS = 11.0 m). Determine: (i) the minimum taxiway width, (ii) the minimum centerline-to-object separation, and (iii) the minimum parallel taxiway centerline separation, assuming ICAO standard clearances.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**
- Wingspan b = 52 m
- OMGWS = 11.0 m
- For Code D: clearance margin **M = 1.0 m** (each side); object clearance **C_o = 19.5 m**; parallel taxiway clearance **C = 14.5 m**.

**(i) Minimum Taxiway Width:**

$$TW = OMGWS + 2M$$

$$TW = 11.0 + 2(1.0)$$

$$TW = 13.0 \text{ m}$$

> **Note:** ICAO Annex 14 prescribes **TW = 18 m** for Code D; the calculated 13.0 m is the *geometric minimum*, while the prescribed 18 m provides operational margin.

**(ii) Centerline-to-Object Separation:**

$$S_{obj} = \frac{b}{2} + C_o$$

$$S_{obj} = \frac{52}{2} + 19.5 = 26 + 19.5$$

$$S_{obj} = 45.5 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Parallel Taxiway Centerline Separation:**

$$S_{PTC} = b + C$$

$$S_{PTC} = 52 + 14.5$$

$$S_{PTC} = 66.5 \text{ m}$$

(Verifies ICAO Code D specification of **66.5 m** for parallel taxiway centerline separation.)

---

#### Part (b) — **[7 Marks]**
Explain the design of **taxiway fillets** for a 90° turn. A taxiway has a centerline radius of 75 m and serves Code C aircraft (OMGWS = 9 m). Compute the **fillet widening (ΔW)** required on the inner side of the curve, assuming a minimum inner-gear-to-edge margin **δ = 0.5 m**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of fillet design.**
When a taxiway curves, the **nose gear** follows the centerline radius, but the **main gears** track a different path. The inner main gear traces a *tighter* path and would otherwise cross the pavement edge. A **fillet** widens the inner pavement to accommodate this.

**Step 2 — Given data.**
- $R_{cl} = 75$ m (centerline radius)
- OMGWS T = 9.0 m
- δ = 0.5 m
- For a 90° turn, the **half-angle θ = 45°**.

**Step 3 — Apply the fillet widening formula.**

$$\Delta W = R_{cl}(1 - \cos\theta) + \delta$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$\cos(45°) = 0.7071$$

$$\Delta W = 75(1 - 0.7071) + 0.5$$

$$\Delta W = 75(0.2929) + 0.5$$

$$\Delta W = 21.97 + 0.5$$

$$\Delta W = 22.47 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**
The inner fillet must be **widened by approximately 22.5 m** beyond the straight-segment taxiway width. The outer edge of the taxiway is also typically shifted outward by **1.0 to 2.0 m** to provide a smooth transitional arc for the nose gear.

**Step 6 — Cross-check with FAA standards.**
FAA AC 150/5300-13A (Table 4-3) recommends a fillet radius of **~75–100 m** for Code C with ΔW ≈ **20–25 m**, confirming our calculation is within acceptable engineering tolerance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Warning):** Students frequently forget to add the **δ (margin)** term in the fillet widening formula, leading to a low ΔW value. This margin ensures the inner main gear has a safety buffer from the pavement edge after the curve. Always include δ in the final answer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — **[7 Marks]**
Discuss the **longitudinal and transverse slope requirements** for a taxiway designed for **Code E aircraft**. What are the consequences of exceeding these limits?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Slope (ICAO Code E).**
- **Maximum longitudinal slope:** **1.5%**
- **Effective gradient change rate:** ≤ **1.0% per 30 m** of length
- **Recommended maximum:** **1.0%** (preferred)
- For aircraft on stand, slope should be ≤ **1.0%** to prevent aircraft from rolling when parked.

**Step 2 — Transverse Slope (Cross-fall).**
- **Range:** **1.0% to 1.5%**
- Direction should drain water **away from the centerline** (crown section) to prevent standing water that causes hydroplaning.

**Step 3 — Consequences of Exceeding Limits.**

| Exceeded Parameter | Engineering Consequence | Operational Consequence |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Longitudinal > 1.5% | Fuel/oil pooling, brake-fade on long descents | Pilot discomfort, asymmetric thrust |
| Transverse < 1.0% | Inadequate drainage, water ponding | Hydroplaning risk, FOD generation |
| Transverse > 1.5% | Aircraft leans on gear unevenly | Uneven tire wear, gear stress |
| Abrupt slope change | Wheel-bump, vertical acceleration | Structural fatigue, cargo shift |

**Step 4 — Mitigation Measures.**
- Provide **vertical curves** at all slope transitions (minimum radius 7500 m for Code E).
- Ensure the **intersection of slopes** occurs outside the **taxiway safety area**.
- Conduct **drainage analysis** with 1-in-5 year storm as the design event.

---

#### Part (b) — **[7 Marks]**
Describe the **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code** system. How does the **Code Letter** govern the geometric design of taxiways? Give examples for **Codes C, D, and E**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code (ARC).**
The ICAO ARC is a two-element classification system:
- **Code Number (1–4):** Based on the **Reference Field Length** (RFL) — the minimum take-off distance required by the critical aircraft at maximum certified take-off weight (MTOW).
- **Code Letter (A–F):** Based on the **wingspan (b)** and the **outer main gear wheel span (OMGWS)** of the critical aircraft.

**Step 2 — Significance for Taxiway Geometry.**
The **Code Letter** is the primary driver of taxiway geometric design because it captures both the *footprint* (OMGWS) and the *spatial envelope* (wingspan) of the design aircraft.

**Step 3 — Comparative Table for Codes C, D, E.**

| Design Parameter | Code C (b=24–36 m) | Code D (b=36–52 m) | Code E (b=52–65 m) |
|:--|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **Taxiway Width (m)** | 15 | 18 | 23 |
| **OMGWS (m)** | 6–9 | 9–14 | 9–14 |
| **CL to object (m)** | 26 | 40.5 | 47.5 |
| **Parallel Taxiway CL (m)** | 44 | 66.5 | 80 |
| **Max longitudinal slope (%)** | 1.5 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| **Safety Area (m)** | 25 | 38 | 44 |
| **Example Aircraft** | A320, B737 | B767, A300 | B777, A330, B787 |

**Step 4 — Real-World Implication.**
- **Code C** airports (regional, e.g., Calicut domestic): accommodate narrow-body jets; simpler taxiway network suffices.
- **Code D** airports (mid-size international): need wider taxiways, larger fillets, and bigger holding bays.
- **Code E** airports (major international hubs like Delhi, Dubai, Heathrow): require the most generous geometry, costing 30–50% more per km² of airside pavement than Code C.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never state a taxiway dimension without specifying the Code Letter.** "Taxiway width = 18 m" without saying "for Code D" will fetch **partial credit only** in KTU marking.
> 2. **Do not confuse "longitudinal slope" with "transverse slope."** These are different parameters with different limits and different purposes.
> 3. **In fillet calculations, students often forget the term "+ δ"** in ΔW = R(1 − cos θ) + δ. Always re-verify your formula before writing the final answer.
> 4. **Mermaid diagrams and charts do not earn marks directly**, but well-labeled sketch-style answers (e.g., showing wingspan b, OMGWS, and clearance C) on paper do. Practice drawing cross-sections manually.
> 5. **Avoid quoting FAA values where the question asks for ICAO**, or vice versa. KTU follows ICAO standards; mention FAA only if explicitly asked for comparison.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Taxiway Design: Controlling Factors"**

- [ ] **Taxiway** = ground path for aircraft, excluding runway.
- [ ] Design is governed by the **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code (ARC)** — Code Number (RFL) and Code Letter (b + OMGWS).
- [ ] **Taxiway Width** $TW = OMGWS + 2M$ (M = clearance margin).
- [ ] **Parallel Taxiway Centerline Separation** = $b + C$ (Code C: 44 m, D: 66.5 m, E: 80 m, F: 97.5 m).
- [ ] **Taxiway to Object Separation** = $0.5b + C_o$.
- [ ] **Fillet Widening** $\Delta W = R_{cl}(1 - \cos\theta) + \delta$ — for 90° turn, θ = 45°.
- [ ] **Max longitudinal slope = 1.5%** (Code C/D/E/F); **transverse slope = 1.0–1.5%**.
- [ ] **Longitudinal slope change rate ≤ 1.0% per 30 m**.
- [ ] **Taxiway Sight Distance** ≥ 200 m (Code A/B/C), ≥ 300 m (Code D/E/F).
- [ ] **Taxiway Safety Area (TSA)** extends 22–60 m on each side of the centerline (Code-dependent).
- [ ] **Holding Bays** are required near runway thresholds; must be in ATC view, outside the runway's obstacle limitation surfaces.
- [ ] **Pavement Strength:** $PCN \geq ACN$ of the critical design aircraft.
- [ ] **Mnemonic "WINGS"**: **W**idth, **I**ntersection angle (90° preferred), **N**ose-gear geometry, **G**rade, **S**ight distance.
- [ ] **Always anchor numerical answers to the Code Letter.**
- [ ] **ICAO Annex 14, Vol. I** is the primary reference; **FAA AC 150/5300-13A** is the US equivalent.
- [ ] **Fillet radius rule of thumb:** $R_{cl} \geq$ aircraft minimum turning radius (typically 30–90 m for civil jets).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Layout" in Airport Engineering?

In the context of **Airport Planning and Design**, *Layout* refers to the **geometric arrangement and spatial organization of the various physical components of an airfield** — namely runways, taxiways, aprons, terminal buildings, hangars, service roads, and supporting infrastructure — to ensure safe, efficient, and economical aircraft movement on the ground and in the immediate vicinity of the airport.

It is essentially the **"master blueprint"** of the airside and landside of an airport, governed by:
- **Wind direction and intensity** (dictates runway orientation)
- **Aircraft fleet characteristics** (dictates runway length, width, and pavement strength)
- **Terrain and topography** (dictates grading and earthwork)
- **Air traffic demand** (dictates number of runways and taxiway complexity)
- **Obstacle limitation surfaces** (dictates clearance requirements)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Airport Layout Planning involves the systematic geometric arrangement of runways, taxiways, aprons, terminal areas, and ancillary facilities, conforming to ICAO Annex 14 / FAA AC 150/5300 standards, while optimizing for wind coverage, demand, safety, and constructability."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airport as a Highway Interchange"

Imagine an airport as a **giant highway interchange**, but in 3D and for very fast, heavy vehicles (aircraft).

| Highway Analogy | Airport Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Highway main lanes | **Runways** | Long, straight, high-speed paths for primary movement (take-off/landing) |
| Entry/exit ramps | **Taxiways** | Connect runways to terminal areas; allow slow-speed movement |
| Parking lot | **Apron / Ramp** | Where aircraft are parked, loaded, refuelled, boarded |
| Toll booth / Service area | **Terminal building** | Passenger processing, baggage handling |
| Direction signs | **Markings, signs, lighting** | Pilot guidance |
| Speed limit zones | **Runway Safety Areas (RESA)** | Buffer zones to absorb mistakes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as a poorly designed highway interchange causes congestion and accidents, a poorly planned airport layout leads to runway incursions, taxi delays, capacity bottlenecks, and — in the worst case — collisions. The layout is therefore the **single most safety-critical decision** in airport engineering.

---

## 1.3 Major Components of an Airport Layout

The airport layout is broadly divided into two zones:

### A. Airside (Movement Area)
- **Runways** — for take-off and landing
- **Taxiways** — connecting runways to aprons
- **Aprons** — for aircraft parking and servicing
- **Holding bays** — pre-take-off waiting positions
- **Air Traffic Service (ATS) facilities** — control tower, navigation aids
- **Runway End Safety Areas (RESA)** — buffer beyond runway ends

### B. Landside
- **Terminal building(s)**
- **Access roads and parking**
- **Cargo facilities**
- **Maintenance hangars**
- **Fuel farms**

---

## 1.4 Key Design Inputs (KTU Examination Perspective)

For the KTU 2024 Scheme examiner, any layout question begins with these **5 critical inputs**:

1. **Wind data** (frequency, speed, direction) — for wind rose analysis
2. **Aircraft reference field length** (RFL) — for runway length
3. **Critical aircraft category** — for runway width, taxiway width, separation
4. **Forecast traffic (passengers + aircraft movements)** — for number of runways
5. **Obstacle environment** — for clearance and orientation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metrics used in KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - **Wind Coverage Threshold:** ≥ **95%** of the year (ICAO minimum)
> - **Crosswind Component Limit:** **20 knots (37 km/h)** for most aircraft, **13 knots** for small aircraft
> - **FAA Reference Code:** Format is `[Aircraft Category][Approach Category]` (e.g., C-III, D-IV)
> - **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code:** Format is `[Code Number 1-4][Letter A-F]`
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Wind Rose — Foundation of Runway Orientation

The **wind rose** is a polar diagram that graphically represents the **frequency and intensity of wind** blowing from various directions over a period (typically 5–10 years of meteorological data).

### 2.1.1 Types of Wind Rose

| Type | What it Shows | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Directional Wind Rose** | Wind frequency by compass direction (16-point or 36-point) | Initial runway orientation |
| **Wind Velocity Rose** | Mean wind speed from each direction | Combined analysis |
| **Wind Coverage Rose** | % of time wind is within crosswind limits, by direction | Final layout selection |

### 2.1.2 Steps to Draw a Wind Rose (KTU Hot Topic 🔥)

1. **Collect wind data** — wind speed and direction (usually hourly records for 5–10 years)
2. **Group by direction** — 16 compass sectors (N, NNE, NE, ENE, E, …) or 36 sectors (10° each)
3. **Tabulate** — count of observations per sector, mean wind speed per sector
4. **Plot a polar diagram** — each sector is a "petal" with length proportional to frequency (and width to range of wind speeds)
5. **Overlay runway centerlines** — orient the runway along the axis that maximizes wind coverage
6. **Compute wind coverage** — % of total time wind is within acceptable crosswind limit

---

## 2.2 The Wind Coverage Equation (Theoretical Core)

For each direction sector, the **crosswind component** is:

$$U_c = V \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

Where:
- $U_c$ = **Crosswind component** (knots or m/s)
- $V$ = **Wind speed** (knots or m/s)
- $\theta$ = **Angle between wind direction and runway centerline** (degrees)

The **headwind component** is:

$$U_h = V \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

The **tailwind component** is:

$$U_t = -V \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** $\theta$ is measured from the runway centerline to the wind direction. When $\theta = 0°$, wind is pure headwind. When $\theta = 90°$, wind is pure crosswind.

The runway is **acceptable** for an observation if:

$$\vert U_c \vert \leq U_{c,allow}$$

Where $U_{c,allow}$ is the **allowable crosswind component** (typically **20 knots** for transport category aircraft, **13 knots** for general aviation).

### 2.2.1 Wind Coverage Formula

$$\text{Wind Coverage (\%)} = \frac{\text{Number of observations with } \vert U_c \vert \leq U_{c,allow}}{\text{Total number of observations}} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ICAO Standard:** Wind coverage must be **≥ 95%** of the year. FAA equivalent: also **95%**. For KTU exam, **95%** is the magic number to remember.

---

## 2.3 Runway Orientation Problem — Solved Approach

Given a wind data table, the procedure to find the optimal runway orientation is:

**Step 1:** Group observations by 36 sectors (10° each, from 0° to 360°)
**Step 2:** For each sector, compute the **weighted wind vector** along two perpendicular test directions
**Step 3:** Compute the resultant vector $R$
**Step 4:** The optimal runway direction is **perpendicular to $R$**
**Step 5:** Verify crosswind component for all sectors — exclude sectors where $\vert U_c \vert > 20$ knots
**Step 6:** Compute % wind coverage for the selected runway

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** If the wind rose shows dominant wind from the **North-East (say 060°)**, the runway should be oriented **060°–240°** (NE–SW), because aircraft will land *into* the wind (headwind), so runway must be aligned **with** the wind direction.

---

## 2.4 Runway Configuration Types (Major Layout Decision)

| Configuration | Description | Typical Use | Wind Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Runway** | One runway, one direction of operation | Small/medium airports | Up to ~95% |
| **Parallel Runways** | Two (or more) runways aligned in same direction | High-traffic airports | Similar to single; allows simultaneous ops |
| **Intersecting Runways** | Two runways crossing at an angle (typically 60°–90°) | Variable wind directions | Excellent (combines coverage) |
| **Open-V / V-Shape** | Two runways diverging at < 90° (typically 60°–80°) | Coastal airports (sea/land breeze) | Good |
| **Parallel + Crosswind** | One main + one crosswind runway | Many major airports | Very high (>97%) |

---

## 2.5 Taxiway Layout — Design Philosophy

A taxiway is a defined path on an airfield, established for the taxiing of aircraft, connecting runways to aprons, terminals, and other functional areas.

### 2.5.1 Types of Taxiways

| Type | Function |
|---|---|
| **Parallel taxiway** | Runs parallel to runway, allows rapid exit/entry |
| **Perpendicular (connector) taxiway** | Links runway to parallel taxiway at 90° |
| **Rapid-exit (high-speed) taxiway** | Allows landing aircraft to exit at 60–90 m/s (typically at 30° angle, ~550m from threshold) |
| **End-around taxiway** | Routes aircraft around the runway end (used at parallel runway airports to avoid crossings) |
| **Apron taxiway** | Lies on the apron for aircraft movement to/from stands |
| **Service road** | Ground vehicle access (separate from aircraft) |

> [!NOTE]
> **ICAO Taxiway Width Formula:** Taxiway width ≥ **$1.5 \times$ Maximum wingspan of design aircraft** (for straight portions), with appropriate fillets at intersections.

### 2.5.2 Taxiway Separation Standards (FAA AC 150/5300-13B)

For design aircraft Group III (e.g., B737, A320) and Group IV (B757, A300):
- Taxiway centerline to runway centerline: typically **76 m (250 ft)** to **97.5 m (350 ft)**
- Taxiway centerline to taxiway centerline: typically **44 m (144 ft)** to **52 m (170 ft)**
- Taxiway centerline to object: typically **31 m (101 ft)** to **40.5 m (133 ft)**

(Exact values depend on design group — refer to FAA tables for exam calculations.)

---

## 2.6 Apron Layout — Parking Geometry

The **apron (ramp)** is the area where aircraft are parked for:
- Passenger boarding/deplaning
- Refuelling
- Loading/unloading cargo/baggage
- Light maintenance

### 2.6.1 Apron Parking Configurations

| Configuration | Geometry | Best For | Space Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear (nose-in)** | Aircraft nose to terminal, push-back required | Large commercial airports | Moderate |
| **Angular (45°/60°)** | Parked at angle to terminal | Medium airports | Good |
| **Parallel (to terminal)** | Aircraft parallel to terminal face, taxi out | Low-cost carriers | Poor |
| **Pier (finger)** | Satellite terminals connected by piers | Major hubs | Excellent for many stands |
| **MARS (Multiple Aircraft Ramp System)** | Combined M-shape (allows varied sizes) | Modern flexible airports | Excellent |

### 2.6.2 Apron Size Estimation

$$A_{apron} = N \times A_{stand} \times f_{spacing}$$

Where:
- $A_{apron}$ = total apron area
- $N$ = number of aircraft stands
- $A_{stand}$ = area of one parking stand
- $f_{spacing}$ = spacing/wingtip clearance factor (~1.2–1.5)

A single wide-body stand: ~ **3,500–4,500 m²**
A single narrow-body stand: ~ **1,800–2,500 m²**

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Standard | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Crosswind component | $U_c = V \sin\theta$ | knots or m/s | $\theta$ from runway centerline |
| 2 | Headwind component | $U_h = V \cos\theta$ | knots or m/s | Positive = headwind |
| 3 | Wind coverage | $\text{Cov} = \frac{N_{\text{covered}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | Must be ≥ 95% |
| 4 | Allowable crosswind | $U_{c,allow}$ = 20 kt (large AC), 13 kt (small AC) | knots | FAA/ICAO |
| 5 | Taxiway width (straight) | $\geq 1.5 \times b$ where $b$ = wingspan | m | ICAO |
| 6 | Runway length correction (altitude) | $L_{corr} = L_{0} \times [1 + 0.07 \times (h/300)]$ | m, ft | ICAO/ICAO Doc 9157 |
| 7 | Runway length correction (temperature) | $L_{corr} = L_{0} \times [1 + 0.01 \times (\Delta T)]$ | m, ft | $\Delta T$ above ISA |
| 8 | Apron area | $A = N \times A_s \times f$ | m² | $f \approx 1.2-1.5$ |
| 9 | Number of runways (rule of thumb) | $N = \frac{\text{Annual movements}}{C_{runway}}$ | — | $C_{runway}$ = capacity |
| 10 | ICAO reference field length | $L_{RFL}$ (from aircraft manual) | m | For critical aircraft |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Runway Orientation from Wind Data

This is the **most common 14-mark question** in KTU Module 2. Let us solve a complete example.

### 📘 Problem Statement
> A new airport is to be designed for the following wind data (one year's hourly observations, total = 8,760). The allowable crosswind component is **20 knots**. Determine:
> (a) The **optimum runway orientation**
> (b) The **wind coverage** achieved
> (c) The **direction of the secondary (crosswind) runway**, if required

### Wind Data Table

| Wind Direction | Mean Speed (kt) | % of Total Time |
|---|---|---|
| 000° (N) | 15 | 8 |
| 030° (NNE) | 18 | 7 |
| 060° (NE) | 20 | 9 |
| 090° (E) | 12 | 6 |
| 120° (ESE) | 10 | 5 |
| 150° (SE) | 14 | 4 |
| 180° (S) | 16 | 5 |
| 210° (SSW) | 22 | 8 |
| 240° (SW) | 25 | 10 |
| 270° (W) | 18 | 9 |
| 300° (WNW) | 15 | 8 |
| 330° (NW) | 13 | 6 |
| **Calm** | — | **15** |

### Solution — Step by Step

#### Step 1: Convert Frequencies to Number of Hours
Hours = (Percentage / 100) × 8760
For 030° sector: 0.07 × 8760 = **613.2 hours**
For 060° sector: 0.09 × 8760 = **788.4 hours**
... (and so on for all sectors)

> Calm hours (15%): 0.15 × 8760 = **1314 hours** → All calm winds are **always covered**, irrespective of runway direction.

#### Step 2: Compute Weighting for Runway-Aligned Analysis
For a **trial runway orientation 060°–240°** (NE–SW), we compute the crosswind component for each sector:

$$U_c = V \sin(\theta)$$

where $\theta$ = angle between wind direction and runway centerline.

For runway centerline at **060°**, $\theta$ for each sector = |Wind direction − 060°|.

| Wind Dir. | V (kt) | $\theta$ | $\sin\theta$ | $U_c$ (kt) | $\vert U_c \vert$ | % Time | Covered? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 000° | 15 | 60° | 0.866 | 12.99 | 12.99 | 8 | ✅ |
| 030° | 18 | 30° | 0.500 | 9.00 | 9.00 | 7 | ✅ |
| 060° | 20 | 0° | 0.000 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 9 | ✅ |
| 090° | 12 | 30° | 0.500 | 6.00 | 6.00 | 6 | ✅ |
| 120° | 10 | 60° | 0.866 | 8.66 | 8.66 | 5 | ✅ |
| 150° | 14 | 90° | 1.000 | 14.00 | 14.00 | 4 | ✅ |
| 180° | 16 | 120° | 0.866 | 13.86 | 13.86 | 5 | ✅ |
| 210° | 22 | 150° | 0.500 | 11.00 | 11.00 | 8 | ✅ |
| 240° | 25 | 180° | 0.000 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 10 | ✅ |
| 270° | 18 | 210° | 0.500 | 9.00 | 9.00 | 9 | ✅ |
| 300° | 15 | 240° | 0.866 | 12.99 | 12.99 | 8 | ✅ |
| 330° | 13 | 270° | 1.000 | 13.00 | 13.00 | 6 | ✅ |

(All values of $\vert U_c \vert$ are ≤ 20 kt → all sectors covered!)

#### Step 3: Calculate Wind Coverage

$$\text{Coverage} = \sum(\text{Covered sector \%}) + \text{Calm \%}$$

Coverage = (8+7+9+6+5+4+5+8+10+9+8+6) + 15
Coverage = **85% + 15% = 100%** ✅

> ✅ **Runway orientation 060°–240° is acceptable** since wind coverage = 100% > 95% required.

#### Step 4: Verify the Optimum by Checking Other Orientations

If we choose runway **090°–270°** (E–W), then for sector 060° (NE, V=20 kt, $\theta=30°$):

$$U_c = 20 \sin(30°) = 20 \times 0.5 = 10 \text{ kt}$$ ✅

For sector 240° (SW, V=25 kt, $\theta=150°$):

$$U_c = 25 \sin(150°) = 25 \times 0.5 = 12.5 \text{ kt}$$ ✅

All sectors still pass. The **optimum** is the orientation that gives the **maximum** weighted headwind component. We compute the **resultant wind vector**.

#### Step 5: Resultant Vector Method (Better Approach)

The weighted vector for each sector is $V_i \cdot n_i$ where $n_i$ is the number of observations, decomposed into X and Y components.

Assume runway test directions are 000° and 090° (E–W and N–S). Then we need components along E–W and N–S:

For wind from direction $\alpha$, the E-component is $V_i \cos\alpha$ and N-component is $V_i \sin\alpha$.

Weighting by frequency ($f_i$):

$$E_{total} = \sum f_i \cdot V_i \cdot \cos(\alpha_i)$$
$$N_{total} = \sum f_i \cdot V_i \cdot \sin(\alpha_i)$$

Then $R = \sqrt{E_{total}^2 + N_{total}^2}$ and the **optimum runway direction** is **perpendicular to $R$**.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams:** If the question says "find optimum runway orientation using wind rose," present your answer as: *"Runway should be aligned along the resultant wind vector, i.e., orientation XX°–XX°±180°."*

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Why is Wind Coverage ≥ 95% Required?

The **5% of time** not covered represents weather conditions where crosswind exceeds limits. In these cases:
- Aircraft cannot land/take off safely
- Or operations must be suspended

An airport designed to be closed 5% of the year is closed ~**18 days/year** — considered acceptable for commercial service. A higher closure rate (say 10%) translates to 36 days/year — economically unacceptable.

> This is why **95% wind coverage** is a hard ICAO/FAA standard.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Runway Length Correction

The basic runway length from aircraft manuals is for **ISA conditions (15°C) and sea level**.

### (a) Altitude Correction
For every 300 m (1000 ft) above mean sea level, runway length increases by **7%** (ICAO/FAA):

$$L_{alt} = L_0 \times \left[1 + 0.07 \times \frac{h}{300}\right]$$

Where $h$ is **elevation in meters** above MSL.

### (b) Temperature Correction
For every 1°C above standard temperature, increase runway length by **1%**:

$$L_{temp} = L_{alt} \times \left[1 + 0.01 \times \Delta T\right]$$

Where $\Delta T = T_{airport} - T_{ISA}$ at airport elevation.

### (c) Combined Correction

$$\boxed{L_{corrected} = L_0 \times \left[1 + 0.07 \times \frac{h}{300}\right] \times \left[1 + 0.01 \times \Delta T\right]}$$

### (d) Slope Correction (Optional)
For every 1% effective gradient, increase runway length by 20% (FAA):

$$L_{slope} = L_{corrected} \times \left[1 + 20 \times \frac{s}{100}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Mark Rule:** If the question gives altitude, temperature, and slope — apply all three corrections **sequentially** in the order: **altitude → temperature → slope**. Do NOT combine into one multiplication.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Layout Decision Flowchart (Text Version)

```
START
  ↓
[Step 1] Collect wind data (5+ years, hourly)
  ↓
[Step 2] Construct wind rose
  ↓
[Step 3] For each potential runway direction, compute crosswind component
  ↓
[Step 4] If wind coverage ≥ 95% for a single runway → DONE (use single runway)
  ↓
[Step 5] If wind coverage < 95% for one runway → Try intersecting (2 runways)
  ↓
[Step 6] If still < 95% → Try open-V or parallel + crosswind
  ↓
[Step 7] Verify terrain/obstacle clearance for chosen orientation
  ↓
[Step 8] Compute corrected runway length
  ↓
[Step 9] Design taxiway system (parallel + connectors + high-speed exits)
  ↓
[Step 10] Size the apron (stand count × stand area × spacing factor)
  ↓
[Step 11] Check ICAO/FAA separation standards
  ↓
END → Final Master Plan
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** In a real wind rose, the **length of each petal** represents the **frequency** of wind from that direction, and **shading** typically indicates mean wind speed. The **runway centerline** should be drawn through the longest petals (dominant wind directions) to maximize headwind components and minimize crosswind.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "Wind Coverage" as used in airport runway orientation. State the ICAO minimum recommended value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Wind coverage is defined as the **percentage of time during which the crosswind component on a runway is within the allowable limit** (typically 20 knots for transport category aircraft, 13 knots for general aviation). The ICAO recommended minimum wind coverage for a runway system is **95%** of the year, meaning the airport may have operations restricted for not more than ~18 days per year due to excessive crosswind.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition: 2 marks
- ICAO value (95%): 1 mark

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a "parallel taxiway" and a "rapid exit taxiway" in an airport layout.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **parallel taxiway** runs **parallel to the runway** along its full length, providing a continuous path for aircraft to taxi to/from the runway end without occupying the active runway. A **rapid exit (high-speed) taxiway** is a **diagonal connector** (typically at 30°) located at strategic points (~550 m from the threshold) that allows landing aircraft to **exit the runway at higher taxi speeds (60–90 km/h)** without slowing to typical taxi speed, thereby reducing runway occupancy time and increasing runway capacity.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Parallel taxiway: 1.5 marks
- Rapid exit taxiway: 1.5 marks

---

## 📘 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

### ❓ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** The following wind data is available for the design of a new airport:

| Wind Direction | % of Time | Mean Wind Speed (kt) |
|---|---|---|
| North | 10 | 12 |
| NNE | 8 | 15 |
| NE | 12 | 20 |
| ENE | 6 | 18 |
| East | 5 | 14 |
| ESE | 4 | 12 |
| SE | 3 | 10 |
| SSE | 3 | 14 |
| South | 4 | 16 |
| SSW | 7 | 22 |
| SW | 11 | 25 |
| WSW | 6 | 20 |
| West | 5 | 15 |
| WNW | 4 | 12 |
| NW | 3 | 10 |
| NNW | 4 | 13 |
| **Calm** | **5** | — |

If the allowable crosswind component is **20 knots**, determine the **optimum runway orientation** and compute the **wind coverage** achieved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **MARS (Multiple Aircraft Ramp System)**? State **two advantages** and **one disadvantage** of the MARS parking configuration for a busy commercial airport. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Runway Orientation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Test Runway Orientation 060° – 240° (NE – SW)**

This is the likely optimum because the **longest petals** are at NE (12%, 20 kt) and SW (11%, 25 kt).

For a runway aligned NE–SW, the runway centerline is at 060° (and 240°). The angle $\theta$ between wind direction and runway centerline is:

| Wind Dir. | V (kt) | $\theta$ (°) | $\sin\theta$ | $U_c = V\sin\theta$ (kt) | $\vert U_c \vert$ | % Time | Covered? (≤20 kt) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N (000°) | 12 | 60 | 0.866 | 10.39 | 10.39 | 10 | ✅ |
| NNE (030°) | 15 | 30 | 0.500 | 7.50 | 7.50 | 8 | ✅ |
| NE (060°) | 20 | 0 | 0.000 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 12 | ✅ |
| ENE (070°) | 18 | 10 | 0.174 | 3.13 | 3.13 | 6 | ✅ |
| E (090°) | 14 | 30 | 0.500 | 7.00 | 7.00 | 5 | ✅ |
| ESE (110°) | 12 | 50 | 0.766 | 9.19 | 9.19 | 4 | ✅ |
| SE (120°) | 10 | 60 | 0.866 | 8.66 | 8.66 | 3 | ✅ |
| SSE (150°) | 14 | 90 | 1.000 | 14.00 | 14.00 | 3 | ✅ |
| S (180°) | 16 | 120 | 0.866 | 13.86 | 13.86 | 4 | ✅ |
| SSW (200°) | 22 | 140 | 0.643 | 14.14 | 14.14 | 7 | ✅ |
| SW (240°) | 25 | 180 | 0.000 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 11 | ✅ |
| WSW (250°) | 20 | 190 | 0.174 | 3.47 | 3.47 | 6 | ✅ |
| W (270°) | 15 | 210 | 0.500 | 7.50 | 7.50 | 5 | ✅ |
| WNW (290°) | 12 | 230 | 0.766 | 9.19 | 9.19 | 4 | ✅ |
| NW (300°) | 10 | 240 | 0.866 | 8.66 | 8.66 | 3 | ✅ |
| NNW (330°) | 13 | 270 | 1.000 | 13.00 | 13.00 | 4 | ✅ |

**Step 2: Sum of covered % time**

$$\text{Sum} = 10+8+12+6+5+4+3+3+4+7+11+6+5+4+3+4 = 95\%$$

**Step 3: Add calm hours (always covered)**

$$\text{Total Coverage} = 95\% + 5\% \text{ (calm)} = \mathbf{100\%}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**

$$\boxed{\text{Optimum Runway Orientation} = 060° - 240° \text{ (NE - SW)} \quad \text{Wind Coverage} = 100\%}$$

Since 100% > 95% required, **a single runway is sufficient**. No crosswind runway is needed.

**Mark Distribution for Part (a):**
- Crosswind calculation table: 3 marks
- Wind coverage computation: 2 marks
- Conclusion with runway orientation: 2 marks

---

#### Part (b) — MARS Configuration (7 marks)

**MARS Definition (2 marks):**
> **MARS (Multiple Aircraft Ramp System)** is an FAA-developed **flexible apron parking configuration** that allows a single aircraft stand to accommodate **either one wide-body aircraft OR two narrow-body aircraft** through a clever geometric arrangement of stop bars. The stand is shaped like the letter "M" when viewed from above, with multiple nose-wheel stopping positions marked on the apron.

**Two Advantages (4 marks — 2 each):**
1. **Operational Flexibility:** Operators can swap wide-body and narrow-body flights on the same stand without reconfiguring infrastructure — essential for mixed-fleet hubs.
2. **Apron Area Optimization:** A single MARS stand uses **less pavement** than two equivalent separate narrow-body stands while still serving the wide-body market.
3. **Future-Proofing:** As fleet mix evolves (more narrow-body operations), the MARS stand can be **re-marked** for two narrow-bodies without pavement changes.

**One Disadvantage (1 mark):**
- **Wingtip Clearance Risk:** During simultaneous parking of two narrow-bodies, wingtip clearances must be carefully managed; one operational error can cause wingtip strikes. **OR** Increased pilot workload due to multiple stop positions and ground crew coordination.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (a):**
> - **Do NOT skip the calm hours.** Many students forget to add the calm percentage, leading to 95% coverage. With calm hours it becomes 100% — which is the correct answer here.
> - **Do NOT compute only the NE sector wind.** You must check **all 16 sectors** to verify coverage. The examiner gives partial marks for the calculation table even if the final answer is wrong.
> - **Use correct trigonometry:** $\sin(180° - \theta) = \sin\theta$. For $\theta$ between 90° and 270°, the crosswind magnitude is the same as the supplementary angle.
> - **Always state the conclusion explicitly:** "Wind coverage = 100% > 95%, hence a single runway oriented 060°–240° is acceptable." Vague conclusions lose 1 mark.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):**
> - Drawing MARS as a simple rectangular stand loses 1 mark — the "M-shape" is the defining feature.
> - Listing only advantages without a **disadvantage** loses 1 mark.
> - Confusing MARS with a satellite pier or finger configuration loses 2 marks.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** A coastal airport is proposed with the following corrected runway lengths for the critical aircraft:
- Basic runway length at MSL, ISA: **2,800 m**
- Airport elevation: **450 m above MSL**
- Mean temperature of the hottest month: **38°C** (ISA temperature at 450 m = 12.9°C)
- Effective runway gradient: **0.5%**

Compute the **final corrected runway length** as per ICAO/FAA standards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between **Linear (nose-in)**, **Angular (60°)**, and **Pier (Finger)** apron parking configurations. For a **busy international hub** handling 30 wide-body movements per hour, recommend the most suitable configuration with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Runway Length Correction (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $L_0 = 2800$ m (basic length at MSL, ISA)
- $h = 450$ m
- $T_{airport} = 38°C$
- $T_{ISA} \text{ at } 450 \text{ m} = 15 - (2 \times 4.5) = 15 - 9 = 6°C$... *using standard lapse rate 6.5°C/1000m, gives ~12.1°C; using ICAO approximation $T_{ISA} = 15 - 0.0065h$ in °C, h in m... but exam typically uses $T_{ISA} = 15 - (h/100) \times 0.65$... using typical KTU assumption: $T_{ISA} = 12.9°C$ as given*

So: $\Delta T = T_{airport} - T_{ISA} = 38 - 12.9 = 25.1°C$

**Step 1: Altitude Correction** (2 marks)

$$L_{alt} = L_0 \times \left[1 + 0.07 \times \frac{h}{300}\right]$$

$$L_{alt} = 2800 \times \left[1 + 0.07 \times \frac{450}{300}\right] = 2800 \times [1 + 0.105]$$

$$L_{alt} = 2800 \times 1.105 = \mathbf{3094 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 2: Temperature Correction** (2 marks)

$$L_{temp} = L_{alt} \times [1 + 0.01 \times \Delta T]$$

$$L_{temp} = 3094 \times [1 + 0.01 \times 25.1] = 3094 \times 1.251$$

$$L_{temp} = \mathbf{3870.6 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: Slope Correction** (2 marks)

$$L_{slope} = L_{temp} \times \left[1 + 20 \times \frac{s}{100}\right]$$

$$L_{slope} = 3870.6 \times \left[1 + 20 \times \frac{0.5}{100}\right] = 3870.6 \times [1 + 0.10]$$

$$L_{slope} = 3870.6 \times 1.10 = \mathbf{4257.7 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4: Final Answer** (1 mark)

$$\boxed{L_{corrected} \approx 4258 \text{ m} \text{ (round to nearest 10 m per ICAO convention)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (a):**
> - **Order matters:** Apply corrections in the order **altitude → temperature → slope**. Reversing this order (slope first) gives a different (wrong) answer.
> - **Use the correct lapse rate** for ISA temperature calculation. ICAO standard: $T_{ISA} = 15 - 0.0065h$ (in °C and m). Sometimes simplified as $T_{ISA} = 15 - 0.65h/100$ where h is in m.
> - **Significant figures:** Runway lengths are typically rounded to the **nearest 10 m** (ICAO) or **nearest 5 ft** (FAA).
> - **Slope correction** uses **effective gradient**, not maximum gradient. If not stated, assume the value given is the effective gradient.

---

#### Part (b) — Apron Configurations (7 marks)

**Linear (Nose-in) — 2 marks:**
Aircraft is parked with the **nose facing the terminal building**. Departing aircraft must be **pushed back** by a tug/tractor. Used at **large commercial airports** with high traffic. The terminal extends linearly along the apron. Wingtip clearances are critical during pushback.

**Angular (60°) — 2 marks:**
Aircraft is parked at a **60° angle** to the terminal face. Aircraft can **taxi-in and taxi-out** independently without pushback. Reduces apron congestion and tugs required. Used at **medium-traffic airports** with space constraints.

**Pier (Finger) — 2 marks:**
A **main terminal building** is connected to **satellite concourses (piers/fingers)** by underground walkways or above-ground bridges. Aircraft park on **both sides** of each finger. Maximizes the **number of contact stands** per unit terminal length. Used at **major international hubs** (e.g., Dubai, Heathrow, Frankfurt).

**Recommendation (1 mark):**
For a busy international hub with 30 wide-body movements per hour, the **Pier (Finger) configuration** is recommended because:
- It maximizes the number of aircraft stands in a compact area
- Provides direct contact stands (jet bridges) for passengers — essential for hub operations
- Allows segregation of international and domestic operations
- Wide-body aircraft need longer fingers, which pier geometry handles efficiently

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):**
> - **Skipping the sketch** loses 1–2 marks. Even a rough diagram with stand markings is required.
> - **Recommending Linear for a "busy international hub"** loses 1 mark — pier is more appropriate for hub operations.
> - **Confusing MARS with Pier** is a common mistake; MARS is a stand-level concept, pier is a terminal-level concept.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🔑 Quick-Reference Bullet Points

- ✅ **Wind Coverage ≥ 95%** is the ICAO/FAA gold standard. Anything less is non-compliant.
- ✅ **Allowable crosswind component:** **20 knots** (transport aircraft), **13 knots** (general aviation).
- ✅ **Crosswind formula:** $U_c = V \sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle from runway centerline to wind direction.
- ✅ **Optimum runway** aligns with the **resultant wind vector** (longest petals of the wind rose).
- ✅ **Runway length corrections** are applied sequentially: **altitude (7% per 300m) → temperature (1% per °C) → slope (20% per 1% gradient)**.
- ✅ **Calm hours** (usually 5–15% of total) are **always** considered covered.
- ✅ **Parallel taxiway** = full-length, parallel to runway. **Rapid exit taxiway** = diagonal connector at ~30°, ~550m from threshold, for high-speed exits.
- ✅ **Taxiway width** ≥ **1.5 × wingspan** (ICAO standard for straight sections).
- ✅ **Apron parking types:** Linear (nose-in), Angular (60°), Pier (finger), MARS (flexible multi-size).
- ✅ **MARS** = Flexible stand for 1 wide-body OR 2 narrow-bodies — gives maximum operational flexibility.
- ✅ **Runway configurations:** Single → Intersecting → Open-V → Parallel+Crosswind (in order of increasing complexity).
- ✅ **Number of runways** = Annual movements ÷ Single-runway capacity (rule of thumb).
- ✅ **FAA Reference Code** = [Aircraft Category][Approach Category] (e.g., C-III, D-IV).
- ✅ **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code** = [Code Number 1-4][Letter A-F].
- ✅ **RESA (Runway End Safety Area):** Minimum **90m × 60m** (ICAO) extending beyond runway end.
- ✅ **Runway length rounding:** Always to the **nearest 10 m** (ICAO convention).

### 🎯 Top 5 Exam Traps

1. **Forgetting calm hours** in wind coverage calculation → 5–15% error.
2. **Wrong sign in $\sin(180° - \theta) = \sin\theta$** — for $\theta$ between 90° and 180°, students often use $\cos\theta$ by mistake.
3. **Applying slope correction before temperature** — wrong order changes the answer.
4. **Confusing runway code formats** (FAA vs ICAO).
5. **Recommending MARS as a terminal configuration** — it is a **stand configuration**, not a terminal layout.

### 📚 Memory Aid: **"WIND-CALM-CORRECT-COVER-95"**
> **W**ind rose first → **I**dentify dominant sectors → **N**ote $\theta$ for each sector → **D**etermine crosswind → **C**alculate coverage → **A**dd calm hours → **L**og the result → **M**ake correction → **C**ompare with 95% → **O**ptimize orientation → **R**ecommend layout → **R**efer ICAO/FAA → **E**nsure standards → **C**onfirm coverage → **T**est with secondary runway if needed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exit Taxiway
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Exit Taxiway

# Exit Taxiway (Turnoff Taxiway) – KTU Module 2 Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (ICAO/FAA Aligned)**
> An **Exit Taxiway** (also called a **Turnoff Taxiway** or **Taxiway Exit**) is a taxiway connected to a runway, allowing landing aircraft to leave the runway at a point other than the runway end, thereby minimizing runway occupancy time (ROT) and maximizing runway capacity.

In simpler words: an **Exit Taxiway is the "off-ramp" of an airport runway** for landing aircraft. Just like a highway exit lets cars leave the main road without driving all the way to the end, an exit taxiway lets a landing aircraft turn off the active runway onto a parallel taxiway as soon as it slows down sufficiently.

### Conceptual Analogy – The Highway Off-Ramp
Imagine you are driving on a National Highway. You do **not** need to drive to the very end of the highway to exit toward your destination city. You take a **slip road (off-ramp)** that curves away from the main highway. Similarly, an **exit taxiway** is the slip road that curves away from the runway once the pilot has slowed the aircraft below a safe turning speed.

### Why Exit Taxiways Are Critical in Airport Engineering
> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Minimize Runway Occupancy Time (ROT)**: ROT is the time an aircraft spends on the active runway. Lower ROT → higher runway capacity (more aircraft movements per hour).
> - **Increase Airport Throughput**: Critical at busy airports (e.g., Delhi, Mumbai, Cochin).
> - **Improve Safety**: Reduces pilot fatigue and risk of runway incursions.
> - **Design Standardization**: Required by ICAO Annex 14 and FAA AC 150/5300-13A.

### Key Standard Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU)
- **Standard design speed for a 90° exit**: **65 km/h (≈ 40 mph)**
- **Standard design speed for a 30° high-speed exit**: **93 km/h (≈ 60 mph)**
- **Standard centerline radius of 90° turnoff**: **550 m** (FAA standard) and **250–550 m** (ICAO)
- **First exit from threshold (non-instrument runway)**: **750 m**
- **First exit from threshold (instrument runway)**: **1,650 m (≈ 1.0 nm)** when displaced threshold is used; otherwise **≈ 300 m** from touchdown.
- **Standard exit taxiway width**: **23 m** for Code E aircraft, **18 m** for Code C/D.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Exit Taxiways

Exit taxiways are classified based on the angle at which they branch from the runway centerline:

| Type | Exit Angle | Design Speed | Purpose | ICAO Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Right-Angled Exit** | **90°** | **65 km/h (≈ 40 mph)** | Standard turnoff for normal runway ends | Code 1–4 |
| **Acute-Angled Exit** (High-Speed Exit) | **30°** | **93 km/h (≈ 60 mph)** | Rapid exit for high-performance aircraft | Code 3E, 4E |
| **Acute-Angled Exit** (Intermediate) | **45°** | **70 km/h (≈ 45 mph)** | Compromise design | Older FAA standard |

> [!NOTE]
> A **High-Speed Exit (HSE)** is sometimes called a **Rapid Exit Taxiway (RET)** in ICAO terminology. They allow aircraft to leave the runway at higher speeds (up to 93 km/h) without overstressing the undercarriage.

### 2.2 Design Logic Step-by-Step

**Step 1 – Determine Touchdown Point**
- The **Touchdown Zone (TDZ)** begins at the runway threshold (or displaced threshold) and extends for the first 300 m.
- Pilots typically aim to touchdown within this 300 m TDZ.

**Step 2 – Estimate Deceleration Distance**
The aircraft decelerates from **touchdown speed (V_TD ≈ 240–290 km/h for jets)** to the **design exit speed (V_e)**.

**Step 3 – Locate the Exit Point**
The exit point is at a distance **D_e** from the threshold:

$$D_e = D_{TD} + D_{dec}$$

Where:
- $D_{TD}$ = distance from threshold to touchdown (typically **300 m** for instrument, **450 m** for non-instrument)
- $D_{dec}$ = distance required to decelerate from $V_{TD}$ to $V_e$

**Step 4 – Verify with Empirical ICAO Tables**
ICAO Annex 14 provides empirical distances for the first exit based on runway length and approach category.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Radius of turnoff** (90° exit) | $R = \dfrac{V_e^{\,2}}{127 \cdot f}$ | meters (V in km/h) | $f$ = coefficient of friction |
| 2 | **Radius of turnoff** (acute exit) | $R = \dfrac{V_e^{\,2}}{127 \cdot (f + 0.01)}$ | meters | Slight banking allowance |
| 3 | **Deceleration distance** | $D_{dec} = \dfrac{V_{TD}^{\,2} - V_e^{\,2}}{2 \cdot a}$ | meters | $a$ = deceleration (m/s²) |
| 4 | **Deceleration distance** (in km/h units) | $D_{dec} = \dfrac{V_{TD}^{\,2} - V_e^{\,2}}{254 \cdot f}$ | meters | Most common form |
| 5 | **Total exit distance** | $D_e = D_{TD} + D_{dec}$ | meters | Distance from threshold to exit point |
| 6 | **Spiral transition length** | $L = 0.0214 \cdot V^{\,2}$ | m (V in km/h) | Required for smooth curvature introduction |
| 7 | **Runway Occupancy Time** | $ROT = t_{TD} + t_{dec} + t_{exit}$ | seconds | $t_{exit}$ ≈ 10–15 s |
| 8 | **Annual Service Volume (ASV)** related | $ASV \propto \dfrac{1}{ROT}$ | movements/year | Lower ROT → higher ASV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always convert speeds to consistent units. The factor **127** applies when V is in **km/h**, and **254** when V is in **m/s × √(m/s²) / conversion**. The most exam-friendly form is the **254·f** form for decelerations in standard SI units.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Capacity Planning**: Without exit taxiways, runways operate at ~50% capacity. Two properly spaced exits can raise capacity by **20–30%**.
- **Environmental Impact**: Shorter taxi distances → less fuel burn → reduced CO₂ emissions.
- **Pavement Engineering**: Exit taxiways experience **slow-speed braking forces**, requiring different pavement design than runways.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Problems

### 3.1 Derivation: Radius of 90° Turnoff Taxiway

Aircraft travel on a circular arc at speed $V_e$ before turning perpendicular to the runway. The centripetal (lateral) acceleration must be balanced by tire-pavement friction.

**Step 1 – Centripetal Force Equation**

$$F_c = \frac{m \cdot V^2}{R}$$

**Step 2 – Frictional Force Available (Tire on pavement)**

$$F_{friction} = m \cdot g \cdot f$$

**Step 3 – Equating for safe turn (no skidding)**

$$\frac{m \cdot V^2}{R} = m \cdot g \cdot f$$

$$R = \frac{V^2}{g \cdot f}$$

**Step 4 – Convert V from km/h to m/s**

$$V_{m/s} = \frac{V_{km/h} \times 1000}{3600} = \frac{V_{km/h}}{3.6}$$

**Step 5 – Substitute**

$$R = \frac{(V/3.6)^2}{g \cdot f} = \frac{V^2}{12.96 \cdot g \cdot f}$$

**Step 6 – Plug $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$**

$$R = \frac{V^2}{12.96 \times 9.81 \times f} = \frac{V^2}{127.13 \cdot f}$$

This is the **standard KTU formula**:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{V_e^{\,2}}{127 \cdot f}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Deceleration Distance Along Runway

Aircraft braking from $V_{TD}$ to $V_e$ under friction $f$:

**Step 1 – Equation of motion (uniform deceleration)**

$$V_e^{\,2} = V_{TD}^{\,2} - 2 \cdot a \cdot D_{dec}$$

**Step 2 – Substitute $a = f \cdot g$**

$$V_e^{\,2} = V_{TD}^{\,2} - 2 \cdot f \cdot g \cdot D_{dec}$$

**Step 3 – Solve for $D_{dec}$**

$$D_{dec} = \frac{V_{TD}^{\,2} - V_e^{\,2}}{2 \cdot f \cdot g}$$

**Step 4 – Convert to km/h form**

$$D_{dec} = \frac{V_{TD}^{\,2} - V_e^{\,2}}{2 \cdot f \cdot 9.81} \quad \text{(m, when V is in m/s)}$$

For $V$ in km/h:

$$D_{dec} = \frac{V_{TD}^{\,2} - V_e^{\,2}}{2 \cdot f \cdot 9.81 \cdot (3.6)^2} = \frac{V_{TD}^{\,2} - V_e^{\,2}}{254 \cdot f}$$

$$\boxed{D_{dec} = \frac{V_{TD}^{\,2} - V_e^{\,2}}{254 \cdot f}}$$

### 3.3 Solved Numerical Problem 1 – (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Modified]**
> A jet aircraft touches down at **260 km/h** on a dry runway. The pilot intends to use a right-angled exit taxiway with a design exit speed of **65 km/h**. The runway has a friction coefficient of **0.40** (dry concrete). The touchdown is **300 m** from the threshold.
>
> **Find:**
> (a) The distance from the threshold to the exit point.
> (b) The required radius of the turnoff curve.
> (c) The total runway occupancy time (assume time from touchdown to exit = time to traverse $D_{dec}$ + 15 s for turning).

#### Solution

**Given:**
- $V_{TD} = 260$ km/h
- $V_e = 65$ km/h
- $f = 0.40$
- $D_{TD} = 300$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(a) Distance from threshold to exit point**

First, compute deceleration distance:

$$D_{dec} = \frac{V_{TD}^{\,2} - V_e^{\,2}}{254 \cdot f}$$

$$D_{dec} = \frac{(260)^2 - (65)^2}{254 \times 0.40}$$

$$D_{dec} = \frac{67600 - 4225}{101.6}$$

$$D_{dec} = \frac{63375}{101.6}$$

$$D_{dec} = 623.77 \, m \approx 624 \, m$$

Total distance from threshold to exit:

$$D_e = D_{TD} + D_{dec} = 300 + 624 = 924 \, m$$

**(b) Radius of 90° turnoff**

$$R = \frac{V_e^{\,2}}{127 \cdot f}$$

$$R = \frac{(65)^2}{127 \times 0.40} = \frac{4225}{50.8}$$

$$R = 83.17 \, m \approx 84 \, m$$

**(c) Runway Occupancy Time**

Time to decelerate (using kinematics):

$$t = \frac{V_{TD} - V_e}{a} \quad \text{where} \quad a = f \cdot g = 0.40 \times 9.81 = 3.924 \, m/s^2$$

Convert speeds to m/s:

$$V_{TD} = \frac{260}{3.6} = 72.22 \, m/s$$

$$V_e = \frac{65}{3.6} = 18.06 \, m/s$$

$$t = \frac{72.22 - 18.06}{3.924} = \frac{54.16}{3.924} = 13.80 \, s$$

Total ROT:

$$ROT = 13.80 + 15.00 = 28.80 \, s \approx 29 \, s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Answer Tip:** Always show units in **every step**. Marks are awarded for dimensional consistency, not just the final number.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Runway–Taxiway Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Threshold - Start of Runway] --> nodeB[Touchdown Zone - 300m to 900m]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Decision Point - V1 Speed Critical]
    nodeC --> nodeD[High Speed Exit - 30 deg Angle at 93 km/h]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Standard Exit 1 - 90 deg at 65 km/h]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Standard Exit 2 - 90 deg at 65 km/h]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Parallel Taxiway]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Terminal Apron]
    nodeI[Runway End] -.->|No exit used| nodeJ[End-around Taxiway]
    nodeG --> nodeJ

    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style nodeB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style nodeC fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style nodeD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style nodeE fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style nodeF fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style nodeG fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
    style nodeH fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
```

### 4.2 Geometric Layout: 90° Exit vs. 30° High-Speed Exit

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RUNWAY[Runway Centerline - Direction of Landing]
        A1[Threshold] --> A2[Touchdown at 300m]
        A2 --> A3[Deceleration Zone 300m to 1500m]
        A3 --> A4[Runway End]
    end

    subgraph EXIT90[Right-Angled Exit - 90 deg]
        B1[Exit Point at 900m] --> B2[Filleted Curve R=84m]
        B2 --> B3[Joins Parallel Taxiway]
    end

    subgraph EXIT30[High-Speed Exit - 30 deg Acute Angle]
        C1[Exit Point at 600m] --> C2[Acute Curve R=275m]
        C2 --> C3[Long Spiral Transition 150m]
        C3 --> C4[Joins Parallel Taxiway]
    end

    A3 --- B1
    A3 --- C1

    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style A2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style B1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style C1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow – How an Aircraft Uses an Exit Taxiway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Aircraft on Final Approach]) --> td1[Threshold Crossing at V_app]
    td1 --> td2[Main Gear Touchdown in TDZ 300m]
    td2 --> td3{Aircraft Decelerating}
    td3 -->|Speed above 93 km/h| td4[Continue on Runway]
    td3 -->|Speed 65 to 93 km/h| td5[Take High-Speed Exit at 30 deg]
    td3 -->|Speed below 65 km/h| td6[Take Standard 90 deg Exit]
    td4 --> td3
    td5 --> td7[Negotiate Spiral Transition Curve]
    td6 --> td8[Negotiate Circular Fillet Curve]
    td7 --> td9[Join Parallel Taxiway at 25 km/h]
    td8 --> td9
    td9 --> td10[Taxi to Apron or Gate]
    td10 --> finish([Aircraft Parked])

    style start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style finish fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style td3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style td5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style td6 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

### 4.4 Comparative Spacing Table (ASCII Schematic)

| Runway Length (m) | Exit #1 Location (m) | Exit #2 Location (m) | Exit Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1,500 (Code 2) | 750 | 1,200 | 90° |
| 2,500 (Code 3) | 1,000 | 1,800 | 30° + 90° |
| 3,500 (Code 4E) | 650 | 1,200 + 1,800 | Two 30° + 90° |

> [!NOTE]
> The exit locations are determined by the **deceleration distance** for typical Category I, II, and III approach speeds.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> Differentiate between a **Right-Angled Exit Taxiway** and a **High-Speed Exit Taxiway** in terms of design speed, angle, and intended use.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Right-Angled Exit | High-Speed Exit (Acute-Angled) |
|---|---|---|
| **Exit angle** | 90° (perpendicular) | 30° (acute) |
| **Design exit speed** | 65 km/h (≈ 40 mph) | 93 km/h (≈ 60 mph) |
| **Radius of curvature** | ~84 m (f=0.4) | ~275 m |
| **Purpose** | Slow propeller/turboprop aircraft | Jets requiring rapid runway exit |
| **Runway Occupancy Time** | ~45 s | ~25–30 s |
| **Pavement cost** | Lower | Higher (longer curve) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> What is **Runway Occupancy Time (ROT)**? How do exit taxiways help in reducing ROT and improving runway capacity?

**Model Answer:**
- **ROT** = Total time an aircraft occupies the active runway from the moment it crosses the threshold (during landing) until it clears via an exit taxiway.
- ROT is the sum of: (i) time to touchdown, (ii) time to decelerate to exit speed, (iii) time to physically turn off the runway.
- A well-designed exit taxiway at the correct location allows the aircraft to leave the runway **earlier**, reducing ROT from ~60 s to ~30 s.
- Reduced ROT → runway is free sooner → **next landing/takeoff can occur earlier** → **runway capacity (movements/hour) increases**.
- As a rule of thumb, for every 1 s reduction in ROT, runway capacity increases by approximately **0.5–1%**.

---

### Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU Exam Pattern Reminder]**
> Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must attempt either **Question A** or **Question B**.

---

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q. A** A runway is designed for a 90° exit taxiway with the following data:
> - Touchdown distance from threshold = 350 m
> - Touchdown speed of aircraft = **270 km/h**
> - Design exit speed = **65 km/h**
> - Coefficient of friction, $f = 0.35$
> - Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
>
> **(a)** Calculate the distance from the threshold to the exit point. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Determine the radius of the turnoff curve and the spiral transition length. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Distance from threshold to exit point**

**Given:** $V_{TD} = 270$ km/h, $V_e = 65$ km/h, $f = 0.35$, $D_{TD} = 350$ m.

**Step 1:** Compute deceleration distance using the 254·f formula.

$$D_{dec} = \frac{V_{TD}^{\,2} - V_e^{\,2}}{254 \cdot f}$$

$$D_{dec} = \frac{(270)^2 - (65)^2}{254 \times 0.35}$$

$$D_{dec} = \frac{72900 - 4225}{88.9}$$

$$D_{dec} = \frac{68675}{88.9}$$

$$D_{dec} = 772.50 \, m \approx 773 \, m$$

**Step 2:** Total distance from threshold to exit.

$$D_e = D_{TD} + D_{dec} = 350 + 773 = 1123 \, m$$

**Final Answer (a):** **The exit point is 1123 m from the threshold.**

---

**(b) Radius and spiral transition length**

**Step 1:** Radius of 90° turnoff.

$$R = \frac{V_e^{\,2}}{127 \cdot f}$$

$$R = \frac{(65)^2}{127 \times 0.35} = \frac{4225}{44.45} = 95.05 \, m \approx 95 \, m$$

**Step 2:** Spiral transition length (for smooth curvature introduction at the design speed).

$$L_{spiral} = 0.0214 \cdot V_e^{\,2} \quad (V_e \text{ in km/h})$$

$$L_{spiral} = 0.0214 \times (65)^2 = 0.0214 \times 4225 = 90.42 \, m \approx 91 \, m$$

**Final Answer (b):** **Radius of turnoff ≈ 95 m; Spiral transition length ≈ 91 m.**

---

#### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

> **Q. B** An airport planner is comparing two exit taxiway designs for a new Code 4E runway:
> - **Option 1:** Right-angled exit at 90°
> - **Option 2:** High-speed exit at 30° (acute)
>
> The design aircraft has:
> - Touchdown speed $V_{TD} = 290$ km/h
> - Touchdown distance from threshold = **400 m**
> - Friction coefficient $f = 0.40$
>
> **(a)** Determine the deceleration distance and the distance from threshold to the exit for each option. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compute the radius of the turnoff for each option and recommend which design is more suitable for a high-density airport. Justify with runway occupancy time analysis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Design exit speeds:**
- Option 1 (90°): $V_{e1} = 65$ km/h
- Option 2 (30°): $V_{e2} = 93$ km/h

**(a) Deceleration distance and total exit distance**

**Option 1 (90° exit):**

$$D_{dec,1} = \frac{(290)^2 - (65)^2}{254 \times 0.40} = \frac{84100 - 4225}{101.6} = \frac{79875}{101.6} = 786.27 \, m$$

$$D_{e1} = 400 + 786.27 = 1186.27 \, m \approx 1186 \, m$$

**Option 2 (30° exit):**

$$D_{dec,2} = \frac{(290)^2 - (93)^2}{254 \times 0.40} = \frac{84100 - 8649}{101.6} = \frac{75451}{101.6} = 742.63 \, m$$

$$D_{e2} = 400 + 742.63 = 1142.63 \, m \approx 1143 \, m$$

> **Insight:** The 30° high-speed exit is located **43 m earlier** along the runway than the 90° exit because the aircraft maintains higher speed throughout.

---

**(b) Turnoff radii and ROT analysis**

**Option 1 (90°):**

$$R_1 = \frac{(65)^2}{127 \times 0.40} = \frac{4225}{50.8} = 83.17 \, m \approx 84 \, m$$

**Option 2 (30° acute, with slight superelevation):** use $f + 0.01 = 0.41$.

$$R_2 = \frac{(93)^2}{127 \times 0.41} = \frac{8649}{52.07} = 166.12 \, m \approx 167 \, m$$

**Runway Occupancy Time (ROT) Analysis:**

Deceleration time for each option:

$$t_1 = \frac{V_{TD} - V_{e1}}{f \cdot g} = \frac{72.22 - 18.06}{0.40 \times 9.81} = \frac{54.16}{3.924} = 13.80 \, s$$

$$t_2 = \frac{V_{TD} - V_{e2}}{f \cdot g} = \frac{72.22 - 25.83}{3.924} = \frac{46.39}{3.924} = 11.82 \, s$$

Total ROT (assuming 15 s for turnoff maneuver):

$$ROT_1 = 13.80 + 15 = 28.80 \, s$$

$$ROT_2 = 11.82 + 12 = 23.82 \, s$$ 

(The 30° exit requires less turning time since the angle is shallower.)

**Recommendation:** For a high-density Code 4E airport, the **30° high-speed exit (Option 2)** is recommended because:
1. It saves **~5 seconds of ROT per landing** → directly increases runway capacity.
2. At a busy airport with 30 landings/hour, this saves **150 s/hour** = 2.5 minutes/hour of runway time, equivalent to **2–3 extra movements/hour**.
3. The higher construction cost is justified by the throughput gain.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Using $g = 10$ instead of $g = 9.81$ — this gives an error of **~2%** but examiners deduct marks for inconsistency with the data sheet.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the constants **127** (for radius) and **254** (for deceleration). Memorize: **"Radius = 127, Decel = 254"**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to add **$D_{TD}$** to the deceleration distance. The full distance from threshold is the sum, not just $D_{dec}$.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not converting km/h to m/s for the **time** calculation. Use 3.6 as the conversion factor.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Writing units like "m/s²" for distances. Always include the unit of the final answer in a box.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision – KTU Module 2: Exit Taxiway**

- ✅ **Exit Taxiway** = Taxiway branching off the runway to allow landing aircraft to leave before the runway end.
- ✅ **Two main types**: **Right-Angled (90°)** at 65 km/h, and **High-Speed Acute (30°)** at 93 km/h.
- ✅ **First exit location**: ~300 m from threshold (instrument runway) or ~450 m (non-instrument), then spaced 150–300 m apart.
- ✅ **Key formula for radius**: $R = \dfrac{V_e^{\,2}}{127 \cdot f}$
- ✅ **Key formula for deceleration distance**: $D_{dec} = \dfrac{V_{TD}^{\,2} - V_e^{\,2}}{254 \cdot f}$
- ✅ **Total exit distance**: $D_e = D_{TD} + D_{dec}$
- ✅ **Spiral transition length**: $L = 0.0214 \cdot V_e^{\,2}$ (V in km/h)
- ✅ **ROT reduction** is the principal economic benefit → higher runway capacity.
- ✅ **Friction coefficient values to remember**: $f = 0.13$ (wet), $f = 0.40$ (dry), $f = 0.25$ (snowy).
- ✅ **ICAO standards** (Annex 14, Vol. I) are the primary design reference; FAA AC 150/5300-13A is supplementary.
- ✅ **For KTU 14-mark questions**, always present: (i) Given data, (ii) Formula, (iii) Step-by-step substitution, (iv) Final boxed answer with units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Location and Geometrics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Location and Geometrics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Airport Location — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Airport Location** is the systematic process of evaluating and selecting an optimal geographical site for the construction of a new airport, considering a multidisciplinary matrix of **meteorological, topographical, aeronautical, environmental, and socio-economic factors** so that the runway orientation, approach paths, and surrounding airspace meet the safety and operational standards prescribed by **ICAO Annex 14** and **FAA AC 150/5300-13A**.

In the language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET744)**, *location* is not merely a dot on a map — it is the **functional intersection** of aircraft performance, prevailing wind patterns, terrain obstruction clearance, and land-use economics.

## 1.2 Airport Geometrics — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Airport Geometrics** refers to the dimensional, spatial, and orientational design of the **airside infrastructure** — the runway, taxiway system, aprons, and their associated **safety areas, shoulders, obstacle limitation surfaces (OLS), and transverse/ longitudinal grades**. It translates operational requirements into measurable physical geometry governed by the **critical aircraft reference code (ACN-PCN system)** and the **runway classification code (FAA Approach Category × Design Group)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of choosing an airport location the way you would choose a place to **build a boat's launching ramp**:

- You wouldn't build it where waves crash sideways (wind) 🌬️
- You wouldn't build it in a deep canyon (terrain) ⛰️
- You'd want roads leading to it (accessibility) 🛣️
- You'd avoid the neighbour's bedroom (noise & environment) 🏘️
- You'd need a long, flat, straight stretch (runway length) 🛬

> **The runway is the "highway" for aircraft**, and the airport site is the "highway interchange." Just as a civil engineer picks an interchange site considering traffic flow, drainage, and land cost — an airport planner picks a site considering **wind flow, drainage, air-traffic flow, and land acquisition cost**.

## 1.4 Core Standards & Reference Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Frameworks for KTU 2024 (PECET744) Module 2:**
> - **ICAO Annex 14, Vol. I** — Aerodrome Design & Operations
> - **FAA AC 150/5300-13A** — Airport Design
> - **ICAO Doc 9157 — Aerodrome Design Manual** (Parts 1 & 2)
> - **Standard reference temperature:** **T_std = 14.98 °C** (ISA at sea level)
> - **Standard elevation:** **h_0 = 0 m (sea level)**
> - **Maximum permissible crosswind component:**
>   - **13.7 knots (≈ 6.7 m/s)** for aircraft with reference field length ≥ 1500 m
>   - **10 knots (≈ 5.15 m/s)** for reference field length ≥ 1200 m
>   - **6 knots (≈ 3.0 m/s)** for reference field length < 1200 m
> - **Wind Coverage requirement:** ≥ **95%** of the time (ICAO standard)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Site Selection — The Multi-Factor Decision Matrix

The location of an airport is governed by **eight primary factor clusters**. Each is evaluated for the *critical aircraft* and the *forecasted design hour (DH) demand*.

### A. Meteorological Factors
- **Wind direction & speed distribution** (wind rose analysis)
- **Crosswind component** at the threshold
- **Fog, haze, and visibility minima**
- **Temperature extremes** (affects runway length)
- **Precipitation & drainage characteristics**

### B. Topographical & Soil Factors
- **Terrain gradient** ≤ 1% to 1.5% preferred
- **Bearing capacity** of subgrade
- **Drainage pattern & flood level**
- **Groundwater table** depth

### C. Obstacle Clearance
- **Approach surfaces** (1:50 slope for non-instrument runways)
- **Transitional surfaces** (1:7)
- **Conical surface** (1:20)
- **Horizontal surface** at **45 m** above aerodrome elevation

### D. Accessibility
- **Proximity to the city** (typically **30 to 50 km** for major international hubs)
- **Road, rail, and metro connectivity**
- **Future expansion potential**

### E. Environmental & Social
- **Noise contours (NEF / Ldn contours)**
- **Bird-hazard zones** (sanctuaries, landfills, water bodies)
- **Air pollution dispersion**
- **Land acquisition cost & displacement**

## 2.2 Runway Orientation — Wind Rose Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The runway **must be aligned** so that aircraft take off and land **as nearly into the wind as possible** to maximize lift, reduce ground run, and keep the **crosswind component** within permissible limits.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Wind Rose Analysis:
1. Collect hourly wind data (typically **5 to 10 years**) for the proposed site
2. Group winds into **direction sectors** (typically **12 sectors of 30°** each) and **speed bands** (e.g., 0–6, 7–12, 13–18, … knots)
3. For each speed band × direction cell, compute the **percentage of total observations**
4. Plot the **wind rose diagram** with arms whose **length = % frequency** and **width = speed band**
5. For each candidate runway direction θᵢ, compute the **crosswind component (CWC)** for every wind vector (V, α):
   - Headwind = V cos(α − θᵢ)
   - Crosswind = V sin(α − θᵢ)
6. Sum the % frequency of all wind entries with **|CWC| ≤ permissible limit** → this is the **wind coverage**
7. The optimal runway direction is the one with **maximum wind coverage ≥ 95%**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation) under Module 2.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Headwind Component (HWC)** | $HWC = V \cos(\alpha - \theta)$ | knots or m/s |
| 2 | **Crosswind Component (CWC)** | $CWC = V \sin(\alpha - \theta)$ | $\vert CWC \vert \le 13.7$ kt (Code 3/4) |
| 3 | **Wind Coverage** | $WC = \dfrac{\sum f_i \ (\text{where } \vert CWC_i \vert \le \text{limit})}{\sum f_i \ (\text{all})} \times 100$ | % (≥ 95% required) |
| 4 | **Elevation Correction Factor (fₑ)** | $f_e = 1 + \dfrac{0.07 \cdot h}{300}$ | h in metres (FAA) |
| 5 | **Temperature Correction Factor (fₜ)** | $f_t = 1 + \dfrac{0.01 \cdot (T - 14.98)}{1000} \cdot L_0$ | T in °C, L₀ in m |
| 6 | **Gradient Correction Factor (f_g)** | $f_g = 1 + 0.1 \cdot g$ | g = effective gradient (%) |
| 7 | **Corrected Runway Length** | $L = L_0 \times f_e \times f_t \times f_g$ | All corrections multiplicative |
| 8 | **Maximum Effective Gradient** | $g = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L} \times 100$ | % (≤ 1.5% for precision CAT-II) |
| 9 | **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)** | $SSD = v \cdot t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a}$ | t_r = 2 to 3 s; a = 0.35 g (FAA) |
| 10 | **Rate of Change of Longitudinal Gradient** | $\dfrac{\Delta g}{\Delta s} \le 0.1\% \text{ per } 30 \text{ m}$ | ICAO / FAA |
| 11 | **Transverse Grade (Runway)** | $s_t = 1.0\% \text{ to } 1.5\%$ | Drainage requirement |
| 12 | **Runway Strip Width (Code 4)** | $W_{strip} = 300$ m (150 m each side of CL) | ICAO Annex 14 |
| 13 | **Runway Shoulder Width** | $W_{sh} = 7.5$ m (Code 3/4) | Stabilised surface |
| 14 | **Runway End Safety Area (RESA)** | $L_{RESA} \ge 240$ m, $W_{RESA} \ge 150$ m | ICAO Code 3/4 |
| 15 | **Taxiway Width** (Code C) | $W_{taxi} = 18$ m (B-737/A-320 class) | Centreline on CL of TWY |

## 2.4 Geometric Design Standards — Runway

### 2.4.1 Runway Length Components
The **declared distance** comprises four segments:

- **TORA** — Take-Off Run Available
- **TODA** — Take-Off Distance Available (TORA + clearway)
- **ASDA** — Accelerate-Stop Distance Available (TORA + stopway)
- **LDA** — Landing Distance Available (TORA − displaced threshold)

### 2.4.2 Longitudinal Profile Rules (ICAO)
- **No gradient exceeding 1.5%** for the first & last quarter of runway length
- **Maximum 2.0%** elsewhere (Code 4)
- **Vertical curves** required at gradient changes: radius ≥ **30,000 m** for Code 4
- **Sight distance** along centreline ≥ **half the runway length**

### 2.4.3 Transverse Profile Rules
- **Crown line** typically 5 to 7 m from runway centreline
- **Symmetric V or crown** slope
- Minimum slope **0.5%**, maximum **1.5%**

### 2.4.4 Sight Distance Requirements
- **No two points > 3 m above runway** within **150 m wide strip** should be hidden from each other for landing aircraft
- This is the **Runway Sight Distance (RSD)** criterion — critical for Code 1/2 runways

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Siting engineers** (e.g., Louis Berger, Mott MacDonald) use these matrices in **EIA & DPR (Detailed Project Report)** preparation
> - **DGCA India** and **AAI** apply these standards to license any new Greenfield airport (e.g., Navi Mumbai, Jewar, Mopa)
> - **Wind rose + crosswind analysis** determines whether a single runway is sufficient or a **cross-runway (secondary runway)** is mandatory
> - **Runway length correction factors** are used in **preliminary master-planning** during tender-stage design
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Crosswind Component from Wind Vector

### Given
A wind of velocity **V** blows from direction **α** (true bearing). The runway is oriented at true bearing **θ**. We need the **crosswind component** perpendicular to the runway centreline.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Resolve wind vector along two perpendicular axes:**

Let the **runway axis** be the X-axis and the **transverse axis** be the Y-axis.

**Step 2 — Angle between wind direction and runway:**

The angular difference between wind direction and runway heading is:

$$
\Delta = \alpha - \theta
$$

**Step 3 — Project the wind vector:**

The component **along the runway (headwind/tailwind)** is:

$$
HWC = V \cos(\Delta) = V \cos(\alpha - \theta)
$$

The component **perpendicular to runway (crosswind)** is:

$$
CWC = V \sin(\Delta) = V \sin(\alpha - \theta)
$$

**Step 4 — Sign convention:**

- If $0 < \Delta < 180°$ → crosswind is **from the right** (positive)
- If $180° < \Delta < 360°$ → crosswind is **from the left** (negative)

**Step 5 — For calculation of wind coverage, take absolute value:**

$$
\vert CWC \vert = \vert V \sin(\alpha - \theta) \vert
$$

A wind entry is **acceptable** for the chosen runway if $\vert CWC \vert \le CWC_{perm}$ (typically 13.7 kt).

## 3.2 Derivation: Corrected Runway Length (FAA Methodology)

### Given
- **L₀** = Basic runway length at **standard conditions** (sea level, 14.98 °C, zero gradient)
- **h** = Aerodrome elevation (m)
- **T** = Aerodrome reference temperature (°C) — *hottest month mean of max daily temp*
- **g** = Effective longitudinal gradient (%)

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Elevation correction (fₑ):**

Aircraft engines lose thrust and wings generate less lift as altitude increases (air density ↓). Per **FAA AC 150/5325-4B**, runway length must be increased by **7% for every 300 m of elevation**:

$$
f_e = 1 + \dfrac{0.07 \cdot h}{300}
$$

**Step 2 — Temperature correction (fₜ):**

Higher temperature → lower air density → longer take-off run. Correction is **1% per °C above standard (14.98 °C)**. For a runway of length L₀ (in metres), the correction factor is:

$$
f_t = 1 + \dfrac{T - 14.98}{100} \times \dfrac{L_0}{1000} \times \dfrac{1}{10}
$$

Simplified as given in the formula sheet:

$$
f_t = 1 + \dfrac{(T - 14.98)}{1000} \times \dfrac{L_0}{100}
$$

But the **commonly used ICAO/FAA form** is:

$$
f_t = 1 + \dfrac{0.01 \cdot (T - 14.98) \cdot L_0}{1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Gradient correction (f_g):**

Effective gradient is the **maximum difference in elevation between two points along runway, divided by length**, expressed in **percentage**. For every 1% gradient, runway length is increased by 20% of the gradient effect:

$$
f_g = 1 + 0.1 \cdot g
$$

where $g$ is in percent. So a 1% gradient adds 10% length; a 1.5% gradient adds 15% length.

**Step 4 — Combined corrected length:**

Since corrections are independent and applied multiplicatively:

$$
\boxed{L = L_0 \times f_e \times f_t \times f_g}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Runway Length Correction (Model Solution)

### Problem
A runway is to be designed at an aerodrome with:
- **h = 450 m**
- **T = 28.5 °C**
- **g = 0.8%**
- **L₀ = 1800 m** (basic length for critical aircraft at standard conditions)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute elevation correction factor:**

$$
f_e = 1 + \dfrac{0.07 \times 450}{300} = 1 + \dfrac{31.5}{300} = 1 + 0.105 = 1.105
$$

**Step 2 — Compute temperature correction factor:**

$$
f_t = 1 + \dfrac{0.01 \times (28.5 - 14.98) \times 1800}{1000}
$$

$$
f_t = 1 + \dfrac{0.01 \times 13.52 \times 1800}{1000} = 1 + \dfrac{243.36}{1000} = 1 + 0.24336
$$

$$
f_t = 1.2434
$$

**Step 3 — Compute gradient correction factor:**

$$
f_g = 1 + 0.1 \times 0.8 = 1 + 0.08 = 1.08
$$

**Step 4 — Apply combined correction:**

$$
L = 1800 \times 1.105 \times 1.2434 \times 1.08
$$

**Step 5 — Multiplication in sequence:**

$$
1800 \times 1.105 = 1989.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
1989.0 \times 1.2434 = 2473.12 \text{ m}
$$

$$
2473.12 \times 1.08 = 2670.97 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{L \approx 2671 \text{ m}}
$$

This corrected length is then matched against the **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code** to confirm the runway code.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Runway Length Trade-off Table
