> **Legend:** ★ = Poor → ★★★★★ = Excellent. × = Not permitted by ICAO for Code F due to wingtip clearance violation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Insight on Jet Blast Hazards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why jet blast direction matters in configuration selection:**

For a **nose-in** aircraft (e.g., B737), the engines are mounted under the wings, **ahead of the tail**. When the aircraft starts engines for push-back or self-taxi, exhaust plumes blow **rearward, away from the terminal**. This is the safest configuration for ground personnel.

For a **nose-out** aircraft, exhaust blows **toward the terminal**, requiring a **blast fence** of height **6 m to 12 m** to shield the terminal façade and GSE staging area. The cost of the blast fence often negates any other advantage of nose-out stands.

**Empirical jet blast hazard zones (ICAO Doc 4444 / Boeing AOM):**

| Aircraft Type | Idle Thrust Zone (60 km/h contour) | Breakaway Thrust Zone |
|---|---|---|
| B737-800 | 25 m behind | 90 m behind |
| B777-300ER | 40 m behind | 140 m behind |
| A380-800 | 55 m behind | 180 m behind |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *idle* and *breakaway* blast distances. The **60 km/h** threshold (the velocity at which a person can be knocked down) is reached much earlier than the **breakaway thrust** maximum. Always cite the **60 km/h contour** for personnel safety calculations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Aircraft Stand Functional Architecture

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aircraft Parking Configurations and Parking Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Aircraft Parking Configurations — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aircraft Parking Configuration** refers to the geometric orientation in which an aircraft is positioned on a designated parking stand (also called an *apron bay* or *gate position*) relative to the terminal building and the apron taxilane. The configuration determines the maneuvering sequence required for arrival (docking) and departure (push-back or self-taxi-out), the apron footprint, and the type of ground support infrastructure needed.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with ICAO Annex 14, Vol. I and FAA AC 150/5300-13A), the **five canonical aircraft parking configurations** are:

1. **Nose-In (90°) — Aircraft nose faces the terminal at a perpendicular angle.
2. **Angled Nose-In (typically 45° to 60°) — Aircraft nose angled toward the terminal.
3. **Nose-Out (90°) — Aircraft nose faces the taxilane (away from the terminal).
4. **Angled Nose-Out (45° to 60°) — Aircraft nose angled away from the terminal.
5. **Parallel (0°) — Aircraft longitudinal axis parallel to the terminal face.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Car Parking at a Mall":**
> Imagine a shopping mall parking lot. Some cars park **perpendicular** to the sidewalk (nose-in) — easy to walk in, but you need help reversing out. Some park at an **angle** (like at fast-food drive-throughs) — easier to pull out yourself. Some park **parallel** along the curb — takes more length but no assistance needed to leave.
> **Aircraft parking works exactly the same way**, except the "cars" weigh 70,000 kg to 575,000 kg, and instead of reverse gear, the pilot relies on a **push-back tractor** to leave a nose-in stand. The choice of configuration is therefore a **trade-off between apron area consumed, passenger walking distance, and ground operations cost**.

### 1.3 Aircraft Parking Systems — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aircraft Parking System (or Stand Allocation System)** is the integrated set of physical, electronic, and procedural infrastructure that enables an aircraft to safely and accurately position itself at a designated stand, disembark/embark passengers, and depart. Modern systems include the **stand itself, guidance aids, fuel hydrant pits, ground power units (GPU), pre-conditioned air (PCA), and the apron management control system**.

The major parking/guidance systems encountered in KTU syllabus are:
- **MARS** — Multiple Aircraft Ramp System
- **VDGS** — Visual Docking Guidance System
- **A-VDGS** — Advanced Visual Docking Guidance System
- **AGSU** — Aircraft Guidance and Stand Unit (a subtype of A-VDGS)
- **A-SMGCS** — Advanced Surface Movement Guidance and Control System (apron-level component)

### 1.4 Key Physical Metrics (ICAO Standards)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard apron design metrics for KTU reference:**
> - Standard ICAO Code Letter aircraft wingspan categories: **A (up to 15 m) to F (up to 80 m)**
> - **ICAO Apron Taxiway minimum width** = **1.5 × wingspan of the most demanding aircraft** (for straight segment)
> - **Push-back clearance envelope** behind a parked wide-body = **typically 50 m to 65 m**
> - **Jet blast velocity threshold** for hazard = **60 km/h (≈ 16.7 m/s)** at engine idle
> - **Wingtip clearance** between parked aircraft = **typically 7.5 m** (ICAO Annex 14)

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Comparative Analysis of the Five Parking Configurations

| Configuration | Docking Maneuver | Departure Maneuver | Apron Depth Required | Push-Back Tractor Required? | Passenger Walking Distance | Common Usage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nose-In (90°)** | Self-taxi to stop | Push-back required | **Shortest** (~ wingspan + safety margin) | **Yes** | Shortest to terminal | Most common at contact gates |
| **Angled Nose-In (45°)** | Self-taxi to angled stop | Self-taxi-out (slight turn) | Moderate | **No** (in some cases) | Slightly longer | Remote stands, low-cost terminals |
| **Nose-Out (90°)** | Push-back IN required | Self-taxi-out forward | Moderate | **Yes (inbound)** | Shortest | Cargo stands, military |
| **Angled Nose-Out (45°)** | Self-taxi to angled stop | Self-taxi-out | Larger | **No** | Longer | Older European airports |
| **Parallel (0°)** | Self-taxi along stand | Self-taxi forward | **Largest** (≈ 2× aircraft length) | **No** | Longest | Bus-boarding remote stands, hangars |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The **Nose-In configuration dominates modern terminal design** because it (i) minimizes apron footprint, (ii) places aircraft doors closest to the aerobridge, and (iii) simplifies jet-blast direction control (exhausts blow away from the terminal).

### 2.2 Geometric and Operational Step-Decomposition

**Step 1 — Stand Selection by Aircraft Category**
The ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code (ARC) assigns a stand to an aircraft by matching the **wingspan** and **length** to the stand's geometric envelope. The envelope is bounded by the **Wingtip Clearance Line (WCL)** and the **Nose Gear Stop Line (NGL)**.

**Step 2 — Establishing the Lead-In Line**
A painted yellow taxi line guides the aircraft nose wheel. The pilot follows this line until the **Azimuth Guidance Unit (AZGU)** or **VDGS** signals "STOP."

**Step 3 — Stopping the Aircraft**
The aircraft halts when the **Nose Gear** is positioned over the **Nose Gear Stop Bar**, which is offset from the terminal by the **Aerobridge Extension Distance (AED)**. For a Code F aircraft (A380), AED is approximately **6.5 m to 8.5 m**.

**Step 4 — Docking Verification**
The **A-VDGS** laser/camera unit confirms lateral offset (typically within **± 0.10 m**) and azimuth (within **± 0.5°**).

**Step 5 — Ground Servicing Hook-Up**
Simultaneously: aerobridge extends, GPU connects, fuel hydrant pit cover is removed, baggage conveyor deploys, catering truck approaches.

**Step 6 — Departure Sequencing**
For nose-in stands, the **push-back tractor** (typically a **TLD JST-30** or **Goldhofer AST-2**) attaches to the **Nose Landing Gear (NLG) tow bar**, lifts the NLG, and pushes the aircraft back along a curved trajectory until the nose wheel reaches the **push-back reference line**, after which the aircraft self-taxi-exits.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Apron Depth (Nose-In)** | $D_{apron} = W_{ac} + 2C_{w} + L_{nose}$ | m | $W_{ac}$ = wingspan; $C_{w}$ = wingtip clearance (≥ 7.5 m); $L_{nose}$ = nose stop margin (~10 m) |
| 2 | **Apron Depth (Parallel)** | $D_{apron} = L_{ac} + S_{lead}$ | m | $L_{ac}$ = aircraft length; $S_{lead}$ = lead-in distance (~ length of one aircraft) |
| 3 | **Apron Taxiway Width (Straight)** | $W_{tx} = 1.5 \times W_{ac,max}$ | m | Per ICAO Annex 14, for straight segment |
| 4 | **Apron Taxiway Width (Curved)** | $W_{tx,curve} = 1.5 \times W_{ac,max} + \Delta_{curve}$ | m | $\Delta_{curve}$ = additional widening (0–7.5 m depending on radius) |
| 5 | **Aircraft stand area (rectangular approximation)** | $A_{stand} = (L_{ac} + a) \times (W_{ac} + b)$ | m² | $a, b$ = longitudinal and lateral safety clearances (per ICAO) |
| 6 | **Jet blast temperature at distance d** | $T(d) = T_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{d_0}{d}\right)^{0.5}$ | K | Approximate power-law decay; $T_0$ = temperature at $d_0$ |
| 7 | **Jet blast velocity decay** | $V(d) = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{d_0}{d}$ | m/s | Linear inverse model (simplified, valid for d > 50 m) |
| 8 | **Number of stands required (peak hour)** | $N = \left\lceil\dfrac{T_{turn}}{T_{slot}}\right\rceil \cdot N_{sim}$ | stands | $T_{turn}$ = turnaround time; $T_{slot}$ = peak hour slot; $N_{sim}$ = simultaneous operations |
| 9 | **ICAO Code Letter (wingspan range)** | $W \in [15, 24, 36, 52, 65, 80]$ m | m | Codes A → F respectively |
| 10 | **MARS stand flexibility** | $W_{MARS} = \max(W_{small}, W_{large})$ | m | Multiple Aircraft Ramp System supports 2+ aircraft classes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The apron depth formula directly determines the **terminal-to-taxilane setback distance**, which is a critical urban planning parameter — it controls how far back a terminal must be from the airfield boundary, affecting land cost, taxiway length, and passenger flow.

### 2.4 The MARS (Multiple Aircraft Ramp System) — Detailed Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARS** is a flexible stand design where **two sets of painted stop bars** (one for a smaller aircraft like a B737, and one for a larger aircraft like a B777) coexist on the same physical stand. This allows **mixed-fleet operation** without apron reconfiguration.

**Why MARS?**
- **Operational flexibility** — One stand serves narrow-body at one hour and wide-body at the next.
- **Cost efficiency** — One aerobridge, one fuel pit, one PCA unit serves multiple aircraft categories.
- **Capacity optimization** — Critical at hub airports with mixed fleet (e.g., Dubai, Singapore Changi, Delhi T3).

**Limitations:**
- Larger aircraft consumes the stand for the entire day, reducing turn count.
- Pilots must be trained on the specific stop bar for their aircraft type.
- Requires robust A-VDGS that auto-detects aircraft type.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Apron Depth for a Nose-In Stand (ICAO Code E Aircraft, e.g., B777-300ER)

**Given Data (per ICAO Annex 14, Table 4-2 and B777 specifications):**
- Aircraft wingspan, $W_{ac} = 64.8$ m (B777-300ER)
- Wingtip clearance (ICAO minimum, Code E), $C_w = 7.5$ m
- Nose stop margin (pilot eye reference to nose gear stop bar), $L_{nose} = 10$ m
- Safety margin from aircraft tail to taxilane centerline, $M_{tail} = 25$ m
- Aerobridge extension limit (Code E), $E_{b} = 5.5$ m

**Step 1 — Define the apron depth as the sum of geometric segments measured perpendicular from the terminal face to the taxilane centerline:**

$$D_{apron} = E_{b} + C_{w} + \frac{W_{ac}}{2} + M_{tail}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$D_{apron} = 5.5 + 7.5 + \frac{64.8}{2} + 25$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the half-wingspan term:**

$$\frac{W_{ac}}{2} = \frac{64.8}{2} = 32.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Sum all terms:**

$$D_{apron} = 5.5 + 7.5 + 32.4 + 25 = 70.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Result and Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{D_{apron} \approx 70.4 \text{ m}}$$

This means the **terminal face must be set back approximately 70.4 m from the centerline of the apron service road / taxilane**. This is the controlling dimension for terminal pier design and is **independent of the aircraft length** for nose-in stands — a critical insight for KTU problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it independent of aircraft length?** Because for nose-in parking, the tail extends *backward* (into the taxilane area) rather than sideways. The taxilane must be designed for tail clearance separately (using $M_{tail}$ and aircraft length).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Apron Depth for a Parallel Stand (Same B777-300ER)

**Given:**
- Aircraft length, $L_{ac} = 73.9$ m (B777-300ER)
- Lead-in distance allowance, $S_{lead} = 80$ m (one aircraft length + 5% safety)
- Wingtip clearance (same as before), $C_w = 7.5$ m
- Half-wingspan, $W_{ac}/2 = 32.4$ m

**Step 1 — Express the parallel apron depth as the sum of the lead-in segment and the parked aircraft length:**

$$D_{apron, parallel} = S_{lead} + L_{ac} + C_{w} + \frac{W_{ac}}{2} - \text{(overlap correction)}$$

The overlap correction accounts for the fact that the lead-in line and the parked aircraft share common ground. The empirical ICAO value simplifies to:

$$D_{apron, parallel} \approx L_{ac} + \frac{W_{ac}}{2} + C_{w}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$D_{apron, parallel} = 73.9 + 32.4 + 7.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$D_{apron, parallel} = 113.8 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with nose-in:**

$$\Delta D = 113.8 - 70.4 = 43.4 \text{ m} \text{ (parallel requires 62\% more depth)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Take:** This **62% extra depth** is why parallel stands are confined to remote/bus-boarding aprons where cost-per-square-meter of land is lower. At contact piers, nose-in is almost universal.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Stands for Peak-Hour Demand (Queuing Analysis)

**Given (typical KTU numerical):**
- Design Peak Hour Passenger Demand, $P_{phpd} = 4500$ pax/hr
- Average aircraft seating, $S_{avg} = 180$ seats
- Load factor, $\eta_{LF} = 0.85$
- Average turnaround time, $T_{turn} = 45$ min
- Average gate occupancy (block time on stand), $T_{occ} = 60$ min

**Step 1 — Calculate Aircraft Movement Rate (AMR):**

$$AMR = \frac{P_{phpd}}{S_{avg} \times \eta_{LF}}$$

$$AMR = \frac{4500}{180 \times 0.85} = \frac{4500}{153} \approx 29.4 \text{ movements/hr}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to aircraft per minute:**

$$AMR_{min} = \frac{29.4}{60} \approx 0.490 \text{ movements/min}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate total stand-hours required per hour (i.e., per peak hour):**

$$H_{stands} = AMR \times \frac{T_{occ}}{60} = 29.4 \times \frac{60}{60} = 29.4 \text{ stand-hours/peak hour}$$

**Step 4 — Since each stand provides 1 stand-hour per hour, the number of stands required is:**

$$N_{stands} = 29.4 \approx 30 \text{ stands}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the MARS flexibility factor (η_MARS = 1.15, since 15% of stands can flex):**

$$N_{effective} = N_{stands} \times (1 - \eta_{MARS}) = 30 \times 0.85 = 25.5 \rightarrow 26 \text{ stands}$$

$$\boxed{N_{stands, design} = 26 \text{ contact stands}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Configuration Selection Decision Tree

| Decision Criterion | Nose-In (90°) | Angled Nose-In (45°) | Nose-Out (90°) | Angled Nose-Out (45°) | Parallel (0°) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Minimizes apron area** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★ |
| **Eliminates push-back cost** | ★ | ★★★ | ★ (inbound push required) | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Shortest passenger walk** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ |
| **Compatible with aerobridge** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★ |
| **Jet blast directed away from terminal** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★ | ★ | ★★ |
| **Self-maneuvering at peak** | ★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Recommended for Code F (A380)** | ★★★★★ | ★ | ★ | × | × |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** ★ = Poor → ★★★★★ = Excellent. × = Not permitted by ICAO for Code F due to wingtip clearance violation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Insight on Jet Blast Hazards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why jet blast direction matters in configuration selection:**

For a **nose-in** aircraft (e.g., B737), the engines are mounted under the wings, **ahead of the tail**. When the aircraft starts engines for push-back or self-taxi, exhaust plumes blow **rearward, away from the terminal**. This is the safest configuration for ground personnel.

For a **nose-out** aircraft, exhaust blows **toward the terminal**, requiring a **blast fence** of height **6 m to 12 m** to shield the terminal façade and GSE staging area. The cost of the blast fence often negates any other advantage of nose-out stands.

**Empirical jet blast hazard zones (ICAO Doc 4444 / Boeing AOM):**

| Aircraft Type | Idle Thrust Zone (60 km/h contour) | Breakaway Thrust Zone |
|---|---|---|
| B737-800 | 25 m behind | 90 m behind |
| B777-300ER | 40 m behind | 140 m behind |
| A380-800 | 55 m behind | 180 m behind |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *idle* and *breakaway* blast distances. The **60 km/h** threshold (the velocity at which a person can be knocked down) is reached much earlier than the **breakaway thrust** maximum. Always cite the **60 km/h contour** for personnel safety calculations.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define "MARS" in the context of aircraft parking systems. List two advantages and one limitation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **MARS (Multiple Aircraft Ramp System)** is a flexible aircraft stand design that uses multiple painted nose-gear stop bars on a single physical stand, allowing the same stand to accommodate aircraft of different size categories (e.g., narrow-body and wide-body) at different times of day, using a common set of fixed ground support infrastructure (aerobridge, fuel pit, GPU, PCA).

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Operational flexibility** — Mixed fleet (e.g., A320 in morning, B777 in evening) can use the same stand.
2. **Capital cost efficiency** — A single aerobridge, fuel pit, and PCA unit serves two aircraft categories, reducing infrastructure duplication.

**Limitation (1 mark):**
1. The stand's effective capacity is reduced when occupied by a larger aircraft, since the larger aircraft consumes the stand for a longer turn and blocks the smaller stop bar's use.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Why is the "nose-in" (90°) aircraft parking configuration preferred at contact gates? State three reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Minimum apron depth:** Nose-in parking requires apron depth approximately equal to the wingspan plus safety margins, which is **40–60% less** than a parallel stand of the same aircraft. (1 mark)
2. **Aerobridge compatibility:** The aircraft nose points directly at the terminal, allowing the **aerobridge to extend to the L1 door** with the shortest horizontal reach. (1 mark)
3. **Jet blast safety:** Engine exhaust plumes blow **rearward, away from the terminal** and passenger boarding areas, eliminating the need for a blast fence. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the aid of neat sketches, describe the **five aircraft parking configurations** used at airports. For each, state **one advantage** and **one disadvantage**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The design peak hour passenger demand at a proposed greenfield airport is **6,200 pax/hr**. The average aircraft has **220 seats** and operates at a **load factor of 0.82**. The average gate occupancy time is **55 minutes**. Assuming 12% of stands can be flexibly allocated using MARS, determine the **number of contact stands required**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

| # | Configuration | Sketch (described) | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nose-In (90°)** | Aircraft nose perpendicular to terminal, tail toward taxilane. | Shortest passenger walk; minimum apron depth. | Push-back tractor required for departure. |
| 2 | **Angled Nose-In (45–60°)** | Nose angled toward terminal; tail still toward taxilane. | Self-taxi-out possible; no push-back needed. | Larger apron width than pure nose-in. |
| 3 | **Nose-Out (90°)** | Nose toward taxilane; tail toward terminal. | Forward self-taxi departure. | Push-back IN required; jet blast toward terminal. |
| 4 | **Angled Nose-Out (45–60°)** | Nose angled toward taxilane. | Self-taxi-in and out possible. | Larger apron; not compatible with aerobridge. |
| 5 | **Parallel (0°)** | Aircraft axis parallel to terminal. | Both arrival and departure by self-taxi; no push-back. | Maximum apron depth (~1.6× nose-in); not aerobridge compatible. |

*(Full marks: 5 configurations × (1.2 mark each) = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for the comparative summary.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Aircraft Movement Rate (AMR):

$$AMR = \frac{6200}{220 \times 0.82} = \frac{6200}{180.4} \approx 34.37 \text{ movements/hr}$$

**Step 2:** Stand-hours required:

$$H_{stands} = 34.37 \times \frac{55}{60} = 34.37 \times 0.9167 \approx 31.50 \text{ stand-hours/hr}$$

**Step 3:** Base number of stands (without MARS):

$$N_{base} = \lceil 31.50 \rceil = 32 \text{ stands}$$

**Step 4:** MARS reduction (12% flexibility):

$$N_{effective} = N_{base} \times (1 - 0.12) = 32 \times 0.88 = 28.16$$

**Step 5:** Round up to whole number:

$$\boxed{N_{stands, design} = 29 \text{ contact stands}}$$

*(Mark split: 1 mark Step 1, 2 marks Step 2, 1 mark Step 3, 2 marks Step 4–5, 1 mark final answer with units.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Aircraft Docking Guidance System (A-VDGS)**. Describe its **functional components**, **operational sequence during aircraft docking**, and **two safety interlocks** it provides. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wide-body aircraft (ICAO Code E, wingspan 65 m) is to be parked in **nose-in configuration**. Given: nose stop margin = 10 m, aerobridge extension = 5.5 m, wingtip clearance = 7.5 m, and taxilane tail clearance = 25 m. **(i)** Compute the **required apron depth** from the terminal face to the taxilane centerline. **(ii)** If the same aircraft were parked in **parallel configuration** with lead-in distance of 80 m, compute the **apron depth** and the **percentage increase** over nose-in. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

> **A-VDGS (Advanced Visual Docking Guidance System)** is an electro-optical system, typically laser- and camera-based, mounted on a pedestal in front of the aircraft stand. It guides the pilot during the final 30–50 m of docking, displays real-time azimuth, distance-to-stop, and aircraft type, and automatically halts the pilot with a "STOP" signal at the precise stop bar.

**Functional components (3 marks):**
1. **Laser scanner / LIDAR unit** — measures aircraft lateral position and azimuth.
2. **Display panel (Pilot Display Unit, PDU)** — shows arrow / closing-rate bar / numeric distance to pilot.
3. **Central control electronics** — auto-detects aircraft type, computes guidance.
4. **Emergency stop button** — marshaller can override.

**Operational sequence (2 marks):**
1. Aircraft enters stand lead-in line → A-VDGS detects entry via break-beam sensor.
2. Display shows "Aircraft type: B777" and red/yellow/green closing rate bar.
3. Display shows lateral arrow (left/right) to correct azimuth.
4. Display shows countdown "12 m … 6 m … 3 m … STOP".
5. Automatic "STOP" at exact nose-gear stop bar; brake confirmation logged.

**Two safety interlocks (2 marks):**
1. **Overshoot prevention:** If aircraft passes stop bar, A-VDGS flashes "STOP / TOO FAR" and triggers alarm.
2. **Wrong aircraft type detection:** If A320 enters a B777 stop envelope, system halts and alerts ramp control.
3. *(Acceptable: stop bar misalignment alarm, wingtip proximity alarm.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Nose-In apron depth:**

$$D_{nose} = E_b + C_w + \frac{W_{ac}}{2} + M_{tail}$$

$$D_{nose} = 5.5 + 7.5 + \frac{65}{2} + 25$$

$$D_{nose} = 5.5 + 7.5 + 32.5 + 25 = 70.5 \text{ m}$$

*(2 marks for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer with units.)*

**(ii) Parallel apron depth:**

Given $L_{ac}$ for Code E (e.g., B777-200 = 63.7 m). Use the simplified formula:

$$D_{parallel} = L_{ac} + \frac{W_{ac}}{2} + C_w = 63.7 + 32.5 + 7.5 = 103.7 \text{ m}$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer.)*

**Percentage increase:**

$$\% \text{ increase} = \frac{103.7 - 70.5}{70.5} \times 100 = \frac{33.2}{70.5} \times 100 \approx 47.1\%$$

*(1 mark for the calculation and final answer.)*

$$\boxed{D_{nose} = 70.5 \text{ m}, \quad D_{parallel} = 103.7 \text{ m}, \quad \text{Increase} \approx 47.1\%}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing configuration names:** Students often write "nose-in" when they mean "angled nose-in." Examiners expect the **exact ICAO/AAI terminology**.
> 2. **Forgetting wingtip clearance $C_w$:** Many students compute apron depth using only the wingspan, ignoring the mandatory **7.5 m** (Code C–E) or **14.5 m** (Code F) wingtip safety clearance. **−2 marks** typical penalty.
> 3. **Mixing up push-back direction:** At a nose-in stand, the tractor pushes the aircraft **backward into the taxilane**, not forward into the terminal. Direction of push-back is **away from the terminal**.
> 4. **MARS misconception:** MARS is **not** a stand with *physical* adjustable infrastructure; it is **painted multiple stop bars** on a single stand.
> 5. **Unit omission in numerical answers:** Always state units (m, m², stands) explicitly.
> 6. **A-VDGS ≠ A-SMGCS:** A-VDGS is **stand-level** (one gate); A-SMGCS is **apron-wide** (multiple stands). Do not interchange in definitions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Module 2: Aircraft Parking Configurations and Parking Systems**

- **Five configurations:** Nose-In (90°), Angled Nose-In (45–60°), Nose-Out (90°), Angled Nose-Out (45–60°), Parallel (0°). — **Most preferred: Nose-In.**
- **Nose-in depth formula:** $D = E_b + C_w + W_{ac}/2 + M_{tail}$ — **independent of aircraft length.**
- **Parallel depth formula:** $D = L_{ac} + W_{ac}/2 + C_w$ — **~ 45–60% greater than nose-in.**
- **Apron taxiway width (ICAO):** $1.5 \times W_{ac,max}$ for straight segments.
- **Wingtip clearance $C_w$:** Minimum **7.5 m** for Code C–E; **14.5 m** for Code F (A380).
- **MARS:** Multiple painted stop bars on one stand → mixed-fleet flexibility → capital cost reduction.
- **A-VDGS:** Laser/camera-based real-time docking guidance; auto stop at nose-gear stop bar; aircraft-type detection.
- **Push-back tractor:** Required for **nose-in** departure; attaches to NLG tow bar; pushes backward into taxilane.
- **Jet blast hazard threshold:** **60 km/h** (≈ 16.7 m/s) velocity contour is the personnel-safety boundary; **never use breakaway thrust for personnel safety design.**
- **Jet blast direction advantage of nose-in:** Exhaust blows **away** from terminal — no blast fence needed.
- **Stand number design equation:** $N = \lceil AMR \times T_{occ}/60 \rceil$, then apply MARS / contact-vs-remote split (~70:30 typical).
- **Code F (A380) restriction:** Only nose-in (90°) permitted; angled/parallel configurations violate wingtip clearance.
- **Aerobridge extension limit:** Code E ≈ 5.5 m; Code F (A380) ≈ 6.5–8.5 m.
- **Stop bar function:** Painted line where the **nose gear** must be positioned; defines $E_b$.
- **Aircraft stand area (rectangular):** $A = (L_{ac}+a) \times (W_{ac}+b)$, where $a, b$ are ICAO longitudinal/lateral safety clearances.
- **Push-back clearance envelope (Code E):** typically **50–65 m** behind the stand.
- **Critical for KTU exam:** Always state **configuration name, depth formula used, ICAO code letter, and units** in the final answer line.

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Aircraft Parking Configurations and Parking Systems (PECET744, KTU 2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hangar Site Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hangar Site Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Hangar Site Selection

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **hangar** is a large enclosed structure specifically designed to house aircraft, providing protection from environmental elements (sun, rain, snow, wind) and facilitating maintenance, repair, and overhaul (MRO) operations. The term originates from the French word "hangar," meaning "shed" or "shelter."

**Hangar Site Selection** is the systematic engineering process of identifying and evaluating the most suitable geographic location within an airport's airside for the construction of aircraft hangars, considering operational efficiency, safety regulations, environmental factors, and future expansion needs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> Hangar site selection is fundamentally a **multi-criteria decision-making process** integrating airspace safety (FAA/ICAO surfaces), climatology (wind rose), operational logistics, and geometric clearance requirements.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Garage Logic"

Think of selecting a hangar site exactly like choosing the best spot to build a **multi-truck garage** on a large farm:

1. **Wind Direction Analogy**: Just as you would never build a barn door facing the direction from which strong winds frequently blow (so the door doesn't get ripped off), you wouldn't orient hangar doors into the prevailing wind, because opening a massive hangar door in a headwind creates structural pressure and dust ingestion into engines.

2. **Runway Distance Analogy**: Imagine building the garage too close to the farm's main driveway — trucks constantly passing by would be dangerous and noisy. Similarly, hangars cannot be too close to active runways, as jet blast and wake turbulence would damage parked aircraft.

3. **Sun Exposure Analogy**: A wise farmer places a barn on the leeward side of a hill, sheltered from prevailing weather. Hangar planners do the same — placing structures where they are protected from crosswinds, sun glare, and prevailing bad weather.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics at a Glance:**
> - **Hangar Floor Area**: Typically ranges from **1,500 m²** (small regional) to over **10,000 m²** (wide-body).
> - **Door Height**: Single-story clear span from **12 m to 35 m**.
> - **Standard ICAO Annex 14 Reference Code** affects clearance dimensions.

### Types of Hangars (Foundation Concept)

| Hangar Type | Typical Use | Aircraft Capacity |
|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **T-Hangar** | General Aviation | Small private aircraft (1 each) |
| **Box Hangar** | General Aviation | 1-2 small aircraft |
| **Conventional Rectangular** | Commercial/Regional | Multiple small-medium aircraft |
| **Wide-Body Hangar** | International/Heavy MRO | 1-2 large jets (B747, A380) |
| **Maintenance Hangar** | MRO Operations | Specialized tooling setup |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis: Site Selection Criteria

### The 7 Pillars of Hangar Site Selection

The selection of an optimal hangar site is governed by seven critical engineering criteria. Each must be evaluated in a structured decision matrix.

#### Pillar 1: Wind Direction & Wind Rose Analysis
The most critical factor is the prevailing wind direction, as it dictates:
- **Door orientation** (hangar doors should open into the wind, but not face the strongest winds)
- **Approach path alignment** for taxying aircraft
- **Crosswind exposure** for aircraft on the apron

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wind Rose Rule of Thumb:** Hangar doors should be oriented **perpendicular to the prevailing wind direction** with a tolerance of ± 30° for approximately **70-80% of the time** during operational hours.

#### Pillar 2: Distance from Active Runways (FAA/ICAO Compliance)
Per **FAA Advisory Circular 150/5300-13A** and **ICAO Annex 14**:

- Hangars must be **clear of Runway Protection Zones (RPZ)**.
- The structure must not penetrate **Approach Surfaces**, **Transitional Surfaces**, or **Conical Surfaces**.
- Minimum setback from runway centerline: **150 m to 300 m** depending on aircraft category.

#### Pillar 3: Topography & Soil Conditions
- **Flat terrain** preferred (gradient less than **1% to 2%**)
- **High water table** areas must be avoided
- **Soil bearing capacity** must support heavy concrete floors (typically **50 kN/m²** minimum)
- **Drainage gradient** must slope away from the hangar (1:200 minimum)

#### Pillar 4: Accessibility & Taxying Distance
- **Taxiway connectivity**: Short taxi distance from runway to hangar
- **Apron adequacy**: Sufficient space for ground service equipment (GSE)
- **Road access**: Ground transportation for personnel and parts

#### Pillar 5: Future Expansion
- Reserve **at least 100% of initial footprint** for future expansion
- Adjacent land acquisition should be planned in the master layout

#### Pillar 6: Utilities & Infrastructure
- **Power**: 3-phase high-voltage supply (typically 11 kV or 33 kV)
- **Water**: Industrial-grade supply with adequate pressure
- **Fuel**: Proximity to fuel farm (safety distance must be maintained)
- **Sewage**: Industrial effluent treatment capability

#### Pillar 7: Security & Safety
- **Airside security perimeter** compliance
- **Fire fighting access roads** (minimum 6 m wide, all-around)
- **Crash-rescue services** response time (less than 3 minutes)

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula/Parameter | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| **Wind Frequency Index (WFI)** | $\text{WFI} = \sum_{i=1}^{16} (P_i \cdot \cos\theta_i)$ | % | Door orientation optimization |
| **Crosswind Component** | $C_w = V \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | m/s | Runway alignment check |
| **Runway Setback (FAA Part 77)** | $S = 0.2 \cdot (h - 5)$ for inner approach | m | Height-restriction check |
| **Drainage Gradient** | $G = \frac{\Delta h}{L} \times 100$ | % | Site grading |
| **Hangar Floor Area (single wide-body)** | $A = L \cdot W + 2(L \cdot 3) + (W \cdot 3)$ | m² | Area estimation |
| **Aircraft Turning Radius** | $R = \frac{WB}{\sin(\phi_{max})}$ | m | Apron design |
| **Transitional Surface Slope** | $1:7$ (FAA) or $1:5$ (ICAO) | ratio | Obstruction clearance |
| **Clear Span Requirement** | $W_{min} = W_{a/c} + 2 \cdot S_{c}$ | m | Hangar door width |

Where:
- $P_i$ = Wind percentage in direction $i$
- $\theta_i$ = Angle deviation from optimal orientation
- $V$ = Wind speed
- $h$ = Structure height in meters
- $WB$ = Aircraft wheelbase
- $\phi_{max}$ = Maximum nose wheel steering angle (typically ± 75°)
- $W_{a/c}$ = Aircraft wingspan
- $S_c$ = Side clearance (**3 m minimum** for wide-body)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Wind Frequency Index (WFI)** is the most commonly tested KTU parameter — it directly determines hangar door orientation to minimize structural fatigue and dust ingestion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Optimal Hangar Door Orientation Using Wind Rose Data

**Problem Setup:** Given the following wind data (16-point wind rose), determine the optimal hangar door orientation.

| Wind Direction (N=0°) | Frequency (%) | Mean Speed (m/s) |
|----------------------|---------------|------------------|
| 360° (N) | 8 | 4.2 |
| 30° (NNE) | 6 | 3.8 |
| 60° (ENE) | 4 | 3.5 |
| 90° (E) | 5 | 4.0 |
| 120° (ESE) | 7 | 4.5 |
| 150° (SSE) | 12 | 5.0 |
| 180° (S) | 18 | 5.5 |
| 210° (SSW) | 15 | 5.2 |
| 240° (WSW) | 10 | 4.8 |
| 270° (W) | 6 | 4.0 |
| 300° (WNW) | 4 | 3.5 |
| 330° (NNW) | 5 | 3.8 |

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

The total weight of each wind direction must be computed. We use the formula:

$$W = P \cdot V^2$$

where $P$ is the frequency (%) and $V$ is the mean wind speed (m/s). The factor $V^2$ represents the **dynamic pressure** proportional to wind force.

**Step 1: Calculate wind force index for each direction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N (360°):\quad W_1 &= 8 \times (4.2)^2 = 8 \times 17.64 = 141.12 \\
NNE (30°):\quad W_2 &= 6 \times (3.8)^2 = 6 \times 14.44 = 86.64 \\
ENE (60°):\quad W_3 &= 4 \times (3.5)^2 = 4 \times 12.25 = 49.00 \\
E (90°):\quad W_4 &= 5 \times (4.0)^2 = 5 \times 16.00 = 80.00 \\
ESE (120°):\quad W_5 &= 7 \times (4.5)^2 = 7 \times 20.25 = 141.75 \\
SSE (150°):\quad W_6 &= 12 \times (5.0)^2 = 12 \times 25.00 = 300.00 \\
S (180°):\quad W_7 &= 18 \times (5.5)^2 = 18 \times 30.25 = 544.50 \\
SSW (210°):\quad W_8 &= 15 \times (5.2)^2 = 15 \times 27.04 = 405.60 \\
WSW (240°):\quad W_9 &= 10 \times (4.8)^2 = 10 \times 23.04 = 230.40 \\
W (270°):\quad W_{10} &= 6 \times (4.0)^2 = 6 \times 16.00 = 96.00 \\
WNW (300°):\quad W_{11} &= 4 \times (3.5)^2 = 4 \times 12.25 = 49.00 \\
NNW (330°):\quad W_{12} &= 5 \times (3.8)^2 = 5 \times 14.44 = 72.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Resolve each wind vector into East-West and North-South components.**

Using $\theta$ measured clockwise from North:
- East component: $E_i = W_i \cdot \sin(\theta_i)$
- North component: $N_i = W_i \cdot \cos(\theta_i)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= \sum_{i=1}^{12} W_i \cdot \sin(\theta_i) \\
N_{total} &= \sum_{i=1}^{12} W_i \cdot \cos(\theta_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute total East-West resultant.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 141.12 \sin(0°) + 86.64 \sin(30°) + 49.00 \sin(60°) \\
&\quad + 80.00 \sin(90°) + 141.75 \sin(120°) + 300.00 \sin(150°) \\
&\quad + 544.50 \sin(180°) + 405.60 \sin(210°) + 230.40 \sin(240°) \\
&\quad + 96.00 \sin(270°) + 49.00 \sin(300°) + 72.20 \sin(330°)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 0 + 43.32 + 42.44 + 80.00 + 122.76 + 150.00 \\
&\quad + 0 - 203.16 - 199.53 - 96.00 - 42.44 - 36.10
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
E_{total} = -138.71 \text{ (Westward bias)}
$$

**Step 4: Compute total North-South resultant.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= 141.12 \cos(0°) + 86.64 \cos(30°) + 49.00 \cos(60°) \\
&\quad + 80.00 \cos(90°) + 141.75 \cos(120°) + 300.00 \cos(150°) \\
&\quad + 544.50 \cos(180°) + 405.60 \cos(210°) + 230.40 \cos(240°) \\
&\quad + 96.00 \cos(270°) + 49.00 \cos(300°) + 72.20 \cos(330°)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= 141.12 + 75.04 + 24.50 + 0 - 70.88 - 259.81 \\
&\quad - 544.50 - 351.07 - 115.20 + 0 + 24.50 + 62.52
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
N_{total} = -1013.78 \text{ (Strong southward bias)}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the resultant wind direction.**

$$
\theta_{resultant} = \arctan\left(\frac{E_{total}}{N_{total}}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{-138.71}{-1013.78}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_{resultant} = \arctan(0.1368) = 7.79° \text{ in the SW quadrant}
$$

Adding 180° since both components are negative:

$$\theta_{resultant} = 180° + 7.79° = 187.79° \approx 188°$$

**Step 6: Determine optimal hangar door orientation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Principle:** Hangar doors should be oriented **perpendicular** to the resultant wind vector. This means the door normal (center-line) points **into the prevailing wind**, allowing the door to open without working against strong winds.

$$
\boxed{\theta_{door} = 188° - 90° = 98° \text{ (i.e., door faces East-West, opening to the South-Southwest)}}
$$

Equivalently, the door opening direction is **188° (S)**, meaning the hangar door opens facing the resultant wind source.

---

### Derivation 2: Hangar Floor Area Calculation for Wide-Body Aircraft

**Given:** A maintenance hangar is to be designed for one Boeing 747-400 aircraft.

**Aircraft Dimensions (B747-400):**
- Length (L) = **70.6 m**
- Wingspan (W) = **64.4 m**
- Height (H) = **19.4 m**
- Tail height = 19.4 m

**Step 1: Determine the minimum hangar clear span (width).**

The width must accommodate the wingspan plus side maintenance access:

$$
W_{hangar} = W_{a/c} + 2 \cdot S_c
$$

Substituting values:

$$
W_{hangar} = 64.4 + 2 \times 7.5 = 64.4 + 15.0 = 79.4 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Determine the minimum hangar length.**

The length must accommodate the aircraft length plus nose and tail access for tow tractors and maintenance stands:

$$
L_{hangar} = L_{a/c} + L_{nose} + L_{tail}
$$

$$
L_{hangar} = 70.6 + 7.5 + 7.5 = 85.6 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate minimum clear height (vertical clearance).**

The clear height must accommodate the tail height plus overhead crane and lighting:

$$
H_{hangar} = H_{tail} + H_{crane} + H_{clearance}
$$

$$
H_{hangar} = 19.4 + 3.0 + 2.6 = 25.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the floor area.**

$$
A_{hangar} = W_{hangar} \times L_{hangar}
$$

$$
A_{hangar} = 79.4 \times 85.6
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{hangar} = 6796.64 \text{ m}^2 \approx 6800 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**Step 5: Add auxiliary space (offices, parts storage, tool cribs).**

Typically **20% to 30%** additional area is added:

$$
A_{total} = 1.25 \times 6800 = 8500 \text{ m}^2
$$

---

### Derivation 3: Setback from Runway Centerline (FAA Part 77 Surface)

**Given:** A proposed hangar with a maximum height of **25 m** is to be located near a runway.

**FAA Part 77 Inner Approach Surface slope:** **50:1** (50 horizontal : 1 vertical) for the first 1500 m, then **40:1** for the next 4500 m.

**Step 1: Compute horizontal distance to clear inner approach surface.**

$$
d = h \times 50
$$

Substituting $h = 25$ m:

$$
d = 25 \times 50 = 1250 \text{ m from runway threshold}
$$

**Step 2: Compute transitional surface clearance.**

The transitional surface has a slope of **7:1** outward from the runway edge. If the runway is **45 m wide**, the edge is **22.5 m** from the centerline. For a 25 m high structure:

$$
d_{trans} = h \times 7
$$

$$
d_{trans} = 25 \times 7 = 175 \text{ m lateral from runway edge}
$$

Total lateral setback from centerline:

$$
d_{lateral,total} = 22.5 + 175 = 197.5 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Setback Requirement:** The hangar must be located at least **1250 m from the runway threshold** (longitudinally) and **197.5 m from the runway centerline** (laterally) to satisfy FAA Part 77 imaginary surfaces.

---

### Derivation 4: Aircraft Maneuvering Curve on the Apron

**Given:** A Boeing 737-800 with wheelbase WB = **12.6 m** and maximum nose wheel steering angle $\phi_{max}$ = **75°**.

**Step 1: Compute the minimum turning radius.**

$$
R_{min} = \frac{WB}{\sin(\phi_{max})}
$$

$$
R_{min} = \frac{12.6}{\sin(75°)} = \frac{12.6}{0.9659}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{min} = 13.04 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 2: Add safety margin (typically 50% for apron design).**

$$
R_{design} = 1.5 \times R_{min} = 1.5 \times 13.04 = 19.56 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to hangar door width allowance.**

The hangar door width should allow the aircraft to enter straight without excessive maneuvering:

$$
W_{door} = 2 \cdot R_{design} = 2 \times 19.56 = 39.12 \text{ m}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4.1 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Hangar Site Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Airport Master Plan]) --> stepA[Identify Candidate Zones]
    stepA --> stepB{Wind Direction Analyzed?}
    stepB -->|No| stepC[Construct Wind Rose]
    stepC --> stepD[Compute Resultant Vector]
    stepD --> stepB
    stepB -->|Yes| stepE{Runway Separation OK?}
    stepE -->|No| stepA
    stepE -->|Yes| stepF{Topography Suitable?}
    stepF -->|No| stepA
    stepF -->|Yes| stepG{Utilities Available?}
    stepG -->|No| stepA
    stepG -->|Yes| stepH{Future Expansion Possible?}
    stepH -->|No| stepA
    stepH -->|Yes| stepI{Fire Safety Compliance?}
    stepI -->|No| stepA
    stepI -->|Yes| finalNode([OPTIMAL HANGAR SITE SELECTED])
    stepA --> inputData[Geotechnical Data, Wind Records, Master Plan]
    inputData --> stepA
```

### Diagram 2: Hangar Site Clearance Zone Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ZONE_A[Inner Approach Surface Zone]
        A1[Inner Approach: 50:1 slope]
        A2[Length: First 1500 m from threshold]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_B[Transitional Surface Zone]
        B1[Rises 7:1 from runway edges]
        B2[Extends outward to 150 m height cone]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_C[Horizontal Surface Zone]
        C1[45 m elevation cap around runway]
        C2[Radius: 4000 m from runway midpoint]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_D[Hangar Building Footprint]
        D1[Floor: 6800 sq m for B747]
        D2[Doors face resultant wind]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
```

### Diagram 3: Hangar Types Classification Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootNode((HANGAR TYPES)) --> typeA[T-HANGAR]
    rootNode --> typeB[BOX HANGAR]
    rootNode --> typeC[RECTANGULAR]
    rootNode --> typeD[WIDE BODY]
    typeA --> subA1[GA Aircraft]
    typeA --> subA2[Nested T Shape]
    typeB --> subB1[1-2 GA Aircraft]
    typeB --> subB2[Single Door]
    typeC --> subC1[Multiple Small Jets]
    typeC --> subC2[Multi Span Doors]
    typeD --> subD1[B747 and A380]
    typeD --> subD2[Clear Span Truss Roof]
```

### Diagram 4: Aircraft Clearance Geometry Block Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    start1([Aircraft Positioning]) --> stepA1[Tail Clearance Check]
    stepA1 --> stepA2[Side Wingtip Clearance]
    stepA2 --> stepA3[Nose Gear Pivot Radius]
    stepA3 --> stepA4[Overhead Crane Sweep]
    stepA4 --> stepA5[Door Track Path]
    stepA5 --> end1([Clearance Validated])
    stepA1 -.-> sideA[Min 7.5 m from tail]
    stepA2 -.-> sideB[Min 7.5 m from wingtip]
    stepA3 -.-> sideC[Turning radius 13 m plus safety]
    stepA4 -.-> sideD[3 m below roof truss]
    stepA5 -.-> sideE[Horizontal sliding door]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

### PART A (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Define a hangar. List any four factors that influence hangar site selection at an airport.**

**Model Answer:**

A **hangar** is a large enclosed structure designed to house aircraft, providing protection from weather and facilitating maintenance, repair, and overhaul (MRO) operations.

**Four factors influencing hangar site selection:**

1. **Wind direction** (wind rose analysis) – determines door orientation
2. **Distance from active runways** – must satisfy FAA Part 77 / ICAO Annex 14 imaginary surfaces
3. **Topography and soil bearing capacity** – flat, well-drained terrain preferred
4. **Availability of utilities** – power, water, fuel, sewage connections
5. **Future expansion potential** – reserve adjacent land
6. **Fire safety and accessibility** – crash-rescue response time, road access

*(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks)*

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**What is a wind rose? Why is it important in hangar site selection?**

**Model Answer:**

A **wind rose** is a circular graphical representation of wind data for a specific location, showing:
- Wind **direction** (16-point compass)
- Wind **frequency** (percentage of time)
- Wind **speed** (typically grouped into ranges)

**Importance in Hangar Site Selection:**

The wind rose is used to determine the **prevailing wind direction**, which directly governs:
- **Hangar door orientation** — doors should open into the wind but not face the strongest winds
- **Apron layout** — aircraft should be parked with nose into wind when possible
- **Taxiway alignment** — to reduce crosswind effects on taxying aircraft
- **Ventilation planning** — for engine test cells and paint shops

By computing the **resultant wind vector**, planners optimize door orientation to minimize structural stress, dust ingestion, and fuel vapor accumulation inside the hangar.

---

### PART B (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven critical factors** that govern the selection of a hangar site at an airport. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A maintenance hangar is proposed to be built for a **Boeing 777-300ER** aircraft. The aircraft has the following dimensions: Length = 73.9 m, Wingspan = 64.8 m, Height = 18.5 m. Determine the minimum hangar dimensions (length, width, clear height) and total floor area including 25% allowance for auxiliary spaces. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The seven critical factors governing hangar site selection are:

**1. Wind Direction (Wind Rose Analysis)**
The prevailing wind direction is the single most important factor. The resultant wind vector is computed, and hangar doors are oriented **perpendicular** to it. This minimizes wind pressure on closed doors and allows safe door opening during high winds.

**2. Distance from Runways (Airspace Compliance)**
The hangar must lie **clear of imaginary surfaces** defined in FAA Part 77 / ICAO Annex 14:
- Inner Approach Surface: 50:1 slope
- Transitional Surface: 7:1 slope
- Conical Surface: 20:1 slope outward

A typical minimum setback is **150-300 m from the runway centerline** depending on aircraft category.

**3. Topography and Drainage**
- Gradient less than **1-2%**
- **High water table** avoided
- Storm-water drainage designed with **1:200 minimum slope** away from the building
- Soil bearing capacity minimum **50 kN/m²** to support heavy floor loads

**4. Accessibility and Taxying Distance**
- Short taxi distance from runway to hangar (fuel savings, reduced congestion)
- Adequate **apron space** (typically 1.5× hangar floor area)
- Direct **road access** for personnel and equipment

**5. Future Expansion**
- Reserve at least **100% of the initial footprint** for future hangar bays
- Plan for **2× to 3× growth** in MRO capacity over 20-30 years

**6. Utilities Infrastructure**
- High-voltage 3-phase power (11 kV / 33 kV)
- Industrial-grade water supply
- Proximity to fuel farm (with safety distance)
- Compressed air, fire-fighting water mains

**7. Security and Fire Safety**
- Airside perimeter security
- **Crash-rescue response time** less than 3 minutes
- All-around fire tender access road (minimum 6 m wide)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Aircraft length $L_{a/c} = 73.9$ m
- Aircraft wingspan $W_{a/c} = 64.8$ m
- Aircraft height $H_{a/c} = 18.5$ m

**Step 1: Compute minimum hangar width.**

The width must accommodate the wingspan plus side maintenance access of **7.5 m** on each side:

$$
W_{hangar} = W_{a/c} + 2 \times S_c
$$

$$
W_{hangar} = 64.8 + 2 \times 7.5 = 64.8 + 15.0 = 79.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Compute minimum hangar length.**

The length must accommodate aircraft length plus nose access (7.5 m) and tail access (7.5 m):

$$
L_{hangar} = L_{a/c} + L_{nose} + L_{tail}
$$

$$
L_{hangar} = 73.9 + 7.5 + 7.5 = 88.9 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Compute minimum clear height.**

The clear height must accommodate aircraft height, plus overhead crane (3.0 m), plus safety clearance (2.5 m):

$$
H_{hangar} = H_{a/c} + H_{crane} + H_{clearance}
$$

$$
H_{hangar} = 18.5 + 3.0 + 2.5 = 24.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the base floor area.**

$$
A_{base} = W_{hangar} \times L_{hangar}
$$

$$
A_{base} = 79.8 \times 88.9 = 7094.22 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Add 25% allowance for auxiliary spaces.**

$$
A_{total} = 1.25 \times 7094.22
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{total} = 8867.78 \text{ m}^2 \approx 8868 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Hangar Width | 79.8 m |
| Hangar Length | 88.9 m |
| Clear Height | 24.0 m |
| Base Floor Area | 7094.22 m² |
| **Total Floor Area (with 25% allowance)** | **8867.78 m²** |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With the help of a sketch, describe the **FAA Part 77 imaginary surfaces** relevant to hangar construction. How do they influence the location of a hangar relative to the runway? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A proposed hangar site lies **250 m from the runway threshold** and **180 m from the runway centerline**. The runway is **45 m wide**. The hangar height is **22 m**. Using the FAA Part 77 inner approach (50:1) and transitional (7:1) surface slopes, check whether the hangar violates the imaginary surfaces. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **FAA Part 77 imaginary surfaces** are protective airspace surfaces defined around a runway. The surfaces relevant to hangar siting are:

**1. Primary Surface**
A rectangular surface symmetrically located about the runway centerline. It extends **60 m beyond each runway end** and has a width equal to the runway width plus **30 m on each side** (for a 45 m runway: 45 + 60 = 105 m wide).

**2. Horizontal Surface**
An elliptical (or circular) surface located **45 m above the runway elevation**, with a radius of approximately **4000 m** from the runway midpoint. No object may penetrate this surface.

**3. Conical Surface**
Extends outward from the horizontal surface at a slope of **20:1** (20 horizontal : 1 vertical) for a horizontal distance of **2100 m**.

**4. Approach Surface**
- **Inner section** (first 1500 m from threshold): slope **50:1**, width expands from runway width to **300 m**
- **Outer section** (next 4500 m): slope **40:1**, width expands from 300 m to **1200 m**

**5. Transitional Surface**
Rises at a slope of **7:1** from the edges of the primary surface and approach surface.

**Influence on Hangar Location:**
A hangar must lie entirely **below** all these surfaces. This typically requires the structure to be:
- Located at least **150-300 m laterally** from the runway centerline
- At a sufficient longitudinal distance from the threshold
- Limited in height to remain clear of the conical and approach surfaces

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Distance from runway threshold (longitudinal): $d_L = 250$ m
- Distance from runway centerline (lateral): $d_{lat} = 180$ m
- Runway width: $W_{rw} = 45$ m
- Hangar height: $h = 22$ m
- Inner approach slope: 50:1
- Transitional slope: 7:1

**Step 1: Compute the maximum allowable height under the inner approach surface.**

The inner approach surface is 50:1, meaning it rises 1 m vertically for every 50 m horizontally from the threshold:

$$
h_{allowable,approach} = \frac{d_L}{50}
$$

$$
h_{allowable,approach} = \frac{250}{50} = 5.0 \text{ m}
$$

Since the hangar is 250 m from the threshold, the approach surface at that point is only 5 m high. The hangar at **22 m violates** the approach surface.

**Step 2: Compute the lateral distance from the runway edge.**

The runway edge is at:
$$
d_{edge} = \frac{W_{rw}}{2} = \frac{45}{2} = 22.5 \text{ m from centerline}
$$

Lateral distance from runway edge:
$$
d_{from\,edge} = 180 - 22.5 = 157.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the maximum allowable height under the transitional surface.**

The transitional surface rises 7:1 from the runway edge:

$$
h_{allowable,transitional} = 7 \times d_{from\,edge}
$$

Wait — the correct interpretation is that the transitional surface slope is **7 horizontal : 1 vertical** outward. So the height at distance $d$ from the edge is:

$$
h_{trans} = \frac{d_{from\,edge}}{7}
$$

$$
h_{trans} = \frac{157.5}{7} = 22.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Compare with hangar height.**

The hangar is 22 m tall, and the transitional surface allows up to 22.5 m at 157.5 m from the runway edge. Therefore, the hangar **clears the transitional surface** by a margin of 0.5 m (very tight).

**Step 5: Final Assessment.**

| Surface | Allowable Height | Hangar Height | Status |
|---------|------------------|---------------|--------|
| Inner Approach (at 250 m) | 5.0 m | 22 m | **VIOLATION** |
| Transitional (at 157.5 m) | 22.5 m | 22 m | OK (margin 0.5 m) |

**Conclusion:** The proposed hangar site **violates the FAA Part 77 inner approach surface** at the given longitudinal distance of 250 m. The site is **not acceptable** unless either:
- The hangar is moved further from the threshold (minimum distance = $22 \times 50 = 1100$ m), **OR**
- The hangar height is reduced below 5 m at this location, **OR**
- The site is shifted laterally to remain only under the transitional surface (which is acceptable at 157.5 m lateral distance for a 22 m structure, but the longitudinal violation still applies).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - Many students confuse **7:1** as "1 horizontal : 7 vertical." The correct interpretation per FAA is **7 horizontal : 1 vertical** (the surface is gentle, not steep).
> - Do not forget to subtract the **runway half-width (22.5 m)** from the lateral distance before applying the transitional surface.
> - Always check **both** the approach and transitional surfaces — even if one passes, the other may fail.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember:**

- **Hangar** = enclosed structure housing aircraft; primary function = protection + MRO.
- **Wind rose** = graphical tool; resultant vector determines door orientation.
- **Optimum door orientation** = perpendicular to resultant wind vector.
- **Wind Frequency Index (WFI)** = $\sum P_i \cos\theta_i$ for door optimization.
- **FAA Part 77 inner approach surface** = 50:1 slope for first 1500 m.
- **FAA Part 77 transitional surface** = 7:1 slope from runway edge.
- **FAA Part 77 conical surface** = 20:1 slope, 2100 m horizontal extent.
- **Hangar width** = wingspan + 2 × side clearance (typically 7.5 m each side).
- **Hangar length** = aircraft length + 7.5 m nose + 7.5 m tail.
- **Hangar clear height** = aircraft height + crane (3 m) + clearance (2.5 m).
- **Side clearance** minimum = 7.5 m for wide-body aircraft.
- **Floor area allowance** = 20-30% extra for offices, parts storage, tool cribs.
- **Drainage gradient** = minimum 1:200 (0.5%) away from building.
- **Soil bearing capacity** = minimum 50 kN/m².
- **Crash-rescue response** = less than 3 minutes.
- **Fire access road** = minimum 6 m wide, all-around perimeter.
- **Future expansion** = reserve at least 100% of initial footprint.
- **T-hangar** = for single GA aircraft; **Box hangar** = 1-2 GA; **Wide-body hangar** = B747/A380 class.
- **Turning radius formula** = $R = WB / \sin(\phi_{max})$.
- **Aircraft category** determines minimum setback distance from runway.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Hangar Site Selection**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) | PECET744 Airport Planning and Design*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning and Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning and Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planning and Design Considerations** in airport engineering refer to the **systematic, multi-disciplinary framework** that integrates aeronautical, architectural, structural, environmental, and operational requirements to convert an airport master plan into **physically constructible and operationally efficient infrastructure**. According to ICAO Annex 14 and FAA Advisory Circular 150/5070-6B, this includes terminal building layout, apron configuration, taxiway geometry, passenger processing facilities, cargo handling zones, and landside access systems.

The process is governed by three foundational principles:
1. **Functional Efficiency** – minimizing passenger walking distance and aircraft taxiing time.
2. **Capacity Adequacy** – sizing facilities to meet the **Design Hour (DH)** or **Ultimate Design Aircraft (UDA)** demand.
3. **Future Expandability** – modular design enabling phased growth up to the **ultimate planning horizon (usually 20–30 years)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an airport as a **"City's Living Room"** that must simultaneously serve three different types of guests:
> - **Air-side guests** (aircraft) → need runways, taxiways, and aprons (like a driveway + garage).
> - **Terminal-side guests** (passengers) → need lobbies, check-in counters, gates, and security (like a hotel lobby).
> - **Landside guests** (vehicles) → need roads, parking, and metro links (like the city street outside).
>
> **Planning & Design Considerations** is essentially the **"interior architect's blueprint"** that arranges these three zones so they never interfere with each other yet remain seamlessly connected.

## 1.3 Key Design Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Standard Value | Authority |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak Hour Passenger Volume (PHPV)** | 3,000 – 80,000 pax/hr | IATA ADRM |
| **Aircraft Apron Area per Code C aircraft** | **3,600 m²** (with taxilane) | ICAO Annex 14 |
| **Gate Occupancy Time (GOT)** | 30 – 60 minutes (domestic) | IATA |
| **Terminal Floor Area per Peak Hour Pax** | 16 – 30 m²/pax | IATA Level of Service B |
| **Design Aircraft Wingspan (Code E)** | 65 m (e.g., B777, A330) | ICAO |
| **Ultimate Planning Horizon** | **20 Years** | FAA AC 150/5070-6B |
| **Stand-off distance from runway centerline** | **150 m** (Code 4) | ICAO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PECET744 specifically focuses on the **airside, terminal, and landside integration** — students must remember that these are **NOT designed independently**; any change in one zone cascades a redesign in the others.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Planning Pyramid

The KTU syllabus structures airport planning under a **three-tier hierarchy**:

### **Tier 1: Airport Master Plan (AMP)**
- **Purpose:** Defines the airport's vision, growth strategy, and ultimate capacity.
- **Components:** Regional setting, demand forecasting, capacity analysis, site selection, facility requirements, development concept, and financial plan.
- **Horizon:** Short (0–5 yrs), Intermediate (6–10 yrs), Long (11–20 yrs).

### **Tier 2: Airport Layout Plan (ALP)**
- **Purpose:** Geometric layout of runways, taxiways, aprons, and terminal area.
- **Constraint:** Wind rose analysis (crosswind ≤ 20.6 km/hr per ICAO).

### **Tier 3: Detailed Design Drawings**
- **Purpose:** Engineering-level drawings for construction — pavement design, terminal structural layout, HVAC, baggage handling systems (BHS), and visual aids.

## 2.2 Seven Critical Design Considerations (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> Each consideration below is a **favourite KTU Part A (3-mark) topic** — at least one is asked every examination cycle.

### **Consideration 1: Selection of Design Aircraft**
- **Why:** All pavement thickness, gate sizing, and taxiway width depend on the **most demanding aircraft** expected in the design period.
- **How:** ICAO Reference Code uses the **Aerodrome Reference Field Length (ARFL)** and **Wingspan** to classify aircraft into Reference Codes (A → F) and Aerodrome Codes (1 → 4).
- **Real-world:** Mumbai airport terminal was expanded around the **B777-300ER** (Code E), forcing all gates to handle 65 m wingspans.

### **Consideration 2: Passenger Flow Analysis**
- **Why:** Determines terminal dimensions and number of security lanes.
- **How:** Uses **Level of Service (LoS)** standards — LoS C is the KTU design benchmark.
- **How measured:** Maximum walking distance from curb to gate ≤ **300 m** for LoS C.

### **Consideration 3: Aircraft Movement Area Geometry**
- **Why:** Taxiway and runway separation prevents jet blast damage.
- **How:** Standard separation = **97.5 m** (between parallel taxiway centerline and runway centerline) for Code 4.

### **Consideration 4: Cargo and Ground Support**
- **Why:** Cargo terminals require **Level loading docks**, bonded warehousing, and segregation from passenger areas.
- **How:** Cargo area = **0.04 × Total Terminal Area** (rule of thumb per IATA).

### **Consideration 5: Landside Access**
- **Why:** A 5% increase in landside access capacity reduces terminal dwell time by 8%.
- **How:** Curb frontage = **1.5 m per Peak Hour Passenger** (IATA).

### **Consideration 6: Environmental & Obstacle Limitation**
- **Why:** Triggers like bird-strike zones, noise contours (NEF 40 dB), and obstacle height restrictions (Conical surface slope 1:20).
- **How:** Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) is mandatory for >50 hectare airports in India.

### **Consideration 7: Sustainability & Resiliency**
- **Why:** Green buildings (GRIHA 4-star rating is the new MoEF norm).
- **How:** Use of **LEED-certified** terminal design (e.g., Hyderabad RGIA is 100% solar-powered).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it covers 70% of Part B derivations asked in the University Examination.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Peak Hour Demand (PHPV)** | $\text{PHPV} = \text{Annual Pax} \times 0.00035$ | pax/hr | Gate sizing |
| 2 | **Terminal Area Required** | $A_t = \text{PHPV} \times A_{\text{per pax}}$ | m² | Terminal design |
| 3 | **Number of Gates** | $n_g = \dfrac{\text{PHPV}}{G \times U$ | gates | Apron design |
| 4 | **Apron Depth (ICAO)** | $D = \text{WS} + 2 \times \text{MS} + \text{ITWS}$ | m | Apron layout |
| 5 | **Gate Occupancy Time (GOT)** | $\text{GOT} = T_a + T_b + T_s$ | minutes | Capacity calc. |
| 6 | **Runway-Taxiway Separation** | $S_{RT} = 97.5 \text{ m (Code 4)}$ | m | Airside geometry |
| 7 | **Wind Coverage** | $W_c = \dfrac{N_{cw}}{N_t} \times 100 \geq 95\%$ | % | Runway orientation |
| 8 | **Curb Frontage Length** | $L_c = 1.5 \times \text{PHPV}$ | m | Landside |
| 9 | **Annual Service Volume (ASV)** | $\text{ASV} = C \times D \times H$ | movements/yr | Capacity analysis |
| 10 | **Basic Runway Length correction (Altitude)** | $L_{\text{corrected}} = L_0 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{0.07 \times h}{300}\right)$ | m | Runway design |

> ⚠️ **Pipeline note:** In the formula $n_g = \dfrac{\text{PHPV}}{G \times U}$, the parameters are $G = \text{gate throughput}$ (pax/hr) and $U = \text{utilization factor (0.8)}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation Authorities:** Use these formulas for **Aerodrome Certification** under Rule 78 of the Aircraft Rules, India.
- **Consultancy:** Firms like AECOM, Mott MacDonald, and Dar Al-Handasah deploy these exact equations in **Greenfield Airport Design** (e.g., Navi Mumbai International Airport).
- **Research:** IATA's **Airport Development Reference Manual (ADRM, 11th Edition)** is the global bible; KTU 2024 syllabus directly references its Level of Service standards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Determination of Number of Aircraft Stands (Gates)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **guaranteed** in the KTU End Semester Examination — last 5 consecutive years carry this question.

### **Given Data (Typical Exam Question)**
- Annual passengers = **30,00,000**
- Peak Hour Factor = 0.00035
- Gate throughput $G$ = 200 pax/hr
- Utilization factor $U$ = 0.85
- Average Gate Occupancy Time = **45 minutes**

### **Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1 — Compute Peak Hour Passenger Volume (PHPV)**

$$
\text{PHPV} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.00035
$$

$$
\text{PHPV} = 1050 \text{ passengers/hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Number of Gates Required**

$$
n_g = \dfrac{\text{PHPV}}{G \times U}
$$

$$
n_g = \dfrac{1050}{200 \times 0.85}
$$

$$
n_g = \dfrac{1050}{170}
$$

$$
n_g = 6.17 \rightarrow 7 \text{ gates (round up)}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-check with Gate Occupancy Time**

A single gate can handle $\dfrac{60}{45} = 1.33$ aircraft/hr.

So the **practical handling capacity** per gate is:

$$
C_g = \dfrac{60}{\text{GOT}} \times G
$$

$$
C_g = 1.33 \times 200 = 266 \text{ pax/hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Validation**

The total system capacity with 7 gates:

$$
C_{total} = 7 \times 266 = 1862 \text{ pax/hr}
$$

Since 1862 > 1050, the design is **safe** with **25% spare capacity** for peak surges.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuer's Trick:** Always add **15–25% spare capacity** to the design — this is the universal "thumb rule" the examiner expects to see written.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Apron Area Calculation (ICAO Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a 14-mark favourite** — appearing almost every alternate semester.

### **Given Data**
- Aircraft Reference Code = **C** (e.g., A320, B737)
- Wingspan (WS) = **36 m**
- Maximum length (ML) of aircraft = **38 m**
- Taxilane width (TLW) = **25 m** (ICAO Code C)
- Inter-taxilane wingtip separation (ITWS) = **6 m**
- Maneuvering strip (MS) = **4.5 m** (each side)

### **Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1 — Apron Width Formula (Perpendicular to Taxilane)**

$$
W_a = \text{ML} + 2 \times \text{MS} = 38 + 2(4.5) = 47 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apron Depth Formula (Along Taxilane)**

$$
D_a = \text{WS} + 2 \times \text{MS} + \text{ITWS} = 36 + 9 + 6 = 51 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apron Area for Single Aircraft**

$$
A_a = W_a \times D_a = 47 \times 51 = 2397 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Add Service Road Buffer (10% extra)**

$$
A_{\text{total}} = 1.10 \times 2397 = 2636.7 \text{ m}^2 \approx 2637 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Against ICAO Code C Standard**

ICAO Annex 14, Table 3-2 specifies: **2,820 m² (stand + taxilane combined)**.

Our value 2637 m² (without taxilane) is acceptable; **KTU full credit** is given.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Framework for Planning Considerations

| Design Factor | Critical Threshold | KTU Standard | Penalty if Ignored |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wind Coverage | ≥ **95%** | ICAO Doc 9188 | Crosswind accidents |
| Peak Hour Factor | **0.00035** | IATA ADRM | Terminal overcrowding |
| Gate Occupancy | 30 – 60 min | IATA | Gate under-utilization |
| Runway Separation | 97.5 m (Code 4) | ICAO Annex 14 | Jet blast damage |
| Terminal Curbage | 1.5 m/pax | IATA | Traffic congestion |
| Apron Slope | **0.5 – 1.0%** | FAA AC 150/5320-6F | Fuel pooling, FOD |
| Fire Access Road Width | ≥ **6 m** | NFPA 403 | Rescuer delay |
| Obstacle Height | Conical 1:20 | ICAO Annex 14 | Aircraft strike |
| Noise Contour | ≤ 65 dB residential | MoEF India | Litigation |
| Terminal Setback | ≥ **300 m** from runway | ICAO | Crash hazard |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Airport Planning Hierarchy (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Airport Master Plan AMP] --> B0[Regional Setting Analysis]
    A0 --> C0[Demand Forecasting]
    A0 --> D0[Site Selection]
    A0 --> E0[Capacity and Facility Requirements]
    A0 --> F0[Development Concept]
    A0 --> G0[Financial Feasibility]
    B0 --> H0[Airport Layout Plan ALP]
    C0 --> H0
    D0 --> H0
    E0 --> H0
    F0 --> H0
    G0 --> H0
    H0 --> I0[Airside Geometry]
    H0 --> J0[Terminal Area Plan]
    H0 --> K0[Landside Access Plan]
    H0 --> L0[Support Facilities]
    I0 --> M0[Detailed Design Drawings]
    J0 --> M0
    K0 --> M0
    L0 --> M0
    M0 --> N0[Construction Phase]
    N0 --> O0[Operational Commissioning]
```

## 4.2 Terminal Passenger Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PA1[Landside Curb Drop] --> PA2[Terminal Forecourt]
    PA2 --> PA3[Departure Check In Hall]
    PA3 --> PA4[Security Screening Zone]
    PA4 --> PA5[Departure Concourse]
    PA5 --> PA6[Gate Holdroom]
    PA6 --> PA7[Aircraft Boarding Bridge]
    PA8[Arrival Aircraft] --> PA9[Arrival Gate]
    PA9 --> PA10[Immigration and Customs]
    PA10 --> PA11[Baggage Claim Hall]
    PA11 --> PA12[Landside Exit]
    PA12 --> PA13[Parking and Transport Hub]
```

## 4.3 Apron and Gate Layout Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AP0[Runway Threshold] --> AP1[Exit Taxiway]
    AP1 --> AP2[Parallel Taxiway]
    AP2 --> AP3[Apron Taxilane]
    AP3 --> AP4A[GATE 01 STAND 01]
    AP3 --> AP4B[GATE 02 STAND 02]
    AP3 --> AP4C[GATE 03 STAND 03]
    AP3 --> AP4D[GATE 04 STAND 04]
    AP3 --> AP4E[GATE 05 STAND 05]
    AP4A --> AP5[Service Road Network]
    AP4B --> AP5
    AP4C --> AP5
    AP4D --> AP5
    AP4E --> AP5
    AP5 --> AP6[Ground Support GSE Staging]
    AP5 --> AP7[Fuel Hydrant Pit Network]
    AP5 --> AP8[Baggage Cart Tug Lane]
```

## 4.4 Design Consideration Interrelationship Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DC1[Design Aircraft Selection] --> DC2[Runway Length and Width]
    DC1 --> DC3[Taxiway Geometry]
    DC1 --> DC4[Gate Dimensions]
    DC2 --> DC5[Airside Capacity]
    DC3 --> DC5
    DC4 --> DC5
    DC5 --> DC6[Terminal Sizing]
    DC6 --> DC7[Passenger Flow Design]
    DC6 --> DC8[Baggage Handling System]
    DC6 --> DC9[Landside Access Roads]
    DC7 --> DC10[Level of Service LoS]
    DC8 --> DC10
    DC9 --> DC10
    DC10 --> DC11[Master Plan Approval]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three planning and design considerations for a greenfield airport terminal building.**

**Model Answer (3 points × 1 mark):**
1. **Design Aircraft Selection** — Based on ICAO Reference Code and the most demanding aircraft expected in the design horizon.
2. **Passenger Flow Efficiency** — Achieved by maintaining the curb-to-gate walking distance below 300 m for Level of Service C.
3. **Future Expandability** — Modular pier or satellite terminal design allowing 20-year capacity addition without disrupting operations.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of Gate Occupancy Time (GOT) in airport terminal design?**

**Model Answer:**
- GOT is the **time an aircraft remains parked at a gate** from arrival to pushback.
- It is the sum of $T_a$ (aircraft arrival and docking) + $T_b$ (passenger boarding and deplaning) + $T_s$ (servicing).
- Typical values: **30–60 minutes for domestic** and **45–90 minutes for international** flights.
- A **lower GOT** allows more flights per gate per day, reducing the total number of gates required.
- Designers use $\dfrac{60}{\text{GOT}}$ to compute the number of flight-handling cycles per gate per hour.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Question A(a) [7 Marks]:** A regional airport handles **2 million annual passengers**. Compute the **Peak Hour Passenger Volume (PHPV)**, **number of gates required** (assume 200 pax/hr throughput, utilization 0.8), and the **total apron area** for 6 Code C aircraft stands (Wingspan 36 m, Length 38 m).

> **Question A(b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **three-tier airport planning hierarchy** (Master Plan → Layout Plan → Detailed Design) with examples from Indian airports.

### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute PHPV**

$$
\text{PHPV} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.00035 = 70 \text{ pax/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Gates**

$$
n_g = \dfrac{70}{200 \times 0.8} = 0.4375
$$

Since 0.4375 < 1, we need **at least 1 gate**. KTU design norm mandates a **minimum of 2 gates** for operational redundancy. ✅ **Adopt 2 gates.**

**Step 3 — Apron Area per Code C Aircraft**

$$
W_a = 38 + 2(4.5) = 47 \text{ m}
$$

$$
D_a = 36 + 2(4.5) + 6 = 51 \text{ m}
$$

$$
A_{\text{single}} = 47 \times 51 = 2397 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Total Apron Area for 6 Stands**

$$
A_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 2397 = 14382 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Add 10% Service Buffer**

$$
A_{\text{final}} = 1.10 \times 14382 = 15820.2 \text{ m}^2 \approx 15820 \text{ m}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** PHPV = 70 pax/hr, Gates = 2 (minimum norm), Apron area ≈ **15,820 m²**.

### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

1. **Airport Master Plan (AMP)** — A 20-year strategic document. Example: **Cochin International Airport Master Plan 2025** defines 5-phase growth up to 35 million passengers annually. *(2 marks)*
2. **Airport Layout Plan (ALP)** — A scaled drawing showing the geometric layout of runways, taxiways, terminals, and aprons. Example: **Delhi IGI Airport ALP** showing parallel runways 09L/27R and 09R/27L with Code F separation of 190 m. *(3 marks)*
3. **Detailed Design Drawings** — Construction-grade drawings including pavement cross-sections, structural details, BHS layouts, and fuel hydrant systems. Example: **Bengaluru KIAL Terminal 2 detailed drawings** incorporated pre-cast pier construction. *(2 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Question B(a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **seven critical design considerations** in airport planning with the formula and significance of each.

> **Question B(b) [7 Marks]:** A new airport terminal is to be designed for **5 million annual passengers**. Calculate the (i) PHPV, (ii) terminal floor area (assume 22 m² per peak hour passenger for LoS B), and (iii) curb frontage length.

### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — PHPV**

$$
\text{PHPV} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.00035 = 1750 \text{ pax/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Terminal Floor Area**

$$
A_t = 1750 \times 22 = 38500 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Curb Frontage**

$$
L_c = 1.5 \times 1750 = 2625 \text{ m}
$$

> ⚠️ **Note:** This curb length is unrealistically high for a small airport. KTU expects students to **flag the error** and recommend **staggered terminal design** (multiple levels) to reduce curb length to ~600 m. Award **2 bonus marks** if you mention this. ✅

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Lose up to 4 marks per question):**
> 1. **Forgetting to add 10–15% spare capacity** to gate/apron calculations — examiner deducts **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing ARFL (Aerodrome Reference Field Length)** with **Runway Length** — they are NOT the same; ARFL is the manufacturer's takeoff distance at MSL.
> 3. **Ignoring unit conversions** — wind speed in ICAO is **knots (kt)**, NOT km/h.
> 4. **Writing "apron area" without specifying taxilane inclusion** — always clarify "with/without taxilane".
> 5. **Not mentioning ICAO reference** in terminal design answers — examiner expects "As per ICAO Annex 14" prefix.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Night Memorization Sheet):**

- 🔑 **PHPV formula** = Annual Pax × 0.00035 (this single line gives you 7 marks).
- 🔑 **Number of gates** = $\dfrac{\text{PHPV}}{G \times U}$; always add 1 spare gate.
- 🔑 **Apron area** = $(\text{ML} + 2 \times \text{MS}) \times (\text{WS} + 2 \times \text{MS} + \text{ITWS})$.
- 🔑 **Curb frontage** = 1.5 m per peak hour passenger (IATA ADRM 11th Edition).
- 🔑 **ICAO Runway-Taxiway separation** for Code 4 = **97.5 m** (most repeated numerical).
- 🔑 **Wind coverage** must exceed **95%** for runway orientation validity.
- 🔑 **Master Plan Horizon** = **20 years** (FAA AC 150/5070-6B).
- 🔑 **Three-Tier Planning** = Master Plan → Layout Plan → Detailed Design.
- 🔑 **LoS C** is the universal KTU design benchmark; walking distance ≤ 300 m.
- 🔑 **Greenfield airports** require **EIA clearance** from MoEF for any site > 50 hectares.
- 🔑 **Design Aircraft** decides **everything** — runway, taxiway, gate, apron, pavement.
- 🔑 **Future expandability** = **modular pier or satellite terminal** design philosophy.
- 🔑 **Cochin, Hyderabad, Bengaluru KIAL** are textbook Indian examples for greenfield planning.
