| 4 | Gate Requirement | $G = \dfrac{PHP}{g_c \times U_g}$ | $g_c$ = gate capacity (pass/hr/gate), $U_g$ = gate utilisation (typ. **0.8**) |
| 5 | Aircraft Stands (Apron) | $S = \dfrac{\text{Daily Movements} \times T_t}{60 \times U_a}$ | $T_t$ = turnaround time (min), $U_a$ = apron utilisation (typ. **0.7**) |
| 6 | Holdroom Area | $A_h = PHP \times S_h \times LF$ | $S_h$ = space standard (typ. **1.4 m²/pax**), $LF$ = load factor |
| 7 | Baggage Claim Area | $A_{bc} = \dfrac{PHP \times t_c}{60} \times L_f$ | $t_c$ = claim time (typ. **25 min**), $L_f$ = linear frontage per pax |
| 8 | Check-in Counter | $C_c = \dfrac{PHP \times T_c}{60 \times U_c}$ | $T_c$ = check-in time per pax (typ. **1.5 min**), $U_c$ = counter utilisation |
| 9 | Public Parking | $P_s = \dfrac{0.6 \times PHP}{P_{cap}}$ | $P_{cap}$ = cars per space per hour (typ. **1.0**) |
| 10 | Terminal Building Area | $TBA = PHP \times \sum (S_i \times LF_i)$ | $S_i$ = IATA space standards per sub-area |

### 2.4 Level of Service (LoS) — IATA Frame

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 emphasises IATA LoS as the **design driver** for terminal sizing, not peak hour alone.

| LoS | Space per Pax (m²) | Walking Speed | Queuing Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A — Optimum** | $> 2.7$ | Free flow | No queue |
| **B** | $1.95$–$2.7$ | Free flow | Short queue |
| **C — Design (KTU target)** | $1.40$–$1.95$ | Steady | Acceptable queue |
| **D** | $0.95$–$1.40$ | Restricted | Tolerable |
| **E** | $0.50$–$0.95$ | Crowded | At capacity |
| **F — Unacceptable** | $< 0.50$ | Stop & go | Spillover |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Terminal area design directly determines:
- **Airport revenue** (retail per pax m² — Dubai Duty Free benchmark ≈ **USD 1,900 per m²**).
- **Passenger experience** (IATA surveys rate ASQ scores against LoS).
- **Airline operating cost** (gate turnaround translates to aircraft utilisation; a 1-min gain on a wide-body saves ≈ **USD 50–80** in lease cost).
- **Carbon footprint** (passenger km vs. walking distance in terminal — energy per pax at Changi ≈ **3.2 kWh/pax**).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The two derivations below are KTU-favourite long-answer items.** Practice until every intermediate step is reflexive.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Curb Length Requirement (Full Closure Form)

The curb must absorb the **peak hour enplaning passengers** using private vehicles, taxis, buses, and rental cars within the **peak 30-minute window**. We derive the total linear metres of kerb.

**Step 1 — Split the PHP into mode share.**

$$
PHP = PHP_{enp} + PHP_{dep}
$$

$$
PHP_{enp} = \alpha_e \times PHP \quad ; \quad PHP_{dep} = (1-\alpha_e) \times PHP
$$

where $\alpha_e$ is the enplanement share (domestic: 0.55, international: 0.60).

**Step 2 — Convert passengers to vehicles using the modal split.**

$$
V_{mode} = \dfrac{PHP_{enp} \times \beta_{mode}}{O_{mode}}
$$

where $\beta_{mode}$ = modal share (car **0.50**, taxi **0.25**, bus **0.15**, rental **0.10**) and $O_{mode}$ = average occupancy (car **1.4**, taxi **1.6**, bus **30**, rental **1.3**).

**Step 3 — Determine the number of active kerb positions for each mode.**

A kerb position can serve only one vehicle at a time. If the average dwell time is $T_{d,mode}$ (car **3 min**, taxi **2 min**, bus **8 min**, rental **4 min**) and the kerb is utilised to factor $U$ (**0.6–0.8**), then:

$$
N_{mode} = \dfrac{V_{mode} \times T_{d,mode}}{60 \times U}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert kerb positions to linear metres using the vehicle equivalent length.**

$$
L_{mode} = N_{mode} \times V_e
$$

with $V_e$ = **7.5 m** for a single-lane stopping bay.

**Step 5 — Sum across all modes to obtain the total curb length.**

$$
L_{total} = \sum_{m} L_{m} = \sum_{m} \dfrac{PHP_{enp} \times \beta_m \times T_{d,m} \times V_e}{60 \times U \times O_m}
$$

**Compact closed form used in KTU examinations:**

$$
L = \dfrac{PHP \times T_{avg} \times V_e}{60 \times U}
$$

with $T_{avg}$ = weighted average dwell time (typically **3 min** for blended enplanement).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Aircraft Gates (Apron Stands)

A gate is **shared** across multiple flights per day. We derive the static number of contact gates.

**Step 1 — Compute the daily aircraft movements at the gate.**

$$
M = \dfrac{PHP}{P_{flight}}
$$

where $P_{flight}$ = average passengers per flight (narrow body **150**, wide body **280**).

**Step 2 — Compute the occupancy time per gate per day.**

Each gate is occupied for the **turnaround time** $T_t$ plus a **buffer** $B$ (typ. **10 min**) for push-back, taxi, and gate clearance.

$$
t_{occ} = T_t + B
$$

For narrow body $T_t$ = 45 min, wide body $T_t$ = 90 min.

**Step 3 — Compute the number of daily cycles a single gate can support.**

$$
N_{cycle} = \dfrac{T_{oper}}{t_{occ}}
$$

where $T_{oper}$ = daily operational window (typ. **16 h = 960 min** for commercial terminals).

**Step 4 — Compute the static gate requirement.**

$$
G = \dfrac{M}{N_{cycle} \times U_g}
$$

with $U_g$ = gate utilisation factor (**0.7–0.8**).

**Step 5 — Closed-form gate equation (KTU board form).**

$$
G = \dfrac{PHP \times T_t \times 60}{P_{flight} \times (T_{oper}) \times U_g \times 60} \quad \Rightarrow \quad
G = \dfrac{PHP \times (T_t + B)}{P_{flight} \times T_{oper} \times U_g}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always round **up** to the next whole number; KTU examiners deduct a half-mark if the ceiling function $\lceil G \rceil$ is not applied.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Model)

**Problem (Dec 2023 KTU type):**
An airport handles **5 million passengers per annum (MPPA)**. The peak hour factor is **0.035 %**. The enplanement share is **60 %**, with mode share: cars 50 %, taxis 25 %, buses 15 %, rentals 10 %. Determine the **total curb length** required. Assume $T_{avg}$ = 3 min, $U$ = 0.7, $V_e$ = 7.5 m.

**Step 1 — Compute the PHP.**

$$
PHP = 0.00035 \times 5{,}000{,}000 = 1750 \text{ pax/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the enplaning PHP.**

$$
PHP_{enp} = 0.60 \times 1750 = 1050 \text{ pax/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the closed-form curb length equation.**

$$
L = \dfrac{1050 \times 3 \times 7.5}{60 \times 0.7}
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{23{,}625}{42} = 562.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply design safety / engineering judgement.**

Adopt $L$ = **565 m** (round up to nearest 5 m, the KTU standard modular unit).

**Step 5 — Verification by mode share (for full marks).**

$$
V_{car} = \dfrac{1050 \times 0.50}{1.4} = 375 ; \quad L_{car} = \dfrac{375 \times 3}{60 \times 0.7} \times 7.5 = 200.9 \text{ m}
$$

$$
V_{taxi} = \dfrac{1050 \times 0.25}{1.6} = 164 ; \quad L_{taxi} = \dfrac{164 \times 2}{60 \times 0.7} \times 7.5 = 58.6 \text{ m}
$$

$$
V_{bus} = \dfrac{1050 \times 0.15}{30} = 5.25 ; \quad L_{bus} = \dfrac{5.25 \times 8}{60 \times 0.7} \times 7.5 = 7.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
V_{rental} = \dfrac{1050 \times 0.10}{1.3} = 80.8 ; \quad L_{rental} = \dfrac{80.8 \times 4}{60 \times 0.7} \times 7.5 = 57.7 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{total} = 200.9 + 58.6 + 7.5 + 57.7 \approx 324.7 \text{ m} \text{ (lower bound, since } T_{avg} \neq 3 \text{ for all modes)}
$$

The two estimates bracket the true value; the **closed-form is the design value** because it implicitly averages dwell times.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sub-System Sizing Standards (IATA Reference)

| Sub-System | Space Standard (m²/pax) | Throughput Driver | KTU Design LoS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Check-in Hall | **1.7** | Counter / kiosk service rate | C |
| Security | **1.0** | Walk-through + body scanner | C |
| Holdroom | **1.4** | Boarding rate (pax/min) | C |
| Baggage Claim | **2.0** | Claim device frontage | C |
| Arrivals Hall | **1.5** | Customs / immigration | C |
| Concourse | **2.5** | Walking flow | C |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Terminal Area

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GROUND ACCESS RING\nRoads, Metro, Bus, Rail] --> B[CURBSIDE RING\nKerb, Forecourt, Kiss-Fly, Taxi Rank]
    B --> C[DEPARTURES RING\nCheck-in, Baggage Drop, Security, Holdroom]
    C --> D[AIRSIDE RING\nApron, Contact Gate, Jet Bridge, Remote Stand]
    D --> E[AIRCRAFT TURNAROUND\nFuelling, Catering, Cleaning, Push-back]
    A --> F[PARKING RING\nShort-Stay, Long-Stay, Staff, Rental]
    B --> G[BAGGAGE CLAIM RING\nClaim Devices, Customs, Arrivals Hall]
    E --> H[OPERATIONS CONTROL\nATC, AOC, Security Ops]
    C --> H
    G --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I[TERMINAL MANAGEMENT CENTRE]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Passenger & Baggage Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Enplaning Pax] --> P2[Kerb Drop]
    P2 --> P3[Check-in Counter]
    P3 --> P4[Security Hold]
    P4 --> P5[Concourse / Holdroom]
    P5 --> P6[Gate / Jet Bridge]
    P6 --> P7[Aircraft]
    B1[Inbound Baggage] --> B2[Baggage Make-up]
    B2 --> B3[Aircraft Hold]
    B3 --> B4[Claim Device]
    B4 --> B5[Customs]
    B5 --> B6[Arrivals Kerb]
    P7 -.Tow Back.-> P8[Push-back Tug]
    P8 --> P9[Taxiway]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Apron Stand Geometry (Contact vs Remote)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APRON_LAYOUT
        S1[Contact Stand NB\n90 deg Nose-In\nJet Bridge] --- S2[Contact Stand WB\n90 deg Nose-In\nDual Jet Bridge]
        S2 --- S3[Angled Stand 45 deg\nSelf Taxy-Out]
        S3 --- S4[Parallel Stand 0 deg\nMRO Apron]
        S5[Remote Stand NB\nBus Boarding Lounge] --- S6[Remote Stand WB\nBus Boarding Lounge]
    end
    T1[Taxiway Centreline] --- S1
    T1 --- S5
    R1[Taxi Lane Outer] --- APRON_LAYOUT
    R1 --- PUSH[Push-back Zone]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Level of Service Decision Tree (KTU Design Path)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Define APV] --> PHP[Compute PHP via 0.03 to 0.05 percent rule]
    PHP --> LOS{Choose LoS}
    LOS -->|A| O1[Oversized 1.4x Budget]
    LOS -->|B| O2[Conservative 1.15x]
    LOS -->|C| O3[KTU Reference Design]
    LOS -->|D| O4[Stretch Mode]
    LOS -->|E and F| O5[Reject Re-design]
    O3 --> SIZE[Sizing per IATA Tables]
    SIZE --> CHECK[Verify Peak 30 min]
    CHECK --> FINAL[Final TBA and Apron]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. (3 Marks) Define the term "Terminal Area" and list its four major functional rings.**

**Model Answer:**
The terminal area is the composite ground-side complex forming the interface between access modes, the terminal building, and the aircraft. Its four functional rings are:

1. **Ground Access Ring** — roads, parking, public transport interchange.
2. **Curbside Processing Ring** — forecourt kerb, check-in, security.
3. **Concourse / Holdroom Ring** — holdrooms, retail, immigration, lounges.
4. **Airside / Apron Interface** — contact gates, remote stands, taxiways.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: Listing *fewer than four* rings deducts one mark; omitting the words *interface* or *apron* deducts half a mark.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. (3 Marks) What is the IATA Level of Service (LoS)? Why is LoS C adopted as the KTU design standard?**

**Model Answer:**
The IATA LoS is a six-point (A to F) qualitative and quantitative scale measuring the **operational performance and spatial adequacy** of terminal sub-systems, expressed in **m² per passenger** and **walking time**.

LoS C is adopted because it offers a balanced trade-off:
- **Space** ≈ 1.40 to 1.95 m²/pax (economically feasible).
- **Walking** unrestricted with mild density.
- **Queue** acceptable dwell, no spillover.

LoS A and B are uneconomical; D, E, F are operationally degrading.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 3 / Q. Module 3]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Terminal Building Sub-System Sizing*

**(a) (7 Marks)** An airport serves **4.2 MPPA** with a peak hour factor of **0.04 %**. The mode share for enplaning passengers is: cars 55 %, taxis 20 %, buses 10 %, rentals 10 %, two-wheelers 5 %. Occupancy: car 1.4, taxi 1.6, bus 32, rental 1.3, two-wheeler 1.2. Dwell times: car 3 min, taxi 2 min, bus 8 min, rental 4 min, two-wheeler 2 min. Utilisation factor **0.7**, $V_e$ = **7.5 m**. Calculate the **total curb length** by mode and the overall blended length.

**(b) (7 Marks)** With reference to the IATA LoS, design the **holdroom area** for a peak hour passenger volume of **1800 pax/hr**, of which **70 %** will be in the holdroom simultaneously, at LoS C. Compare with the requirement at LoS A and discuss the cost implications.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute the PHP and enplaning share.**

$$
PHP = 0.0004 \times 4{,}200{,}000 = 1680 \text{ pax/hr}
$$

$$
PHP_{enp} = 0.60 \times 1680 = 1008 \text{ pax/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute vehicles per mode.**

$$
V_{car} = \dfrac{1008 \times 0.55}{1.4} = 396 ; \quad V_{taxi} = \dfrac{1008 \times 0.20}{1.6} = 126
$$

$$
V_{bus} = \dfrac{1008 \times 0.10}{32} = 3.15 ; \quad V_{rental} = \dfrac{1008 \times 0.10}{1.3} = 77.5
$$

$$
V_{2w} = \dfrac{1008 \times 0.05}{1.2} = 42
$$

**Step 3 — Compute curb length per mode.**

$$
L_{car} = \dfrac{396 \times 3}{60 \times 0.7} \times 7.5 = 211.1 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{taxi} = \dfrac{126 \times 2}{60 \times 0.7} \times 7.5 = 45.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{bus} = \dfrac{3.15 \times 8}{60 \times 0.7} \times 7.5 = 4.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{rental} = \dfrac{77.5 \times 4}{60 \times 0.7} \times 7.5 = 55.4 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{2w} = \dfrac{42 \times 2}{60 \times 0.7} \times 7.5 = 15.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Total curb length.**

$$
L_{total} = 211.1 + 45.0 + 4.5 + 55.4 + 15.0 \approx 331 \text{ m}
$$

**Adopt L = 335 m** (rounded to 5 m module).

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Pax in holdroom simultaneously.**

$$
N_h = 0.70 \times 1800 = 1260 \text{ pax}
$$

**Step 2 — Holdroom area at LoS C (1.4 m²/pax).**

$$
A_h^C = 1260 \times 1.4 = 1764 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Holdroom area at LoS A (2.7 m²/pax).**

$$
A_h^A = 1260 \times 2.7 = 3402 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Cost discussion.**
At a typical capital cost of **USD 4,500/m²** for terminal interiors, LoS A requires an additional **1638 m²** ≈ **USD 7.37 M**. This is justified only for premium hubs (e.g., Singapore Changi, Doha) where the retail yield is high.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Apron, Gates & Stand Geometry*

**(a) (7 Marks)** Compute the number of **contact gates** required for a terminal handling **1500 PHP** with an average of **160 pax/flight**, turnaround time **45 min** (narrow body), buffer **10 min**, and a **16 h operating window**. Take gate utilisation **0.75**.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Compare **nose-in, angled, and parallel parking** in terms of apron depth, stand throughput, and operational flexibility. Draw a neat block schematic of the apron stand geometry indicating the MARS (Multiple Aircraft Ramp System) capability.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Daily aircraft movements at the gate.**

$$
M = \dfrac{PHP \times 16}{P_{flight}} = \dfrac{1500 \times 16}{160} = 150 \text{ movements}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of cycles per gate per day.**

$$
t_{occ} = 45 + 10 = 55 \text{ min}
$$

$$
N_{cycle} = \dfrac{16 \times 60}{55} = 17.45 \text{ cycles/gate/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply utilisation factor.**

$$
G = \dfrac{150}{17.45 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{150}{13.09} = 11.46
$$

**Step 4 — Round up.**

$$
G = \lceil 11.46 \rceil = 12 \text{ contact gates}
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Configuration | Apron Depth | Throughput | Flexibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nose-In 90°** | Lowest (~ Aircraft Length + Tug) | High (single push-back tug) | Low — needs tug |
| **Angled 45°–60°** | Medium | Medium (self-taxi-out) | Medium |
| **Parallel 0°** | Highest | Low | Highest (self-park) |

**MARS Schematic (Block Representation):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MARS_STAND
        M1[1 NB at MARS Position 1] --- M2[2 NB at MARS Position 2]
        M2 --- M3[1 WB at MARS Position 1 Extended]
    end
    TL[Taxiway Centreline] --- MARS_STAND
    JB1[Jet Bridge 1 NB] --- M1
    JB2[Jet Bridge 2 NB] --- M2
    JB3[Jet Bridge 3 WB] --- M3
```

MARS allows one wide-body stand to flexibly host **two narrow-body** aircraft during low-demand hours, raising stand utilisation by **20–30 %**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** $\lceil G \rceil$ on gate counts: a 0.5 mark penalty.
> 2. **Confusing PHP and PHP$_{enp}$**: peak hour includes both directions; curb is sized on enplaning only.
> 3. **Mixing units**: $T_d$ in minutes must be divided by 60; many students forget the conversion.
> 4. **Omitting buffer B** in turnaround: results in 8–10 % under-provisioning.
> 5. **Not stating IATA LoS assumption** in terminal area: examiners insist on the LoS line in the answer.
> 6. **Forgetting MARS / stand flexibility**: a common sub-part (b) in Part B; not mentioning it loses 2 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> **Important Things to Remember (Bullet Density)**
> - **Terminal Area = 4 Rings:** Access, Curbside, Concourse, Apron.
> - **PHP = 0.0003–0.0005 × APV**; for design use **0.00035–0.0004**.
> - **Enplaning share:** domestic **0.55**, international **0.60**.
> - **Curb Length Formula:** $L = \dfrac{PHP_{enp} \times T_d \times V_e}{60 \times U}$; $V_e$ = 7.5 m, $U$ = 0.6–0.8.
> - **Gates Formula:** $G = \dfrac{PHP \times (T_t + B)}{P_{flight} \times T_{oper} \times U_g}$; **always round UP**.
> - **IATA LoS C** is the KTU design standard; space ≈ **1.4 m²/pax** for holdrooms.
> - **Stand Types:** Nose-in (90°), Angled (45–60°), Parallel (0°); **MARS** gives flexibility.
> - **Aircraft Turnaround:** NB = 30–45 min, WB = 60–90 min; **Buffer B = 10 min**.
> - **Baggage Claim:** sized on **25 min** average claim time, **2.0 m²/pax** at LoS C.
> - **Check-in:** 1.5 min per pax; counter utilisation 0.7–0.85.
> - **Gate Utilisation $U_g$** typical 0.75; **Apron Utilisation $U_a$** typical 0.70.
> - **Holdroom concurrency factor** = 0.7 (i.e., 70 % of PHP present at one time).
> - **Service Vehicles:** 7.5 m equivalent; **modal split** car 50 %, taxi 25 %, bus 15 %, rental 10 %.
> - **Vehicle Occupancy:** car 1.4, taxi 1.6, bus 30, rental 1.3 — remember these for curb sub-parts.
> - **MARS Capability:** one WB stand hosts two NB at off-peak — always mention in stand geometry questions.
> - **TBA Total:** sum of all sub-system areas at IATA LoS C; total ≈ 25–35 m²/PHP at LoS C for medium hub.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terminal Concepts & Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Terminal Concepts & Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Airport Terminal

The **Airport Terminal Building** (often called the **Passenger Terminal Complex** or **PTC**) is the ground-based facility that provides the interface between **airside** (runways, taxiways, aprons) and **landside** (curbside, parking, ground transport) of an airport. It functions as a processing, transfer, and holding center for passengers, baggage, cargo, and crew between different modes of transportation (air–road, air–rail, air–air).

As per ICAO Annex 14 and FAA AC 150/5360-13A, the terminal building must accommodate the following sequential processing functions:

1. **Pre-flight processing** — ticketing, check-in, security, immigration (international), customs.
2. **Hold/boarding functions** — lounges, gates, FIDS (Flight Information Display Systems).
3. **Post-flight processing** — baggage claim, customs, immigration, ground transport.
4. **Transfer functions** — interline connections, transit lounges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET744 / Module 3):**
> Terminal concepts describe the **geometric configuration** of the terminal relative to the apron, while terminal **types** refer to the **vertical circulation strategy** (single-level, two-level, multi-level). Both are critical for KTU University Exam short and long answer questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Mall with Runways" Concept

Think of the airport terminal like a **shopping mall**, but instead of shops, the "outlets" are aircraft parking stands. The terminal's job is to:
- **Welcome** arriving shoppers (passengers) at the entrance,
- **Guide** them through narrow gates (security, check-in, immigration) like escalators in a mall,
- **Settle** them into **waiting lounges** (like benches near a food court) before they "board the ride" (aircraft),
- **Receive** them back and **deliver** them to the parking lot (curbside).

The **shape** of this mall (long, T-shaped, ringed by aircraft, etc.) defines the **terminal concept**, while the **number of floors** (single, double, multi-storied) defines the **terminal type**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Design Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum acceptable walking distance (gate to aircraft) | **< 300 m** | IATA ADRM |
| Preferred walking distance | **< 200 m** | IATA ADRM |
| Dwell time (domestic peak) | **45–60 min** | FAA/ICAO |
| Dwell time (international peak) | **90–120 min** | FAA/ICAO |
| Peak hour factor (PHF) of annual passengers | **0.0007 to 0.0010** | Empirical |
| Curb-to-gate time (design target) | **< 30 min** | IATA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these metrics matter:** Walking distance is the *single most important* driver of terminal concept selection. If a linear concept is too long, pier, satellite, or transporter concepts must be adopted to keep walking distances below **300 m**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Terminal Concepts

Terminal concepts are classified into **two major families** based on how processing and aircraft parking are spatially organized:

### A. Centralized Concept (Single Main Processor)
A **single** central building (the processor) handles all passenger processing, and aircraft are parked on the apron around it via extending piers, satellites, or a linear curb.

#### 1. Simple Linear Concept
- Terminal is a **straight rectangular block** running parallel to a row of aircraft stands.
- Aircraft nose-in parking along one (or both) sides.
- **Advantages:** Simplest design, easy phasing, low CAPEX.
- **Disadvantages:** Long walking distances at scale; apron congestion.
- **Best for:** Low/medium traffic (< 5 MAPHP — Million Annual Passengers, design).

#### 2. Pier (Finger) Concept
- Central processor with **one or more rectangular piers** projecting into the apron.
- Aircraft parked on **both sides** of each pier.
- **Advantages:** Compact footprint, more gates per unit area, good for medium-large airports.
- **Disadvantages:** Long walking distances to far gates, dead-end pier congestion.
- **Examples:** Mumbai T2, Dubai T1, Paris CDG T2 (old).

#### 3. Satellite Concept
- A **separate circular/rectangular building** (satellite) is built away from the main processor on the apron.
- Passengers reach the satellite via **underground tunnels, elevated walkways, or automated people movers (APM)**.
- Aircraft surround the satellite on **all sides**, giving a 360° gate frontage.
- **Advantages:** Maximum gate density, no pier dead-ends, modern A380/B777 compatible.
- **Disadvantages:** High CAPEX (tunnels/APMs), complex operations.
- **Examples:** Paris CDG T1, Dubai T3, Bengaluru T2, Hong Kong.

#### 4. Pier–Satellite Hybrid
- Combines piers and one or more satellites.
- **Best for:** Very high traffic > 30 MAPHP.

#### 5. Transporter Concept
- Central processor on the landside, with **remote parking areas** on the apron.
- Passengers transported to aircraft via **APMs, buses, or mobile lounges**.
- **Advantages:** Decouples processor size from gate count — easy expansion.
- **Disadvantages:** Slow transit, weather exposure, high OPEX.
- **Examples:** Washington Dulles (mobile lounges), old JFK, Paris Orly (old).

### B. Decentralized Concept (Unit Terminal)
- **Multiple independent terminals**, each fully self-sufficient (own check-in, security, baggage, gates).
- Connected by an **inter-terminal transit system** (people mover, bus, walkway).
- **Advantages:** Modular growth, lower peak congestion per unit, simple phasing.
- **Disadvantages:** Duplicate facilities (high CAPEX), transfer inconvenience.
- **Examples:** Los Angeles LAX, Dallas/Fort Worth, Chicago O'Hare (old), Delhi IGI T1+T2+T3.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** When a question asks *"Compare Centralized and Decentralized terminals"*, always structure your answer with a **5-row comparison table** (passenger transfer, CAPEX, OPEX, growth, typical traffic threshold). Students who omit OPEX and growth lose **2 marks**.

## 2.2 Classification of Terminal Types (Vertical Strategy)

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Level** | All operations on one floor (arrivals & departures mixed). Curbside, processing, gates all on ground. | Small airports, < 3 MAPHP. |
| **Two-Level (Stacked)** | Departures on upper floor, arrivals on lower floor. Vertical separation by escalators/lifts. | Medium airports, 3–15 MAPHP. Most common globally. |
| **Multi-Level (>2)** | Three or more levels (e.g., mezzanine for offices, basement for APM, roof for VIP). | Mega hubs > 25 MAPHP, e.g., Hong Kong, Beijing PEK, Dubai T3. |

### Why Vertical Separation?
- **One-way passenger flow** (departures in, arrivals out) eliminates cross-flows.
- **Security** is centralized on a single departures level.
- **Baggage** can flow on a dedicated level without crossing passenger paths.
- **Curb congestion** is split (departure curb upper, arrival curb lower).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Terminal Sizing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU Module 3 University Exam. Memorize units and constants.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Peak Hour Passengers (PHP)** | $PHP = AP \times 0.0008$ | passengers/hr | AP = Annual Passengers. Use **0.0008** unless given otherwise. |
| 2 | **Gate Requirement** | $G = \dfrac{PHP}{g \times u}$ | gates | $g$ = gates per hour per gate (typically 1.5–2.0), $u$ = utilization (0.8). |
| 3 | **Terminal Building Area** | $A_T = PHP \times a_s$ | m² | $a_s$ = area standard: 8–12 m²/PHP (domestic), 14–20 m²/PHP (international). |
| 4 | **Curb Length** | $L_c = \dfrac{PHP \times d_t}{60 \times v}$ | m | $d_t$ = dwell time at curb (min), $v$ = vehicles per meter of curb. |
| 5 | **Check-in Counter Count** | $N_{ci} = \dfrac{PHP}{60/T_{ci}}$ | counters | $T_{ci}$ = service time per pax (1.5–2.5 min). |
| 6 | **Baggage Claim Area** | $A_{bc} = f_{bc} \times PHP_a$ | m² | $f_{bc}$ = 1.0–1.5 m²/PHP arriving, $PHP_a$ = arriving peak hour. |
| 7 | **Number of Baggage Belts** | $N_b = \dfrac{PHP_a}{T_b \times R_b}$ | belts | $T_b$ = time per bag on belt, $R_b$ = bags/flight. |
| 8 | **Aircraft Stand Requirement** | $S = G + \text{remote stands} + \text{buffer}$ | stands | 10–15% buffer for maintenance/turnaround. |
| 9 | **Walking Distance Check** | $D_w \leq 300 \text{ m}$ | m | IATA ADRM optimum. |
| 10 | **Curb-to-Gate Time** | $t_{cg} = \dfrac{D_w}{v_w} + t_{proc}$ | min | $v_w$ = walking speed (1.0–1.3 m/s), $t_{proc}$ = processing time. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL SYNTAX NOTE:** In the table above, division bars are rendered as `\dfrac` to **avoid the markdown table pipe `|` collision**. Do not write `|x|` for absolute value inside any KTU table — always use `\vert x \vert`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Master Planners** use concept selection at the 20-year horizon to size the **processor**, **piers**, and **APM tunnels**.
- **Architects** use area standards to design **circulation, hold rooms, and concession zones**.
- **Operations teams** use the gate/curb formulas to plan **staff rosters, security lanes, and bus schedules**.
- **IATA Level of Service (LoS)** uses these formulas to certify airports at LoS C or better.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Gates Required from Peak Hour Traffic

### Problem Setup
An airport handles **8 Million Annual Passengers (MAP)**. Each gate can handle **1.8 flights per hour** at **80% utilization**. Find the number of gates required. (Mixed domestic-international, average.)

### Step 1 — Compute Peak Hour Passengers (PHP)

$$
PHP = AP \times \alpha
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **peak hour factor**. We adopt the **IATA standard** $\alpha = 0.0008$ unless otherwise stated.

$$
PHP = 8{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0008 = 6{,}400 \text{ pax/hr}
$$

*Text row:* Multiply annual passengers by the peak hour factor to get the design peak hour volume.

### Step 2 — Compute Number of Flights in Peak Hour

Assume **average load factor per aircraft** $\bar{L} = 150$ pax (mixed fleet). Thus:

$$
F_{php} = \frac{PHP}{\bar{L}} = \frac{6{,}400}{150} = 42.67 \approx 43 \text{ flights/hr}
$$

*Text row:* Divide peak hour passengers by average load to get flights per peak hour.

### Step 3 — Apply Gate Capacity Formula

Each gate handles $g = 1.8$ flights/hr at utilization $u = 0.8$. The **effective capacity per gate** is:

$$
g_{eff} = g \times u = 1.8 \times 0.8 = 1.44 \text{ flights/hr/gate}
$$

### Step 4 — Number of Gates Required

$$
G = \frac{F_{php}}{g_{eff}} = \frac{42.67}{1.44} = 29.6 \approx 30 \text{ gates}
$$

*Text row:* Round up to the next whole gate. Always add a **5–10% buffer** for remote stands and maintenance, so design = **30 + 3 = 33 stands**.

### Step 5 — Terminal Area

$$
A_T = PHP \times a_s = 6{,}400 \times 12 = 76{,}800 \text{ m}^2
$$

*Text row:* This is the **total processor + pier/satellite area**, using $a_s = 12$ m²/PHP for a mixed-use terminal.

## 3.2 Derivation: Terminal Concept Selection Using Walking Distance

The choice of concept is driven by the **maximum tolerable walking distance**. Suppose an airport needs $G = 30$ gates and uses an **aircraft stand depth** of 80 m (nose-in parking with taxilane). Each gate is **centered on a 65 m stand frontage** (Code C aircraft, ICAO).

### Step 1 — Pier (Finger) Concept

A double-loaded pier (aircraft on both sides) provides **2 gates per 65 m of pier length**.

$$
L_{pier} = \frac{G}{2} \times 65 = \frac{30}{2} \times 65 = 975 \text{ m}
$$

*Text row:* Pier length exceeds the IATA 300 m limit — **infeasible** for a single pier.

### Step 2 — Two-Pier Solution

Splitting into **2 piers** of 15 gates each:

$$
L_{pier} = \frac{15}{2} \times 65 = 487.5 \text{ m}
$$

*Text row:* Still too long. Walk from processor end to far gate is ~487 m > 300 m. **Still infeasible.**

### Step 3 — Satellite Solution

Use a **single satellite** with $G = 30$ gates on all 4 sides. Connect via a **300 m tunnel** (walking time ~5 min).

$$
D_w = L_{tunnel} + 30 \text{ m (gate to aircraft stand)} = 330 \text{ m}
$$

*Text row:* Marginal. A pier-satellite **hybrid** (1 satellite + 2 short piers of 60 m each) is optimal. The walking distance from the satellite center to any gate is bounded by the satellite's diagonal ≈ 90 m, so $D_w \approx 300 + 90 = 390$ m. **Acceptable** with APM (3-min ride) so perceived walking = 0.

**Conclusion:** For 30 gates, a **centralized processor + satellite + 2 piers (hybrid)** is the correct KTU-recommended concept.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Concept vs. Traffic

| Traffic (MAPHP) | Recommended Concept | Recommended Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 2 | Linear / simple pier | Single-level | Lowest CAPEX |
| 2 – 8 | Pier (Finger) | Two-level | Balances CAPEX + walking distance |
| 8 – 20 | Pier-Satellite Hybrid | Two-level | Manages walking distance with APM |
| 20 – 50 | Multi-satellite or Hybrid | Multi-level | Handles A380, B777 wide-bodies |
| > 50 | Decentralized Unit Terminals | Multi-level with APM | Modular growth, airline clustering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The threshold of **8 MAPHP** for pier→satellite transition is a *frequently asked* data point. Always cite IATA's **300 m walking distance** rule as the basis.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Curb Length Calculation

An airport has PHP = 4,000 pax/hr, with **30% arriving by private car** (dwell = 3 min), **20% by taxi** (dwell = 2 min), and **50% by bus/rail** (no curb). Vehicles are spaced at 2.5 m per vehicle.

### Step 1 — Arriving Curb Demand

$$
V_a = 0.30 \times 4{,}000 = 1{,}200 \text{ pax/hr}
$$

Assume 1.5 pax per car (arriving):

$$
V_{cars} = \frac{1{,}200}{1.5} = 800 \text{ cars/hr}
$$

### Step 2 — Cars per Minute

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{800}{60} = 13.33 \text{ cars/min}
$$

### Step 3 — Curb Length

$$
L_c = \dot{V} \times d_t \times s = 13.33 \times 3 \times 2.5 = 100 \text{ m}
$$

*Text row:* For **arrivals + departures**, double it (separated curbs) to **200 m**. This is a typical KTU 14-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Terminal Concept Family Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Airport Terminal Concepts]
    root --> A[Centralized]
    root --> B[Decentralized]

    A --> A1[Linear]
    A --> A2[Pier / Finger]
    A --> A3[Satellite]
    A --> A4[Pier-Satellite Hybrid]
    A --> A5[Transporter]

    B --> B1[Unit Terminal]
    B --> B2[Cluster Terminal]
    B --> B3[Hybrid Unit Terminal]

    A1 --> N1[Best below 2 MAPHP]
    A2 --> N2[Best 2 to 8 MAPHP]
    A3 --> N3[Best 8 to 20 MAPHP]
    A4 --> N4[Best 20 to 50 MAPHP]
    A5 --> N5[Mobile lounge concept]
    B1 --> N6[LAX, DFW, JFK]
    B2 --> N7[FRA old, NRT]
    B3 --> N8[Multiple unit terminals connected by APM]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Centralized Terminal with Satellite

```mermaid
graph LR
    landside[Landside Curb and Parking]
    landside --> checkin[Check-in Hall]
    checkin --> security[Security Screening]
    security --> hold[Departure Hold Room]
    hold --> corridor[Connecting Corridor or APM]
    corridor --> sat[Satellite Processor]
    sat --> gate1[Gate A1]
    sat --> gate2[Gate A2]
    sat --> gate3[Gate B1]
    sat --> gate4[Gate B2]
    sat --> gate5[Gate C1]
    sat --> gate6[Gate C2]
    gate1 --> airside1[Aircraft Stand 1]
    gate2 --> airside2[Aircraft Stand 2]
    gate3 --> airside3[Aircraft Stand 3]
    gate4 --> airside4[Aircraft Stand 4]
    gate5 --> airside5[Aircraft Stand 5]
    gate6 --> airside6[Aircraft Stand 6]
    airside1 --> apron1[Apron and Taxiway]
    apron1 --> runway[Runway]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **centralized processor** (left side) handles check-in, security, and hold room functions. Passengers are then transferred via a corridor or automated people mover to the **satellite processor** (right), which has gates on all sides. Aircraft parked on the apron are connected to the gates by **jet bridges** (not shown, but implied).

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Vertical Type Comparison (Single vs. Two-Level vs. Multi-Level)

```mermaid
graph TD
    type[Terminal Vertical Type]
    type --> SL[Single Level]
    type --> TL[Two Level Stacked]
    type --> ML[Multi Level]

    SL --> SL1[Curbside at Ground]
    SL1 --> SL2[Check-in and Security at Ground]
    SL2 --> SL3[Hold Room at Ground]
    SL3 --> SL4[Gates at Ground]
    SL4 --> SL5[Baggage Claim at Ground]

    TL --> TL1[Departure Curb Upper]
    TL1 --> TL2[Departure Level Check-in Security Hold]
    TL2 --> TL3[Arrival Level Baggage Claim Ground Transport]

    ML --> ML1[Basement APM and Baggage Tunnel]
    ML1 --> ML2[Ground Arrival Curb and Baggage Claim]
    ML2 --> ML3[Level 1 Departure Check-in and Security]
    ML3 --> ML4[Level 2 Hold Rooms and Concessions]
    ML4 --> ML5[Level 3 Offices Mezzanine VIP Lounges]
    ML5 --> ML6[Roof Helipad or Solar Plant]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Pier (Finger) concept of an airport terminal. State two advantages and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Pier (Finger) concept** is a centralized terminal configuration in which a main processing building is connected to one or more **rectangular piers (fingers)** that project into the apron. Aircraft are parked nose-in on **both sides** of each pier, providing direct gate access via jet bridges.

*Advantages (2 marks — 1 each):*

1. **Compact footprint:** A single pier with double-loaded gates can accommodate 8–10 aircraft per 200 m of pier length, increasing gate density per unit land area.
2. **Phased expansion:** Additional piers can be added incrementally without disturbing existing operations.

*Disadvantage (1 mark):*

3. **Long walking distances:** As the number of gates grows, pier length can exceed **300 m**, breaching the IATA recommended walking distance and forcing use of moving walkways or APMs.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare Centralized and Decentralized terminal concepts on the basis of (i) passenger transfer convenience, (ii) CAPEX, and (iii) modular growth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):**

| Parameter | Centralized | Decentralized |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Passenger transfer convenience** | Excellent — single processor, short walk between gates of the same airline. | Poor — passengers must use inter-terminal transit (APM/bus), adding 15–30 min. |
| **(ii) CAPEX** | Lower initial CAPEX (single processor). | Higher (duplicate facilities at each unit terminal). |
| **(iii) Modular growth** | Limited — extensions are appendages to one processor. | Excellent — new unit terminals can be built and activated independently. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.A.** An airport is being designed for **10 Million Annual Passengers (MAP)** of which **60% are domestic and 40% are international**. The peak hour factor is **0.0009**, average aircraft load is **160 pax**, gate throughput is **1.7 flights/hr at 80% utilization**, and the area standard is **14 m² per peak hour passenger**.

#### (a) Compute the number of gates required and the total terminal building area. **(7 marks)**

#### (b) Recommend a suitable terminal concept and type with proper justification, citing the IATA 300 m walking distance rule. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Peak Hour Passengers (1 mark)**

$$
PHP = 10{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0009 = 9{,}000 \text{ pax/hr}
$$

*Logic:* Multiply annual passengers by the peak hour factor.

**Step 2: Peak Hour Flights (1 mark)**

$$
F_{php} = \frac{9{,}000}{160} = 56.25 \approx 57 \text{ flights/hr}
$$

*Logic:* Divide by average load to get number of flights.

**Step 3: Effective Gate Capacity (1 mark)**

$$
g_{eff} = 1.7 \times 0.8 = 1.36 \text{ flights/hr/gate}
$$

*Logic:* Multiply nominal gate throughput by utilization.

**Step 4: Number of Gates (2 marks)**

$$
G = \frac{57}{1.36} = 41.9 \approx 42 \text{ gates}
$$

*Logic:* Round up. Add 10% buffer for remote/maintenance stands: 42 + 4 = **46 stands** (1 mark for buffer reasoning).

**Step 5: Terminal Area (2 marks)**

$$
A_T = 9{,}000 \times 14 = 1{,}26{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$

*Logic:* Multiply PHP by area standard. This is a **large terminal** (>120,000 m²) — clearly a hub scale.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Recommended Concept: Centralized Processor + 2 Satellites + 2 Piers (Hybrid) (3 marks)**

Reasoning:

1. With **42 gates**, a single pier would require $L_{pier} = (42/2) \times 65 = 1{,}365$ m — far beyond IATA's 300 m walking limit. (1 mark)
2. A **single satellite** with 360° gate frontage (4 sides × 11 gates = 44 gates, all within a 90 m diameter) gives a maximum walking distance of about 90 m inside the satellite. (1 mark)
3. A **second satellite or two piers** is needed to absorb the remaining gates and allow modular growth. (1 mark)

**Recommended Type: Two-Level Stacked (departures up, arrivals down) (2 marks)**

Reasoning:

1. PHP of 9,000 pax/hr demands **vertical separation** to avoid cross-flows. (1 mark)
2. The international/domestic mix (40/60) requires segregated **immigration and customs** zones — a two-level design provides a clean landside-airside interface. (1 mark)

**Justification via IATA 300 m rule (2 marks):**
A satellite-connected concept keeps the **processor-to-gate walking distance at 300 m (tunnel) + 30 m (gate) = 330 m**, which IATA classifies as **Level of Service C (acceptable)**. With APM (3-min ride) instead of walking, perceived distance is 0.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.B.** Explain the **classification of airport terminal buildings** based on **vertical circulation strategy**. With neat sketches (describe in words), differentiate between single-level, two-level, and multi-level terminals. State one example airport for each type. **(14 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Single-Level Terminal — 4 marks**

- All passenger processing (curb → check-in → security → hold room → boarding → baggage claim → exit) is on a **single floor**.
- Used at small airports with PHP < 2,000 pax/hr.
- **Example:** Cochin (old domestic terminal, pre-2017), smaller regional airports.
- **Pros:** Lowest CAPEX, simple wayfinding, no vertical circulation equipment.
- **Cons:** Cross-flows between arriving and departing passengers, security risk, cannot scale to high traffic.

**(b) Two-Level (Stacked) Terminal — 5 marks**

- **Departures on the upper level** (curbside drop-off → check-in → security → hold room → gates).
- **Arrivals on the lower level** (gates → baggage claim → customs → curbside pick-up).
- Baggage and services often occupy a **basement or mezzanine**.
- Used at 3–15 MAPHP. **Most common type globally.**
- **Example:** Mumbai T2 (departures upper, arrivals lower), Singapore Changi T2, Bengaluru KIA T1.
- **Pros:** Eliminates cross-flows, centralizes security, allows simultaneous curb usage for both directions.
- **Cons:** Higher CAPEX than single-level, requires escalators/lifts, longer evacuation routes.

**(c) Multi-Level Terminal — 5 marks**

- **Three or more** functional levels.
- Typical levels:
  - **Basement:** APM tunnels, baggage conveyor, utilities.
  - **Ground level:** Arrivals curb, baggage claim, ground transport.
  - **Level 1:** Departures curb, check-in, security, immigration.
  - **Level 2:** Hold rooms, retail, lounges.
  - **Level 3:** Offices, VIP, mezzanine.
  - **Roof:** Solar plant, helipad (rare).
- Used at mega-hubs > 25 MAPHP.
- **Example:** Hong Kong HKIA, Beijing PEK T3, Dubai DXB T3, Incheon ICN T1/T2.
- **Pros:** Maximum vertical segregation, large capacity, accommodates A380, supports APM networks.
- **Cons:** Highest CAPEX and OPEX, complex wayfinding, longer passenger journey.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Concept" with "Type":** Many students mix up **Pier (concept)** and **Two-level (type)**. These are **two different axes** of classification. Always clarify: *Concept = horizontal layout; Type = vertical layout.* **(−2 marks if confused)**
> 2. **Omitting numerical justification:** For 14-mark concept-selection questions, you must compute the **pier length** or **walking distance** numerically. A purely qualitative answer loses **3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the buffer:** Always add **5–10%** to gate count and **30%** to terminal area for circulation, MEP, and concessions. KTU key shows this as a **mandatory step**.
> 4. **Units mistakes:** Peak hour factor is dimensionless. PHP is in pax/hr. Area is in m². Mixing units is a common **−1 mark** deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the IATA 300 m citation:** In any concept-selection answer, **citing the IATA 300 m walking distance** is worth **1 mark** explicitly. Don't omit it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Terminal Concepts & Types**

- **Two classification axes:** Concept (horizontal — Linear / Pier / Satellite / Hybrid / Transporter / Unit) and Type (vertical — Single / Two / Multi-level).
- **IATA's 300 m walking distance** is the *primary* driver of concept selection.
- **Pier (Finger):** Compact, double-loaded gates, dead-end congestion.
- **Satellite:** 360° gate frontage, connected by tunnel/APM, ideal for A380/B777.
- **Linear:** Simplest, used for < 2 MAPHP.
- **Transporter:** Decouples processor from gates, high OPEX.
- **Decentralized (Unit):** Multiple independent terminals, easy growth, transfer inconvenience.
- **Two-level (stacked):** Most common globally; departures up, arrivals down.
- **Multi-level:** Used at > 25 MAPHP mega-hubs.
- **Key formulas:**
  - $PHP = AP \times 0.0008$
  - $G = \dfrac{PHP}{g \times u}$
  - $A_T = PHP \times a_s$
  - $L_{pier} = \dfrac{G}{2} \times 65$ m
- **Always add 5–10% buffer** to gate count and **30%** to area for circulation/concessions.
- **Cite IATA 300 m** in every concept-selection answer.
- **Distinguish clearly** between concept and type — KTU examiners deduct marks for confusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Passenger Requirements at Terminal Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Passenger Requirements at Terminal Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Passenger Requirements at a Terminal Building** refers to the quantitative and qualitative demand parameters (spatial, temporal, and service-based) that dictate the sizing, layout, and operational capacity of every functional zone within an airport terminal — from kerb-side approach and check-in halls to boarding gates and baggage claim belts.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the term encompasses:

- **Passenger Demand Parameters:** Annual passenger volume, peak hour passengers (PHP), busy hour, and design hour.
- **Time-Based Requirements:** Maximum allowable processing/waiting time at each sub-system (check-in, security, immigration, boarding).
- **Spatial Requirements:** Floor area per passenger, seating ratios, and queue lengths.
- **Service Requirements:** Number of check-in counters, security lanes, immigration counters, baggage belts, and aircraft stands.

Mathematically, the governing relationship used universally in airport planning is:

$$P_{\text{design}} = f(\text{Annual Passengers},\ \text{Load Factor},\ \text{Peak Month Ratio},\ \text{Peak Day Ratio},\ \text{Peak Hour Ratio})$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Terminal Building as a Five-Star Hotel during a Wedding Weekend.**
>
> Just as a hotel must know — in advance — how many guests will arrive on Friday evening, how many will queue at the reception desk in 15-minute bursts, how many will use the restaurant, and how long each guest will occupy a room, an airport terminal must forecast the **same statistics** for travelers. The hotel manager doesn't size the lobby for the *average* guest; they size it for the *peak Saturday morning rush*. Airports do exactly the same — but with much tighter tolerances because missed flights have legal, safety, and economic consequences far greater than a delayed check-in.

### The "Why" Behind Passenger Requirement Studies

1. **Avoid under-sizing:** A terminal that cannot handle peak passenger volumes creates cascading delays (missed connections, security breaches, passenger dissatisfaction).
2. **Avoid over-sizing:** A terminal that is too large wastes capital, increases walking distances, and inflates operational costs (air-conditioning, cleaning, staffing).
3. **Maintain Level of Service (LoS):** IATA's *Airport Development Reference Manual (ADRM)* defines acceptable waiting times and area densities; passenger requirement analysis ensures these standards are met.

### Key Standard Metrics Used in KTU-aligned Problems

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Peak Hour Factor (PHF) | $PHF$ | **0.85 – 0.95** |
| Mode Split (Road/Rail/Metro) | $M_i$ | Sum = **1.00** |
| Design Hour Volume | $DHV$ | 30th busiest hour of the year |
| Peak Month / Average Month | $PM$ | **1.10 – 1.40** |
| Passenger Time in Terminal (Domestic) | $T_d$ | **45 – 60 min** |
| Passenger Time in Terminal (International) | $T_i$ | **60 – 90 min** |

## 1.3 Classification of Passengers

Passengers are broadly classified based on their journey pattern, which directly influences facility design:

1. **Originating Passengers** — Start their journey at the airport (use full check-in, security, boarding process).
2. **Terminating Passengers** — End their journey at the airport (use baggage claim, exit).
3. **Transfer / Transit Passengers** — Change aircraft at the airport (use minimum facilities, may need transfer desks).
4. **Transit-Without-Visa (TWOV)** — Special international category requiring segregated holding areas.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Most numerical problems in Module 3 deal with **originating passengers** because their flow is the most facility-intensive. Always read the problem carefully to identify the passenger category.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Structure of Passenger Demand

The passenger requirement analysis is built on a **pyramid of demand conversion** — moving from annual statistics down to design-hour figures. Each step uses a multiplicative ratio:

$$\text{Annual Passengers} \xrightarrow{\div 12} \text{Peak Month} \xrightarrow{\div 30} \text{Peak Day} \xrightarrow{\times \text{PHR}} \text{Peak Hour} \xrightarrow{\times \text{PDHF}} \text{Design Hour}$$

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Annual to Peak Month:** The peak month typically has **10 % – 40 %** more passengers than the average month.
- **Step 2 — Peak Month to Peak Day:** The peak day has **1.2 – 1.5 times** the average day of the peak month.
- **Step 3 — Peak Day to Peak Hour:** The peak hour ratio is the **busiest hour of the peak day**, usually **8 % – 15 %** of the day's traffic.
- **Step 4 — Peak Hour to Design Hour:** The Design Hour is often taken as the **30th busiest hour of the year** (a more economical design choice than absolute peak).

## 2.2 The "Typical Peak Hour" Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IATA Definition — Typical Peak Hour (TPH):**
> The passenger volume during the average peak hour of the peak month. It is the most commonly used design parameter in KTU numerical problems.

$$TPH = \frac{\text{Annual Passengers} \times PMR \times PHR \times PDHR}{12 \times 30}$$

Where:
- $PMR$ = Peak Month Ratio
- $PHR$ = Peak Hour Ratio (of day)
- $PDHR$ = Peak Day-to-Hour Ratio

## 2.3 Mode of Arrival/Departure Split

Airports are not isolated entities — passengers reach/leave the terminal using various transport modes. The split affects:
- Curb-side design (kerb length)
- Parking area
- Access road width
- Metro/rail station sizing

$$P_{\text{road}} + P_{\text{rail}} + P_{\text{metro}} + P_{\text{taxi}} + P_{\text{bus}} = P_{\text{total}}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PHP = \dfrac{ADMP \times PMR \times PHR}{12}$ | Peak Hour Passengers from Average Daily Peak Month | pax/hr |
| 2 | $DPH = PHP \times \alpha$ | Design Peak Hour (α = 1.0 to 1.3) | pax/hr |
| 3 | $N_{\text{cntr}} = \dfrac{PHP}{S_{\text{cntr}} \times t_{\text{open}}}$ | Number of check-in counters | counters |
| 4 | $N_{\text{security}} = \dfrac{PHP \times f_{\text{security}}}{S_{\text{security}} \times t_{\text{open}}}$ | Number of security lanes | lanes |
| 5 | $N_{\text{immig}} = \dfrac{PHP}{S_{\text{immig}} \times t_{\text{open}}}$ | Number of immigration counters | counters |
| 6 | $A_{\text{check-in}} = N_{\text{cntr}} \times A_{\text{per-counter}}$ | Check-in area | m² |
| 7 | $A_{\text{holdroom}} = PHP \times a_{\text{hold}}$ | Holding room area | m² |
| 8 | $A_{\text{concourse}} = PHP \times a_{\text{concourse}}$ | Concourse area | m² |
| 9 | $A_{\text{claim}} = N_{\text{belt}} \times L_{\text{belt}} \times w$ | Baggage claim area | m² |
| 10 | $L_{\text{kerb}} = PHP \times l_{\text{kerb}}$ | Kerb length required | metres |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important: Pipe Symbol Safety.** All absolute values in the formulas above are written using simple notation (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ is replaced by $\vert x \vert$ only when used in a context where it cannot break tables). Standard KTU tables prefer non-pipe symbols for safety.

## 2.5 Standard Service Rates Used in KTU Problems

| Facility | Service Rate ($S$) | Service Time (s) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Check-in counter (manual) | **60 – 80 pax/hr** | 45 – 60 | Self-service kiosks: 120 pax/hr |
| Security lane (X-ray + ETD) | **200 – 250 pax/hr** | 14 – 18 | For economy class |
| Immigration counter | **120 – 180 pax/hr** | 20 – 30 | Varies domestic/international |
| Boarding gate | **30 – 40 pax/min** | 1.5 – 2.0 s/pax | Per gate agent |
| Baggage claim (per metre) | **15 – 20 pax/min/m** | — | Depends on bag size |

## 2.6 IATA Level of Service (LoS) — Area Standards

| LoS | Description | Hold Room (m²/pax) | Check-in Queue (m²/pax) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Excellent | 1.4 | 1.7 |
| **B** | High | 1.2 | 1.4 |
| **C** | Good | 1.0 | 1.2 |
| **D** | Adequate | 0.8 | 1.0 |
| **E** | Inadequate | 0.6 | 0.8 |
| **F** | Unacceptable | < 0.6 | < 0.8 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Convention:** Most Indian airports are designed for **LoS C (Good)** — balanced between cost and comfort. Always state the assumed LoS in numerical answers.

## 2.7 Why Passenger Requirements Matter in Real Engineering

- **Capital Investment:** Terminal construction costs ₹ 50,000 – 1,00,000 per m². A 10 % over-sizing wastes crores.
- **Operational Efficiency:** Under-sized security lanes cause the famous "queue spillover" seen at Indian airports.
- **Safety & Emergency Egress:** Capacity directly determines the number, width, and location of emergency exits (NBC 2016, Part 4).
- **Concession Revenue:** Retail areas are sized based on dwell time × passenger flow — directly tied to passenger requirements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Annual Passengers to Peak Hour

Let us derive the **Typical Peak Hour (TPH)** formula explicitly.

**Given:**
- $A$ = Annual passengers = 30,00,000
- $PMR$ (Peak Month Ratio) = 1.30
- $PDR$ (Peak Day Ratio) = 1.20
- $PHR$ (Peak Hour Ratio) = 0.10

**Step 1:** Compute the Peak Month Passengers.

The peak month ratio is defined as:
$$PMR = \frac{\text{Peak Month Passengers}}{\text{Average Month Passengers}}$$

Average month passengers:
$$\bar{M} = \frac{A}{12} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{12} = 2{,}50{,}000 \ \text{pax/month}$$

Peak month passengers:
$$M_{\text{peak}} = \bar{M} \times PMR = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 1.30 = 3{,}25{,}000 \ \text{pax/month}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Peak Day Passengers.

Average peak-month daily traffic:
$$\bar{D} = \frac{M_{\text{peak}}}{30} = \frac{3{,}25{,}000}{30} = 10{,}833.33 \ \text{pax/day}$$

Peak day passengers:
$$D_{\text{peak}} = \bar{D} \times PDR = 10{,}833.33 \times 1.20 = 13{,}000 \ \text{pax/day}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Peak Hour Passengers.

$$PHP = D_{\text{peak}} \times PHR = 13{,}000 \times 0.10 = 1{,}300 \ \text{pax/hr}$$

### Consolidated Master Equation

$$\boxed{PHP = \frac{A}{12 \times 30} \times PMR \times PDR \times PHR}$$

$$\boxed{PHP = \frac{A \cdot PMR \cdot PDR \cdot PHR}{360}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Check-in Counters

**Given:**
- $PHP = 1{,}300$ pax/hr (computed above)
- Service rate per counter: $S = 75$ pax/hr
- Counter operating time: $t_{\text{open}} = 1.0$ hr

**Logic:** Each counter can serve $S$ passengers per hour, so the number of counters required is:

$$N_{\text{check-in}} = \frac{PHP}{S} = \frac{1{,}300}{75} = 17.33$$

Rounding up (counters must be whole, and queues should not be allowed):
$$N_{\text{check-in}} = 18 \ \text{counters}$$

**Including queue time:** For a target maximum wait of $W$ minutes with arrival rate $\lambda = PHP/60$ pax/min:

$$N_{\text{check-in,queue}} = \frac{\lambda \cdot W}{60} + N_{\text{check-in}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Security Lane Count

**Given:**
- $PHP = 1{,}300$ pax/hr
- Service rate per security lane: $S_{\text{sec}} = 225$ pax/hr
- Fraction of passengers using security = 1.0 (all)
- $t_{\text{open}} = 1.0$ hr

$$N_{\text{security}} = \frac{PHP \times 1.0}{S_{\text{sec}}} = \frac{1{,}300}{225} = 5.78$$

Round up:
$$N_{\text{security}} = 6 \ \text{lanes}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Holding Room Area

**Given:**
- $PHP = 1{,}300$ pax/hr
- Average dwell time in holdroom: $T_h = 30$ min = 0.5 hr
- IATA LoS C: $a_{\text{hold}} = 1.0$ m²/pax

Passengers present simultaneously:
$$P_{\text{hold}} = PHP \times T_h = 1{,}300 \times 0.5 = 650 \ \text{pax}$$

Area required:
$$A_{\text{holdroom}} = P_{\text{hold}} \times a_{\text{hold}} = 650 \times 1.0 = 650 \ \text{m}^2$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Baggage Claim Belt Length

**Given:**
- Terminating passengers: 60 % of $PHP$ = $0.60 \times 1{,}300 = 780$ pax/hr
- Belt capacity: $C_b = 18$ pax/min per metre of belt frontage
- Maximum waiting time: $W = 20$ min

Required belt length (frontage):
$$L_b = \frac{780}{18 \times 60} \times 20 = 14.44 \ \text{m}$$

Round up to standard belt size:
$$L_b = 15 \ \text{m (1 belt of standard 18 m size)}$$

## 3.6 Master Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for KTU Problems

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Typical Value (illustrative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Annual Passengers | $A$ | Given | 30,00,000 |
| Peak Month Ratio | $PMR$ | Given | 1.30 |
| Peak Day Ratio | $PDR$ | Given | 1.20 |
| Peak Hour Ratio | $PHR$ | Given | 0.10 |
| Peak Hour Passengers | $PHP$ | $\dfrac{A \cdot PMR \cdot PDR \cdot PHR}{360}$ | 1,300 pax/hr |
| Check-in Counters | $N_c$ | $\dfrac{PHP}{S_c}$ | 18 |
| Security Lanes | $N_s$ | $\dfrac{PHP}{S_s}$ | 6 |
| Immigration Counters | $N_i$ | $\dfrac{PHP}{S_i}$ | 8 |
| Holdroom Area | $A_h$ | $PHP \cdot T_h \cdot a_h$ | 650 m² |
| Concourse Area | $A_{cc}$ | $PHP \cdot a_{cc}$ | 1,950 m² |
| Baggage Belt Length | $L_b$ | $\dfrac{0.6 \cdot PHP \cdot W}{C_b \cdot 60}$ | 15 m |
| Kerb Length | $L_k$ | $PHP \cdot l_k$ | 130 m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Safety:** All $\vert \cdot \vert$ in any potential KTU exam answer should be written with $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or as plain context. Tables above use no vertical pipe symbols to remain markdown-safe.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** An airport handles 1.5 crore (15 million) annual passengers. Peak month ratio = 1.25, peak day ratio = 1.15, peak hour ratio = 0.09. Compute the Typical Peak Hour Passengers.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Average daily traffic:
$$\bar{D} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{365} = 4{,}109.59 \ \text{pax/day}$$

Step 2 — Peak day traffic:
$$D_{\text{peak}} = 4{,}109.59 \times 1.15 = 4{,}726.03 \ \text{pax/day}$$

Step 3 — Peak hour:
$$PHP = 4{,}726.03 \times 0.09 = 425.34 \ \text{pax/hr}$$

Rounded:
$$PHP \approx 426 \ \text{pax/hr}$$

> [!TIP]
> Always carry **at least 2 decimal places** in intermediate steps and round only the final answer. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for premature rounding.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Passenger Flow Architecture (Block-Level Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[KERB SIDE APPROACH] --> B[CHECK IN HALL]
    B --> C[SECURITY HOLD]
    C --> D[IMMIGRATION DEPARTURE]
    D --> E[HOLD ROOM]
    E --> F[BOARDING GATE]
    F --> G[AIRCRAFT STAND]

    G --> H[DISEMBARKATION]
    H --> I[IMMIGRATION ARRIVAL]
    I --> J[BAGGAGE CLAIM]
    J --> K[CUSTOMS]
    K --> L[ARRIVAL HALL]
    L --> M[KERB SIDE EXIT]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Passenger Demand Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[ANNUAL PASSENGERS] --> A1[PEAK MONTH PASSENGERS]
    A1 --> A2[PEAK DAY PASSENGERS]
    A2 --> A3[PEAK HOUR PASSENGERS]
    A3 --> A4[DESIGN PEAK HOUR]
    A4 --> A5[FACILITY SIZING]
```

## 4.3 Terminal Functional Area Allocation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DEPARTURES
        D1[KERB]
        D2[CHECK IN HALL]
        D3[SECURITY]
        D4[DEPARTURE IMMIGRATION]
        D5[CONCOURSE]
        D6[HOLD ROOM]
        D7[GATE]
    end
    subgraph ARRIVALS
        R1[GATE DISEMBARK]
        R2[ARRIVAL IMMIGRATION]
        R3[BAGGAGE CLAIM]
        R4[CUSTOMS]
        R5[ARRIVAL HALL]
        R6[KERB EXIT]
    end
    subgraph TRANSFER
        T1[TRANSFER DESK]
        T2[SECURITY RESCREEN]
    end
```

## 4.4 Facility Sizing Logic — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[PEAK HOUR PASSENGERS] --> Q{FACILITY TYPE}
    Q -->|Counter| R[Divide by Service Rate]
    Q -->|Area| S[Multiply by Area per Pax]
    Q -->|Length| T[Multiply by Length per Pax]
    R --> U[Round Up]
    S --> V[Apply LoS Factor]
    T --> W[Apply Mode Split]
    U --> X[FINAL FACILITY COUNT]
    V --> X
    W --> X
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `P`, `Q`, `R`, `X`) to satisfy Mermaid parser safeguards. No reserved keywords are used as node IDs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "Peak Hour Passengers" (PHP) as used in airport terminal planning. State any two standard ratios used in the conversion from annual to peak hour passengers.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Peak Hour Passengers (PHP)** is the maximum number of passengers expected to arrive at or depart from the terminal during the single busiest 60-minute period of the design day.

The two standard ratios used in the conversion are:
1. **Peak Month Ratio (PMR):** Ratio of the busiest month's passengers to the average month's passengers.
2. **Peak Hour Ratio (PHR):** Ratio of the busiest hour's passengers to the total passengers of the peak day.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three IATA Level of Service (LoS) standards for terminal hold rooms and state which LoS is typically adopted for Indian airports.**

**Model Answer:**

IATA Level of Service standards for hold rooms (area per passenger):

| LoS | Area (m²/pax) |
|---|---|
| A — Excellent | 1.4 |
| B — High | 1.2 |
| C — Good | 1.0 |

**Typically adopted LoS in India: C (Good)** — providing a balance between passenger comfort and capital cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** An airport handled 1.2 crore (12 million) passengers in the year 2023. The peak month ratio is 1.30, peak day ratio is 1.20, and peak hour ratio is 0.10. Calculate the Typical Peak Hour Passengers (TPH). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the design service rate of a check-in counter is 75 pax/hr and the operating time is 1 hour, determine the number of check-in counters required. State the assumption regarding LoS. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Peak Hour Calculation:**

Step 1 — Average daily traffic:
$$\bar{D} = \frac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000}{365} = 32{,}876.71 \ \text{pax/day}$$

Step 2 — Peak day traffic:
$$D_{\text{peak}} = 32{,}876.71 \times 1.20 = 39{,}452.05 \ \text{pax/day}$$

Step 3 — Peak hour passengers:
$$TPH = 39{,}452.05 \times 0.10 = 3{,}945.21 \approx 3{,}946 \ \text{pax/hr}$$

**Part (b) — Check-in Counters:**

$$N_{\text{check-in}} = \frac{TPH}{S_c} = \frac{3{,}946}{75} = 52.61$$

Rounded up:
$$\boxed{N_{\text{check-in}} = 53 \ \text{counters}}$$

**Assumption:** IATA **LoS C (Good)** adopted, with a service rate of 75 pax/hr per counter.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the flow of departing and arriving passengers through a typical airport terminal. Identify any three key processing areas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The Peak Hour Passenger (PHP) of a terminal is 2,000 pax/hr. If 60 % are departing and the average dwell time in the hold room is 30 minutes, determine the hold room area required at LoS C. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Passenger Flow:**

**Departing passenger flow:**
Kerb-side → Check-in Hall → Security Hold → Departure Immigration → Concourse → Hold Room → Boarding Gate → Aircraft

**Arriving passenger flow:**
Aircraft → Disembarkation → Arrival Immigration → Baggage Claim → Customs → Arrival Hall → Kerb-side Exit

**Three key processing areas:**
1. **Check-in Hall** — Passenger acceptance and baggage acceptance.
2. **Security Screening** — Mandatory hold-baggage and personal security check.
3. **Baggage Claim** — Return of checked baggage to terminating passengers.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[KERB] --> C1[CHECK IN] --> S1[SECURITY] --> I1[IMMIGRATION] --> H1[HOLD ROOM] --> G1[GATE] --> AC1[AIRCRAFT]
    AC1 --> AC2[AIRCRAFT] --> G2[GATE] --> I2[IMMIGRATION] --> B1[BAGGAGE CLAIM] --> CU1[CUSTOMS] --> A1[ARRIVAL HALL] --> K2[KERB EXIT]
```

**Part (b) — Hold Room Area:**

Departing passengers:
$$P_{\text{dep}} = 0.60 \times 2{,}000 = 1{,}200 \ \text{pax/hr}$$

Passengers present simultaneously in hold room (dwell time 0.5 hr):
$$P_{\text{hold}} = 1{,}200 \times 0.5 = 600 \ \text{pax}$$

At LoS C, $a_{\text{hold}} = 1.0$ m²/pax:
$$A_{\text{hold}} = 600 \times 1.0 = \boxed{600 \ \text{m}^2}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always round UP** facility counts (counters, lanes, belts). Rounding down causes immediate deduction of 1 mark.
> 2. **Carry intermediate decimals** until the final step — premature rounding is a common 0.5 – 1 mark loss.
> 3. **State your LoS assumption** explicitly. Skipping this costs 1 mark in Part (b) of any area-calculation question.
> 4. **Distinguish "departing" vs "arriving"** passengers when applying ratios. International examiners will not assume it for you.
> 5. **Use correct units** in final answer (pax/hr for PHP, m² for area, m for length). Unit errors are an automatic 0.5 mark deduction in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Don't confuse Peak Day Ratio (PDR) with Peak Hour Ratio (PHR).** PDR applies to daily conversion, PHR to hourly. A swap leads to a 10×–12× error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Passenger Requirements at Terminal Building**

- **Peak Hour Passengers (PHP)** = $(A / 365) \times PMR \times PDR \times PHR$ — the core working equation.
- **Design Hour** is usually the **30th busiest hour** of the year, NOT the absolute peak (for cost economy).
- **Typical Peak Hour** = average peak hour of the peak month — most common in KTU problems.
- **Five major passenger categories:** Originating, Terminating, Transfer, TWOV, and Transit.
- **Check-in count:** $N_c = PHP / S_c$ — always round up.
- **Security lanes:** $N_s = PHP / S_s$ — typically $S_s = 200$–$250$ pax/hr/lane.
- **Hold room area:** $A_h = PHP \times T_h \times a_h$ — depends on dwell time and LoS.
- **IATA LoS C** is the default Indian standard ($a_h = 1.0$ m²/pax).
- **Baggage claim belt:** Length is governed by terminating passengers and waiting time tolerance.
- **Kerb length:** $L_k = PHP \times l_k$ — typically 0.05 m/pax.
- **Mode split** is essential for access planning — sum of all modes = 1.0.
- **Always carry 2 decimal places** in intermediate steps; round only at the end.
- **Unit consistency:** pax/hr for flow, m² for area, m for length, minutes for dwell time.
- **LoS assumption** must always be explicitly stated in any area calculation.
- **Block diagrams of passenger flow** are a high-yield topic for both 3-mark and 7-mark sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Space Requirements - Design Peak Hour Demand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Space Requirements - Design Peak Hour Demand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design Peak Hour Demand (DPHD)** is the standardized level of traffic demand adopted by airport planners as the basis for sizing all physical facilities of an airport. It represents the *average hourly passenger or aircraft volume* that an airport's infrastructure must be capable of processing while maintaining an acceptable **Level of Service (LoS)** as defined by IATA or FAA.

The demand is generally derived from a deterministic or probabilistic analysis of the **Design Day** of the **Peak Month** of the **Average Week** of the year, and is quantified in terms of *passengers per hour (pphpd)*, *aircraft movements per hour*, or *vehicles per hour*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Design Peak Hour Demand is the **30th (or 40th) busiest hour of the year** (FAA convention) — also called the *Standard Busy Hour* — chosen so that facilities operate within acceptable service standards for approximately 99.9% of all hours annually, avoiding over-design for the few absolute peak hours.

### 1.2 Hierarchy of Time-Based Demand Parameters

The following nested hierarchy is used in airport demand characterization:

| Time Horizon | Definition | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Average Annual Day (AAD) | Mean daily traffic over 365 days | Strategic capacity planning |
| Average Day Peak Month (ADPM) | Average day of the busiest month | Terminal sizing baseline |
| Design Day | ADPM or 90th percentile busy day | Facility design input |
| Peak Hour (PH) | The 60-min interval of max activity | Operational staffing |
| Design Peak Hour | 30th–40th busiest hour of the year | **Sizing all physical facilities** |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Movie Theatre" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Designing a Movie Theatre Complex**
> Imagine designing a 10-screen multiplex. The architect does **not** build for the *single most sold-out Saturday night* of the year (that would mean a 50-screen monster). Instead, the architect sizes parking, lobby, food courts, washrooms, and seating access for a *typical busy Friday evening* — say, the 30th busiest hour out of 8,760 hours of the year. This ensures the cinema handles **almost all** busy evenings comfortably without wasteful over-construction.
>
> In the *exact same way*, airport planners use **Design Peak Hour Demand** to size terminals, runways, aprons, parking lots, and security lanes. Building for the absolute peak would cost billions in idle infrastructure; building for the *30th-busiest hour* gives near-perfect service 99.9% of the time at reasonable cost.

---

## 1.4 Why Design Peak Hour Demand Matters in Airport Engineering

- **Capital Cost Optimization**: Over-design inflates construction cost; under-design causes chronic congestion.
- **Service Quality (LoS)**: IATA assigns **A (Excellent) → F (Unacceptable)** levels. The design hour targets LoS C or D.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: ICAO Annex 14 and FAA AC 150/5360-13 mandate specific design-hour assumptions for licensing.
- **Land-Use Planning**: Terminal footprints, apron layouts, and access roads are irrevocably decided by the chosen design hour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used:**
> - FAA Design Standard: **30th busiest hour** (Commercial Service Airports)
> - IATA Recommended LoS: **C–D** (Optimum–Sub-optimum) for design year
> - Planning Horizon: **5, 10, 15, and 20 years** (Phased Master Plan)
> - Peak Hour Factor (PHF): Typically **0.80 – 0.95**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Peak Hour Factor (PHF)

The **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** reconverts sub-hourly spikes into equivalent peak-hour volumes and is the cornerstone of stochastic demand modeling.

$$\text{PHF} = \frac{V_{\text{peak hour}}}{4 \times V_{\text{peak 15-min interval}}}$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{peak hour}}$ = total volume in the busiest 60-min period
- $V_{\text{peak 15-min}}$ = volume in the busiest 15-min sub-interval

A **PHF = 1.0** means perfectly uniform flow; **PHF = 0.80** indicates that 25% of the peak hour's demand arrives in 15 minutes (typical of banked airline schedules).

---

## 2.2 Design Day Selection Logic

The Design Day must be statistically robust. Three principal approaches are taught in KTU 2024:

1. **Average Day of Peak Month (ADPM)** — the most widely used (FAA/IATA default).
2. **Busy Day of Average Week** — used when the peak month has a single disruptive event.
3. **Standard Busy Day** — the day containing the 30th-busiest hour; used for very large hub airports.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem does not specify which day to use, default to **ADPM** and the **30th-busiest hour of that day**.

---

## 2.3 Space Requirement Categories Driven by DPHD

The Design Peak Hour Passenger (DPHP) value drives sizing of **seven** primary space categories:

| # | Facility | Driven By | IATA / FAA Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Terminal Floor Area | DPHP | 150–200 sq.ft per DPHP |
| 2 | Apron / Gate Positions | DPHP + Aircraft mix | Gate occupancy time |
| 3 | Check-in Counters | DPHP × Check-in time | 90–120 min service window |
| 4 | Security Lanes | DPHP × Security rate | 8–12 s per passenger |
| 5 | Baggage Claim Units | Arriving DPHP | 60–75 pax per device |
| 6 | Curbside Length | DPHP × dwell time | 30–45 s drop-off |
| 7 | Car Parking Spaces | DPHP × Mode-share | 0.5–0.8 spaces per DPHP |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~70% of all numerical questions on this topic in KTU ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Peak Hour Factor (PHF) | $\text{PHF} = \dfrac{V_{60}}{4 \cdot V_{15}}$ | dimensionless | $0.80 \le \text{PHF} \le 1.0$ |
| 2 | Design Peak Hour Passengers (DPHP) | $\text{DPHP} = \dfrac{\text{ADPM passengers}}{24} \times \text{PH}$ | pphpd | Multiply by 2 if only AM peak known |
| 3 | DPHP from Annual Total | $\text{DPHP} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Pax}}{365} \times \text{Peak Day Ratio} \times \text{PH Ratio}$ | pphpd | Ratios are jurisdiction-specific |
| 4 | Terminal Floor Area (FAA) | $A_t = \text{DPHP} \times a$ | sq.ft | $a = 150\text{–}200$ sq.ft/DPHP |
| 5 | Terminal Floor Area (IATA) | $A_t = \sum (n_i \cdot s_i)$ | sq.m | Sum of (passengers × space standard) |
| 6 | Number of Gates (FAA) | $G = \dfrac{T_g \cdot N_f}{U \cdot 60}$ | integer | $T_g$ = gate occupancy time, min |
| 7 | Check-in Counters | $N_c = \dfrac{\text{DPHP} \cdot t_{\text{checkin}}}{S_c \cdot 60}$ | integer | $S_c$ = service time per pax, min |
| 8 | Security Lanes | $N_s = \dfrac{\text{DPHP} \cdot t_{\text{security}}}{3600}$ | integer | $t \approx 8\text{–}12$ s per pax |
| 9 | Baggage Claim Units | $N_b = \dfrac{\text{DPHP}_{\text{arr}}}{C_b}$ | integer | $C_b \approx 60\text{–}75$ pax/device/hour |
| 10 | Curbside Length | $L_c = \text{DPHP}_{\text{veh}} \cdot d \cdot f_s$ | metres | $d$ = dwell time, $f_s$ = spill factor |
| 11 | Parking Spaces | $P = \text{DPHP} \cdot k_p + P_{\text{emp}}$ | integer | $k_p \approx 0.5\text{–}0.8$ |
| 12 | Peak Hour Demand Ratio | $R_{ph} = \dfrac{\text{PH volume}}{\text{Daily volume}}$ | % | Typically 8–12% for terminals |

> **Notation Note:** In every formula above, $\vert$ is rendered as $\mid$ to preserve markdown table integrity. All hourly rates use **60** as the conversion constant from minutes, and **3600** from seconds.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Runway Length Sizing**: DPHD determines annual service volume (ASV) used in FAA's capacity curves.
2. **Environmental Clearance**: India's MoEF mandates Noise & Air Quality modeling for the *design hour*.
3. **PPP Concession Agreements**: AAI and global PPP contracts (e.g., Delhi, Mumbai T2) define **Minimum Annual Guarantee (MAG)** based on DPHP forecasts.
4. **Master Plan Horizons**: 20-year forecast at growth rate $g$: $\text{DPHP}_{t+n} = \text{DPHP}_t (1+g)^n$.

---

## 2.6 FAA vs IATA — Comparative Philosophy

| Aspect | FAA (USA) | IATA (International) |
|---|---|---|
| Design Hour | 30th busiest hour of year | Busy hour of typical peak day |
| LoS Target | LOS C–D | LoS C–D |
| Floor Area Standard | Empirical 150–200 sq.ft/DPHP | Functional-area summation |
| Use Case | Single-region (US) | Global airports |
| Forecasting | 5-yr, 10-yr, 20-yr horizons | Same, but with airline-by-airline data |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Board Exams (India context)**, prefer **AAI (Airports Authority of India) guidelines** which closely follow IATA philosophy with FAA design-hour assumptions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Peak Hour Factor from Sub-Hourly Counts

**Statement:** Derive PHF given peak-hour and peak-15-minute flow rates.

**Step 1 — Define the peak hour volume.**
The peak hour volume $V_{60}$ is the total number of vehicles or passengers crossing a point in the busiest 60 consecutive minutes.

**Step 2 — Define the peak 15-minute volume.**
The peak 15-minute volume $V_{15}$ is the busiest 15 consecutive minutes within (or overlapping) the peak hour.

**Step 3 — Establish the homogeneity assumption.**
If flow were uniform, the peak 15 minutes would contain exactly $V_{60}/4$ of the hour's volume. The PHF measures the *deviation* from this ideal.

**Step 4 — Construct the ratio.**

$$\text{PHF} = \frac{V_{60}}{\left(4 \times V_{15}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute and interpret.**

$$\text{PHF} = \frac{2000}{4 \times 600} = \frac{2000}{2400} = 0.833$$

A PHF of 0.833 indicates that the 15-minute sub-peak is 20% higher than the average quarter-hour, reflecting banked-arrival scheduling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Design Peak Hour Passengers (DPHP) from Annual Traffic

**Statement:** Compute DPHP for an airport handling 18 million annual passengers, given an ADPM ratio of 1.20 and a peak-hour ratio of 10%.

**Step 1 — Compute Average Day of the Year (ADY).**

$$\text{ADY} = \frac{\text{Annual Passengers}}{365} = \frac{18{,}000{,}000}{365} = 49{,}315 \text{ pax/day}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Peak-Month Ratio to obtain Average Day Peak Month (ADPM).**

$$\text{ADPM} = \text{ADY} \times R_{pm} = 49{,}315 \times 1.20 = 59{,}178 \text{ pax/day}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Peak-Hour Ratio to obtain DPHP.**

$$\text{DPHP} = \text{ADPM} \times R_{ph} = 59{,}178 \times 0.10 = 5{,}918 \text{ pphpd}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check using reverse derivation.**

$$\text{Annual} = 5{,}918 \times \frac{1}{0.10} \times \frac{1}{1.20} \times 365 = 18{,}001{,}475 \approx 18\text{ M} \;\; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of Aircraft Gates Required

**Statement:** A terminal handles 25 peak-hour aircraft movements. Average gate occupancy time is 45 minutes. Compute gates required.

**Step 1 — State the gate-occupancy model.**

$$G = \frac{N_f \times T_g}{U \times 60}$$

Where $G$ = number of gates, $N_f$ = peak-hour flights, $T_g$ = average gate occupancy (min), $U$ = gate utilization factor.

**Step 2 — Substitute values** ($N_f = 25$, $T_g = 45$ min, $U = 0.85$).

$$G = \frac{25 \times 45}{0.85 \times 60}$$
