**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator.**

$$G = \frac{1125}{51.0} = 22.06$$

**Step 4 — Round up to the next integer (gates are discrete).**

$$G = 23 \text{ gates}$$

**Step 5 — Apply aircraft-mix correction** (e.g., 70% narrow-body, 30% wide-body).

$$G_{nb} = 0.70 \times 23 = 16 \text{ NB gates}, \quad G_{wb} = 0.30 \times 23 = 7 \text{ WB gates}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Terminal Floor Area (FAA Empirical Method)

**Statement:** For a DPHP of 6,000 pax, compute terminal floor area using FAA standard of **180 sq.ft/DPHP** with a 15% circulation allowance.

**Step 1 — Compute base functional area.**

$$A_{base} = \text{DPHP} \times a = 6{,}000 \times 180 = 1{,}080{,}000 \text{ sq.ft}$$

**Step 2 — Add 15% for circulation, mechanical, and structural cores.**

$$A_{gross} = A_{base} \times 1.15 = 1{,}080{,}000 \times 1.15 = 1{,}242{,}000 \text{ sq.ft}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to square metres** (1 sq.ft = 0.0929 sq.m).

$$A_{gross} = 1{,}242{,}000 \times 0.0929 \approx 115{,}382 \text{ sq.m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **IATA Cross-Check:** A medium-large international hub typically allocates **20,000 sq.m per million annual passenger (MAP)**, giving 360,000 sq.m for 18 MAP — much higher because IATA includes international transfer and process-specific buffers. The FAA method undervalues international terminals.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Check-In Counter Requirement

**Statement:** DPHP = 5,918; 80% are originating passengers; average check-in time = 90 min; service time per counter = 1.5 min per pax.

**Step 1 — Compute originating passengers per hour.**

$$P_o = 0.80 \times 5{,}918 = 4{,}734 \text{ pax/hr}$$

**Step 2 — State the counter equation.**

$$N_c = \frac{P_o \times t_{\text{checkin window}}}{S_c \times 60}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** (90 min window, 1.5 min service).

$$N_c = \frac{4{,}734 \times 90}{1.5 \times 60} = \frac{426{,}060}{90} = 4{,}734$$

**Step 4 — Apply 80% utilization (pax do not arrive in one instant).**

$$N_c^{\text{design}} = \frac{4{,}734}{0.80} = 5{,}918 \text{ counters}$$

**Step 5 — Add 10% redundancy for breakdowns.**

$$N_c^{\text{final}} = 5{,}918 \times 1.10 = 6{,}510 \;\;\text{(unrealistically high — implies staggered schedule assumption)}\;\;\rightarrow\;\;\text{use 30-min peak window instead:}$$

$$N_c = \frac{4{,}734 \times 30}{1.5 \times 60} = \frac{142{,}020}{90} = 1{,}578 \text{ counters}$$

The chosen time-window drives the answer; **KTU expects the 60-min window for the design hour**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of PHF on Facility Sizing

| PHF Value | Operational Meaning | Design-Hour Volume | LoS Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.00 | Perfectly uniform flow | Equals $4 \times V_{15}$ | Excellent (A) |
| 0.95 | Mild peaking | $3.8 \times V_{15}$ | Optimum (C) |
| 0.85 | Moderate peaking (typical hub) | $3.4 \times V_{15}$ | Sub-optimum (D) |
| 0.75 | Heavy banking | $3.0 \times V_{15}$ | Critical (E) |
| 0.60 | Single flight-wave airport | $2.4 \times V_{15}$ | Unacceptable (F) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** A lower PHF **increases** sub-hourly congestion even if the peak-hour volume is unchanged. Facilities like security lanes, check-in, and baggage claim must be sized for the **peak 15-minute rate**, not the peak hour.

---

## 3.7 Master Plan Growth Equation (KTU Favourite)

For a forecasted growth rate $g$ over $n$ years:

$$\text{DPHP}_{t+n} = \text{DPHP}_t \times (1+g)^n$$

**Example:** DPHP = 5,918; $g = 6\%$; $n = 15$ years.

$$\text{DPHP}_{t+15} = 5{,}918 \times (1.06)^{15} = 5{,}918 \times 2.3966 = 14{,}183 \text{ pphpd}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Trap:** Do not confuse *compound growth* $(1+g)^n$ with *linear growth* $(1 + gn)$. The first is correct; the second is the most common KTU mistake.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram A — Demand Hierarchy Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Annual Passenger Demand 18 Million"] --> A2["Average Day of Year 49315 pax day"]
    A2 --> A3["Average Day Peak Month 59178 pax day"]
    A3 --> A4["Design Day 59178 pax day"]
    A4 --> A5["Average Peak Hour 5918 pphpd"]
    A5 --> A6["Peak 15 min Sub Interval 1775 pax 15min"]
    A6 --> A7["Design Peak Hour Demand Final Sizing Input"]

    style A1 fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style A2 fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style A3 fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style A4 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff

    style A6 fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000000
    style A7 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000
```

> **Interpretation:** Each downward level concentrates annual demand into the critical design hour. The bottom node (yellow) is the *single scalar value* used for all facility sizing.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram B — Facility-Sizing Architecture from DPHD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DPHD["Design Peak Hour Demand DPHP in pphpd"] --> F1["Terminal Floor Area"]
    DPHD --> F2["Apron and Gate Layout"]

    DPHD --> F4["Security Screening Lanes"]
    DPHD --> F5["Baggage Claim Devices"]
    DPHD --> F6["Curbside Length"]
    DPHD --> F7["Vehicle Parking Spaces"]
    DPHD --> F8["Concourse and Holdrooms"]

    F1 --> LoS1["IATA LoS C or D"]
    F2 --> LoS2["Aircraft Mix Gates by Size"]
    F3 --> LoS3["90 min Peak Service Window"]
    F4 --> LoS4["8 to 12 sec per Passenger"]
    F5 --> LoS5["60 to 75 pax per Device per Hour"]
    F6 --> LoS6["30 to 45 sec Dwell Time"]
    F7 --> LoS7["0.5 to 0.8 Spaces per DPHP"]
    F8 --> LoS8["1.5 to 2.0 sqm per Seated Pax"]

    style DPHD fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style LoS1 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000000
    style LoS2 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000000
    style LoS3 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000000
    style LoS4 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000000
    style LoS5 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000000
    style LoS6 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000000
    style LoS7 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000000
    style LoS8 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram C — Master-Plan Sizing Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1["Step 1: Forecast Annual Demand"] --> B2["Step 2: Apply Peak Ratios"]
    B2 --> B3["Step 3: Compute DPHP"]
    B3 --> B4["Step 4: Compute Each Facility Size"]
    B4 --> B5{"Step 5: Check LoS"}
    B5 -- Acceptable --> B6["Step 6: Lock Design"]
    B5 -- Over Capacity --> B7["Step 7: Increment Capacity"]
    B7 --> B3
    B5 -- Under Utilised --> B8["Step 8: Downsize or Phase"]
    B8 --> B4
    B6 --> B9["Step 9: Final Master Plan Output"]

    style B1 fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style B3 fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style B5 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000
    style B6 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style B7 fill:#ea580c,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style B8 fill:#ea580c,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style B9 fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#3b0764,color:#ffffff
```

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The sizing process is **iterative**, not linear. When LoS fails (red diamond), the DPHP input is increased or facility parameters are adjusted until acceptable service is achieved.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Design Peak Hour Demand" as used in airport terminal planning. Explain why the 30th busiest hour of the year is commonly chosen as the design hour.**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

Design Peak Hour Demand is the standardized hourly passenger or aircraft volume adopted for sizing all physical facilities of an airport. It represents the demand level at which the airport's infrastructure must operate at an acceptable **Level of Service (LoS C or D)** as defined by IATA.

The **30th busiest hour** of the year is preferred over the absolute peak hour for three reasons:

1. The absolute peak occurs in only a few hours annually; sizing for it leads to **unjustified capital expenditure** for capacity that is unused 99.99% of the time.
2. The 30th hour corresponds to approximately the **99.9 percentile** of all hours, providing near-perfect service for 8,760 – 30 ≈ 8,730 hours of the year.
3. It statistically represents a *typical busy day* of the *peak month*, balancing economy and service quality in line with FAA Advisory Circular 150/5360-13.

> *Marker Allocation (3 marks):* Definition (1) + 30th-hour justification (2).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is the Peak Hour Factor (PHF)? An airport terminal has a peak-hour flow of 2,400 pax and a peak 15-min flow of 750 pax. Compute the PHF and comment on the operational implication.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

PHF is the ratio of the total hourly volume to four times the peak 15-minute volume; it quantifies the uniformity (or peaking) of demand within the peak hour.

$$\text{PHF} = \frac{V_{60}}{4 \times V_{15}} = \frac{2400}{4 \times 750} = \frac{2400}{3000} = 0.80$$

A PHF of 0.80 indicates **moderate peaking**: the busiest 15 minutes handle 20% more passengers than the average quarter-hour. This is typical of hubs with **banked flight schedules** (waves of arrivals/departures). Facilities such as **security lanes and check-in counters** must be sized using the *peak 15-min rate* of 750 pax, not the smoothed hourly rate of 600 pax.

> *Marker Allocation (3 marks):* Definition (1) + Calculation (1) + Implication (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

A greenfield regional airport is being planned. Forecasts indicate **20 million annual passengers** with a **peak-month ratio of 1.25** and a **peak-hour ratio of 11%** of the day. The peak hour shows 2,800 pax in the busiest 15-minute interval.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Compute the Design Peak Hour Passengers (DPHP), the Peak Hour Factor (PHF), and classify the demand uniformity. State the engineering implication for security-lane sizing.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]** — Using FAA empirical norms, compute the terminal floor area (assume 175 sq.ft per DPHP with 15% circulation allowance), the number of aircraft gates required (25 peak-hour flights, 50 min gate occupancy, 0.85 utilization), and the number of check-in counters (70% originating pax, 1.5 min service time, 60-min window).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Average Day of the Year (ADY).**

$$\text{ADY} = \frac{20{,}000{,}000}{365} = 54{,}795 \text{ pax/day}$$

**Step 2: Average Day Peak Month (ADPM).**

$$\text{ADPM} = 54{,}795 \times 1.25 = 68{,}493 \text{ pax/day}$$

**Step 3: Design Peak Hour Passengers (DPHP).**

$$\text{DPHP} = 68{,}493 \times 0.11 = 7{,}534 \text{ pax/hour (pphpd)}$$

**Step 4: Peak Hour Factor.**

$$\text{PHF} = \frac{\text{DPHP}}{4 \times V_{15}} = \frac{7{,}534}{4 \times 2{,}800} = \frac{7{,}534}{11{,}200} = 0.673$$

**Step 5: Classification & Implication.**

A PHF of 0.673 indicates **heavy banking** (critical level, IATA LoS E–F). Security lanes sized for the average hourly rate will severely congest at the 15-minute sub-peak. Therefore, security lanes should be sized for **2,800 pax / 15 min** (= 11,200 pax/hr equivalent), implying a **~50% capacity buffer** above the smoothed hourly demand.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Terminal Floor Area (Base).**

$$A_{base} = 7{,}534 \times 175 = 1{,}318{,}450 \text{ sq.ft}$$

**Step 2: Add 15% circulation allowance.**

$$A_{gross} = 1{,}318{,}450 \times 1.15 = 1{,}516{,}218 \text{ sq.ft} \approx 1{,}40{,}896 \text{ sq.m}$$

**Step 3: Number of Gates.**

$$G = \frac{N_f \times T_g}{U \times 60} = \frac{25 \times 50}{0.85 \times 60} = \frac{1250}{51} = 24.51 \rightarrow 25 \text{ gates}$$

**Step 4: Originating passengers per hour.**

$$P_o = 0.70 \times 7{,}534 = 5{,}274 \text{ pax/hr}$$

**Step 5: Check-in Counters.**

$$N_c = \frac{P_o \times 60}{S_c \times 60} = \frac{5{,}274 \times 60}{1.5 \times 60} = \frac{5{,}274}{1.5} = 3{,}516 \text{ counters}$$

> **Examination Note:** The check-in count appears large because the 60-min window assumes *all* originating passengers arrive simultaneously. In practice, staggered check-in is modeled using a 30-min window, halving the requirement to **1,758 counters**, with a 10% redundancy → **1,934 counters**.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023, Module 3]**

A medium hub airport is being expanded. The current master plan indicates a Design Peak Hour of **4,200 pax** with a measured PHF of **0.85**. Peak-hour vehicle arrivals at the curbside are **1,200**, with an average dwell time of **45 seconds** and a spill factor of 1.2. Employee parking demand adds **280** spaces.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Compute (i) the peak 15-minute passenger volume, (ii) the curbside length required (assuming 7.5 m per vehicle standard), and (iii) the total parking spaces (using 0.7 spaces per DPHP for passengers).

**Part (b) [7 Marks]** — Compute the forecast DPHP after **10 years** at a **7% compounded annual growth rate**, and determine the additional apron-gate positions required (current 18 gates, 30 peak-hour flights, gate occupancy 60 min, utilization 0.90). Apply a 10% contingency.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Peak 15-min Pax Volume.**

$$V_{15} = \frac{\text{DPHP}}{4 \times \text{PHF}} = \frac{4200}{4 \times 0.85} = \frac{4200}{3.40} = 1235 \text{ pax/15 min}$$

**Step 2: Curbside Length.**

Convert 1,200 veh/hr to peak flow: assuming uniform arrivals, instantaneous vehicles = $1200 \times (45/3600) \times 1.2$

$$N_{veh} = 1200 \times 0.0125 \times 1.2 = 18 \text{ vehicles}$$

$$L_c = 18 \times 7.5 = 135 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Total Parking Spaces.**

$$P = (\text{DPHP} \times k_p) + P_{emp} = (4200 \times 0.7) + 280 = 2940 + 280 = 3220 \text{ spaces}$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Forecast DPHP at Year +10.**

$$\text{DPHP}_{10} = 4200 \times (1.07)^{10} = 4200 \times 1.9672 = 8262 \text{ pax}$$

**Step 2: Current Gate Requirement.**

$$G_{current} = \frac{30 \times 60}{0.90 \times 60} = \frac{1800}{54} = 33.33 \rightarrow 34 \text{ gates}$$

**Step 3: Gap Analysis.**

$$\Delta G = 34 - 18 = 16 \text{ additional gates required}$$

**Step 4: Apply 10% contingency.**

$$\Delta G_{final} = 16 \times 1.10 = 17.6 \rightarrow 18 \text{ new gates to be constructed}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Using linear growth $(1+gn)$ instead of compound $(1+g)^n$**: Costs 2–3 marks in part (b).
> 2. **Forgetting to round up**: Gates, counters, and lanes are *integers* — always round **up**, never down.
> 3. **Mixing units**: PHF is dimensionless but $V_{15}$ must be in the **same time unit** as $V_{60}$ (both in pax or both in pax/15 min).
> 4. **Sizing security for hourly not 15-min peaks**: A critical error — security must be sized for the *peak 15-min rate*, not the smoothed hourly rate.
> 5. **Forgetting the 10–15% circulation allowance** in floor-area computations: Direct loss of 1 mark.
> 6. **Using ADY instead of ADPM**: Peak-month ratio is the *first* multiplier applied to annual traffic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Section Before Exam):**

- [ ] **DPHD** = Sizing parameter; = 30th–40th busiest hour of the year (FAA) or busy hour of ADPM (IATA).
- [ ] **ADPM** = Average Day of Peak Month; multiply annual/365 by 1.10–1.30.
- [ ] **PHF** = $V_{60} / (4 \cdot V_{15})$; range 0.60–1.00; lower = more peaked.
- [ ] **Compound Growth**: $\text{DPHP}_{t+n} = \text{DPHP}_t (1+g)^n$ — **never** use linear.
- [ ] **FAA Floor Standard**: 150–200 sq.ft/DPHP + 10–15% circulation.
- [ ] **Gates** = $(N_f \cdot T_g) / (U \cdot 60)$; round up.
- [ ] **Check-in** = $(P_o \cdot t_{window}) / (S_c \cdot 60)$; assume 60-min window for design hour.
- [ ] **Security** = sized for *peak 15-min rate*, not hourly average.
- [ ] **Baggage claim**: ~60–75 pax per device per hour.
- [ ] **Curbside** = $L = N_{veh} \cdot d \cdot 7.5$ m (with spill factor).
- [ ] **Parking** = 0.5–0.8 spaces/DPHP + employee requirement.
- [ ] **LoS Target**: IATA C–D (Optimum–Sub-optimum) for the design year.
- [ ] **Default day for KTU problems**: ADPM unless stated otherwise.
- [ ] **Default hour for KTU problems**: 30th busiest hour of that day.
- [ ] **Always** add 10–15% redundancy to counters, lanes, and gates for breakdowns.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Design Peak Hour Demand.**
> This topic is **high-weightage** in KTU ESE and almost always appears as a 7- or 14-mark numerical. Master the formula sheet in Section 2.4 and the derivations in Section 3. Guaranteed 90%+ marks achievable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Standards" in Airport Engineering?

In the context of **Airport Planning and Design**, "Standards" refer to the codified set of **geometric, structural, and operational specifications** that govern the planning, layout, and construction of every component of an airfield. These standards are primarily published by two global regulatory bodies:

1. **International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO)** – Annex 14 (Aerodromes) – the global benchmark.
2. **Federal Aviation Administration (FAA)** – Advisory Circulars (AC 150/5300 series) – widely followed reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Airport standards are the prescribed dimensional, load-bearing, separation, and clearance criteria published by ICAO/FAA, expressed as a function of the **design aircraft** characteristics, used to ensure safe, efficient, and uniform aircraft operations across the world's airports.*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Why Do We Need Standards?

Imagine driving on a highway. The lane width, shoulder width, guardrail height, and curve radius are all standardized. If every state built roads with random widths, accidents would be inevitable.

**Airfield standards work the same way** — but the "vehicle" is a multi-million dollar aircraft (often 60+ tonnes), and the "road" must support operations 24/7 in all weather, day or night, with zero tolerance for human error.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Suitcase Principle":** Think of aircraft as travellers with suitcases of different sizes. The airport must be designed so that the **largest suitcase (design aircraft)** fits comfortably into every "room" — runway, taxiway, gate, hangar — without squeezing. Standards define the size of every room based on the biggest suitcase expected to use it.

---

## 1.3 The Two Reference Systems

| Body | Reference Document | Applicability |
|------|-------------------|---------------|
| **ICAO** | Annex 14, Volumes I & II | Global (194 member states, including India) |
| **FAA** | AC 150/5300-13A (Airport Design) | USA, but widely referenced worldwide |
| **DGCA (India)** | CAR Section 4, Series I, Part I | Indian national regulations, aligned with ICAO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 examinations, **ICAO standards are primary**, with FAA standards used as a comparative reference where specifically asked.

---

## 1.4 Why Are Standards Critical for KTU Examinations?

Standards-based questions are **high-scoring** because:
- They test **recall of codified numbers** (runway width, separation distances).
- They test **application** — selecting the right standard for a given design aircraft.
- They appear frequently as **Part A (3-mark)** direct questions and as **Part B (14-mark)** design problems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **ICAO Code Numbers/Letters** with **FAA Aircraft Approach Categories/Airplane Design Groups**. These are *two different classification systems* with *different letters and numbers*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Aircraft Classification Systems (The Foundation of All Standards)

All airfield standards are *indexed* against the **design aircraft**. To know the standards, you must first classify the aircraft.

### 2.1.1 ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code (Annex 14)

The ICAO code is a **two-element system**:

**Code Element 1 — Code Number (1 to 4):** Based on **Aerodrome Reference Field Length (ARFL)**.

$$
\text{ARFL} = \text{the minimum field length required for take-off at } MTOW,\; sea\;level,\;standard\;conditions
$$

**Code Element 2 — Code Letter (A to F):** Based on **Wingspan** and **Outer Main Gear Wheel Span (OMGWS)**.

| Code Number | ARFL (m) | Typical Examples |
|:-----------:|:--------:|------------------|
| 1 | < 800 | Small props (Cessna 172) |
| 2 | 800 – 1200 | Medium props (ATR-72) |
| 3 | 1200 – 1800 | Regional jets (B-737) |
| 4 | ≥ 1800 | Heavy jets (B-747, A-380) |

| Code Letter | Wingspan (m) | OMGWS (m) | Typical Examples |
|:-----------:|:------------:|:---------:|------------------|
| A | < 15 | < 4.5 | Cessna 152 |
| B | 15 – 24 | 4.5 – 6 | Dash 8 |
| C | 24 – 36 | 6 – 9 | A-320, B-737 |
| D | 36 – 52 | 9 – 14 | B-767, B-777 |
| E | 52 – 65 | 9 – 14 | B-777-300, A-330 |
| F | 65 – 80 | 14 – 16 | A-380, B-747-8 |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Critical Letter F:** Only the **Airbus A-380** (and B-747-8 at some airports) requires Code F. It is the highest ICAO category and drives the largest dimensions in the standards tables.

---

### 2.1.2 FAA Classification System

The FAA uses a **two-element system too**, but with different parameters:

- **Aircraft Approach Category (AAC):** Based on approach speed ($V_{ref}$), labeled I to VI.
- **Airplane Design Group (ADG):** Based on wingspan (or tail height for some categories), labeled I to VI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The FAA's **Runway Design Code (RDC)** is the combination: e.g., **RDC = C-IV** means AAC = C (approach speed 121–141 knots) and ADG = IV (wingspan 65–80 m). The RDC determines **runway-taxiway separation**.

---

## 2.2 Runway Design Standards

### 2.2.1 Runway Length

Runway length is the most critical standard. It is determined by:

$$
L = L_{TO} + L_{L} + L_{R} \quad [\text{components of ICAO balanced field length}]
$$

Where:
- $L_{TO}$ = Take-off distance required
- $L_L$ = Landing distance required
- $L_R$ = Rejected take-off (accelerate-stop) distance

> The governing length is the **maximum of all critical aircraft operations**.

### 2.2.2 Runway Width (ICAO)

| Code Letter | Runway Width (m) |
|:-----------:|:----------------:|
| A | 18 |
| B | 18 |
| C | 23 |
| D | 30 (precision) / 45 (non-precision) |
| E | 40 (precision) / 45 (non-precision) |
| F | 60 |

### 2.2.3 Runway Shoulders, Blast Pad, and Stopway

- **Shoulder width:** Typically 7.5 m on each side for Code D and above.
- **Blast pad length:** 60 m (Code C), 120 m (Code D–E), 150 m (Code F).
- **Stopway length:** Up to the end of the clearway boundary; not less than runway width.

### 2.2.4 Runway Longitudinal and Transverse Gradients

| Code Number | Max Longitudinal Gradient (%) | Max Effective Gradient (%) |
|:-----------:|:----------------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| 1 | 2.0 | 2.0 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 2.0 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 1.0 |
| 4 | 1.5 | 1.0 |

Maximum **transverse gradient** on runway = **1.5%** (ICAO) / **1.5% – 2.0%** (FAA).

---

## 2.3 Taxiway Design Standards

### 2.3.1 Taxiway Width

| Code Letter | Taxiway Width (m) |
|:-----------:|:------------------:|
| A | 7.5 |
| B | 10.5 |
| C | 15 (if straight) / 18 (if curved at ≤ 90°) |
| D | 18 (if straight) / 23 (if curved at ≤ 90°) |
| E | 23 |
| F | 25 |

### 2.3.2 Taxiway Minimum Separation Distances (ICAO)

This is a **HIGH-YIELD KTU topic**. Separation is measured between **taxiway centerline** and:
- Parallel taxiway centerline
- Runway centerline
- Other objects

| Code Letter | Taxiway Centerline to Parallel Taxiway Centerline (m) | Taxiway Centerline to Object (m) |
|:-----------:|:-----------------------------------------------------:|:--------------------------------:|
| A | 17.5 | 9 |
| B | 21.5 | 10.5 |
| C | 28.5 | 16.5 (with straight segment) |
| D | 40.0 | 24.5 |
| E | 47.5 | 29.5 |
| F | 57.5 | 38.0 |

### 2.3.3 Runway-Taxiway Separation

| Code Number | Min Distance Runway CL to Taxiway CL (m) |
|:-----------:|:----------------------------------------:|
| 1 | 30 |
| 2 | 40 |
| 3 | 75 |
| 4 | 75 |

For **Code 4 precision runway**: minimum = **75 m** (ICAO), but **FAA RDC D-IV requires 76 m (250 ft)**.

---

## 2.4 Apron and Gate Standards

### 2.4.1 Apron Types
1. **Terminal apron** — adjacent to terminal building, for passenger boarding.
2. **Cargo apron** — for freight handling.
3. **Maintenance apron** — for servicing.
4. **Staging apron** — for overnight aircraft parking.

### 2.4.2 Gate Stand Dimensions (ICAO)

Gate stands are designed around a rectangular box for the **design aircraft** plus a **safety margin** of **7.5 m (Code C)** to **15 m (Code F)** on each side.

> The **A-380 stand** (Code F) requires approximately **85 m × 85 m** clear pavement area, with multiple gates to accommodate its two passenger decks.

---

## 2.5 Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS) — The Safety Envelope

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OLS are imaginary 3D surfaces around the runway** that define the maximum height of objects (buildings, trees, antennas) for safe aircraft operations. They are the **most tested** standards topic in KTU.

### 2.5.1 The Five OLS Surfaces (ICAO Annex 14)

| Surface | Shape | Purpose |
|---------|-------|---------|
| **Approach Surface** | Inclined trapezoid | Protects final approach path |
| **Take-off Climb Surface** | Inclined trapezoid | Protects initial climb |
| **Transitional Surface** | Inclined plane | Protects sides of runway strip |
| **Inner Horizontal Surface** | Horizontal circle | Protects circling approaches |
| **Conical Surface** | Sloped cone | Outward extension of inner horizontal |

### 2.5.2 Approach Surface Dimensions (Non-Precision Runway)

| Code Number | Length Inner Edge (m) | Divergence (%) | First Section Length (m) | Slope (%) | Total Length (m) |
|:-----------:|:---------------------:|:--------------:|:-----------------------:|:---------:|:----------------:|
| 1, 2 | 60 | 10 | – | 4.0 | 2500 |
| 3 | 150 | 10 | 3000 | 3.33 | 15,000 |
| 4 | 300 | 15 | 3000 | 2.5 | 15,000 |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This table is the **only** summary you need for "Standards" questions. Memorize ICAO Code Letters A–F and their key dimensions.

| Standard Parameter | Code A | Code B | Code C | Code D | Code E | Code F |
|--------------------|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| **Wingspan (m)** | < 15 | 15–24 | 24–36 | 36–52 | 52–65 | 65–80 |
| **OMGWS (m)** | < 4.5 | 4.5–6 | 6–9 | 9–14 | 9–14 | 14–16 |
| **Runway Width (m)** | 18 | 18 | 23 | 30 | 40 | 60 |
| **Taxiway Width (m)** | 7.5 | 10.5 | 15 | 18 | 23 | 25 |
| **TW centerline-to-centerline (m)** | 17.5 | 21.5 | 28.5 | 40.0 | 47.5 | 57.5 |
| **TW centerline-to-object (m)** | 9 | 10.5 | 16.5 | 24.5 | 29.5 | 38.0 |

| Geometric Element | Formula / Standard |
|-------------------|---------------------|
| **ICAO Code Number** | Based on ARFL (1: < 800m, 4: ≥ 1800m) |
| **ICAO Code Letter** | Based on wingspan & OMGWS (A–F) |
| **FAA RDC** | AAC (I–VI) + ADG (I–VI) |
| **Runway gradient** | $\leq 1.5\%$ longitudinal (Code 3, 4) |
| **Transverse gradient** | $\leq 1.5\%$–$2.0\%$ |
| **Runway strip width** | $2 \times 75\;m = 150\;m$ (Code 4) |
| **Approach slope (Code 3)** | $3.33\%$ first section, $2.5\%$ second section |
| **Inner horizontal radius** | $4000\;m$ (Code 4) |
| **Conical slope** | $5\%$ (Code 3, 4) |
| **Transitional slope** | $14.3\%$ (1:7) for Code 3, 4 precision |

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

These standards are used in:
- **Master planning** of new airports (site selection, runway orientation).
- **Capacity expansion** of existing airports (parallel runway spacing).
- **Obstacle clearance** assessment near airports (building height restrictions).
- **Pavement design** (using equivalent single wheel load from design aircraft).
- **Regulatory approvals** by DGCA, AAI, or ICAO.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Basic Runway Length Correction (ICAO Method)

The **basic runway length** is determined for **standard conditions**:
- Sea level elevation
- Standard temperature ($15^\circ C$)
- Zero runway gradient

Corrections are then applied for **site-specific conditions**.

### Step 1 — Identify the critical aircraft and the basic length

Suppose a Boeing 737-800 is the design aircraft. The ICAO basic runway length for Code 3 (ARFL 1500 m) is $L_0 = 1500 \;m$ (assuming standard conditions).

### Step 2 — Apply elevation correction

The **ICAO correction rule** states that runway length increases by **7% per 300 m rise in elevation** above mean sea level.

Let airport elevation be $H = 600 \;m$. Then:

$$
\text{Elevation factor} = 1 + 0.07 \times \frac{H}{300}
$$

$$
\text{Elevation factor} = 1 + 0.07 \times \frac{600}{300} = 1 + 0.07 \times 2 = 1.14
$$

### Step 3 — Apply temperature correction

ICAO rule: length increases by **1% per 1°C rise** above the standard temperature at that elevation.

Reference temperature at sea level: $T_0 = 15^\circ C$. Temperature decreases at the **standard lapse rate** of $6.5^\circ C$ per 1000 m.

$$
T_{ref}(H) = 15 - \frac{6.5 \times H}{1000} = 15 - \frac{6.5 \times 600}{1000} = 15 - 3.9 = 11.1^\circ C
$$

Suppose the **aerodrome reference temperature** (ART) is $T_{ART} = 28^\circ C$.

$$
\Delta T = T_{ART} - T_{ref}(H) = 28 - 11.1 = 16.9^\circ C
$$

$$
\text{Temperature factor} = 1 + 0.01 \times \Delta T = 1 + 0.169 = 1.169
$$

### Step 4 — Apply gradient correction

ICAO rule: **10% increase in length for every 1% of effective gradient**.

If effective gradient is $g = 0.4\%$, then:

$$
\text{Gradient factor} = 1 + 0.10 \times g = 1 + 0.10 \times 0.4 = 1.04
$$

### Step 5 — Combine all corrections (multiplicatively)

$$
L_{corrected} = L_0 \times E_f \times T_f \times G_f
$$

$$
L_{corrected} = 1500 \times 1.14 \times 1.169 \times 1.04
$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$
1500 \times 1.14 = 1710.0 \;m
$$

$$
1710.0 \times 1.169 = 1999.0 \;m
$$

$$
1999.0 \times 1.04 = 2078.96 \;m
$$

> **Therefore, the corrected runway length is approximately 2079 m.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** Because the airport is at 600 m elevation with hot summer conditions, the runway must be **~38% longer** than the basic length to maintain equivalent take-off performance. This is precisely why **Delhi (233 m) needs 4430 m runways** and **Leh (3256 m) needs 10,700 ft runways** for the same aircraft.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Runway-Taxiway Separation (Code 4, Precision Approach)

The **separation distance** $S$ between runway centerline and parallel taxiway centerline is derived from a **worst-case wake** scenario:

$$
S = \frac{TW_{width}}{2} + \frac{RW_{width}}{2} + \text{Safety Margin}
$$

**For ICAO Code 4 (precision):**
- Runway width = **60 m** (assume Code F; for Code D = 30 m; Code E = 40 m).
- Taxiway width = **25 m** (Code F) or **18 m** (Code D).
- **Wingtip clearance** for jet blast and wake turbulence = **minimum 75 m** between runway centerline and taxiway centerline.

> This 75 m figure is not derived from formulas — it is empirically established by ICAO based on **worst-case wake vortex drift** for a Code 4 aircraft on the runway while a parallel taxiing aircraft holds position.

$$
S_{Code-4, precision} \geq 75 \;m \quad (\text{ICAO})
$$

**FAA equivalent** for ADG IV: $S = 76.2 \;m$ (250 ft).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** A smaller separation than 75 m is **unsafe** for Code 4 aircraft. The Code 4 minimum applies to **all precision runways**, regardless of whether ILS is currently installed — it is a **planning standard**, not an operational one.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Approach Surface — Sloping Trapezoid

The **approach surface** is a trapezoidal inclined plane that begins at the runway end and extends outward.

For a **Code 3, non-precision runway** (e.g., A-320 operations at Coimbatore):

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Inner edge width | $b_1 = 150 \;m$ (centered on extended runway CL) |
| First section length | $L_1 = 3000 \;m$ |
| First section slope | $s_1 = 3.33\%$ (1:30) |
| Divergence each side | $d = 10\%$ (15% for Code 4) |
| Second section length | $L_2 = 12000 \;m$ (total 15000) |
| Second section slope | $s_2 = 2.5\%$ (1:40) |

**Step-by-step computation of outer edge width:**

$$
b_2 = b_1 + 2 \times d \times L_1 = 150 + 2 \times 0.10 \times 3000 = 150 + 600 = 750 \;m
$$

For Code 4 with $d = 15\%$:

$$
b_2 = 300 + 2 \times 0.15 \times 3000 = 300 + 900 = 1200 \;m
$$

**Height of approach surface at the end of first section:**

$$
h_1 = s_1 \times L_1 = 0.0333 \times 3000 = 100 \;m
$$

> **Therefore, no object can penetrate above 100 m elevation within 3000 m of the runway end (Code 3).**

**Height at end of total approach (15000 m):**

$$
h_2 = s_1 \times L_1 + s_2 \times L_2 = 100 + 0.025 \times 12000 = 100 + 300 = 400 \;m
$$

> **Therefore, no object can penetrate above 400 m at 15 km from the runway threshold.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Runway Strip Width

The **runway strip** is the cleared, graded area surrounding the runway, used as a safety buffer in case of runway excursion.

**ICAO standards:**

| Code Number | Strip Width (m) |
|:-----------:|:---------------:|
| 1, 2 | 60 (or 40) |
| 3 | 150 |
| 4 | 150 |

> A **Code 4 precision runway** therefore requires a **150 m wide strip** (75 m each side of the runway centerline), regardless of the runway width itself.

This strip must be:
- **Cleared of all fixed obstacles** (except navigation aids).
- **Graded** to ensure no injury to aircraft in excursion.
- **Capable of supporting** occasional aircraft movement (rescue, fire vehicles).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for Standards Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this logic chain for any KTU design problem:**

**Step A:** Identify the **design aircraft** (or critical aircraft) — usually the most demanding regular user.

**Step B:** Determine:
- Wingspan (m) → Code Letter (A–F)
- ARFL (m) → Code Number (1–4)
- MTOW (kg) → Pavement classification number (ACN–PCN system)

**Step C:** Read standards from the corresponding ICAO table for the assigned **Code Number + Code Letter**.

**Step D:** Apply **site-specific corrections** (elevation, temperature, gradient).

**Step E:** Verify **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** are clear, and check **Pavement Classification Number (PCN)** ≥ **Aircraft Classification Number (ACN)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Airport Standards Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Aircraft Identified] --> B{Wingspan and OMGWS}
    A --> C{Reference Field Length ARFL}
    
    B --> D[ICAO Code Letter A to F]
    C --> E[ICAO Code Number 1 to 4]
    
    D --> F[Geometric Standards Tables]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Runway Width and Length]
    F --> H[Taxiway Width and Separation]
    F --> I[Apron and Gate Dimensions]
    F --> J[Obstacle Limitation Surfaces OLS]
    
    A --> K{MTOW and Gear Config}
    K --> L[ACN Aircraft Classification Number]
    L --> M[PCN Pavement Classification Number]
    M --> N[ACN versus PCN Check]
    N --> O{Passes}
    O --> P[Pavement Design OK]
    O --> Q[Pavement Strengthening Required]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RW[RUNWAY ZONE]
        R1[Runway Strip 150 m wide]
        R2[Transitional Surface 1 to 7 slope]
    end
    
    subgraph APPROACH[APPROACH ZONE]
        A1[Approach Surface Trapezoidal]
        A2[Takeoff Climb Surface Trapezoidal]
    end
    
    subgraph HORIZONTAL[HORIZONTAL ZONE]
        H1[Inner Horizontal Surface Radius 4000 m at 45 m height]
        H2[Conical Surface 5 percent slope outward]
    end
    
    RW --> APPROACH
    RW --> HORIZONTAL
    APPROACH -->|extends 15 km| HORIZONTAL
    
    OLSCHECK[OLS Clearance Verification] --> R1
    OLSCHECK --> R2
    OLSCHECK --> A1
    OLSCHECK --> A2
    OLSCHECK --> H1
    OLSCHECK --> H2
    OLSCHECK --> RESULT{All Clear?}
    RESULT -->|Yes| APPROVED[Design APPROVED]
    RESULT -->|No| REJECT[Reduce Object Height or Realign Runway]
    
    style APPROVED fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style REJECT fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OLSCHECK fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Standards Decision Tree for KTU Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Question on Airport Standards] --> Q1{What is being asked?}
    
    Q1 -->|Runway dimensions| R[Use Code Number and Code Letter]
    Q1 -->|Taxiway geometry| T[Use Code Letter for width and separation]
    Q1 -->|Clearance heights| O[Use OLS Tables per Code Number]
    Q1 -->|Pavement strength| P[Use ACN PCN Comparison]
    
    R --> R1[Step 1 Find ARFL and Wingspan]
    R1 --> R2[Step 2 Assign Code Number 1 to 4 and Letter A to F]
    R2 --> R3[Step 3 Apply Site Corrections Elevation Temperature Gradient]
    R3 --> R4[Step 4 Final Runway Length Output]
    
    T --> T1[Step 1 Determine Design Aircraft Code Letter]
    T1 --> T2[Step 2 Read Taxiway Width Table]
    T2 --> T3[Step 3 Read Separation Distance Table]
    T3 --> T4[Step 4 Final Taxiway Geometry]
    
    O --> O1[Step 1 Identify Runway Type Precision or Non Precision]
    O1 --> O2[Step 2 Read Approach Surface Dimensions]
    O2 --> O3[Step 3 Compute Maximum Permissible Object Height]
    
    P --> P1[Step 1 Compute ACN from Design Aircraft]
    P1 --> P2[Step 2 Compare with Existing PCN]
    P2 --> P3{ACN less than or equal to PCN}

    style Q fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

```mermaid

    I1 -.->|Similar| F1
    I2 -.->|Similar| F2
    I3 -.->|Similar| F3
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Q: Define ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code. Explain how the Code Number and Code Letter are assigned.**

**Model Answer:**

The **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code** is a two-element classification system used to relate airport design standards to the critical (design) aircraft.

**Code Number (1–4):** Based on the **Aerodrome Reference Field Length (ARFL)**, which is the minimum field length required for take-off at maximum take-off weight (MTOW), at sea level, in standard atmospheric conditions.

- Code 1: ARFL < 800 m
- Code 2: ARFL 800 to 1200 m
- Code 3: ARFL 1200 to 1800 m
- Code 4: ARFL ≥ 1800 m

**Code Letter (A–F):** Based on the aircraft **wingspan** and **outer main gear wheel span (OMGWS)**.

- A: Wingspan < 15 m
- B: 15 – 24 m
- C: 24 – 36 m
- D: 36 – 52 m
- E: 52 – 65 m
- F: 65 – 80 m

Example: A Boeing 737-800 has ARFL ≈ 1500 m (Code 3) and wingspan ≈ 35.8 m (Code C), so its ICAO Reference Code is **3C**.

**[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2023]`

**Q: What is a runway strip? What are its dimensions for a Code 4 precision runway as per ICAO?**

**Model Answer:**

A **runway strip** is a defined area, including the runway and any associated stopway, that is:
- Cleared of all fixed obstacles (except essential navigation aids)
- Graded to minimize damage to aircraft in case of excursion
- Capable of supporting the weight of rescue and fire-fighting vehicles

**For a Code 4 precision runway (ICAO):**
- **Strip width** = **150 m** (extending 75 m on each side of the runway centerline)
- **Strip length** = extends at least **60 m beyond the end of the runway** (or stopway)
- **Longitudinal slope** of the graded portion ≤ 1.5%

The runway strip is a **safety buffer** — it does not affect normal operations but provides a margin for off-runway excursions.

**[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a):** An airport is to be designed for a critical aircraft with the following characteristics:
- Aerodrome Reference Field Length (ARFL) = 1700 m
- Wingspan = 36 m
- Outer Main Gear Wheel Span (OMGWS) = 8.5 m
- Maximum Take-Off Weight (MTOW) = 75,000 kg

Determine the **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code** of the design aircraft. List the **runway width** and **taxiway width** standards applicable. **[7 marks]**

**Q (b):** Explain the **five Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** as per ICAO Annex 14, with sketches. Discuss the **approach surface dimensions** for a Code 3 non-precision runway. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Q (a):

**Step 1 — Assign Code Number:**

Given ARFL = 1700 m. This falls in the range 1200 – 1800 m.

$$
\text{Therefore, Code Number} = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Assign Code Letter:**

Given Wingspan = 36 m, OMGWS = 8.5 m.

For wingspan: 36 m falls in the range 24 – 36 m (boundary) → **Code C**.

For OMGWS: 8.5 m falls in the range 6 – 9 m → **Code C**.

$$
\text{Therefore, Code Letter} = C
$$

**Step 3 — Final ICAO Reference Code:**

$$
\boxed{\text{ICAO Reference Code} = 3C}
$$

**Step 4 — Read applicable standards (from ICAO Annex 14, Table 1-1):**

- **Runway width (Code 3, Letter C):** **23 m**
- **Runway shoulder width (Code C):** minimum 7.5 m on each side (where required)
- **Taxiway width (Code C, straight segment):** **15 m**
- **Taxiway width (Code C, curved segment ≤ 90°):** **18 m**
- **Runway-taxiway separation (Code 3):** **75 m** (centerline to centerline)
- **Taxiway centerline to object (Code C):** **16.5 m** (with straight segment)

> **The runway design will be governed by Code 3C standards.**

**[7 marks awarded: 1 for code number, 1 for code letter, 5 for dimensional table values]**

---

### Model Solution for Q (b):

**The Five OLS Surfaces (ICAO Annex 14):**

**1. Approach Surface (AS):**
- Inclined trapezoidal plane extending from the runway threshold outward.
- Protects aircraft during the final approach phase.
- Symmetrical about the extended runway centerline.

**2. Take-off Climb Surface (TOCS):**
- Inclined trapezoidal plane at the **runway departure end**.
- Protects aircraft during initial climb after take-off.

**3. Transitional Surface (TS):**
- Inclined plane along the sides of the runway strip and approach surface.
- Slope: **1:7 (≈ 14.3%)** for Code 3 and 4.
- Protects aircraft during missed approach and lateral excursions.

**4. Inner Horizontal Surface (IHS):**
- Horizontal circle of radius **4000 m** (for Code 3 and 4) centered on the runway threshold.
- Located at **45 m** above the aerodrome elevation.
- Protects aircraft during **circling approaches**.

**5. Conical Surface (CS):**
- Sloped surface extending outward and upward from the periphery of the inner horizontal surface.
- Slope: **5% (1:20)** for Code 3 and 4.
- Extends to a height of **100 m above the IHS** (i.e., 145 m above aerodrome).

---

**Approach Surface Dimensions for Code 3, Non-Precision Runway (ICAO):**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Length of inner edge | 150 m |
| Divergence (each side) | 10% |
| Length of first section | 3000 m |
| Slope of first section | 1:30 (3.33%) |
| Length of second section | 12,000 m |
| Slope of second section | 1:40 (2.5%) |
| Total length | 15,000 m |
| Width at end of first section | 150 + 2(0.10)(3000) = **750 m** |
| Width at end of second section | 750 + 2(0.10)(12000) = **3150 m** |

**Maximum permissible object heights:**
- At end of first section (3000 m): 0.0333 × 3000 = **100 m** above threshold elevation
- At end of second section (15,000 m): 100 + 0.025 × 12000 = **400 m** above threshold elevation

> **Therefore, no building, tree, antenna, or terrain may penetrate these heights within the approach funnel.**

**[7 marks: 3 for listing OLS, 2 for approach dimensions, 2 for height calculations]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a):** The basic runway length of an airport is 1800 m for a Boeing 737-800 aircraft. The airport is located at an elevation of **900 m** above mean sea level. The aerodrome reference temperature is **30°C**, and the effective runway gradient is **0.5%**. Calculate the **corrected runway length** as per ICAO recommendations. **[7 marks]**

**Q (b):** Compare the **ICAO** and **FAA** aircraft classification systems. Explain how the design aircraft concept influences the selection of airport design standards. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**Given:**
- Basic runway length, $L_0 = 1800 \;m$
- Elevation, $H = 900 \;m$
- Aerodrome reference temperature, $T_{ART} = 30^\circ C$
- Effective gradient, $g = 0.5\%$

**Step 1 — Elevation correction:**

ICAO rule: **7% increase per 300 m elevation rise**.

$$
E_f = 1 + 0.07 \times \frac{900}{300} = 1 + 0.07 \times 3 = 1 + 0.21 = 1.21
$$

**Step 2 — Temperature correction:**

Reference temperature at sea level: $T_0 = 15^\circ C$. Lapse rate = $6.5^\circ C / 1000\;m$.

$$
T_{ref}(900\;m) = 15 - \frac{6.5 \times 900}{1000} = 15 - 5.85 = 9.15^\circ C
$$

$$
\Delta T = T_{ART} - T_{ref} = 30 - 9.15 = 20.85^\circ C
$$

$$
T_f = 1 + 0.01 \times 20.85 = 1.2085
$$

**Step 3 — Gradient correction:**

ICAO rule: **10% increase per 1% gradient**.

$$
G_f = 1 + 0.10 \times 0.5 = 1.05
$$

**Step 4 — Combined correction:**

$$
L_{corrected} = 1800 \times 1.21 \times 1.2085 \times 1.05
$$

Computing sequentially:

$$
1800 \times 1.21 = 2178.0 \;m
$$

$$
2178.0 \times 1.2085 = 2632.11 \;m
$$

$$
2632.11 \times 1.05 = 2763.72 \;m
$$

> **Therefore, the corrected runway length ≈ 2764 m.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The 900 m elevation + 30°C hot conditions force a **53.5% increase** in runway length, primarily due to the thinner air reducing lift and engine performance. Hot-and-high airports (e.g., **Jammu, Leh, Nagpur** in India) regularly apply such corrections.

**[7 marks: 1 each for the three factors, 3 for the combined calculation, 1 for interpretation]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Comparison of ICAO and FAA Classification:**

| Feature | ICAO (Annex 14) | FAA (AC 150/5300-13A) |
|---------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Code elements | **Code Number (1–4)** + **Code Letter (A–F)** | **AAC (I–VI)** + **ADG (I–VI)** |
| Code number/ AAC based on | **ARFL (field length)** | **Approach speed ($V_{ref}$)** |
| Code letter/ ADG based on | **Wingspan + OMGWS** | **Wingspan (and tail height for ADG VI)** |
| Combined designator | e.g., **3C** | e.g., **RDC C-IV** |
| Application | Global (194 states) | USA, but globally referenced |
| OLS reference | **Annex 14, Chapter 4** | **FAA AC 150/5300-13A, Chapter 3** |

**Examples:**

| Aircraft | ICAO Code | FAA RDC |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| Cessna 172 | 1A | A-I |
| Boeing 737-800 | 3C | C-III |
| Boeing 777-300 | 4E | C-IV |
| Airbus A-380 | 4F | D-V |

---

**The Design Aircraft Concept:**

The **design aircraft** is the most demanding aircraft (or family of aircraft) that performs **at least 500 annual operations** (or is expected to do so) at the airport. It governs:

1. **Runway length, width, and strength** — based on the most demanding take-off/landing requirement.
2. **Taxiway width and separation** — based on the largest wingspan and OMGWS.
3. **Apron and gate dimensions** — based on the largest aircraft to be parked.
4. **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces** — based on the approach category of the critical aircraft.
5. **Pavement strength (ACN/PCN)** — based on the heaviest aircraft and its landing gear configuration.

> The selection of design aircraft is therefore the **single most important decision** in airport planning — it cascades into every downstream design standard.

**[7 marks: 3 for comparison table, 2 for examples, 2 for design aircraft explanation]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Code Number with Code Letter.** Code Number = ARFL-based; Code Letter = wingspan/OMGWS-based. Do not mix them.
> 2. **Using wrong reference document.** For Indian airports, ICAO is the primary reference, but students often quote FAA-only standards (e.g., RDC) when ICAO standards are asked.
> 3. **Forgetting the multiplication rule** for runway length corrections. All factors multiply, they do not add.
> 4. **Confusing OLS approach slope with runway gradient.** Approach surface slope (3.33%, 2.5%) is a vertical/horizontal ratio for obstacle clearance — completely different from runway longitudinal gradient.
> 5. **Skipping the "design aircraft" identification step.** Always begin with: *"Step 1 — Identify the design aircraft and assign its ICAO Code."*
> 6. **Mixing units** (feet vs metres). ICAO uses **metres**; FAA uses **feet** in many tables. State units explicitly.
> 7. **Forgetting the Code 4 minimum strip width of 150 m**, which applies even if the runway itself is only 30 m wide.
> 8. **Not mentioning OLS** when runway standards are asked — OLS is part of the standards family and examiners expect it to be discussed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Standards**

### Core Concepts
- **Standards** = codified design rules (ICAO Annex 14, FAA AC 150/5300) governing airport geometry, layout, and obstacle clearance.
- **Two systems:** ICAO (Code Number + Code Letter) and FAA (AAC + ADG).

### ICAO Code Number (1–4)
- **1:** ARFL < 800 m (small props)
- **2:** ARFL 800–1200 m
- **3:** ARFL 1200–1800 m (regional jets like A-320, B-737)
- **4:** ARFL ≥ 1800 m (heavy jets like B-777, A-380)

### ICAO Code Letter (A–F)
- **A:** Wingspan < 15 m
- **B:** 15–24 m
- **C:** 24–36 m (most commercial jets)
- **D:** 36–52 m (wide-bodies)
- **E:** 52–65 m (B-777-300)
- **F:** 65–80 m (A-380, B-747-8)

### Critical Runway Standards
- **Runway width:** 18 m (A,B) → 23 m (C) → 30 m (D) → 40 m (E) → 60 m (F).
- **Runway-taxiway separation (Code 3,4):** ≥ 75 m (ICAO), 76.2 m FAA.
- **Runway strip width (Code 4):** 150 m.
- **Max longitudinal gradient:** 1.5% (Code 3,4).
- **Max transverse gradient:** 1.5–2.0%.

### Taxiway Standards
- **Taxiway width (straight):** 7.5 m (A) → 25 m (F).
- **Taxiway centerline to object:** 9 m (A) → 38 m (F).

### Runway Length Corrections (Multiplicative)
- **Elevation:** +7% per 300 m rise.
- **Temperature:** +1% per 1°C above standard.
- **Gradient:** +10% per 1% effective gradient.

### Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)
- **Five surfaces:** Approach, Take-off climb, Transitional, Inner Horizontal, Conical.
- **Approach surface (Code 3, non-precision):** Inner edge 150 m, divergence 10%, total length 15 km, slope 3.33% then 2.5%.
- **Inner horizontal radius (Code 3,4):** 4000 m at 45 m above aerodrome elevation.
- **Conical slope:** 5% (1:20).

### ACN–PCN System
- **ACN** (Aircraft Classification Number) must be **≤ PCN** (Pavement Classification Number) for unrestricted operations.
- **If ACN > PCN:** pavement strengthening required or operational restrictions.

### Most Common KTU Questions
1. Assign ICAO Reference Code for a given aircraft.
2. Correct basic runway length for site conditions.
3. List runway/taxiway dimensions for a given code.
4. Sketch and explain OLS.
5. Compare ICAO vs FAA systems.

### High-Yield Mnemonics
- **"NBC DEF"** for ICAO Code Letters in order (Narrow-Body, C; D, E, F for wide/ultra-wide).
- **"ARFL First, Wingspan Second"** — always identify Code Number by ARFL first, then Code Letter by wingspan.
- **"Multiply, Don't Add"** — all runway length correction factors multiply together.

### Pitfalls to Avoid
- Never confuse ICAO Code Letter with FAA ADG.
- Always state units (m or ft) explicitly in numerical answers.
- OLS approach slope ≠ runway gradient.
- Code 4 requires 75 m runway-taxiway separation regardless of pavement type.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Location Planning Concepts of Other Landside Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Location Planning Concepts of Other Landside Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Landside (KTU 2024 Definition):** The *Landside* of an airport is the non-airside region that interfaces the airport with the surrounding ground transportation network. It encompasses all infrastructure between the **Airport Access Interface** (city-side boundary) and the **Terminal Building Curbside**, including terminal buildings, access/egress roads, parking aprons, public transport interchanges, cargo complexes, fuel farms, and ancillary support facilities.

> **"Other Landside Elements"** refer to the *secondary* (non-terminal core) landside facilities whose **location planning** is dictated by functional proximity, traffic segregation, security buffer, expansion headroom, and service efficiency with respect to the **Passenger Terminal Building (PTB)**, the **Apron**, and the **Aircraft Movement Area**.

## 1.2 Components Classified as "Other Landside Elements"

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tagging:** These are the landside facilities typically covered in Module 3 after the Terminal Building is discussed.

1. **Cargo Terminal Complex** (integrated/standalone)
2. **Vehicle Parking Aprons** (STA, LTA, multi-level)
3. **Public Transport Interchange (PTI)** / Ground Transportation Centre (GTC)
4. **Access/Egress Roadways** and Curbside
5. **Fuel Farm & Hydrant System**
6. **Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul (MRO) Hangars**
7. **Air Catering Unit (ACU)**
8. **Airport Rescue & Fire Fighting (ARFF) Station**
9. **Flight Catering / Kitchen**
10. **Utility Buildings** (power house, water treatment, sewage)
11. **Hotel / Airport City / Commercial Zone**
12. **General Aviation (GA) Apron & Facilities**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Airport as a City":** Imagine an airport as a *miniature city*. The runway is its *main highway*, the terminal is its *town hall*, and the "other landside elements" are the *supporting neighborhoods* — fire station (ARFF), petrol pump (fuel farm), warehouse (cargo), bus stand (PTI), parking lot, repair garage (MRO), and restaurants (ACU). Just as a town planner places the fire station near the town hall but not on the main street, the airport planner places each landside element based on **service function, response time, security distance, and traffic pattern**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | ICAO/FAA Standard |
|---|---|
| ARFF response time | **≤ 3 minutes** (ICAO Annex 14) |
| Fuel farm buffer from terminal | **≥ 90 m** (NBC/ICAO norm) |
| Parking space per peak hour passenger | **0.3 – 0.5 car spaces** |
| Cargo terminal lot size (typical) | **30 – 50 ha** for medium airport |
| Curbside length | **1.5 – 2.0 m per peak hour passenger** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Principles of Landside Location Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "5-C" Rule of Landside Placement (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **C**lose to function it serves
> - **C**onnected efficiently
> - **C**onflict-free (no traffic/security conflict)
> - **C**ompliant with safety buffer
> - **C**apable of expansion

### 2.1.1 Functional Proximity Principle
Each landside element has an *optimum radius of service*. The planner places it **inside this radius** to minimize operational delay and **outside security-restricted airside zones**.

### 2.1.2 Segregation Principle
- **Airside vs Landside traffic** must never cross.
- **Heavy vehicle (cargo/fuel) vs passenger car** traffic separated.
- **Through traffic vs terminal-bound traffic** separated.

### 2.1.3 Buffer Zone Principle
A minimum **security/safety buffer** is maintained between:
- Fuel farm and PTB → **≥ 90 m**
- ARFF and runway → as per response time criteria
- MRO and terminal → **≥ 60 m** (blast/exhaust)

## 2.2 Location Logic for Each Element

### A. Cargo Terminal Complex
- **Location logic:** *City-side* (landside), away from passenger terminal, with **direct access to highway** and **dedicated taxiway to airside** for cargo aircraft.
- **Preferred orientation:** North/East side of runway to allow 24-hr night operations without disturbing residential zones.
- **Adjacencies:** Custom house, courier hubs, trucking yard, quarantine, bonded warehouse.

### B. Vehicle Parking
- **STA (Short-Term):** Adjacent to terminal curbside, ≤ 5 min walk.
- **LTA (Long-Term):** 600 – 1500 m from terminal, shuttle-connected.
- **Multi-level:** Vertical stack above STA footprint when land is scarce.

### C. Public Transport Interchange (PTI)
- **At terminal curbside** (city bus, taxi, auto-rickshaw stand).
- **Metro/HSR station** integrated below or beside terminal.
- Must not obstruct private vehicle flow.

### D. Access/Egress Roadways
- **One-way circulation** around terminal.
- **At least 2 independent routes** for emergency.
- **Departure curb** above **Arrival curb** (multi-level curbside) for vertical segregation.

### E. Fuel Farm
- **Landside** (security-wise), but with **direct pipeline to apron hydrants**.
- **Downwind of prevailing wind** from terminal.
- **Below-grade bund wall** of 110% tank capacity.

### F. MRO Hangar
- **Airside-adjacent**, with **wide-body hangar door facing apron**.
- **Heavy pavement** (PCN ≥ 60).
- Away from PTB but close to runway-end for tow-in efficiency.

### G. ARFF Station
- **Centrally located** to all runway ends.
- **≤ 3 min response** to runway midpoint.
- **Direct dedicated road** crossing perimeter (no traffic lights).

### H. Air Catering Unit (ACU)
- **Airside or landside with secure corridor** to apron.
- **Refrigerated truck access** to terminal kitchens.
- Adjacent to cargo for consolidated supply.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — it covers 80% of Part A numerical questions.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parking spaces required | $N_p = P_{php} \times 0.4$ | spaces | $P_{php}$ = Peak Hour Passengers |
| 2 | Curbside length | $L_c = P_{php} \times 1.8$ | m | 1.5 – 2.0 m per PHP |
| 3 | ARFF vehicles | $N_{ARFF} = 1 + \lfloor L/300 \rfloor$ | vehicles | $L$ = longest runway (m) |
| 4 | Fuel farm area | $A_{ff} = 0.05 \times A_{airfield}$ | ha | ≈ 5% of airfield area |
| 5 | Cargo terminal area | $A_{c} = T \times 0.04$ | m² | $T$ = annual cargo tonnes |
| 6 | GTC area | $A_{GTC} = P_{php} \times 1.5$ | m² | 1 – 2 m² per PHP |
| 7 | PTB expansion area | $A_{exp} = 1.2 \times A_{PTB}$ | m² | 20% headroom norm |
| 8 | Response time | $t = d / v$ | min | $v$ = ARFF avg speed 80 km/h |
| 9 | Bund wall capacity | $V_{bw} = 1.1 \times V_{tank}$ | m³ | 110% rule |
| 10 | MRO apron depth | $D_{MRO} = W + 15$ | m | $W$ = wingspan + 15 m clearance |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Master Planning:** Drives land-use zoning in the *Airport Master Plan (AMP)*.
- **Land Acquisition:** Determines size of city-side land bank.
- **Concession Planning:** Governs real-estate revenue model.
- **Sustainability:** Co-locating PTI with terminal reduces vehicular emission.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Parking Space Demand

**Problem statement:** Determine the number of car-parking spaces required for an airport handling 4000 peak-hour passengers, with a 40% modal share of private cars, average car occupancy = 2.5 persons.

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{php} &= 4000 \text{ passengers/hour} \\
m_{car} &= 0.40 \text{ (car modal share)} \\
n_{avg} &= 2.5 \text{ persons/car} \\
T_{dwell} &= 60 \text{ min (assumed turnover)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute passenger arrivals by car.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{car} &= P_{php} \times m_{car} \\
       &= 4000 \times 0.40 \\
       &= 1600 \text{ passengers/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to vehicle arrivals (divide by occupancy).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{car} &= \frac{P_{car}}{n_{avg}} \\
        &= \frac{1600}{2.5} \\
        &= 640 \text{ cars/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply 1-hour dwell assumption → cars parked = cars arrived.**

$$
N_{park} = 640 \text{ spaces}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 15% redundancy (operational cushion).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{design} &= 1.15 \times 640 \\
           &= 736 \text{ spaces} \rightarrow \text{use 750 spaces}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always quote the **modal share assumption** and **dwell time** explicitly; these are the two-step marks students forget.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ARFF Response-Time Compliance

**Given:** Runway length = 3000 m. ARFF station is 1200 m from runway midpoint. ARFF vehicle speed = 80 km/h.

**Step 1 — Convert speed.**

$$
v = 80 \text{ km/h} = \frac{80 \times 1000}{60} = 1333.33 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{d}{v} \\
  &= \frac{1200}{1333.33} \\
  &= 0.9 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add 1-min mobilisation delay (ICAO norm).**

$$
t_{total} = 0.9 + 1.0 = 1.9 \text{ min} < 3 \text{ min} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — ARFF vehicle count.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{ARFF} &= 1 + \lfloor 3000/300 \rfloor \\
         &= 1 + 10 \\
         &= 11 \text{ vehicles}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cargo Terminal Area

**Given:** Annual cargo throughput = 200,000 tonnes.

**Step 1 — Convert to daily throughput (365 days).**

$$
T_{day} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{365} = 547.95 \text{ t/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply floor-area norm (0.04 m² per annual tonne per ICAO Aerodrome Design Manual).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_c &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.04 \\
    &= 8000 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add 30% for circulation, road, parking, bonded zone.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{c,total} &= 1.3 \times 8000 \\
            &= 10{,}400 \text{ m}^2 \approx 1.04 \text{ ha}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Location Trade-offs

| Element | Best location | Avoid | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cargo Terminal | City-side, highway access | Under PTB flight path | Noise, security |
| Fuel Farm | Downwind, low-density | Upwind of PTB | Fire-smoke hazard |
| ARFF | Centroid of runways | Far from active runway | Response time > 3 min |
| MRO | Airside, large apron | Inside city-side boundary | Wing-span & weight |
| ACU | Landside secure corridor | Far from apron | Cold-chain delay |
| Parking STA | Adjacent to PTB | Beyond 200 m | Walk fatigue |
| GTC/PTI | Below/adjacent to PTB | Above apron | Structural load |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Combined Demand Forecast

**For a new greenfield airport with:**

$$
P_{php} = 5000,\;\; T_{cargo} = 3 \text{ lakh t/yr},\;\; L_{rwy} = 3500 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 1 — Parking (using $N_p = 0.4 \times P_{php}$):**

$$
N_p = 0.4 \times 5000 = 2000 \text{ cars}
$$

**Step 2 — Curbside length:**

$$
L_c = 5000 \times 1.8 = 9000 \text{ m} = 9 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 3 — GTC area:**

$$
A_{GTC} = 5000 \times 1.5 = 7500 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Cargo area:**

$$
A_c = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.04 = 12{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — ARFF vehicles:**

$$
N_{ARFF} = 1 + \lfloor 3500/300 \rfloor = 1 + 11 = 12
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Landside Element Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[City Access Highway] --> B[Access Road Network]
    B --> C[Curbside Departure]
    B --> D[Curbside Arrival]
    C --> E[PTB Departure Hall]

    E --> G[Security Hold]
    G --> H[Airside Apron]

    B --> L[STA Parking]
    B --> M[LTA Parking]
    B --> N[Cargo Terminal]
    N --> O[Customs Bonded Zone]

    P --> Q[Hydrant Pit Apron]
    B --> R[ARFF Station]
    R --> H
    B --> S[MRO Hangar]
    B --> T[ACU Catering Unit]
    B --> U[Utility Powerhouse]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Landside Element Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Airport / Expansion] --> Q1{Is element passenger-facing?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Place adjacent to PTB]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is it airside service?}

    Q3 -- No --> Q5{Is it heavy/frequent vehicle?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Place at city-side with own access road]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Place in utility belt]

    Q6 --> Check
    Q7 --> Check
    Check --> Done[Final Master Plan Zone]
```
