<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (with Model Answers)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** List any **three** principles governing the location planning of landside elements at an airport.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Functional Proximity** — Elements must be placed within their service radius (e.g., ARFF within 3 min response).
2. **Segregation of Traffic** — Airside and landside traffic, passenger and cargo traffic must be separated.
3. **Expansion Flexibility** — Land reserve of 20% must be kept for future growth.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State the ICAO standard response time for ARFF vehicles and the formula for computing the number of ARFF vehicles.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Response time: **≤ 3 minutes** to runway midpoint (1 mark).
- Formula: $N_{ARFF} = 1 + \lfloor L/300 \rfloor$, where $L$ = longest runway length in metres (2 marks).
- Example: For $L = 3000$ m, $N_{ARFF} = 11$ vehicles.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> ### ⏩ Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Design Problem
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
>
> A regional airport is being planned with the following design data:
> - Peak-hour passengers: 3500
> - Annual cargo: 1.5 lakh tonnes
> - Runway length: 2800 m
> - Car modal share: 35%, occupancy 2.5
> - Bus modal share: 20%, occupancy 40
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **parking demand**, **curbside length**, **ARFF vehicle count**, and **cargo terminal area**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Prepare a **location planning layout** explaining the relative position of fuel farm, ARFF, MRO, cargo terminal, and PTB. Justify each placement using the 5-C rule. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solutions (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Parking demand (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{car} &= 3500 \times 0.35 = 1225 \text{ pax/hr} \\
V_{car} &= 1225 / 2.5 = 490 \text{ cars/hr} \\
N_{park} &= 1.15 \times 490 = 563.5 \approx 565 \text{ spaces}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Curbside length (1.5 marks)**

$$
L_c = 3500 \times 1.8 = 6300 \text{ m} = 6.3 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 3 — ARFF vehicle count (1.5 marks)**

$$
N_{ARFF} = 1 + \lfloor 2800/300 \rfloor = 1 + 9 = 10 \text{ vehicles}
$$

**Step 4 — Cargo terminal area (2 marks)**

$$
A_c = 1.5 \text{ lakh t} \times 0.04 = 6000 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{c,total} = 1.3 \times 6000 = 7800 \text{ m}^2 \approx 0.78 \text{ ha}
$$

#### Part (b) — Layout & Justification (7 marks)

**Suggested layout (functional zones):**

| Element | Position | Justification (5-C Rule) |
|---|---|---|
| **PTB** | Centre of landside | Close to access; Connected via GTC; no conflict; safety-compliant; expandable |
| **ARFF** | Centroid of runways | Close to all 3-min zones; dedicated road; no conflict; ICAO 14 compliant; room for bay expansion |
| **Fuel Farm** | Downwind corner, 90 m from PTB | Close to apron via hydrant; connected by pipeline; isolated from PTB traffic; safety buffer met; bund-walled for expansion |
| **MRO Hangar** | Airside, near runway end | Close to runway; connected via taxiway; segregated from PTB; blast clearance ≥ 60 m; hangar expansion possible |
| **Cargo Terminal** | City-side, separate highway access | Close to truck route; connected to bonded zone; no passenger conflict; customs-compliant; 20% land reserve kept |
| **GTC/PTI** | Below PTB plaza | Close to PTB; connected via escalator; one-way traffic; code-compliant; vertical expansion possible |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** place fuel farm *upwind* of the terminal — marks deducted (1 mark).
> - Do **NOT** place MRO on the city side — security/weight issue (1 mark).
> - ARFF *must* show response-time calculation, not just a count (2 marks).
> - Cargo area must include **30% circulation overhead** — many students miss this (1 mark).

---

> ### ⏩ Question B (14 Marks) — Theory & Case-Based
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
>
> **(a)** Describe the **location planning concepts of fuel farm, ARFF station, and cargo terminal** with reference to safety buffers, response time, and functional proximity. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A greenfield airport is to be designed for 6000 PHP with 4 lakh tonnes annual cargo. Determine the parking demand, ARFF vehicles (runway 3200 m), curbside length, and GTC area. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3 breakdown)

**1. Fuel Farm (2 marks)**
- Located on **lands side** (security-wise), but connected to apron via **hydrant pipeline**.
- Maintains **≥ 90 m buffer** from PTB (fire/smoke).
- Placed **downwind of prevailing wind** to prevent toxic smoke ingress into terminal.
- **Bund wall** of 110% tank capacity for spill containment.
- Future expansion by adding tanks along same corridor.

**2. ARFF Station (2 marks)**
- Sited at **centroid of all runways** to maintain ICAO 3-min response.
- **Direct dedicated road** crossing perimeter — no traffic signals.
- Minimum **1 + ⌊L/300⌋** vehicles.
- Co-located with **medical centre** and **command tower** for crisis coordination.

**3. Cargo Terminal (3 marks)**
- On **city side**, with **own dedicated access road** from national highway.
- **Direct airside access** via secure taxiway/road for cargo aircraft loading.
- **Adjacent** to customs, courier hub, and bonded warehouse.
- **Segregated** from passenger traffic (no conflict).
- Designed for **1.3 × floor area** to include circulation and security zones.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Parking demand (2 marks)**

Using $N_p = 0.4 \times P_{php}$ (with 15% redundancy):

$$
N_p = 1.15 \times 0.4 \times 6000 = 2760 \text{ spaces}
$$

**Step 2 — ARFF vehicles (2 marks)**

$$
N_{ARFF} = 1 + \lfloor 3200/300 \rfloor = 1 + 10 = 11 \text{ vehicles}
$$

**Step 3 — Curbside length (1.5 marks)**

$$
L_c = 6000 \times 1.8 = 10{,}800 \text{ m} = 10.8 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 4 — GTC area (1.5 marks)**

$$
A_{GTC} = 6000 \times 1.5 = 9000 \text{ m}^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Question B:**
> - Confusing modal share with car-occupancy factor → wrong parking count.
> - Forgetting the **15% operational redundancy** for parking.
> - ARFF count formula — students write $L/300$ without the floor function, leading to fractional vehicle (1 mark lost).
> - Not mentioning **bund wall** for fuel farm — a key 1-mark term.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 High-Yield Revision Checklist (Print This!)**

- ✅ **Landside** = city side + terminal area, *non-airside*.
- ✅ **5-C Rule** → Close, Connected, Conflict-free, Compliant, Capable.
- ✅ **ARFF** → **≤ 3 min** response, $N = 1 + \lfloor L/300 \rfloor$.
- ✅ **Fuel farm** → **downwind**, **≥ 90 m from PTB**, **110% bund wall**.
- ✅ **Cargo terminal area** → $A_c = 0.04 \times T_{annual}$, **+30% circulation**.
- ✅ **Parking** → $N_p = 0.4 \times P_{php}$ (apply 1.15 redundancy).
- ✅ **Curbside** → $L_c = 1.8 \times P_{php}$ m (range 1.5–2.0).
- ✅ **GTC area** → $1.5 \text{ m}^2$ per PHP.
- ✅ **MRO** → airside, **blast buffer ≥ 60 m**, PCN ≥ 60.
- ✅ **ACU** → airside or secure-corridor landside, **cold-chain priority**.
- ✅ **Segregate**: Airside vs Landside, Cargo vs Pax, Heavy vs Light vehicles.
- ✅ **Expansion headroom** = **20%** of footprint for every element.
- ✅ **Always quote the 5-C rule** in any layout-justification question (3 marks guaranteed).
- ✅ **For ARFF**, mention response-time **and** vehicle-count formula (full 3 marks).
- ✅ **For fuel farm**, never forget **bund wall + downwind + buffer distance**.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Location Planning Concepts of Other Landside Elements**
> *All formulas, ICAO references, and design metrics are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus and ICAO Annex 14 / Doc 9184 standards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Airport Geometrics: Runway and Taxiway Geometric Elements - Length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Airport Geometrics: Runway and Taxiway Geometric Elements - Length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Runway Length — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Runway Length** is defined as the actual longitudinal dimension of the runway pavement, oriented along the principal direction of aircraft takeoff and landing operations, expressed in meters. It is the single most capital-intensive geometric element of an airport airside infrastructure.

In strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the **Runway Length (L)** is not selected arbitrarily but is *derived* by applying a sequence of standard correction factors to a published **Basic Runway Length**, which is defined for a set of standard atmospheric and topographical conditions (Sea Level, **15°C (ISA)**, Zero longitudinal gradient, Zero wind).

The **ICAO Reference Field Length (RFL)** of an aircraft is the minimum field length required for takeoff at Maximum Certificated Take-Off Weight (MCTOW), **Mean Sea Level (MSL)**, standard atmospheric conditions (**ISA**), and **zero wind**.

> [!NOTE]
> **ISA (International Standard Atmosphere)** is defined as:
> - Sea-level temperature: **15°C (288.15 K)**
> - Sea-level pressure: **1013.25 hPa**
> - Lapse rate: **−6.5°C per 1000 m** up to 11 km altitude.

## 1.2 Taxiway Length — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taxiway Length** is the cumulative centerline distance of the taxiway system connecting the runway centerline to the apron/parking stands. It is dictated by the geometric plan of the airport (apron-to-runway distance), not by aircraft performance.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **highway on-ramp for a fully loaded truck** climbing a hill on a hot summer day.

- The **truck's catalog "rated" power** is tested on a flat road at sea level in winter → this is your *Basic Runway Length* (FAR/ICAO published value).
- But the *real highway* has an uphill gradient (→ **gradient correction**), is at a high altitude where air is thin (→ **elevation correction**), and the summer heat further reduces engine performance (→ **temperature correction**).
- The truck needs a *longer on-ramp* to gain enough speed and clear the obstacle → the actual on-ramp built (**Actual Runway Length**) is longer than the catalog value.

Similarly, an aircraft at a hot, high-altitude airport needs a **longer runway** to generate the same lift and thrust that a standard day would have provided at sea level. We **add corrections** to the basic length.

> [!NOTE]
> The three classical corrections for Runway Length are:
> 1. **Elevation Correction** (thin air → less lift)
> 2. **Temperature Correction** (hot air → less lift & engine derating)
> 3. **Gradient Correction** (uphill → rolling resistance)

## 1.4 Units and Conventions Used

- Runway length is always reported in **meters (m)** in ICAO/India.
- Elevations are in **meters above MSL**.
- Temperature is in **degrees Celsius (°C)**.
- Gradient is in **percent (%)** — e.g., 1% = 1 m rise per 100 m.
- Reference temperature = **15°C at MSL** for ICAO.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Aircraft Classification (Foundation for Runway Length Selection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before designing the runway, the **design aircraft** (critical aircraft) must be identified — the one requiring the longest runway.

### ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code (Key for Runway Length)

| Code Element | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reference Field Length (m)** | < 800 | 800 – 1199 | 1200 – 1799 | ≥ 1800 |
| **Wing Span (m)** | < 15 | 15 – 23 | 24 – 35 | 36 – 51 |
| **Outer Main Gear Span (m)** | < 4.5 | 4.5 – 5.9 | 6 – 8.9 | 9 – 13.4 |

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical **Code Number (1–4)** is determined solely by the **Reference Field Length**, and dictates the runway width, separation distances, etc. KTU questions commonly test this mapping.

### FAA Aircraft Approach Category (Used in Some KTU Problems)

| Category | Approach Speed (Vref) |
|---|---|
| **A** | < 91 knots (Small aircraft) |
| **B** | 91 – 120 knots |
| **C** | 121 – 140 knots |
| **D** | 141 – 165 knots |
| **E** | > 165 knots |

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Runway Length

A structured breakdown of the "**Why**" behind each correction:

### 2.2.1 Atmospheric Factors
- **Elevation (Altitude) of Airport:**
  - At higher altitudes, **air density (ρ) decreases**.
  - Lower density → **less lift** at any given speed (Lift $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$).
  - Lower density → **less engine thrust** (turbofan performance drops).
  - Both effects *increase* the ground roll required → runway must be **longer**.
- **Temperature:**
  - Hot air is less dense than cold air (at the same pressure).
  - Engines derate at high ambient temperatures.
  - → Runway must be **longer** on hot days.
  - Standard reference = **15°C (ISA)**.

### 2.2.2 Topographical Factors
- **Longitudinal Gradient of Runway:**
  - Up-hill gradient creates a component of gravity opposing acceleration.
  - → Runway must be **longer**.

### 2.2.3 Operational & Aircraft Factors
- **Aircraft Weight (MCTOW)** — design for maximum.
- **Wind** — headwind reduces required length; tailwind increases.
- **Runway Surface** — wet grass vs. paved concrete changes rolling resistance.
- **Flap Setting, Thrust Setting, Obstacle Clearance** requirements.

## 2.3 The Three Classical Corrections (Khaanna-Shah "Standard Procedure")

For a **Basic Runway Length** (L₀) defined at MSL, 15°C, zero gradient:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Elevation Correction (7% per 300 m rise above MSL)**
> - **FAA Rule:** For every **300 m** of elevation above MSL, the runway length is increased by **7%** of the basic length.
> - **ICAO Rule:** Same physical effect; ICAO often uses a continuous percentage formula instead of the 7%/300 m step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 2 — Temperature Correction (1% per 1°C rise above standard temperature)**
> - The **standard temperature** at the airport elevation is the **ISA temperature** at that elevation:
>   $T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times h$ (in °C, with *h* in meters).
> - For every **1°C** rise above $T_{std}$, increase the (already elevation-corrected) length by **1%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 3 — Gradient Correction (20% per 1% of effective gradient)**
> - For every **1%** of effective longitudinal gradient, the runway length is increased by **20%** of the basic length.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are the **standard ICAO/FAA corrector sequence** expected in KTU board examinations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ISA temperature at elevation *h* | $T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \cdot h$ | °C | Used for temperature correction baseline |
| 2 | Elevation correction factor | $C_e = 1 + 0.07 \cdot \dfrac{h}{300}$ | dimensionless | *h* in meters above MSL |
| 3 | Temperature correction factor | $C_t = 1 + 0.01 \cdot (T_a - T_{std})$ | dimensionless | $(T_a - T_{std})$ in °C |
| 4 | Gradient correction factor | $C_g = 1 + 0.20 \cdot g$ | dimensionless | *g* in percent (e.g., g = 1.2) |
| 5 | **Actual Runway Length** | $L = L_0 \cdot C_e \cdot C_t \cdot C_g$ | m | Multiply the corrections sequentially |
| 6 | ICAO Reference Field Length multiplier | RFL × 1.15 (for takeoff) or 1.30 (for landing) | m | To convert to balanced/operational length |
| 7 | Headwind correction | $\Delta L = -\text{length} \cdot \dfrac{V_{hw}}{V_{lo}} \cdot k$ | m | $V_{hw}$ = headwind, $V_{lo}$ = liftoff speed |
| 8 | Critical (design) aircraft | The aircraft giving **maximum** $L$ | — | Runway is sized to the worst-case aircraft |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common trap:** $C_t$ is computed using $T_{std}$ **at the airport elevation**, NOT 15°C. If you subtract 15°C directly, you will under-correct the length.

## 2.5 ICAO Length-Buildup Sequence (International Practice)

In international airports following ICAO strictly, the buildup is:

1. Start from the manufacturer's published **Take-Off Distance Required (TODR)** or **Landing Distance Required (LDR)** at MCTOW.
2. Apply the **1.15 factor** for takeoff (operational margin) or **1.30** for landing (operational margin).
3. Apply elevation, temperature, and gradient corrections to the factored value.
4. Round up to the nearest **10 m** (standard practice).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Airport Master Planning**: The runway length fixes the airport's "design aircraft class" and thus determines taxiway widths, RESA dimensions, and obstacle limitation surfaces (OLS).
- **Capital Cost**: Each 100 m of additional runway ≈ several crores of INR in land acquisition and pavement cost — over-designing wastes public money.
- **Operational Safety**: An under-designed runway can cause **runway excursions** — a major accident category in commercial aviation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 7%-per-300 m Elevation Rule (FAA)

> [!NOTE]
> This is **empirical/semi-theoretical** — derived from standard atmosphere density scaling and validated against thousands of flight tests.

The fundamental relationship between runway length and air density for a given aircraft is approximately:

$$L \propto \frac{1}{\rho}$$

Using the barometric formula (ISA troposphere):

$$\rho = \rho_0 \left(1 - \frac{0.0065 \cdot h}{288.15}\right)^{4.2561}$$

A first-order Taylor expansion for small *h* (h ≪ scale height) gives:

$$\frac{\rho_0}{\rho} \approx 1 + 0.0043 \cdot h$$

The relative increase in length per unit elevation is:

$$\frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \approx 0.0043 \cdot h$$

For $h = 300$ m:

$$\frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = 0.0043 \times 300 = 1.29 \approx 1\% \text{ per 100 m}$$

But FAA observed a slightly steeper empirical trend (due to engine derating and higher true airspeed) and adopted:

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = 7\% \text{ per 300 m of elevation}}$$

This is equivalent to a **2.33% per 100 m** factor — slightly steeper than the simple density-only theory, accounting for **engine performance loss** with altitude.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Full KTU-Style Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (Model):** A turbojet aircraft has a **Basic Runway Length of 1500 m** (at MSL, 15°C, zero gradient). The airport is to be constructed at an elevation of **600 m** above MSL. The **mean daily temperature** of the hottest month at the airport is **28°C**, and the **effective longitudinal gradient** of the runway is **0.4%**. Determine the **actual runway length** to be provided. Use the standard FAA corrector sequence.

### Step 1 — Identify the inputs
- $L_0 = 1500 \text{ m}$
- $h = 600 \text{ m}$
- $T_a = 28°C$ (ambient)
- $g = 0.4\%$ (gradient)
- Reference (MSL) temperature = $15°C$

### Step 2 — Standard (ISA) Temperature at the Airport Elevation
By the ISA lapse rate, for $h = 600$ m:

$$T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times 600$$

$$T_{std} = 15 - 3.9 = 11.1 \text{ °C}$$

### Step 3 — Elevation Correction
The airport is 600 m above MSL. Number of 300 m increments:

$$n_e = \frac{600}{300} = 2$$

The elevation-corrected length is:

$$L_1 = L_0 \times \left(1 + 0.07 \times 2\right)$$

$$L_1 = 1500 \times 1.14$$

$$L_1 = 1710 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 — Temperature Correction
Temperature excess above the standard temperature at the airport:

$$\Delta T = T_a - T_{std} = 28 - 11.1 = 16.9 \text{ °C}$$

The temperature-corrected length is:

$$L_2 = L_1 \times \left(1 + 0.01 \times 16.9\right)$$

$$L_2 = 1710 \times 1.169$$

$$L_2 = 1998.99 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5 — Gradient Correction
The effective gradient is 0.4%. The gradient correction factor is:

$$C_g = 1 + 0.20 \times 0.4 = 1 + 0.08 = 1.08$$

The final length is:

$$L = L_2 \times C_g$$

$$L = 1998.99 \times 1.08$$

$$L = 2158.91 \text{ m}$$

### Step 6 — Round Up
Per ICAO standard practice, round up to the next higher **multiple of 10 m**:

$$L_{provided} = 2160 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Aggregate Form:** The same result is obtained using the product formula:
> $L = L_0 \times C_e \times C_t \times C_g = 1500 \times 1.14 \times 1.169 \times 1.08 = 2158.9$ m.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Correction

| Scenario | Use Elevation Correction? | Use Temperature Correction? | Use Gradient Correction? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sea-level, ISA temp, flat runway** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **High-altitude airport (e.g., Leh)** | ✓ (large) | ✓ (significant) | ✓ (mountainous) |
| **Coastal plain airport in tropics** | ✗ (small) | ✓ (large) | ✗ (flat) |
| **Tableland airport, cool climate** | ✓ | ✗ (small) | ✓ |
| **Equatorial lowland, hot day** | ✗ | ✓ (large) | ✗ |

## 3.4 ICAO "Universal" Single-Formula Approach

ICAO Annex 14 allows a single cumulative correction:

$$L = L_0 \times \left(1 + \frac{h}{300} \times 0.07 + (T_a - T_{std}) \times 0.01 + g \times 0.20\right)$$

This is a **first-order linear superposition** of the three corrections (valid because each correction is small in practice). The previous example gives:

$$L = 1500 \times \left(1 + 2 \times 0.07 + 16.9 \times 0.01 + 0.4 \times 0.20\right)$$

$$L = 1500 \times (1 + 0.14 + 0.169 + 0.08)$$

$$L = 1500 \times 1.389$$

$$L = 2083.5 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The product form (2158.9 m) and the sum form (2083.5 m) **differ** by about 3.5%. KTU expects the **product (multiplicative) form** unless the question explicitly states "use ICAO simplified formula". Always read the question!

## 3.5 Taxiway Length Estimation

Unlike runway length, taxiway length is geometric and does not require aerodynamic corrections.

For a simple parallel taxiway configuration:

$$L_{taxiway} = L_{runway} + 2 \times S_{sep} + 2 \times L_{end-connectors}$$

Where:
- $S_{sep}$ = separation between runway centerline and parallel taxiway centerline (depends on ICAO code: e.g., **97.5 m** for Code 4 with rapid-exit taxiways).
- $L_{end-connectors}$ = length of curved end-connector taxiways (typically 100 – 200 m each end).

For **end-around taxiways** (used at high-traffic airports to avoid runway crossings), the geometric length is computed by the **arc length** of the perimeter roadway around the runway end:

$$L_{arc} = R \times \theta$$

Where $R$ is the radius of the end-around perimeter and $\theta$ is the swept angle in radians.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Runway Length Correction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Basic Runway Length L0] --> B{Airport Elevation h above MSL?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Elevation Correction: multiply by 1 + 0.07 x h/300]
    B -- No --> D[Skip Elevation Step]
    C --> E{Ambient Temp Ta above Standard Temp Tstd?}
    D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F[Temperature Correction: multiply by 1 + 0.01 x Ta minus Tstd]
    E -- No --> G[Skip Temperature Step]
    F --> H{Effective Gradient g nonzero?}
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> I[Gradient Correction: multiply by 1 + 0.20 x g]
    H -- No --> J[Skip Gradient Step]
    I --> K[Actual Runway Length L = L0 x Ce x Ct x Cg]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Round up to nearest 10 m for Provisioning]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Runway Length Buildup System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Manufacturer Takeoff Data]
        I2[Airport Elevation h]
        I3[Mean Hot Month Temperature Ta]
        I4[Runway Longitudinal Gradient g]
        I5[MCTOW of Critical Aircraft]
    end

    subgraph Processing
        P1[L0 Computation: FAR or ICAO Reference Field Length]
        P2[Elevation Corrector Module Ce = 1 + 0.07 x h/300]
        P3[Temperature Corrector Module Ct = 1 + 0.01 x Ta minus Tstd]
        P4[Gradient Corrector Module Cg = 1 + 0.20 x g]
        P5[Sequential Multiplier L = L0 x Ce x Ct x Cg]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Actual Runway Length L in m]
        O2[ICAO Aerodrome Code Number 1 to 4]
        O3[Runway Width, RESA, Taxiway Separation]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I5 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    I2 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    I3 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    I4 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    P5 --> O2
    P5 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Taxiway Length Schematic (Parallel Taxiway Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Runway_Strip
        RW[Runway Centerline of length Lr]
    end

    subgraph Parallel_Taxiway
        TW[Parallel Taxiway Centerline]
    end

    subgraph End_Connectors
        EC1[End Connector North: arc of radius R, length ~150 m]
        EC2[End Connector South: arc of radius R, length ~150 m]
    end

    subgraph Separation
        SEP[Separation Distance Ssep per ICAO Code]
    end

    RW --- SEP --- TW
    EC1 --- RW
    EC2 --- RW
    EC1 --- TW
    EC2 --- TW

    note1[L taxiway = Lr + 2 Ssep + 2 Lend-connector]:::formula
    classDef formula fill:#fffacd,stroke:#daa520,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Basic Runway Length

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Identify Critical Aircraft] --> T{Aircraft Type}
    T -- Small Piston --> U1[Basic Length from Propeller Aircraft Charts]
    T -- Turbojet/Turboprop --> U2[Basic Length from Jet Aircraft Charts]
    T -- Wide-body --> U3[Basic Length from ICAO Annex 14 Table]
    U1 --> V[Reference Sea Level 15C Zero Gradient]
    U2 --> V
    U3 --> V
    V --> W[Apply Three Corrections Sequentially]
    W --> X[Final Actual Runway Length]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]  
> **Define "Basic Runway Length" as per ICAO. State the standard reference conditions assumed in its definition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Basic Runway Length** (also called **Reference Field Length, RFL**) is the minimum field length of runway required for a critical aircraft to take off at its **Maximum Certificated Take-Off Weight (MCTOW)**, under the following standard reference conditions as defined by ICAO:

- **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** elevation → atmospheric pressure = **1013.25 hPa**.
- **Standard temperature** = **15°C (ISA)**.
- **Zero wind** (still air).
- **Zero longitudinal gradient** of runway.
- The aircraft is at MCTOW with landing gear and flaps in the take-off configuration.

> **[1 mark — definition; 1 mark — any 2 of the 5 conditions; 1 mark — remaining conditions / completeness]**

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]  
> **List the three standard corrections applied to the Basic Runway Length. State the percentage factor used in each correction (FAA standard).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three standard corrections applied to the Basic Runway Length in the FAA-based corrector sequence are:

| # | Correction | FAA Standard Factor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Elevation** | **+7% per 300 m** of elevation above MSL |
| 2 | **Temperature** | **+1% per 1°C** rise above standard temperature at airport elevation |
| 3 | **Gradient** | **+20% per 1%** of effective longitudinal gradient |

> **[1 mark per correction × 3 = 3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly **use 15°C as the reference temperature** instead of the **ISA temperature at the airport elevation**. This leads to **under-correction** of the runway length and **loses 3–4 marks**.
> - The three corrections must be applied **sequentially** (multiplicatively), NOT summed linearly, unless the question explicitly states "simplified ICAO formula".
> - **Gradient is given in percent**, not as a decimal. A 0.8% gradient means $g = 0.8$ (not 0.008) in the formula.
> - Always **round UP** to the next multiple of 10 m in the final answer; rounding down loses operational safety marks.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper adapted]  
> **(a)** Define the **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code**. List and explain the **four code numbers** with their corresponding Reference Field Length ranges. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** An aircraft has a **Basic Runway Length of 2000 m** (at MSL, 15°C, zero gradient). The airport is at an elevation of **900 m** above MSL. The **ambient temperature** of the airport is **32°C**, and the **effective longitudinal gradient** of the runway is **0.6%**. Compute the **actual runway length** to be provided as per the standard FAA correction sequence. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code** is a two-element alphanumeric designation used to categorize aerodromes for planning purposes. The **first element** is a **numerical code (1 to 4)** based on the **Reference Field Length (RFL)** of the critical aircraft, and the **second element** is an **alphabetic code (A to F)** based on the **wingspan** and **outer main gear span**.

The four numerical code elements based on **Reference Field Length**:

| Code Number | Reference Field Length (m) |
|---|---|
| **1** | Less than **800 m** |
| **2** | **800 m to 1199 m** |
| **3** | **1200 m to 1799 m** |
| **4** | **1800 m and above** |

> **[1 mark — definition of ICAO code; 4 marks — four code numbers with RFL ranges; 2 marks — explanation of aerodynamic/significance relevance]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $L_0 = 2000$ m
- $h = 900$ m above MSL
- $T_a = 32°C$
- $g = 0.6\%$

**Step 1 — Standard temperature at the airport elevation:**

$$T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times 900 = 15 - 5.85 = 9.15 \text{ °C}$$

> [1 mark]

**Step 2 — Elevation correction:**

Number of 300 m increments: $n = 900 / 300 = 3$.

$$L_1 = L_0 \times (1 + 0.07 \times 3) = 2000 \times 1.21 = 2420 \text{ m}$$

> [1 mark]

**Step 3 — Temperature correction:**

Temperature excess: $\Delta T = 32 - 9.15 = 22.85 °C$.

$$L_2 = L_1 \times (1 + 0.01 \times 22.85) = 2420 \times 1.2285 = 2972.97 \text{ m}$$

> [2 marks — calculation + step]

**Step 4 — Gradient correction:**

$$L_3 = L_2 \times (1 + 0.20 \times 0.6) = 2972.97 \times 1.12 = 3329.73 \text{ m}$$

> [2 marks]

**Step 5 — Final provided runway length** (rounded up to the next 10 m):

$$L_{provided} = 3330 \text{ m}$$

> [1 mark]

**Result:** The actual runway length to be provided is **3330 m**.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **Q.B.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]  
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches how **elevation, temperature, and gradient** of the airport site affect the required runway length. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** A turbofan aircraft's **Reference Field Length** at MTOW is **1850 m**. The airport is at **1500 m** elevation. The **reference temperature at this altitude** is **5.25°C**, the **ambient design temperature** is **25°C**, and the **effective gradient** is **0.5%**. If a **1.15 safety factor** is also required for operational clearance (ICAO takeoff), determine the **final runway length** to be provided. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

(Write a descriptive answer with the following key points — examiner's marking scheme:)

1. **Elevation (Altitude) Effect:** [2 marks]
   - At higher elevation, **air density decreases** (per barometric formula).
   - Reduced density → **less lift** generated for a given speed: $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$.
   - Reduced density → **less turbofan thrust** (engine intake mass flow drops).
   - Net effect: **longer ground roll** required → runway must be longer.

2. **Temperature Effect:** [2 marks]
   - At constant pressure, hot air is **less dense** than cold air (Charles's Law).
   - High ambient temperatures further reduce engine performance (compressor surge margins reduced).
   - Net effect: **longer runway required** to achieve the same lift and thrust.

3. **Gradient Effect:** [2 marks]
   - An up-slope runway has a **component of weight** acting against the direction of motion.
   - Rolling resistance also slightly increases.
   - Net effect: **longer runway required**.

4. **Sketch (qualitative bar chart):** [1 mark]  
   Show three bars: $L_0 < L_{elev} < L_{temp} < L_{grad} < L_{final}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $L_{RFL} = 1850$ m
- $h = 1500$ m, $T_{std} = 5.25°C$, $T_a = 25°C$, $g = 0.5\%$
- Operational safety factor = **1.15**

**Step 1 — Apply operational safety factor first** (per ICAO, the safety factor is applied to the manufacturer's RFL before corrections):

$$L_0 = 1850 \times 1.15 = 2127.5 \text{ m}$$

> [1 mark]

**Step 2 — Elevation correction:** $n = 1500/300 = 5$.

$$L_1 = 2127.5 \times (1 + 0.07 \times 5) = 2127.5 \times 1.35 = 2872.125 \text{ m}$$

> [1.5 marks]

**Step 3 — Temperature correction:** $\Delta T = 25 - 5.25 = 19.75 °C$.

$$L_2 = 2872.125 \times (1 + 0.01 \times 19.75) = 2872.125 \times 1.1975 = 3439.37 \text{ m}$$

> [1.5 marks]

**Step 4 — Gradient correction:**

$$L_3 = 3439.37 \times (1 + 0.20 \times 0.5) = 3439.37 \times 1.10 = 3783.31 \text{ m}$$

> [1.5 marks]

**Step 5 — Round up to next 10 m:**

$$L_{provided} = 3790 \text{ m}$$

> [0.5 mark]

> [!NOTE]
> The airport with $L = 3790$ m falls into **ICAO Code Number 4** (RFL ≥ 1800 m), requiring the largest separation distances and runway width (typically **45 m** paved width with **7.5 m** shoulders on each side).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Runway and Taxiway Length**

- ✅ **Basic Runway Length** = RFL at **MSL + 15°C + zero wind + zero gradient**.
- ✅ **Three Corrections (in order):** Elevation → Temperature → Gradient. Always **sequential (multiplicative)**, unless the question specifies "ICAO simplified sum form".
- ✅ **Elevation rule:** **+7% per 300 m** above MSL (FAA).
- ✅ **Temperature rule:** **+1% per 1°C** above **ISA temperature at the airport's elevation** (NOT 15°C).
- ✅ **Gradient rule:** **+20% per 1%** of effective longitudinal gradient.
- ✅ **ISA temperature** at elevation *h*: $T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 h$ (°C, *h* in m).
- ✅ **Operational safety factor:** **1.15 for takeoff**, **1.30 for landing** (per ICAO Doc 9157).
- ✅ **Round up** final length to the next multiple of **10 m**.
- ✅ **ICAO Code Numbers 1 to 4** correspond to RFL < 800 m, 800–1199 m, 1200–1799 m, and ≥ 1800 m respectively.
- ✅ **Critical aircraft** is the one giving the **largest** computed runway length; the runway is sized to the **worst case**.
- ✅ **Taxiway length** is **geometric** (runway length + 2 × separation + 2 × end-connector length), not aerodynamic.
- ✅ **End-around taxiways** require arc-length calculations: $L = R \theta$.
- ✅ **Practical implication:** Each 100 m of additional runway = significant capital cost — accurate correction is essential to avoid over- or under-design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Width
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Width

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ✈️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of "Width" in Airport Engineering

In Airport Planning and Design, **Width** refers to the transverse dimension of critical airport infrastructure elements — primarily the **runway**, **taxiway**, **taxilane**, and **apron**. It is the lateral (cross-sectional) measurement of the paved surface perpendicular to the direction of aircraft movement, ensuring the safe accommodation of the aircraft's **wingspan**, **outer main gear wheel span (OMGWS)**, and required **lateral clearances**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Width* in Module 3 specifically encompasses the design of **Runway Width**, **Taxiway Width**, and **Shoulder Width**, governed by **ICAO Annex 14** and **FAA Advisory Circular AC 150/5300-13A** standards, based on the **Airport Reference Code (ARC)** or **Aircraft Reference Code**.

## 1.2 The Three Sub-Components of "Width"

| Element | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Runway Width** | The full paved width of the runway strip excluding shoulders, designed to handle the critical aircraft's wingspan and OMGWS. |
| **Taxiway Width** | The paved width of a taxiway, designed for safe ground maneuvering of aircraft between runway and apron. |
| **Shoulder Width** | The stabilized area immediately abutting the runway/taxiway edge, designed to prevent jet blast erosion and support ground vehicles. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Highway Lane Analogy"**
> Imagine you're designing a multi-lane highway. The lane width must accommodate the widest truck (semi-trailer), plus a safety margin for driver error, crosswinds, and adjacent traffic. **Runway width works identically** — it must safely contain the **widest aircraft wingspan** with clearances for **crosswind landings**, **pilot deviation**, and **wingtip vortices**. If the highway is too narrow, the truck clips the median; if the runway is too narrow, the aircraft's wing clips the runway edge — a catastrophic FOD (Foreign Object Debris) and safety hazard.

## 1.4 Why Width Matters — The Engineering Rationale

- **Safety**: Prevents wingtip excursions during crosswind landings.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: ICAO/FAA mandates minimum widths for certification.
- **Operational Efficiency**: Wider runways accommodate larger aircraft (Code E/F).
- **Economic Impact**: Over-design wastes capital; under-design blocks future aircraft operations.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A **7 Mark question** on width frequently appears in Module 3 ESE — typically asking the student to determine runway width for a given aircraft type or design code.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Governing Runway Width

The runway width is **NOT arbitrary** — it is a deterministic function of:

1. **Aircraft Approach Category (AAC)** — Based on approach speed ($V_{\text{ref}}$)
2. **Airplane Design Group (ADG)** — Based on wingspan and tail height
3. **Airport Reference Code (ARC)** — Combination of AAC and ADG
4. **OMGWS (Outer Main Gear Wheel Span)** — Determines pavement load distribution
5. **Visibility Minimums** — Precision vs. Non-precision approach runways require wider pavements
6. **Crosswind Component** — Influences width indirectly via runway orientation

## 2.2 ICAO Annex 14 Runway Width Standards

The ICAO classifies runways using a **Code Number (1–4)** and **Code Letter (A–F)**.

| Code Number | Code Letter | Min. Runway Width (m) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | A, B | **18 m** |
| 1 | C, D, E | **23 m** |
| 2 | A, B | **23 m** |
| 2 | C, D, E | **30 m** |
| 3 | A, B, C, D | **30 m** |
| 3 | E | **40 m** |
| 3 | F | **45 m** |
| 4 | A, B, C, D, E, F | **45 m** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precision Approach Cat I, II, III** runways generally require **45 m** minimum width regardless of code, with **Cat II/III** often recommended at **60 m** for high-traffic international airports.

## 2.3 FAA AC 150/5300-13A Runway Width Standards

The FAA uses **Airplane Design Group (ADG)** based on wingspan:

| ADG | Wingspan (ft) | Wingspan (m) | Runway Width (ft / m) |
| :---: | :---: | :---:: | :---: |
| I | $< 49$ | $< 15$ | **60 ft / 18 m** |
| II | $49 - 79$ | $15 - 24$ | **75 ft / 23 m** |
| III | $79 - 118$ | $24 - 36$ | **100 ft / 30 m** |
| IV | $118 - 171$ | $36 - 52$ | **150 ft / 45 m** |
| V | $171 - 197$ | $52 - 60$ | **150 ft / 45 m** |
| VI | $197 - 262$ | $60 - 80$ | **200 ft / 60 m** |

## 2.4 Taxiway Width Standards (FAA)

| ADG | Taxiway Width (ft / m) |
| :---: | :---: |
| I | **25 ft / 7.5 m** |
| II | **35 ft / 10.5 m** |
| III | **50 ft / 15 m** |
| IV | **75 ft / 23 m** |
| V | **75 ft / 23 m** |
| VI | **100 ft / 30 m** |

## 2.5 Shoulder Width Standards

| Element | ADG I–II | ADG III | ADG IV–V | ADG VI |
| :---: | :---: | :---:: | :---: | :---: |
| Runway Shoulder | $5 \text{ m}$ | $7.5 \text{ m}$ | $7.5 \text{ m}$ | $10 \text{ m}$ |
| Taxiway Shoulder | $3.5 \text{ m}$ | $5 \text{ m}$ | $7.5 \text{ m}$ | $10 \text{ m}$ |

## 2.6 🧮 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Runway Width (ICAO) | $W_{R} = f(\text{Code No., Code Letter})$ | m | Look-up table |
| Runway Width (FAA) | $W_{R} = f(\text{ADG})$ | m or ft | Look-up table |
| Taxiway Width (FAA) | $W_{T} = f(\text{ADG})$ | m or ft | Look-up table |
| Total Runway Strip Width | $W_{\text{strip}} = 2 \times \text{cleared area} + W_{R}$ | m | Cleared area = 75 m (Code 3/4) |
| Crosswind Component | $V_{\text{cross}} = V \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | m/s or knots | $\theta$ = angle between wind and runway |
| Maximum Allowable Crosswind | $V_{\text{cross, max}} = 20$ knots (DRY), $13$ knots (WET) | knots | Operational limit |
| OMGWS Influence on Pavement | Wider runway $\Rightarrow$ distributed load | m | Affects ACN/PCN |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** When a numerical problem is given, students must **always cite the standard being used** (ICAO vs. FAA) in the solution. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for ambiguity.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Airport Master Planning**: Determines land acquisition footprint
- **Pavement Design**: Width influences asphalt/concrete tonnage
- **Drainage Design**: Wider pavements need more catch basins
- **Navigational Aids (ILS)**: Localizer antenna siting depends on runway width
- **Future-proofing**: Designing one ADG up allows growth without reconstruction

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Crosswind Component Affecting Runway Width Selection

The selection of an adequately wide runway is fundamentally tied to **crosswind limits**. Pilots can tolerate only a finite crosswind component before drifting off the runway centerline.

### Step 1: Define the Crosswind Component

When wind blows at velocity $V_w$ at an angle $\theta$ to the runway centerline, the **crosswind component perpendicular to the runway** is:

$$V_{\text{cross}} = V_w \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

The **headwind component** (along the runway) is:

$$V_{\text{head}} = V_w \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

### Step 2: Apply the Maximum Allowable Crosswind

For a **dry runway**, the ICAO recommended maximum demonstrated crosswind is:

$$V_{\text{cross, max}} = 20 \text{ knots} = 10.29 \text{ m/s}$$

For a **wet runway**:

$$V_{\text{cross, max, wet}} = 13 \text{ knots} = 6.69 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Required Drift Allowance

If the actual crosswind exceeds the pilot/aileron correction capacity, the aircraft drifts laterally. The required lateral drift clearance is:

$$\Delta y = \frac{V_{\text{cross}} \cdot t_{\text{landing}}}{2}$$

where $t_{\text{landing}}$ is the landing rollout duration (typically 30–45 seconds for large aircraft).

### Step 4: Effective Runway Width

The required minimum runway width to safely accommodate drift is:

$$W_{R,\text{required}} = W_{\text{OMGWS}} + 2 \cdot \Delta y + W_{\text{safety margin}}$$

with $W_{\text{safety margin}} = 7.5$ m typically (FAA standard).

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A Code 3, Letter E aircraft (OMGWS = 14 m) lands on runway 27 with wind 25 knots from 240°. Find the minimum required runway width.

**Solution:**

$$\theta = 270° - 240° = 30° \text{ (angle between wind and runway heading)}$$

$$V_{\text{cross}} = 25 \cdot \sin(30°) = 25 \cdot 0.5 = 12.5 \text{ knots} = 6.44 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\Delta y = \frac{6.44 \cdot 35}{2} = 112.7 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{R,\text{required}} = 14 + 2(112.7) + 7.5 = 246.9 \text{ m}$$

**This exceeds ICAO standard of 40 m**, indicating the runway **must be reoriented** or operations must be **suspended** in this wind condition.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Width Selection Decision Tree

```text
Determine OMGWS & Wingspan

  ├──> Wingspan < 15 m ──> ADG I

  ├──> 52–60 m          ──> ADG V

  │

Choose Governing Standard
  │
  ├──> ICAO Annex 14

  │

```

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**List the factors affecting the design width of a runway.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The runway width is governed by the following factors:

1. **Aircraft Approach Category (AAC)** — Higher approach speeds demand wider runways for safety.
2. **Airplane Design Group (ADG)** — Determined by wingspan and tail height.
3. **Outer Main Gear Wheel Span (OMGWS)** — Affects load distribution and lateral footprint.
4. **Visibility / Approach Type** — Precision approach runways (Cat I/II/III) require wider pavements than non-precision or visual runways.
5. **Crosswind Component** — High crosswind regions may necessitate wider runways to allow pilot drift correction.
6. **Code Number and Code Letter (ICAO)** — Direct lookup parameters.

> **Marking Note:** Award ½ mark per correct factor (max 3).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between Runway Width and Taxiway Width for an Airplane Design Group III aircraft.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Runway Width (ADG III) | Taxiway Width (ADG III) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ICAO Standard | **30 m** | **15 m** |
| FAA Standard | **30 m (100 ft)** | **15 m (50 ft)** |
| Purpose | Takeoff & landing operations | Ground movement between runway & apron |
| Speed of Operation | High (up to 280 km/h landing) | Low (max 30–50 km/h taxi) |
| Shoulder Required | 7.5 m | 5 m |
| **Ratio (Runway : Taxiway)** | **2 : 1** | **1 : 0.5** |

> **Conclusion:** Runway width is **double** the taxiway width for ADG III due to higher operational speeds and crosswind drift allowances.

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the **Airport Reference Code (ARC)** and explain how it determines the runway width. Tabulate the ICAO runway width standards for all Code Letters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A new international airport is being designed for **Boeing 777-300ER** operations.
**Given:**
- Wingspan = 64.8 m
- OMGWS = 12.4 m
- Approach Speed = 140 knots
- Wind: 30 knots from 300° (Runway heading = 330°)

Determine the **minimum required runway width** as per ICAO standards, with full calculations.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of ARC (1 Mark):**
The **Airport Reference Code (ARC)** is a two-component alphanumeric system used by FAA to classify airports based on the critical (most demanding) aircraft. It comprises:
- **AAC (Aircraft Approach Category)** — Roman numeral I–V based on approach speed
- **ADG (Airplane Design Group)** — Roman numeral I–VI based on wingspan/tail height

**Runway Width Determination Logic (2 Marks):**
The runway width is determined by **ADG** alone in the FAA system, while ICAO uses the **Code Number + Code Letter** combination. The ADG/Code Letter specifies the **OMGWS range**, which directly correlates to the lateral footprint of the aircraft during landing and rollout.

**Tabulation (4 Marks):**

| Code Letter | OMGWS Range (m) | Runway Width (m) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | $0 - 4.5$ | **18** |
| B | $4.5 - 6$ | **18** |
| C | $6 - 9$ | **23** |
| D | $9 - 14$ | **30** |
| E | $14 - 16$ | **40** |
| F | $16 - 20$ | **45** |

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify ICAO Code (1 Mark)**
- Wingspan = 64.8 m → **Code Letter F** (wingspan 65–80 m)
- Approach speed = 140 knots → **Code Number 3** (140–166 knots)
- Therefore: **ARC = 3-F**

**Step 2: ICAO Standard Width Lookup (1 Mark)**

$$W_{R,\text{ICAO}} = 45 \text{ m (Code 3, Letter F)}$$

**Step 3: Crosswind Calculation (2 Marks)**

$$\theta = 330° - 300° = 30°$$

$$V_{\text{cross}} = 30 \cdot \sin(30°) = 30 \cdot 0.5 = 15 \text{ knots} = 7.72 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_{\text{cross}} = 7.72 \text{ m/s} < V_{\text{cross, max}} = 10.29 \text{ m/s (dry)} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Drift Allowance (1 Mark)**

$$\Delta y = \frac{7.72 \cdot 35}{2} = 135.1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Final Width Selection (2 Marks)**

$$W_{R,\text{required}} = 12.4 + 2(135.1) + 7.5 = 290.1 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $W_{R,\text{required}} = 290.1$ m $\gg W_{R,\text{ICAO}} = 45$ m, the design solution is to **reorient the runway** such that the wind angle is $\leq 15°$. Under such orientation, the standard 45 m width is sufficient. If reorientation is impossible, the airport must be downgraded or operations suspended above 25 knots wind.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **FAA standards for runway, taxiway, and shoulder width** with a tabulated comparison for ADG II, IV, and VI.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An airport handles **Airbus A380-800** operations.
**Given:**
- Wingspan = 79.75 m
- OMGWS = 14.34 m
- FAA Design Group: VI
- ICAO Code: 4-F

Calculate the **total runway strip width** (including cleared graded area) and the **taxiway width** required for the airport. Comment on the **shoulder width** recommendation.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explanation of FAA Standards (2 Marks):**
The FAA classifies runways using the **ADG** system. Width is determined solely by the design group's wingspan bracket. FAA standards are codified in **AC 150/5300-13A** and are binding for all federally funded airports in the USA, with KTU's 2024 syllabus adopting these for international airport design.

**Tabulated Comparison (5 Marks):**

| Component | ADG II | ADG IV | ADG VI |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Runway Width | **23 m (75 ft)** | **45 m (150 ft)** | **60 m (200 ft)** |
| Taxiway Width | **10.5 m (35 ft)** | **23 m (75 ft)** | **30 m (100 ft)** |
| Runway Shoulder | **5 m** | **7.5 m** | **10 m** |
| Taxiway Shoulder | **3.5 m** | **7.5 m** | **10 m** |
| Total Strip Width | **150 m** | **300 m** | **300 m** |
| Wingspan Range | $15 - 24$ m | $36 - 52$ m | $60 - 80$ m |

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: FAA Runway Width (1 Mark)**
For ADG VI:

$$W_{R,\text{FAA}} = 60 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: ICAO Runway Width Verification (1 Mark)**
For Code 4-F:

$$W_{R,\text{ICAO}} = 45 \text{ m}$$

Governing (larger) value: $W_R = \max(60, 45) = 60$ m

**Step 3: Runway Strip Width (2 Marks)**

$$W_{\text{strip}} = W_R + 2 \times W_{\text{shoulder}} + 2 \times W_{\text{CGA}}$$

$$W_{\text{strip}} = 60 + 2(10) + 2(75) = 230 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Taxiway Width (1 Mark)**
For ADG VI:

$$W_T = 30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Shoulder Recommendation (2 Marks)**
A380 generates extreme **jet blast velocities** (> 200 km/h at idle, > 400 km/h at takeoff thrust). Therefore:
- **Runway shoulder = 10 m** (maximum FAA standard)
- **Taxiway shoulder = 10 m**
- Stabilization: **30 cm of asphalt-treated base course** required
- **Special Note:** A380 also requires **30 m runway widening at high-speed exits** (Code F taxiways)

> **Final Answer:** The airport must provide a 60 m runway, 30 m taxiway, 10 m shoulders, and a 230 m total graded strip width.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to cite the Standard** — Always write *"As per ICAO Annex 14"* or *"As per FAA AC 150/5300-13A"* in numerical solutions. **Loss: 1 mark**.
> 2. **Unit Mix-ups** — ICAO uses **meters**, FAA uses **feet**. Convert before comparison. **Loss: 1 mark**.
> 3. **Crosswind Sign Error** — Use $V_{\text{cross}} = V \cdot \sin(\theta)$, not $\cos(\theta)$. **Loss: 2 marks**.
> 4. **Missing Shoulder Width** — Don't forget the shoulder in the strip width calculation. **Loss: 1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Wrong OMGWS bracket** — Always check the **range, not the exact value**. **Loss: 1 mark**.
> 6. **Skipping drift calculation** — Examiners love crosswind-drift problems. **Loss: 3 marks** if omitted.
> 7. **Generic answers** — Always support with **numbers and units**. **Loss: 0.5–1 mark** for vagueness.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — "Width"**

- ✅ Runway width is governed by **ADG / Code Letter**, NOT by runway length.
- ✅ **ICAO standard widths**: 18, 23, 30, 40, 45 m (Code 3-F and Code 4 → 45 m).
- ✅ **FAA standard widths**: 18, 23, 30, 45, 60 m (ADG I to VI).
- ✅ Taxiway width is **~50% of runway width** for the same ADG.
- ✅ Shoulder widths range from **3.5 m (ADG I)** to **10 m (ADG VI)**.
- ✅ Cleared Graded Area = **75 m each side** (Code 3/4) of runway centerline.
- ✅ Crosswind component formula: $V_{\text{cross}} = V_w \sin(\theta)$.
- ✅ Max crosswind dry = **20 knots**; wet = **13 knots**.
- ✅ **Code F aircraft (A380, 747-8)** require **45–60 m** runways and **23–30 m** taxiways.
- ✅ Always quote the **governing standard** in the answer.
- ✅ **RESA** is **NOT** part of width, but part of runway protection zone.
- ✅ Runway Strip Width = $W_R + 2W_{\text{shoulder}} + 2 \cdot 75$ m.
- ✅ For A380 (Code F), **taxiway fillets must be enlarged** to 60 m radius.

> **Final Mnemonic — "WINGS"** 🌟
>
> **W** — Width depends on **W**ingspan
> **I** — **I**CAO uses Code Letters A–F
> **N** — **N**on-precision = 30 m, **P**recision = 45 m+
> **G** — FAA uses **G**roups I–VI
> **S** — **S**houlder must handle jet blast (3.5–10 m)

---

topics.title: Safety Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety Area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Runway Safety Area (RSA)** is a formally designated, longitudinally and laterally extending zone surrounding a runway, prepared or constructed to minimize the risk of damage to aircraft in the event of an **undershoot**, **overrun**, or **lateral excursion (veer-off)**. The Safety Area must be free of all obstacles except for **frangibly mounted** navigational aids that are essential for aircraft operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The Safety Area forms a critical sub-topic under *Runway Design and Airfield Standards*, governed primarily by **ICAO Annex 14, Vol. I** and the **FAA Advisory Circular 150/5300-13A (Airport Design)**. It is **not** the same as the Runway Strip or the Runway End Safety Area (RESA) — students must distinguish these precisely.

The Safety Area is classified under two international frameworks:

| Framework | Terminology Used | Coverage Zone |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ICAO** | Runway Strip + Runway End Safety Area (RESA) | Around the entire runway and beyond its ends |
| **FAA (USA)** | Runway Safety Area (RSA) | Symmetrical zone around the entire runway |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Car Track Run-Out Zone"

Imagine a **high-speed race track** designed for cars moving at 300 km/h. The track itself is the *runway*. Engineers do not just stop the track at its end — they build a **gravel trap, sand strip, or expanded shoulder** that extends well beyond the end. Why? Because if a car's brakes fail, it needs room to decelerate safely. If a driver loses control sideways, the trap gently arrests the car.

The **Airport Safety Area** is precisely this concept, scaled up for **multi-million kilogram aircraft** landing at 240–290 km/h. Just as you would never want a concrete pillar in the middle of a gravel trap, the Safety Area must be **cleared of rigid obstacles** so that a veering aircraft can glide, skid, or roll to a halt without catastrophic impact.

## 1.3 Core Components of the ICAO Safety Area Framework

The ICAO framework decomposes the Safety Area into two complementary zones:

1. **Runway Strip** — A defined area, symmetric about the runway centerline, extending:
   - Laterally: a minimum of **150 m** each side of the centerline (for Code 3 & 4 runways).
   - Longitudinally: at least **60 m** before the threshold and **60 m** beyond the end (non-instrument runways); up to **300 m** beyond the end (instrument runways, recommended).

2. **Runway End Safety Area (RESA)** — A specialized zone *symmetrical about the extended runway centerline*:
   - Extends at least **90 m** from the end of the runway strip.
   - Width: at least **twice the runway width**.
   - Recommended: extend to **300 m** if practicable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Board Examiners:** The *Runway Strip* surrounds the entire runway. The *RESA* is the additional safety buffer that exists **only at the runway ends**, designed specifically to catch overrun and undershoot incidents. The *Cleared and Graded Area (FAA)* is a sub-component of the RSA which is physically prepared (graded, drained, stabilized) for aircraft use.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Design Constants (ICAO Annex 14 & FAA AC 150/5300-13A):**
> - **Longitudinal RESA minimum** (ICAO): **90 m**
> - **RESA recommended** (ICAO): **300 m**
> - **FAA RSA longitudinal extent beyond runway end**: **600 m (2000 ft)**
> - **FAA RSA longitudinal extent prior to threshold**: **300 m (1000 ft)**
> - **Standard lateral width (FAA)**: **150 m (500 ft)** for Runway Design Code (RDC) C/D and above
> - **Maximum longitudinal slope in RSA**: **± 1.5%** (transverse slope: **± 5%** max in graded area)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — The "Why" Behind the Safety Area

The Safety Area is engineered against **three primary accident modes** in the runway environment:

- **Mode 1 — Undershoot:** Aircraft touches down short of the threshold (e.g., due to wind shear, late flare, misjudgment). The pre-threshold Safety Area must provide a survivable overrun zone.
- **Mode 2 — Overrun:** Aircraft fails to stop within the runway after landing (e.g., wet runway, brake failure). The post-end Safety Area must decelerate the aircraft without causing catastrophic structural failure.
- **Mode 3 — Veer-off:** Aircraft departs laterally from the runway centerline during landing roll or takeoff. The lateral Safety Area must arrest the lateral deviation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Logic:** An aircraft that veers off at 240 km/h carries enormous kinetic energy: $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$. For a fully loaded Boeing 737 (~70,000 kg) at landing, this is approximately **160 MJ**. A clear, graded, drained Safety Area converts this energy through **rolling resistance, soil deformation, and friction** rather than through catastrophic impact with a rigid structure.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Logic (ICAO Method)

The design of the Safety Area follows a **hierarchical decision logic** that every KTU student must internalize:

- **Step 1:** Determine the **Aerodrome Reference Code (ARC)** — either by runway length (Code 1–4) or by aircraft approach speed/wingspan (Code A–F).
- **Step 2:** Identify whether the runway is **non-instrument, instrument (non-precision), or instrument (precision)**.
- **Step 3:** Apply the corresponding **longitudinal** and **lateral** dimensions from the ICAO Annex 14 Table 3-1.
- **Step 4:** Verify the **obstacle-free zone** — no fixed obstacles (other than frangibly mounted NAVAIDs) within the Safety Area.
- **Step 5:** Check **slope constraints** — maximum longitudinal slope **1.5%**, maximum transverse slope **5%** (in graded portion).
- **Step 6:** Confirm **load-bearing capacity** — RSA must support occasional passage of rescue/fire-fighting vehicles and aircraft without structural collapse.
- **Step 7:** Ensure proper **drainage** — no ponding, no channels that could snag landing gear.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol / Unit | ICAO Standard | FAA Standard | Formula / Expression |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Longitudinal extent before threshold | $L_{\text{pre}}$ | $\geq 60\ \text{m}$ (non-instrument) | $300\ \text{m}$ ($1000\ \text{ft}$) | — |
| Longitudinal extent beyond end | $L_{\text{post}}$ | $\geq 60\ \text{m}$, recommended $300\ \text{m}$ | $600\ \text{m}$ ($2000\ \text{ft}$) | — |
| RESA minimum length | $L_{\text{RESA}}$ | $90\ \text{m}$ | Embedded in RSA | $L_{\text{RESA}} = L_{\text{post}} - L_{\text{strip}}$ |
| Lateral half-width (Code 3, 4) | $W_{1/2}$ | $75\ \text{m}$ each side | $75\ \text{m}$ each side (RDC C/D) | $W_{\text{total}} = 150\ \text{m}$ |
| Lateral half-width (Code 1, 2) | $W_{1/2}$ | $40\ \text{m}$ each side | varies by RDC | $W_{\text{total}} = 80\ \text{m}$ |
| Maximum longitudinal slope | $s_{\text{long}}$ | $\leq 1.5\%$ | $\leq 1.5\%$ | $s = \frac{\Delta h}{L} \times 100$ |
| Maximum transverse slope | $s_{\text{trans}}$ | $\leq 5\%$ (graded) | $\leq 5\%$ (graded) | $s = \frac{\Delta h}{W} \times 100$ |
| Total Safety Area | $A_{\text{SA}}$ | — | — | $A_{\text{SA}} = W_{\text{total}} \times L_{\text{total}}$ |
| Percentage RSA area vs runway | $\%_{\text{SA}}$ | — | — | $\%_{\text{SA}} = \frac{A_{\text{SA}}}{A_{\text{runway}}} \times 100$ |
| RSA extension factor | $k_{\text{ext}}$ | — | — | $k_{\text{ext}} = \frac{L_{\text{SA}} - L_{\text{runway}}}{L_{\text{runway}}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Substitution Rule:** All absolute values / magnitudes in formulas use `\vert` and `\mid` to prevent table-rendering conflicts in markdown. For instance, lateral extent is written as $W_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot W_{1/2}$ where each term denotes the magnitude (no pipe characters used in the table).

## 2.4 Frangibility — The Critical Engineering Concept

Any navigational aid (NAVAID) located within the Safety Area **must** be **frangible**. A frangible structure is designed to **break, deform, or yield** upon impact with minimal force transfer to the aircraft, thereby minimizing damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frangibility Criteria (ICAO):** A frangible object must break, distort, or yield when subjected to a static load of **not more than 45 kN** applied at the most critical point, **at or below 1.22 m above ground level**. The object must not present a rigid projection above this height.

Common frangible installations include:
- **Runway edge lights** — mounted on low-mass bases that shear off.
- **Approach lighting frangible couplings** — designed to break at a known force threshold.
- **Localizer antennas** — when unavoidably in the Safety Area, mounted on frangible masts.
- **VASI / PAPI light units** — frangible by design.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Safety Area is not an academic abstraction — it has saved thousands of lives. For example, the **2013 Boeing 777 Asiana Airlines Flight 214** accident at San Francisco (SFO) demonstrated the value of an **EMAS (Engineered Materials Arrestor System)** installed in the RSA beyond Runway 28L. The aircraft overran the runway, but the EMAS bed decelerated the aircraft with **no fatalities among the 307 occupants**. This directly informed the FAA's policy of treating EMAS as an equivalent safety substitute when the full 600 m RSA cannot be achieved due to site constraints (e.g., runway ends at a sea wall or public road).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Safety Area Dimensions (FAA / ICAO Composite)

**Given Data (Assumed for a KTU numerical):**
- Runway length: $L_{\text{rwy}} = 3000\ \text{m}$
- Runway width: $W_{\text{rwy}} = 45\ \text{m}$
- Aerodrome Reference Code: **4** (large international airport)
- Runway classification: **Instrument (Precision Approach)**
- Compliance required for **both ICAO and FAA**

**Required:** Calculate the total Safety Area dimensions, total area, and the percentage expansion relative to the runway footprint.

### Step 1 — Longitudinal Extent (FAA RSA)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{RSA, FAA}} &= L_{\text{pre}} + L_{\text{rwy}} + L_{\text{post}} \\
&= 300\ \text{m} + 3000\ \text{m} + 600\ \text{m} \\
&= 3900\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The FAA RSA extends 300 m before the threshold, encompasses the full 3000 m runway, and extends 600 m beyond the end. This gives the **total longitudinal Safety Area length**.

### Step 2 — Longitudinal Extent (ICAO Strip + RESA)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{ICAO, total}} &= L_{\text{pre, strip}} + L_{\text{rwy}} + L_{\text{strip end}} + L_{\text{RESA}} \\
&= 60\ \text{m} + 3000\ \text{m} + 60\ \text{m} + 300\ \text{m} \\
&= 3420\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The ICAO strip extends 60 m before threshold and 60 m beyond the runway end. The RESA adds 300 m beyond the strip. Total longitudinal buffer = **3420 m**.

### Step 3 — Lateral Width (Both Frameworks, Code 4)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{SA}} &= 2 \cdot W_{1/2} = 2 \cdot 75\ \text{m} = 150\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** For a Code 4 runway, the lateral extent is **75 m on each side of the centerline**, giving a total Safety Area width of **150 m**.

### Step 4 — Total Safety Area (FAA RSA)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{RSA, FAA}} &= L_{\text{RSA, FAA}} \times W_{\text{SA}} \\
&= 3900\ \text{m} \times 150\ \text{m} \\
&= 585{,}000\ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Area = Length × Width. This represents the **full geometric footprint of the FAA Runway Safety Area**.

### Step 5 — Runway Footprint for Comparison

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{rwy}} &= L_{\text{rwy}} \times W_{\text{rwy}} \\
&= 3000\ \text{m} \times 45\ \text{m} \\
&= 135{,}000\ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This is the geometric area of the runway pavement alone, used as a **reference baseline**.

### Step 6 — Percentage Expansion Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%_{\text{exp}} &= \frac{A_{\text{RSA, FAA}} - A_{\text{rwy}}}{A_{\text{rwy}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{585{,}000 - 135{,}000}{135{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{450{,}000}{135{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 333.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The Safety Area is **333% larger** than the runway footprint itself. This dramatically illustrates that airport land-use planning is dominated by **safety buffers**, not the runway pavement.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Slope Verification

**Given:** A RESA 300 m long, with a downstream elevation **3 m lower** than the runway end.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{\text{long}} &= \frac{\Delta h}{L_{\text{RESA}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3\ \text{m}}{300\ \text{m}} \times 100 \\
&= 1.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The computed longitudinal slope is **1.0%**, which satisfies the **≤ 1.5%** ICAO limit. The design is compliant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why slopes matter:** A slope exceeding 1.5% longitudinally can cause an aircraft to **tumble, pitch, or become airborne again** during an overrun event, transforming a survivable excursion into a catastrophic one.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ICAO vs FAA

| Design Aspect | ICAO (Annex 14) | FAA (AC 150/5300-13A) | KTU Board Preference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Terminology** | Runway Strip + RESA | Runway Safety Area (RSA) | Both; state the framework |
| **Lateral width (Code 4)** | $150\ \text{m}$ (75 m each side) | $150\ \text{m}$ (75 m each side) | Match — both 150 m |
| **Pre-threshold extent** | $60\ \text{m}$ | $300\ \text{m}$ (1000 ft) | Distinguish explicitly |
| **Post-end extent** | $60\ \text{m}$ (min), $300\ \text{m}$ (recommended) | $600\ \text{m}$ (2000 ft) | Distinguish explicitly |
| **Slope limit (longitudinal)** | $\leq 1.5\%$ | $\leq 1.5\%$ | Same |
| **Slope limit (transverse)** | $\leq 5\%$ (graded) | $\leq 5\%$ (graded) | Same |
| **Frangibility** | Required | Required | Same |
| **EMAS equivalent** | Acceptable as RESA alternative | Acceptable as RSA equivalent | Note in Part B answers |
| **Surface load-bearing** | Must support RFF vehicles | Must support aircraft, RFF vehicles | Same intent |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Safety Area Zone Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Runway Safety Area Master Zone] --> nodeB[Pre-Threshold Buffer Zone]
    nodeA[Runway Safety Area Master Zone] --> nodeC[Lateral Buffer Zone Left]
    nodeA[Runway Safety Area Master Zone] --> nodeD[Runway Pavement Zone]
    nodeA[Runway Safety Area Master Zone] --> nodeE[Lateral Buffer Zone Right]
    nodeA[Runway Safety Area Master Zone] --> nodeF[Post-End Buffer Zone]

    nodeB[Pre-Threshold Buffer Zone] --> nodeB1[ICAO Strip 60 m]
    nodeB[Pre-Threshold Buffer Zone] --> nodeB2[FAA RSA 300 m]

    nodeC[Lateral Buffer Zone Left] --> nodeC1[Half Width 75 m Code 4]
    nodeC[Lateral Buffer Zone Left] --> nodeC2[Cleared and Graded Area]
    nodeC[Lateral Buffer Zone Left] --> nodeC3[Frangible NAVAID Zone]

    nodeD[Runway Pavement Zone] --> nodeD1[Runway Length Lrwy]
    nodeD[Runway Pavement Zone] --> nodeD2[Runway Width Wrwy]
    nodeD[Runway Pavement Zone] --> nodeD3[Runway Shoulder]

    nodeE[Lateral Buffer Zone Right] --> nodeE1[Half Width 75 m Code 4]
    nodeE[Lateral Buffer Zone Right] --> nodeE2[Cleared and Graded Area]
    nodeE[Lateral Buffer Zone Right] --> nodeE3[Frangible NAVAID Zone]

    nodeF[Post-End Buffer Zone] --> nodeF1[ICAO Strip 60 m]
    nodeF[Post-End Buffer Zone] --> nodeF2[ICAO RESA 90 m min]
    nodeF[Post-End Buffer Zone] --> nodeF3[ICAO RESA 300 m recommended]
    nodeF[Post-End Buffer Zone] --> nodeF4[FAA RSA 600 m]
    nodeF[Post-End Buffer Zone] --> nodeF5[EMAS Optional Substitute]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Functional Architecture — Transverse Profile of Safety Area

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP[Runway Centerline] --> nodeQ[Runway Edge 22.5 m]
    nodeQ[Runway Edge 22.5 m] --> nodeR[Runway Shoulder 7.5 m]
    nodeR[Runway Shoulder 7.5 m] --> nodeS[Cleared and Graded Area]
    nodeS[Cleared and Graded Area] --> nodeT[Frangible NAVAID Zone]
    nodeT[Frangible NAVAID Zone] --> nodeU[Outer Safety Area Limit 75 m]
    nodeU[Outer Safety Area Limit 75 m] --> nodeV[Non-Critical Zone beyond RSA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The transverse profile shows how the **75 m lateral half-width** is functionally subdivided from the runway edge outward. The first segment (runway shoulder, ~7.5 m) is paved or stabilized to support the runway edge. The **Cleared and Graded Area** is the safety-critical zone, free of obstacles and graded to slope limits. The outer 75 m boundary marks the **legally defined RSA limit**.

## 4.3 Longitudinal Functional Architecture — Runway End and RESA Profile

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeAA[Runway Pavement End] --> nodeBB[Runway Strip Extension 60 m]
    nodeBB[Runway Strip Extension 60 m] --> nodeCC[RESA Start Point]
    nodeCC[RESA Start Point] --> nodeDD[RESA Minimum 90 m]
    nodeDD[RESA Minimum 90 m] --> nodeEE[RESA Recommended 300 m]
    nodeEE[RESA Recommended 300 m] --> nodeFF[Optional EMAS Bed Location]
    nodeFF[Optional EMAS Bed Location] --> nodeGG[FAA RSA Full 600 m Boundary]
    nodeGG[FAA RSA Full 600 m Boundary] --> nodeHH[Property / Obstacle Limit]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Runway End Safety Area (RESA) as per ICAO. State its minimum and recommended longitudinal extent. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Runway End Safety Area (RESA)** is a symmetrical area extending from the end of the runway strip, designed to reduce the risk of damage to an aircraft in the event of an undershoot or overrun.

As per **ICAO Annex 14, Vol. I**:
- **Minimum longitudinal extent:** $L_{\text{RESA, min}} = 90\ \text{m}$ from the end of the runway strip
- **Recommended longitudinal extent:** $L_{\text{RESA, rec}} = 300\ \text{m}$ (where practicable)
- **Width:** At least twice the runway width, symmetric about the extended runway centerline

### Question 2: What is meant by "frangibility" in the context of the Safety Area? Give two examples. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Frangibility** refers to the design characteristic of an object (typically a navigational aid) that causes it to **break, deform, or yield at a predetermined impact force**, thereby minimizing damage to an aircraft that may strike it during an excursion into the Safety Area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ICAO Criterion:** A frangible object must fail when subjected to a static load of not more than **45 kN** applied at the most critical point, at or below **1.22 m** above ground level.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Runway edge lights** — mounted on frangible couplings that shear on impact.
2. **Approach lighting masts** — designed to break away cleanly upon aircraft contact.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. The model answers below provide full worked solutions suitable for **ESE valuation scripts**.

### Question A: Design of Runway Safety Area (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** For a Code 4 precision-instrument runway of length **3500 m** and width **45 m**, determine the longitudinal and lateral dimensions of the Safety Area as per both ICAO and FAA standards. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Code Classification:**
- Aerodrome Reference Code: **4** (runway length > 1800 m, intended for large aircraft).
- Runway type: **Instrument (Precision)**.

**Step 2 — ICAO Longitudinal Dimensions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{pre, strip}} &= 60\ \text{m (non-instrument) ; up to 300 m recommended for precision} \\
L_{\text{rwy}} &= 3500\ \text{m} \\
L_{\text{strip end}} &= 60\ \text{m} \\
L_{\text{RESA, rec}} &= 300\ \text{m} \\
\Rightarrow L_{\text{ICAO, total}} &= 60 + 3500 + 60 + 300 = 3920\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — FAA RSA Longitudinal Dimensions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{RSA, FAA}} &= L_{\text{pre, RSA}} + L_{\text{rwy}} + L_{\text{post, RSA}} \\
&= 300 + 3500 + 600 = 4400\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Lateral Width (Both Frameworks, Code 4):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{SA}} &= 2 \times 75 = 150\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tabulated Result:**

| Framework | Longitudinal Extent | Lateral Width |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ICAO | $3920\ \text{m}$ (with recommended RESA) | $150\ \text{m}$ |
| FAA | $4400\ \text{m}$ | $150\ \text{m}$ |

**(b)** Calculate the **total geometric area of the FAA RSA** and express it as a **percentage** of the runway footprint. Comment on the engineering significance of this ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — FAA RSA Area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{RSA, FAA}} &= L_{\text{RSA, FAA}} \times W_{\text{SA}} \\
&= 4400 \times 150 \\
&= 660{,}000\ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Runway Footprint:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{rwy}} &= 3500 \times 45 \\
&= 157{,}500\ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%_{\text{RSA/rwy}} &= \frac{A_{\text{RSA, FAA}}}{A_{\text{rwy}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{660{,}000}{157{,}500} \times 100 \\
&= 419.05\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Significance:**

> The Safety Area is approximately **419% of the runway pavement area** — i.e., the safety footprint is over **4 times** the runway itself. This underscores the principle that **land-use planning for safety dominates airport geometric design**, far outweighing the actual paved surface. For a 3500 m Code 4 runway, the airport must reserve a buffer zone equivalent to **over 66 hectares** purely for safety, not counting parallel taxiways, service roads, or drainage reserves.

---

### Question B: Safety Area Slopes, Frangibility, and EMAS (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **slope constraints** in the Safety Area, distinguishing between longitudinal and transverse slopes. Why is the longitudinal slope limit (1.5%) more stringent than the transverse slope limit (5%)? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Slope Definitions:**
- **Longitudinal slope ($s_{\text{long}}$):** The change in elevation per unit length measured **along** the runway centerline (or its extension into the Safety Area).
- **Transverse slope ($s_{\text{trans}}$):** The change in elevation per unit width measured **perpendicular** to the runway centerline (across the lateral Safety Area).

**Step 2 — ICAO / FAA Limits:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{\text{long, max}} &= 1.5\% \quad \text{(more stringent)} \\
s_{\text{trans, max}} &= 5.0\% \quad \text{(relaxed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Physical Reasoning for Stricter Longitudinal Limit:**

- An aircraft in a **longitudinal overrun** is moving **parallel** to the centerline. A positive (downward) longitudinal slope **assists deceleration by gravity**, but if too steep, can cause the aircraft to **nose-dive, become airborne, or pitch violently**.
- A negative (upward) longitudinal slope **opposes motion** and can cause the aircraft to **tumble backwards** if the landing gear catches.
- Therefore, the limit is kept conservative at **1.5%** to balance gravitational deceleration benefit against loss of aircraft control.

**Step 4 — Physical Reasoning for Relaxed Transverse Limit:**

- In a **lateral excursion (veer-off)**, the aircraft is moving **sideways** relative to the runway. A modest transverse slope encourages the aircraft to **drift away** from the active runway, returning to a safe area.
- A steeper transverse slope (up to 5%) is acceptable because lateral drainage requirements demand it, and a veering aircraft will not pitch longitudinally due to cross-slope.

**(b)** What is an **EMAS (Engineered Materials Arrestor System)**? Under what conditions is it permitted as a substitute for the full RSA, and what are the critical design parameters? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Definition:**

An **Engineered Materials Arrestor System (EMAS)** is a bed of low-density, crushable cellular cementitious material installed at the end of a runway, beyond the paved surface, designed to **decelerate an aircraft that overruns the runway** by exerting a controlled, predictable drag force on the landing gear.

**Step 2 — When EMAS is Permitted:**

EMAS is permitted by the **FAA** (per AC 150/5220-22B) and accepted by **ICAO** as an equivalent level of safety when the full RSA dimensions cannot be achieved due to site constraints such as:
- A runway end abutting a **public road, railway, or water body**.
- A runway end constrained by **incompatible land use** (e.g., dense urban development).
- **Topographical impossibility** (e.g., cliff face or deep ravine within the RSA footprint).

**Step 3 — Critical Design Parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Arrestor bed length } (L_{\text{EMAS}}) &= \text{Aircraft-specific, typically } 60\ \text{m to } 120\ \text{m} \\
\text{Material density} &= 240 \text{ to } 320\ \text{kg/m}^3 \text{ (low-density cellular concrete)} \\
\text{Compressive strength} &= 0.35 \text{ to } 0.70\ \text{MPa} \text{ (crushes progressively under load)} \\
\text{Drag force} &= \text{Calibrated per aircraft type (e.g., B737, B747, A320)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Real-World Validation:**

> The **most-cited validation** is the 2013 Asiana Airlines Flight 214 overrun at SFO, where a Boeing 777-200ER overran Runway 28L, entered the EMAS bed, and was decelerated with **no fatalities among 307 occupants**. This event led the FAA to **mandate EMAS** at all certificated airports where full RSA is infeasible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
