   - Grading ensures water is shed off the **Pavement Surface (PS)** within a *Time of Concentration* of less than **30 minutes** (ICAO recommendation for porous friction courses).
   - Without proper grading, water infiltrates pavement layers → causes **pumping, settlement, and stripping** in bituminous layers.

2. **Operational Safety (Aeronautical Function):**
   - Cross-winds combined with standing water = **hydroplaning risk**.
   - Transverse grades must be uniform to prevent aircraft from experiencing **asymmetric lift and rolling moments** during touchdown.
   - Longitudinal grades must be limited so that a landing aircraft does not overshoot or float excessively.

3. **Structural Integrity (Geotechnical Function):**
   - Uniform subgrade support reduces **differential settlement**.
   - Properly compacted graded surfaces prevent **frost heave** in cold climates by ensuring proper drainage of the sub-base.
   - Poor grading → water ingress → **loss of CBR strength** of the subgrade.

4. **Geometric Compliance (Regulatory Function):**
   - ICAO Annex 14, Vol. I, Chapter 3 specifies the maximum permissible grades.
   - FAA AC 150/5300-13A (Airport Design) prescribes the grading for the **Runway Safety Area (RSA)**, **Object Free Area (OFA)**, and **Runway Protection Zone (RPZ)**.

5. **Pavement Life (Economic Function):**
   - Correct grading extends pavement life by **20–30 %** by preventing water-induced failures.
   - Reduces **maintenance cost** of resurfacing and patching.

---

### 2.2 The Engineering Logic of Grading — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The process of grading is essentially a *geometric transformation* of the natural ground:

**Step 1 — Site Survey and Geotechnical Investigation**
- Establish a **grid of spot levels** (typically 10 m × 10 m or 20 m × 20 m depending on terrain).
- Determine the natural ground elevation at every grid point.
- Identify soil type, water table, and bearing capacity (CBR).

**Step 2 — Setting the Design Profiles**
- Fix the **Runway Profile Grade Line (PGL)** along the runway centerline.
- Fix the **transverse slopes** (1.5 % crown or 1.0 % uniform).
- Compute the **Formation Level (FL)** at every grid point:

$$FL = PGL - \text{slope} \times \text{horizontal offset}$$

**Step 3 — Cut and Fill Calculation**
- For each grid point, calculate: 
$$\text{Cut} = NG - FL \quad (\text{if } NG > FL)$$
$$\text{Fill} = FL - NG \quad (\text{if } FL > NG)$$
- Plot **contour lines** of cut and fill to determine the **balancing line** (zero cut/fill line).

**Step 4 — Mass Haul Diagram**
- Plot cumulative cut (positive) and cumulative fill (negative) versus station.
- Identify **borrow pits** and **waste areas** to balance the earthwork economically.

**Step 5 — Execution by Earthmoving Equipment**
- Use **scrapers, motor graders, bulldozers, and compactors**.
- Achieve **95 % Modified Proctor compaction** (FAA standard for airfield subgrade).

**Step 6 — Surface Drainage Integration**
- Provide **gullies, inlets, manholes** at designed spacing.
- Provide **longitudinal side ditches** beyond the pavement edge.

**Step 7 — Quality Control (QC) and Quality Assurance (QA)**
- Verify grades using **total station / GNSS survey**.
- Check **cross-slope** at every 30 m station.
- Perform **field density test** and **sand replacement test** for compaction.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **key formulas, geometric limits, and standards** that are repeatedly tested in KTU examinations.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------|------------------------|------|---------|
| 1 | Longitudinal grade of runway | $\leq 1.0\,\%$ (FAA) or $\leq 1.5\,\%$ (ICAO) | % | First/Last quarter of runway may be up to 0.8 % (FAA) |
| 2 | Transverse grade (Runway crown) | $1.5\,\%$ to $2.0\,\%$ | % | Sloping away from centerline |
| 3 | Longitudinal grade (Taxiway) | $\leq 3.0\,\%$ | % | ICAO Annex 14 |
| 4 | Transverse grade (Taxiway) | $1.5\,\%$ to $2.0\,\%$ | % | Uniform one-way slope (no crown) |
| 5 | Apron grade | $1.0\,\%$ to $2.0\,\%$ | % | Sloped toward drainage gully |
| 6 | Shoulder transverse grade | $2.0\,\%$ to $3.0\,\%$ | % | For stabilized shoulder |
| 7 | RSA longitudinal grade | $\leq 5\,\%$ (changes in grade) | % | Runway Safety Area grading (FAA) |
| 8 | RSA transverse grade | $\leq 3\,\%$ to $5\,\%$ | % | FAA AC 150/5300-13A |
| 9 | Cut at grid point | $C_i = NG_i - FL_i$ | m | If $C_i > 0$ → cut |
| 10 | Fill at grid point | $F_i = FL_i - NG_i$ | m | If $F_i > 0$ → fill |
| 11 | Formation Level (offset) | $FL = PGL \pm s \cdot x$ | m | $s$ = slope, $x$ = offset |
| 12 | Volume (end-area method) | $V = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (A_1 + A_2) \cdot L$ | m³ | Between two cross-sections |
| 13 | Compaction requirement | $\geq 95\,\%$ Modified Proctor | % | FAA subgrade standard |
| 14 | Time of concentration (drainage) | $\leq 30$ minutes | min | ICAO P-401 (porous friction course) |
| 15 | Drainage pipe slope (storm sewer) | $\geq 0.3\,\%$ to $0.5\,\%$ | % | Minimum self-cleansing velocity |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In actual airport projects, grading is performed **before** any pavement layer is laid. A poorly graded subgrade is the single largest cause of:
- Premature **rutting** in asphalt runways.
- **Pumping** of fines through concrete joints.
- **Hydroplaning** accidents.

For example, **Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport (Mumbai)** sits on reclaimed land and required a near-perfect **uniform 1 % cross-fall** over a 3.9 km runway — a grading tolerance of $\pm 25$ mm over the entire runway width of **60 m**. This level of precision is achievable only through modern **GPS-guided motor graders (e.g., Caterpillar AccuGrade™)** combined with **GNSS-based total stations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **practical engineering utility** of grading is therefore threefold: (1) hydrological safety, (2) structural durability, and (3) regulatory compliance. KTU exams expect students to identify which justification applies in a given scenario.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem — Determining Formation Levels and Earthwork Quantities

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
A **30 m wide runway** is to be constructed on a subgrade. The **Profile Grade Line (PGL)** is fixed at elevation **100.000 m** at station **0+000**, with a **+0.5 % longitudinal grade**. The runway has a **crown** with a **2.0 % transverse slope** falling away from the centerline on both sides. Determine the **Formation Levels (FL)** at:
- Station 0+060, 5 m left of centerline
- Station 0+060, 15 m right of centerline
- Station 0+120, 15 m right of centerline

Given that natural ground (NG) at the same points are:
- (0+060, 5L) → NG = 100.150 m
- (0+060, 15R) → NG = 99.850 m
- (0+120, 15R) → NG = 100.100 m

**Compute cut/fill at each point and the cumulative earthwork between sections.**

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the PGL elevation at each station.**

The longitudinal grade is **+0.5 %** (rising in the direction of increasing station).

$$PGL_{60} = PGL_{0} + (0.5\%) \times 60$$
$$PGL_{60} = 100.000 + 0.005 \times 60$$
$$PGL_{60} = 100.000 + 0.300 = 100.300 \text{ m}$$

$$PGL_{120} = 100.000 + 0.005 \times 120$$
$$PGL_{120} = 100.000 + 0.600 = 100.600 \text{ m}$$

---

**Step 2 — Apply the transverse slope correction.**

The runway crown is at the centerline. The transverse slope of **2.0 %** falls *away* from the centerline. So at a horizontal offset $x$ from the centerline:

$$FL = PGL - s \cdot \vert x \vert$$

where $s = 2.0\% = 0.02$.

**Point A:** Station 0+060, 5 m **left** of centerline ($x = 5$ m)
$$FL_A = 100.300 - 0.02 \times 5 = 100.300 - 0.100 = 100.200 \text{ m}$$

**Point B:** Station 0+060, 15 m **right** of centerline ($x = 15$ m)
$$FL_B = 100.300 - 0.02 \times 15 = 100.300 - 0.300 = 100.000 \text{ m}$$

**Point C:** Station 0+120, 15 m **right** of centerline ($x = 15$ m)
$$FL_C = 100.600 - 0.02 \times 15 = 100.600 - 0.300 = 100.300 \text{ m}$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute Cut / Fill at each point.**

Using the rule:
$$\text{Cut} = NG - FL \quad (\text{positive value means cut})$$
$$\text{Fill} = FL - NG \quad (\text{positive value means fill})$$

**Point A:** Station 0+060, 5L
$$Cut_A = 100.150 - 100.200 = -0.050 \text{ m} \Rightarrow \text{Fill} = 0.050 \text{ m}$$

**Point B:** Station 0+060, 15R
$$Cut_B = 99.850 - 100.000 = -0.150 \text{ m} \Rightarrow \text{Fill} = 0.150 \text{ m}$$

**Point C:** Station 0+120, 15R
$$Cut_C = 100.100 - 100.300 = -0.200 \text{ m} \Rightarrow \text{Fill} = 0.200 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention adopted in this solution:**
> - **Positive Cut** = earth must be excavated.
> - **Positive Fill** = earth must be added.
> - All three points in this example require **FILL** (the natural ground is *below* the formation level).

---

**Step 4 — Tabulate the Analytical Matrix.**

| Point | Station | Offset (m) | PGL (m) | FL (m) | NG (m) | Cut (m) | Fill (m) |
|-------|---------|------------|---------|--------|--------|---------|----------|
| A | 0+060 | 5 L | 100.300 | 100.200 | 100.150 | — | 0.050 |
| B | 0+060 | 15 R | 100.300 | 100.000 | 99.850 | — | 0.150 |
| C | 0+120 | 15 R | 100.600 | 100.300 | 100.100 | — | 0.200 |

---

**Step 5 — Earthwork Volume Computation (End-Area Method).**

Let us compute the volume of fill between Section 0+060 and Section 0+120 at the **15 m Right** offset. We assume the typical cross-section between two stations is approximated linearly.

The cross-sectional area of fill at a section is:
$$A = \sum (Fill_i \times w_i)$$

where $w_i$ is the width over which the fill applies. For a 30 m wide runway with crown, the fill "block" can be approximated.

For a single line of points:
- Area at Section 0+060 (15R) = $0.150 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m (unit width)} = 0.150 \text{ m}^2$
- Area at Section 0+120 (15R) = $0.200 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m (unit width)} = 0.200 \text{ m}^2$

Distance between sections: $L = 120 - 60 = 60 \text{ m}$

Volume by average end-area method:
$$V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (A_1 + A_2) \cdot L$$
$$V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (0.150 + 0.200) \cdot 60$$
$$V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.350 \cdot 60$$
$$V = 10.50 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (per metre width)}$$

---

**Step 6 — Interpretation of the Result.**

This means that for every **1 m width** of pavement at the 15 m right offset, approximately **10.5 m³ of earth** must be imported as fill and compacted to achieve the design formation level. In practice, the contractor multiplies this by the full pavement width to obtain the total fill quantity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compaction shrinkage factor:** A 15–20 % bulking factor is typically added because excavated soil compacts to a smaller volume than its loose state. The final *imported fill volume* would therefore be: $V_{actual} = 10.50 \times 1.18 \approx 12.39 \text{ m}^3$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Crown Formation Level Formula

For a **symmetrical crowned runway** of width $W$ and crown slope $s_c$ (rising from centerline outward? No — falling from centerline outward):

$$FL(y) = PGL - s_c \cdot \left\vert y \right\vert$$

where $y$ is the lateral offset from the runway centerline. The maximum offset is $y = \pm W/2$, and the elevation difference between the centerline and the runway edge is:

$$\Delta h_{edge} = s_c \cdot \frac{W}{2}$$

For a 45 m wide runway with 2 % crown:
$$\Delta h_{edge} = 0.02 \times 22.5 = 0.45 \text{ m}$$

This is the elevation difference the survey team must physically set out at the edge of the runway.

---

### 3.4 Cumulative Earthwork Balance — Conceptual Check

The **mass haul diagram** of a balanced grading project has equal areas of cut and fill. For a runway on flat terrain, designers often target a *cut-fill ratio* close to **1.0** to minimize borrow or waste.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of Airport Grading Operations

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end operational flow** of the grading activity — from initial survey to final quality check. It illustrates how each stage of the grading process feeds into the next, and where quality control gates are placed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Survey and Geotech Investigation] --> B[Establish Profile Grade Line PGL]
    B --> C[Fix Transverse Crown and Longitudinal Slope]
    C --> D[Compute Formation Levels FL at Grid Points]
    D --> E[Compute Cut and Fill per Grid]
    E --> F[Plot Mass Haul Diagram]
    F --> G[Identify Borrow and Waste Areas]
    G --> H[Execute Earthwork with Scrapers and Graders]
    H --> I[Compact Subgrade to 95 percent Modified Proctor]
    I --> J[Install Surface and Subsurface Drainage]
    J --> K[Final Survey Verification and QC Check]
    K --> L{QC Pass?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Pavement Construction Begins]
    L -- No --> H[Regrade and Recompact]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the diagram top-to-bottom:** every block represents a sequential operation in the grading process. The decision node **L** acts as a quality gate, ensuring that no substandard work proceeds to pavement laying.

---

### 4.2 Schematic — Runway Cross-Section with Crown and Drainage

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **cross-sectional architecture** of a graded runway, mapping the geometric relationships between the crown, the transverse slope, the shoulder, and the side drainage ditch.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT_HALF["Runway Left Side"]
        L1[Drainage Ditch Beyond RSA] --> L2[Runway Safety Area RSA Graded at 3 to 5 percent]
        L2 --> L3[Shoulder Graded at 2 to 3 percent]
        L3 --> L4[Runway Edge Elevation Lower than Centerline]
    end
    subgraph CROWN["Runway Centerline Crown"]
        C1[PGL at Elevation 100.000 m] --> C2[Crown High Point at Centerline]
    end
    subgraph RIGHT_HALF["Runway Right Side"]
        R1[Runway Edge Elevation Lower than Centerline] --> R2[Shoulder Graded at 2 to 3 percent]
        R2 --> R3[Runway Safety Area RSA]
        R3 --> R4[Drainage Ditch at Outer Boundary]
    end
    C2 --> L4
    C2 --> R1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from the schematic:** The runway crown acts as a *watershed line* — water shed from the crown flows laterally across the runway, off the shoulder, across the safety area, and finally into the perimeter drainage ditch. The grading must be *continuous and uniform* in this direction; any local depression (sag) will cause water to pond and is unacceptable.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Cut-Fill Balance Logic

The following diagram shows the **decision logic** for classifying earthwork as cut, fill, or balanced, which is fundamental in airport grading design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Grid Point with NG and FL] --> Q{NG greater than FL?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[CUT Excavation Required]
    Q -- No --> S{FL greater than NG?}
    S -- Yes --> T[FILL Embankment Required]
    S -- No --> U[NO EARTHWORK Balanced Point]
    R --> V[Add to Total Cut Volume]
    T --> W[Add to Total Fill Volume]
    U --> X[Skip Both Volumes]
```

This logic is implemented in **digital terrain modelling (DTM) software** such as **AutoCAD Civil 3D**, **Bentley OpenRoads**, or **Trimble Business Center**, which the modern airport engineer uses for mass earthwork calculations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> *"Why is proper grading essential in the design and construction of an airport runway? List any **three** key reasons."*

**Model Answer:**

Airport grading is essential for the following reasons:

1. **Surface Drainage:** Grading ensures that rainwater is rapidly shed from the runway surface into side ditches, preventing the formation of standing water that can cause **hydroplaning** of aircraft tyres.

2. **Operational Safety:** Uniform transverse and longitudinal grades ensure that aircraft experience predictable lift and braking characteristics during takeoff and landing, especially on wet runways.

3. **Pavement Durability:** Proper grading prevents water infiltration into pavement layers, thereby protecting the subgrade from **loss of bearing capacity (CBR)** and extending the **fatigue life** of both flexible and rigid pavements.

*(Additional valid points: regulatory compliance with ICAO/FAA, frost heave prevention in cold regions, and reduction in maintenance costs.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> *"State the ICAO recommended values for (a) maximum longitudinal grade of a runway, and (b) the standard transverse slope (crown) of a runway."*

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) Maximum longitudinal grade of runway:** **1.5 %** (as per ICAO Annex 14, Vol. I).
  *(FAA AC 150/5300-13A recommends a stricter value of 1.0 %.)*

- **(b) Standard transverse slope (crown) of runway:** **1.5 % to 2.0 %** falling away from the runway centerline towards both edges.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "1 % longitudinal grade for ICAO" is **incorrect**. ICAO specifies **1.5 %**. FAA specifies the tighter **1.0 %**. Examiners strictly differentiate between the two codes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
>
> *"With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **operations involved in airport grading**. Discuss the importance of grading with reference to **(a) drainage**, **(b) pavement life**, and **(c) safety**."* [14 Marks]

**Distribution of Marks (As per KTU Valuation Key):**

- Part (a) — Sketch and explanation of operations: **7 marks**
- Part (b) — Discussion of importance across three domains: **7 marks**

---

**Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Sketch (To be drawn in exam; textual description follows):**

The sketch should show a **cross-section of a runway** with:
- A crown at the centerline (PGL).
- A transverse slope of **1.5–2.0 %** on both sides.
- The runway shoulder with a steeper slope of **2.0–3.0 %**.
- The **Runway Safety Area (RSA)** beyond the shoulder.
- A **perimeter drainage ditch** at the edge of the graded zone.
- A side view of the longitudinal profile showing a small grade (≤ 1.5 %).

**Operations involved in Airport Grading (Stepwise):**

1. **Site Reconnaissance and Survey:** Establish a grid (typically 20 m × 20 m) and record natural ground (NG) levels using total station / GNSS.

2. **Fixing the Profile Grade Line (PGL):** The PGL is set along the runway centerline such that the *longitudinal grade* does not exceed the ICAO limit of 1.5 %.

3. **Setting Transverse Slopes:** A **crown** is introduced at the centerline; the surface falls at 1.5–2.0 % towards each edge.

4. **Computing Formation Levels (FL):** Using the relation $FL = PGL \pm s \cdot x$ at every grid point.

5. **Earthwork Calculation:** Cut and fill volumes are computed using the **end-area method**:

   $$V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (A_1 + A_2) \cdot L$$

6. **Mass Haul Analysis:** Plot the cumulative cut-fill curve to optimize the use of excavated material and minimize borrow/waste.

7. **Execution and Compaction:** Earthmoving is done with motor graders and scrapers; compaction is verified to **95 % Modified Proctor**.

8. **Drainage Integration:** Surface inlets and side ditches are constructed concurrently.

9. **Quality Control:** Final survey and level checks ensure $\pm 25$ mm tolerance on the formation level.

**Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Importance of Airport Grading:**

**(i) Drainage (2–3 marks):**
A properly graded runway sheds water laterally within minutes. The **time of concentration** must be less than **30 minutes** (ICAO P-401). Water ponding on a runway can cause:
- **Hydroplaning** at aircraft speeds above **120 km/h**.
- **Aquaplaning** of high-speed aircraft.
- **Ingress of water** into pavement sub-layers, leading to stripping in bitumen and pumping in concrete.

**(ii) Pavement Life (2–3 marks):**
Water trapped in pavement layers is the single largest cause of premature failure. The **AASHO Road Test** and FAA accelerated pavement testing have demonstrated that a well-drained pavement lasts **30–40 % longer** than a poorly drained one. Grading:
- Maintains subgrade CBR.
- Prevents **frost heave** in cold climates.
- Reduces the **equivalent single axle load (ESAL)** damage rate.

**(iii) Safety (2 marks):**
- A uniform cross-fall prevents **asymmetric lift** on the aircraft.
- Longitudinal grade control prevents **overshooting** during landing.
- Proper grading of the **Runway Safety Area (RSA)** and **Runway End Safety Area (RESA)** ensures that an aircraft that runs off the runway does not impact an obstacle or suffer structural damage.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
>
> *"A 45 m wide runway is to be graded with a 2 % crown. The Profile Grade Line (PGL) at Station 0+000 is fixed at elevation 50.000 m with a longitudinal grade of +0.6 %. The runway is divided into a 10 m × 10 m grid.*
>
> *Determine the Formation Levels (FL) at the following points and calculate the cut or fill required if the natural ground (NG) levels are as given:*
>
> *Point P1: Station 0+100, 10 m left of centerline, NG = 50.450 m*
> *Point P2: Station 0+100, 20 m right of centerline, NG = 50.250 m*
> *Point P3: Station 0+200, 15 m left of centerline, NG = 50.850 m*
>
> *"Also comment on whether the grading complies with ICAO standards."* [14 Marks]

**Distribution of Marks:**
- Part (a) Computation of FL: **7 marks**
- Part (b) Cut/fill and ICAO compliance: **7 marks**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: PGL Elevation Computation**

Longitudinal grade $g_L = +0.6\,\% = 0.006$

$$PGL_{100} = 50.000 + 0.006 \times 100 = 50.000 + 0.600 = 50.600 \text{ m}$$

$$PGL_{200} = 50.000 + 0.006 \times 200 = 50.000 + 1.200 = 51.200 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Formation Level Computation**

Using $FL = PGL - s_c \cdot \vert x \vert$, with $s_c = 2\,\% = 0.02$:

**P1:** Station 0+100, $x = 10$ m left
$$FL_{P1} = 50.600 - 0.02 \times 10 = 50.600 - 0.200 = 50.400 \text{ m}$$

**P2:** Station 0+100, $x = 20$ m right
$$FL_{P2} = 50.600 - 0.02 \times 20 = 50.600 - 0.400 = 50.200 \text{ m}$$

**P3:** Station 0+200, $x = 15$ m left
$$FL_{P3} = 51.200 - 0.02 \times 15 = 51.200 - 0.300 = 50.900 \text{ m}$$

**Part (b) — Cut/Fill Calculation and ICAO Compliance (7 Marks):**

| Point | Station | Offset (m) | PGL (m) | FL (m) | NG (m) | Cut (m) | Fill (m) |
|-------|---------|------------|---------|--------|--------|---------|----------|
| P1 | 0+100 | 10 L | 50.600 | 50.400 | 50.450 | — | 0.050 |
| P2 | 0+100 | 20 R | 50.600 | 50.200 | 50.250 | — | 0.050 |
| P3 | 0+200 | 15 L | 51.200 | 50.900 | 50.850 | 0.050 | — |

**ICAO Compliance Check:**

- **Longitudinal grade = 0.6 %** → **Compliant** (ICAO maximum is 1.5 %).
- **Transverse slope = 2.0 %** → **Compliant** (ICAO maximum is 2.0 %).
- **Earthwork required:** Fill at P1 and P2, Cut at P3. The grading is **balanced locally**, indicating an efficient earthwork design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently make the mistake of writing $FL = PGL + s \cdot x$ (forgetting the *negative sign*). Since the crown is *high* at the centerline, all lateral points have FL **lower** than the PGL. A sign error here results in **complete loss of 2–3 marks** in the entire problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **Second Pitfall:** Students often quote "1 % longitudinal grade" as the ICAO standard. This is the **FAA** standard. The correct ICAO value is **1.5 %**. Mark deduction: 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Airport Grading**

- ✅ **Definition:** Airport grading = shaping the subgrade to prescribed slopes for drainage, safety, and compliance.

- ✅ **Three primary slopes to memorize:**
  - Longitudinal (Runway): **≤ 1.5 % (ICAO) / ≤ 1.0 % (FAA)**
  - Transverse (Runway): **1.5 % to 2.0 %**
  - Crown slope = same as transverse slope of runway.

- ✅ **Other gradient limits:**
  - Taxiway longitudinal: ≤ 3.0 %
  - Apron: 1.0 % to 2.0 %
  - Shoulder: 2.0 % to 3.0 %
  - RSA transverse: 3.0 % to 5.0 %

- ✅ **Five justifications for grading:** Drainage, Safety, Structural integrity, Geometric compliance, Pavement life.

- ✅ **Earthwork Calculation:**
  - $FL = PGL - s \cdot \vert x \vert$
  - $V = \frac{1}{2}(A_1 + A_2) \cdot L$ (Average end-area method)

- ✅ **Compaction standard:** 95 % Modified Proctor (FAA subgrade).

- ✅ **Drainage threshold:** Time of concentration ≤ 30 minutes (ICAO P-401).

- ✅ **Mass haul concept:** Plot cumulative cut vs. station; balance the curve around the zero line.

- ✅ **Key ICAO/FAA standards to differentiate:**
  - FAA is **stricter** on longitudinal grade (1.0 % vs 1.5 %).
  - ICAO permits **higher** transverse slope (2.0 % vs 1.5 % FAA maximum for crown).

- ✅ **Sign convention:** Positive Cut = excavate; Positive Fill = import.

- ✅ **Practical equipment to know:** Motor grader, scraper, bulldozer, vibratory compactor, total station, GNSS receiver.

- ✅ **Modern software tools:** AutoCAD Civil 3D, Bentley OpenRoads, Trimble Business Center, AGTEK Earthwork 4D.

> **End of Notes — PECET744 Module 3: Airport Grading — Importance and Operations**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Airport Drainage Aims
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Airport Drainage Aims

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

Airport drainage refers to the systematic collection, conveyance, and disposal of surface water and sub-surface water from the operational and non-operational areas of an airport in a controlled and efficient manner. The **Aims of Airport Drainage** constitute the set of functional, structural, and safety-oriented objectives that govern the planning, design, and maintenance of the entire drainage infrastructure of an airfield.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET744 — Module 3):**
> Airport drainage aims are the engineering objectives that ensure the **rapid removal of surface and subsurface water** from runways, taxiways, aprons, and surrounding land so as to preserve **pavement structural integrity**, ensure **aircraft operational safety**, prevent **flooding**, and eliminate **hydroplaning hazards** under all climatic conditions stipulated by the **ICAO Annex 14** and **FAA Advisory Circular 150/5320-6F** design guidelines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airport as a Giant Bath-Tub Roof"

Imagine an airport as a **large, flat, slightly tilted concrete roof** — except instead of stopping rain from entering a house, it has to:
1. **Catch** every drop of rain (water collection)
2. **Channel** it sideways quickly before aircraft land or take off (rapid conveyance)
3. **Drain** it into rivers, ponds, or soak pits away from the movement area (safe disposal)

If any of these three steps fail, water stands on the runway like a puddle on a road. For cars, a puddle is an inconvenience; for a landing aircraft travelling at **240–290 km/hr**, even a thin film of water as small as **3 mm** can cause the tires to lose contact with the pavement — a phenomenon known as **hydroplaning**. The aims of airport drainage are, therefore, the **engineering guarantees** that this never happens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Operational Fact:** A commercial jet lands with approximately **3,00,000 kg** of weight on its landing gear. Any water film that is not drained within **15 to 30 seconds** of rainfall cessation can critically compromise braking action.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source/Authority |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum allowable water film thickness on runway | **3 mm** | ICAO / FAA |
| Recommended runway cross-slope | **1.0% to 1.5%** | ICAO Annex 14 |
| Maximum cross-slope (for code letter A/B) | **2.0%** | FAA AC 150/5320-6F |
| Time to drain runway surface | **15 – 30 minutes** | ICAO |
| Design rainfall return period (pavement drainage) | **5 years** | FAA |
| Design rainfall return period (critical areas) | **10 – 50 years** | FAA |
| Manning's roughness coefficient (concrete gutter) | **0.013** | Standard hydraulic tables |
| Manning's roughness coefficient (asphalt gutter) | **0.016** | Standard hydraulic tables |

---

## 1.4 Primary Classification of Airport Drainage Aims

The aims of airport drainage can be broadly grouped into **four functional categories**:

1. **Safety Aims** — Protection of aircraft operations from hydroplaning and bird strikes
2. **Structural Aims** — Preservation of pavement life and subgrade stability
3. **Hydrological Aims** — Control of flooding and erosion in the airport catchment
4. **Environmental Aims** — Prevention of soil erosion, waterlogging of surrounding land, and pollution control

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Mnemonic — "SSHE"** for KTU exams:
> **S**afety → **S**tructure → **H**ydrology → **E**nvironment

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Enumeration of Airport Drainage Aims

### A. SAFETY AIMS

**Aim 1: Prevention of Hydroplaning (Aquaplaning)**

Hydroplaning occurs when a film of water builds up between the aircraft tire and the pavement surface, causing the tire to lift off and lose braking and steering control. The drainage system must:
- Limit water film thickness to less than **3 mm** under the design rainfall intensity.
- Provide sufficient cross-slope so water runs off laterally rather than ponding.
- Maintain texture depth (HSTP — High Speed Texture Spacing) of the pavement surface to break the water film.

The critical speed at which hydroplaning begins is given by:

$$V_{c} = 10.4 \sqrt{P}$$

Where:
- $V_{c}$ = Critical hydroplaning speed in **knots**
- $P$ = Tire inflation pressure in **psi**

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** A tire inflated to **150 psi** will begin to hydroplane at approximately **127 knots (~235 km/h)** — well within typical landing speeds of commercial aircraft.

**Aim 2: Prevention of Bird Strike Hazards**

Standing water bodies within or near the airfield attract waterfowl. The drainage design must:
- Avoid creating permanent ponds inside the operational boundary.
- Ensure that detention basins are located outside the **Runway End Safety Area (RESA)** and obstacle limitation surfaces.
- Cover or steepen the slopes of open channels to discourage bird nesting.

**Aim 3: Maintenance of Friction Coefficient**

Wet pavement drastically reduces the friction coefficient ($\mu$) between tire and runway. ICAO recommends that the **braking action** be reported as "Good" when $\mu \geq 0.34$, "Medium to Good" when $0.34 > \mu \geq 0.30$, and "Poor" when $\mu < 0.25$. Effective drainage keeps $\mu$ in the safe range.

---

### B. STRUCTURAL AIMS

**Aim 4: Preservation of Pavement Structural Integrity**

Water infiltration into the pavement layers is the single largest cause of premature pavement failure. The drainage aims to:
- Prevent saturation of the **subgrade** (which loses bearing capacity when soaked).
- Protect the **base course and sub-base course** from fines migration and pumping.
- Limit infiltration through pavement joints, cracks, and edges.

The California Bearing Ratio (CBR) of subgrade soil reduces with moisture content according to an empirical exponential relation:

$$CBR_{soaked} = CBR_{unsoaked} \cdot e^{-k(M_{s} - M_{o})}$$

Where:
- $CBR_{soaked}$ = Bearing ratio after soaking
- $CBR_{unsoaked}$ = Original bearing ratio
- $M_{s}$ = Saturation moisture content (%)
- $M_{o}$ = Optimum moisture content (%)
- $k$ = Soil-specific degradation constant (typically **0.05 – 0.15**)

**Aim 5: Prevention of Frost Heave and Freeze-Thaw Damage**

In cold climates, trapped water under the pavement freezes and expands, lifting and cracking the surface. Subsurface drainage systems aim to **lower the water table** below the frost line to prevent this.

**Aim 6: Prevention of Erosion of Pavement Edges**

Run-off water falling off the edge of the runway or taxiway can erode the shoulders. Drainage design must include proper shoulder grading and edge drains.

---

### C. HYDROLOGICAL AIMS

**Aim 7: Control of Flooding within the Airport Boundary**

The storm drainage system must be capable of carrying the **design rainfall intensity** without overflowing onto the operational surfaces. The hydrological aim is to size the drains using:

$$Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Peak run-off in **m³/s**
- $C$ = Run-off coefficient (rational method, dimensionless)
- $i$ = Rainfall intensity in **m/s** for chosen return period and time of concentration
- $A$ = Catchment area in **m²**

**Aim 8: Management of the Surrounding Catchment**

The airport must intercept and divert natural streams and overland flow that would otherwise pass through the airfield. This is achieved through:
- Diversion channels upstream of the runway strip
- Perimeter drainage around the airport boundary

**Aim 9: Prevention of Erosion in Surrounding Land**

High-velocity run-off can cut gullies in unprotected soil. The drainage aim is to limit flow velocities using:

$$V_{max} = \left( \frac{\tau_{allow}}{\rho \cdot g \cdot n^{1.5}} \right) \cdot R^{0.17} \cdot S^{0.5}$$

Where:
- $V_{max}$ = Maximum permissible channel velocity (m/s)
- $\tau_{allow}$ = Allowable tractive shear stress of channel lining (N/m²)
- $\rho$ = Density of water (1000 kg/m³)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient
- $R$ = Hydraulic radius (m)
- $S$ = Bed slope

---

### D. ENVIRONMENTAL AIMS

**Aim 10: Prevention of Waterlogging of Agricultural/Adjacent Land**

The airport's drainage outfall must not cause back-water logging of neighbouring land. This is achieved through proper **outfall design** and, if necessary, construction of **detention ponds**.

**Aim 11: Control of Pollution from Run-off**

Run-off from aprons may contain fuel, oil, de-icing chemicals, and other pollutants. Modern drainage aims include provision of **oil-water separators (OWS)** and **sand traps** before discharge.

---

## 2.2 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET / CHEAT SHEET

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rational Method Run-off | $Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$ | m³/s | Peak run-off estimation |
| 2 | Manning's Equation (open channel) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ | m³/s | Gutter and channel sizing |
| 3 | Manning's Equation (pipe) | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ | m/s | Storm sewer sizing |
| 4 | Hydroplaning Critical Speed | $V_{c} = 10.4 \sqrt{P}$ | knots | Aircraft safety check |
| 5 | Time of Concentration (Kirpich) | $t_{c} = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$ | minutes | Small catchment design |
| 6 | Storm Return Period (ICAO) | **5 to 50 years** | years | Drainage design intensity |
| 7 | Rainfall Intensity (IDF curve) | $i = \dfrac{a}{(t_{c} + b)^{c}}$ | mm/hr | Local IDF relation |
| 8 | Water Film Thickness Limit | $h \leq 3$ mm | mm | Hydroplaning prevention |
| 9 | Runway Cross-slope | $1.0\% - 1.5\%$ | % | Surface drainage slope |
| 10 | Run-off Coefficient $C$ (paved runway) | $0.8 - 0.95$ | dimensionless | Rational method |
| 11 | Run-off Coefficient $C$ (grass area) | $0.1 - 0.35$ | dimensionless | Rational method |
| 12 | Detention Pond Sizing | $V_{pond} = Q_{in} \cdot t_{det}} - Q_{out} \cdot t_{det}$ | m³ | Storage volume |
| 13 | Allowable Velocity in Turf Lined Channel | $1.5 - 1.8$ | m/s | Erosion control |
| 14 | Pipe Flow Full Capacity | $Q_{full} = \dfrac{0.3117}{n} \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ | m³/s | Storm sewer pipe |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember for KTU Numerical Problems:**
> Always convert rainfall intensity $i$ from **mm/hr** to **m/s** by multiplying with $\dfrac{1}{3.6 \times 10^{6}}$ before substituting into the Rational Method equation $Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The aims of airport drainage are not abstract academic objectives — they translate directly into:
- **Pavement Life Extension:** Properly drained runways last **20–30 years** versus **8–12 years** for poorly drained ones.
- **Operational Availability:** Drainage failures are the leading cause of runway closure during monsoon and snow-melt seasons.
- **Aircraft Accident Prevention:** Statistical analyses by NTSB and ICAO indicate that **wet runway excursions** account for nearly **30% of all runway incidents** worldwide — most of which are drainage-design related.
- **Cost Reduction:** Proper drainage reduces pavement maintenance costs by approximately **40–60%** over the design life.

---

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Rational Method for Run-off Estimation

The Rational Method is the cornerstone of airport storm drainage design for small catchments (typically $A < 50$ hectares, which covers most airport sub-catchments).

### Step 1 — Conceptual Basis

The Rational Method assumes that the **peak run-off rate** occurs when the **entire catchment** is contributing to the flow at the outlet, and that this happens when the rainfall duration equals the **time of concentration** ($t_c$).

### Step 2 — Statement of the Rational Equation

The fundamental form is:

$$Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Peak run-off rate (m³/s)
- $C$ = Run-off coefficient (dimensionless, $0 \leq C \leq 1$)
- $i$ = Rainfall intensity (m/s) for duration = $t_c$ and chosen return period
- $A$ = Catchment area (m²)

### Step 3 — Derivation from Continuity

Consider a catchment of area $A$ receiving rainfall at uniform intensity $i$ for a duration equal to $t_c$. The volume of water falling on the catchment per unit time is:

$$V_{in} = i \cdot A$$

A fraction $C$ of this water becomes surface run-off (the remainder is lost to infiltration, evaporation, depression storage, and interception):

$$V_{runoff} = C \cdot i \cdot A$$

Since run-off rate is volume per unit time:

$$\boxed{Q = C \cdot i \cdot A}$$

### Step 4 — Unit Conversion Requirement

If $i$ is given in **mm/hr** and $A$ in **hectares (ha)**, $Q$ comes out in **m³/s** only after conversion:

$$Q \, (\text{m}^3/\text{s}) = \dfrac{C \cdot i \, (\text{mm/hr}) \cdot A \, (\text{ha})}{360}$$

This is the form most commonly used in KTU numerical problems.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A runway catch basin collects run-off from a paved area of **2.5 hectares**. The area includes **0.8 ha of concrete runway** ($C = 0.9$), **0.6 ha of asphalt taxiway** ($C = 0.85$), and **1.1 ha of grass infield** ($C = 0.25$). The rainfall intensity for a 5-year return period and 20-minute duration is **90 mm/hr**. Determine the peak run-off.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute weighted $C$:

$$C_{avg} = \dfrac{(0.9 \times 0.8) + (0.85 \times 0.6) + (0.25 \times 1.1)}{2.5}$$

$$C_{avg} = \dfrac{0.72 + 0.51 + 0.275}{2.5} = \dfrac{1.505}{2.5} = 0.602$$

Step 2: Apply Rational Formula:

$$Q = \dfrac{C_{avg} \cdot i \cdot A}{360}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{0.602 \times 90 \times 2.5}{360}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{135.45}{360} = 0.376 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Step 3: Convert to litres/second for drainage inlet design:

$$Q = 376.25 \, \text{L/s}$$

This is the design flow that the storm drain inlet must be capable of receiving.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Manning's Equation for Open Channel Flow

### Step 1 — Empirical Foundation

Robert Manning (1890) derived the empirical equation for uniform open channel flow using the Chezy formula:

$$V = C \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot S}$$

Where $C$ is the Chezy coefficient. Manning replaced $C$ with $\dfrac{R^{1/6}}{n}$, giving:

$$V = \dfrac{R^{1/6}}{n} \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot S} = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

### Step 2 — Discharge Form

Multiplying by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$Q = A \cdot V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

### Step 3 — Application to a Triangular Airport Gutter

Airport pavements commonly use a **triangular gutter section** formed by the runway cross-slope and the shoulder slope. For a triangular gutter with side slopes $z_1 : 1$ and $z_2 : 1$ and depth $y$:

$$A = \dfrac{y^2}{2} (z_1 + z_2)$$

$$P = y (\sqrt{1 + z_1^{2}} + \sqrt{1 + z_2^{2}})$$

$$R = \dfrac{A}{P}$$

The discharge capacity of the triangular gutter becomes:

$$Q = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot \dfrac{y^2}{2} (z_1 + z_2) \cdot \left[ \dfrac{y^2 (z_1 + z_2)}{2 y (\sqrt{1 + z_1^{2}} + \sqrt{1 + z_2^{2}})} \right]^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

Simplifying:

$$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot \dfrac{(z_1 + z_2)^{5/3}}{2^{5/3} (\sqrt{1 + z_1^{2}} + \sqrt{1 + z_2^{2}})^{2/3}} \cdot y^{8/3} \cdot S^{1/2}}$$

This non-linear relation is the reason FAA recommends the use of **shallow, wide gutters** rather than deep narrow ones for airport pavements.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Aims to Design Solutions

| Aim | Engineering Mechanism | Design Component | KTU Module Mapping |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prevent hydroplaning | Cross-slope + texture | Runway crown, grooving | Module 3 — Geometric Design |
| Limit water film thickness | Rapid lateral flow | Inlet spacing, gutter | Module 3 — Surface Drainage |
| Preserve pavement | Subsurface drainage | French drains, edge drains | Module 4 — Subsurface Drainage |
| Prevent flooding | Adequate capacity | Storm sewers, detention pond | Module 3 — Storm Sewer Design |
| Avoid bird strike | Eliminate stagnant water | Covered drains, steep slopes | Module 3 — Environmental Drainage |
| Prevent subgrade saturation | Lower water table | Capillary cut-off, underdrains | Module 4 — Subdrainage |
| Avoid frost heave | Drain below frost line | Deep edge drains | Module 4 — Cold Climate Design |
| Erosion control | Limit velocity | Lined channels, energy dissipators | Module 3 — Erosion Protection |
| Pollution control | Treatment of run-off | OWS, sand trap | Module 3 — Environmental Aims |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Critical Hydroplaning Speed

### Step 1 — Physical Model

When water pressure under the tire equals the tire inflation pressure, the tire lifts off. Equating these two pressures gives the critical condition.

### Step 2 — Empirical Equation (Horne & Leland)

Based on full-scale NASA tests:

$$V_{c} = 10.4 \sqrt{P}$$

Where:
- $V_{c}$ = Critical hydroplaning speed in **knots**
- $P$ = Tire inflation pressure in **psi**

### Step 3 — Worked Example

A Boeing 737-800 lands at **140 knots** with main gear tire pressure **195 psi**. Determine safety margin.

$$V_{c} = 10.4 \sqrt{195} = 10.4 \times 13.96 = 145.2 \, \text{knots}$$

Safety margin:

$$\text{Margin} = V_{c} - V_{landing} = 145.2 - 140 = 5.2 \, \text{knots}$$

Since the margin is only **5.2 knots (≈ 9.6 km/hr)**, even a small amount of standing water (≥ 3 mm) can push the aircraft into hydroplaning. This is why the drainage aim of keeping water film below **3 mm** is non-negotiable.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Drainage Aims Priority

| Airport Type | Critical Aims | Drainage Priority |
|---|---|---|
| Tropical coastal airport (e.g., Kerala) | Hydroplaning + Erosion | Surface drainage dominant |
| Cold region airport (e.g., Leh) | Frost heave + Snow-melt | Subsurface drainage dominant |
| Desert airport (e.g., Dubai) | Flash flood + Sand ingress | Perimeter + Detention dominant |
| Heavy rainfall airport (e.g., Cherrapunji) | Flood control + Structural | Storm sewer + Detention dominant |

> [!NOTE]
> For Kerala airports (like Trivandrum, Cochin, Calicut), the dominant aims are **hydroplaning prevention during monsoon** and **erosion control of lateritic soil** in the surrounding catchment. Therefore, **surface drainage with adequate cross-slope and rapid gutter disposal** is the primary design driver.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"List and briefly explain any four aims of airport drainage."**

**Model Answer (12 lines):**

1. **Prevention of hydroplaning:** Water films exceeding 3 mm on the runway cause aircraft tires to lose contact with the pavement during landing or take-off. Drainage aims to keep the water film thickness below 3 mm by providing adequate cross-slope (1.0–1.5 %) and rapid gutter disposal.

2. **Preservation of pavement structural integrity:** Water infiltration into the subgrade reduces the California Bearing Ratio (CBR) and accelerates pavement failure. Subsurface drainage aims to maintain subgrade moisture below the saturation level.

3. **Prevention of flooding of operational areas:** The drainage system must carry the design storm run-off (typically 5-year to 50-year return period) without overtopping into runways, taxiways, or aprons.

4. **Reduction of bird strike hazard:** Stagnant water bodies within the airfield attract waterfowl. Drainage design avoids permanent ponds near movement areas by using covered drains and steep-slope open channels.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Why is runway cross-slope critical to the aim of hydroplaning prevention?"**

**Model Answer (8 lines):**

The cross-slope drains water laterally from the runway crown toward the shoulders. If the cross-slope is insufficient, water ponds and forms a continuous film. ICAO mandates a cross-slope of **1.0 % to 1.5 %**; FAA allows up to **2.0 %** for low-speed taxiways. A higher cross-slope reduces the time water remains on the pavement and directly limits the water film depth below the **3 mm hydroplaning threshold**. The drainage aim is, therefore, inseparable from correct geometric design of the pavement crown.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (Choice Option): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Explain in detail the various aims of airport drainage. Discuss how these aims influence the design of (a) surface drainage system, and (b) subsurface drainage system of an airport."**

#### Part (a) — Surface Drainage System (7 Marks)

The **surface drainage system** of an airport comprises the runway and taxiway crowns, cross-slopes, gutters, inlets, and the storm sewer network. Its design is driven by the following aims:

1. **Hydroplaning prevention** — Achieved by:
   - Cross-slope of **1.0–1.5 %** on runway centerline.
   - High-friction texture depth of at least **1.0 mm**.
   - Inlet spacing of **30–60 m** along the gutter line.

2. **Rapid removal of storm run-off** — Achieved by:
   - Sizing gutters using **Manning's equation**.
   - Providing adequate inlet capacity using the **Rational Method** ($Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$).

3. **Avoidance of flooding of movement areas** — Achieved by:
   - Designing for the **5-year to 10-year return period storm** (FAA).
   - Using detention ponds sized for the **50-year return period** storm.

4. **Erosion control in surrounding soil** — Achieved by:
   - Limiting velocities in turf-lined channels to **1.5–1.8 m/s**.
   - Providing energy dissipators at pipe outlets.

**Numerical Illustration:**

For a triangular gutter with $z_1 = z_2 = 50$ (i.e., 50:1 side slopes), $n = 0.016$, $S = 0.01$, and flow depth $y = 0.05$ m, the discharge capacity is:

$$A = \dfrac{0.05^2}{2} (50 + 50) = 0.125 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$P = 0.05 (\sqrt{1 + 2500} + \sqrt{1 + 2500}) = 0.05 \times 100.02 = 5.001 \, \text{m}$$

$$R = \dfrac{0.125}{5.001} = 0.025 \, \text{m}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{1}{0.016} \times 0.125 \times (0.025)^{2/3} \times (0.01)^{1/2}$$

$$Q = 62.5 \times 0.125 \times 0.0855 \times 0.1 = 0.0668 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 66.8 \, \text{L/s}$$

This is the design gutter capacity that must be matched by inlet placement.

---

#### Part (b) — Subsurface Drainage System (7 Marks)

The **subsurface drainage system** addresses the structural and frost-protection aims. Its components are:

1. **Sub-base drains (French drains):** Permeable aggregate trenches with perforated PVC or HDPE collector pipes, placed along the pavement edges to intercept lateral seepage.

2. **Capillary cut-off:** A layer of coarse, free-draining material placed beneath the subgrade to break capillary rise of ground water into the pavement.

3. **Underdrains for aprons:** Deep perforated pipes below fuel hydrant pits and aircraft stands to prevent contamination spread.

The design is governed by:

4. **Permeability criterion** — Sub-base material must have permeability $k \geq 10^{-4}$ m/s.

5. **Drawdown time** — The water table must be lowered by 0.5 m within **24 hours** of rainfall cessation.

6. **Pipe flow equation:**

$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

For a 100 mm perforated HDPE pipe laid at slope $S = 0.002$ with $n = 0.013$:

$$V = \dfrac{1}{0.013} \times (0.025)^{2/3} \times (0.002)^{1/2}$$

$$V = 76.9 \times 0.0855 \times 0.0447 = 0.294 \, \text{m/s}$$

This velocity must exceed **0.6 m/s** for self-cleansing (it does not — so a steeper slope or larger pipe would be needed).

7. **Structural aim outcome:** By maintaining subgrade moisture below saturation, the CBR remains within design value, and pavement design life of **20–30 years** is achieved.

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Discuss the role of airport drainage in ensuring (a) aircraft operational safety during wet weather, and (b) prevention of pavement damage due to subsurface water. Include relevant design criteria and equations."**

#### Part (a) — Aircraft Operational Safety (7 Marks)

Wet weather operations are governed by the **friction coefficient** ($\mu$) and **water film depth**. ICAO classifies runways as "wet" when more than 25 % of the surface area has a water film of **3 mm or greater**. The drainage aims to prevent this condition.

**Design Criteria:**

| Parameter | Specified Value | Authority |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum water film depth | **3 mm** | ICAO |
| Minimum friction coefficient (good) | $\mu \geq 0.34$ | ICAO |
| Recommended cross-slope | **1.0 – 1.5 %** | ICAO |
| Inlet spacing along runway edge | **30 – 60 m** | FAA |
| Texture depth (HSTP) | **≥ 1.0 mm** | ICAO Annex 14 |
| Maximum gutter depression depth | **≤ 100 mm** | FAA AC 150/5320-6F |

**Hydroplaning Critical Speed Equation:**

$$V_{c} = 10.4 \sqrt{P}$$

For an aircraft with tire pressure $P = 180$ psi:
- $V_{c} = 10.4 \sqrt{180} = 139.5$ knots

**Engineering implication:** The runway must be drained within the time required for the next aircraft arrival, which is typically **2–5 minutes** for busy commercial airports.

---

#### Part (b) — Prevention of Pavement Damage (7 Marks)

Subsurface water is the **silent killer** of airport pavements. The damage mechanisms and drainage aims are:

1. **Pumping Effect:** Under repeated wheel loads, water trapped under the pavement slab ejects the fines, creating voids and slab cracking. Drainage aim: prevent free water under the slab.

2. **Subgrade Saturation:** Water table rise saturates the subgrade, reducing CBR. Drainage aim: maintain subgrade moisture content below optimum + 2 %.

3. **Frost Heave:** In cold climates, freezing of trapped water causes upward thrust. Drainage aim: lower water table **0.5 m below frost line**.

4. **Edge Erosion:** Lateral seepage erodes shoulder material. Drainage aim: provide edge drains with permeability $k \geq 10^{-4}$ m/s.

**Quantitative Assessment using CBR Loss Equation:**

$$CBR_{soaked} = CBR_{unsoaked} \cdot e^{-k(M_{s} - M_{o})}$$

For $CBR_{unsoaked} = 8$, $k = 0.10$, $M_o = 12$%, $M_s = 22$%:

$$CBR_{soaked} = 8 \cdot e^{-0.10(22-12)} = 8 \cdot e^{-1.0} = 8 \times 0.368 = 2.94$$

This **63 % loss in bearing capacity** demonstrates why subsurface drainage is non-negotiable.

**Design Equation for Underdrain Spacing (Hooghoudt's Equation):**

$$L^{2} = \dfrac{4 K \cdot H^{2}}{q}$$

Where:
- $L$ = Drain spacing (m)
- $K$ = Hydraulic conductivity (m/s)
- $H$ = Height of water table above drain level (m)
- $q$ = Design drainage rate (m/s)

For $K = 10^{-5}$ m/s, $H = 0.5$ m, $q = 10^{-8}$ m/s:

$$L^{2} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.25}{10^{-8}} = 1000$$

$$L = 31.6 \, \text{m}$$

So the underdrain pipes must be spaced at approximately **30 m centres**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** in the Rational Method — failing to convert mm/hr rainfall intensity to m/s. Marks deducted: typically 1–2 out of 7.
> 2. **Confusing runway with taxiway cross-slopes** — Runway is **1.0–1.5 %**, taxiway is **1.5 % maximum**. Mixing them up loses marks.
> 3. **Not stating the 3 mm hydroplaning threshold** — This is the most-asked value in KTU papers. Omitting it is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Ignoring the bird-strike aim** — Many students focus only on pavement and hydroplaning. The bird-strike aim is a **favourite 2-mark short question** in KTU exams.
> 5. **Wrong formula for Manning's equation** — Students often write $V = (1/n) R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ but with $S$ written as slope in degrees instead of dimensionless ratio. Always use **S as m/m** (e.g., 0.01 for 1 % slope).
> 6. **Not differentiating surface vs. subsurface drainage** — In part (a) vs (b) questions, surface drainage covers gutters, inlets, and storm sewers; subsurface covers French drains, edge drains, and capillary cut-offs. Confusing them leads to **3–4 mark deductions**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Airport Drainage Aims**

- ☐ The primary aim of airport drainage is **prevention of hydroplaning** by keeping water film below **3 mm**.
- ☐ The four functional categories of drainage aims are **Safety, Structural, Hydrological, Environmental** (memory mnemonic: **SSHE**).
- ☐ Hydroplaning critical speed equation: $V_{c} = 10.4 \sqrt{P}$ (P in psi, V in knots).
- ☐ Runway cross-slope = **1.0 – 1.5 %** (ICAO); up to **2.0 %** for taxiways (FAA).
- ☐ Rational method formula: $Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$; unit conversion $\dfrac{C \cdot i_{mm/hr} \cdot A_{ha}}{360}$ for $Q$ in m³/s.
- ☐ Manning's equation: $V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ — $S$ must be dimensionless (m/m).
- ☐ Design return period: **5 years (pavement)** to **50 years (critical flood protection)**.
- ☐ Subgrade CBR reduces exponentially with moisture: $CBR_{soaked} = CBR_{unsoaked} \cdot e^{-k(M_s - M_o)}$.
- ☐ Subsurface drains must lower water table by **0.5 m within 24 hours** of rainfall.
- ☐ Standing water within the airfield attracts **birds** — covered drains and steep slopes are used to prevent bird strike.
- ☐ Modern apron drainage must include **oil-water separators (OWS)** to treat polluted run-off before discharge.
- ☐ Detention ponds are mandatory for storms exceeding **10-year return period**.
- ☐ For Kerala and tropical airports, **monsoon hydroplaning prevention** is the dominant design driver.
- ☐ The rational method applies only to catchments smaller than **50 ha** — for larger areas, unit hydrograph methods are used (advanced topic).
- ☐ **French drains** are the standard subsurface drainage element along runway and taxiway edges.
- ☐ The drainage aims directly extend pavement design life from **8–12 years (poor drainage)** to **20–30 years (proper drainage)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Functions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Airport Planning and Design**, the term **Functions** refers to the *systematic classification of activities, services, and operational roles* that an airport performs to achieve its primary purpose of **safe, efficient, and economically sustainable movement of passengers, aircraft, cargo, and information** between the air transport mode and the surface transport mode.

An airport is a **multimodal interchange node** where air-transportation interfaces with land-transportation. The *functions* of an airport can therefore be categorized as the **operational, commercial, and support activities** that collectively enable this interchange.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Airport functions are the operational and service roles performed by an airport, classified under three major domains — **Airside Functions**, **Terminal Functions (Passenger and Cargo)**, and **Landside Functions** — together facilitating the **transfer of passengers, baggage, aircraft, and cargo** between air and surface transport modes in compliance with **ICAO Annex 14** and **IATA Airport Development Reference Manual (ADRM)** standards."*

### 1.2 The Three Functional Domains of an Airport

An airport's functions are structurally organized into **three primary functional domains**:

| Functional Domain | Primary Function | Interface Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Airside** | Aircraft movement, parking, servicing | Air ↔ Ground (Aircraft) |
| **Terminal Area** | Passenger & cargo transfer processing | Aircraft ↔ Public |
| **Landside** | Ground access, parking, utilities | Airport ↔ City |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Airport is a Hotel-Lobby-on-the-Move"** 🛬
>
> Imagine an airport as a **giant, high-security, multi-purpose hotel lobby** sitting at the edge of a city, except the "rooms" (aircraft) are constantly arriving and departing, and the "guests" (passengers) are international travelers carrying luggage.
>
> - The **runway** is like the *front porch* — where guests (planes) pull in.
> - The **apron** is the *parking lot* — where planes are temporarily stationed.
> - The **terminal building** is the *lobby* — where guests (passengers) are checked in, their belongings are screened, and they are routed to the right "room" (gate).
> - The **landside** is the *driveway and city road* — how guests (passengers) actually reached the hotel.
>
> Every *function* of the airport simply answers: *"What role does this part play in getting passengers and planes safely and comfortably from the city to the sky?"*

### 1.4 Why "Functions" is a Critical Planning Concept

The *functional analysis* of an airport is the **first step in Master Planning** because:

1. It determines **land-use zoning** (where airside, terminal, and landside activities occur).
2. It establishes **capacity requirements** (passenger throughput, aircraft movements).
3. It defines **inter-functional relationships** (e.g., terminal-to-runway distance).
4. It drives **revenue models** (aeronautical vs. non-aeronautical functions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):**
> This topic directly maps to **CO3** of PECET744 — *"Analyze the functional requirements and design criteria of airport components."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Airport Functions

Airport functions are **not random** — they follow a **strict hierarchical and spatial logic** rooted in **operational efficiency** and **passenger flow continuity**.

#### 2.1.1 Airside Functions

The **airside** is the **security-restricted movement area** of an airport, regulated under **ICAO Annex 14** and protected by **perimeter fencing** and **access control**.

**Primary Airside Functions:**

- **Aircraft Movement Functions:**
  - Take-off and landing (Runway operations)
  - Taxi-in and taxi-out (Taxiway operations)
  - Holding and queuing (Holding bays)
- **Aircraft Servicing Functions (Turnaround):**
  - Refuelling, catering, cleaning, lavatory service
  - Water and waste disposal
  - Pushback and tow operations
- **Aircraft Parking Functions:**
  - Apron (Ramp) parking at gates
  - Remote stands (bus-boarding)
  - Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO) hangars
- **Air Traffic Control (ATC) Functions:**
  - Air Traffic Services (ATS) via Control Tower
  - Approach and Departure control
  - Ground Movement Control (GMC)
  - Aerodrome Flight Information Service (AFIS)
- **Safety & Rescue Functions:**
  - **Aerodrome Rescue and Fire Fighting (ARFF)** — Cat I to Cat X
  - **Runway End Safety Area (RESA)** operations
  - Bird-strike prevention and wildlife management
- **Navigational Aid Functions:**
  - ILS (Instrument Landing System)
  - VOR/DME, NDB installations
  - PAPI, Approach lighting systems

#### 2.1.2 Terminal Functions

The **terminal area** is the **passenger and cargo processing core** of the airport. Its functions are classified into **passenger functions** and **cargo functions**.

**A. Passenger Terminal Functions:**

- **Pre-departure functions:**
  - Check-in (counter, kiosk, online)
  - Baggage drop and screening
  - Security screening (hold baggage + cabin baggage)
  - Emigration / Passport control
  - Waiting and retail (concession area)
- **Pre-arrival functions:**
  - Immigration / Passport control
  - Baggage claim (carousel allocation)
  - Customs and quarantine
  - Meet-and-greet hall
- **Transfer functions:**
  - Interline transfer counters
  - Transit security re-screening
  - Connecting flight routing
- **Service functions:**
  - F&B (Food & Beverage)
  - Retail (duty-free, shopping)
  - Lounges (airline, CIP, VIP)
  - Restrooms, prayer rooms, medical

**B. Cargo Terminal Functions:**

- **Export cargo functions:** Acceptance, documentation, X-ray screening, build-up, ULD (Unit Load Device) loading
- **Import cargo functions:** ULD break-down, customs clearance, storage, delivery
- **Transhipment cargo functions:** Direct transfer between aircraft
- **Special cargo:** Dangerous Goods (DGR), perishables, valuables, live animals, mail

#### 2.1.3 Landside Functions

The **landside** is the **public-accessible area** outside the security perimeter.

- **Ground Access Functions:**
  - Curbside drop-off and pick-up
  - Public transport (metro, bus, rail)
  - Taxi stands, ride-share zones
- **Parking Functions:**
  - Short-term, long-term, premium parking
  - Car rental facilities
  - Employee parking
- **Support Functions:**
  - Fuel farms and hydrant systems
  - Power stations and substations
  - Water treatment plants
  - Solid waste management
  - Sewage treatment
- **Commercial Functions:**
  - Hotels, business centers
  - Cargo complexes
  - Maintenance hangars (MRO)
  - Free trade zones (airport economic zones)

### 2.2 Functional Inter-Relationship Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Three-Node Functional Loop"**
>
> A well-designed airport achieves its purpose when the following functional loop is **seamless and time-optimized**:
>
> `City → Landside Access → Terminal Processing → Airside Movement → Aircraft → Reverse Loop on Arrival`

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **essential for solving numerical and design problems** in the KTU examination on airport functions and capacity.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Expression | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Annual Passenger Throughput** | $P_a = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times 365)$ | Passengers/year | Master Plan capacity sizing |
| 2 | **Peak Hour Passenger Flow (PHPF)** | $PHPF = P_a \times k \times d$ | Passengers/hour | Terminal sizing |
| 3 | **Busy Hour (BH) Demand** | $BH = \frac{PHPF}{60}$ | Passengers/minute | Check-in queue design |
| 4 | **Design Hour Volume (DHV) — Aircraft** | $DHV = \frac{Annual\ Movements}{8760} \times k_{peak}$ | Movements/hr | Runway capacity |
| 5 | **Saturation Capacity (Runway)** | $C_s = \frac{3600}{T_a}$ | Movements/hr | Theoretical max throughput |
| 6 | **Practical Hourly Capacity** | $C_p = C_s \times f$ | Movements/hr | Where $f$ = correction factor (0.5–0.8) |
| 7 | **Gate Occupancy Time (GOT)** | $GOT = T_{arr} + T_{serv} + T_{dep}$ | Minutes | Apron gate requirement |
| 8 | **Gate Requirement** | $G = \frac{M \times GOT}{60 \times U}$ | Gates | Where $U$ = gate utilization (0.7–0.85) |
| 9 | **Service Level Standards (IATA ADRM)** | $A = \frac{S}{P_{peak}}$ | m²/passenger | Terminal area per passenger |
| 10 | **Cargo Throughput Rate** | $C_t = \frac{Total\ Annual\ Tonnage}{Working\ Days \times Hours}$ | Tonnes/hour | Cargo terminal sizing |

**Standard $k$ and $d$ values (FAA & IATA):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Design Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak Hour Factor (Annual) | $k$ | 0.0008 – 0.0025 | **0.0015** |
| Day-of-Peak Factor | $d$ | 1.3 – 2.0 | **1.5** |
| Gate Utilization | $U$ | 0.6 – 0.85 | **0.75** |
| Runway Time Service | $T_a$ | 60 – 180 s | **90 s** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Capacity Planning:** Functional analysis drives **terminal expansion** (e.g., T2 at Delhi, T4 at Changi).
- **Revenue Engineering:** Non-aeronautical functions contribute **40–60%** of airport revenue.
- **Safety Engineering:** ARFF function category determines **fire-station location and equipment**.
- **Sustainable Design:** Functional separation enables **LEED certification** of terminal buildings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Annual Passenger Capacity to Peak-Hour Functional Demand

> **Problem Setup (Standard KTU Pattern):**
> An airport handles **15 million passengers per annum (MPPA)**. The peak hour factor is $k = 0.0015$ and the day-of-peak factor is $d = 1.5$. Determine the **Peak Hour Passenger Flow (PHPF)** and the **busy-hour passenger flow per minute**.

#### Step 1 — State the Given Data

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= 15{,}000{,}000 \text{ passengers/year} \\
k &= 0.0015 \text{ (peak hour fraction of annual)} \\
d &= 1.5 \text{ (day-of-peak concentration factor)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the Peak Hour Formula

The **Peak Hour Passenger Flow (PHPF)** is derived from the relation:

$$PHPF = P_a \times k \times d$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute the Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
PHPF &= 15{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0015 \times 1.5 \\
&= 15{,}000{,}000 \times 0.00225 \\
&= 33{,}750 \text{ passengers/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Convert to Busy-Hour Rate (per minute)

$$
\begin{aligned}
BH &= \frac{PHPF}{60} \\
&= \frac{33{,}750}{60} \\
&= 562.5 \text{ passengers/minute}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

This means the **terminal functions** (check-in counters, security lanes, immigration) must be sized to handle a **continuous flow of ~563 passengers per minute at peak**. The IATA ADRM benchmark for **Level of Service (LoS) C "Optimum"** recommends approximately **23 m² of terminal area per peak-hour passenger**.

#### Step 6 — Calculate Required Terminal Floor Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{terminal} &= PHPF \times A_{per-pax} \\
&= 33{,}750 \times 23 \\
&= 776{,}250 \text{ m}^2 \approx 0.78 \text{ km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Comparison:** Delhi IGI T3 ≈ 502,000 m², Bengaluru T2 ≈ 255,000 m² — both well below the unconstrained LoS C figure because airports use **gate-grouping** and **pier-finger** designs to share space.

### 3.2 Derivation: Airside Gate Requirement from Functional Demand

> **Problem Setup:**
> An airport operates **120 aircraft movements/day** at a hub terminal. The average **Gate Occupancy Time (GOT)** for narrow-body aircraft is **45 minutes**, and the desired **gate utilization** is $U = 0.75$. Determine the **number of gates required**.

#### Step 1 — Daily Movement Density

The airport operates 16 effective hours/day, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{hourly} &= \frac{120}{16} = 7.5 \text{ movements/hour} \\
M_{peak} &= M_{hourly} \times 1.5 = 11.25 \text{ movements/hour (DHV)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the Gate Requirement Formula

$$G = \frac{M_{peak} \times GOT}{60 \times U}$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \frac{11.25 \times 45}{60 \times 0.75} \\
&= \frac{506.25}{45} \\
&= 11.25 \approx 12 \text{ gates}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Add Functional Reserve

IATA recommends adding **10–15% spare gates** for unscheduled maintenance and irregular operations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{final} &= 12 \times 1.15 \\
&\approx 14 \text{ gates}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Functional Demand vs. Design Response

| Functional Activity | Demand Driver | Quantitative Metric | Design Response (Engineering Output) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Check-in | $PHPF$ | Counters × 30 pax/hr | Counter island sizing |
| Security Screening | $PHPF$ | Lanes × 200 pax/hr | Queue area, X-ray machines |
| Immigration | $PHPF$ | Counters × 35 pax/hr | Passport control desks |
| Baggage Claim | $PHPF$ | Carousels × 200 pax/hr | Claim hall, carousel rotation |
| Aircraft Parking | $M_{peak}$ | Gates required | Apron depth, jet-bridges |
| Refuelling | $M_{peak}$ | Litres/movement | Hydrant system design |
| Fire Rescue | $M_{peak}$ + Cat | Response time ≤ 3 min | ARFF station location |
| Ground Access | $PHPF$ | Vehicles/hour | Curbside length, parking bays |

### 3.4 Multi-Step Worked Example (Full KTU Pattern)

> **Comprehensive Problem:**
> A regional airport has the following functional demand:
> - Annual passengers: 6 MPPA
> - Peak hour factor: $k = 0.0018$
> - Day-of-peak factor: $d = 1.4$
> - Average aircraft movement: 80/day
> - Runway time service: $T_a = 120$ s
> - Gate occupancy time: 50 min
> - Gate utilization: $U = 0.80$
>
> **Determine:** (a) PHPF (b) Runway saturation capacity (c) Number of gates required.

#### Part (a) — PHPF Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
PHPF &= 6{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0018 \times 1.4 \\
&= 6{,}000{,}000 \times 0.00252 \\
&= 15{,}120 \text{ passengers/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Runway Saturation Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_s &= \frac{3600}{T_a} = \frac{3600}{120} \\
&= 30 \text{ movements/hour (theoretical max)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For practical capacity with correction factor $f = 0.65$ (mixed traffic, single runway):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= C_s \times f \\
&= 30 \times 0.65 \\
&= 19.5 \approx 20 \text{ movements/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (c) — Number of Gates Required

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{hourly} &= \frac{80}{16} = 5 \text{ movements/hour} \\
G &= \frac{5 \times 50}{60 \times 0.80} \\
&= \frac{250}{48} \\
&= 5.21 \approx 6 \text{ gates}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Sanity Check:** Always verify that the *gate capacity* is not the bottleneck. Since $6$ gates can serve $\sim 5.76$ movements/hr and runway can serve $20$/hr, the **gates are the limiting functional element** at this airport — a critical planning insight.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Airport Functional Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["AIRPORT MASTER FUNCTIONS"]:::root
    A0 --> A1["AIRSIDE FUNCTIONS"]:::air
    A0 --> A2["TERMINAL FUNCTIONS"]:::ter
    A0 --> A3["LANDSIDE FUNCTIONS"]:::land

    A1 --> B1["Aircraft Movement"]
    A1 --> B2["Aircraft Servicing"]
    A1 --> B3["Air Traffic Control"]
    A1 --> B4["Safety ARFF"]
    A1 --> B5["Navigational Aids"]

    B1 --> C1["Runway Operations"]
    B1 --> C2["Taxiway Network"]
    B1 --> C3["Apron Parking"]

    B2 --> D1["Refuelling"]
    B2 --> D2["Catering"]
    B2 --> D3["Pushback Tow"]

    B3 --> E1["Tower Control"]
    B3 --> E2["Ground Movement"]

    B4 --> F1["Fire Category 1 to 10"]
    B4 --> F2["RESA Operations"]

    B5 --> G1["ILS VOR DME"]
    B5 --> G2["PAPI Lighting"]

    A2 --> H1["Passenger Terminal"]
    A2 --> H2["Cargo Terminal"]

    H1 --> I1["Check-in"]
    H1 --> I2["Security"]
    H1 --> I3["Immigration"]
    H1 --> I4["Baggage Claim"]
    H1 --> I5["Retail Concession"]

    H2 --> J1["Export Cargo"]
    H2 --> J2["Import Cargo"]
    H2 --> J3["Transhipment"]

    A3 --> K1["Ground Access"]
    A3 --> K2["Parking"]
    A3 --> K3["Support Utilities"]
    A3 --> K4["Commercial Zone"]

    K1 --> L1["Curbside Drop off"]
    K1 --> L2["Public Transport"]
    K1 --> L3["Taxi Ride Share"]

    K3 --> M1["Power Substation"]
    K3 --> M2["Water Treatment"]
    K3 --> M3["Fuel Farm"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef air fill:#0ea5e9,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ter fill:#22c55e,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef land fill:#f59e0b,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Passenger Functional Flow (Departure–Arrival Loop)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Landside Curbside"]:::land --> P2["Check in Hall"]:::ter
    P2 --> P3["Baggage Drop"]:::ter
    P3 --> P4["Security Hold Screening"]:::ter
    P4 --> P5["Emigration Passport"]:::ter
    P5 --> P6["Departure Lounge Retail"]:::ter
    P6 --> P7["Gate Boarding"]:::air
    P7 --> P8["Aircraft Pushback"]:::air
    P8 --> P9["Taxi out to Runway"]:::air
    P9 --> P10["Take off"]:::air
    P10 --> P11["Cruise"]:::air
    P11 --> P12["Landing"]:::air
    P12 --> P13["Taxi in to Apron"]:::air
    P13 --> P14["Gate Arrival"]:::air
    P14 --> P15["Immigration Passport"]:::ter
    P15 --> P16["Baggage Claim"]:::ter
    P16 --> P17["Customs"]:::ter
    P17 --> P18["Arrivals Hall Meet Greet"]:::ter
    P18 --> P19["Landside Exit"]:::land

    classDef air fill:#0ea5e9,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ter fill:#22c55e,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef land fill:#f59e0b,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Inter-Functional Relationship Matrix

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SP1["AIRSIDE DOMAIN"]
        SA["Runway"]:::air
        SB["Taxiway"]:::air
        SC["Apron"]:::air
    end

    subgraph SP2["TERMINAL DOMAIN"]
        TA["Terminal Building"]:::ter
        TB["Cargo Complex"]:::ter
    end

    subgraph SP3["LANDSIDE DOMAIN"]
        LA["Access Road"]:::land
        LB["Parking"]:::land
    end

    SA -- aircraft movement --> SB
    SB -- taxi operations --> SC
    SC -- passenger transfer --> TA
    SC -- cargo transfer --> TB
    TA -- ground access --> LA
    TB -- ground access --> LA
    LA -- vehicle parking --> LB

    classDef air fill:#0ea5e9,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ter fill:#22c55e,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef land fill:#f59e0b,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The three functional domains are **not isolated** — they are linked by *transfer nodes* (gates, curbside, cargo docks). A failure in any link (e.g., a blocked taxiway) cascades into a **system-wide functional degradation**. This is why **redundancy** in functions is a key design principle (e.g., parallel taxiways, multiple baggage carousels).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

*Classify the various functions of an airport under the three major functional domains. Give two examples of functions under each domain.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The functions of an airport are classified into three functional domains as per **ICAO Annex 14** and **IATA ADRM** standards:

1. **Airside Functions (1 Mark):** Concerned with aircraft movement, servicing, and safety.
   - *Example 1:* Runway take-off and landing operations.
   - *Example 2:* Aircraft refuelling and turnaround servicing.

2. **Terminal Functions (1 Mark):** Concerned with passenger and cargo transfer processing.
   - *Example 1:* Check-in, immigration, and baggage claim for passengers.
   - *Example 2:* Export and import cargo handling in the cargo terminal.

3. **Landside Functions (1 Mark):** Concerned with ground access, parking, and utilities.
   - *Example 1:* Curbside drop-off and public transport connectivity.
   - *Example 2:* Fuel farms and power substation operations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

*What is meant by Peak Hour Passenger Flow (PHPF)? State the formula and the typical range of the peak hour factor (k).*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The **Peak Hour Passenger Flow (PHPF)** is the maximum number of passengers handled by the terminal in a single clock-hour during the design year. It is the primary design parameter for sizing all **passenger-processing functions** of the terminal.

**Formula (1 Mark):**

$$PHPF = P_a \times k \times d$$

where:
- $P_a$ = Annual passenger volume
- $k$ = Peak hour factor (fraction of annual passengers in the peak hour)
- $d$ = Day-of-peak factor

**Range of $k$ (1 Mark):** Typically **0.0008 to 0.0025**, with a standard design value of **0.0015** (FAA Advisory Circular 150/5360-13).

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) Explain in detail the various functions of an airport terminal building, classifying them into pre-departure, pre-arrival, transfer, and service functions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The terminal building is the **central processing hub** of an airport, and its functions can be grouped into four major categories:

**1. Pre-Departure Functions (2 Marks):**
These functions handle the outbound passenger flow from landside entry to aircraft boarding.
- **Curbside Drop-off:** Passenger alighting zone at terminal entrance.
- **Check-in:** Allocation of seat, generation of boarding pass and baggage tag. Modern airports use *self-service kiosks* and *online check-in* to reduce counter demand.
- **Baggage Drop and Hold Screening:** Checked baggage is X-rayed and routed to the sorting system.
- **Security Screening:** Passengers and cabin baggage are screened using walk-through metal detectors (WTMD) and X-ray scanners.
- **Emigration / Passport Control:** Validated by immigration officers for international flights.

**2. Pre-Arrival Functions (2 Marks):**
These functions handle inbound passenger flow from aircraft to landside exit.
- **Immigration / Passport Control:** Verification of travel documents.
- **Baggage Claim:** Baggage delivered via carousels; carousel rotation time is **8–12 minutes**.
- **Customs and Quarantine:** Inspection of dutiable goods and agricultural products.
- **Meet-and-Greet Hall:** Public-access area with seating, F&B, and information.

**3. Transfer Functions (1 Mark):**
For passengers connecting between flights at the same airport.
- Interline transfer counters
- Transit security re-screening (mandatory for many airports)
- Connecting flight routing and gate assignment
- Transit hotel and lounge facilities for long layovers

**4. Service Functions (2 Marks):**
Commercial and passenger-amenity functions.
- **Retail and Duty-Free:** High-revenue non-aeronautical functions.
- **Food & Beverage (F&B):** Restaurants, cafés, bars.
- **Lounges:** Airline lounges, CIP (Commercially Important Persons), VIP lounges.
- **Amenities:** Restrooms, prayer rooms, medical centers, baby-care rooms, business centers, smoking zones, Wi-Fi connectivity.

The terminal's functional design follows the **IATA ADRM Level of Service (LoS) framework** ranging from **A (Over-Design)** to **F (Failure)**.

---

**(b) An airport handles 10 MPPA. The peak hour factor is 0.0014 and day-of-peak factor is 1.6. The average gate occupancy time is 55 minutes and gate utilization is 0.78. The runway time service is 100 seconds.
