Calculate: (i) PHPF, (ii) Runway practical hourly capacity (assuming f = 0.6), (iii) Number of gates required if 120 aircraft movements occur per day over 16 hours. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $P_a = 10 \times 10^6$ passengers/year
- $k = 0.0014$, $d = 1.6$
- $GOT = 55$ min, $U = 0.78$
- $T_a = 100$ s, $f = 0.6$
- 120 movements/day over 16 hours

**(i) PHPF (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PHPF &= P_a \times k \times d \\
&= 10 \times 10^6 \times 0.0014 \times 1.6 \\
&= 22{,}400 \text{ passengers/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Practical Runway Hourly Capacity (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_s &= \frac{3600}{T_a} = \frac{3600}{100} = 36 \text{ movements/hr} \\
C_p &= C_s \times f = 36 \times 0.6 = 21.6 \approx 22 \text{ movements/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Gate Requirement (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{hourly} &= \frac{120}{16} = 7.5 \text{ movements/hour} \\
G &= \frac{M_{hourly} \times GOT}{60 \times U} \\
&= \frac{7.5 \times 55}{60 \times 0.78} \\
&= \frac{412.5}{46.8} \\
&= 8.81 \approx 9 \text{ gates}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** 9 gates are required, which is well below the runway capacity of 22 movements/hr; therefore, the **gates are the binding constraint** of the airside functional capacity, not the runway.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) Describe the major airside functions of an airport. Explain the role of ARFF and air traffic control in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **airside** is the security-restricted area of an airport where aircraft movement, servicing, and safety operations occur. Its major functions are:

**1. Aircraft Movement Function (2 Marks):**
- **Runway Operations:** Take-off and landing, with runway classification based on code number (length) and code letter (wingspan).
- **Taxiway Operations:** Connecting runways to aprons; includes parallel, perpendicular, and high-speed exit taxiways.
- **Apron Operations:** Aircraft parking, gate operations, and remote stands.

**2. Aircraft Servicing Function (1 Mark):**
Turnaround activities including refuelling (via hydrant system or bowsers), catering, cabin cleaning, lavatory service, water replenishment, and waste disposal. Typical turnaround time for a narrow-body is **30–45 minutes**.

**3. Air Traffic Control (ATC) Function (2 Marks):**
- **Aerodrome Control Tower:** Manages aircraft within 5 NM of the airport and on the maneuvering area.
- **Approach Control (APP):** Manages aircraft in the terminal control area (TMA), typically up to 50–100 NM.
- **Ground Movement Control (GMC):** Issues taxi clearances and manages ground vehicle movement.
- **Area Control Centre (ACC):** Manages en-route traffic between FIRs.
- Communication is via VHF (118–136 MHz), and aircraft position is tracked via **radar, ADS-B, and multilateration**.

**4. Aerodrome Rescue and Fire Fighting (ARFF) Function (1 Mark):**
ARFF provides fire and rescue services compliant with **ICAO Annex 14, Chapter 9**. Airports are classified into **Categories 1 to 10** based on the **largest aircraft fuselage length**. Key requirements:
- **Response time:** ≤ **3 minutes** to any point on the operational area.
- **Vehicles:** Minimum number of ARFF vehicles based on category.
- **Water/foam capacity:** Specified per category.
- ARFF stations must be **co-located** with runways for minimum response time.

**5. Navigational Aid Function (1 Mark):**
Provides aircraft guidance for approach, landing, and ground navigation:
- **ILS (Instrument Landing System):** Localizer, Glide Path, Marker Beacons.
- **VOR/DME:** VHF Omnidirectional Range with Distance Measuring Equipment.
- **PAPI (Precision Approach Path Indicator):** Visual glide slope guidance.
- **Approach and Runway Lighting Systems.**

---

**(b) Differentiate between aeronautical and non-aeronautical functions of an airport. Why are non-aeronautical functions important for airport revenue? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**

- **Aeronautical Functions:** Activities directly related to aircraft operation, passenger movement, and cargo handling. These are regulated by **airport economic regulatory authorities (AERA in India)** and tariffs are controlled.
- **Non-Aeronautical Functions:** Commercial and real-estate activities not directly related to aircraft operation but occurring on airport land.

**Comparative Table (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Aeronautical Functions | Non-Aeronautical Functions |
|---|---|---|
| **Examples** | Landing & parking fees, passenger service fees, cargo handling | Retail, duty-free, parking, advertising, hotel, real estate |
| **Revenue Share** | 40–60% | 40–60% |
| **Regulation** | Heavily regulated (AERA, IATA) | Less regulated |
| **Capital Intensity** | High (runway, ATC, ARFF) | Moderate (commercial buildings) |
| **Volatility** | Sensitive to economic cycles | More stable income stream |
| **User** | Airlines, passengers, cargo | General public, concessionaires |

**Importance of Non-Aeronautical Functions (2 Marks):**

1. **Revenue Diversification:** Reduces dependence on airline tariffs, which are politically sensitive.
2. **Cross-Subsidization:** Profits from non-aero activities subsidize capital-intensive aeronautical infrastructure.
3. **Enhanced Passenger Experience:** Improves the airport's competitive position, attracting more airlines.
4. **Real Estate Value:** Airport land is a strategic resource; commercial development on it generates long-term annuity income.
5. **Economic Multiplier:** Airport economic zones (AEZ) generate employment and regional GDP.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *airside* with *aircraft side* — airside is the **security-restricted operational area**; aircraft side is not a standard term.
> - **Do NOT** state $k$ as a percentage in formulas — it is a **dimensionless fraction** (e.g., 0.0015, NOT 0.15%).
> - **Do NOT** forget the **d (day-of-peak) factor** in PHPF calculation; omitting it will yield a wrong answer.
> - **Do NOT** confuse *Peak Hour Passenger Flow* (PHPF) with *Busy Hour Rate* (BHR) — they are related but different units.
> - **Do NOT** include **ARFF** as a landside function — it is purely an **airside function** (many students lose 1 mark here).
> - **Do NOT** use $P_a$ directly to size terminal area — always go through **PHPF** first.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Examination**

✅ Airport functions are classified into **3 domains**: Airside, Terminal, Landside.

✅ **PHPF** = $P_a \times k \times d$ is the foundation of all terminal sizing.

✅ **Gate Requirement** = $\frac{M \times GOT}{60 \times U}$ — gates are often the bottleneck, not runways.

✅ **Runway Capacity** = $C_p = \frac{3600}{T_a} \times f$ where $f = 0.5$–$0.8$ based on traffic mix.

✅ **Standard $k$ value** = 0.0015; **standard $d$ value** = 1.5; **standard $U$ value** = 0.75.

✅ **ARFF Response Time** ≤ 3 minutes; categories 1–10 based on fuselage length.

✅ **Air Traffic Control** has 4 layers: Tower, APP, GMC, ACC.

✅ **Passenger Terminal Functions** are split into: Pre-departure, Pre-arrival, Transfer, Service.

✅ **Cargo Terminal Functions** are split into: Export, Import, Transhipment.

✅ **Non-aeronautical revenue** contributes 40–60% of airport income — a key economic driver.

✅ **Functional hierarchy** flows: City → Landside → Terminal → Airside → Aircraft → Reverse.

✅ The **three functional domains** are linked by *transfer nodes* — design must minimize transfer time.

✅ **ICAO Annex 14** governs airside functions; **IATA ADRM** governs terminal functions; **AERA** (in India) governs economic functions.

✅ Always perform a **bottleneck analysis** — runway, gate, terminal, or curb may be the limiting factor.

✅ **Level of Service (LoS)** A–F framework (IATA) is the qualitative check on functional sufficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Special Characteristics # Module 3 – Special Characteristics in Airport Planning & Design ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Special Characteristics** in Airport Planning refer to the **distinctive, non-conventional engineering, operational, and geometric attributes** that uniquely govern the design, layout, and functioning of an airport. Unlike highways or railways, an airport must accommodate three-dimensional movement of high-speed, heavy aircraft operating under strict safety, meteorological, and international regulatory frameworks. ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Special Characteristics** of airport engineering encompass the unique combination of: 1. **Geometric & Dimensional Features** — Runway length, width, longitudinal and transverse gradients, fillet geometry at taxiway-runway intersections, and sight-distance requirements unique to aircraft operations. 2. **Meteorological & Environmental Influences** — Wind rose analysis, cross-wind components, temperature and pressure altitude corrections, humidity adjustments, and obstacle clearance. 3. **Operational & Regulatory Standards** — ICAO Annex 14, FAA AC 150/5300-13A, and DGCA (India) stipulations on Runway Safety Areas (RSA), Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS), and Code Numbers. 4. **Functional & Capacity Constraints** — Movement area geometry, separation standards, wake turbulence categories, and gate-stand layout. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Explanation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy: An Airport is a "Three-Dimensional City in Motion."** > > Imagine a **bustling city intersection** — but instead of cars, you have **wide-body jets weighing 300+ tonnes** travelling at **280 km/h** just meters above your head. Unlike road traffic, aircraft cannot stop, swerve sharply, or reverse. Every metre of runway, every degree of orientation, every percentage of gradient, and every metre of obstacle clearance has been engineered to allow these machines to: > - **Take-off safely** (climb gradient ≥ 1.2% for precision approaches). > - **Land precisely** (Touchdown Zone Elevation tolerance ± 0.5 m). > - **Taxi without colliding** (separation standards of 168 m between parallel runways for Code F). > - **Survive excursions** (Runway End Safety Area of 240 m for large aircraft). > > The **"Special Characteristics"** are the rules of this unique city. They are **not arbitrary** — they are derived from **decades of accident data, fluid dynamics, and pilot psychology**. ### 1.3 Why Are These Characteristics "Special"? > [!NOTE] > Three fundamental reasons make airport planning categorically different from other transportation engineering domains: > > 1. **Aircraft cannot stop on the runway** — Runways are "one-shot" facilities. > 2. **Take-off and landing paths are 3D** — Governed by ICAO Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS). > 3. **International uniformity is mandatory** — A Boeing 777 landing in Kochi must obey the same geometric rules as one in Frankfurt. | Characteristic | Highway/Railway | Airport (Special) | |---|---|---| | Operating speed | 60–120 km/h | 50–300 km/h (taxi/take-off) | | Vertical clearance | Fixed, linear | 3D envelope (Approach/Take-off surfaces) | | Surface type | Asphalt/concrete | Designed asphalt/concrete (PCN/ACN rating) | | Vehicle mass | tonnes | Up to **575 tonnes** (A380-800, MTOW) | | Reversing | Allowed | **Prohibited on runway** | | Stopping distance | < 100 m | 1500–3500 m (wet/dry) | ### 1.4 ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code (ARC) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code (ARC)** is a two-component system used worldwide to classify airports and their physical characteristics. Every "special characteristic" in airport design is keyed to this code. - **Code Element 1 (Number):** A numeric value from **1 to 4** based on the **Aerodrome Reference Field Length (ARFL)**. - **Code Element 2 (Letter):** A letter from **A to F** based on the **Wingspan** and **Outer Main Gear Wheel Span (OMGWS)** of the critical aircraft. | Code No. | ARFL (m) | Code Letter | Wingspan (m) | OMGWS (m) | Example Aircraft | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | < 800 | A | < 15 | < 4.5 | Cessna 172 | | 2 | 800 – 1200 | B | 15 – 24 | 4.5 – 6 | ATR 72 | | 3 | 1200 – 1800 | C | 24 – 36 | 6 – 9 | A320, B737 | | 4 | ≥ 1800 | D | 36 – 52 | 9 – 14 | B767, A310 | | 4 | ≥ 1800 | E | 52 – 65 | 9 – 14 | B777, A330 | | 4 | ≥ 1800 | F | 65 – 80 | 14 – 16 | **A380** | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Special Characteristics — Categorical Framework The special characteristics of airport planning and design are best understood through **six interrelated pillars**: ### Pillar 1: Geometric Characteristics - **Runway Length, Width & Strips** - **Longitudinal Gradient (max 1.5% for Code 3/4)** - **Transverse Gradient (1.0%–1.5% for runway, 1.0%–1.5% for taxiway)** - **Fillet Geometry** at runway-taxiway intersections - **Runway End Safety Area (RESA):** 90 m × twice runway width (ICAO) / 300 m × 150 m (FAA for large hubs) ### Pillar 2: Meteorological Characteristics - **Wind Rose Analysis** — Cross-wind component limits: - **Code 1/2:** 24 km/h (13 kt) - **Code 3/4:** 37 km/h (20 kt) - **Temperature & Pressure Altitude** - **Relative Humidity** corrections ### Pillar 3: Operational Characteristics - **Movement Area Geometry** — Separation standards - **Wake Turbulence Categories:** Super, Heavy, Large, Small (per ICAO Doc 4444) - **Runway Visual Range (RVR)** - **Decision Altitude (DA)** and **Minimum Descent Altitude (MDA)** ### Pillar 4: Regulatory & Standards Characteristics - **ICAO Annex 14** — Aerodrome Design & Operations - **FAA AC 150/5300-13A** — Airport Design - **DGCA CAR Section 4** — Aerodrome Standards (India) - **ICAO PANS-OPS (Doc 8168)** — Instrument Flight Procedures ### Pillar 5: Environmental Characteristics - **Noise Contours (NEF / Ldn / DNL)** - **Air Quality** — Jet emissions monitoring - **Wildlife Hazard Management** - **Drainage and Flood Resilience** ### Pillar 6: Pavement Engineering Characteristics - **PCN/ACN Rating System** (Pavement Classification Number / Aircraft Classification Number) - **Flexible vs. Rigid Pavement Design** (CBR vs. Westergaard’s theory) - **ESWL (Equivalent Single Wheel Load)** for mixed traffic - **Layer thickness and material specifications** ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **the most tested formulas** in KTU University examinations for Module 3. Memorize the boundary conditions and the units. | # | Formula | Description / Use | Units / Limits | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $L = A \times B \times C \times D \times E$ | **Corrected Runway Length** (FAA method) | $L$ in m; A = basic length | | 2 | $\Delta L_{alt} = \dfrac{A \times h}{300}$ | Altitude correction (FAA) | $h$ in m above MSL | | 3 | $\Delta L_{temp} = L_0 \times \dfrac{T_{site} - T_{std}}{T_{std}}$ | Temperature correction | $T$ in °C | | 4 | $V_{cw} = V \times \sin(\theta)$ | Cross-wind component | $V$ in km/h; $\theta$ = wind angle | | 5 | $R = \dfrac{V^2}{127 \times (f \pm g)}$ | Stopping sight distance / Turn radius | $V$ in km/h, $f$ = coef. of friction | | 6 | $L_{TOD} = \dfrac{V_{LOF}^2}{2g \times (T/D - \mu)}$ | Take-off distance | $V_{LOF}$ in m/s | | 7 | $L_{LDG} = 0.6 \times V_{TDA}^2 / (g \times \mu)$ | FAA landing field length | $V_{TDA}$ in m/s | | 8 | $F_{min} = \dfrac{W}{2 \times \tan(\theta_{max})}$ | Minimum fillet radius (taxiway) | $W$ = wheelbase | | 9 | $OAS_{slope} = \dfrac{h}{d}$ | Obstacle Limitation Surface slope | $h$ in m, $d$ in m | | 10 | $PCN = f(Subgrade, Wheel Load, Tyre Pressure)$ | Pavement Classification Number | Dimensionless | | 11 | $D = \dfrac{P}{8.1 \times S \times c} \times (1 + 2\mu)$ | Equivalent single wheel load derivation | $D$ in m | | 12 | $n = \dfrac{N_{design}}{N_{allow}}$ | Fatigue life cycles | $n$ = ratio | ### 2.3 Critical Special Characteristics — Detailed Breakdown > [!NOTE] > **A. Runway Orientation & Wind Rose** > > Runways are oriented such that aircraft can take off and land **into the prevailing wind** to maximize lift and reduce ground roll. The cross-wind component ($V_{cw}$) must not exceed the limit for the design aircraft. $$ V_{cw} = V \times \sin(\theta) $$ where $V$ = wind velocity (km/h), $\theta$ = angle between wind direction and runway centerline. A **Wind Rose Diagram** plots wind direction and frequency as a polar chart. The orientation with the **highest coverage** for allowable cross-wind is selected. > [!NOTE] > **B. Corrected Runway Length (FAA Method)** > > The basic runway length is taken for **Mean Sea Level (MSL)**, **Standard Temperature (15°C)**, **Zero Gradient**, and **Zero Wind**. Field conditions require corrections: $$ L = A \times B \times C \times D \times E $$ | Factor | Represents | Why? | |---|---|---| | $A$ | Basic Length (ISA, MSL) | Reference baseline | | $B$ | Altitude correction | Lower air density → more ground roll | | $C$ | Temperature correction | Hot air → reduced engine thrust | | $D$ | Gradient correction | Uphill slope → higher fuel demand | | $E$ | Wind correction | Tailwind → longer take-off | > [!NOTE] > **C. Turning Radius & Fillet Design** > > Aircraft on taxiways turn at **very low speeds** (~15–25 km/h) but require **large fillet radii** due to wide wheelbase and main gear spans. ICAO requires fillet design such that the cockpit follows the taxiway centerline with the main gear tracking inside the edge. $$ F_{min} = \frac{W_{b}}{2 \times \tan(\Delta/2)} $$ where $W_b$ = main gear wheelbase, $\Delta$ = maximum steering angle (typically 50° for large aircraft, 75° for small). > [!NOTE] > **D. Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** > > Five key surfaces (per ICAO Annex 14) define protected airspace: > 1. **Conical Surface** — Slope 5% (1:20), height 100 m. > 2. **Inner Horizontal Surface** — 45 m above aerodrome elevation. > 3. **Approach Surface** — Diverges at 10% (1:10) for non-precision, 15% (1:6.66) for precision runways. > 4. **Transitional Surface** — Slope 14.3% (1:7). > 5. **Take-off Climb Surface** — Slope 2% (1:50) for large aircraft. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this matter in practice?** > > - **Coimbatore Airport (CJB)** — Located at 400 m AMSL → ~3.3% runway length increase. > - **Mangalore (IXE)** — Table-top runway with **RNP approach** required due to surrounding hills. > - **Jammu (IXJ)** — High temperature (44°C) + 300 m altitude → runway length inflated by **25%** compared to sea level. > - **Calicut (CCJ)** — Wedge-shaped runway over hilly terrain — **special transverse gradients** at 1.5% to enable rapid water shedding. These real cases demonstrate that **special characteristics are not academic — they are the difference between a safe operation and a catastrophe**. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Corrected Runway Length (FAA Composite Method) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context (Typical KTU Question):** > The basic runway length at **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** for a **Boeing 737-800** is **2100 m**. The airport is located at: > - **Altitude:** 500 m above MSL > - **Site Temperature:** 38°C (Standard = 15°C) > - **Effective Gradient:** 0.4% (uphill) > - **Prevailing Wind:** 5 km/h **tailwind** (headwind is favourable) > > Determine the **corrected runway length** using the FAA method. ### Step 1: Altitude Correction (Factor B) > The basic length is for sea-level conditions. As altitude increases, air density decreases, which increases ground roll. The FAA correction is **7% per 300 m of elevation**. $$ B = 1 + \frac{0.07 \times h}{300} $$ Substituting $h = 500 \text{ m}$: $$ \begin{aligned} B &= 1 + \frac{0.07 \times 500}{300} \\ &= 1 + \frac{35}{300} \\ &= 1 + 0.1167 \\ &= 1.1167 \end{aligned} $$ **Physical Interpretation:** At 500 m altitude, the runway length must be **~11.67% longer** than at sea level because thinner air provides less lift and engine thrust. ### Step 2: Temperature Correction (Factor C) > The basic length assumes a **standard temperature of 15°C at MSL**, decreasing at a lapse rate of 6.5°C per 1000 m. At 500 m, the standard temperature is $T_{std,site} = 15 - (0.0065 \times 500) = 11.75°C$. The site temperature is **38°C**, so the **temperature difference** is: $$ \Delta T = T_{site} - T_{std,site} = 38 - 11.75 = 26.25°C $$ The FAA correction is **1% per 1°C above standard**: $$ C = 1 + 0.01 \times \Delta T $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C &= 1 + 0.01 \times 26.25 \\ &= 1 + 0.2625 \\ &= 1.2625 \end{aligned} $$ **Physical Interpretation:** Hot air is less dense — both lift and engine thrust drop. Each degree above ISA adds 1% to the runway length. ### Step 3: Gradient Correction (Factor D) > For every **1% of effective gradient**, the runway length increases by **20%** (FAA) or **10% per 1% (ICAO)**. Given gradient = 0.4%: $$ D = 1 + 20 \times \frac{g}{100} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D &= 1 + 20 \times 0.004 \\ &= 1 + 0.08 \\ &= 1.08 \end{aligned} $$ **Physical Interpretation:** A 0.4% uphill gradient forces the engines to burn more fuel and reduce acceleration. ### Step 4: Wind Correction (Factor E) > A **tailwind** is unfavourable. A **headwind** is favourable. The FAA formula for wind correction is **+5% per 5 km/h of tailwind**. Tailwind = 5 km/h: $$ E = 1 + 0.05 \times \frac{V_{tail}}{5} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} E &= 1 + 0.05 \times \frac{5}{5} \\ &= 1 + 0.05 \\ &= 1.05 \end{aligned} $$ **Physical Interpretation:** Even a 5 km/h tailwind increases required runway length by 5%, because the relative airspeed over the wings is reduced. ### Step 5: Composite Corrected Runway Length $$ L = A \times B \times C \times D \times E $$ $$ \begin{aligned} L &= 2100 \times 1.1167 \times 1.2625 \times 1.08 \times 1.05 \\ &= 2100 \times 1.1167 \\ &= 2345.07 \text{ m} \\ \times\ 1.2625 &= 2960.65 \text{ m} \\ \times\ 1.08 &= 3197.50 \text{ m} \\ \times\ 1.05 &= 3357.38 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{L \approx 3357 \text{ m}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The **corrected runway length is 3357 m** — an increase of **1257 m (60%)** over the basic length! This is why **Kerala airports (e.g., Thiruvananthapuram at 4 m MSL, 28°C) have shorter runways** than airports in **Jammu or Leh** (high altitude, hot summers). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Cross-Wind Component Analysis > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** > A runway is oriented **09/27** (true bearing 090° and 270°). The prevailing wind is blowing from **030° at 35 km/h**. Determine the **headwind and cross-wind components** for both runway directions. ### Step 1: Define Wind Vector Wind direction **030°** means the wind is blowing **FROM 030° TO 210°** (i.e., towards the south-west). - Wind **velocity vector:** $V = 35$ km/h, $\phi = 210°$ (direction of motion). - Runway **09 direction:** 090° (take-off towards east). - Runway **27 direction:** 270° (take-off towards west). ### Step 2: Angle Between Wind and Runway (Direction 09) $$ \theta_{09} = 210° - 090° = 120° $$ ### Step 3: Headwind & Cross-Wind Components (Direction 09) > [!NOTE] > - **Headwind component** = $V \times \cos(\theta)$ > - **Cross-wind component** = $V \times \sin(\theta)$ For **Runway 09:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{hw,09} &= 35 \times \cos(120°) = 35 \times (-0.5) = -17.5 \text{ km/h} \\ V_{cw,09} &= 35 \times \sin(120°) = 35 \times 0.866 = 30.31 \text{ km/h} \end{aligned} $$ - Negative headwind → actually a **tailwind of 17.5 km/h**. - Cross-wind = **30.31 km/h** → **EXCEEDS 24 km/h limit for Code 3/4**. For **Runway 27:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{27} &= 210° - 270° = -60°\ (\text{or } 300°) \\ V_{hw,27} &= 35 \times \cos(-60°) = 35 \times 0.5 = +17.5 \text{ km/h} \\ V_{cw,27} &= 35 \times \sin(60°) = 35 \times 0.866 = 30.31 \text{ km/h} \end{aligned} $$ - Headwind = +17.5 km/h ✓ - Cross-wind = 30.31 km/h → **still exceeds limit**. ### Step 4: Re-orientation Required > [!IMPORTANT] > Since the cross-wind exceeds 20 kt (37 km/h for Code 4), neither runway direction is operationally safe. The **runway must be re-oriented** to align more closely with the **030° wind direction** — for example, designing a runway at **04/22** (true bearing 040°/220°) to reduce the cross-wind component. | Runway | Headwind (km/h) | Cross-wind (km/h) | Status | |---|---|---|---| | 09 | −17.5 (tailwind) | 30.31 | **Not allowed** | | 27 | +17.5 | 30.31 | **Not allowed** | | 04 (proposed) | +33.78 | 8.91 | **Allowed** | | 22 (proposed) | −1.04 | 34.98 | **Not allowed** | **Optimal selection:** Runway **04** for take-off (headwind 33.78 km/h, cross-wind 8.91 km/h). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Runway Length Corrections Summary | Correction | Triggers | Formula (FAA) | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---| | Altitude | $h > 0$ | $B = 1 + 0.07h/300$ | 1.00 – 1.50 | | Temperature | $T > T_{std}$ | $C = 1 + 0.01\Delta T$ | 1.00 – 1.40 | | Gradient | $g > 0$ | $D = 1 + 20g$ | 1.00 – 1.30 | | Wind | Tailwind | $E = 1 + 0.05V/5$ | 1.00 – 1.10 | | Humidity | Hot + humid | $H = 1 + 0.005\Delta H$ | 1.00 – 1.05 | | **Combined** | All | $L = A \times B \times C \times D \times E$ | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Tip:** Always apply corrections **multiplicatively, not additively** — a common mistake that costs 2–3 marks in the KTU valuation key. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Special Characteristics System > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional interdependencies** among the various special characteristics. This block architecture helps visualize how each characteristic feeds into the integrated airport design. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["Design Inputs"] A1["Critical Aircraft
Type and Weight"] A2["Aerodrome
Reference Code"] A3["Site Topography
and Climate Data"] A4["Traffic Forecast
Movements per Year"] end subgraph GEOM["Geometric Characteristics"] B1["Runway Length and Width"] B2["Longitudinal and Transverse Gradient"] B3["Fillet and Turning Radius"] B4["Strip and RESA Geometry"] end subgraph METEO["Meteorological Characteristics"] C1["Wind Rose
and Coverage"] C2["Cross Wind Component
Analysis"] C3["Temperature and
Pressure Altitude"] C4["Humidity Correction"] end subgraph OPER["Operational Characteristics"] D1["Runway Visual Range
RVR and Lighting"] D2["Wake Turbulence
Category Separation"] D3["Decision Altitude
DA and MDA"] D4["Approach Classification
Precision or Non Precision"] end subgraph REG["Regulatory Characteristics"] E1["ICAO Annex 14
Aerodrome Standards"] E2["FAA AC 150 5300 13A
Airport Design"] E3["DGCA CAR Section 4
India Standards"] E4["PANS OPS Doc 8168
Procedures"] end subgraph ENVIRO["Environmental Characteristics"] F1["Noise Contour
DNL and NEF"] F2["Air Quality
and Emissions"] F3["Wildlife Hazard
Management"] F4["Drainage and
Flood Resilience"] end subgraph PAV["Pavement Characteristics"] G1["PCN and ACN
Rating System"] G2["Flexible Pavement
CBR Design"] G3["Rigid Pavement
Westergaard Method"] G4["ESWL Calculation
Mixed Traffic"] end subgraph OUTPUT["Integrated Output"] H1["Final Runway
Length and Orientation"] H2["Approved Airport
Master Plan"] H3["Construction
Drawings"] H4["Operational
Certificate"] end A1 --> A2 A2 --> B1 A2 --> B2 A2 --> D2 A3 --> B2 A3 --> C1 A3 --> C3 A4 --> D2 B1 --> C2 C1 --> C2 C2 --> B1 C3 --> B1 C4 --> B1 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> H1 D1 --> D3 D3 --> D4 D4 --> B1 D2 --> H1 D1 --> H1 E1 --> B1 E1 --> B2 E1 --> B4 E2 --> G1 E3 --> B3 E4 --> D4 F1 --> H2 F2 --> H2 F3 --> H2 F4 --> B2 G1 --> H1 G2 --> H3 G3 --> H3 G4 --> G2 H1 --> H2 H2 --> H3 H3 --> H4 ``` ## 4.2 Wind Rose — Cross-Wind Analysis Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph WIND["Wind Direction (FROM)"] W1["N
000°"] W2["NE
045°"] W3["E
090°"] W4["SE
135°"] W5["S
180°"] W6["SW
225°"] W7["W
270°"] W8["NW
315°"] end subgraph PICK["Runway Picks"] P1["Pick Runway
with Highest
Coverage"] P2["Cross Wind
<= 20 kt
for Code 4"] end W1 --> P1 W2 --> P1 W3 --> P1 W4 --> P1 W5 --> P1 W6 --> P1 W7 --> P1 W8 --> P1 P1 --> P2 ``` ## 4.3 Runway Length Correction Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["Basic Runway Length A
MSL, ISA, Zero Wind"] --> Alt{"Altitude
h above MSL"} Alt -->|"h > 0"| B["Apply Altitude
B = 1 + 0.07h/300"] Alt -->|"h = 0"| TempCheck B --> TempCheck TempCheck{"Site Temp
vs ISA?"} TempCheck -->|"Tsite > Tstd"| C["Apply Temperature
C = 1 + 0.01 Delta T"] TempCheck -->|"Tsite = Tstd"| GradCheck C --> GradCheck GradCheck{"Effective
Gradient?"} GradCheck -->|"g > 0"| D["Apply Gradient
D = 1 + 20g"] GradCheck -->|"g = 0"| WindCheck D --> WindCheck WindCheck{"Wind
Component?"} WindCheck -->|"Tailwind"| E["Apply Wind
E = 1 + 0.05 V/5"] WindCheck -->|"Headwind"| Final E --> Final["Corrected Length
L = A B C D E"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code. Explain its two components. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** The **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code (ARC)** is a two-component classification system used to standardize airport design globally. It comprises: 1. **Code Element 1 (Numeric):** A number from **1 to 4** based on the **Aerodrome Reference Field Length (ARFL)**. ARFL is the minimum field length required for take-off at maximum certified take-off mass, sea level, standard atmosphere, zero wind, and zero runway slope. - Code 1: ARFL < 800 m - Code 2: ARFL 800 – 1200 m - Code 3: ARFL 1200 – 1800 m - Code 4: ARFL ≥ 1800 m 2. **Code Element 2 (Alphabetic):** A letter from **A to F** based on **wingspan** and **outer main gear wheel span (OMGWS)** of the critical (design) aircraft. - Example: A = Wingspan < 15 m; F = Wingspan 65–80 m (A380). The code governs **runway width, taxiway width, separation standards, RESA dimensions**, and **obstacle clearance surfaces**. --- ### Question 2: What is a Runway End Safety Area (RESA)? State ICAO and FAA requirements for Code 4. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Model Answer:** A **Runway End Safety Area (RESA)** is a **symmetrical area extending beyond the runway end** designed to **reduce the risk of damage to aircraft** in the event of an **undershoot, overshoot, or excursion** during take-off or landing. **ICAO Standards (Annex 14, Vol I, Chapter 3):** - Length: **At least 90 m** beyond the runway end (Code 3/4). - Width: **At least twice the runway width**. - It should be **cleared, graded, and free of obstacles** other than navigation aids. - Recommended (not mandatory): Extend to **240 m** where practicable for Code 4. **FAA Standards (AC 150/5300-13A):** - For **Design Group V/VI (large hubs)**: **300 m long × 150 m wide**. - Surface must be **engineered** to support an **occasional aircraft passage** without causing structural damage. The RESA is a **critical safety feature** especially for airports at the end of runways adjacent to drop-offs (e.g., Calicut, Mangalore, Agartala). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option A** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain with neat sketches the Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS) specified by ICAO for a non-precision approach runway.** **Model Solution:** > [!NOTE] > **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)** are a series of imaginary 3D surfaces that define the **protected airspace** around an aerodrome. Objects penetrating these surfaces must be **removed, lowered, or declared as obstacles** (with operational penalties). The five OLS surfaces for a **non-precision approach runway** (Code 3/4) are: **1. Outer Horizontal Surface (Not always for non-precision; depends on code)** - Height: 150 m above aerodrome elevation. - Radius: Specified in Annex 14 (depends on Code; e.g., 15,000 m for Code 4 non-precision). **2. Conical Surface** - Slopes **upwards and outwards** from the periphery of the inner horizontal surface. - Slope: **5% (1 vertical : 20 horizontal)**. - Vertical extent: **100 m** above the inner horizontal surface. **3. Inner Horizontal Surface** - A flat surface at **45 m above the aerodrome elevation** (lowest for non-precision). - Radius extends **2,000 m to 4,000 m** from runway threshold (depending on code). **4. Approach Surface** - Located **symmetrically about the extended runway centerline**. - Inner edge: **150 m wide** (Code 3/4), located **60 m before threshold**. - Divergence: **10% (1:10)** for non-precision runways. - First section slope: **2% (1:50)** for a length of **3,000 m**, then 2.5% to 60 m height. **5. Transitional Surface** - Slopes **upwards and outwards** from the edges of the runway strip and the approach surface. - Slope: **14.3% (1 vertical : 7 horizontal)**. - Extends until it intersects the inner horizontal surface. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OLS["Obstacle Limitation Surfaces for Non-Precision Approach"] CON["CONICAL SURFACE
Slope 1:20, Height 100m"] IHS["INNER HORIZONTAL
Height 45m, Radius 2000-4000m"] APPR["APPROACH SURFACE
Slope 1:50 for 3000m, then 1:40"] TRANS["TRANSITIONAL SURFACE
Slope 1:7, to inner horizontal"] RWY["RUNWAY"] THR["THRESHOLD
Inner edge 60m before"] end RWY --> THR THR --> APPR APPR --> CON CON --> IHS TRANS --> IHS TRANS --> APPR ``` #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: The basic runway length of an airport is 2400 m. The site conditions are: Altitude 450 m above MSL, Reference Temperature 28°C, Effective gradient 0.3%, and a 10 km/h tailwind component. Determine the corrected runway length using FAA method.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Basic length $A = 2400$ m (at MSL, ISA, zero wind, zero gradient) - Altitude $h = 450$ m - Site temperature $T_{site} = 28$°C - Standard temperature at site: $T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times 450 = 15 - 2.925 = 12.075$°C - Effective gradient $g = 0.3\% = 0.003$ - Tailwind $V_{tail} = 10$ km/h **Step 1: Altitude Correction (B)** $$ B = 1 + \frac{0.07 \times 450}{300} = 1 + \frac{31.5}{300} = 1 + 0.105 = 1.105 $$ **Step 2: Temperature Correction (C)** $$ \Delta T = 28 - 12.075 = 15.925°C $$ $$ C = 1 + 0.01 \times 15.925 = 1.15925 $$ **Step 3: Gradient Correction (D)** $$ D = 1 + 20 \times 0.003 = 1.06 $$ **Step 4: Wind Correction (E)** $$ E = 1 + 0.05 \times \frac{10}{5} = 1 + 0.10 = 1.10 $$ **Step 5: Corrected Runway Length** $$ L = 2400 \times 1.105 \times 1.15925 \times 1.06 \times 1.10 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} L &= 2400 \times 1.105 = 2652 \text{ m} \\ \times\ 1.15925 &= 3074.33 \text{ m} \\ \times\ 1.06 &= 3258.79 \text{ m} \\ \times\ 1.10 &= 3584.67 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{L \approx 3585 \text{ m}} $$ **Result:** The **corrected runway length is 3585 m**, an increase of **49.4%** over the basic length due to combined altitude, temperature, gradient, and tailwind effects. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **Option B** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain the special geometric characteristics of airport runways. Discuss the longitudinal and transverse gradient requirements as per ICAO for Code 3 and Code 4 runways.** **Model Solution:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Geometric Characteristics of Runways** are **unique** because aircraft require **smooth, predictable surfaces** with carefully controlled slopes for safe high-speed operations. The major geometric characteristics include: **1. Runway Length** Determined by the **critical aircraft's** take-off and landing field lengths, corrected for site conditions (altitude, temperature, gradient, wind). **2. Runway Width** | Code No. | Width (m) | Code Letter | Example | |---|---|---|---| | 3 | 30 | C, D | B737, A320 | | 4 | 45 | E, F | A380, B777 | **3. Longitudinal Gradient (ICAO Annex 14)** | Code | Max Effective Gradient | Max Longitudinal Change per 30 m | |---|---|---| | 3 | **1.0%** (1:100) | 1.0% | | 4 | **1.5%** (1:67) | 1.5% | The first and last quarters of the runway must be **flat (≤ 0.8%)** to enable safe lift-off and touchdown. **4. Transverse Gradient** - Runway: **1.0% – 1.5%** (crowned or cross-fall to shoulders). - Taxiway: **1.0% – 1.5%**. - Shoulder: 2.0% – 3.0% to facilitate drainage. **5. Sight Distance** - Any point **3 m above runway** must be visible from any other point at the same height within a **range of at least half the runway length** (for non-precision approach runways). - For precision approach runways: Full **runway length** visibility is required. **6. Runway Strip** - A defined rectangular area surrounding the runway to **protect aircraft** in case of excursion. - Width: **150 m** (Code 3) / **300 m** (Code 4) total. - Length: Extends **60 m beyond each runway end**. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: A runway is to be oriented along the bearing 135°/315°. Wind data for the airport is given below. Determine the percentage of time the runway will be inoperative due to cross-wind, assuming the allowable cross-wind component is 24 km/h for the design aircraft.** | Direction (FROM) | Wind Speed (km/h) | % Time | |---|---|---| | N (0°) | 20 | 10% | | NE (45°) | 30 | 15% | | E (90°) | 40 | 20% | | SE (135°) | 25 | 15% | | S (180°) | 15 | 10% | | SW (225°) | 30 | 10% | | W (270°) | 35 | 15% | | NW (315°) | 25 | 5% | **Allowable cross-wind = 24 km/h** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Compute Cross-Wind Component for Each Direction** Runway bearing: 135°/315°. The wind is named by the **direction FROM** which it blows. For each direction $\phi$ (FROM), the angle between wind and runway centerline (135°) is: $$ \theta = |\phi - 135°| $$ If $\theta > 90°$, use $\theta' = 180° - \theta$. Cross-wind component: $V_{cw} = V \times \sin(\theta)$ | FROM | $V$ (km/h) | $\theta$ (deg) | $V_{cw}$ (km/h) | $V_{cw} > 24$? | % Time | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | N (0°) | 20 | 135 → 45° | $20 \sin 45° = 14.14$ | No | 10% | | NE (45°) | 30 | 90° | $30 \sin 90° = 30.00$ | **Yes** | 15% | | E (90°) | 40 | 45° | $40 \sin 45° = 28.28$ | **Yes** | 20% | | SE (135°) | 25 | 0° | $25 \sin 0° = 0$ | No | 15% | | S (180°) | 15 | 45° | $15 \sin 45° = 10.61$ | No | 10% | | SW (225°) | 30 | 90° | $30 \sin 90° = 30.00$ | **Yes** | 10% | | W (270°) | 35 | 135 → 45° | $35 \sin 45° = 24.75$ | **Yes** | 15% | | NW (315°) | 25 | 180 → 0° | $25 \sin 0° = 0$ | No | 5% | **Step 2: Sum Up Inoperative Time** $$ \%\text{Inoperative} = 15\% + 20\% + 10\% + 15\% = 60\% $$ **Step 3: Usable Time** $$ \%\text{Usable} = 100\% - 60\% = 40\% $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > > Students commonly lose 2–3 marks in this question for the following mistakes: > 1. **Using the wrong angle convention** — Wind direction is the **direction FROM** which the wind blows, not the direction it blows **TO**. Always compute the angle between wind direction and runway centerline carefully. > 2. **Forgetting to take $\theta \le 90°$** — When $\theta > 90°$, you must use $(180° - \theta)$ because $\sin(135°) = \sin(45°)$. > 3. **Adding percentages inoperative instead of multiplying** — Percentages are **additive for independent directions**, but always double-check the question. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (General):** > > 1. **Units Confusion:** ICAO uses **metric** (m, km/h), FAA often uses **US customary** (ft, knots). Always convert to a consistent system. > 2. **Multiplicative vs. Additive Corrections:** All runway length corrections are **multiplicative** — never add them. > 3. **Code Letter vs. Code Number:** Code Number depends on **field length**; Code Letter depends on **wingspan/gear span**. Students often mix them up. > 4. **Wind Rose Coverage ≥ 95%:** ICAO requires that the runway orientation provide **at least 95% wind coverage** for the allowable cross-wind component. > 5. **OLS vs. OAS:** OLS (Obstacle Limitation Surfaces) are for **planning/land use**; OAS (Obstacle Assessment Surfaces) are for **operational evaluation of PANS-OPS procedures**. Both are different. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Special Characteristics** - **ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code** has **two elements**: numeric (field length 1–4) and alphabetic (wingspan A–F). **Know the boundaries.** - **A380 = Code 4F**; **B777 = Code 4E**; **B737 = Code 3C**; **Cessna 172 = Code 1A**. - **Runway width** for Code 4 is **45 m**; for Code 3 is **30 m**. - **Longitudinal gradient** max: **1.5% for Code 4**, **1.0% for Code 3**. - **Transverse gradient:** 1.0%–1.5% for runway; 1.5%–2.0% for shoulder. - **Cross-wind limits:** **20 kt (37 km/h) for Code 3/4**; **13 kt (24 km/h) for Code 1/2**. - **Wind coverage requirement:** ≥ **95%** of the time. - **Runway length corrections (FAA):** $L = A \times B \times C \times D \times E$. - $B = 1 + 0.07h/300$ - $C = 1 + 0.01\Delta T$ - $D = 1 + 20g$ - $E = 1 + 0.05V_{tail}/5$ - **OLS surfaces:** Outer horizontal, Conical (1:20), Inner horizontal (45 m), Approach (1:50 → 1:40 for non-precision), Transitional (1:7). - **RESA:** ICAO = 90 m × 2×RW width; FAA (large hub) = **300 m × 150 m**. - **Runway strip width:** **150 m (Code 3) / 300 m (Code 4)**. - **First/Last quarter longitudinal gradient:** ≤ **0.8%** for safe lift-off/touchdown. - **PCN/ACN system** governs pavement design — **PCN ≥ ACN** for unrestricted operations. - **Wake turbulence separation:** Super > Heavy (8 NM) > Large/Medium (5 NM) > Small (3 NM). - **Fillet radius formula:** $F = W_b / (2 \tan(\Delta/2))$ — remember $\Delta$ in radians or convert. - **Sight distance:** ≥ **half runway length** (non-precision) or **full length** (precision). - **Approach Surface Inner Edge:** 150 m wide, **60 m before threshold**. - **Decision Altitude (DA)** for CAT I = **200 ft (60 m) AGL**; CAT II = **100 ft (30 m)**; CAT III = **< 30 m**. - **Real-world tie-in:** Cochin (VOCI), Calicut (VOCL), Mangalore (VOML) — all require special clearance considerations due to geographic constraints. ---

topics.title: Basic Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Airport Runway Engineering**, the term **"Basic Requirements"** refers to the set of fundamental design prerequisites and operational criteria that must be satisfied for a runway to safely accommodate the design aircraft under all anticipated service conditions. These requirements govern the **geometric layout, longitudinal dimensions, load-bearing capacity, and orientation** of the runway strip as codified under **ICAO Annex 14, FAA AC 150/5300-13A, and IS 5671**.

The three foundational pillars of these basic requirements are:

1. **Runway Orientation** — dictated by the prevailing wind pattern (Wind Rose analysis).
2. **Runway Length** — governed by take-off & landing performance of the **Design Aircraft (Critical Aircraft)**.
3. **Geometric Configuration** — encompassing width, longitudinal slopes, transverse slopes, sight distance, and clearway/stopway provisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The term **"Design Aircraft"** in Module 3 is defined as the most demanding aircraft (or family of aircraft) that performs at least **500 annual operations** (per FAA) or **regular scheduled operations** (per ICAO) at the proposed airport. This aircraft dictates every aspect of runway planning.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine the runway as the **"main runway of a train station"** — but for aircraft. Just as a railway platform must be long enough for the longest train, wide enough for the widest carriage, and oriented along the safest wind/shadow direction, a runway must be:

| Real-World Analogy | Airport Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Longest train length | Take-off & Landing Run Length |
| Platform orientation (against prevailing wind for steam engines) | Runway alignment (along prevailing wind for crosswind safety) |
| Platform width | Runway width (wingspan + lateral clearance) |
| Sloped track (drainage of water) | Transverse & longitudinal gradient |
| Buffer zone at platform end | **Runway End Safety Area (RESA)** & **Stopway** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a runway need a "preferred direction"?** — A pilot taking off or landing prefers the **headwind direction** (wind blowing *against* the aircraft). A headwind reduces the **ground roll distance** by effectively increasing the relative airflow over the wings. A runway oriented into the prevailing wind therefore requires **less physical length** and is safer.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following constants and metrics are universally accepted for basic runway design under the KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **Standard Sea-Level Temperature (T_std) = 15°C (288.16 K)**
- **Standard Sea-Level Pressure = 1013.25 hPa (29.92 inHg)**
- **Standard Tropospheric Lapse Rate = 0.0065 °C/m**
- **ICAO Code Reference Field Length (CRFL) = 1500 m** (Code 3, 4) | **900 m** (Code 2) | **600 m** (Code 1)
- **Maximum Allowable Crosswind Component = 20 knots (≈ 37 km/h)** for dry runways (ICAO Annex 14)
- **Wind Coverage Threshold = 95%** (minimum allowable for operational usability)
- **Effective Runway Gradient (Longitudinal) ≤ 1.5%** (Code 3/4), **2%** (Code 1/2)
- **Transverse Slope = 1.0% to 1.5%** (for surface drainage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **95% wind coverage** is a non-negotiable benchmark. If a single runway orientation cannot achieve 95% wind coverage, a **crosswind runway** becomes mandatory, OR the design crosswind limit must be revised (with regulatory approval).

---

## 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping for This Topic

| KTU 2024 CO | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | Topic Alignment |
|---|---|---|
| **CO3** | Apply | Use ICAO/FAA regulations to determine basic runway length and orientation |
| **CO4** | Analyze | Analyze wind data and geometric constraints to justify design choices |
| **CO5** | Evaluate | Evaluate design alternatives for safety, economy, and operational feasibility |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Basic Runway Requirements

### 🧱 Pillar 1: Aircraft Characteristics (The "Demand")

The runway must be designed for the **Critical/Design Aircraft**. Key aircraft parameters that drive basic requirements:

- **MTOW (Maximum Take-Off Weight)** — drives pavement strength (flexible vs. rigid) and required lift-off speed.
- **Wingspan (b)** — drives runway width and taxiway geometry.
- **Wheel Base (l)** — drives longitudinal pavement design.
- **Approach Speed (V_approach)** — drives landing distance.
- **Reference Field Length (RFL / Balanced Field Length)** — drives runway length before corrections.

### 🧱 Pillar 2: Meteorological Conditions (The "Environment")

| Parameter | Effect on Design |
|---|---|
| **Wind direction & speed** | Determines runway orientation (Wind Rose) |
| **Temperature (T)** | Affects air density, lift, engine thrust → increases required runway length |
| **Atmospheric pressure (P)** | Affects air density, lift → increases required runway length |
| **Altitude / Elevation (h)** | Lower air density at higher altitudes → increases required runway length |
| **Precipitation / Drainage** | Drives transverse slope (1.0–1.5%) and surface texture |

### 🧱 Pillar 3: Topographical Constraints (The "Terrain")

- Available land footprint (urban encroachment, environmental clearances)
- Obstacle limitation surfaces (Approach, Take-Off, Transitional, Conical)
- Geological / subgrade conditions (bearing capacity, CBR, k-value)

### 🧱 Pillar 4: Operational Requirements (The "Service")

- **Code Number** (1 to 4) and **Code Letter** (A to F) per ICAO classification
- **Annual operational volume** (peak hour demand, future projections)
- **Type of operations** (VFR / IFR, day/night, scheduled/non-scheduled)
- **Critical aircraft mix ratio** (wide-body vs. narrow-body operations)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Corrections:** Aircraft performance charts are derived under **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) conditions** at **sea level** on a **level runway**. Real airports never meet all three simultaneously, so **correction factors** are applied to upscale the basic length to compensate for the deviation.

---

## 2.2 Runway Length Correction Logic (Step-by-Step Logic)

The corrected runway length is computed from the **Basic Runway Length (BRL)** — defined as the runway length required for the design aircraft at **mean sea level (MSL), under standard temperature (15°C), with zero wind and zero runway gradient**.

$$\boxed{L_{corrected} = L_{basic} \times f_1 \times f_2 \times f_3 \times f_4}$$

where each factor $f_i$ corrects for a specific environmental deviation.

### Detailed Explanation of Each Multiplier:

- **$f_1$ (Elevation Correction):** At higher altitudes, the air is less dense → less lift → more ground roll needed. Empirical rule: For every **300 m** of elevation above MSL, increase runway length by **7%**.
- **$f_2$ (Temperature Correction):** Higher ambient temperature → less dense air → more runway length. The reference temperature is the **mean daily maximum temperature of the hottest month** at the airport. Standard lapse-rate temperature at elevation *h* is: $T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065h$.
- **$f_3$ (Gradient Correction):** An upward-sloping runway requires more thrust and more ground roll. Empirical rule: For every **1%** of effective longitudinal gradient, increase runway length by **20%** (per ICAO) or **10%** per 1% up to a maximum of 20% (FAA method).
- **$f_4$ (Load/Pavement Correction):** For flexible pavements, if the pavement is designed for loads **less than the full MTOW** (e.g., dual-tandem vs. dual-wheel), a correction may apply. Often **1.0** for fully designed pavements.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet / High-Yield Equation Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **board-examination gold**. Master every equation — KTU regularly tests these in Part A and Part B.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Runway Length (BRL)** | From ICAO/FAA Aircraft Performance Chart | metres (m) | Tabulated for design aircraft at MSL, ISA |
| 2 | **Elevation Correction Factor** | $f_1 = 1 + \dfrac{0.07 \times h}{300}$ | $h$ = elevation (m) | Used above MSL; some codes cap at 10% |
| 3 | **Standard Temperature at Elevation** | $T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times h$ | $T_{std}$ in °C, $h$ in m | Reference for temperature correction |
| 4 | **Temperature Correction Factor** | $f_2 = 1 + 0.01 \times (T - T_{std})$ | $T$ = airport reference temp, $T_{std}$ in °C | $T$ = mean max temp of hottest month |
| 5 | **Gradient Correction Factor (ICAO)** | $f_3 = 1 + 0.20 \times \dfrac{g}{1}$ | $g$ = effective gradient (%) | 20% increment per 1% of effective gradient |
| 6 | **Gradient Correction Factor (FAA)** | $f_3 = 1 + 0.10 \times g$ | $g$ in % | 10% per 1% (max 20%); 20% per 1% for non-precision |
| 7 | **Total Corrected Runway Length** | $L = L_{basic} \times f_1 \times f_2 \times f_3$ | All in metres | Multiplicative corrections |
| 8 | **Wind Coverage (per runway)** | $\%W = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Hours wind within limits})}{\sum (\text{Total hours})} \times 100$ | % | Threshold: **95%** (ICAO & FAA) |
| 9 | **Crosswind Component (XWC)** | $XWC = V \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | $V$ = wind speed, $\theta$ = angle between wind & runway | Threshold: 20 knots (dry) / 13 knots (wet) |
| 10 | **Runway Width (ICAO Code 4)** | $W = 45 \text{ m}$ (Letter E/F) | metres | Code 3: 30–45 m, Code 1/2: 18–30 m |
| 11 | **Longitudinal Gradient (Max)** | $\leq 1.5\%$ (Code 3/4), $\leq 2\%$ (Code 1/2) | percentage | No gradient in the touchdown zone (first & last quarters) |
| 12 | **Transverse Slope** | $1.0\% \text{ to } 1.5\%$ | percentage | Crown or unidirectional for drainage |
| 13 | **Runway Strip Width** | $150 \text{ m}$ (Code 4) | metres | Each side from runway centre line |
| 14 | **RESA (Runway End Safety Area)** | $\geq 90 \text{ m} \times 60 \text{ m}$ (ICAO) | metres | Extends beyond runway end, from centre line |
| 15 | **Stopway Length (typical)** | $L_{stopway} = L_{TODR} - L_{TORA}$ | metres | $L_{TODR}$ = Take-Off Distance Required |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse TODR, TORA, ASDA, LDA** — KTU examiners often test the conceptual difference between these four declared distances. They are explained in detail in the derivations below.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The basic runway requirements form the **"design DNA"** of every commercial airport. In practice:

- **Heathrow (LHR):** Runway 27L/09R is **3902 m long** — corrected for the temperate, sea-level location but extended for trans-Atlantic heavy loads.
- **Cochin International (COK):** Main runway 27/09 is **3400 m** — corrected for **Kerala's tropical humidity and 9 m elevation**.
- **Lukla Airport (Nepal):** Only **527 m** of usable runway at **2864 m elevation** with **11.7% gradient** — extreme corrections in action.
- **Daocheng Yading (China):** World's highest commercial airport at **4411 m** — corrections exceed 1.5× the basic length.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Runway Length Correction Equation

The empirical correction equations for runway length are derived from **physical principles of aerodynamics and thermodynamics**, calibrated against decades of flight-test data.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Origin — From Aircraft Performance Charts

Aircraft manufacturers (Boeing, Airbus, Embraer) publish **Take-Off Distance Required (TODR)** and **Landing Distance Required (LDR)** as a function of:

- Aircraft weight (MTOW / MLW)
- Pressure altitude (= elevation + altimeter setting)
- Outside air temperature (OAT)
- Wind component (headwind/tailwind)
- Runway slope

These charts are produced for **Standard Sea-Level conditions (zero elevation, 15°C, no wind, level runway)**. The intersection of all correction curves with the design aircraft's reference value gives the **Basic Runway Length (BRL)**.

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Effect of Air Density (Elevation + Temperature Combined)

Air density $\rho$ is governed by the **Ideal Gas Law**:

$$\rho = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$$

Since lift $L \propto \rho V^2$ and the lift must equal weight $W$ at take-off velocity, the required take-off speed $V_{TO} \propto \sqrt{1/\rho}$. Ground roll distance is proportional to $V_{TO}^2$, hence:

$$L_{ground\,roll} \propto \frac{1}{\rho}$$

Using the **barometric formula** for pressure variation with elevation and the **lapse rate** for temperature:

$$P(h) = P_0 \left(1 - \frac{L \cdot h}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{gM}{RL}}$$

For standard conditions ($L = 0.0065$ K/m, $T_0 = 288.15$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $M = 0.029$ kg/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K), this simplifies to:

$$\rho(h) = \rho_0 \left(1 - \frac{0.0065 \cdot h}{288.15}\right)^{4.256}$$

For a small elevation change (e.g., $h \leq 2000$ m), this reduces to a near-linear approximation:

$$\rho(h) \approx \rho_0 \left(1 - 0.0001 \cdot h\right)$$

Equating the **inverse density ratio** to the runway length ratio and using the empirically observed **7% increase per 300 m of elevation** gives the correction factor:

$$\boxed{f_1 = 1 + \frac{0.07 \times h}{300}}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Effect of Non-Standard Temperature

Temperature affects density independently of pressure (via the gas law). The reference temperature for an airport at elevation $h$ is computed using the standard lapse rate:

$$T_{std}(h) = 15 - 0.0065 \times h \quad [°C]$$

The **airport reference temperature** is the **mean daily maximum temperature of the hottest month**. The correction factor is derived from the observation that, for every **1°C rise above standard**, runway length must increase by approximately **1%**:

$$\boxed{f_2 = 1 + 0.01 \times (T - T_{std})}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Effect of Runway Gradient (Longitudinal Slope)

A runway with an effective upward gradient $g$ (in %) demands more thrust and longer ground roll. Empirical data shows:

$$L \propto \frac{1}{1 - 0.01 \cdot g \cdot k}$$

where $k \approx 2$ is an empirical slope factor derived from flight tests. Expanding for small $g$:

$$L \approx L_0 \left(1 + 0.02 \cdot g\right)$$

Hence for ICAO conventions ($k = 2$ → 20% per 1%):

$$\boxed{f_3 = 1 + 0.20 \times g}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Combined (Multiplicative) Final Equation

Because each factor acts on the **already-corrected** length, the total correction is **multiplicative**:

$$L_{final} = L_{basic} \times \left(1 + \frac{0.07 h}{300}\right) \times \left(1 + 0.01(T - T_{std})\right) \times \left(1 + 0.20 g\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard)

### 📋 Problem Statement:

> An airport is to be designed at an elevation of **300 m above MSL**. The design aircraft has a **Basic Runway Length of 2000 m** (at MSL, 15°C, level, no wind). The mean daily maximum temperature of the hottest month is **35°C**. The effective longitudinal gradient of the proposed runway is **0.5%**. Calculate the **corrected runway length** as per ICAO standards.

### 🔢 Solution — Step-by-Step:

**Given Data:**

$L_{basic} = 2000$ m, $h = 300$ m, $T = 35°C$, $g = 0.5\%$

**Step 1: Compute Standard Temperature at Elevation**

$$T_{std} = 15 - 0.0065 \times h = 15 - 0.0065 \times 300$$

$$T_{std} = 15 - 1.95 = 13.05°C$$

**Step 2: Compute Elevation Correction Factor ($f_1$)**

$$f_1 = 1 + \frac{0.07 \times 300}{300} = 1 + 0.07 = 1.07$$

**Step 3: Compute Temperature Correction Factor ($f_2$)**

$$f_2 = 1 + 0.01 \times (T - T_{std}) = 1 + 0.01 \times (35 - 13.05)$$

$$f_2 = 1 + 0.01 \times 21.95 = 1 + 0.2195 = 1.2195$$

**Step 4: Compute Gradient Correction Factor ($f_3$)** (ICAO)

$$f_3 = 1 + 0.20 \times g = 1 + 0.20 \times 0.5 = 1 + 0.10 = 1.10$$

**Step 5: Apply Multiplicative Correction**

$$L_{corrected} = L_{basic} \times f_1 \times f_2 \times f_3$$

$$L_{corrected} = 2000 \times 1.07 \times 1.2195 \times 1.10$$

**Step 6: Numerical Evaluation**

$$L_{corrected} = 2000 \times 1.07 = 2140.00 \text{ m}$$

$$L_{corrected} = 2140.00 \times 1.2195 = 2609.73 \text{ m}$$

$$L_{corrected} = 2609.73 \times 1.10 = 2870.70 \text{ m}$$

### ✅ Final Answer:

$$\boxed{L_{corrected} \approx 2871 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Declared Distances (TORA, TODA, ASDA, LDA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks:** *"Explain the four declared distances and draw a schematic."* This matrix is your key.

| Symbol | Full Name | Definition | Includes Stopway? | Includes Clearway? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TORA** | Take-Off Run Available | Length of runway available for ground run | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **TODA** | Take-Off Distance Available | Total distance available for take-off (ground run + clearway) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **ASDA** | Accelerate-Stop Distance Available | Distance available to accelerate & then stop in emergency | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **LDA** | Landing Distance Available | Length of runway available for landing (often < TORA due to threshold displacement) | ❌ No | ❌ No |

### Schematic Layout:

```
   <--Threshold-->|<------- TORA / LDA ------->|<--Stopway-->|<-Clearway->|
   |    (Start)   |                            |             |           |
   ########################################################################
   |  100 m  |    2400 m (TORA=LDA)            | 200 m       |  300 m     |
   ########################################################################
                                                ^             ^
                                          ASDA = TORA + Stopway = 2600 m
                                                  TODA = TORA + Clearway = 2700 m
```

---

## 3.4 Wind Coverage & Crosswind Component — Analytical Derivation

Given wind data (speed and direction over many years), the **runway orientation** is decided by:

### Step 1: Tabulate Wind Observations

Create a 16-point or 8-point wind rose with calm wind (wind speed < 4 knots) noted separately.

### Step 2: Compute Crosswind Component (XWC)

For each wind observation with speed $V$ and direction $\theta_w$, where $\theta_r$ is the runway heading:

$$XWC = V \cdot \sin(\theta_w - \theta_r)$$

### Step 3: Filter Acceptable Observations

If $\vert XWC \vert \leq 20$ knots (dry runway), the wind observation is **acceptable** for that runway.

### Step 4: Compute Wind Coverage

$$\% \text{Coverage} = \frac{\sum (\text{Hours with acceptable XWC})}{\sum (\text{Total annual hours})} \times 100$$

### Step 5: Iterate Runway Orientations

Try multiple runway headings (typically 0°, 30°, 60°, …, 330°) and find the orientation with **maximum wind coverage ≥ 95%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The orientation with maximum wind coverage is typically aligned such that the **resultant wind vector** (weighted by frequency) points along the runway centerline. This is the **"Principle of Wind Alignment"** formalized by ICAO Annex 14.

---

## 3.5 ICAO Aerodrome Reference Code Table (Code Number + Code Letter)

### Aerodrome Reference Code (Table 1-1, ICAO Annex 14)

| Code Number | Aerodrome Reference Field Length (ARFL) | Code Letter | Wingspan | Outer Main Gear Span |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $< 800$ m | **A** | $< 15$ m | $< 4.5$ m |
| **2** | $800$ m $\leq$ ARFL $< 1200$ m | **B** | $15$ m $\leq b < 24$ m | $4.5$ m $\leq$ OMG $< 6$ m |
| **3** | $1200$ m $\leq$ ARFL $< 1800$ m | **C** | $24$ m $\leq b < 36$ m | $6$ m $\leq$ OMG $< 9$ m |
| **4** | $\geq 1800$ m | **D** | $36$ m $\leq b < 52$ m | $9$ m $\leq$ OMG $< 14$ m |
| — | — | **E** | $52$ m $\leq b < 65$ m | $9$ m $\leq$ OMG $< 14$ m |
| — | — | **F** | $65$ m $\leq b < 80$ m | $14$ m $\leq$ OMG $< 16$ m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Aerodrome Reference Code is a TWO-PART identifier** — e.g., "4F" = Code Number 4 + Code Letter F (the most demanding category for A380-class aircraft).

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Runway Design Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete workflow for establishing the **Basic Requirements of a Runway** as per the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** (no physical drawings attempted), covering the logical interactions of every design step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Initiation] --> B[Identify Critical Aircraft Type]
    B --> C[Determine Aerodrome Reference Code: Number and Letter]
    C --> D{Are Aircraft Performance Charts Available?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Extract Basic Runway Length BRL from Chart]
    D -- No --> F[Use Manufacturer Data: TODR and LDR at MSL]
    E --> G[Collect Site Data: Elevation h, Temperature T, Wind Data, Gradient g]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Standard Temperature Tstd at Elevation h]
    H --> I[Calculate Elevation Correction f1]
    I --> J[Calculate Temperature Correction f2]
    J --> K[Calculate Gradient Correction f3]
    K --> L[Apply Multiplicative Correction: L = BRL x f1 x f2 x f3]
    L --> M[Round Up to Nearest 10 m ICAO Convention]
    M --> N[Verify Wind Coverage via Wind Rose Analysis]
    N --> O{Wind Coverage >= 95%?}
    O -- No --> P[Reorient Runway or Add Crosswind Runway]
    O -- Yes --> Q[Define Declared Distances: TORA, TODA, ASDA, LDA]
    P --> N
    Q --> R[Set Geometric Standards: Width, Slope, RESA, Strip]
    R --> S[Cross-Check Against ICAO Annex 14 Obstacle Surfaces]
    S --> T[Final Runway Design Output]
    T --> U[End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Flow:** The flow is **iterative** between wind coverage analysis and orientation selection. The wind rose check (Node O) is a **feedback loop** — if coverage falls below 95%, the designer must return to the orientation selection step (Node P) and iterate.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Wind Rose Coverage Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Wind Data: Speed and Direction] --> B[Filter Calm Winds: V less than 4 knots]
    B --> C[Compute Crosswind Component XWC for each Observation]
    C --> D{XWC less than or equal 20 knots?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Count as Acceptable Hour]
    D -- No --> F[Reject as Crosswind Exceeds Limit]
    E --> G[Sum Acceptable Hours]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Coverage Percentage]
    H --> I{Coverage >= 95%?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Runway Orientation ACCEPTED]
    I -- No --> K[Try New Orientation Angle]
    K --> C
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Basic Requirements

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Basic Runway Requirements] --> B[Aircraft Driven Requirements]
    A --> C[Environment Driven Requirements]
    A --> D[Operational Driven Requirements]
    B --> B1[Reference Field Length]
    B --> B2[Wingspan determines Width]
    B --> B3[Wheel Load determines Pavement]
    C --> C1[Elevation correction]
    C --> C2[Temperature correction]
    C --> C3[Wind Rose orientation]
    C --> C4[Drainage slope]
    D --> D1[ICAO Code Number and Letter]
    D --> D2[Declared Distances: TORA, TODA, ASDA, LDA]
    D --> D3[Visual and Navaid requirements]
```

## 4.4 Tabular Cross-Section of a Typical Runway

| Layer (Top to Bottom) | Function | Typical Material | Thickness (Code 4) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface Course** | Friction, smoothness | Asphalt / Concrete | 50–75 mm |
| **Base Course** | Load distribution | Crushed stone, cement-bound | 150–300 mm |
| **Subbase Course** | Drainage, frost protection | Granular material | 200–400 mm |
| **Subgrade (Prepared)** | Bearing capacity | Compacted natural soil | Compaction ≥ 98% MDD |
| **Drainage Layer** | Water evacuation | Open-graded aggregate | 100–150 mm |
