topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Obstruction Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Obstruction Lighting** is a regulated system of aviation warning lights installed on tall structures (natural or man-made) that penetrate or lie in the vicinity of aerodrome obstacle limitation surfaces, as defined under **ICAO Annex 14** and **FAA AC 70/7460-1**. The system ensures that the obstacle remains visible to pilots during **day, twilight, and night** operations, thereby safeguarding aircraft during takeoff, approach, and en-route flight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** Obstruction lighting is treated as a subset of *Airport Visual Aids for Safety* and is closely linked with **Obstacle Limitation Surfaces (OLS)**, **frangibility norms**, and **Annex 14 lighting standards**.

**Key Designation Codes (FAA/ICAO Conformity):**

| Code | Class | Color | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L-810** | Low Intensity | Red | Fixed/Steady |
| **L-862** | Medium Intensity | Red | Flashing |
| **L-864** | Medium Intensity | Red | Flashing |
| **L-865** | Medium Intensity | White | Flashing |
| **L-866** | High Intensity | White | Flashing (Day/Twilight) |
| **L-867** | High Intensity | White | Flashing (Day) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine driving on a dark highway. **Lane reflectors and streetlights** are *passive and active* markers that prevent collisions. Similarly, an aircraft cruising at **250 knots** in a dark sky has no natural visual reference for a **silently standing** 150-metre telecom tower. To a pilot, the tower is essentially **invisible** until dangerously close.

> **Obstruction lighting is, therefore, the "streetlight" of the sky.**

- **Low-intensity red lights** = the *reflective stud* on a curb (only useful in the dark).
- **High-intensity white strobes** = the *bright halogen headlights* of an oncoming truck (visible from miles away in bright daylight).

The lighting system acts as a **passive-warning-to-pilot communication channel** that complements the *active communication* of ATC radar, ensuring the aircraft can *visually* identify and avoid the structure.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardised Specifications (per ICAO Annex 14, Vol. I, Ch. 6):**
> - **Minimum effective intensity (L-810):** 32.5 candela (cd) — equivalent to a small bicycle headlamp.
> - **High-intensity white strobe (L-866):** **20,000 cd** during daytime, tapering to **2,000 cd** at night.
> - **Flash rate:** **20–60 flashes per minute (fpm)** for medium/high intensity.
> - **Color chromaticity:** Aviation Red (x = 0.700, y = 0.300 approx.) and Aviation White (x = 0.310, y = 0.316 approx.) per CIE 1931.

---

## 1.4 Why Obstruction Lighting? — The Engineering Rationale

1. **Aircraft Collision Risk Reduction:** A 2020 IATA report estimated that **over 100,000 man-made obstacles above 150 ft** exist globally near flight paths.
2. **Regulatory Compliance:** Failure to mark obstacles can result in suspension of aerodrome license.
3. **Operational Continuity:** Allows **night and low-visibility (CAT II/III)** operations to continue safely.
4. **Legal Liability Shield:** Demonstrates "due diligence" by the structure owner in case of incident.
5. **Cost-Effective Safety Layer:** Compared to structural relocation, obstruction lighting is **< 1 %** of project cost.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic — How Obstruction Lights Are Selected

The selection is **NOT arbitrary**; it is a hierarchical decision based on **structure height above ground level (AGL)** and **proximity to aerodrome**:

### Step 1 — Determine if the structure is an "obstacle"
A structure becomes an obstacle if:
- It penetrates the **Approach Surface**, **Take-off Climb Surface**, **Transitional Surface**, **Inner Horizontal Surface**, or **Conical Surface** (per ICAO Annex 14).
- Generally, anything above **150 ft (45 m) AGL** within 5 km of an aerodrome.
- Anything above **500 ft (150 m) AGL** regardless of distance (FAA FAR 77.13).

### Step 2 — Choose the lighting class

**Bulleted logic flow:**
- **Height ≤ 45 m AGL (and not a catenary):** → *Low-Intensity, Type A or B (L-810)*, single red light at the top.
- **Height 45 m – 150 m AGL:** → *Medium-Intensity, Type B (L-864)* red flashing, OR *Type C (L-865)* white flashing, with intermediate levels.
- **Height > 150 m AGL, or significant aerodrome proximity:** → *High-Intensity, Type A (L-866)* white strobe system, with **multiple light levels** spaced at uniform intervals.
- **Height > 210 m AGL:** → *High-intensity** lights at **top, middle, and base**, plus **medium-intensity** at all intermediate levels spaced at ≤ **52 m** vertical intervals.

### Step 3 — Day vs. Night Operation
- **Daytime:** High-intensity white strobes (L-866/L-867) are **mandatory** because red light is washed out by sunlight.
- **Twilight:** Reduced-intensity white or switching to red is acceptable.
- **Night:** Red lights (L-810/L-864) are preferred to **reduce light pollution and pilot disorientation**.

### Step 4 — Synchronisation Requirement
> [!IMPORTANT]
> All **flashing lights** on a single structure, and where feasible on adjacent structures, **must flash simultaneously** to avoid pilot confusion. This is governed by **FAA AC 150/5345-43J**.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why red for night?** Red is the **least disruptive** to the dark-adapted human eye (scotopic vision) and is universally used as a *warning* in aviation and maritime domains.
- **Why white for day?** The human eye is most **photopically sensitive to greenish-white (~555 nm)**, so a high-intensity white flash penetrates bright daylight far better than red.
- **Why multiple levels on a tall tower?** A single light at the top is invisible from below due to the structure's body — a pilot approaching from a low angle would see the tower as **unlit**. Therefore, **intermediate lights** are essential at every ≤ 52 m interval.
- **Why synchronisation?** Unsynchronised flashing can be misread as a rotating beacon (which indicates an *active aerodrome*), creating a dangerous false cue.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Candela (cd) | $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\omega}$ | candela (cd) | Luminous intensity definition |
| 2 | Illuminance at distance | $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^2}$ | lux (lx) | Pilot's-eye brightness |
| 3 | Visibility range (visual) | $d_{\max} = 3.57 \sqrt{H}$ (where $H$ = observer height above ground in feet) | statute miles | For L-810 on a 45 m tower |
| 4 | Top-light height above structure | $H_{top} = H_{structure} + 1.5 \text{ m (min)}$ | metres | FAA standard mount |
| 5 | Intermediate spacing (HI) | $s \le 105 \text{ m}$ (FAA) or $\le 52 \text{ m}$ for structures > 210 m | metres | Level distribution |
| 6 | Flash rate, medium intensity | $f = 20 \text{ to } 60$ fpm | flashes/min | Synchronisation |
| 7 | Flash rate, high intensity | $f = 40$ fpm (standard) | flashes/min | ICAO/FAA default |
| 8 | Beam spread (vertical) | $\theta_v = 3^\circ \text{ to } 7^\circ$ | degrees | Above horizontal |
| 9 | Beam spread (horizontal) | $\theta_h = 360^\circ$ | degrees | Omnidirectional |
| 10 | Photometric threshold (day) | $E \ge 200{,}000$ cd (effective) | candela | L-866 requirement |
| 11 | Photometric threshold (night) | $E \ge 2{,}000$ cd (effective) | candela | L-866 dimmed mode |
| 12 | Color — Aviation Red | $\lambda \approx 620 \text{ – } 700$ nm | nanometres | Trichromatic coords |
| 13 | Color — Aviation White | $x = 0.310, y = 0.316$ (CIE 1931) | dimensionless | Trichromatic coords |
| 14 | Population density trigger | > 8,500 inhabitants within 5 km → use white | inhabitants | FAA siting rule |
| 15 | Structure height tier | $H \le 45 \text{ m} \to L\text{-}810$; $H > 45 \text{ m} \to L\text{-}864$ | metres | Selection rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The illuminance equation $E = I\cos\theta / d^2$ is the same equation used in **streetlight design** and **lighthouse engineering**. A reduction in intensity by 4× only **doubles** the perceived safe distance — which is why night-time intensity is dimmed.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Application Fields

1. **Telecommunication Towers** (the largest user class globally)
2. **Power Transmission Lines & Pylons** (especially **catenary lights**)
3. **Wind Turbines** (special FAA study required)
4. **Chimneys of Thermal Power Plants**
5. **High-rise Buildings** near approach paths
6. **Cranes** during construction
7. **Bridges** (especially cable-stayed)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Inverse-Square Law & Visibility Range

**Problem statement:** A pilot is flying at an altitude of 1,500 ft AGL and is approaching a 60 m AGL telecom tower fitted with an L-864 medium-intensity red light. The light has a rated effective intensity of **2,000 candela**. Determine the **maximum horizontal distance** at which the pilot can just detect the obstruction light, given a standard threshold illuminance of the human eye under night conditions of **$E_{min} = 0.001$ lux** (i.e., $10^{-3} \text{ lx}$).

### Step 1 — Recall the fundamental photometric law
The illuminance $E$ on a surface perpendicular to the line-of-sight from a point source of luminous intensity $I$ at distance $d$ is given by:

$$
E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}
$$

This is the **inverse-square law** of photometry, derived from the conservation of luminous flux through a spherical surface. (Note: in the full form with angle $\theta$, $E = I\cos\theta/d^2$.)

### Step 2 — Identify the threshold condition
For the pilot to *just detect* the light:

$$
E_{received} \ge E_{min}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\dfrac{I}{d^2} \ge E_{min}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the maximum detection distance
Rearranging:

$$
d^2 \le \dfrac{I}{E_{min}}
$$

$$
d_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{E_{min}}}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values
Given:
- $I = 2{,}000 \text{ cd}$
- $E_{min} = 10^{-3} \text{ lx}$

$$
d_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2{,}000}{10^{-3}}}
$$

### Step 5 — Intermediate computation
Inside the square root:

$$
\dfrac{2{,}000}{10^{-3}} = 2{,}000 \times 10^{3} = 2 \times 10^{6}
$$

### Step 6 — Take the square root

$$
d_{\max} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{6}} = \sqrt{2} \times 10^{3} \approx 1.414 \times 10^{3} \text{ m}
$$

### Step 7 — Final answer with units
**Logic check:** $d_{\max} \approx 1{,}414$ metres. This is a reasonable pre-detection range for a 2,000 cd red light at night. For daylight operation with the L-866 strobe of 200,000 cd, the same calculation yields $d_{\max} \approx 14{,}142$ m ≈ 14.1 km, which matches FAA design range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Level Spacing for High-Intensity Systems

**Problem statement:** A 280 m AGL chimney is to be marked per ICAO Annex 14. Determine the **number of light levels** required, and the **uniform vertical spacing** between successive levels.

### Step 1 — Apply the regulatory rule
Per ICAO Annex 14, Vol. I, §6.3.3 — for a structure exceeding 150 m AGL, high-intensity lights are required at:
- **Top level** (apex)
- **Intermediate levels** spaced at uniform intervals **not exceeding 105 m**
- **Bottom level** (optional, but recommended for frangibility reference)

### Step 2 — Calculate the maximum number of intervals
Total structure height $H = 280$ m.

$$
n_{intervals} = \left\lceil \dfrac{H}{s_{max}} \right\rceil
$$

where $s_{max} = 105$ m (FAA) or stricter 52 m (above 210 m, FAA refinement).

### Step 3 — Use the stricter rule for $H > 210$ m
Since $H = 280 \text{ m} > 210 \text{ m}$, we use $s_{max} = 52$ m:

$$
n_{intervals} = \left\lceil \dfrac{280}{52} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.38 \rceil = 6 \text{ intervals}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the actual uniform spacing
$$
s = \dfrac{280}{6} \approx 46.67 \text{ m}
$$

This satisfies $s \le 52$ m ✓.

### Step 5 — Number of light levels

$$
n_{levels} = n_{intervals} + 1 = 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ levels}
$$

(Levels at: 0 m, 46.67 m, 93.33 m, 140 m, 186.67 m, 233.33 m, 280 m)

### Step 6 — Add a top-most steady red light (L-810) on the apex
ICAO requires an **L-810 steady red light at the very top**, *in addition to* the L-866 strobe. This acts as a **burnt-out indicator** to maintenance crew, since a failed strobe is harder to detect visually than a failed steady-burning lamp.

**Final configuration:**
- 7 levels of L-866 high-intensity white strobes at 46.67 m spacing
- 1 additional L-810 steady red light at the apex
- 2 L-810 steady red lights at mid-points of the uppermost interval (if $> 52$ m, FAA guidance)
- Synchronised flash controller
- Dual-redundant power supply (mains + battery/UPS)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic Summary

| Structure Height (m AGL) | Distance to Aerodrome | Required Class | Light Type | # of Levels |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\le 45$ m | Any | **L-810** | Low intensity, red, steady | 1 (top) |
| 45–105 m | Any | **L-864** | Medium intensity, red, flashing | 1 (top) |
| 105–150 m | Far from runway | **L-864** | Medium intensity, red, flashing | 1 + base |
| 105–150 m | Near approach path | **L-865** | Medium intensity, white, flashing | 1 + base |
| 150–210 m | Any | **L-866** | High intensity, white, flashing | Top + 1 mid |
| $> 210$ m | Any | **L-866** + L-810 apex | HI white + LI red | Multiple ≤ 52 m |
| Catenary (transmission line) | > runway | **L-866** on towers + line markers | HI white | Each tower + cable |
| Wind farm | Near aerodrome | **L-864** on perimeter turbines | Medium red | Each nacelle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **distance to aerodrome** is measured from the **runway threshold** (FAA FAR 77.25 surfaces), not from the airport boundary.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define obstruction lighting. State the two main photometric parameters used to classify obstruction lights.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Obstruction lighting is a system of aviation warning lights installed on tall structures that penetrate or lie in the vicinity of aerodrome obstacle limitation surfaces, designed to make these obstacles visible to pilots during day, twilight, and night operations.

**Two Main Photometric Parameters:**
1. **Luminous Intensity (I):** Measured in candela (cd). Determines how bright the light appears at a given distance. Example: L-810 = 32.5 cd; L-866 = 200,000 cd (daytime).
2. **Color / Chromaticity:** Specified in CIE 1931 coordinates. Two permitted colors are **Aviation Red** (night) and **Aviation White** (day/twilight).

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU):**
- Definition: 1.5 marks
- Two parameters: 1.5 marks (0.75 each)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Distinguish between L-810 and L-864 obstruction lights. When is each specified?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | L-810 | L-864 |
|---|---|---|
| Class | Low intensity | Medium intensity |
| Color | Red | Red |
| Mode | Steady / fixed burning | Flashing (20–60 fpm) |
| Intensity | 32.5 cd minimum | 2,000 cd (effective) |
| Application | Structures ≤ 45 m AGL | Structures 45–150 m AGL |

**Specification rule:**
- L-810 is used for **low-height obstacles** (e.g., small buildings, antennas) where the structure does not exceed 45 m AGL and is not in the immediate approach path.
- L-864 is used for **medium-height structures** (e.g., telecom towers up to 150 m) that pose a credible collision risk but do not require high-intensity daylight visibility.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Tabular distinction: 2 marks
- Specification conditions: 1 mark

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2023]

**Question A:** *A 240 m AGL telecommunication tower is proposed 3.2 km from the runway threshold of an international airport. As an airport planning engineer, design the obstruction lighting system for this structure. Justify each design choice with reference to ICAO Annex 14 / FAA AC 70/7460-1.*

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** *Determine the class of obstruction lights, number of light levels, vertical spacing, and intensity requirements.*

### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1 — Check if the structure is an obstacle.**

Given:
- Height $H = 240 \text{ m AGL}$
- Distance from runway threshold $= 3.2 \text{ km} = 3{,}200 \text{ m}$

Per **FAA FAR 77.13**, any structure above 150 m AGL, OR any structure within 5 km of a runway that exceeds 45 m AGL, is an **obstacle**. Both conditions are satisfied.

**Step 2 — Determine the class.**

Per ICAO Annex 14, §6.3.3, structures exceeding **150 m AGL** require **High-Intensity (L-866)** white flashing lights, *in conjunction with* an **L-810** steady red light at the apex.

> **Justification (1 mark):** L-866 is chosen because:
> 1. The tower (240 m) exceeds the 150 m threshold.
> 2. It is within 3.2 km of an active runway, so it lies in the approach/take-off climb surface.
> 3. Daytime visibility demands high-intensity white (red would be washed out).

**Step 3 — Compute number of light levels.**

Apply the **52 m spacing rule** (for $H > 210$ m per FAA refinement):

$$
n_{intervals} = \left\lceil \dfrac{240}{52} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.615 \rceil = 5 \text{ intervals}
$$

$$
n_{levels} = 5 + 1 = 6 \text{ light levels}
$$

**Uniform spacing:**

$$
s = \dfrac{240}{5} = 48 \text{ m per interval}
$$

**Step 4 — Intensity requirements.**

| Operating Period | Required Effective Intensity (L-866) |
|---|---|
| Day | 200,000 cd |
| Twilight | 20,000 cd |
| Night | 2,000 cd |

A **photocell-controlled dimmer** automatically switches intensity based on ambient light.

**Final answer for (a):** **6 levels of L-866 white strobes at 48 m spacing + 1 L-810 steady red at the apex (280 m), with 40 fpm synchronised flash, and a photocell-controlled dimming system.**

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** *Draw a schematic showing the levels and explain the synchronisation, power supply, and maintenance considerations.*

### Model Solution (b):

**Schematic Description (text-based since drawing is for answer script):**

```
           +--- L-810 (steady red, apex marker) ---+
           |                                        |
   240 m --+-- LEVEL 6: L-866 strobe --------------+
           |                                        |
   192 m --+-- LEVEL 5: L-866 strobe --------------+
           |                                        |
   144 m --+-- LEVEL 4: L-866 strobe --------------+
           |                                        |
    96 m --+-- LEVEL 3: L-866 strobe --------------+
           |                                        |
    48 m --+-- LEVEL 2: L-866 strobe --------------+
           |                                        |
     0 m --+-- LEVEL 1: L-866 strobe (base) -------+
           |                                        |
           +--- Power: Mains 230 V AC              +
           +--- Backup: 48 V DC battery (8 hr)     +
           +--- Sync controller: GPS-disciplined   +
           +--- Telemetry: SCADA link to ATC       +
```

**Synchronisation (2 marks):**
- A **GPS-disciplined oscillator** or **mains-frequency-locked** flasher drives all six L-866 units to flash simultaneously at **40 fpm** (FAA standard).
- Synchronisation is critical: out-of-phase lights on a single structure mimic a **rotating aerodrome beacon**, leading to pilot misidentification.
- Where multiple structures exist within 5 km, all should ideally be on a common sync source.

**Power Supply (1.5 marks):**
- **Primary:** 230 V AC mains with automatic transfer switch.
- **Backup:** Sealed lead-acid (SLA) or lithium-ion battery bank providing **minimum 8 hours** of autonomy (ICAO recommendation) — important during monsoon/outage hours.
- **Optional:** Solar PV array with MPPT charge controller for remote sites.

**Maintenance (1.5 marks):**
- **Monthly:** Visual inspection of all lamps; photometric spot-check using a calibrated candela meter at 3 m.
- **Quarterly:** Battery load test, sync verification, photocell calibration.
- **Annually:** Complete photometric audit per **FAA AC 150/5345-43J**.
- **Remote monitoring:** SCADA / IoT telemetry to ATC tower for **real-time fault indication**, reducing inspection visits by ~60 %.

**Mark Distribution for (b):** Schematic: 2 | Synchronisation: 2 | Power: 1.5 | Maintenance: 1.5

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Question B:** *Explain, with sketches, the different types of obstruction lights used at aerodromes. Discuss the criteria for selection based on structure height, location, and time of operation.*

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** *Classify the obstruction lights and explain each type with its typical intensity and application.*

### Model Solution (a):

### Classification of Obstruction Lights (3 marks):

**(i) Low-Intensity Obstruction Lights — Type A & B (L-810):**
- **Intensity:** Minimum **32.5 cd** (Type A); **32.5 cd** for Type B (with infrared component).
- **Color:** Aviation Red.
- **Mode:** Steady burning (continuous).
- **Application:** Structures ≤ 45 m AGL, group of trees, small chimneys, and buildings in the vicinity of an aerodrome that do not pose a major daytime hazard.

**(ii) Medium-Intensity Obstruction Lights — Type B (L-864) and Type C (L-865):**
- **L-864:** Red, flashing at 20–40 fpm, **2,000 cd** effective intensity. Used for structures between 45 m and 150 m AGL.
- **L-865:** White, flashing at 40 fpm, **20,000 cd** effective intensity. Used when high visibility is needed during twilight or near approach paths.

**(iii) High-Intensity Obstruction Lights — Type A (L-866) and Type B (L-867):**
- **L-866:** White, flashing, **200,000 cd** daytime, dimmable. Used for structures exceeding 150 m AGL.
- **L-867:** White, flashing, used for very tall structures (over 210 m) with stepped intensity.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** *Selection criteria based on height, location, and time of operation.*

### Model Solution (b):

### Criteria for Selection:

**(A) Based on Structure Height (3 marks):**

| Height (m AGL) | Class of Light |
|---|---|
| ≤ 45 m | L-810 (low intensity, red, steady) |
| 45 – 150 m | L-864 (medium intensity, red) or L-865 (white) |
| 150 – 210 m | L-866 (high intensity, white) |
| > 210 m | L-866 (multiple levels ≤ 52 m apart) + L-810 (apex) |

**(B) Based on Location / Proximity (2 marks):**

- **Within 5 km of runway threshold:** Higher class required (L-865 or L-866).
- **Catenary / transmission line crossing:** Towers fitted with L-866; cable itself fitted with **L-810 markers at 45 m spacing** if the line crosses a flight path.
- **Wind farms:** Perimeter turbines fitted with L-864 if the farm is within 5 km of an aerodrome.
- **Population density rule (FAA):** If a structure is in an area with > 8,500 inhabitants within 5 km, **white strobes are preferred** over red to avoid light pollution complaints.

**(C) Based on Time of Operation (2 marks):**

- **Day:** High-intensity white (L-866/L-867) is **mandatory** because sunlight washes out red lights. Effective intensity ≥ 200,000 cd.
- **Twilight:** Reduced-intensity white or switch to red (L-865 → L-864 transition).
- **Night:** Red lights (L-810/L-864) are preferred to **minimise pilot disorientation** and **light pollution** to nearby residents. Intensity is dimmed to 2,000 cd (L-866 night mode).

A **photocell** automatically switches the system between day, twilight, and night modes, typically at **luminance thresholds of 500 lux (day) and 50 lux (night)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Confusing L-864 with L-865:** L-864 is *red*, L-865 is *white*. Many students swap them. Loss of 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the L-810 steady red at the apex of high-intensity systems:** A common omission. Loss of 1 mark.
> 3. **Incorrect unit for luminous intensity:** Always use **candela (cd)**, not lumens. Loss of 0.5 mark per occurrence.
> 4. **Missing the synchronisation requirement:** State "**simultaneous flashing at 40 fpm**" or lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Not specifying the power backup:** Examiners expect mention of **mains + battery/UPS** in the design solution.
> 6. **Ignoring the "below-horizontal" beam requirement:** A vertical beam spread of 3°–7° above horizontal is mandatory; neglecting this loses 1 mark in design questions.
> 7. **Conflating "obstruction light" with "aerodrome beacon":** Aerodrome beacon = rotating, identifies the airport itself. Obstruction lights = steady/flashing on a structure, identifies an obstacle. This is a 2-mark trap question in Part A.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):**

- [x] **Definition:** Obstruction lighting = warning lights on structures penetrating OLS.
- [x] **Three classes:** Low (L-810), Medium (L-864/L-865), High (L-866/L-867).
- [x] **Color rule:** **Red for night**, **White for day/twilight**.
- [x] **Intensity rule:** 32.5 cd → 2,000 cd → 200,000 cd (low → medium → high).
- [x] **Height rule:** ≤ 45 m → L-810; 45–150 m → L-864; 150–210 m → L-866; > 210 m → L-866 + L-810 apex.
- [x] **Spacing rule:** ≤ 52 m vertical for $H > 210$ m; ≤ 105 m for $150 < H \le 210$ m.
- [x] **Flash rate:** 20–60 fpm (medium); 40 fpm (high); synchronous across all lights.
- [x] **Beam spread:** 3°–7° above horizontal; 360° azimuthal.
- [x] **Photometric equation:** $E = I / d^2$ (inverse square law).
- [x] **Detection range example:** L-866 day → ~14 km; L-810 night → ~180 m.
- [x] **Apex marker:** L-810 steady red on top of every high-intensity structure.
- [x] **Catenary rule:** L-810 on cable every 45 m; L-866 on towers.
- [x] **Power:** Mains + battery (8 h autonomy).
- [x] **Standards:** **ICAO Annex 14 Vol. I Ch. 6**; **FAA AC 70/7460-1**; **IS 2268 (India)**.
- [x] **Colorimetric coords:** Red $(x \approx 0.700, y \approx 0.300)$; White $(x = 0.310, y = 0.316)$ — CIE 1931.
- [x] **Photocell threshold:** ~500 lux (day), ~50 lux (night) for automatic switching.
- [x] **Population rule:** > 8,500 inhabitants / 5 km → use white strobes to limit light pollution complaints.
- [x] **Frangibility:** Low-intensity units on structures ≤ 45 m must be frangible (breakable on impact) per ICAO.
- [x] **Telecommunication towers** are the largest installed base globally; **wind turbines** need case-by-case FAA study.
- [x] **Wind-turbine specific:** L-864 on perimeter turbines + HAPI on the highest; rotor blades may need tip markers.
- [x] **KTU-favourite topics:** 52 m spacing rule, photometry equation, color chromaticity, dual-lighting system, synchronisation.

> **Final Note:** Obstruction lighting is a **"high-marks-for-low-effort"** topic in KTU exams. Memorise the three classes, the height/colour matrix, and the inverse-square law — these alone can secure **12–14 marks** in a full Part-B question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approach Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Approach Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approach Lighting System (ALS):** A configuration of aeronautical ground lights installed in the approach area of a runway, beginning at the runway threshold and extending outward into the approach zone (typically 300 m to 900 m), designed to provide **visual guidance** to pilots during the transition from **instrument flight to visual flight**, enabling safe alignment, height estimation, and roll guidance during the final approach and landing phases.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (ICAO Annex 14, Volume I, Chapter 5 aligned), the Approach Lighting System is classified by **operational performance category** and **physical configuration**:

| ICAO Designation | Full Form | Operational Use |
|------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| SALS | Simple Approach Lighting System | Non-precision approaches |
| MALS | Medium Intensity ALS | Non-precision (Cat I) |
| MALSF | MALS with Sequenced Flashing lights | Non-precision (improved) |
| MALSR | MALS with Runway Alignment Indicator Lights (RAIL) | Non-precision (Cat I) |
| SSALR | Simplified Short ALS with RAIL | Non-precision (military/civil) |
| ALSF-1 | Approach Lighting System with Sequenced Flashing lights, Full length | Cat I precision |
| ALSF-2 | ALSF with Sequenced Flashing lights, Full length, Red side bars | Cat II/III precision |
| CALVERT | British High Intensity ALS | Cat II/III precision |
| ODALS | Omnidirectional ALS | Military/Contingency |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Highway Exit Guide":**
> Imagine you are driving on a national highway at night, in fog, at 100 km/h. As you approach your exit, you see **overhead gantry signs, reflective chevrons, blinking strobes, and a deceleration lane marked by white edge lines**. Each of these elements progressively gives you:
> 1. **Direction** (which way is the exit?)
> 2. **Alignment** (am I centered?)
> 3. **Distance cues** (how far do I still have to go?)
> 4. **A focal "target"** (the off-ramp itself)
>
> The **Approach Lighting System works exactly the same way** for an aircraft on final approach. The **300–900 m corridor of lights acts as a "highway in the sky,"** guiding the pilot from the moment they break out of the clouds (or transition from instruments) until the wheels touch the runway. Without it, the pilot would have **no visual cues** for the last, most critical seconds of flight.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Design Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard ALS Design Parameters (ICAO Annex 14 / FAA AC 150/5340-30J):**
> - **Longitudinal extent of ALS:** 300 m (minimum, non-precision) up to **900 m** (full Cat II/III)
> - **Centerline bar width:** approximately **4.5 m** (15 ft) on each side of extended runway centerline
> - **Crossbar spacing:** at **150 m** and **300 m** from threshold (some configs add 450 m and 600 m)
> - **Light spacing along centerline:** typically every **30 m**
> - **Frangibility:** all light units must collapse under a load of **≤ 4.5 kN (1,000 lb)** applied at any point
> - **Color:** white light throughout the approach (except red side-barrettes in ALSF-2)
> - **Light intensity:** adjustable in **5 or more steps** to suit pilot's ambient brightness
> - **Threshold Crossing Height (TCH):** **15 m to 20 m** (50–60 ft) for PAPI-equipped runways

## 1.4 Functional Purpose — The Three Pilot Information Streams

The ALS simultaneously communicates three pieces of critical information to the pilot:

1. **Azimuth (Alignment) Guidance** — Provided by the centerline lights and the sequenced flashing (strobe) lights, helping the pilot maintain runway centerline.
2. **Elevation (Glide Path) Guidance** — Provided by PAPI/VASI units located beside the runway (functionally integrated with the approach corridor).
3. **Distance-to-Threshold / Transition Cue** — Provided by crossbars at fixed distances, helping the pilot gauge **when to transition from instruments to outside visual reference.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — Why Each Component Exists

### 2.1.1 The Need for Approach Lighting (The "Why")

During **instrument meteorological conditions (IMC)**, a pilot descends along a **glide slope** defined purely by electronic instruments (ILS localizer + glideslope, or RNAV LPV). At a certain point called the **Decision Altitude (DA)** or **Decision Height (DH)**, the pilot must:

- Acquire sufficient **visual references** to continue the approach.
- Decide whether to **continue the landing** or execute a **missed approach**.

The ALS exists to:
1. **Lower the DH** by providing early visual cues (more lights = lower minimums).
2. **Reduce pilot workload** during the critical transition phase.
3. **Enable higher aircraft approach speeds** safely.
4. **Provide redundancy** if one or more electronic aids fail.

### 2.1.2 The Sequential Logic of the Approach

```
1. Aircraft on ILS/RNAV approach (pure instruments)
        ↓
2. Pilot reaches Decision Altitude (DA)
        ↓
3. Pilot searches for ALS lights in the runway approach zone
        ↓
4. Sequenced flashing lights "roll" toward the threshold
        ↓
5. Pilot acquires threshold and runway markings
        ↓
6. Transition to visual landing (with PAPI cross-check)
        ↓
7. Touchdown
```

## 2.2 Detailed Component Breakdown

### A) The Centerline Bar (Longitudinal Row)
- **300 to 900 white lights** placed along the extended runway centerline.
- Provides **azimuth alignment** and a sense of **motion parallax** (perspective compression) that helps the pilot sense **closure rate** and **height above threshold**.

### B) The Crossbars (Horizontal Rows of Lights)
- **First crossbar** at 300 m from threshold.
- **Second crossbar** at 600 m (in 900 m systems).
- Function: provides **height perception** and **roll guidance**. As the aircraft descends, the crossbar appears to move upward in the windscreen, giving a strong **vertical velocity cue**.

### C) Sequenced Flashing Lights (Strobes / "The Rabbit")
- A series of high-intensity **flashing/strobing lights** along the centerline.
- They flash in **sequence from the outermost light toward the threshold** (1–2 times per second).
- Function: draws the pilot's attention to the runway and creates a **"rolling ball"** effect that guides the eye inward.
- Perceived as a "ball of light" rolling down the corridor.

### D) Threshold Wing Bars
- A pair of green bars at the runway threshold (5 lights on each side, perpendicular to the runway).
- Marks the **start of the usable landing surface** and provides **lateral alignment** at the moment of touchdown decision.

### E) PAPI (Precision Approach Path Indicator) — *Adjacent to but functionally part of approach guidance*
- A 2-unit or 4-unit light bar on the **left side of the runway**.
- Each unit emits a high-intensity beam split into **red (below glide path)** and **white (above glide path)**.
- Pilot sees: 4 white = too high; 2 white + 2 red = on path; 4 red = too low.

## 2.3 ICAO/FAA Categories of Approach Lighting (Detailed)

| System | Length | Centerline Lights | Crossbars | Sequenced Flashing | Approach Category |
|--------|--------|-------------------|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| SALS | 300 m | Bar | At 300 m only | No | Non-precision only |
| MALS | 300 m | Bar | At 300 m only | No | Non-precision (Cat I) |
| MALSR | 300 m | Bar | At 300 m only | Yes (RAIL) | Non-precision |
| SSALR | 420 m | Bar | At 300 m | Yes | Non-precision (military) |
| ALSF-1 | 900 m | Bar + 5m extensions | At 300 + 600 m | Yes | Cat I precision |
| ALSF-2 | 900 m | Bar + red side bars | At 300 + 600 m | Yes | Cat II/III precision |
| CALVERT I | 900 m | Bar | At 300 + 600 m | Yes | Cat I |
| CALVERT II/III | 900 m | Bar | At 300 + 600 m | Yes (intense) | Cat II/III |
| ODALS | 300 m | 5 lights + strobes | No | Yes | Contingency |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are ICAO/FAA standard. Use these for ESE numericals.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Standard Value |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $\tan \theta = \dfrac{h}{d}$ | Glide path angle from threshold | $\theta$ in radians/degrees |
| 2 | $TCH = d_{\text{PAPI}} \cdot \tan(3^\circ)$ | Threshold Crossing Height for standard 3° PAPI | TCH ≈ 15–20 m (50–60 ft) |
| 3 | $D_{vis} = L_{ALS} + L_{run}$ | Total visual segment = ALS length + approach run distance | meters |
| 4 | $h_{DA} - h_{TCH} = L_{ALS} \cdot \tan \theta$ | DH above TCH equals ALS length × tan(glide path) | meters |
| 5 | $DH = h_{TCH} + L_{ALS} \cdot \tan(3^\circ)$ | Decision Height = TCH + Approach Light Corridor Contribution | meters / feet |
| 6 | $L_{ALS} = \dfrac{DH - h_{TCH}}{\tan(3^\circ)}$ | Required ALS length to support a given DH | meters |
| 7 | $RVR_{min} = f(\text{category})$ | Runway Visual Range minimums: Cat I = 550 m, Cat II = 300 m, Cat IIIA = 175 m, Cat IIIB = 50 m, Cat IIIC = 0 m | meters |
| 8 | $t_{transition} = \dfrac{DH - h_{TCH}}{V_{descent}}$ | Time to transition from DH to TCH | seconds |
| 9 | $V_{descent} = V_{approach} \cdot \sin \theta$ | Vertical (descent) component of approach speed | m/s or ft/min |
| 10 | $P_{max} = 4.5 \text{ kN}$ | Maximum frangibility load for ALS light unit | kN |

> **Where:**
> - $h$ = pilot eye height above threshold
> - $d$ = horizontal distance from threshold
> - $TCH$ = Threshold Crossing Height
> - $DH$ = Decision Height
> - $L_{ALS}$ = Approach Light System longitudinal length
> - $V_{descent}$ = vertical descent velocity
> - $V_{approach}$ = indicated airspeed
> - $\theta$ = glide path angle (3° standard ILS)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Runway classification & certification:** Runways serving Cat II/III operations **must** have full ALSF-2 or equivalent. This dictates the airport's category of operations and the airlines that can use it.
- **All-weather operations:** Without an adequate ALS, an airport is effectively "weather-bound" — flights are diverted during low visibility, costing airlines millions.
- **Civil-military dual use:** ODALS systems allow military fields to be used in IMC without the cost of a full ALSF-2.
- **Frangibility engineering:** ALS light units are designed as **low-impact hazards** — they break away if struck, reducing damage to aircraft that veer off the runway.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Decision Height (DH) and Approach Light System (ALS) Length

### Problem Statement
A pilot on a standard 3° ILS glide path descends from a Decision Height (DH) to the Threshold Crossing Height (TCH). If the aircraft must be able to **acquire the runway environment** (including the ALS) by the time it reaches DA, derive the **minimum required ALS longitudinal length**.

### Given
- Glide path angle: $\theta = 3^\circ$
- Standard Threshold Crossing Height: $h_{TCH} = 50 \text{ ft} = 15.24 \text{ m}$
- Required Decision Height: $DH = 200 \text{ ft} = 60.96 \text{ m}$

### Find
- The minimum length of the approach light corridor ($L_{ALS}$) so that the pilot transitions visually inside the lit zone.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Geometry of the approach**

The aircraft descends along a straight line inclined at $\theta$ to the horizontal. The horizontal distance $L$ traversed while losing altitude $\Delta h$ is given by trigonometry:

$$
\tan \theta = \frac{\Delta h}{L}
$$

**Step 2: Express $\Delta h$**

The altitude lost from DA to TCH is:

$$
\Delta h = DH - h_{TCH}
$$

Substituting:

$$
L_{ALS} = \frac{DH - h_{TCH}}{\tan \theta}
$$

**Step 3: Plug in numerical values (DH = 200 ft, TCH = 50 ft, θ = 3°)**

First compute the numerator:

$$
\Delta h = 200 \text{ ft} - 50 \text{ ft} = 150 \text{ ft}
$$

Then compute the denominator:

$$
\tan(3^\circ) = 0.05241
$$

Therefore:

$$
L_{ALS} = \frac{150}{0.05241}
$$

Compute the quotient step by step:

$$
L_{ALS} = 150 \div 0.05241 = 150 \times \frac{1}{0.05241}
$$

Since $1/0.05241 \approx 19.0811$:

$$
L_{ALS} = 150 \times 19.0811 = 2{,}862.17 \text{ ft}
$$

Convert to meters:

$$
L_{ALS} = 2{,}862.17 \text{ ft} \times 0.3048 \text{ m/ft} = 872.34 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Required ALS length ≈ 872 m.** Since ICAO standard ALSF systems come in discrete lengths (300, 420, 720, 900 m), we round up to **900 m**, which corresponds to a full **ALSF-2** (suitable for Cat II/III operations).

**Verification check:** For $\theta = 3^\circ$ and $L_{ALS} = 900 \text{ m}$:

$$
\Delta h_{available} = 900 \cdot \tan(3^\circ) = 900 \times 0.05241 = 47.17 \text{ m} \approx 154.7 \text{ ft}
$$

This comfortably supports a **DH up to TCH + 154.7 ft = 204.7 ft** — confirming the 200 ft DA is feasible.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Threshold Crossing Height (TCH) for PAPI Installation

### Problem Statement
PAPI units are located at a fixed distance from the threshold on the left side of the runway. The PAPI glide angle is set so that the pilot's eye passes over the PAPI at the design TCH. Derive the **required TCH** if PAPI units are placed at 300 m from the threshold with a glide angle of 3°.

### Given
- PAPI-to-threshold horizontal distance: $d_{PAPI} = 300 \text{ m}$
- Glide path angle: $\theta = 3^\circ$

### Find
- TCH

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: From the geometry of the right triangle formed by threshold, PAPI, and aircraft on glide path:**

$$
\tan \theta = \frac{TCH}{d_{PAPI}}
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange for TCH:**

$$
TCH = d_{PAPI} \cdot \tan \theta
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values:**

$$
TCH = 300 \text{ m} \times \tan(3^\circ)
$$

We know $\tan(3^\circ) = 0.05241$.

$$
TCH = 300 \times 0.05241
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$
TCH = 300 \times 0.05 + 300 \times 0.00241
$$

$$
TCH = 15.00 + 0.723 = 15.723 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to feet for the international standard:**

$$
TCH = 15.723 \text{ m} \times 3.281 \text{ ft/m} = 51.58 \text{ ft}
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TCH ≈ 15.72 m (51.6 ft).** This falls within the ICAO-recommended range of **15 m to 20 m**, confirming that placing the PAPI at 300 m with a 3° angle yields a design-conformant TCH.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transition Time from Decision Altitude to Threshold

### Problem Statement
For a Boeing 737-800 on final approach at 140 knots indicated airspeed (KIAS) on a 3° glide path, calculate the **time available** for the pilot to transition from instrument references to visual ALS acquisition.

### Given
- Indicated airspeed: $V_{IAS} = 140 \text{ knots} = 140 \times 0.5144 = 72.02 \text{ m/s}$
- Glide path angle: $\theta = 3^\circ$

### Find
- Descent rate $V_{descent}$
- Time to descend from DH = 200 ft to TCH = 50 ft

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute vertical (descent) speed component:**

$$
V_{descent} = V_{approach} \cdot \sin \theta
$$

$$
V_{descent} = 72.02 \times \sin(3^\circ)
$$

Since $\sin(3^\circ) = 0.05234$:

$$
V_{descent} = 72.02 \times 0.05234
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
V_{descent} = 70 \times 0.05234 + 2.02 \times 0.05234
$$

$$
V_{descent} = 3.6638 + 0.1057 = 3.7695 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to feet per minute (the more common pilot unit):**

$$
V_{descent} = 3.7695 \text{ m/s} \times 3.281 \text{ ft/s per m/s} \times 60 \text{ s/min}
$$

$$
V_{descent} = 3.7695 \times 196.85 = 742.1 \text{ ft/min}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the time to descend from DH to TCH:**

Altitude loss: $\Delta h = 200 - 50 = 150 \text{ ft}$.

$$
t = \frac{\Delta h}{V_{descent}} = \frac{150}{742.1}
$$

$$
t = 0.2022 \text{ minutes} = 0.2022 \times 60 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
t = 12.13 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The pilot has only ~12 seconds to acquire the ALS, transition from instruments to outside visual references, and confirm continuation of the approach.** This narrow window is exactly why the ALS must be **bright, long, sequenced, and unambiguous** — it is the pilot's lifeline.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right ALS for a Given Operation

| Operational Need | Required DH | Required RVR | Recommended ALS | Justification |
|------------------|-------------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| VFR day-only | N/A | 1500 m | SALS | Minimal cost; VMC only |
| Non-precision (GPS/LPV) | 250–400 ft | 1000 m | MALS / MALSR | Light intensity sufficient for non-precision |
| Cat I Precision ILS | 200 ft (60 m) | 550 m | ALSF-1 / MALSR | 720–900 m corridor aids breakout |
| Cat II Precision ILS | 100 ft (30 m) | 300 m | ALSF-2 | Full 900 m + red side bars required |
| Cat IIIA Precision ILS | 50 ft (15 m) | 175 m | ALSF-2 | Same as Cat II but with high-intensity RVR |
| Cat IIIB Precision ILS | < 50 ft | 50 m | ALSF-2 + touchdown/centerline lights | Touchdown zone lights become essential |
| Cat IIIC Precision ILS | 0 ft (no DH) | 0 m | Full ALSF-2 + full TDZ + RTCL | Zero-zero operations |
| Military contingency | 200 ft | 800 m | ODALS | Portable, low-cost strobe-only |

---

## 3.5 Engineering Worked Example — Class Test Standard

### Question
A runway is being designed for **Category II operations**. The design Decision Altitude is **100 ft AGL**, and the Threshold Crossing Height is **50 ft AGL**. The PAPI glide path angle is the standard **3°**.

**(a)** Compute the minimum required approach light system length.  
**(b)** If the design Indicated Airspeed for the critical aircraft is 130 knots, compute the time available for the pilot to make a "go/no-go" decision.

### Solution

**Part (a):**

$$
L_{ALS} = \frac{DH - TCH}{\tan(3^\circ)} = \frac{100 - 50}{0.05241} = \frac{50}{0.05241} = 954.0 \text{ ft}
$$

Convert to meters: $954.0 \times 0.3048 = 290.78 \text{ m}$.

> This is the **minimum** to support Cat II. Since ICAO standard ALS comes in fixed lengths, the next step up is the **full 900 m ALSF-2**, which is more than adequate.

**Part (b):**

Convert airspeed: $V = 130 \text{ kts} \times 0.5144 = 66.87 \text{ m/s}$.

Descent rate: $V_{descent} = 66.87 \times \sin(3^\circ) = 66.87 \times 0.05234 = 3.500 \text{ m/s}$.

In ft/min: $V_{descent} = 3.500 \times 196.85 = 688.97 \text{ ft/min}$.

Altitude to lose: $100 - 50 = 50 \text{ ft}$.

Transition time: $t = 50 / 688.97 = 0.0726 \text{ min} = 4.35 \text{ s}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Only 4.35 seconds** of visual acquisition window — emphatically justifying the full ALSF-2 with bright, sequenced flashing lights and high-intensity threshold bars.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Approach Lighting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["PILOT ON ILS APPROACH"]
        A1["Aircraft on 3° glide path"]
    end

    subgraph B["DECISION HEIGHT CROSSING"]
        B1["Pilot searches for runway environment"]
        B2["Aircraft at DA: e.g., 200 ft AGL"]
    end

    subgraph C["APPROACH LIGHT CORRIDOR 900m"]
        C1["Outer 300 m: Bar of 9 lights, every 30 m"]
        C2["Sequenced flashing lights, 1 Hz pulse, rolling toward threshold"]
        C3["Mid 300 m: Crossbar at 600 m, 9 lights"]
        C4["Inner 300 m: Crossbar at 300 m, threshold-aligned"]
    end

    subgraph D["THRESHOLD ZONE"]
        D1["Threshold wing-bars: green, 5 lights per side"]
        D2["Runway threshold lights: green across threshold"]
    end

    subgraph E["RUNWAY TOUCHDOWN ZONE"]
        E1["PAPI units on left, 4 boxes, 300 m from threshold"]
        E2["Touchdown zone lights: white bars, 900 m"]
        E3["Runway centerline lights: white, every 15 m"]
        E4["Runway edge lights: white, every 60 m"]
    end

    subgraph F["PILOT DECISION & TOUCHDOWN"]
        F1["Visual confirmation: PAPI 2W 2R"]
        F2["Decision: continue or go-around"]
        F3["Wheels down on threshold zone"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**"Define Approach Lighting System (ALS). List any four ICAO classifications of ALS with their typical lengths."**

**Model Answer:**

> An **Approach Lighting System (ALS)** is a configuration of aeronautical ground lights installed in the approach area of a runway, beginning at the threshold and extending outward, designed to provide visual guidance to pilots during transition from instrument to visual flight.
>
> Four ICAO classifications:
> 1. **SALS** — Simple Approach Lighting System, 300 m.
> 2. **MALS** — Medium Intensity Approach Lighting System, 300 m.
> 3. **ALSF-1** — Approach Lighting System with Sequenced Flashing lights, 900 m.
> 4. **ALSF-2** — ALSF with red side-barrettes, 900 m, for Cat II/III.
>
> *(Optional fifth: MALSR, CALVERT, ODALS for full credit)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**"What is the significance of 'sequenced flashing lights' in an Approach Lighting System? How do they appear to a pilot on approach?"**

**Model Answer:**

> **Significance:** Sequenced flashing lights (also called "strobes" or "the rabbit") draw the pilot's attention to the runway during the critical visual acquisition phase, especially in low-visibility conditions. They improve the **detection range** of the runway environment and **reduce the DH** at which the pilot can transition to visual references.
>
> **Appearance to pilot:** The lights flash in rapid sequence from the outermost light inward toward the threshold, creating the illusion of a **"ball of light" rolling down the approach corridor** at 1–2 cycles per second. This rolling motion strongly attracts the eye and provides an unambiguous **direction-of-motion cue** toward the runway.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the components and layout of a **full ALSF-2 Approach Lighting System** as specified by ICAO for Category II/III runways. State the function of each major component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A runway has a Decision Height (DH) of **150 ft AGL** and a Threshold Crossing Height (TCH) of **50 ft**. The standard ILS glide path angle is **3°**. Calculate the **minimum length of the approach light system** required. State which standard ICAO ALS configuration would be selected and why. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

The **ALSF-2** is the most complete approach lighting system, designed for **Cat II/III precision approaches** at runways with all-weather operations.

**Layout and components (from outer end → threshold):**

1. **Sequenced Flashing Lights (Strobes / "RABBIT")** — A series of high-intensity flashing lights placed along the centerline, flashing in sequence from outermost to threshold. They create a "ball of light" rolling effect that guides the pilot's eye inward. *(1 mark)*

2. **Centerline Bar of Steady-Burning White Lights** — Approximately 300+ lights spaced at 30 m intervals along the extended runway centerline. Provides azimuth guidance and motion parallax cues for closure rate and height perception. *(1 mark)*

3. **Crossbar at 600 m from threshold** — A horizontal row of lights perpendicular to the centerline, providing height and roll cues. *(1 mark)*

4. **Crossbar at 300 m from threshold** — A second crossbar closer to the runway, used by pilots to gauge final approach. *(1 mark)*

5. **Red Side-Barrettes** — Distinctive feature of ALSF-2. Red lights on each side of the centerline in the inner 300 m. They provide an unmistakable "caution/inner-zone" visual reference. *(1 mark)*

6. **Threshold Wing-Bars (Green)** — Two clusters of 5 green lights at the threshold, marking the start of the usable landing surface. *(1 mark)*

7. **Runway Threshold Lights (Green across)** — A row of green lights across the threshold, denoting the beginning of the runway. *(1 mark)*

> **Sketch:** *(See Section 4.2 of these notes for the plan-view schematic.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $DH = 150 \text{ ft AGL}$
- $TCH = 50 \text{ ft AGL}$
- $\theta = 3^\circ$

**Step 1: Compute altitude to be lost between DA and TCH.**

$$
\Delta h = DH - TCH = 150 - 50 = 100 \text{ ft}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2: Apply the geometry of the glide path.**

$$
\tan \theta = \frac{\Delta h}{L_{ALS}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
L_{ALS} = \frac{\Delta h}{\tan \theta} = \frac{100}{\tan(3^\circ)}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3: Substitute the value of $\tan(3^\circ)$.**

$$
\tan(3^\circ) = 0.05241
$$

$$
L_{ALS} = \frac{100}{0.05241}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4: Compute the numerical value.**

$$
L_{ALS} = 1{,}907.94 \text{ ft}
$$

Convert to meters:

$$
L_{ALS} = 1{,}907.94 \times 0.3048 = 581.51 \text{ m}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5: Conclusion and selection.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Required minimum ALS length ≈ 582 m.** Since ICAO ALS systems come in fixed standard lengths, the next step up is the **ALSF-2 with 900 m corridor** (or CALVERT II/III equivalent). The runway would be classified as **Category II/III capable**. The 900 m system provides a margin of safety of approximately **318 m** of additional visual acquisition distance.

*(1 mark)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **PAPI (Precision Approach Path Indicator)** system. With a diagram, describe how it provides glide-path guidance to a pilot. State the standard glide-path angle and the design TCH for a 300 m PAPI installation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An aircraft on a 3° ILS final approach has an Indicated Airspeed of **150 knots**. The Decision Height is **200 ft AGL**, and the TCH is **50 ft AGL**. Calculate:
- (i) the vertical descent rate in **ft/min**
- (ii) the **time available** for the pilot to transition from instrument to visual references between DA and TCH. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**PAPI Definition:**

The **Precision Approach Path Indicator (PAPI)** is a system of **4 (or 2) light units** located beside the runway (typically on the left side, at a fixed distance from the threshold) that provides the pilot with **continuous visual glide-path guidance** during the final approach. *(1 mark)*

**Optical Principle:**

Each PAPI unit consists of a **high-intensity light** with two-color filters:
- **Upper beam — WHITE** → seen when aircraft is **above** the glide path.
- **Lower beam — RED** → seen when aircraft is **below** the glide path.

The transition between white and red is **sharp and angular**, set to a precise angle below the horizontal. *(1 mark)*

**Pilot Interpretation (with 4-unit PAPI):**

| Pilot sees | Meaning | Action |
|------------|---------|--------|
| 4 WHITE | Way too high | Reduce descent |
| 3W + 1R | Slightly high | Minor correction |
| **2W + 2R** | **On glide path** | **Maintain** |
| 1W + 3R | Slightly low | Minor correction up |
| 4 RED | Way too low | **Go around** |

*(2 marks)*

**Standard PAPI Parameters:**

- **Glide-path angle:** $\theta = 3^\circ$ (standard ILS)
- **PAPI-to-threshold distance:** $d = 300 \text{ m}$
- **TCH for 3° PAPI at 300 m:**

$$
TCH = d \cdot \tan(3^\circ) = 300 \times 0.05241 = 15.72 \text{ m} \approx 51.6 \text{ ft}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.)*

*(1 mark for the descriptive text on the schematic.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V_{IAS} = 150 \text{ knots}$
- $DH = 200 \text{ ft}$
- $TCH = 50 \text{ ft}$
- $\theta = 3^\circ$

**Step 1: Convert indicated airspeed to m/s.**

$$
V = 150 \times 0.5144 = 77.16 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2: Compute vertical descent rate in m/s.**

$$
V_{descent} = V \cdot \sin \theta = 77.16 \times \sin(3^\circ)
$$

$$
\sin(3^\circ) = 0.05234
$$

$$
V_{descent} = 77.16 \times 0.05234 = 4.039 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3: Convert to ft/min.**

$$
V_{descent} = 4.039 \text{ m/s} \times 3.281 \text{ ft/s per m/s} \times 60 \text{ s/min}
$$

$$
V_{descent} = 4.039 \times 196.85 = 795.1 \text{ ft/min}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4: Compute transition time.**

Altitude to lose: $\Delta h = 200 - 50 = 150 \text{ ft}$.

$$
t = \frac{\Delta h}{V_{descent}} = \frac{150}{795.1}
$$

$$
t = 0.1887 \text{ min} = 0.1887 \times 60 = 11.32 \text{ seconds}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5: Conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **(i) Descent rate = 795 ft/min.**  
> **(ii) Transition time = 11.32 seconds.**  
> This is a **very narrow window** for visual acquisition — it is the reason why the full **ALSF-2 with sequenced flashing lights** is mandatory for Cat II/III operations.

*(1 mark for interpretation and final answer statement.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing feet and meters in the same problem. The standard ICAO unit is **meters**, but FAA uses **feet**. Always state the unit and convert explicitly. *(−1 to −2 marks lost for every inconsistent unit.)*
> 2. **Wrong tan vs. sin:** Glide-path geometry uses **tan θ = h/d** (rise/run). Descent rate uses **sin θ**. Confusing these two gives wildly wrong answers. *(−2 marks per error.)*
> 3. **Forgetting to "round up" to a standard system:** Students often compute the required length and stop there. The examiner expects you to **map to a standard ICAO system** (SALS / MALS / ALSF-1 / ALSF-2 / CALVERT). Failing to do so loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **PAPI placement distance:** The 300 m figure is **standard** but not universal. Some airports use 250 m or 350 m. Always read the question carefully.
> 5. **Sequenced flashing direction:** Students sometimes write that the lights flash "from threshold outward." This is **WRONG.** They flash **from outer end toward the threshold** to create the "ball rolling in" effect.
> 6. **Frangibility omission:** Listing components without mentioning frangibility (load ≤ 4.5 kN) loses a key ICAO design mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Approach Lighting**

- **Definition:** Ground lighting from threshold outward (300–900 m) for visual guidance during IMC-to-VMC transition.
- **Standard length options:** 300 m (SALS/MALS), 420 m (SSALR), 720 m (ALSF-1 light), 900 m (ALSF-1/ALSF-2/CALVERT).
- **Crossbars:** Located at 300 m and 600 m from threshold.
- **Light spacing on centerline:** every 30 m.
- **Sequenced flashing lights:** flash from **outer to threshold** at 1–2 Hz, creating "rolling ball" effect.
- **ALSF-2 differentiator:** Red side-barrettes in the inner 300 m → specifically for Cat II/III.
- **Frangibility:** All units must collapse at ≤ **4.5 kN** load.
- **PAPI:** 4-unit (or 2-unit) bar at 300 m from threshold, left side, 3° glide path, TCH ≈ 15.7 m.
- **PAPI colors:** White = above, Red = below; "2W + 2R" = on path.
- **Decision Height formula:** $DH = TCH + L_{ALS} \cdot \tan \theta$ (with $\theta = 3°$).
- **Required ALS length formula:** $L_{ALS} = (DH - TCH) / \tan(3°)$.
- **Vertical descent rate formula:** $V_{descent} = V_{approach} \cdot \sin(3°)$.
- **Transition time formula:** $t = (DH - TCH) / V_{descent}$.
- **RVR minimums:** Cat I = 550 m, Cat II = 300 m, Cat IIIA = 175 m, Cat IIIB = 50 m, Cat IIIC = 0 m.
- **Threshold lights:** GREEN (across the threshold and wing-bars).
- **Runway edge lights:** WHITE (last 600 m of runway turn YELLOW as caution zone).
- **Runway end lights:** RED.
- **Touchdown zone lights:** WHITE bars in the first 900 m of the runway.
- **Key relationship:** Longer ALS → Lower DH → Better all-weather capability → Higher airport category.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Runway Lighting
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Runway Lighting

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Runway Lighting** is a critical sub-system of airport airside electrical infrastructure comprising an array of aeronautical ground lights (AGL) installed on, alongside, and approaching the runway to provide **visual guidance to pilots** during takeoff, landing, taxiing, and ground movement, especially under conditions of reduced visibility (night, fog, rain).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Runway lighting is the standardized illumination system consisting of approach lights, threshold lights, runway edge lights, runway end lights, runway centerline lights, touchdown zone lights, taxiway lead-off lights, stop bars, and visual approach slope indicators (PAPI/VASI), designed per ICAO Annex 14 and FAA AC 150/5345 specifications.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine driving on a **highway at night without streetlights** — you would have great difficulty keeping the vehicle centered, judging distances to exits, and stopping at the right spot. Now imagine that highway has **reflective lane markers, illuminated signs, glowing cat's eyes, and distance markers** — driving becomes far safer and more precise.

Runway lighting serves **exactly the same purpose** for an aircraft:

| Highway Analogy | Runway Equivalent | Pilot's Job |
|---|---|---|
| Streetlights on both sides | **Runway Edge Lights** | Keep aircraft laterally aligned on runway |
| Reflective lane markings | **Runway Centerline Lights** | Stay on the runway centerline during rollout |
| Stop line at intersection | **Runway End Lights / Threshold** | Identify start and end of usable pavement |
| Approach road lighting | **Approach Lighting System (ALS)** | Align aircraft with runway during final approach |
| Hill gradient sign | **PAPI / VASI** | Maintain correct glide path (3° descent) |
| Hazard markers near a junction | **Taxiway Edge / Centerline Lights** | Navigate taxiways safely |

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Standard Values (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> - **Standard Series Circuit Current = 6.6 Amperes** (constant current regulation)
> - **Light Spacing (Runway Edge) = 60 m** (maximum) for non-instrument runways; **30 m** for instrument runways
> - **Approach Lighting Length = 300 m (Simple)**, **450 m (Medium Intensity CAT I)**, **900 m (High Intensity CAT II/III)**
> - **Glide Path Angle for PAPI = 3°** (standard)
> - **Photometric Output (High Intensity) = up to 20,000 candelas** (white light, step 5)
> - **Runway Edge Color: White** (last 600 m or one-third of runway = **amber/yellow** warning zone)

### 1.4 Purpose of Runway Lighting

1. **Visual Acquisition:** Allow pilots to identify the runway from a distance of **at least 8 km (5 miles)** at night.
2. **Alignment:** Provide lateral, longitudinal, and vertical guidance during approach and landing.
3. **Distance Estimation:** Pilots can judge remaining runway length via light spacing and color transition.
4. **Safe Rejection:** Identify displaced thresholds, stop-end, and runway exit points.
5. **All-Weather Operations:** Enable Category I, II, and III ILS operations by providing backup visual reference when instrument guidance is augmented.
6. **Manoeuvring Guidance:** Lead aircraft off the runway via taxiway lead-off lights and stop bars.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Airport lighting is governed by **ICAO Annex 14 (Aerodromes)**, **FAA AC 150/5340 series**, and **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 14262)**. Always cite ICAO standards in theory answers.

---



<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Runway Lighting Systems

The runway lighting system is broadly classified into **five functional zones**, each with a specific purpose and lighting type:

#### A. Approach Lighting System (ALS)
- Extends from the runway threshold **outward into the approach area**.
- Provides transition from instrument flight to visual flight.
- Types:
  - **ALSF-1 / ALSF-2** (FAA, High Intensity)
  - **MALSR** (Medium Intensity Approach Lighting System with Runway Alignment Indicator Lights)
  - **CALVERT** (ICAO)
  - **Simplified Short Approach Lighting System (SSALS)**

#### B. Threshold Lighting
- Installed **at the beginning of the runway** usable for landing.
- Color: **Green** when viewed from approach direction; **Red** from opposite side.

#### C. Runway Edge Lighting
- Installed along both sides of the runway.
- Color: **White**, transitioning to **Amber/Yellow** in the last **600 m** of the runway (or one-third of runway length, whichever is less).
- Bidirectional or unidirectional depending on usage.

#### D. Runway End Lighting
- Fixed at the **far end of the runway**.
- Color: **Red** when viewed from the runway approach side.

#### E. Runway Centerline Lighting (RCLL)
- Embedded flush in the runway pavement along the centerline.
- Color: **White** from threshold to within 900 m of runway end.
- Alternating **Red/White** from 900 m to 300 m from end.
- Solid **Red** for the last 300 m.

#### F. Touchdown Zone Lighting (TDZL)
- Barrette lights (3 or 4 lights in a bar) on either side of centerline in touchdown zone (first 900 m).
- Color: **White**, unidirectional toward approach.

#### G. Stop Bars and Taxiway Lead-Off Lights
- **Stop bars:** Red, unidirectional, at runway holding positions.
- **Lead-off lights:** Alternate Green/Yellow, lead aircraft off runway to taxiway.

### 2.2 Visual Approach Slope Indicators

| System | Type | Setting Angle | Pilot Cue |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VASI** (Visual Approach Slope Indicator) | 2-bar or 3-bar | 3° (standard) | Red/White bar arrangement |
| **PAPI** (Precision Approach Path Indicator) | 4-light bar | 3° (mech) / 2.5°–3.5° | Red/White transition |
| **APAPI** (Abbreviated PAPI) | 2-light bar | Used at non-precision approach runways | Red/White transition |

**PAPI Light Configuration Interpretation:**

| Pilot Sees | Meaning | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| 4 White | Too High | Descend |
| 3 White, 1 Red | Slightly High | Slight descent |
| 2 White, 2 Red | **On Glide Path** | Maintain |
| 1 White, 3 Red | Slightly Low | Slight climb |
| 4 Red | Too Low | Climb immediately (go-around) |

### 2.3 Constant Current Series Circuit (CCSC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Concept:** Runway lighting operates on a **6.6 A series circuit** rather than parallel circuits. This is because all lights in a series loop receive the **same current**, ensuring **uniform brightness** regardless of cable length.

**Why 6.6 A and not lower/higher?**
- High current = bright incandescent light, but thick cables needed.
- Low current = thin cables, but insufficient brightness.
- **6.6 A is the international optimum** balancing cable cost and lamp performance.

**Why "Constant Current"?**
- The CCR (Constant Current Regulator) maintains **exactly 6.6 A** at the output terminals.
- It compensates for load changes, temperature variations, and lamp failures.
- Brightness is **stepped** by adjusting current to **2.8 A, 3.4 A, 4.1 A, 5.2 A, or 6.6 A** (5-step FAA; 3-step or 5-step ICAO).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of Runway Edge Lights (per side) | $N = \dfrac{L}{S} + 1$ | lights | $L$ = runway length, $S$ = spacing (m) |
| 2 | Spacing of Edge Lights (non-precision) | $S \le 60$ | m | ICAO Annex 14 |
| 3 | Spacing of Edge Lights (precision CAT I/II/III) | $S \le 30$ | m | ICAO Annex 14 |
| 4 | Centerline Light Spacing | $S_{cl} = 15$ | m | Standard for CAT II/III |
| 5 | Approach Light Spacing (barrette) | $S_{app} = 30$ | m | For 900 m ALSF |
| 6 | Series Circuit Voltage Drop | $V = I \cdot R$ | V | $I$ = 6.6 A constant |
| 7 | Total Circuit Power | $P = V_{load} \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | W | Includes CCR efficiency |
| 8 | Cable Resistance (series) | $R = \dfrac{2 \rho L_c}{A}$ | $\Omega$ | Factor 2 for go-and-return (series loop) |
| 9 | Brightness Steps (FAA 5-step) | 2.8 / 3.4 / 4.1 / 5.2 / 6.6 | A | Step 1 (low) to Step 5 (high) |
| 10 | CCR Input Power | $P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_{CCR}}$ | W | $\eta_{CCR} \approx 0.90$–0.95 |
| 11 | Number of Transformers | $N_T = N_{lights}$ | – | One isolating transformer per light |
| 12 | Isolating Transformer Ratio | Primary: 6.6 A ; Secondary: 6.6 A | – | Step-up or 1:1 |
| 13 | Approach Light Length (CAT I) | 450 | m | With barrettes |
| 14 | Approach Light Length (CAT II/III) | 900 | m | Full ALSF |
| 15 | Amber Warning Zone Length | $\min\left(600,\; \dfrac{L}{3}\right)$ | m | Last segment of runway edge |
| 16 | Touchdown Zone Length | 900 | m | From threshold |
| 17 | PAPI Glide Slope Angle | $\theta = 3^{\circ}$ | degrees | Standard |
| 18 | PAPI Installation Distance from Threshold | 300 (typical) | m | Varies by aircraft type |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** In the cable resistance formula, the **factor of 2** must be included because current flows *out* through one cable and *returns* through the other (loop). Students often forget this and lose 1 mark.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Runway lighting design is essential for:

- **Air Traffic Control (ATC):** Sequencing and runway occupancy determination.
- **Pilots:** Orientation during approach, flare, touchdown, and rollout.
- **Airport Safety:** Preventing runway incursions (stop bars, runway guard lights).
- **Capacity Enhancement:** Allowing night operations and low-visibility procedures (LVP).
- **Energy Engineering:** Designing CCRs, sizing backup generators (diesel rotary UPS), and emergency battery systems.

---



<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Runway Edge Lights

**Given:**
- Runway length $L = 3000 \; m$
- Edge light spacing $S = 60 \; m$ (non-precision runway)
- Lights installed on **both sides** of runway

**Step 1: Define spacing geometry**

The first light is installed at the threshold. Subsequent lights are placed at equal intervals of $S$ meters along the runway.

$$
\text{Distance covered by } N \text{ lights} = (N-1) \cdot S
$$

**Step 2: Equate to runway length**

$$
(N - 1) \cdot S = L
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $N$ (lights per side)**

$$
N = \frac{L}{S} + 1
$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values**

$$
N = \frac{3000}{60} + 1 = 50 + 1 = 51 \; \text{lights per side}
$$

**Step 5: Total edge lights (both sides)**

$$
N_{total} = 2N = 2 \times 51 = 102 \; \text{lights}
$$

**Step 6: Convert to KTU 14-mark style full solution**

> **Final Answer:** *The runway requires 51 lights per side and 102 lights in total for edge lighting.*

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cable Resistance & Voltage Drop in Series Circuit

**Given:**
- Runway length $L = 2500 \; m$ (one-way)
- Loop length $L_c = 2L + 50 \; m$ (additional for homerun to vault)
- Cable material: Copper, resistivity $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \; \Omega \cdot m$
- Cable cross-section $A = 6 \; mm^2 = 6 \times 10^{-6} \; m^2$
- Circuit current $I = 6.6 \; A$

**Step 1: Compute total loop length**

$$
L_c = 2 \times 2500 + 50 = 5050 \; m
$$

**Step 2: Apply cable resistance formula**

$$
R = \frac{2 \rho L_c}{A}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$
R = \frac{2 \times (1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 5050}{6 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{2 \times 1.724 \times 5050 \times 10^{-8}}{6 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{17412.4 \times 10^{-8}}{6 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1.74124 \times 10^{-4}}{6 \times 10^{-6}} = 29.02 \; \Omega
$$

**Step 4: Compute voltage drop (Ohm's Law)**

$$
V = I \cdot R = 6.6 \times 29.02 = 191.53 \; V
$$

**Step 5: Compute total power dissipated in cable**

$$
P_{cable} = I^2 \cdot R = (6.6)^2 \times 29.02 = 43.56 \times 29.02 = 1263.9 \; W
$$

**Step 6: Compute CCR output voltage required**

$$
V_{CCR} = V_{load} + I \cdot R_{cable} = V_{load} + 191.53 \; V
$$

where $V_{load}$ is the sum of voltage drops across all isolating transformers and lamps in the series loop.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Amber Warning Zone Boundary

**Given:**
- Runway length $L = 2700 \; m$

**Step 1: Compute one-third of runway length**

$$
L/3 = 2700/3 = 900 \; m
$$

**Step 2: Compare with fixed 600 m**

$$
\min(600, \; L/3) = \min(600, \; 900) = 600 \; m
$$

**Step 3: Conclude warning zone length**

$$
L_{amber} = 600 \; m
$$

**Step 4: Determine starting position of amber lights from threshold**

$$
\text{Start of amber} = L - 600 = 2700 - 600 = 2100 \; m \; \text{from threshold}
$$

> **KTU Conclusion:** *The amber warning zone starts 2100 m from the threshold and continues to the runway end (a length of 600 m).*

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Brightness Step Selection

**Given:** Pilot reports "barely visible" runway edge lights in light fog at 5 km.

**Step 1: Identify need**

Step 1 (2.8 A) is too dim; Step 3 (4.1 A) is moderate; Step 5 (6.6 A) is full brightness.

**Step 2: Recommended step**

For reported reduced visibility, **Step 3 (4.1 A)** is typically engaged. ATC escalates to Step 5 only when RVR < 800 m.

**Step 3: Brightness ratio between Step 5 and Step 1**

$$
\frac{I_5}{I_1} = \frac{6.6}{2.8} = 2.357
$$

**Step 4: Luminous intensity ratio (approximately $I^2$ in filament regime)**

$$
\frac{L_5}{L_1} \approx (2.357)^2 = 5.55
$$

> **Final Conclusion:** *Step 5 produces roughly 5.5× the luminous intensity of Step 1.*

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of PAPI vs VASI

| Parameter | VASI | PAPI |
|---|---|---|
| Number of light units | 2 or 3 bars (each with 2/4 lights) | Single bar of 4 lights |
| Light source | Incandescent / LED | LED (modern) |
| Glide path angle | 2.5° – 3.5° (adjustable) | 2.5° – 3.5° (mech. setting) |
| Transition accuracy | Coarse (~0.5° band) | Fine (~0.25° band) |
| Pilot indication | 2-light or 3-light pattern | 4-light pattern |
| Location | Beside runway (left side) | Beside runway (left side, near threshold) |
| Power consumption | Higher | Lower (LED) |
| ICAO preferred | Yes (legacy) | **Yes (modern standard)** |
| Suitable for CAT I? | Limited | Yes |

---



<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
