The building is at horizontal distance 2000 m, but the question is its **MSL elevation**, not its height above local ground. The standard approach is to compare the **glide slope elevation** with the **building elevation above MSL**:
$$
z_{glide} - z_{bldg} = 109.816 - 15 = 94.816 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 5: Conclusion
The building is **94.8 m below the glide slope** at the 2000 m mark. It does **not** penetrate the protected approach surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ICAO Annex 14 Approach Surface Trapezoid**
> The protected approach surface is a trapezoid rising at **2.00% (1:50)** for non-precision runways and **2.857% (1:35)** for precision runways. Anything above this surface in the approach area is an obstacle requiring displacement of the threshold or reduction in declared distances.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Control Network Hierarchy and Tolerances

| Network Level | Monument Type | Typical Spacing | Horizontal Accuracy | Vertical Accuracy | Survey Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Control (PCP) | Concrete pillar, 1.5 m deep | 4–6 points per airport | **±2 mm + 2 ppm** | **±3 mm/km** | GNSS static, 24-hour session |
| Secondary Control (SCP) | Concrete post or rebar in pavement | 100–250 m | **±5 mm + 5 ppm** | **±5 mm/km** | Total station traverse tied to PCP |
| Construction Set-out | Wooden stake / paint mark | 5–10 m (working) | **±10 mm** | **±5 mm** | Real-Time Kinematic GNSS (RTK) |
| Pavement Monitoring | Settlement pin in concrete | 50 m grid | **±1 mm** (relative) | **±2 mm** | Precise digital leveling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 2 ppm?**
> "ppm" stands for "parts per million" of the baseline length. For a **1 km baseline**, 2 ppm = 2 mm additional error. For a **10 km baseline**, 2 ppm = 20 mm additional error. This is critical for Cat III ILS, where the localizer course alignment must be referenced to within **±1 m at threshold**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Number of Edge Lights for a Runway

> **Problem Statement (Model):** Compute the number of runway edge lights required for a **3,500 m long** runway, intended for **Cat I precision approach**.

### Step 1: Recall Standard Spacing
Per ICAO Annex 14, Table 5.3:
- For **non-precision / visual runways**: edge light spacing = **60 m** (max).
- For **precision approach runways (Cat I/II/III)**: edge light spacing = **30 m** (typically), or **60 m** for Cat I with good visual cues.

### Step 2: Apply Formula
$$
N_{edge} = \dfrac{L_{R}}{S_{edge}} + 1
$$

For Cat I precision, use $S_{edge} = 30 \text{ m}$:
$$
N_{edge} = \dfrac{3500}{30} + 1 = 116.67 + 1 = 117.67 \approx 118 \text{ lights}
$$

For non-precision (conservative), $S_{edge} = 60 \text{ m}$:
$$
N_{edge} = \dfrac{3500}{60} + 1 = 58.33 + 1 = 59.33 \approx 60 \text{ lights}
$$

> **Always round UP to the next even number for symmetrical installation** across both sides:
> - 60 m spacing → 60 lights (30 per side) on each edge.
> - 30 m spacing → 118 lights (59 per side).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Airfield Control Network — Hierarchical Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[National Geodetic Network WGS-84 Datum] --> B[Primary Control Points 4 to 6 Pillars Baseline 1 in 1000000]
    B --> C1[Secondary Control Points 100 to 250 m Grid Horizontal Accuracy 5 mm]
    B --> C2[Pavement Reference Points Settlement Monitoring]
    C1 --> D1[Construction Set Out Stakes and Marks]
    C1 --> D2[Visual Aid Locations Signs Lights Markings]
    C1 --> D3[Navigation Aid Locations ILS VOR NDB]
    C2 --> E[Annual Precision Leveling Plus Minus 2 mm per km]
    D1 --> F[As Built Survey]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    F --> G[Airport Operating Surface Master Plan]
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style C2 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style D1 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style D2 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style D3 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style E fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram traces the **information flow** from the global datum (WGS-84) down to operating surface elements. Every visual aid, runway, or sign ultimately inherits its coordinates from the **Primary Control Points**, with a documented accuracy chain. This is the "spinal cord" referenced in Section 1.

---

## 4.2 Visual Aids Layout — Runway and Approach Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph APPROACH[Approach Area 420 m to 900 m]
    AL1[Approach Light Bar 1]
    AL2[Approach Light Bar 2]
    ALSQ[Sequenced Flashing Lights]
    AL3[Crossbar at 300 m from threshold]
    end
    subgraph THRESHOLD[Threshold Zone]
    RTIL[RTIL Flashing Strobe]
    THR[Threshold Lights Green]
    PAPI[PAPI 4 Unit Bar 300 m from threshold]
    end
    subgraph TOUCHDOWN[Touchdown Zone 0 to 900 m]
    TDZ1[Touchdown Zone Markings White Pairs]
    TDZ2[Touchdown Zone Lights if Cat II or III]
    AIM[Aiming Point 300 m from threshold]
    end
    subgraph RUNWAY[Runway Body]
    EDGE[Edge Lights White 60 m or 30 m spacing]
    CL[Centerline Lights White to Red]
    end
    subgraph END[Runway End]
    ENDLIGHT[End Lights Red]
    REMSIGN[Runway Distance Remaining Signs Black Background Yellow Numerals]
    end
    AL1 --> AL2 --> AL3 --> THR --> TDZ1 --> TDZ2 --> EDGE --> CL --> ENDLIGHT
    PAPI -.-> THR
    ALSQ -.-> THR
    RTIL -.-> THR
    REMSIGN -.-> ENDLIGHT
    style AL1 fill:#fff2cc
    style AL2 fill:#fff2cc
    style AL3 fill:#fff2cc
    style ALSQ fill:#fff2cc
    style THR fill:#c5e0b4
    style RTIL fill:#c5e0b4
    style PAPI fill:#c5e0b4
    style TDZ1 fill:#bdd7ee
    style TDZ2 fill:#bdd7ee
    style AIM fill:#bdd7ee
    style EDGE fill:#f8cbad
    style CL fill:#f8cbad
    style ENDLIGHT fill:#f4b183
    style REMSIGN fill:#f4b183
```

**Interpretation:** This is a left-to-right "timeline" of the pilot's final approach. The pilot first sees the **Approach Lighting System**, then crosses the **Threshold (Green lights + PAPI guidance)**, then **Touches down in the Touchdown Zone**, rolls along the **Runway Body (centerline and edge lights)**, and finally sees the **Red End Lights** as the runway terminates.

---

## 4.3 PAPI Light Frustum Geometry — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aircraft on Glide Path 3 degrees] -->|Line of Sight| B[PAPI Bar at 300 m from threshold]
    B --> B1[Bar 1 Closest to Runway Frustum 2 deg 30 min]
    B --> B2[Bar 2 Frustum 2 deg 50 min]
    B --> B3[Bar 3 Frustum 3 deg 10 min]
    B --> B4[Bar 4 Furthest Frustum 3 deg 30 min]
    B1 -->|White to Red| R1[On Glide Path All White or 2 White 2 Red]
    B4 -->|Red to White| R2[Slightly Low 1 White 3 Red]
    A -->|High| R3[4 White Too High]
    A -->|Low| R4[4 Red Dangerously Low]
    style A fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#c5e0b4
    style B2 fill:#c5e0b4
    style B3 fill:#c5e0b4
    style B4 fill:#c5e0b4
    style R1 fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style R2 fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style R3 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style R4 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:** The 4 PAPI units are **horizontally separated** but each projects a beam at a different vertical angle (the *frustum angles*). The pilot sees a *combination* of white (when the aircraft is above the beam) and red (when below). Only on the exact glide path do they see **2 white + 2 red**.

---

## 4.4 Landing Information System — Integrated Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PILOT[Aircraft Pilot] -->|Visual Acquisition| ALS[Approach Lighting System 720 m or 900 m]
    PILOT -->|Glide Slope Reference| PAPI[PAPI at 3 deg 00 min]
    PILOT -->|Threshold Identification| RTIL[RTIL REIL Strobe Lights]
    PILOT -->|Windsock| WND[Wind Direction Indicator]
    PILOT -->|Touchdown Reference| TDZ[TDZ Markings and Lights]
    ALS -->|Lead in| THR[Runway Threshold]
    PAPI -->|Slope| THR
    RTIL -->|Identify| THR
    TDZ -->|Land Here| THR
    THR -->|Roll Out| RWY[Runway Edge and Centerline Lights]
    RWY -->|Decelerate| EXIT[Taxiway Centerline Lights Green]
    EXIT -->|Hold| STOP[Stop Bar Lights Red 3 m spacing]
    style PILOT fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style ALS fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style PAPI fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style RTIL fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style WND fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style TDZ fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style THR fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style RWY fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style EXIT fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style STOP fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **complete landing information chain**. The pilot simultaneously receives:
- **Directional guidance** (ALS, RTIL)
- **Slope guidance** (PAPI)
- **Environmental input** (Windsock)
- **Touchdown reference** (TDZ markings/lights)
- **Ground movement guidance** (RWY lights, Taxiway lights, Stop bars)

This integrated information flow is what makes **Cat II/III low-visibility landings** possible — without it, the pilot would have no synthetic visual reference.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
> **Define "Control Network" in the context of an airport. List the different types of control points and their typical accuracy.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)
A **Control Network** is the geodetic framework of monumented reference points that establishes the master horizontal and vertical (WGS-84 / MSL) coordinates for all airport infrastructure. **(1 mark)**

**Types of control points:**
1. **Primary Control Points (PCP):** 4–6 deeply monumented concrete pillars, accuracy **±2 mm + 2 ppm** horizontally, tied to the national geodetic network. **(1 mark)**
2. **Secondary Control Points (SCP):** Densified network at 100–250 m spacing, tied to PCPs, accuracy **±5 mm + 5 ppm** horizontally, used for construction set-out. **(0.5 marks)**
3. **Pavement Reference Points (PRP):** Settlement monitoring points in rigid pavement, vertical accuracy **±2 mm/km** via precise leveling. **(0.5 marks)**

The control network ensures the entire airfield (runways, taxiways, visual aids, navigation aids) is built to a single, consistent, and verifiable coordinate reference.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
> **What is PAPI? Explain how it provides glide path information to the pilot.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)
**PAPI (Precision Approach Path Indicator)** is a visual landing information system consisting of **four light units** arranged in a bar perpendicular to the runway centerline, located **300 m (±10 m)** from the threshold on the **left side** of the runway (for code 3/4 runways). **(1 mark)**

Each light unit projects a beam at a slightly different vertical angle (**2°30', 2°50', 3°10', 3°30'**), creating a "frustum" of guidance. **(1 mark)**

**Pilot interpretation:**
- **4 white:** Too high
- **3 white, 1 red:** Slightly high
- **2 white, 2 red:** **ON GLIDE PATH (3.00°)**
- **1 white, 3 red:** Slightly low
- **4 red:** Dangerously low

The pilot continuously adjusts pitch to maintain the **2 white, 2 red** combination, ensuring a stable **3.00° approach slope**. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
> **(a)** Describe the **Approach Lighting System (ALS)**. Explain the different categories of ALS and their geometrical layout. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** An aircraft is on a **3.00° glide slope** with pilot's eye height of **5 m** above the runway. The PAPI unit is at **0.85 m** above runway surface. Calculate the **horizontal distance** from threshold at which the PAPI bar should be located. Use ICAO frustum transition angle of **2°30'** between the on-glide path and the lower beam. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Approach Lighting System (7 marks)

The **Approach Lighting System (ALS)** is a key component of the Landing Information System, providing a visual lead-in corridor from the runway threshold into the approach area. It allows the pilot to transition from instrument to visual reference during the final stages of approach. **(1 mark)**

**ICAO Categories of ALS (Table from Annex 14, Chapter 5):**

| Category | Length | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple ALS** | 420 m | Non-precision approach runways |
| **CAT I ALS** | 720 m | Precision approach, Cat I (DH ≥ 60 m) |
| **CAT II/III ALS** | 900 m | Precision approach, Cat II/III (DH < 60 m) |

**(2 marks for categories)**

**Geometrical Layout:**
1. **Longitudinal axis** along the runway centerline extended.
2. **Crossbars** at **300 m from threshold** (5 light units), and at **150 m and 300 m to threshold** for Cat II/III. **(1 mark)**
3. **Barrette (side-row) lights** at 30 m spacing along the entire length, forming a visual "channel" of width 60–80 m.
4. **Sequenced Flashing Lights (SFL)** in the outer 600 m of the ALS, strobing toward the threshold, providing "rolling ball" lead-in effect. **(1 mark)**
5. **Approach light plane:** 1:50 (2%) slope for non-precision, 1:35 (2.857%) for precision, and 1:40 (2.5%) for inner 300 m. **(1 mark)**
6. **Frangible fittings:** All lights mounted on frangible supports so that an aircraft impact causes the light to break away without damage. **(1 mark)**

### Part (b) — PAPI Bar Distance Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Glide slope angle: $\theta = 3.00^\circ$
- Pilot eye height: $h_p = 5 \text{ m}$
- PAPI unit height: $h_o = 0.85 \text{ m}$
- PAPI lower frustum angle: $\alpha = 2°30' = 2.50^\circ$

**Step 1: Vertical Separation (1 mark)**
$$
\Delta h = h_p - h_o = 5 - 0.85 = 4.15 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Effective Glide Angle Below PAPI Beam (1 mark)**
The angular separation between the glide path and the lower PAPI frustum beam:
$$
\Delta \theta = \theta - \alpha = 3.00^\circ - 2.50^\circ = 0.50^\circ
$$

**Step 3: Trigonometric Setup (1 mark)**
For the PAPI unit's lower beam to be tangent to the glide slope at the PAPI bar location:
$$
\tan(\Delta \theta) = \dfrac{\Delta h}{D_{PAPI}}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for Distance (2 marks)**
$$
D_{PAPI} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{\tan 0.50^\circ} = \dfrac{4.15}{0.008727}
$$
$$
\boxed{D_{PAPI} = 475.6 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 5: ICAO Compliance Check (2 marks)**
Per **ICAO Annex 14, Table 5.3** (Code 3/4 runways), the PAPI bar is set at **300 m ± 10 m from threshold**. The calculated distance of 475.6 m is for the **on-glide-path line of sight** to the lower frustum.

**Final adopted value (per ICAO Annex 14):**
$$
\boxed{D_{PAPI} = 300 \text{ m from threshold, on the LEFT side of the runway}}
$$

The 300 m standard accounts for the **combined effect of all 4 PAPI units** and the typical pilot eye heights of 3–8 m across aircraft types.

---

### Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Landing Information System (LIS)** and its components. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A runway is **3,500 m long**, designated for **Cat I precision approach**. Calculate: **(i)** the number of **runway edge lights** required, **(ii)** the **standard spacing** between consecutive lights, and **(iii)** the **total length of the ALS** for this runway. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Landing Information System (LIS) (7 marks)

The **Landing Information System (LIS)** is the integrated set of visual and electronic aids that delivers real-time information to the pilot during the final approach and landing. **(1 mark)**

**Block Diagram:**
```
[Aircraft Pilot] → Approach Lighting System (720 m) → Threshold Identification (RTIL)
       ↓
   PAPI (3.00° slope) → Runway Threshold (Green Lights) → Touchdown Zone Markings
       ↓
   Edge & Centerline Lights → End Lights (Red) → Taxiway Centerline (Green)
       ↓
   Stop Bars (Red) → Holding Position
```

**Major Components of LIS:** **(6 marks)**

1. **Approach Lighting System (ALS):** 720 m for Cat I, providing visual lead-in. **(1 mark)**
2. **PAPI/VASI:** 4-unit bar at 300 m from threshold, providing continuous glide path guidance at 3.00°. **(1 mark)**
3. **RTIL (Runway Threshold Identification Lights):** Flashing strobes identifying the threshold. **(1 mark)**
4. **Windsock / Landing Direction Indicator:** Wind direction and active runway. **(1 mark)**
5. **Touchdown Zone Markings and Lights:** Define the safe landing footprint. **(1 mark)**
6. **Runway and Taxiway Lighting:** Edge lights (white), centerline lights (white→red), threshold (green), end (red), taxiway centerline (green), stop bars (red). **(1 mark)**

### Part (b) — Numerical on Edge Lights and ALS (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Runway length: $L_R = 3,500 \text{ m}$
- Runway category: **Cat I precision approach**

**Step 1: Edge Light Spacing (1 mark)**
Per ICAO Annex 14, Table 5.3:
- Non-precision runways: **60 m** spacing
- Precision runways (Cat I, II, III): **30 m or 60 m** spacing (per operational requirement)

For Cat I precision, adopt **30 m** for full precision guidance.

**Step 2: Number of Edge Lights (2 marks)**
$$
N_{edge} = \dfrac{L_R}{S_{edge}} + 1 = \dfrac{3500}{30} + 1 = 116.67 + 1 = 117.67
$$

Round up to ensure coverage:
$$
\boxed{N_{edge} = 118 \text{ lights per side, total 236 lights}}
$$

If spacing is 60 m:
$$
N_{edge} = \dfrac{3500}{60} + 1 = 58.33 + 1 = 59.33 \approx 60 \text{ lights per side, total 120 lights}
$$

**Step 3: Total Length of ALS (2 marks)**
Per ICAO Annex 14, Section 5.3.4:
- For **Cat I precision approach**: ALS length = **720 m**
- The ALS extends 720 m outward from the threshold into the approach area.

**Step 4: ALS Barrette Configuration (1 mark)**
- Number of crossbars = **5** (at 0, 150, 300, 450, 600 m)
- Barrette lights at **30 m** spacing along length.

**Step 5: Verification (1 mark)**
- ALS length 720 m is consistent with the **2.857% (1:35) approach surface slope** for Cat I precision runways.
- PAPI bar at 300 m from threshold is **within the ALS length**, ensuring visual continuity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing PAPI and VASI specifications:** PAPI uses **4 units at 300 m**, VASI uses **2/3/12 units** with different frustum angles. Mixing them is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Wrong frustum angle in PAPI derivation:** Many students use **3°** for the lower beam, but the correct value is **2°30' (transition angle)**. This leads to a 100%+ error in $D_{PAPI}$.
> 3. **Forgetting "per side":** Runway edge lights are installed on **both sides**; the total number is $2 \times N_{side}$. Many students forget this and lose a mark.
> 4. **Cat classification confusion:** Cat I ALS = 720 m, **NOT** 900 m. Cat II/III ALS = 900 m. Wrong category → wrong length.
> 5. **Control network accuracy confusion:** PCPs use **±2 mm + 2 ppm**, not just "mm-level" without specifying ppm. ICAO graders expect the units.
> 6. **PAPI placement side:** PAPI bar is on the **LEFT side** for code 3/4 runways, but on the **RIGHT side** for code 1/2 runways. Wrong side = 0.5 mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Control Network
- **Three-tier hierarchy:** National Geodetic Network → Primary Control Points (PCP) → Secondary Control Points (SCP) → Construction Set-out.
- **Datum:** WGS-84 horizontal, MSL vertical.
- **Accuracy:** PCPs at **±2 mm + 2 ppm**; SCPs at **±5 mm + 5 ppm**.
- **Purpose:** Single source of truth for all airport coordinates.

### Visual Aids
- **Three categories:** Markings (paint), Lights (electrical), Signs (illuminated panels).
- **Color coding is universal:** White = runway, Yellow = taxiway, Red = end/stop, Green = threshold/taxiway centerline, Black-Yellow = distance remaining.
- **ICAO Annex 14 Chapter 5** is the primary reference.

### Landing Information System
- **Components:** ALS + PAPI + RTIL + Windsock + TDZ markings + Runway/Taxiway lights.
- **PAPI key data:** 4 units, 300 m from threshold, **3.00° glide slope**, transition angle **2°30'**.
- **ALS length:** Simple = 420 m, Cat I = 720 m, Cat II/III = 900 m.

### Numerical Cheat Points
- **PAPI distance formula:** $D = (h_p - h_o) / \tan(\theta - \alpha)$.
- **Edge lights:** $N = (L_R / S) + 1$ per side.
- **Glide slope elevation:** $z(x) = z_{eye} + x \cdot \tan 3°$.
- **ICAO Approach Trapezoid:** 1:50 (non-precision) and 1:35 (precision) slopes.

### Key Standards to Memorize
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Pilot eye height (small AC) | **3 m** |
| Pilot eye height (large AC) | **6–8 m** |
| PAPI glide slope | **3.00°** |
| PAPI bar distance | **300 m ± 10 m** |
| PAPI lower frustum | **2°30'** |
| ALS Cat I length | **720 m** |
| ALS Cat II/III length | **900 m** |
| Edge light spacing (precision) | **30 m** |
| Edge light spacing (non-precision) | **60 m** |
| Inset light max flush height | **12 mm** |
| Runway length unit | **m (SI)** |
| Geodetic datum | **WGS-84** |
| Vertical datum | **MSL** |

### Quick-Reference Diagram Identifiers
- **PAPI bar:** Located **300 m** from threshold, **left side** for code 3/4.
- **Aiming point markings:** **Two thick white bars** at **300 m** from threshold (for code 3/4).
- **Touchdown zone markings:** Multiple **paired white bars** from **150 m to 900 m** from threshold.
- **Threshold lights:** **Green**, symmetrical across threshold, **2 m** spacing.
- **End lights:** **Red**, across the physical end of runway.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** Always cite **ICAO Annex 14** references, use **standard SI units (m, m², s)**, and remember that PAPI is a **visual** aid (operates day/night, but degrades in low visibility), while **ILS is a precision electronic aid** that operates in near-zero visibility. The two are complementary, not interchangeable.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Travel Demand Forecast: Macro and Micro Analysis (Intro Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Travel Demand Forecast: Macro and Micro Analysis (Intro Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Air Travel Demand Forecasting** is the systematic, quantitative process of estimating the future volume of air passengers, aircraft movements, and cargo that will utilize an airport or an air transport system over a defined planning horizon (typically 5, 10, 15, or 20 years). It serves as the foundational input for sizing every component of airport infrastructure — runways, taxiways, aprons, terminal buildings, and landside access systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Demand forecasting is the *first step* in the airport master planning cycle. Without a credible forecast, no downstream design capacity can be justified to regulators (DGCA, AAI) or funding agencies.

### 1.2 Macro vs. Micro Analysis — The Two Lenses

| Aspect | Macro Analysis | Micro Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Scale** | National / Regional / State level | Single airport / Airline / Route level |
| **Question Answered** | *"How big is the total aviation market?"* | *"How many passengers will choose THIS airport?"* |
| **Primary Drivers** | GDP, population, disposable income, trade, tourism | Fares, frequency, catchment geography, competition |
| **Typical Use** | Long-term strategic planning, national aviation policy | Master planning of an individual airport |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Restaurant" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you want to open a restaurant in a new city.
> - **Macro Analysis** = Studying whether the *entire city* is growing, average income rising, food-culture trends, tourism inflow. (Is the *pie* getting bigger?)
> - **Micro Analysis** = Studying your *specific locality* — nearby offices, competing restaurants, your menu pricing, parking availability, and what fraction of the neighborhood will choose you. (How big is *my slice* of the pie?)
>
> An airport planner needs **both** lenses. A booming economy (macro) doesn't help if the airport's catchment has poor road access (micro) — passengers will simply drive to a competing airport 80 km away.

### 1.4 Why Both Levels Are Mandatory in KTU Practice

- **Macro alone** gives an inflated estimate (e.g., "Kerala will need 50 million air passengers by 2040") but does not say *which* airport captures them.
- **Micro alone** misses the structural growth opportunity (e.g., a small airport today may sit in a high-growth macro corridor).
- The **correct workflow** is: Macro → estimate total regional demand → Micro → distribute that demand among competing airports → Final forecast for the subject airport.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical / Standard Metric:** Air travel demand is universally expressed in **Annual Passenger Throughput (APT)** measured in *millions of passengers per annum (MPPA)* and **Aircraft Movements** measured as *ATM/year*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Stage Logic of Demand Forecasting

1. **Define the System Boundary** — Identify the airport, its catchment area, and the time horizon (e.g., Calicut International Airport, 50 km catchment radius, horizon 2045).
2. **Collect Historical Data** — Minimum 10 years of past passenger, ATM, GDP, and tourism statistics.
3. **Select Forecasting Method** — Trend extrapolation, econometric (regression), market-share, or stated-preference survey.
4. **Validate and Reconcile** — Cross-check macro and micro outputs; the final forecast must satisfy both top-down and bottom-up logic.

### 2.2 Macro Analysis — Operational Logic

- **Driver Identification:** The planner identifies socio-economic independent variables that historically correlate strongly with air travel demand. Standard drivers used by ICAO, AAI, and IATA are: **GDP per capita, Population, Tourism arrivals, Trade volume, Disposable income, Fuel price.**
- **Model Specification:** A multiple linear regression of the form:
 $$\text{Passengers} = f(\text{GDP}, \text{Population}, \text{Tourism}, \ldots)$$
- **Scenario Projection:** Future values of the drivers (from government 5-year plans, World Bank projections) are plugged in to generate future passenger numbers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Macro forecasts are used by **policy makers and regulators** to decide if a *new greenfield airport* is warranted, or to plan multi-airport systems for a metropolitan region.

### 2.3 Micro Analysis — Operational Logic

- **Catchment Definition:** Using an **isochrone** (drive-time contour) of 60–120 minutes, or a **gravity model** that weights distance against population.
- **Market Share Model:** The airport's forecast = Total regional demand × (airport's competitive share).
- **Service Quality Variables:** Frequency, fare, connectivity (number of direct destinations), and ground-access time are introduced to refine the share.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Principle:** For existing operational airports, **micro analysis dominates** because the catchment, airline network, and competitive dynamics are well-defined. For proposed new airports, **macro analysis dominates** because there is no historical share to extrapolate.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Name | Formula | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_t$ | Forecast passengers at year $t$ | $P_t = P_0 (1 + g)^t$ | passengers | Exponential (compound) growth |
| $g$ | Annual growth rate | $g = \left( \dfrac{P_t}{P_0} \right)^{1/t} - 1$ | dimensionless | Historical CAGR back-calculation |
| $E_d$ | Income elasticity of demand | $E_d = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta \text{GDP}}$ | dimensionless | Macro passenger model |
| $E_p$ | Price elasticity of demand | $E_p = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta \text{Fare}}$ | dimensionless | Micro fare-response model |
| $S_i$ | Market share of airport $i$ | $S_i = \dfrac{P_i}{\sum_j P_j}$ | ratio (0 to 1) | Micro distribution of macro demand |
| $T_{iso}$ | Isochrone travel time | $T_{iso} = \dfrac{d}{v_{avg}}$ | minutes | Catchment boundary |
| $G$ | Geometric mean growth | $G = \sqrt[n]{P_n / P_0} - 1$ | dimensionless | Smoothing volatile year-on-year data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Sign Convention:** Elasticity values for air travel are typically **positive** for income ($E_d \approx 1.2$ to $1.8$ globally) and **negative** for price ($E_p \approx -0.8$ to $-1.5$).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Runway Orientation & Length:** Micro forecast of busiest-hour movements drives runway geometry.
- **Terminal Sizing:** Hourly peak passenger forecast (typically $P_{peak\;hour} = P_{annual} \times 0.0003$ to $0.0005$) drives terminal area.
- **Economic Justification:** Macro forecasts underpin the **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)** that justifies airport expansion loans from World Bank, ADB, or PFC India.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) Back-Calculation

**Problem Logic:** Given a historical passenger count at year 0 ($P_0$) and year $t$ ($P_t$), derive the equivalent constant annual growth rate $g$.

**Step 1 —** Write the compound growth equation linking $P_0$ to $P_t$.

$$
P_t = P_0 (1 + g)^t
$$

**Step 2 —** Divide both sides by $P_0$ to isolate the growth term.

$$
\frac{P_t}{P_0} = (1 + g)^t
$$

**Step 3 —** Take the $t$-th root on both sides to remove the exponent.

$$
\left(\frac{P_t}{P_0}\right)^{1/t} = 1 + g
$$

**Step 4 —** Subtract 1 from both sides to obtain the growth rate.

$$
g = \left(\frac{P_t}{P_0}\right)^{1/t} - 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If an airport grew from 2.0 MPPA in 2015 to 3.5 MPPA in 2025 (t = 10 years), then $g = (3.5/2.0)^{0.1} - 1 = 1.0576 - 1 = 5.76\%$ per annum. This is the *historical* rate. The forecast assumes a similar (or scenario-adjusted) rate continues.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Linear vs. Exponential Forecast Comparison

Given the same historical data, a planner must choose between two simple models.

**Model A — Linear (additive) growth:**

$$
P_t = P_0 + (a \cdot t)
$$

where $a$ = average passengers added per year.

**Model B — Exponential (multiplicative) growth:**

$$
P_t = P_0 \cdot (1 + g)^t
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Linear Model | Exponential Model |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical form | Arithmetic progression | Geometric progression |
| Suitability | Mature/saturated markets | Emerging/growing markets |
| Behaviour at large $t$ | Predicts slowing growth | Predicts accelerating growth |
| KTU preference for India | Limited use | **Preferred** (aviation still in growth phase) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** India is in a *high-growth* aviation phase, so KTU answers should default to **exponential** or **regression-based** models, not linear. Linear extrapolation is a common mistake that loses marks.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Market-Share Allocation (Micro from Macro)

**Step 1 —** Macro model estimates total regional passengers in 2040: $P_{region}^{2040} = 25 \text{ MPPA}$.

**Step 2 —** Identify the three competing airports in the region (e.g., A, B, C) and their current shares.

**Step 3 —** Compute each airport's historical market share.

$$
S_i = \frac{P_i^{\text{current}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j^{\text{current}}}
$$

**Step 4 —** Multiply the macro total by the share to obtain the micro forecast.

$$
P_i^{2040} = S_i \times P_{region}^{2040}
$$

**Numerical Demonstration:**

Suppose current regional throughput = 10 MPPA split as: A = 4.5, B = 3.0, C = 2.5 MPPA. Then $S_A = 0.45$, $S_B = 0.30$, $S_C = 0.25$.

Micro forecast for 2040:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A^{2040} &= 0.45 \times 25 = 11.25 \text{ MPPA} \\
P_B^{2040} &= 0.30 \times 25 = 7.50 \text{ MPPA} \\
P_C^{2040} &= 0.25 \times 25 = 6.25 \text{ MPPA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Check:** The sum $11.25 + 7.50 + 6.25 = 25$ MPPA, which matches the macro total. This *reconciliation* is the most important quality check in KTU-level forecasting.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Macro–Micro Forecasting Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Define Planning Horizon and Study Area] --> B0[Stage 1: Collect Historical Data]
    B0 --> C0[Passenger Traffic 10 yrs]
    B0 --> D0[GDP and Population Data]
    B0 --> E0[Tourism and Trade Stats]
    B0 --> F0[Airline Route Network Data]

    C0 --> G0[Stage 2 Macro Analysis]
    D0 --> G0
    E0 --> G0
    G0 --> H0[Regression Model of Regional Demand]
    H0 --> I0[Regional Passenger Forecast MPPA]

    F0 --> J0[Stage 3 Micro Analysis]
    I0 --> J0
    J0 --> K0[Define Catchment Isochrone 60 to 120 min]
    K0 --> L0[Compute Market Share of Subject Airport]
    L0 --> M0[Apply Share to Regional Forecast]
    M0 --> N0[Subject Airport Forecast MPPA]

    N0 --> O0[Stage 4 Validation and Reconciliation]
    H0 --> O0
    O0 --> P0{Top Down and Bottom Up Match?}
    P0 -->|Yes| Q0[Final Approved Forecast]
    P0 -->|No| R0[Iterate Assumptions and Re Run]

    style A0 fill:#e1f5ff
    style G0 fill:#fff4e1
    style J0 fill:#ffe1f5
    style O0 fill:#e1ffe1
    style Q0 fill:#d0d0d0
```

### 4.2 Driver Categorization Map (Macro vs. Micro)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAC[MACRO DRIVERS]
        M1[GDP per Capita]
        M2[Population Growth]
        M3[Tourism Arrivals]
        M4[Trade and FDI]
        M5[Fuel Price Index]
    end

    subgraph MIC[MICRO DRIVERS]
        N1[Air Fare Levels]
        N2[Flight Frequency]
        N3[Direct Connectivity]
        N4[Ground Access Time]
        N5[Competing Airport Proximity]
    end

    MAC --> OUT[Final Airport Demand Forecast]
    MIC --> OUT

    style MAC fill:#fff4cc
    style MIC fill:#cce5ff
    style OUT fill:#ccffcc
```

### 4.3 Reading the Diagrams

- The first diagram shows the **sequential workflow** from data collection → macro regression → micro catchment allocation → reconciliation. The green reconciliation node is the KTU-critical checkpoint.
- The second diagram emphasizes that **macro and micro drivers are parallel inputs**, not sequential — both feed the final demand number.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between macro-level and micro-level analysis in air travel demand forecasting.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Macro-level analysis operates at the **national or regional scale** and uses broad socio-economic indicators (GDP, population, tourism) to estimate the *total* aviation market size. Micro-level analysis operates at the **airport or route level**, focusing on the specific catchment area, airline service quality, fare, and competition to determine what *share* of the regional demand the subject airport captures. In practice, macro analysis is a **top-down** approach, while micro analysis is a **bottom-up** approach, and both are reconciled to produce the final forecast. **(3 marks — 1 mark for each distinguishing feature: scale, drivers, output type.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any four socio-economic variables used as independent variables in macro-level air travel demand models.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four standard socio-economic independent variables used in macro air travel demand models are: **(1) GDP per capita**, **(2) Population of the region**, **(3) Tourist arrivals**, and **(4) Trade volume / Foreign Direct Investment**. Some models also include disposable income and aviation fuel price as secondary variables. **(3 marks — 0.75 mark per variable, full credit for any four with one-line relevance.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for air travel demand forecasting at an existing operational airport. **(7 marks)**
**Q.A. (b)** Discuss the macro-level approach with a suitable regression model structure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The step-by-step procedure for forecasting demand at an existing airport is:

1. **Define the Study Area and Horizon** (1 mark) — Identify the airport, define the catchment (typically 60–120 minute drive-time isochrone), and fix the planning horizon (commonly 20 years, segmented into 5-year phases).

2. **Historical Data Collection** (1 mark) — Gather at least 10 years of data on annual passengers, aircraft movements, GDP, population, tourism, and airline routes for both the subject airport and competing airports.

3. **Macro Analysis** (1 mark) — Build a regression model correlating regional passenger traffic with socio-economic drivers to estimate the total regional demand.

4. **Micro Analysis** (1.5 marks) — Compute the airport's market share within the region using historical share trends, service-quality adjustments, and stated-preference surveys.

5. **Forecast Application** (1 mark) — Multiply the macro regional forecast by the projected micro market share to obtain the subject airport's demand.

6. **Validation and Reconciliation** (1.5 marks) — Cross-check top-down (macro) and bottom-up (micro) results; iterate assumptions until consistency is achieved; present peak-hour, busy-day, and annual figures.

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

The macro-level approach treats the entire region as a single demand unit and correlates its total passenger volume with socio-economic drivers.

**Model structure (multiple linear regression):**

$$
P_{region} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{GDP} + \beta_2 \cdot \text{Pop} + \beta_3 \cdot \text{Tour} + \beta_4 \cdot \text{Trade} + \varepsilon
$$

where $\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_4$ are regression coefficients estimated by least-squares, and $\varepsilon$ is the error term.

**Step-by-step logic (1 mark per step):**
1. Collect 15–20 years of regional GDP, population, tourism, and trade data from RBI, MoT, and state economic surveys.
2. Plot scatter plots to verify linear correlation between each driver and passenger volume.
3. Estimate $\beta$ coefficients using Ordinary Least Squares (OLS).
4. Test statistical significance ($R^2$, $p$-values) — drop non-significant variables.
5. Plug in *future* values of the drivers (from Planning Commission / World Bank projections) to generate the future regional passenger forecast.
6. The output is a single regional demand curve, e.g., *"Kerala will require 35 MPPA capacity by 2040."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** The macro approach does not specify *which* airport captures this demand — hence the need for micro analysis as a follow-up. **(1 mark for stating this limitation.)**

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**Q.B. (a)** Discuss the micro-level approach with a focus on catchment area definition and market share. **(7 marks)**
**Q.B. (b)** Explain the role of price elasticity and income elasticity in air travel demand forecasting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

Micro-level analysis allocates the macro regional forecast to individual airports using two key concepts: **catchment area** and **market share**.

**Catchment Area Definition (3.5 marks):**
- The catchment is the geographic zone from which an airport can reasonably attract passengers, bounded by an **isochrone** of 60–120 minutes of ground travel time.
- For major airports, the simple isochrone is replaced by a **gravity model**:

$$
T_{ij} = \frac{P_j}{d_{ij}^{\alpha}}
$$

where $T_{ij}$ is the propensity of population zone $j$ to use airport $i$, $P_j$ is the population of zone $j$, $d_{ij}$ is the travel distance, and $\alpha \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ is the distance-decay exponent.

- Competing airports' catchments often **overlap**; the model sums the share from all zones to derive the airport's total addressable market.

**Market Share Computation (3.5 marks):**
- Market share $S_i$ is the ratio of the airport's current passengers to the regional total.
- **Forecast application:** $P_i^{\text{future}} = S_i \times P_{region}^{\text{future}}$.
- **Refinement:** Adjust $S_i$ using a service-quality index $Q_i$ (combining fare, frequency, connectivity):

$$
S_i^{\text{adjusted}} = \frac{S_i \cdot Q_i}{\sum_j (S_j \cdot Q_j)}
$$

This allows the forecast to *respond* to expected service changes (e.g., a new airline launching from the airport).

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

Elasticity measures the **sensitivity** of passenger demand to a change in an economic variable.

**Price Elasticity of Demand (3.5 marks):**

$$
E_p = \frac{\%\Delta Q_{\text{passengers}}}{\%\Delta P_{\text{fare}}}
$$

- $E_p$ is **negative** (law of demand). For Indian air travel, $E_p \approx -0.8$ to $-1.5$.
- **Interpretation:** If $E_p = -1.2$, a 10% fare increase reduces passenger volume by approximately 12%.
- **Use in forecasting:** When an airline announces a fare hike, the planner adjusts the micro demand downward by $E_p \times \Delta\text{fare}\%$.

**Income Elasticity of Demand (3.5 marks):**

$$
E_d = \frac{\%\Delta Q_{\text{passengers}}}{\%\Delta \text{GDP per capita}}
$$

- $E_d$ is **positive** — air travel is a *luxury* good with $E_d > 1$ (typically 1.2 to 1.8 in developing economies).
- **Interpretation:** A 5% rise in GDP per capita produces a 6%–9% rise in air travel demand.
- **Use in forecasting:** Macro regression uses projected GDP growth, and the elasticity converts that into a passenger demand multiplier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Insight:** India's high $E_d$ (>1) is precisely why the Indian aviation market is forecast to grow 2×–3× faster than GDP — a fact the macro forecast must capture. **(Optional 1 bonus mark for this insight.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing macro and micro outputs** — Macro gives *regional total*; micro gives *airport-specific* share. Mixing them in the final answer loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using linear extrapolation by default** — India is in a growth phase; exponential or regression-based forecasts are expected.
> 3. **Forgetting reconciliation** — The final forecast must pass the *top-down = bottom-up* check. Skipping this validation step costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Stating elasticity without sign convention** — Always mention that $E_p < 0$ and $E_d > 0$ for air travel.
> 5. **Ignoring the time horizon** — A 5-year forecast and a 20-year forecast require very different methodologies; KTU questions will penalize you for not specifying the horizon.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Air Travel Demand Forecasting** is the quantitative foundation of every airport master plan; it precedes runway, terminal, and apron design.
- **Macro Analysis** = national/regional scale, socio-economic drivers, regression models, top-down approach.
- **Micro Analysis** = airport scale, catchment isochrone, market-share model, bottom-up approach.
- **Final Forecast Workflow:** Macro regional total → Micro market share → Subject airport demand → Reconciliation.
- **Key formulas to memorize:** CAGR $g = (P_t/P_0)^{1/t} - 1$, Exponential forecast $P_t = P_0(1+g)^t$, Market share $S_i = P_i / \sum P_j$, Elasticity $E = \%\Delta Q / \%\Delta X$.
- **Standard units:** MPPA (millions of passengers per annum) and ATM/year (aircraft movements per year).
- **Elasticity signs to remember:** $E_p < 0$ (price elasticity, negative); $E_d > 0$ and typically > 1 (income elasticity, positive, luxury good).
- **Catchment boundary:** 60–120 minute drive-time isochrone, with gravity-model refinement for overlapping catchments.
- **Validation step is non-negotiable** — the macro and micro outputs must reconcile before the forecast is approved.
- **Indian context:** Prefer exponential / regression models; assume $E_d \approx 1.2$–$1.8$ and $E_p \approx -0.8$–$-1.5$ unless project-specific data is given.
- **Always state the planning horizon** (typically 5/10/15/20 years) at the start of your KTU answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Airfield Capacity: Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET744 - AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Airfield Capacity: Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AIRFIELD CAPACITY (KTU Standard Definition):**
> *Airfield capacity* is the maximum number of aircraft operations (takeoffs and landings) that can be accommodated at an airport's airside components (runways, taxiways, aprons, and gates) over a specified time interval, under a given set of operational, meteorological, and air traffic control (ATC) conditions, while maintaining an acceptable level of service and safety as prescribed by **FAA AC 150/5060-5** and **ICAO Annex 14** standards.

In the context of the **KTU PECET744 (Airport Planning and Design)** syllabus, airfield capacity is sub-divided into three interconnected functional capacities:

1. **Runway (Operational) Capacity** — The throughput of the runway system, expressed in *operations per hour* or *movements per hour*.
2. **Taxiway Capacity** — The maximum flow of aircraft between runway and apron.
3. **Apron / Gate Capacity** — The holding and servicing throughput at terminal piers.

The composite measure used by the FAA is the **Annual Service Volume (ASV)** — a single-number indicator representing the *annual* level of operations the airfield can sustain.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **REAL-WORLD ANALOGY — "The Highway Toll Plaza"**
> Imagine an airport runway as a **multi-lane toll plaza** on a busy national highway. The toll booth (runway) can process cars (aircraft) only as fast as:
> - the **booth operator** (ATC) can hand out tickets,
> - the **car type** allows (a truck takes longer than a bike → heavy jets need longer separation than small turboprops),
> - the **weather** permits (fog = stop-and-go traffic; clear sky = free flow),
> - and the **parking lot beyond** (apron/gate) has space to receive incoming cars.

If *any* one of these is slow, the *entire system* slows down. **Airfield capacity = the bottleneck of this chain.** This is the *system-balance principle* of airport planning.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Hourly Capacity (C_h)** — *operations per hour*.
- **Practical Hourly Capacity (PHCAP)** — throughput at a target *delay of 4 minutes/operation* (FAA standard).
- **Saturation Capacity (C_s)** — theoretical maximum under *continuous demand*.
- **Annual Service Volume (ASV)** — *operations per year* (FAA/ICAO).
- **Service Volume Ratio (SVR)** — actual annual demand ÷ ASV.
- **Aircraft Mix Index (MI)** — *% of Class C aircraft* used in capacity correction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT (Module 4):**
> The student is expected to define *airfield capacity*, list and explain the **factors affecting runway capacity**, compute capacity adjustments for *meteorological conditions, runway configuration, aircraft mix, and touch-and-go operations*, and interpret **ASV** for long-range planning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of the Concept

The term *"airfield capacity"* in KTU Module 4 is **not** a single number — it is a *function* of several interacting parameters. We must analyze it layer by layer:

### Layer A — What is being measured?
- **Capacity (C)** is a *supply* indicator: maximum flow the system *can* handle.
- It is **distinct** from *delay* (a *performance* indicator) and *demand* (a *traffic* indicator).
- Mathematically: $C = f(\text{runways, mix, weather, exits, ATC rules})$.

### Layer B — The "Three Capacities" Hierarchy

| Sub-Capacity | Symbol | Typical Unit | Bottleneck Driver |
|---|---|---|---|
| Runway Capacity | $C_r$ | ops/hr | Separation minima, mix, configuration |
| Taxiway Capacity | $C_t$ | ops/hr | Geometry, exit spacing, crossings |
| Gate/Apron Capacity | $C_g$ | gates/hr | Aircraft turn-time, gate occupancy |

> The **airfield capacity** is governed by the *smallest* of these three — the *system-limiting* capacity.

### Layer C — The Six Principal Factors (KTU Exam Focus)

1. **Runway Configuration** (number, orientation, separation of parallel runways).
2. **Aircraft Mix** (proportion of heavy / large / small jets).
3. **Meteorological Conditions** (VFR vs. IFR — Visual vs. Instrument Flight Rules).
4. **Touch-and-Go Operations** (training flights reduce capacity).
5. **Exit Taxiway Layout** (number, type, location of runway exits).
6. **Air Traffic Control Procedures** (radar vs. non-radar, runway occupancy time).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly exam-expected** under KTU 2024 Scheme ESE for PECET744 Module 4. Master the symbol definitions before applying.

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_{i} \cdot E(T_{i})$ | Basic capacity–flow relationship; $E(T_i)$ = time slot for class $i$, $\mu_i$ = service rate. |
| 2 | $T_{ij} = \gamma \cdot t_{ij}$ | Time-headway between aircraft classes $i$ and $j$; $\gamma$ = wake-turbulence factor, $t_{ij}$ = base separation. |
| 3 | $C = \dfrac{3600}{E[T_{s}]}$ | Hourly capacity from mean inter-arrival service time $E[T_s]$ in seconds. |
| 4 | $C^{\*} = C \cdot J \cdot N$ | Adjusted capacity: $J$ = % mix correction, $N$ = number of runways. |
| 5 | $C_{\text{IFR}} = C_{\text{VFR}} \cdot f_{\text{wx}}$ | IFR capacity (weather adjustment), $f_{\text{wx}} \in [0.5, 1.0]$. |
| 6 | $\text{ASV} = C_{h} \cdot D \cdot H$ | Annual Service Volume; $D$ = daily demand factor, $H$ = hourly ratio. |
| 7 | $\text{SVR} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Annual Demand}}{\text{ASV}}$ | Service Volume Ratio: SVR $\leq 0.6$ ideal; $\geq 1.0$ saturated. |
| 8 | $\text{MI} = \dfrac{(C + 3D)}{D + 3D} \times 100$ | FAA Mix Index: $C$ = heavy jets, $D$ = large jets during peak. |
| 9 | $\tau_{\text{rot}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{exit}}}{V_{\text{taxi}}}$ | Runway occupancy time to reach exit; $L$ = distance to exit, $V$ = taxi speed. |
| 10 | $C_{tg} = C \cdot (1 - p_{tg})$ | Capacity reduction due to touch-and-go fraction $p_{tg}$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM TECHNIQUE:** Always state the **assumed operating condition** (VFR / IFR, single / parallel runway, mix class) *before* substituting values. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing assumptions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Master Planning** — Determines whether an airport needs a *second parallel runway* within 10–20 years.
- **Investment Justification** — Government bodies (AAI, FAA) sanction runway expansion using ASV-based SVR.
- **Environmental Impact** — Capacity ceiling dictates noise contour, fuel-burn footprint, and community annoyance models.
- **ATC Optimization** — Capacity studies drive decisions on introducing *PBN (Performance-Based Navigation)*, *SIDs/STARs*, and *A-CDM* (Airport Collaborative Decision Making).
- **Economic Yield** — Gate capacity directly maps to *airline revenue* and *non-aeronautical concession* earnings.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Capacity Equation

> The **headway** $T_{ij}$ is the heart of runway capacity. Each aircraft *leaves a wake* — heavier aircraft (ICAO Super/Heavy) require longer trail intervals. Thus capacity is **inversely proportional** to the weighted mean headway of the aircraft mix.
> $$C \propto \dfrac{1}{\bar{T}}$$
> The smaller the *headway*, the higher the *capacity*, and the more the airport is *saturated*. This is why **wake-turbulence categories (A380, Heavy, Large, Small)** are central to KTU exam questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Basic Runway Capacity from Mean Headway

> **Starting Premise (FAA AC 150/5060-5):**
> If $E[T_s]$ is the mean service time (in seconds) consumed per aircraft operation on the runway, the runway can theoretically serve $3600 / E[T_s]$ operations in one hour.

### Step 1 — Define the service-time headway

For a homogeneous stream of aircraft, the *expected headway* between two successive operations is the sum of the time required for the first aircraft to vacate the runway (occupancy time) and the minimum separation imposed by ATC and wake-turbulence rules:

$$E[T_s] = T_{\text{occ}} + T_{\text{sep}}$$

where:
- $T_{\text{occ}}$ = runway occupancy time (seconds), typically **45–90 s** for medium jets.
- $T_{\text{sep}}$ = minimum time/position separation imposed by ATC wake-turbulence rules.

### Step 2 — Express the headway in terms of aircraft classes

When the traffic stream contains $K$ classes of aircraft, the headway between a trailing class-$i$ and leading class-$j$ aircraft is:

$$T_{ij} = \gamma_{ij} \cdot t_{\text{base}}$$

with the wake-turbulence multiplier $\gamma_{ij}$ taking values from the ICAO matrix (e.g., Heavy followed by Small: $\gamma \approx 1.5$).

### Step 3 — Compute the weighted mean headway

The expected headway over the stochastic mix is:

$$\bar{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{j=1}^{K} p_i \cdot p_j \cdot T_{ij}$$

where $p_i, p_j$ are the fractions of aircraft in classes $i$ and $j$.

### Step 4 — Compute the hourly capacity

The capacity (operations per hour) is the reciprocal of the mean headway, scaled from seconds to hours:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_h &= \dfrac{3600}{\bar{T}} \quad \text{[ops/hr]} \\[6pt]
C_h &= \dfrac{3600}{\sum_{i,j} p_i p_j \, T_{ij}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Sanity check

If all aircraft are Light/Class A with $T_{ij} = 90$ s, then $C_h = 3600/90 = 40$ ops/hr. For a *pure Heavy* stream with $T_{ij} = 120$ s, $C_h = 30$ ops/hr. These match the FAA single-runway capacity ranges, confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capacity Adjustment for Aircraft Mix (FAA Mix Index)

The FAA simplifies the heterogeneous mix using the **Mix Index (MI)**:

$$\text{MI} = \dfrac{C + 3D}{A + B + C + 3D} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$

where:
- $A$ = % Small single-engine aircraft.
- $B$ = % Small twin-engine aircraft.
- $C$ = % Large aircraft (B-737, A-320 class).
- $D$ = % Heavy aircraft (B-777, A-330, B-747 class).

The **Mix Index correction factor** $J$ (FAA AC 150/5060-5, Table 4-1) is then read from standard tables:

$$J = f(\text{MI})$$

And the capacity becomes:

$$C_{\text{adjusted}} = C_{\text{base}} \cdot J$$

with $J \in [0.6, 1.0]$, since heavier mixes always *reduce* capacity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — IFR Weather Capacity Reduction

Under **IFR** (Instrument Flight Rules — ceiling < 1000 ft or visibility < 3 mi), separation minima increase. The FAA empirical relationship is:

$$C_{\text{IFR}} = f_{\text{wx}} \cdot C_{\text{VFR}}$$

where $f_{\text{wx}}$ is the **weather adjustment factor** (Table 5-1, FAA AC 150/5060-5):

| Configuration | $f_{\text{wx}}$ (IFR) |
|---|---|
| Single runway | **0.50 – 0.60** |
| Two parallel runways (≥ 4 300 ft apart) | **0.65 – 0.75** |
| Three parallel runways | **0.70 – 0.80** |

> [!NOTE]
> **PHYSICAL INTERPRETATION:**
> Under IFR, the *radar-vector spacing* required by ATC increases from ~3 NM to ~5–6 NM, lengthening $\bar{T}$ and hence dropping $C_h$. This is why **Cochin/Kochi (VOCI)** or **Trivandrum (VOTV)** airports have *drastically lower* capacity during the south-west monsoon (June–September).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Touch-and-Go Capacity Reduction

Training flights (touch-and-go, $T_g$) "re-circulate" on the runway with shorter headway, but they must be *mixed* with full-stop operations. The effective capacity is:

$$C_{tg} = C \cdot (1 - \alpha \cdot p_{tg})$$

where:
- $p_{tg}$ = fraction of touch-and-go operations in the demand mix.
- $\alpha$ = empirical FAA constant (≈ 0.3 for Class C airports).

**Numerical example (KTU-style):**
Base capacity $C = 60$ ops/hr, $p_{tg} = 0.20$ (20% touch-and-go), $\alpha = 0.3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{tg} &= 60 \times \big(1 - 0.3 \times 0.20\big) \\
       &= 60 \times (1 - 0.06) \\
       &= 60 \times 0.94 \\
       &= 56.4 \;\text{ops/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

The training mix reduces capacity by **3.6 ops/hr** (≈ 6% loss).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Annual Service Volume (ASV) from Hourly Capacity

The **ASV** is the master planning figure. From hourly capacity $C_h$ and the airport's *demand profile*:

$$\text{ASV} = C_h \cdot D \cdot H \quad \text{[ops/year]}$$

where:
- $D$ = ratio of average daily demand to average *peak-hour* demand (typically **D = 10** for hub airports).
- $H$ = ratio of *average annual daily operations* to *peak-month average daily operations* (typically **H = 290**).

**Example:** A single-runway airport with $C_h = 50$ ops/hr, $D = 10$, $H = 290$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ASV} &= 50 \times 10 \times 290 \\
           &= 145\,000 \;\text{operations/year}
\end{aligned}$$

Now compute the **SVR** for a current demand of 100 000 ops/yr:

$$\text{SVR} = \dfrac{100\,000}{145\,000} = 0.69$$

**Interpretation (FAA bands):**

| SVR Range | Service Quality |
|---|---|
| $\leq 0.6$ | Excellent — minimal delay |
| 0.6 – 0.8 | Good — moderate delay |
| 0.8 – 1.0 | Poor — significant delay |
| $\geq 1.0$ | Saturated — capacity expansion mandatory |

The example airport is in the **"Good"** band — *no expansion needed in the next 5 years*.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Factor Dominates?"

| Scenario | Dominant Factor | Capacity Impact |
|---|---|---|
| South-west monsoon at KTM/VOCB | IFR weather | $f_{\text{wx}} \approx 0.55$ |
| Two parallel runways, < 4 300 ft apart | Closely-spaced parallel limitation | $C \downarrow \downarrow$ |
| 80% turboprop training (e.g., Calicut) | Touch-and-go fraction | $C \downarrow 5$–$8\%$ |
| Single high-speed exit instead of 2 | Exit geometry | $T_{\text{occ}} \uparrow$ |
| 50% wide-body heavy mix | Aircraft mix (MI > 80) | $J \approx 0.6$ |
| No radar vectoring (procedural ATC) | ATC procedure | $\bar{T} \uparrow 15$–$25\%$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Airfield Capacity — Hierarchical Block Architecture (Mermaid)

The following block diagram shows the **functional flow** of the *six principal capacity factors* into the final **ASV/SVR** output used by planners. Use this as your KTU exam sketch — it is exam-credit-eligible.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Runway Configuration] --> B1[Base Capacity Cb]
    A2[Aircraft Mix Index MI] --> B1
    A3[IFR or VFR Weather] --> B2[Weather Factor fwx]
    A4[Touch and Go Fraction] --> B3[Training Penalty]
    A5[Exit Taxiway Layout] --> B4[Occupancy Time Tocc]
    A6[ATC Procedures] --> B5[Headway Increase dT]
    B1 --> C[Saturation Capacity Cs]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> D[Hourly Capacity Ch]
    D --> E[Daily Factor D]
    E --> F[Hourly Ratio H]
    F --> G[Annual Service Volume ASV]
    H[Actual Annual Demand] --> I[Service Volume Ratio SVR]
    G --> I
    I --> J{Decision: Expand or Not}
    J --> K[SVR le 0.6: No expansion]
    J --> L[0.6 to 0.8: Monitor]
    J --> M[0.8 to 1.0: Plan runway]
    J --> N[SVR ge 1.0: Urgent expansion]
```

## 4.2 Capacity-Factor Interaction Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Aircraft Mix] -->|affects headway| H[Weighted Mean Headway Tbar]
    F2[Weather IFR or VFR] -->|scales separation| H
    F3[Touch and Go] -->|reduces C| H
    F4[Exit Geometry] -->|changes Tocc| H
    F5[ATC Rules] -->|sets minima| H
    F6[Runway Config] -->|scales by N| CAP[Hourly Capacity Ch]
    H --> CAP
    CAP --> ASV[Annual Service Volume]
    ASV --> SVR[Service Volume Ratio]
    SVR --> DEC[Planning Decision]
```

## 4.3 Capacity Cascade — From Single Aircraft to ASV (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Single Aircraft Operation] --> occ[Runway Occupancy Time Tocc]
    start --> sep[Separation Minima Tsep]
    occ --> hs[Mean Headway Tbar sec]
    sep --> hs
    hs --> ch[Hourly Capacity Ch ops per hr]
    ch --> cd[Daily Capacity Cd ops per day]
    cd --> asv[Annual Service Volume ASV ops per yr]
    asv --> svr[Service Volume Ratio SVR]
    svr --> plan[Master Plan Output]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECET744]**
**Define "Airfield Capacity". Distinguish between *Saturation Capacity* and *Practical Hourly Capacity (PHCAP)*. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Verbatim Style for KTU Valuation):**

> **Airfield capacity** is defined (per FAA AC 150/5060-5) as the maximum number of aircraft operations that the runway system can accommodate in a specified time interval under given operational and weather conditions.
>
> - **Saturation Capacity (C_s):** The *theoretical maximum throughput* under *continuous, uninterrupted demand*; demand equals or exceeds capacity and the runway is fully occupied throughout the hour. It represents the *upper bound* and is **not** the design value.
> - **Practical Hourly Capacity (PHCAP):** The throughput corresponding to a *target average delay of 4 minutes per operation* (FAA criterion). It is *lower* than C_s and is the value actually used in *capacity analysis* and *facility planning*.
>
> The relationship is: $C_s > C_h$ (PHCAP), with the gap widening as the *aircraft mix* becomes heavier and the *weather* deteriorates to IFR.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for distinguishing each term clearly. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECET744]**
**List any SIX factors affecting runway capacity and state how each one influences it. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Runway configuration** — Number & orientation of runways; more runways = multiplicative increase (factor N).
2. **Aircraft mix** — Heavier mix (more Class C & D) → lower capacity via higher wake-turbulence separations.
3. **Meteorological conditions** — IFR requires ~5 NM radar spacing vs. ~3 NM VFR, dropping $C_h$ by 30–50%.
4. **Touch-and-go operations** — Recirculating training traffic reduces headway but disturbs the mix.
5. **Exit taxiway geometry** — More exits & optimal placement reduce $T_{\text{occ}}$, increasing $C_h$.
6. **ATC procedures** — Radar vectoring allows tighter intervals than procedural (non-radar) control.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Each question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer *one full question* (either OR-pattern within the same module).

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PECET744, Module 4]**

**(a) Explain in detail the FAA method of computing airfield capacity for a *single runway* under VFR and IFR conditions. Discuss how aircraft mix and touch-and-go operations are accounted for. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the runway system and the operating condition.**
A *single runway* is the simplest case. Under **VFR (Visual Flight Rules)**, separation minima are lower (3 NM radar spacing); under **IFR (Instrument Flight Rules)**, they increase to 5–6 NM, and ATC service rate drops.

**Step 2 — Compute the base hourly capacity from headway.**
The FAA assumes a *mean runway occupancy time* $T_{\text{occ}} \approx 50$ s for a typical medium jet, and a *mean inter-arrival separation* $T_{\text{sep}}$ as per wake-turbulence class:

$$\bar{T} = T_{\text{occ}} + T_{\text{sep}}$$

For a *homogeneous mix* (e.g., 100% Class B small twins), $T_{\text{sep}} = 60$ s. Hence:

$$C_{h,\text{VFR}} = \dfrac{3600}{50 + 60} = \dfrac{3600}{110} \approx 32.7 \;\text{ops/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the IFR weather factor.**
From FAA tables, single-runway $f_{\text{wx}} \approx 0.55$:

$$C_{h,\text{IFR}} = 32.7 \times 0.55 \approx 18 \;\text{ops/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Account for aircraft mix using the Mix Index (MI).**
Define $C$ = % large, $D$ = % heavy; then:

$$\text{MI} = \dfrac{C + 3D}{A + B + C + 3D} \times 100$$

Read the corresponding correction factor $J$ from FAA Table 4-1. For MI = 50, $J \approx 0.85$; apply:

$$C_{\text{adj}} = C_{h,\text{VFR}} \times J = 32.7 \times 0.85 \approx 27.8 \;\text{ops/hr}$$

**Step 5 — Account for touch-and-go fraction $p_{tg}$.**

$$C_{tg} = C_{\text{adj}} \times (1 - 0.3 p_{tg})$$

For $p_{tg} = 0.15$:

$$C_{tg} = 27.8 \times (1 - 0.045) = 27.8 \times 0.955 \approx 26.6 \;\text{ops/hr}$$

**Step 6 — State engineering interpretation.**
The capacity drops from 32.7 → 26.6 ops/hr (≈ 19% loss) due to *all factors combined*. This is the **Practical Hourly Capacity (PHCAP)** if the mix delay criterion (4 min/operation) is met.

*(Marks split: 2 marks FAA concept, 2 marks VFR/IFR distinction, 2 marks MI and touch-and-go formulas, 1 mark interpretation.)*

---

**(b) An airport has a single runway with the following hourly capacity data:**
- $C_{h,\text{VFR, base}} = 50$ ops/hr
- Aircraft mix correction factor $J = 0.80$
- IFR weather factor $f_{\text{wx}} = 0.55$
- Touch-and-go fraction $p_{tg} = 0.10$, $\alpha = 0.30$
- Daily demand factor $D = 10$, hourly ratio $H = 290$

**Compute: (i) Practical Hourly Capacity under VFR, (ii) Practical Hourly Capacity under IFR, (iii) Annual Service Volume (ASV), (iv) Service Volume Ratio (SVR) for an actual annual demand of 110 000 ops/yr. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**(i) PHCAP under VFR:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{VFR, mix}} &= C_{h,\text{VFR, base}} \times J \\
                    &= 50 \times 0.80 = 40.0 \;\text{ops/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

Apply touch-and-go correction:

$$C_{\text{VFR,PHCAP}} = 40 \times (1 - 0.30 \times 0.10) = 40 \times 0.97 = 38.8 \;\text{ops/hr}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{VFR,PHCAP}} \approx 38.8 \;\text{ops/hr}}$$

**(ii) PHCAP under IFR:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{IFR,PHCAP}} &= C_{\text{VFR,PHCAP}} \times f_{\text{wx}} \\
                     &= 38.8 \times 0.55 = 21.34 \;\text{ops/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{IFR,PHCAP}} \approx 21.3 \;\text{ops/hr}}$$

**(iii) Annual Service Volume (ASV):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ASV} &= C_{h,\text{PHCAP,VFR}} \times D \times H \\
           &= 38.8 \times 10 \times 290 \\
           &= 112\,520 \;\text{ops/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{ASV} \approx 112\,500 \;\text{ops/yr}}$$

**(iv) Service Volume Ratio (SVR):**

$$\text{SVR} = \dfrac{110\,000}{112\,520} = 0.978$$

$$\boxed{\text{SVR} \approx 0.98 \; \Rightarrow \; \text{"Poor" service band, expansion planning required within 5 years.}}$$

*(Marks split: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 1.5 marks, (iii) 1.5 marks, (iv) 2 marks including interpretation.)*

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024, PECET744, Module 4]**

**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the hierarchical relationship between *airfield capacity*, *runway capacity*, *taxiway capacity*, and *gate/apron capacity*. Why is the *smallest* of the three used for planning? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block-Diagram Explanation (4 marks):**

The airport airside system forms a *series chain* — the *bottleneck governs* the throughput. The hierarchy is:

> Demand → **Gate/Apron Capacity** → **Taxiway Capacity** → **Runway Capacity** → Departure

If the *gate capacity* is, say, 30 ops/hr, but the *runway capacity* is 60 ops/hr, the system cannot exceed **30 ops/hr** — regardless of how good the runway is.

**Mathematical statement (2 marks):**

$$C_{\text{airfield}} = \min\{C_{\text{gate}},\, C_{\text{taxiway}},\, C_{\text{runway}}\}$$

**Planning implication (1 mark):**
This is the *system-balance principle* used in IATA Airport Development Reference Manual (ADRM) and FAA AC 150/5070-6B (Airport Master Plans). Expansion of any single component without balancing the others is *futile investment*.

---

**(b) An airport has Annual Service Volume (ASV) = 200 000 ops. The current annual demand is 150 000 ops/yr. The hourly capacity is 60 ops/hr. If the airport authority wants to *maintain SVR ≤ 0.7*, what is the *minimum hourly capacity* required for the next 20 years, assuming demand grows at 4% per annum (compound)? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the future demand after 20 years (compound growth).**

$$D_{20} = D_0 \times (1 + r)^n = 150\,000 \times (1.04)^{20}$$

Compute $(1.04)^{20}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
(1.04)^{20} &= e^{20 \ln(1.04)} = e^{20 \times 0.03922} = e^{0.7844} \approx 2.191
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{D_{20} = 150\,000 \times 2.191 \approx 328\,650 \;\text{ops/yr}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the SVR constraint.**

$$\text{SVR} = \dfrac{D_{20}}{C_h \cdot D \cdot H} \leq 0.7$$

With $D = 10$, $H = 290$:

$$C_h \geq \dfrac{D_{20}}{0.7 \times D \times H} = \dfrac{328\,650}{0.7 \times 10 \times 290} = \dfrac{328\,650}{2\,030}$$

$$\boxed{C_{h,\min} \approx 162 \;\text{ops/hr}}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering recommendation.**
A single runway typically gives at most **70 ops/hr** (VFR, ideal mix). To achieve 162 ops/hr, the airport needs:
- 2 close-parallel runways with staggered thresholds (~80–100 ops/hr), **OR**
- 2 independent parallel runways ≥ 4 300 ft apart (~120–160 ops/hr), **OR**
- 3 parallel runways (saturated hub).

**Conclusion:** A *second independent parallel runway* is mandatory within the next 10–12 years.

*(Marks split: Step 1 – 2 marks, Step 2 – 3 marks, Step 3 – 2 marks for engineering interpretation.)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — Read Before You Write!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the VFR/IFR distinction** — Always specify the operating condition. Mixing VFR headway with IFR mix factor loses **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Applying $J$ incorrectly** — The Mix Index factor $J$ is *not* 1.0. Many students forget to apply it after computing the base $C_h$, leading to a **30–50% over-estimation** of capacity.
> 3. **Touch-and-go sign error** — The formula is $C_{tg} = C(1 - \alpha p_{tg})$, *not* $C(1 + \alpha p_{tg})$. Touch-and-go *reduces* capacity, not increases it, in the FAA method.
> 4. **ASV unit confusion** — ASV is *annual* (ops/yr), $C_h$ is *hourly* (ops/hr). Many students divide instead of multiply by $D \times H$. Penalty: full marks for ASV computation lost.
> 5. **SVR band interpretation missing** — Always end your ASV/SVR problem with a *band statement* (Excellent / Good / Poor / Saturated). Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** for the verbal interpretation.
> 6. **No assumption statement** — Every numerical answer must begin with "Assume: VFR, single runway, Mix Index = …" Examiners award **1 mark** for stated assumptions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION LIST — Airfield Capacity: Factors**
>
> ☐ **Definition** — Airfield capacity is the *maximum sustainable operations/hr (or /yr)*; the bottleneck of runway + taxiway + gate chain.
> ☐ **Capacity vs. Demand vs. Delay** — Capacity = supply; Demand = traffic; Delay = performance metric.
> ☐ **Three Sub-Capacities** — Runway, Taxiway, Gate/Apron — system-limited by the *minimum*.
> ☐ **Six Key Factors** — (1) Runway configuration, (2) Aircraft mix, (3) Weather, (4) Touch-and-go, (5) Exit geometry, (6) ATC procedures.
> ☐ **VFR vs. IFR** — VFR ≈ 3 NM spacing, IFR ≈ 5–6 NM. IFR capacity = $f_{\text{wx}} \times C_{\text{VFR}}$, $f_{\text{wx}} \in [0.5, 0.8]$.
> ☐ **FAA Mix Index (MI)** — $\text{MI} = (C + 3D)/(A + B + C + 3D) \times 100$; correction factor $J$ reduces $C_h$ for heavy mixes.
> ☐ **Touch-and-Go Penalty** — $C_{tg} = C(1 - \alpha p_{tg})$; reduces capacity by up to ~10%.
> ☐ **PHCAP** — Throughput at *4-min/operation* average delay (FAA criterion).
> ☐ **Saturation Capacity $C_s$** — Theoretical max under continuous demand; **not** the design value.
> ☐ **ASV** — $\text{ASV} = C_h \times D \times H$; standard values $D = 10$, $H = 290$.
> ☐ **SVR** — SVR ≤ 0.6 excellent, 0.6–0.8 good, 0.8–1.0 poor, ≥ 1.0 saturated.
> ☐ **KTU Formula Mastery** — Be fluent with $\bar{T}$, $C_h = 3600/\bar{T}$, $C^{\*} = C \cdot J \cdot N$, ASV, SVR.
> ☐ **ICAO Wake Categories** — Super (A380), Heavy, Large, Small — directly govern $\gamma_{ij}$ in $T_{ij} = \gamma_{ij} t_{\text{base}}$.
> ☐ **Engineering Rule of Thumb** — A single runway maxes at ~60–70 ops/hr (VFR); two independent parallels can reach 120–160 ops/hr; hub airports need ≥ 3 parallels.
> ☐ **Exam-Edge Trick** — Always state operating conditions, units, and SVR band; these are the *easy* marks examiners use to rank answer sheets.
